topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is VLSI?

**VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration)** is the process of creating an **Integrated Circuit (IC)** by combining thousands to billions of **transistors** onto a single semiconductor die (chip) made primarily from **silicon**. The term follows a historical scaling nomenclature established by the semiconductor industry:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition**: VLSI refers to ICs containing between **10,000 and 99,999** logic gates (or roughly 20,000+ transistors) per chip. Below this is **SSI**, **MSI**, and **LSI**; above this lies **ULSI (Ultra Large Scale Integration)**.

The operational scope of VLSI engineering is not merely "drawing transistors." It is a **multi-layered, multi-domain discipline** that spans:
- **System Specification** (what the chip must do)
- **Architecture Design** (how it is structured)
- **Logic Design** (boolean implementation)
- **Circuit Design** (transistor-level realization)
- **Physical / Layout Design** (geometric mask patterns)
- **Fabrication & Testing** (manufacturing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A modern smartphone processor (e.g., Apple M-series or Qualcomm Snapdragon) contains **> 10 billion transistors** within a die area of roughly **100–200 mm²**, all operating at clock frequencies between **3–4 GHz** at power budgets of **5–15 W**.

### 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The City of Transistors"**  
> Imagine building an entire **metropolitan city** on a plot of land the size of your thumbnail.  
> - **Transistors** are the **citizens** (they perform actions).  
> - **Interconnects (wires)** are the **roads and highways** (they transport signals).  
> - **Power and clock distribution networks** are the **electrical grid and traffic signals**.  
> - **The fabrication process** is the **city's construction company**, building everything from a single blueprint.  
> - **VLSI Design Engineers** are the **urban planners, architects, and civil engineers**.  
> Just as a city must be planned for traffic, power, and population density, a chip must be planned for **timing, power, area, and signal integrity**. This is the essence of VLSI.

## 1.3 Why CMOS Dominates VLSI

The semiconductor industry overwhelmingly uses **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)** technology. The reasons are:

1. **Static power dissipation is theoretically zero** (no direct path from VDD to GND in steady state).
2. **High noise immunity** due to full voltage swing between rails.
3. **Scalability** — CMOS scales gracefully to nanometer dimensions.
4. **Manufacturing compatibility** with standard silicon processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard**: As of the 2024–2026 fabrication nodes, **3 nm FinFET CMOS** is in mass production (TSMC N3, Samsung 3GAP), with **2 nm Gate-All-Around (GAAFET)** in early production. KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with **micron and sub-micron CMOS** as the foundational technology.

## 1.4 Moore's Law — The Economic Engine of VLSI

**Moore's Law** is the empirical observation, made by **Gordon E. Moore (co-founder of Intel) in 1965**, that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately **every 18 to 24 months**, while the cost per transistor decreases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight**: Moore's Law is *not* a physical law; it is a **self-fulfilling industry roadmap** that drives capital investment, research goals, and process node generations.

The **Dennard Scaling** corollary (1974) further stated that as transistors shrink, **power density remains constant**, allowing clock frequency to rise. **Dennard scaling broke down around 2006**, which is why modern chip design shifted to **multi-core architectures** rather than aggressive frequency scaling.

| Metric | 1971 (Intel 4004) | 2024 (Apple M3 Max) |
|---|---|---|
| Transistor Count | 2,300 | ~92 Billion |
| Process Node | 10 µm | 3 nm |
| Clock Frequency | 0.74 MHz | ~4 GHz |
| Die Area | 12 mm² | ~600 mm² (multi-die) |
| Power | 0.5 W | ~50–90 W (package) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The VLSI Design Hierarchy (Abstraction Levels)

VLSI design is organized into **abstraction levels** to manage complexity. At each level, we hide irrelevant details and expose only what is needed.

| Level | Description | Key Unit | Primary Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| **System Level** | High-level functional behavior, performance specs | Algorithms, block diagrams | MATLAB, C/C++ models |
| **Behavioral Level** | Algorithmic behavior, no timing | HDL processes | SystemVerilog, VHDL |
| **Register-Transfer Level (RTL)** | Data flow between registers, clocked | Flip-flops, ALUs, muxes | Verilog / VHDL synthesis |
| **Logic Level** | Boolean equations, gate-level structure | AND, OR, NOT, XOR gates | Logic synthesis |
| **Circuit Level** | Transistor schematics with W/L ratios | MOSFETs, R, C | SPICE simulation |
| **Layout (Physical) Level** | Geometric mask patterns in silicon | Rectangles (polygons) | Cadence Virtuoso, Synopsys IC Compiler |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: A 14-mark question frequently asks you to **describe the VLSI design flow** and explain the role of verification at each step. Always close the loop with "verification is performed at every level."

## 2.2 The Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn Chart)

The **Gajski-Kuhn Y-chart** is the canonical diagram of VLSI design abstraction. It organizes design activities along **three axes (domains)** that meet at the center:

- **Behavioral Domain** (top axis): What the system does.
- **Structural Domain** (right axis): How it is composed of subsystems / gates / transistors.
- **Geometric / Physical Domain** (left axis): How it is laid out on silicon.

Each axis is divided into **levels of abstraction** (System → Algorithm → Register-Transfer → Logic → Circuit → Layout). The **outer ring** represents high-level (more abstract); the **center** represents the actual silicon layout. A design path travels from outer to inner, switching between domains as needed.

## 2.3 VLSI Design Styles

The KTU syllabus explicitly requires knowledge of design styles. These differ in **how much the designer customizes** vs. **how much the foundry / vendor provides**.

### 2.3.1 Full Custom Design
- **Every transistor and wire is hand-crafted** by the designer.
- Maximum performance, minimum area.
- Used for: **analog blocks, memory cells, standard cell library design, ultra-high-performance CPUs**.
- Disadvantage: **Enormous design time and cost**; high NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost.

### 2.3.2 Standard Cell Design
- Pre-designed **standard cells** (NAND, NOR, Flip-Flop, Adder, etc.) are placed in fixed-height rows with **automatic routing** between them.
- **Channel routing** or **over-the-cell routing** connects them.
- Used for: **most ASICs, microcontrollers, SoCs**.

### 2.3.3 Gate Array (GA) / Sea-of-Gates
- The foundry pre-fabricates a **master slice** of uncommitted transistors in a regular array.
- Designer only defines the **final metal interconnect layers** (customization layer).
- Faster turnaround, lower NRE, but **lower density and performance** than full custom.

### 2.3.4 Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)
- Pre-fabricated chip with **configurable logic blocks (CLBs)** and a programmable interconnect matrix.
- Programming is done via **SRAM cells, antifuses, or Flash**.
- Used for: **prototyping, low-volume products, reconfigurable computing**.
- Trade-off: **Slowest performance, largest area, highest unit cost**; but **lowest NRE** and instant turnaround.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Comparator**:
> 
> | Style | Density | Performance | NRE Cost | Unit Cost | Turnaround |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Full Custom | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ | Slowest |
> | Standard Cell | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | Slow |
> | Gate Array | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | Medium |
> | FPGA | ★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★★★★ | Fastest |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Scaling & Cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA — KTU 2024 Module 1**

| Formula | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $N = f \cdot A_D$ | Transistor count = (transistor density) × (die area) | Used in cost analysis |
| $D_0 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P}{A_D}$ | Defect density (D₀) where P = probability of zero defects on chip of area A | Poisson yield model |
| $Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A}$ | Yield = exp(−D₀ · A) | **Poisson yield model** |
| $C_{die} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{N_{die} \cdot Y}$ | Cost per die | C_wafer = wafer cost, N_die = dies per wafer, Y = yield |
| $N_{die} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (R/2)^2}{A} - \dfrac{\pi \cdot R}{\sqrt{2A}}$ | Dies per wafer approximation (R = wafer radius) | Includes edge losses |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety**: All absolute values and pipes in this table use `\vert` semantics implicitly; no raw `|` appears inside the math expressions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The VLSI design flow is the **backbone of every electronic product** in existence — from your TV remote (4-bit microcontroller, ~5,000 transistors) to data-center AI accelerators (tens of billions of transistors). Mastering this flow is essential for careers in:
- **ASIC Design** (Qualcomm, Intel, Samsung, MediaTek)
- **FPGA Design** (Xilinx/AMD, Intel/Altera, Lattice, Microchip)
- **Memory Design** (Micron, SK Hynix, Western Digital)
- **EDA Tool Development** (Synopsys, Cadence, Siemens EDA)
- **Foundry Process Engineering** (TSMC, GlobalFoundries, SMIC)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost per Die (C_die)

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU Module 1.

**Step 1 — Number of Dies per Wafer (N_die)**

A silicon wafer of radius $R$ (typically 150 mm, 200 mm, or 300 mm) has total usable area:

$$
A_{wafer} = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot N_{die}}}\right)
$$

For an approximate closed form (commonly used in KTU problems), we use:

$$
N_{die} \approx \frac{\pi \cdot R^2}{4 \cdot A} - \frac{\pi \cdot R}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A}}
$$

where $A$ is the area of a single die. The second term accounts for the **edge loss** when square dies are inscribed in a circular wafer.

**Step 2 — Defect Density and Yield**

The probability of finding a defect-free die follows the **Poisson distribution**. If $\lambda$ is the average number of defects per die, then:

$$
Y = P(\text{zero defects}) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \cdot \lambda^0}{0!} = e^{-\lambda}
$$

Substituting $\lambda = D_0 \cdot A$, where $D_0$ is the **defect density per unit area** (defects/cm²):

$$
Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost per Die**

If $C_{wafer}$ is the cost of processing a single wafer, and only a fraction $Y$ of the $N_{die}$ candidate dies are good, then:

$$
C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{die} \cdot Y} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{die} \cdot e^{-D_0 \cdot A}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{die}} \cdot e^{D_0 \cdot A}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: Cost per die grows **exponentially** with die area. This is why "**die shrink**" (moving to a smaller process node) is so valuable — it cuts $A$ dramatically, which cuts $C_{die}$ superlinearly. This is the economic force behind Moore's Law.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: A 300 mm wafer costs $C_{wafer} = \$5{,}000$ to process. Each die has area $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2$. Defect density is $D_0 = 0.5 \text{ defects/cm}^2$. Find $N_{die}$, $Y$, and $C_{die}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $N_{die}$**

Given $R = 300 \text{ mm} = 30 \text{ cm}$ and $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2$:

$$
N_{die} = \frac{\pi \cdot (30)^2}{4 \cdot 1} - \frac{\pi \cdot 30}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 1}}
$$

$$
N_{die} = \frac{\pi \cdot 900}{4} - \frac{30 \cdot \pi}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

$$
N_{die} = 225 \pi - 21.21 \pi
$$

$$
N_{die} = (225 - 21.21) \cdot 3.14159 \approx 640.4
$$

We discard the fractional die: $N_{die} \approx 640$ candidate dies per wafer.

**Step 2 — Compute Yield $Y$**

$$
Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A} = e^{-0.5 \cdot 1} = e^{-0.5}
$$

$$
Y \approx 0.6065 \quad \text{(i.e., } 60.65\% \text{ yield)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cost per Die**

$$
C_{die} = \frac{\$5{,}000}{640 \cdot 0.6065} = \frac{\$5{,}000}{388.16}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{die} \approx \$12.88 \text{ per die}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Variation**: If the same chip is shrunk to $A = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2$ (i.e., the die area halves), recompute to show the dramatic cost drop. This tests the student's grasp of the **exponential sensitivity** of cost to area.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Style Trade-offs

| Criterion | Full Custom | Standard Cell | Gate Array | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transistor density (gates/mm²) | 100% | 80–90% | 60–70% | 10–20% |
| Clock frequency achievable | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |
| Power per gate | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| Design effort (person-months) | 100+ | 10–30 | 5–15 | 0.5–3 |
| Mask cost (NRE) | Very High | High | Medium | Zero |
| Per-unit cost (high volume) | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| Time-to-market | Slowest | Slow | Medium | Fastest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Insight**: A common 7-mark sub-question asks: *"Justify why a startup building a low-volume IoT prototype should choose FPGA over full custom."* The answer must explicitly mention **NRE amortization, time-to-market, and reprogrammability**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VLSI Design Flow — Block Diagram

The VLSI design flow is a sequence of steps from specification to fabrication, with **verification loops** at every stage. Below is a Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow showing the major phases, their inputs/outputs, and feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification] --> B[Architecture Design]
    B --> C[RTL Design using HDL]
    C --> D[Functional Verification]
    D -->|Pass| E[Logic Synthesis]
    D -->|Fail| C
    E --> F[Gate Level Netlist]
    F --> G[Placement and Routing]
    G --> H[Layout Generation]
    H --> I[Physical Verification DRC LVS]
    I -->|Pass| J[Tapeout GDSII File]
    I -->|Fail| G
    J --> K[Fabrication in Wafer Fab]
    K --> L[Packaging and Testing]
    L --> M[Final IC Product]
    E --> N[Static Timing Analysis]
    G --> N
    N -->|Timing Fail| E
    E --> O[Power Analysis]
    G --> O
    O -->|Power Violation| B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each box is a **major design phase**.
- Forward arrows indicate the **natural progression** of the design.
- Backward arrows (Fail / Violation) indicate the **feedback loops** — the hallmark of iterative VLSI design.
- Verification runs in **parallel** with synthesis, place-and-route, and layout.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Question**: A 7-mark sub-question often asks: *"Draw the VLSI design flow and explain any two steps."* The diagram above is an exam-ready answer; you can redraw it cleanly on paper.

## 4.2 Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart — Functional Architecture

The Y-chart visualizes the **three design domains** (behavioral, structural, geometric) intersecting at multiple **abstraction levels** (system, algorithm, RTL, logic, circuit, layout).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DOM1[Behavioral Domain]
        B1[System Level Spec]
        B2[Algorithm Level]
        B3[RTL Level]
        B4[Logic Level]
        B5[Circuit Level]
        B6[Layout Level]
    end
    subgraph DOM2[Structural Domain]
        S1[System Block Diagram]
        S2[Processor Memory Bus]
        S3[ALU Register Mux]
        S4[Gates FlipFlops]
        S5[Transistor Schematics]
        S6[Standard Cell Library]
    end
    subgraph DOM3[Geometric Domain]
        G1[Floorplan Macros]
        G2[Module Placement]
        G3[Cell Placement]
        G4[Standard Cell Rows]
        G5[Transistor Layout]
        G6[Silicon Mask Layout]
    end
    B1 --- S1
    S1 --- G1
    B2 --- S2
    S2 --- G2
    B3 --- S3
    S3 --- G3
    B4 --- S4
    S4 --- G4
    B5 --- S5
    S5 --- G5
    B6 --- S6
    S6 --- G6
```

**How to read the Y-chart:**
- Each domain has **six concentric abstraction levels** (outermost = most abstract).
- A design path travels **inward** (from outer to center) and switches between domains as required.
- For example: Writing an **algorithm in C** (behavioral/algorithm) → Choosing a **CPU + memory architecture** (structural) → **Floorplanning the chip** (geometric).

## 4.3 Design Style Selection — Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Chip Requirement] --> Q1{Volume Required}
    Q1 -->|> 1 Million Units| Q2{Performance Critical}
    Q1 -->|< 10000 Units| FPGA[Use FPGA]
    Q1 -->|10k to 1M Units| Q3{Time to Market}
    Q2 -->|Yes Analog Mixed Signal| FullCustom[Full Custom Design]
    Q2 -->|No Digital Dominant| StandardCell[Standard Cell ASIC]
    Q3 -->|< 6 Months| GateArray[Gate Array ASIC]
    Q3 -->|> 12 Months| StandardCell
    FPGA --> Done[Implementation]
    FullCustom --> Done
    StandardCell --> Done
    GateArray --> Done
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip**: This decision tree is a **bonus diagram** that, if drawn correctly in a 14-mark design question, can earn full marks even with brief text. Always annotate each branch with a justification.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define VLSI. List the different integration levels of ICs with their gate counts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark)**: VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration) is the technology of integrating **10,000 to 99,999 logic gates** (or roughly 20,000 to 1,00,000 transistors) onto a single silicon chip.

**Integration Levels (2 marks):**

| Level | Full Form | Gate Count |
|---|---|---|
| SSI | Small Scale Integration | < 10 |
| MSI | Medium Scale Integration | 10 – 100 |
| LSI | Large Scale Integration | 100 – 9,999 |
| VLSI | Very Large Scale Integration | 10,000 – 99,999 |
| ULSI | Ultra Large Scale Integration | ≥ 100,000 |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is Moore's Law? Why is it considered the driving force of the semiconductor industry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark)**: Moore's Law, stated by **Gordon E. Moore in 1965**, observes that the **number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every 18 to 24 months**, with the cost per transistor halving in the same period.

**Justification (2 marks):**
- It set an **industry-wide roadmap** that motivated continuous R&D investment.
- It created an **economic virtuous cycle**: more transistors → more functionality → larger market → more revenue → more R&D budget.
- It enabled the **democratization of computing power** — from mainframes to smartphones.
- It drives **process node migration** (10 µm → 3 nm → 2 nm), which in turn enables **die shrink** and the cost-reduction formula $C_{die} \propto e^{D_0 \cdot A}$.

---

## Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is internally divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student answers **either Question A or Question B** (full internal choice).

### Question A (14 Marks) — VLSI Design Flow + Y-Chart

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **VLSI design flow** from system specification to fabrication. Highlight the importance of verification at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw and explain the **Gajski-Kuhn Y-chart**. Discuss how it represents the three domains of VLSI design and the levels of abstraction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) — VLSI Design Flow (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a **board-exam-ready, step-by-step model answer**.

**1. System Specification (1 mark)**
- The design starts with a **high-level specification** of the chip's functional requirements, performance targets (speed, power, area), and interface protocols.
- Output: a **functional specification document** and a **constraints file**.

**2. Architecture Design (0.5 marks)**
- The system is partitioned into **hardware and software blocks**.
- Major decisions: **RISC vs CISC**, **pipeline depth**, **cache size**, **bus architecture (AMBA, Wishbone)**.
- Output: a **block diagram** with defined interfaces.

**3. RTL Design (0.5 marks)**
- The architecture is implemented in a **Hardware Description Language (HDL)** such as **Verilog, SystemVerilog, or VHDL** at the **Register-Transfer Level**.
- Output: synthesizable HDL code.

**4. Functional Verification (1 mark)**
- **Simulation** using testbenches to verify that the RTL matches the specification.
- Tools: **ModelSim, QuestaSim, Synopsys VCS, Cadence Xcelium**.
- Coverage metrics: **code coverage, functional coverage, assertion-based verification**.

**5. Logic Synthesis (0.5 marks)**
- The HDL is translated into a **gate-level netlist** using a **standard cell library**.
- Tools: **Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus**.

**6. Placement and Routing (0.5 marks)**
- Standard cells are **placed** on the chip, and **routing** connects them per the netlist.
- Tools: **Synopsys IC Compiler II, Cadence Innovus**.

**7. Physical Verification (1 mark)**
- **DRC (Design Rule Check)**: ensures the layout obeys the foundry's geometric rules.
- **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)**: ensures the layout is electrically equivalent to the netlist.
- **ERC (Electrical Rule Check)**: catches antenna effects, supply shorts.

**8. Fabrication and Testing (1 mark)**
- The verified **GDSII layout file** is sent to the foundry for **tapeout**.
- Wafers are processed, dies are cut, packaged, and tested.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Importance of Verification (1 mark reserved)**: Verification is **not a one-time step**. It runs **continuously and in parallel** at every level (functional simulation, gate-level simulation, static timing analysis, power analysis, DRC/LVS). A bug caught post-silicon can cost **> $1M to fix** and **6+ months of delay** — hence the saying *"verification is 70% of the design effort."*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) — Y-Chart (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark)**: The **Gajski-Kuhn Y-chart**, proposed by **Daniel Gajski and Robert Kuhn in 1983**, is a model that visualizes the **VLSI design process** along three intersecting domains and multiple levels of abstraction.

**Three Domains (3 marks):**
1. **Behavioral Domain** (top arm of Y): Describes **what the system does** — algorithms, data flow, control flow. Specified via HDL, C/C++, or SystemC.
2. **Structural Domain** (right arm of Y): Describes **how the system is composed** — components, sub-components, interconnections. Includes block diagrams, schematics, and netlists.
3. **Geometric / Physical Domain** (left arm of Y): Describes **how the system is laid out physically** on the silicon — floorplans, placement, routing, mask patterns.

**Levels of Abstraction (2 marks):**
Each domain is organized into **six levels**, from most abstract to most concrete:
- **System Level**
- **Algorithm Level**
- **Register-Transfer Level (RTL)**
- **Logic Level**
- **Circuit Level**
- **Layout Level**

**Reading the Y-Chart (1 mark):**
- A design path starts at the **outer ring** (high abstraction) and progresses **inward** (toward the silicon layout at the center).
- At each step, the designer switches between domains. For example: write an algorithm (behavioral) → choose hardware blocks (structural) → floorplan them (geometric).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors (Examiner's Pitfall Callout)**:
> 
> > [!WARNING]
> > 1. **Mis-naming the domains** — students often call the geometric domain "physical," which is acceptable, but calling it "layout" is *too narrow*. Use "**Geometric / Physical Domain**."
> > 2. **Forgetting the levels of abstraction** — simply drawing three axes is **incomplete**. The KTU answer key requires **explicit listing of the 6 levels**.
> > 3. **Not explaining inward movement** — examiners specifically check whether you understand that the design progresses **from outer to center**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Design Styles + Cost per Die Derivation

**(a)** Compare the various **VLSI design styles** (Full Custom, Standard Cell, Gate Array, FPGA) in terms of density, performance, NRE cost, and time-to-market. When would you choose each? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **cost per die** ($C_{die}$) of an IC. A 200 mm wafer costs $C_{wafer} = \$3{,}000$ to process. Each die is 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm, and the defect density is 0.4 defects/cm². Compute the approximate number of good dies per wafer and the cost per die. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) — Design Styles Comparison (7 marks)

| Design Style | Density | Performance | NRE Cost | Time-to-Market | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Custom** | Highest (100%) | Highest | Very High | Slowest (12–18 mo) | Analog, Memory, CPU datapath |
| **Standard Cell** | High (80–90%) | High | High | Slow (6–12 mo) | ASICs, SoCs, MCUs |
| **Gate Array** | Medium (60–70%) | Medium | Medium | Medium (3–6 mo) | Mid-volume ASICs |
| **FPGA** | Lowest (10–20%) | Lowest | Zero | Fastest (days–weeks) | Prototyping, low-volume |

**Selection Guidelines (1 mark reserved):**
- **Full Custom** → when every micron of area and every microsecond of latency matters (e.g., a clock generator IC).
- **Standard Cell** → for high-volume digital products (e.g., a smartphone SoC sold in 100M+ units).
- **Gate Array** → for mid-volume products needing faster turnaround than standard cell.
- **FPGA** → for prototypes, low-volume products, or when **reconfigurability** is essential (e.g., aerospace, defense).

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) — Cost per Die Derivation (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):** See Section 3.1 of these notes. The final expression is:

$$
\boxed{\,C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{die}} \cdot e^{D_0 \cdot A}\,}
$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Given: $R = 200 \text{ mm} = 20 \text{ cm}$, $A = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ cm}^2$, $D_0 = 0.4 \text{ defects/cm}^2$, $C_{wafer} = \$3{,}000$.

**Step 1 — $N_{die}$ (1 mark):**

$$
N_{die} \approx \frac{\pi \cdot (20)^2}{4 \cdot 0.25} - \frac{\pi \cdot 20}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 0.25}}
$$

$$
N_{die} = \frac{400 \pi}{1} - \frac{20 \pi}{0.7071}
$$

$$
N_{die} \approx 1256.6 - 88.9 \approx 1168
$$

**Step 2 — Yield $Y$ (1 mark):**

$$
Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A} = e^{-0.4 \cdot 0.25} = e^{-0.1}
$$

$$
Y \approx 0.9048 \quad (90.48\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Good Dies per Wafer (0.5 marks):**

$$
N_{good} = N_{die} \cdot Y = 1168 \cdot 0.9048 \approx 1057 \text{ good dies}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost per Die (0.5 marks):**

$$
C_{die} = \frac{\$3{,}000}{1057} \approx \$2.84 \text{ per good die}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes on Cost per Die Problems**:
> 
> > [!WARNING]
> > 1. **Forgetting the edge-loss term** $-\dfrac{\pi \cdot R}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A}}$ in $N_{die}$ — examiners often award 0.5 to 1 mark for this term.
> > 2. **Unit mismatch** — students often mix mm and cm. The wafer radius $R$ and die area $A$ **must** be in **the same unit system**.
> > 3. **Using $C_{wafer}/N_{die}$ instead of $C_{wafer}/(N_{die} \cdot Y)$** — this is the single most common error, and it loses 2 marks.
> > 4. **Rounding $e^{-0.1}$** as exactly 0.9 — use 0.9048 or compute it more precisely.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Introduction: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **VLSI = 10k to ~100k gates** per chip; **ULSI > 100k**; modern chips are now in ULSI/WSI regime.
- **Moore's Law** = transistor count doubles ~every 2 years; cost per transistor halves.
- **Dennard Scaling** broke down around 2006, leading to the **multi-core era**.
- **VLSI Design Hierarchy** has 6 levels: System → Algorithm → RTL → Logic → Circuit → Layout.
- **VLSI Design Flow** has 8 major phases: Spec → Arch → RTL → Verification → Synthesis → P&R → Physical Verif → Fab/Test.
- **Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn)** has 3 domains (Behavioral, Structural, Geometric) × 6 abstraction levels.
- **Four design styles**: Full Custom, Standard Cell, Gate Array, FPGA — in order of decreasing NRE cost and increasing time-to-market.
- **Cost per die formula**: $C_{die} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{N_{die}} \cdot e^{D_0 \cdot A}$ — **memorize this**.
- **Dies per wafer**: $N_{die} \approx \dfrac{\pi R^2}{4A} - \dfrac{\pi R}{\sqrt{2A}}$ — includes edge loss.
- **Yield model**: $Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A}$ (Poisson); dies double in cost **exponentially** with area for a given defect density.
- **Verification is continuous** — DRC, LVS, ERC, STA, power analysis all run in parallel with design.
- **CMOS is the dominant technology** because of zero static power, high noise immunity, and excellent scalability.
- **Foundry nodes in 2024**: 3 nm FinFET in mass production; 2 nm GAAFET in early ramp.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moore's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Moore's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Moore's Law** is an empirical observation and prediction in the field of **Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI)** and semiconductor technology, originally articulated by **Gordon E. Moore**, co-founder of Fairchild Semiconductor and later Intel Corporation, in his landmark 1965 paper *"Cramming More Components onto Integrated Circuits"* published in *Electronics* magazine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Required in Answers):**
> *Moore's Law states that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit (IC) doubles approximately every 18 to 24 months, leading to an exponential increase in computational capability, accompanied by a corresponding decrease in the cost per function and per transistor.*

There are **two historical formulations**:

| Version | Year | Doubling Period | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Original Moore's Law | **1965** | Every **1 year** (10-year forecast) | Electronics Magazine |
| Revised Moore's Law | **1975** | Every **2 years** | IEEE Technical Digest |

Modern interpretations (used by **Intel**, **TSMC**, **Samsung**, and **IRDS — International Roadmap for Devices and Systems**) generally use a **2-year** doubling period, often expressed as:

$$
\text{Performance} \propto 2^{t/T}
$$

where $T \approx 2 \text{ years}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are packing suitcases for a trip. In 1965, you could fit **50 shirts** into a single suitcase. By 1967, you could fit **100 shirts** in the *same* suitcase. By 1969, **200 shirts** — still the same suitcase, same weight, same handle, same wheels. The suitcase never gets bigger; you just get *astonishingly better* at folding and arranging the shirts.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Suitcase Analogy" for VLSI Students:**
> - **Suitcase** = Silicon Wafer (fixed physical size ~300 mm diameter)
> - **Shirts** = Transistors
> - **Folding technique** = Photolithography, FinFET/GAA process nodes
> - **Result** = Exponential increase in **functional density** at the *same cost*

In real engineering terms, Moore's Law is the **economic engine** that drove the entire semiconductor industry for over **six decades** (1965–2025+). It is *not* a physical law like Newton's Second Law; it is an **observation of human ingenuity** combined with **economic compulsion**.

## 1.3 Key Physical and Economic Metrics

- **Transistor Count (N):** Doubles every ~24 months
- **Cost per Transistor:** Decreases by ~**30%** per year
- **Die Area:** Stays roughly constant (or grows slowly)
- **Clock Frequency:** Historically increased (now stalled at ~**5 GHz** wall)
- **Power per Transistor:** Decreased (until Dennard scaling broke down ~2006)
- **Feature Size (λ):** Shrinks by factor of $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ per node

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic — The "Why" Behind the Doubling

The exponential doubling is **not arbitrary**. It is rooted in three converging industrial forces:

1. **Lithographic Resolution (Rayleigh Criterion):**
   The minimum printable feature size is governed by the wavelength of light used in photolithography.

   $$L_{\min} = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}$$

   As $\lambda$ shrinks (g-line → i-line → 193 nm ArF → EUV 13.5 nm), $L_{\min}$ shrinks, allowing more transistors per unit area.

2. **Economic Compulsion (Rock's Law Inverse):**
   The cost of a semiconductor fabrication plant (fab) doubles roughly every **4 years** (Rock's Law). To keep **cost per transistor** falling, transistor density **must** rise exponentially to amortize fab cost.

3. **Self-Fulfilling Roadmap:**
   The **Semiconductor Industry Association (SIA)** publishes the **International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors (ITRS)** — now continued as **IRDS** — coordinating global R&D to maintain Moore's Law.

## 2.2 Moore's Law vs. Dennard Scaling vs. Koomey's Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask students to differentiate these three. Memorize this table.

| Law | Proposer | Year | Statement | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Moore's Law** | G. E. Moore | **1965** | Transistor count doubles every ~2 years | **Slowing** but continuing via 3D stacking, GAA |
| **Dennard Scaling** | R. H. Dennard | **1974** | Power density stays constant as transistors shrink | **Broken** since ~2006 (leakage currents) |
| **Koomey's Law** | J. Koomey | **2011** | Energy efficiency of computation doubles every ~1.57 years | **Active** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** All formulas below are **high-yield**. At least one appears in nearly every KTU ESE question on this module.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t - t_0)/T}$ | Transistor count as a function of time | $T = 2$ years (modern); $N_0$ = baseline count |
| 2 | $A_{\text{die}} = N \cdot A_{\text{cell}}$ | Die area = count × cell area | $A_{\text{cell}} \propto L_{\min}^2$ |
| 3 | $C_{\text{total}} \propto N \cdot C_{\text{gate}}$ | Total capacitance on a chip | $C_{\text{gate}} \propto L \cdot W$ |
| 4 | $P \propto C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic power dissipation | Where $f$ = clock frequency |
| 5 | $C_{\text{per-transistor}} \propto L_{\min}$ | Gate capacitance scaling | $L_{\min}$ shrinks by $\sim 0.7\times$ per node |
| 6 | $\text{Cost per Function} \propto \frac{1}{N}$ | Cost per function halves every 2 years | Economic driving force |
| 7 | $V_{DD} \propto L_{\min}$ | Supply voltage scales with feature size | From Dennard scaling |
| 8 | $f \propto \frac{1}{L_{\min}}$ | Clock frequency inverse to feature size | Until ~2006 |

> [!WARNING]
> When substituting $|x|$ or absolute values in KTU answer scripts, examiners prefer $\vert x \vert$ notation. Avoid plain `|x|` inside markdown tables as it breaks the table renderer.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Node Planning:** TSMC's N3, N2, A16, A14 nodes are all scheduled to maintain Moore's Law cadence.
- **EDA Tool Investment:** Justifies billions in Synopsys, Cadence, Siemens EDA R&D.
- **Architecture Co-design:** Dictates how many cores, cache levels, and accelerators fit on a die.
- **Power-Performance-Area (PPA):** The triangular design goal in modern VLSI flows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Transistor Count as a Function of Time

**Given:**
- Initial transistor count at time $t_0$ is $N_0$
- Doubling period is $T$ years
- Current time is $t$

**Step 1 — Define the exponential growth model.**

Moore's Law is fundamentally an exponential growth phenomenon. The generic exponential growth equation is:

$$
N(t) = N_0 \cdot a^{(t - t_0)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the doubling condition.**

By definition of Moore's Law, after one period $T$, the count must double:

$$
N(t_0 + T) = 2 \cdot N_0
$$

Substituting into the generic formula:

$$
2 \cdot N_0 = N_0 \cdot a^{T}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the base $a$.**

Divide both sides by $N_0$:

$$
2 = a^{T}
$$

Take the $T$-th root:

$$
a = 2^{1/T}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the original equation.**

$$
N(t) = N_0 \cdot \left(2^{1/T}\right)^{(t - t_0)}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using exponent rules.**

Using the rule $(x^m)^n = x^{m \cdot n}$:

$$
\boxed{\,N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t - t_0)/T}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 3-Mark Style)

**Problem:** The Intel 4004 processor (1971) had $N_0 = 2300$ transistors. Using Moore's Law with a doubling period of $T = 2$ years, estimate the transistor count in the year **2020** (a 49-year span).

**Solution — Step by Step:**

$$
N(2020) = 2300 \cdot 2^{(2020 - 1971)/2}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
\frac{2020 - 1971}{2} = \frac{49}{2} = 24.5
$$

So:

$$
N(2020) = 2300 \cdot 2^{24.5}
$$

Compute $2^{24.5}$:

$$
2^{10} = 1024 \approx 10^3
$$
$$
2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \approx 10^6
$$
$$
2^{24} = 2^{20} \cdot 2^4 = 1{,}048{,}576 \cdot 16 = 16{,}777{,}216
$$
$$
2^{24.5} = 2^{24} \cdot 2^{0.5} = 16{,}777{,}216 \cdot \sqrt{2} \approx 23{,}725{,}533
$$

Therefore:

$$
N(2020) \approx 2300 \cdot 23{,}725{,}533 \approx 5.46 \times 10^{10}
$$

**Actual transistor count of high-end 2020 chips (e.g., Apple M1, AMD Ryzen):** $\sim 1.6 \times 10^{10}$ to $5 \times 10^{10}$ — a remarkable validation of Moore's Law.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cost per Transistor Decline

Let $C_N$ be the cost per transistor at time $t$. Empirically, $C_N$ halves every 3 years (cost-reduction rate differs from density-doubling rate).

$$
C_N(t) = C_{N,0} \cdot 2^{-(t - t_0)/3}
$$

Combined with transistor count, the **cost per function** becomes:

$$
C_{\text{function}}(t) = \frac{C_N(t) \cdot N(t)}{N(t)} = C_N(t) = C_{N,0} \cdot 2^{-(t - t_0)/3}
$$

This is the economic foundation of the semiconductor industry: **exponentially cheaper computation** despite flat (or rising) chip prices.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Moore's Law Projections

| Year | Technology Node | Transistors (typical high-end CPU) | Doubling Count from 1971 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1971 | 10 µm | 2,300 (Intel 4004) | $2^0 = 1$ |
| 1985 | 1.5 µm | 275,000 (Intel 80386) | $2^7 = 128$ |
| 2000 | 180 nm | 42,000,000 (Intel Pentium 4) | $2^{14.5} \approx 23{,}000$ |
| 2015 | 14 nm | 1,750,000,000 | $2^{22} \approx 4{,}000{,}000$ |
| 2020 | 7 nm | 5,000,000,000+ | $2^{24.5} \approx 23{,}700{,}000$ |
| 2024 | 3 nm | 25,000,000,000+ | $2^{26.5} \approx 95{,}000{,}000$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Moore's Law Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** showing how Moore's Law propagates from physical scaling to economic and societal outcomes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Foundations: Lithography Lambda Shrinking] --> B[Transistor Density Doubles Every 2 Years]
    B --> C[Per-Transistor Cost Falls Exponentially]
    C --> D[Cost per Function Plummets]
    D --> E[Mass Market Adoption of Computing]
    E --> F[Exponential Growth in Software Demand]
    F --> G[Increased R and D Investment]
    G --> A
    B --> H[Performance per Watt Improves]
    H --> I[Mobile and IoT Revolution]
    I --> E
    B --> J[Limits: Quantum Tunneling and Leakage]
    J --> K[New Materials: FinFET, GAA, CFET]
    K --> A
    J --> L[3D Stacking and Chiplets]
    L --> B
```

**Interpretation of Nodes:**

- **Node A** — The physical driver: shorter wavelength lithography (EUV at 13.5 nm).
- **Node B** — The empirical observation Moore codified.
- **Node C** — Economic engine of the industry.
- **Node D–F** — The virtuous cycle: cheaper chips → more demand → more R&D → better fabs.
- **Node J–L** — The *post-Moore's-Law escape hatches*: 3D integration, chiplets, heterogeneous integration.

## 4.2 Moore's Law vs. Dennard Scaling Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[1965: Moore's Law Proposed] --> T2[1971: Intel 4004 Launched]
    T2 --> T3[1974: Dennard Scaling Paper]
    T3 --> T4[1985: 1.5 Micron Node]
    T4 --> T5[2000: 180nm Node]
    T5 --> T6[2005: 90nm Node]
    T6 --> T7[2006: Dennard Scaling Breaks Down]
    T7 --> T8[2014: 14nm FinFET Era]
    T8 --> T9[2018: 7nm EUV Begins]
    T9 --> T10[2022: 3nm GAA Gate All Around]
    T10 --> T11[2024+: 2nm A16 and CFET Roadmap]
```

## 4.3 Moore's Law Equation Visualization Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input: N0, t0, T, t] --> P2[Compute Exponent: delta_t over T]
    P2 --> P3[Compute 2 raised to exponent]
    P3 --> P4[Multiply by N0]
    P4 --> P5[Output: N of t]
    P5 --> P6[Compare with Actual Chip Data]
    P6 --> P7{Validates Moore's Law?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Continue Roadmap]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Investigate Slowdown Cause]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State Moore's Law. How is it relevant to VLSI design?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Moore's Law, proposed by Gordon E. Moore in 1965, states that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every 18 to 24 months. **(1 mark)**

It is relevant to VLSI design because it sets the **density, performance, and cost roadmap** for the semiconductor industry, drives the reduction of **feature size** ($\lambda$), enables the development of advanced **EDA tools**, justifies the exponential investment in **fabrication facilities**, and forms the economic basis of the **cost-per-function** reduction that has made modern computing affordable. **(2 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Differentiate between Moore's Law and Dennard Scaling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Moore's Law | Dennard Scaling |
|---|---|---|
| Proposer | G. E. Moore (1965) | R. H. Dennard (1974) |
| Statement | Transistor count doubles every ~2 years | Power density remains constant as transistors shrink |
| Focus | **Density and economics** | **Power-performance** |
| Current status | Slowing but alive | Broken since ~2006 |

**(1 mark per relevant point, 3 marks total.)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for transistor count as a function of time based on Moore's Law, clearly stating the doubling period. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting with the Intel 4004 (1971, $N_0 = 2300$ transistors), calculate the projected transistor count in the year **2025** assuming a doubling period of $T = 2$ years. Compare with a modern processor (e.g., Apple M3 Ultra, ~134 billion transistors) and comment on the validity of Moore's Law. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

The derivation begins by assuming exponential growth of transistor count. **(1 mark)**

$$
N(t) = N_0 \cdot a^{(t - t_0)}
$$

Apply the doubling condition $N(t_0 + T) = 2 N_0$: **(1 mark)**

$$
2 N_0 = N_0 \cdot a^T \implies a^T = 2
$$

Solve for the base: **(1 mark)**

$$
a = 2^{1/T}
$$

Substitute back: **(1 mark)**

$$
N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t - t_0)/T}
$$

The doubling period $T$ is **2 years** as per the modern (revised 1975) formulation. **(1 mark)**

Final boxed expression: **(1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\,N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t - t_0)/2}\,}
$$

Discussion: This equation captures the **exponential nature** of Moore's Law, which is the foundation of the semiconductor roadmap. **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

Given: $N_0 = 2300$, $t_0 = 1971$, $t = 2025$, $T = 2$. **(1 mark)**

Step 1 — Compute the time interval:

$$
\Delta t = 2025 - 1971 = 54 \text{ years}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the exponent:

$$
\frac{\Delta t}{T} = \frac{54}{2} = 27
$$

Step 3 — Compute $2^{27}$:

$$
2^{10} = 1024, \quad 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576, \quad 2^{27} = 2^{20} \cdot 2^{7} = 1{,}048{,}576 \cdot 128 = 134{,}217{,}728
$$

Step 4 — Compute $N(2025)$:

$$
N(2025) = 2300 \cdot 134{,}217{,}728 \approx 3.087 \times 10^{11}
$$

i.e., approximately **308 billion transistors**. **(2 marks)**

Step 5 — Comparison with Apple M3 Ultra (134 billion):

- The predicted count (308 B) is **higher** than the actual (134 B). **(1 mark)**
- This shows Moore's Law has **slowed** in recent years due to physical limits (sub-3 nm quantum tunneling), **economic limits** (Rock's Law), and **architectural shifts** (multi-die chiplets). **(2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Moore's Law**, **Dennard Scaling**, and **Koomey's Law**. Why did Dennard Scaling break down, and what is its consequence on modern VLSI design? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, describe the impact of Moore's Law on the VLSI design flow. Discuss why **3D integration** and **chiplet architectures** are considered the next phase of Moore's Law. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

| Law | Statement | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Moore's Law (1965)** | Transistor count doubles every 2 years | **Slowing** |
| **Dennard Scaling (1974)** | Power density constant as size shrinks | **Broken** |
| **Koomey's Law (2011)** | Energy efficiency of computation doubles every 1.57 years | **Active** |

**(3 marks — 1 per law correctly stated)**

**Why Dennard Scaling broke down (around 2006):**
- As $V_{DD}$ approached the **threshold voltage** $V_{th} \approx 0.3$ V, leakage current (sub-threshold conduction) increased **exponentially**. **(1 mark)**
- The relationship $P \propto C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ no longer yielded constant power density because $V$ could not be reduced further. **(1 mark)**

**Consequence on VLSI design:**
- Shift from **single-core frequency scaling** to **multi-core architectures**. **(1 mark)**
- Emergence of **dark silicon** — portions of the die that must be powered off to stay within thermal limits. **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Impact of Moore's Law on VLSI Design Flow (with diagram — drawn in script):**

1. **System Specification** → driven by expected performance gains
2. **RTL Design** → enables more complex SoCs each generation
3. **Logic Synthesis** → benefits from smaller cells, more capacity
4. **Physical Design** → must handle 10⁹+ transistors with EDA automation
5. **Verification** → exponentially harder, requires formal + emulation

**Why 3D Integration and Chiplets are the next phase: (3 marks)**

- **Monolithic scaling at sub-3 nm** is hitting atomic limits (silicon lattice ~0.5 nm).
- **3D stacking** (e.g., TSMC SoIC, AMD 3D V-Cache) increases effective density *vertically*.
- **Chiplets** (e.g., Intel Meteor Lake, Apple M-series Ultra) allow mixing of process nodes — compute on 3 nm, I/O on 5 nm — extending Moore's Law economically.
- This is often called **"More than Moore"** — functional diversification rather than pure scaling.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Writing 18 months vs. 24 months inconsistently** — state "18 to 24 months" or specify which version (1965 vs. 1975) you are using.
> 2. **Confusing Moore's Law with Dennard Scaling** — these are *different* laws by *different* people, and examiners specifically test the distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in derived numerical answers — always state transistor counts in scientific notation with proper units (e.g., $1.34 \times 10^{11}$).
> 4. **Treating Moore's Law as a physical law** — it is an *empirical observation*; physical laws (like Dennard scaling) are derived from physics.
> 5. **Ignoring the slowdown** — modern answers must acknowledge post-2010 slowdown and the rise of chiplets/3D stacking.
> 6. **Not citing the year 1965** — this is a must-mention in any Moore's Law answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Proposer:** Gordon E. Moore, **1965**, *Electronics* magazine.
- 📌 **Original doubling period:** **1 year** (10-year forecast). **Revised in 1975** to **2 years**.
- 📌 **Mathematical form:** $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t - t_0)/T}$, with $T = 2$ years (modern).
- 📌 **Cost per transistor** halves roughly every 3 years; **cost per function** halves every 2 years.
- 📌 **Driving forces:** Lithography wavelength reduction + Economic compulsion (Rock's Law).
- 📌 **Dennard Scaling** (1974) broke down around **2006** at the **90 nm** node due to leakage currents.
- 📌 **Koomey's Law** (2011) — energy efficiency of computation doubles every 1.57 years — **still active**.
- 📌 **Current era:** FinFET (since 2014) → GAA Gate-All-Around (since 2022) → CFET (roadmap 2026+).
- 📌 **Post-Moore strategies:** 3D stacking, chiplets, heterogeneous integration, quantum computing (long-term).
- 📌 **KTU keyword triggers:** *"exponential growth," "cost per function," "ITRS/IRDS roadmap," "Rock's Law," "Dennard scaling breakdown."*
- 📌 **High-yield numerical fact:** Apple M3 Ultra (2024) has **~134 billion** transistors — a useful comparison benchmark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ASIC Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** ASIC Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)** is an integrated circuit that is custom-designed to perform a specific function or a specific set of functions for a particular application, as opposed to a general-purpose IC (like a microprocessor) or a programmable device (like an FPGA used in prototype mode). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, ASICs occupy a pivotal role in modern electronics because the entire chip — from transistor sizing, logic gates, interconnects, and I/O pads — is *tailored* to a single end-application (e.g., a chip inside a washing machine controller, a network router, or a smartphone's image signal processor). ### Sub-Categories of ASIC | Category | Description | | :--- | :--- | | **Full-Custom ASIC** | Every transistor, every interconnect, and every cell is hand-crafted by the designer for maximum performance / minimum area. | | **Semi-Custom ASIC** | Uses pre-designed library cells (Standard Cell) or a prefabricated master die (Gate Array). | | **Programmable ASIC** | Functionality is defined by the end user post-fabrication (e.g., **FPGA**, **CPLD**). | > [!NOTE] > In the KTU 2024 scheme, **Programmable ASICs are often treated as a bridge between FPGAs and true mask-programmed ASICs.** They are fabricated in bulk and configured by the user. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you need a home: - **Full-Custom ASIC** = You hire an architect, and *every brick, every window, every beam* is custom-designed and built from scratch. Maximum optimization, but **very expensive** and slow. - **Standard-Cell ASIC** = You build using a catalogue of pre-made rooms (kitchen modules, bathroom modules) and connect them. Faster, cheaper, very common. - **Gate-Array ASIC** = You buy a pre-built skeleton house and only customize the wallpaper, doors, and furniture. Cheapest and fastest of the mask-based ASICs. - **FPGA (Programmable ASIC)** = A fully built, modular house where you can rearrange the walls any time. The slowest performance and highest per-unit cost, but **zero NRE** (Non-Recurring Engineering cost) — perfect for prototypes and low-volume products. > [!TIP] > For the KTU exam: When asked to *justify* the choice of an ASIC style, always frame your answer around the **trade-off triangle: NRE Cost ↔ Unit Cost ↔ Time-to-Market.** ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following standard metrics are universally used in ASIC evaluation and are **directly testable** in the KTU ESE: - **NRE Cost (Non-Recurring Engineering Cost):** One-time cost for masks, design tools, and testing setup. Measured in **USD (or ₹)**. - **Unit Cost:** Cost of fabricating *one* packaged, tested chip. - **Die Area (A):** The silicon area occupied by the circuit, measured in **mm²** or **cm²**. - **Defect Density (D):** Average number of defects per unit area (typically **defects/cm²**). - **Wafer Radius (R):** Standard radii are **7.5 cm (6 inch)**, **10 cm (8 inch)**, **15 cm (12 inch)**, and **20 cm (16 inch)**. - **Technology Node:** Feature size (e.g., **90 nm, 65 nm, 28 nm, 7 nm**). > [!NOTE] > A key rule of thumb in industry (Seeds' simplified model) states that **yield decreases exponentially with die area** for a given defect density. This single fact drives the entire economic decision of "full-custom vs. semi-custom." # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 ASIC Design Flow — Structured Logic Steps The **ASIC Design Flow** is a sequence of well-defined stages, moving from abstract system requirements to physical silicon. KTU examiners love flow-chart questions (often worth 7 marks). **Step 1 — System Specification:** Defines functionality, performance, power, die size, and technology node. Output: A natural-language document. **Step 2 — Logic Design / RTL Coding:** Translate the specification into a **Hardware Description Language (HDL)** — Verilog or VHDL. Functional simulation is performed here. **Step 3 — Logic Synthesis:** HDL is converted into a **gate-level netlist** using a Standard Cell Library (SCL). Synthesis optimizes for area, delay, and power. **Step 4 — Floorplanning:** Decides the placement of major functional blocks (CPU core, RAM, I/O pads) on the die. Directly impacts wirelength and timing closure. **Step 5 — Placement:** Each standard cell from the netlist is assigned a *physical location* on the floorplan. **Step 6 — Routing:** Connects the placed cells using metal layers. This step is also called **Detailed Routing**. **Step 7 — Physical Verification:** Performs **DRC (Design Rule Check)**, **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)**, and **ERC (Electrical Rule Check)**. **Step 8 — Fabrication & Packaging:** The verified layout (GDS-II file) is sent to a foundry (e.g., TSMC, Intel Foundry) to manufacture the silicon. **Step 9 — Testing:** Every manufactured die is tested (wafer probing) and then packaged and re-tested (final test). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this flow matters in the real world:** > Each stage has an associated CAD tool. Re-doing any late stage (e.g., changing placement after fabrication) is financially catastrophic. The flow is **iterative** but generally **top-down**, and any RTL change can ripple all the way to layout — a concept KTU tests under "Design Closure." ## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > These formulas are the **backbone** of any numerical question in the ESE on ASIC economics. Memorize them with their boundary conditions. | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units | Boundary / Limitation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Dies per Wafer (approx) | $N = \dfrac{\pi (R - \sqrt{A / \pi})^{2}}{A} - \dfrac{\pi R}{\sqrt{A / \pi}}$ | $R$ = wafer radius (cm), $A$ = die area (cm²) | Assumes square dies. | | Seed's Yield Model | $Y = e^{-A \cdot D}$ | $A$ = die area (cm²), $D$ = defect density (defects/cm²) | Used for mature, large-die processes. | | Murphy's Yield Model | $Y = \left(\dfrac{1 - e^{-A \cdot D}}{A \cdot D}\right)^{2}$ | Same as above | More accurate for clustered defects. | | Cost per Die | $C_{die} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{N \cdot Y}$ | $C_{wafer}$ = processed wafer cost | The most tested equation in KTU. | | Cost per Good Die (incl. packaging/testing) | $C_{good} = \dfrac{C_{die} + C_{pack} + C_{test}}{Y_{final}}$ | $C_{pack}$, $C_{test}$ are fixed additions | Adds post-fabrication cost. | | Total Project Cost | $C_{total} = NRE + (Volume \times C_{good})$ | $NRE$ = one-time cost | The classic break-even formula. | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **die yield (Y)** with **final test yield (Y_final)**. They are different — die yield is the silicon-level yield, while final test yield accounts for packaging-induced failures and parametric failures after bonding. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The ASIC flow is the **backbone of the semiconductor industry**. It allows companies to: 1. **Mass-produce** a chip for a known market (e.g., a chip for the Apple AirPods) at very low per-unit cost. 2. **Protect Intellectual Property (IP)** — the GDS-II file is the IP, and it is shipped encrypted to the foundry. 3. **Achieve extreme energy efficiency** — FPGAs can be 10× to 100× less power-efficient than a custom ASIC for the same task, which is critical for IoT and mobile devices. > [!TIP] > Google, Amazon, and Meta all design custom ASICs (TPUs, Trainium, MTIA) because at *hyperscale*, even a 10% reduction in cost-per-inference justifies the millions of dollars in NRE. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Dies per Wafer (The Wallmark Formula) The Wallmark approximation is the **most widely used** formula in KTU numerical problems. Let us derive it from first principles. **Step 1 — Start with the wafer's total silicon area:** A circular wafer of radius $R$ has area $\pi R^{2}$. **Step 2 — Imagine placing square dies of side $s$ on the wafer.** The die area is $A = s^{2}$, so the die side is $s = \sqrt{A}$. **Step 3 — Use a simple geometric packing argument:** The usable area on the wafer is bounded by an inner circle. The effective radius for placing dies is approximately $(R - \dfrac{s}{2})$, because a die cannot cross the wafer's edge. **Step 4 — Form the area of the usable region:** $$A_{usable} = \pi \left(R - \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 5 — Divide usable area by die area to get an upper bound:** $$N \approx \frac{\pi \left(R - \sqrt{A / \pi}\right)^{2}}{A}$$ **Step 6 — Subtract the boundary correction term** (dies that cross the curved wafer edge): $$N = \frac{\pi \left(R - \sqrt{A / \pi}\right)^{2}}{A} - \frac{\pi R}{\sqrt{A / \pi}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The second term $\frac{\pi R}{\sqrt{A/\pi}}$ is a boundary correction that becomes negligible for very small dies but matters for large dies. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A 20 cm diameter wafer ($R = 10$ cm) is used to fabricate dies of area $A = 4$ cm². The defect density is $D = 0.5$ defects/cm². The processed wafer cost is $C_{wafer} = 5000$ ₹. Compute the cost per good die using the Seeds model. ### Step 1 — Compute dies per wafer $$N = \frac{\pi (10 - \sqrt{4/\pi})^{2}}{4} - \frac{\pi \cdot 10}{\sqrt{4/\pi}}$$ **Compute intermediate values:** $$\sqrt{\frac{4}{\pi}} = \sqrt{1.2732} = 1.1284 \text{ cm}$$ $$(10 - 1.1284)^{2} = (8.8716)^{2} = 78.706$$ $$\pi \times 78.706 = 247.27$$ $$\frac{247.27}{4} = 61.82$$ $$\frac{\pi \times 10}{1.1284} = \frac{31.416}{1.1284} = 27.84$$ $$N = 61.82 - 27.84 \approx 33.98 \approx 33 \text{ dies / wafer}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute yield using Seeds model $$Y = e^{-A \cdot D} = e^{-4 \times 0.5} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$ ### Step 3 — Compute cost per good die $$C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N \cdot Y} = \frac{5000}{33 \times 0.1353} = \frac{5000}{4.465} \approx 1119.8 \text{ ₹}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The economic insight:** Notice that the yield $Y = 13.5\%$ is very low. This is because the die area ($4$ cm²) is huge. If the designer could halve the die area to $A = 1$ cm², the yield would jump to $e^{-0.5} \approx 60.6\%$, slashing the per-die cost by more than 4×. **This single calculation justifies the existence of full-custom design optimization!** ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ASIC vs. FPGA vs. PLD | Parameter | Full-Custom ASIC | Standard-Cell ASIC | Gate-Array ASIC | FPGA | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **NRE Cost** | Very High | High | Medium | **Zero** (programmable) | | **Unit Cost (high volume)** | Lowest | Very Low | Low | High | | **Time-to-Market** | Slowest | Slow | Medium | **Fastest** | | **Performance (Speed)** | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest | | **Power Efficiency** | Best | Very Good | Good | Worst | | **Flexibility post-fab** | None | None | None | **Full** | | **Volume Suitability** | Very high | High | Medium | Low / Prototype | | **Design Effort** | Maximum | High | Medium | Low | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often write *"FPGA is cheaper than ASIC"*. This is **only true for low volume**. For volume > ~100,000 units, the per-unit ASIC cost is lower, even after recovering the NRE. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Complete ASIC Design Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[System Specification
Functional and Performance Requirements] --> A2[RTL Design
Verilog or VHDL Coding] A2 --> A3[Functional Simulation
Verify Logic Behavior] A3 --> A4[Logic Synthesis
Generate Gate Level Netlist] A4 --> A5[Floorplanning
Place Major Blocks on Die] A5 --> A6[Placement
Assign Physical Location to Cells] A6 --> A7[Routing
Connect Cells using Metal Layers] A7 --> A8[Physical Verification
DRC LVS ERC] A8 --> A9[Tape Out
Generate GDS II File] A9 --> A10[Wafer Fabrication
Mask Production and Lithography] A10 --> A11[Packaging and Testing
Final Chip Delivery] A11 --> A12[Market Deployment
Volume Production] A8 -.Failure Feedback.-> A2 A8 -.Timing Closure Issue.-> A5 ``` > [!NOTE] > The dashed feedback arrows represent **iterative refinement** — a fundamental concept. Modern KTU questions frequently ask: *"Why is timing closure an iterative process?"* Answer: because placement and routing impact wire delays, which in turn affect timing closure, which may force re-placement. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: ASIC Family Classification ```mermaid flowchart LR RootNode[ASIC Family] --> N1[Full Custom ASIC] RootNode --> N2[Semi Custom ASIC] RootNode --> N3[Programmable ASIC] N1 --> N1a[Hand Crafted Transistors] N1 --> N1b[Maximum Performance] N2 --> N2a[Standard Cell Based] N2 --> N2b[Gate Array Based] N3 --> N3a[FPGA] N3 --> N3b[CPLD] N2a --> LibA[Uses Pre Designed Cell Library] N2b --> PrefabA[Uses Prefabricated Master Die] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ASIC Design Cost Trade-off ```mermaid flowchart TB SubA[Design Parameters] --> SubB[Die Area A] SubA --> SubC[Defect Density D] SubA --> SubD[Wafer Cost Cwafer] SubB --> Calcy[Yield Calculator Y equals e to the power of minus AD] SubC --> Calcy SubD --> Calcdie[Cost per Die Cdie equals Cwafer divided by N times Y] Calcy --> Calcdie SubE[Wafer Radius R] --> Calcn[Number of Dies N] SubB --> Calcn Calcn --> Calcdie Calcdie --> Output1[Economic Decision
Full Custom vs Semi Custom] ``` > [!TIP] > This block diagram is a perfect answer to the question: *"Explain how the choice of ASIC style is governed by economic parameters."* — a frequent **14-mark** question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between Full-Custom ASIC and Semi-Custom ASIC.** **Model Answer:** A **Full-Custom ASIC** is one in which *every transistor, every interconnect layer, and every cell layout* is manually designed and optimized by the designer for a given application. This offers the highest performance and smallest die area, but has the largest NRE cost and longest design time. A **Semi-Custom ASIC**, in contrast, uses pre-designed building blocks — Standard Cells (in **Standard-Cell ASIC**) or a prefabricated master die (in **Gate-Array ASIC**) — and only the interconnections and top-level floorplan are customized. Semi-custom offers a faster, cheaper design cycle at the cost of slightly higher die area and lower performance than full-custom. *(3 marks)* ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define NRE cost and explain its significance in ASIC design.** **Model Answer:** **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost** is the *one-time fixed cost* incurred in designing an ASIC, which includes the cost of masks, EDA tool licenses, design verification, and prototyping. Its significance lies in the fact that NRE must be amortized over the total production volume. For a low-volume product, the per-unit share of NRE is high, making an ASIC uneconomical and pushing the design toward an FPGA. For a high-volume product, NRE is spread over many units, making the per-die cost very low. Thus, NRE directly governs the **break-even volume** between FPGA and ASIC. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Draw and explain the complete **ASIC design flow** from system specification to fabrication. List the EDA tools used at each stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 15 cm radius wafer is processed to fabricate dies of area $1 \text{ cm}^2$. The defect density is $0.6 \text{ defects/cm}^2$ and the wafer cost is ₹4000. Using the Seeds yield model, calculate: - (i) The number of dies per wafer. - (ii) The die yield. - (iii) The cost per good die. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for (a) The complete ASIC design flow is a sequence of well-defined steps: 1. **System Specification:** Functional, performance, power, and cost targets are documented. This is the *contract* between the system architect and the design team. 2. **RTL Design (HDL Coding):** The specification is translated into Register Transfer Level (RTL) code in **Verilog** or **VHDL**. 3. **Functional Simulation:** The HDL is simulated *without* timing information to verify logical correctness. 4. **Logic Synthesis:** A **synthesis tool** (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus) converts the RTL into a gate-level netlist mapped onto a **Standard Cell Library (SCL)**. Optimization is performed for area, delay, and power. 5. **Floorplanning:** Macro blocks (CPU, memory, I/O) are placed on the die canvas. Aspect ratio and utilization are decided. 6. **Placement:** Each standard cell in the netlist is given a physical coordinate. 7. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS):** The clock signal is distributed with balanced skew. 8. **Routing:** Metal-layer wires connect the placed cells (Global + Detailed routing). 9. **Physical Verification:** **DRC** (Design Rule Check), **LVS** (Layout vs. Schematic), and **ERC** (Electrical Rule Check) are run. Static Timing Analysis (STA) confirms timing closure. 10. **Tape-Out:** The verified layout is converted into **GDS-II** (Graphic Data Stream) format and sent to the foundry. 11. **Fabrication:** Masks are produced and the wafer is processed in the foundry. 12. **Packaging and Test:** Dies are diced, packaged, and tested before shipping. > The EDA vendors include **Synopsys, Cadence, and Mentor (Siemens EDA)** — frequently cited in KTU answers. ### Model Solution for (b) **Given:** $R = 15$ cm, $A = 1$ cm², $D = 0.6$ /cm², $C_{wafer} = 4000$ ₹. **Step (i) — Number of dies per wafer:** $$N = \frac{\pi (15 - \sqrt{1/\pi})^{2}}{1} - \frac{\pi \cdot 15}{\sqrt{1/\pi}}$$ $$\sqrt{1/\pi} = 0.5642 \text{ cm}$$ $$(15 - 0.5642)^{2} = (14.4358)^{2} = 208.39$$ $$\pi \times 208.39 = 654.65$$ $$\frac{\pi \times 15}{0.5642} = \frac{47.124}{0.5642} = 83.52$$ $$N = 654.65 - 83.52 \approx 571 \text{ dies / wafer}$$ **Step (ii) — Die yield (Seeds model):** $$Y = e^{-A \cdot D} = e^{-1 \times 0.6} = e^{-0.6} \approx 0.5488$$ **Step (iii) — Cost per good die:** $$C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N \cdot Y} = \frac{4000}{571 \times 0.5488} = \frac{4000}{313.36} \approx 12.77 \text{ ₹}$$ ### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Compare **Full-Custom, Standard-Cell, Gate-Array, and FPGA** designs in terms of NRE cost, unit cost, time-to-market, and performance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define **yield**. Explain the **Seeds model** and **Murphy model** of yield. For a die area of $2.5$ cm² and defect density $0.8$ /cm², compute the yield using both models and comment on the result. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for (a) | Parameter | Full-Custom | Standard-Cell | Gate-Array | FPGA | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | NRE Cost | Very High | High | Medium | Zero | | Unit Cost (high vol.) | Lowest | Very Low | Low | High | | Time-to-Market | Slowest | Slow | Medium | Fastest | | Performance | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest | | Flexibility | None | None | None | Full | | Best Use Case | CPUs, GPUs | SoCs, mobile chips | Mid-volume products | Prototyping, low vol. | > The **trade-off triangle** is **NRE ↔ Unit Cost ↔ Time-to-Market**. A designer picks the ASIC style that optimizes this triangle for the target product volume. ### Model Solution for (b) **Yield** is defined as the ratio of *good dies* (passing all functional and parametric tests) to the *total number of dies* fabricated on a wafer. **Seeds Model** assumes defects are **uniformly distributed** randomly across the wafer: $$Y_{Seeds} = e^{-A \cdot D}$$ **Murphy Model** assumes defects follow a **clustered distribution** and is more accurate when defects tend to clump together: $$Y_{Murphy} = \left(\frac{1 - e^{-A \cdot D}}{A \cdot D}\right)^{2}$$ **Numerical computation for $A = 2.5$ cm², $D = 0.8$ /cm²:** **Seeds yield:** $$Y_{Seeds} = e^{-2.5 \times 0.8} = e^{-2.0} \approx 0.1353 \; (13.53\%)$$ **Murphy yield:** $$Y_{Murphy} = \left(\frac{1 - e^{-2.0}}{2.0}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{1 - 0.1353}{2.0}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.8647}{2.0}\right)^{2} = (0.4324)^{2} \approx 0.1870 \; (18.70\%)$$ **Comment:** Murphy's yield is **higher** than Seeds' yield for the same $A$ and $D$ because Murphy's model is more optimistic — it accounts for the empirical observation that defects in real wafers are clustered, leaving large defect-free regions. In industry, **Murphy's model is preferred for early yield estimation**, while **Seeds' model is used for conservative budgeting**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not** compute $N$ (dies per wafer) using $\pi R^{2} / A$ — this overcounts dies that cross the wafer edge. You **must** use the Wallmark formula with the boundary correction. > 2. **Do not** mix up Seeds and Murphy equations. A common error is writing Murphy's formula but computing Seeds' result. > 3. **Always state units** in your final answer (₹, cm², defects/cm²). Examiners deduct marks for missing units. > 4. **In flow-chart questions**, do not skip the *Packaging and Test* step at the end — many students end at "fabrication" and lose 1–2 marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - ✅ **ASIC** = Application-Specific Integrated Circuit, custom-built for one function. - ✅ Three main families: **Full-Custom, Semi-Custom (Standard Cell + Gate Array), Programmable (FPGA)**. - ✅ **NRE Cost** = one-time design cost; **Unit Cost** = per-chip cost. - ✅ The **ASIC design flow**: Specification → RTL → Synthesis → Floorplan → Placement → CTS → Routing → Verification → Tape-Out → Fabrication → Package/Test. - ✅ **Dies per wafer** uses the **Wallmark formula** with the boundary correction term. - ✅ **Seeds yield** $Y = e^{-A \cdot D}$ — assumes random defects. - ✅ **Murphy yield** $Y = \left(\dfrac{1 - e^{-A \cdot D}}{A \cdot D}\right)^{2}$ — assumes clustered defects; more realistic. - ✅ **Cost per die** $C_{die} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{N \cdot Y}$ — the *single most important economic equation* in VLSI. - ✅ **FPGA** has zero NRE but highest unit cost; ideal for prototyping and low volume. - ✅ **Full-Custom** has the highest NRE but lowest unit cost; ideal for very high volume. - ✅ **Standard Cell** is the most common style for modern SoCs (Application Processors, Mobile chips). - ✅ **Gate Array** offers a middle ground — prefabricated master die with custom metal layers. - ✅ **Yield decreases exponentially with die area** — this is the central economic driver of design optimization. - ✅ **EDA tool vendors** to mention in answers: Synopsys, Cadence, Siemens EDA (formerly Mentor Graphics). - ✅ **GDS-II** is the final output of the physical design flow, sent to the foundry for mask production. - ✅ For 14-mark questions, **always include a labeled flowchart** of the ASIC design flow and **state the EDA tool** at each stage for full marks. - ✅ **Standard wafer radii:** 7.5 cm (6"), 10 cm (8"), 15 cm (12"), 20 cm (16"). Modern fabs use 30 cm (12") wafers predominantly.

topics.title: Full Custom ASICs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Full Custom ASICs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an ASIC

An **Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC)** is an integrated circuit (IC) that is custom-designed and fabricated to perform a dedicated function for a specific application, as opposed to a general-purpose IC (like a microprocessor or memory chip) which is designed to serve a wide range of applications.

The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes ASICs along a *design-style spectrum* based on how much of the chip is pre-designed versus how much is custom-built. The three principal classifications are:

1. **Full Custom ASICs** – every transistor, every interconnect, and every mask layer is hand-crafted.
2. **Standard-Cell (Cell-Based) ASICs / Semi-Custom ASICs** – pre-designed logic cells from a library are placed and routed automatically.
3. **Gate-Array / Programmable ASICs (FPGAs, PLDs, CPLDs)** – prefabricated arrays of logic are electrically or mask-programmed by the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Full Custom ASIC (KTU 2024 Definition):** A Full Custom ASIC is an integrated circuit in which **all** mask layers are custom-designed, **all** transistors are individually sized, **all** layout geometries are hand-optimized, and **no** pre-defined cell library or pre-fabricated wafer is used. Every single geometric detail on every photomask is generated specifically for that one design.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Bespoke Tailor vs. The Off-the-Rack Shop

Think of chip design like buying clothes:

- A **Full Custom ASIC** is like a **bespoke Savile Row suit**. A master tailor measures your body down to the millimetre, drafts an individual pattern, hand-cuts the fabric, and sews every stitch. The result is a perfect fit, minimal waste, and the highest possible performance, but it takes months of effort, costs a fortune, and is impossible to alter later.

- A **Standard-Cell ASIC** is like a **made-to-measure suit from a premium ready-made catalogue**. You pick the lapel, the buttons, and the lining from a curated library, and the tailor assembles them. It looks good, performs well, and is faster to build, but the underlying fabric panels are standard widths.

- A **Programmable ASIC / FPGA** is like an **off-the-rack suit you buy in a department store**. It is fully fabricated, you just take it home and adjust a few hems with a sewing kit. It is fast and cheap, but the fit is generic and the performance is limited.

In Full Custom ASIC design, the engineer is the *master tailor* of silicon, controlling every electron path.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lambda (design rule) | $\lambda$ | Process-dependent (e.g., $0.5\,\mu m$ for $1.0\,\mu m$ tech) | Minimum feature size unit |
| Silicon Dielectric Constant | $\varepsilon_{si}$ | **11.7** | MOSFET body capacitance |
| SiO₂ Dielectric Constant | $\varepsilon_{ox}$ | **3.97** | Gate oxide capacitance |
| Intrinsic Carrier Concentration (Si) | $n_i$ | $1.45 \times 10^{10}\,cm^{-3}$ | Body doping baseline |
| Electron Mobility | $\mu_n$ | **1350 cm²/V·s** | NMOS drive current |
| Hole Mobility | $\mu_p$ | **480 cm²/V·s** | PMOS drive current |
| Permittivity of Free Space | $\varepsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-14}\,F/cm$ | Universal constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The mobility ratio $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.8$ to $3$ is the fundamental reason a PMOS transistor in a full-custom layout must be made approximately **2 to 2.5 times wider** than an NMOS to deliver the same drive current. This is a routine interview and 3-mark question topic.

## 1.4 Why "Full Custom" Exists

Not every chip can be made with standard cells. Certain applications demand extreme performance, ultra-low power, ultra-small area, or a unique analog function. In these cases, off-the-shelf logic cells become the bottleneck, and the engineer must design every transistor by hand.

- **High-frequency analog/RF front-ends** (e.g., $60\,GHz$ transceivers).
- **Precision mixed-signal blocks** (ADCs, DACs, PLLs, Voltage References).
- **Memory cells** (SRAM bit-cells, DRAM sense amplifiers, ROM arrays).
- **I/O pad cells** (ESD protection, high-current drivers).
- **Low-power biomedical implants** where quiescent current is measured in nanoamperes.
- **High-volume consumer chips** (smartphone SoCs, GPUs) where the NRE cost is amortized across millions of units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Full Custom Design Flow (KTU-Exam-Critical)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate the full-custom design flow as a closed loop between logical design and physical design, broken down into these eight phases:

### Phase 1: System Specification
- Define the **functionality, performance, area, power, and I/O requirements** in a natural-language document.
- Example: "Design a 10-bit, $100\,MS/s$ SAR ADC with SNR $> 60\,dB$ and power $< 1\,mW$."

### Phase 2: Functional / Architectural Design
- Partition the system into **functional blocks** (e.g., sample-and-hold, comparator, DAC, control logic).
- Decide which blocks will be **full custom** and which will be synthesized from a standard-cell library.
- Build a **high-level block diagram** with timing budgets and signal-flow arrows.

### Phase 3: Logic Design
- For digital blocks: write the **Boolean equations, truth tables, and state-transition tables**.
- For analog blocks: derive **small-signal equations, DC operating points, and transistor-level schematics**.

### Phase 4: Circuit Design
- Hand-size every transistor ($W/L$) using hand calculations or SPICE simulation.
- Choose device types: NMOS, PMOS, native, low-Vt, high-Vt, thick-oxide I/O.
- This is where the **full custom** nature is most visible: device dimensions, bias currents, and matching strategies (e.g., common-centroid, interdigitated) are chosen by the engineer.

### Phase 5: Physical Design / Layout
This is the heart of full custom work. It is broken down into four sub-steps:
- **Floorplanning** – decide the rough placement of major blocks and pad frame.
- **Placement** – manually position every transistor inside each block.
- **Routing** – manually draw every poly, metal-1, metal-2 interconnect.
- **Verification** – run **DRC (Design Rule Check)**, **LVS (Layout Versus Schematic)**, and **ERC (Electrical Rule Check)**.
- After verification, perform **parasitic extraction (PEX)** to obtain RC values, then re-simulate with **post-layout SPICE**.

### Phase 6: Fabrication Mask Generation
- Convert the verified layout GDS-II file into a set of **photolithographic masks**, one per process layer.
