- For an $N$-well CMOS process with $M$ metal layers, you typically generate $N+2+M$ masks (n-well, p-well, active, poly, n+, p+, contacts, metal-1 … metal-M, vias, pad).

### Phase 7: Fabrication
- Send the mask set to a foundry (TSMC, GlobalFoundries, SCL, etc.).
- The wafer goes through oxidation, implantation, diffusion, deposition, lithography, etch, CMP, and packaging.

### Phase 8: Testing & Packaging
- Wafer-probe test, dicing, wire-bonding/flip-chip, final test.

## 2.2 Lambda-Based Design Rules (Mead-Conway)

The KTU 2024 syllabus places heavy emphasis on $\lambda$-based (Mead-Conway) design rules. Every geometric feature is expressed as a multiple of a single technology parameter, $\lambda$, which is **half the minimum feature size** the process can print.

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{min}}{2}$$

For example, a $1.0\,\mu m$ process has $L_{min} = 1.0\,\mu m$, so $\lambda = 0.5\,\mu m$. A $0.18\,\mu m$ process has $\lambda = 0.09\,\mu m$.

### Why $\lambda$ rules matter
They allow a layout to be **scaled linearly** to a new process simply by multiplying all coordinates by a new $\lambda$. This gives portable, technology-independent teaching layouts.

### Core $\lambda$-Based Design Rules for an N-Well CMOS Process

| Rule | Layer(s) | Constraint | Typical $\lambda$ Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum poly width | Poly | Width of poly gate | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum active width | Active | Width of diffusion | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum metal width | Metal-1, Metal-2 | Width of interconnect | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum metal spacing | Metal-1 to Metal-1 | Same-net distance | $3\lambda$ |
| Poly spacing to poly | Poly to Poly | Different gates | $2\lambda$ |
| Active spacing to active | Active to Active | Same well | $3\lambda$ |
| Poly to active spacing (gate) | Poly to Active | Inside an MOS region | $2\lambda$ |
| Poly overhang of active | Poly to Active | End cap of gate | $2\lambda$ |
| Active overhang of poly | Active to Poly | Source/drain extension | $3\lambda$ |
| Contact size | Cut | Square contact dimension | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ |
| Metal overlap of contact | Metal to Cut | Around a via | $1\lambda$ |
| Poly overlap of contact | Poly to Cut | Poly-to-metal contact | $1\lambda$ |
| Active overlap of contact | Active to Cut | Diffusion-to-metal contact | $1\lambda$ |
| N-well overlap of n+ | n+ to n-well | PMOS body tie | $6\lambda$ (or rule-dependent) |
| Substrate contact enclosure | Active to n-well | Substrate/well tie | $2\lambda$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Trap:** Students often confuse "minimum width" and "minimum spacing". A $3\lambda$ **width** of metal means the metal line itself must be drawn $3\lambda$ wide. A $3\lambda$ **spacing** means the *distance between the edges* of two parallel metal lines must be $3\lambda$ (center-to-center is $6\lambda$).

## 2.3 Stick Diagram Notation (KTU Exam Critical)

A **stick diagram** is an abstract, color-coded, technology-independent symbolic representation of a CMOS layout. It is the bridge between a schematic and a full geometric layout, and KTU frequently asks students to draw stick diagrams.

The KTU-mandated color/line convention is:

| Layer | Color | Drawing Style |
|---|---|---|
| n-diffusion (n+) | **Green** | Solid line with a green dot at contacts |
| p-diffusion (p+) | **Orange / Brown** | Solid line with an orange dot at contacts |
| Polysilicon (poly / gate) | **Red** | Solid line, runs *across* diffusion to form a gate |
| Metal-1 (or generic metal) | **Blue** | Solid line |
| Metal-2 | **Violet / Purple** (optional) | Solid line |
| Contact (cut / via) | **Black** | Solid square or X |
| n-well (boundary) | Dashed green/brown box | Not a drawn layer, just an enclosure |

### Stick Diagram Rules
1. A **red line (poly) crossing a green line (n+)** forms an **NMOS transistor**.
2. A **red line (poly) crossing an orange line (p+)** forms a **PMOS transistor**.
3. Two transistors in **series** share a diffusion strip between their gates.
4. Two transistors in **parallel** have separate diffusion strips connected by a metal-1 wire.
5. **VDD and GND rails** are typically drawn as horizontal metal-1 lines at the top and bottom.
6. The **smallest stick** is a $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ contact cut.

## 2.4 The CMOS Inverter as the Canonical Full Custom Block

The CMOS inverter is the smallest, most fundamental full-custom cell. Its transistor-level schematic, stick diagram, and mask layout are exam staples.

### Transistor-Level Schematic
- **NMOS**: source to GND, drain to output node $Y$, gate to input $A$.
- **PMOS**: source to $V_{DD}$, drain to output node $Y$, gate to input $A$.

### Stick Diagram of CMOS Inverter
- Top horizontal **blue line** = $V_{DD}$ rail.
- Bottom horizontal **blue line** = GND rail.
- Vertical **orange strip** (p+ diffusion) extending from $V_{DD}$ downward.
- Vertical **green strip** (n+ diffusion) extending from GND upward.
- A single **red vertical line (poly gate)** crossing both strips = input $A$.
- A **blue horizontal line** joining the middle (drain-drain connection) = output $Y$.

The widths chosen follow the **mobility ratio** rule: the p+ diffusion strip is drawn $2\times$ wider than the n+ diffusion strip so that the PMOS $W_p$ is $2 W_n$.

## 2.5 Why Engineers Choose Full Custom

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists these specific drivers:

1. **Maximum Performance** – hand-tuned transistor sizing and matching for highest speed.
2. **Minimum Area** – no wasted silicon from pre-defined cell footprints; layouts can share source/drain regions.
3. **Minimum Power** – bias currents and transistor stacks can be individually minimized.
4. **Analog Precision** – required for op-amps, bandgaps, PLLs where transistor matching is critical.
5. **Intellectual Property Protection** – no off-the-shelf library is borrowed, so reverse engineering is harder.
6. **Unique I/O Requirements** – high-voltage drivers, ESD clamps, RF pads.

## 2.6 Disadvantages (Examiner's Favourite 3-Mark Question)

1. **Very High NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) Cost** – mask set + design time can run into millions of dollars and months of effort.
2. **Long Time-to-Market** – full verification, parasitic extraction, and tape-out are slow.
3. **Low Design Reusability** – the layout is single-purpose; the IP cannot be dropped into another product.
4. **High Designer Skill Required** – every layout engineer must be a master of process rules and matching.
5. **Error-Prone** – manual layout is more likely to contain DRC violations than automated standard-cell routing.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = L_{min}/2$ | Define design-rule grid from minimum channel length |
| 2 | $\beta_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n$ | NMOS transconductance parameter |
| 3 | $\beta_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p$ | PMOS transconductance parameter |
| 4 | $\mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2.8$ to $3$ | Mobility ratio → size PMOS wider to match drive |
| 5 | $W_p / W_n = \mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$ to $2.5$ | Width ratio for symmetric CMOS inverter |
| 6 | $t_{pLH} \approx 0.69 R_{eq,p} C_{L}$ | Inverter low-to-high propagation delay |
| 7 | $t_{pHL} \approx 0.69 R_{eq,n} C_{L}$ | Inverter high-to-low propagation delay |
| 8 | $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area |
| 9 | $C_{L} = C_{gate,N+1} + C_{wire}$ | Load capacitance for delay estimation |
| 10 | $I_{Dsat} = (1/2)\mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ | Saturation current (square-law) |
| 11 | $R_{eq} \approx 1/(C_{ox} W \mu (V_{GS}-V_T))$ | Equivalent on-resistance of an MOS switch |
| 12 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic switching power |
| 13 | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} V_{DD}$ | Static leakage power |
| 14 | Noise Margin $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High-level noise margin |
| 15 | Noise Margin $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low-level noise margin |
| 16 | Power Delay Product $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | Energy per switching event (figure of merit) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use the vertical pipe** `|` inside any of these formulas when writing them in your exam answer-sheet tables. The KTU answer sheet evaluates layout, and a broken table may lose presentation marks. Substitute `\vert` (e.g., write $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.8$ as shown above) or use the word "divided by".

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Full custom design is the **lingua franca** of every mixed-signal and high-performance product. A modern smartphone SoC contains dozens of full custom analog/RF blocks (LDOs, PLLs, ADC/DAC, SerDes, MIPI PHYs) wrapped in a sea of synthesized digital logic. The analog IP is always full custom because no standard cell can implement a transconductance amplifier or a bandgap reference. Similarly, the **bit-cell of every SRAM and DRAM** in the world is full custom, because the cell area determines chip density and yield.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Lambda Scaling for a $1.0\,\mu m$ Process to a $0.5\,\mu m$ Process

**Problem Statement:** A designer has a full custom layout drawn for a $1.0\,\mu m$ CMOS process. They wish to migrate it to a $0.5\,\mu m$ process using $\lambda$-based scaling. Compute the new feature sizes if every dimension in the original layout is $X\lambda_{old}$.

**Step 1: Identify the minimum feature size of the original process.**

$$L_{min,old} = 1.0\,\mu m$$

**Step 2: Compute the original lambda.**

$$\lambda_{old} = \frac{L_{min,old}}{2} = \frac{1.0\,\mu m}{2} = 0.5\,\mu m$$

**Step 3: Identify the minimum feature size of the new process.**

$$L_{min,new} = 0.5\,\mu m$$

**Step 4: Compute the new lambda.**

$$\lambda_{new} = \frac{L_{min,new}}{2} = \frac{0.5\,\mu m}{2} = 0.25\,\mu m$$

**Step 5: Express every original dimension in absolute micrometers first.**

For a poly width of $2\lambda_{old}$:
$$W_{poly,old} = 2 \times 0.5\,\mu m = 1.0\,\mu m$$

**Step 6: Compute the new lambda-equivalent of that same absolute width.**

$$W_{poly,new} = \frac{W_{poly,old}}{\lambda_{new}} \lambda = \frac{1.0\,\mu m}{0.25\,\mu m}\lambda = 4\lambda_{new}$$

**Step 7: Generalize.** Any dimension $D$ in absolute micrometers scales as:

$$D_{new} = D_{old} \times \frac{\lambda_{old}}{\lambda_{new}}$$

**Step 8: Verify by computing the new absolute poly width.**
$$D_{poly,new} = 4 \times 0.25\,\mu m = 1.0\,\mu m$$
Note: A $2\lambda$ rule in the $1\,\mu m$ process and a $4\lambda$ rule in the $0.5\,\mu m$ process both yield $1.0\,\mu m$ poly width, confirming the linear-scaling property of $\lambda$ rules.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverter Symmetry Ratio

**Problem Statement:** Derive the PMOS-to-NMOS width ratio $(W_p / W_n)$ needed for a CMOS inverter to have equal rise and fall times, given the electron and hole mobilities in silicon.

**Step 1: Recall the equivalent on-resistance of an MOS transistor in saturation (long-channel, square-law model).**

$$R_{eq,n} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W_n/L)(V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn})}$$

**Step 2: Write the analogous equation for the PMOS.**

$$R_{eq,p} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W_p/L)(V_{GS,p} - V_{Tp}\vert)}$$

**Step 3: For a symmetric inverter, force $R_{eq,n} = R_{eq,p}$ and assume $|V_{GS,n}-V_{Tn}| = |V_{GS,p}-V_{Tp}\vert$, equal $L$, equal $C_{ox}$, and equal oxide thickness $t_{ox}$.**

$$\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W_n}{L}(V_{GS,n}-V_{Tn}) = \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W_p}{L}(V_{GS,p}-V_{Tp}\vert)$$

**Step 4: Cancel $C_{ox}$ and $L$.**

$$\mu_n W_n (V_{GS,n}-V_{Tn}) = \mu_p W_p (V_{GS,p}-V_{Tp}\vert)$$

**Step 5: Assume matched overdrives $(V_{GS,n}-V_{Tn}) = (V_{GS,p}-V_{Tp}\vert)$.**

$$\mu_n W_n = \mu_p W_p$$

**Step 6: Solve for the width ratio.**

$$\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p}$$

**Step 7: Substitute the standard silicon mobility values $\mu_n = 1350\,cm^2/V\cdot s$ and $\mu_p = 480\,cm^2/V\cdot s$.**

$$\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{1350}{480} \approx 2.81$$

**Step 8: Practical design conclusion.** A PMOS drawn $2.5$ to $3$ times wider than the NMOS gives matched drive strength. In real VLSI design, $W_p/W_n \approx 2$ to $2.5$ is used because velocity saturation in short-channel devices reduces the effective mobility gap.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Two-Input NAND Layout Area in $\lambda^2$

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the area of a two-input CMOS NAND gate in $\lambda^2$ using standard $\lambda$-based design rules.

**Step 1: Topological analysis.** A 2-input NAND has **2 PMOS in parallel** and **2 NMOS in series**.

**Step 2: PMOS layout (parallel).** Two PMOS share drain and source columns. Using $\lambda$ rules:
- Each PMOS gate = $2\lambda$ wide (poly width).
- Poly-to-poly spacing (different gates) = $2\lambda$.
- So one PMOS pair (with shared drain) = $2\lambda + 2\lambda + 2\lambda = 6\lambda$ of vertical height.
- p+ diffusion strips = $3\lambda$ wide (active width) each.

**Step 3: NMOS layout (series).** Two NMOS in series with a shared middle diffusion (inner node). Vertical height:
- Two poly gates ($2\lambda + 2\lambda$), one poly-to-poly spacing ($2\lambda$), plus active source/drain overlaps ($3\lambda + 3\lambda$).
- Total = $2\lambda + 2\lambda + 2\lambda + 3\lambda + 3\lambda = 12\lambda$.

**Step 4: Width (horizontal).**
- Source/drain diffusion strips = $3\lambda$ (minimum active).
- Plus $2\lambda$ to $3\lambda$ for n-well/body-contact enclosures on the side.
- Approximate width = $3\lambda + 2\lambda = 5\lambda$ per transistor pair.
- For 4 transistors in two columns, width = $2 \times 5\lambda = 10\lambda$.

**Step 5: Compute the bounding-box area.**

$$A_{NAND2} = 10\lambda \times 12\lambda = 120\,\lambda^2$$

**Step 6: Compare with a 2-input NOR.** A NOR has PMOS in series (taller p-stack) and NMOS in parallel (shorter n-stack). The PMOS series is now the height bottleneck:
- PMOS stack height = $12\lambda$ (using the same logic as the NMOS series above).
- NMOS pair width = $5\lambda \times 2 = 10\lambda$.
- $A_{NOR2} \approx 120\,\lambda^2$.

**Step 7: Observation.** NAND and NOR have similar areas, but their **transistor-arrangement asymmetry** affects performance. NAND has weak PMOS in parallel (faster rise, slower fall), NOR has weak NMOS in parallel (faster fall, slower rise). This is the origin of the well-known digital-design rule "NAND is preferred over NOR in CMOS logic."

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dynamic Switching Power of a Full-Custom Datapath Block

**Problem Statement:** A 32-bit ripple-carry adder in a $65\,nm$ full-custom ASIC is clocked at $f = 1\,GHz$. The total switched capacitance is $C_L = 50\,pF$, the activity factor is $\alpha = 0.1$, and $V_{DD} = 1.0\,V$. Compute the dynamic power dissipation.

**Step 1: Recall the dynamic power formula.**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$P_{dyn} = 0.1 \times 50 \times 10^{-12}\,F \times (1.0\,V)^2 \times 10^9\,Hz$$

**Step 3: Evaluate.**

$$P_{dyn} = 0.1 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0 \times 10^9$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.1 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{dyn} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,W = 5\,mW$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.** Even a moderate-size full-custom block dissipates milliwatts of dynamic power. At $1\,GHz$ clocking, lowering $V_{DD}$ to $0.7\,V$ would reduce this to:

$$P_{dyn}' = 0.1 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.49 \times 10^9 \approx 2.45\,mW$$

A $30\%$ voltage drop gives a $51\%$ power saving – the quadratic dependence of power on voltage is the strongest knob in low-power VLSI design.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Full Custom vs. Semi-Custom vs. Programmable

| Attribute | Full Custom | Standard Cell (Semi-Custom) | FPGA (Programmable) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mask layers unique per design | **All** (full mask set) | Most (interconnect, contacts, vias) | None (pre-fabricated) |
| Transistor geometry | **Hand-sized individually** | From library | Fixed by FPGA vendor |
| Layout effort per gate | **Highest** (hours) | Medium (seconds) | Lowest (place & route) |
| Performance (speed) | **Highest** | Medium | Lowest |
| Power | **Lowest** | Medium | Highest |
| Area | **Smallest** | Medium | Largest |
| NRE cost | **Very high** | Medium | Zero (only device cost) |
| Time-to-market | **Slowest** (months) | Medium (weeks) | Fastest (hours/days) |
| Reusability across products | **Low** | Medium (cell library is reusable) | High (bitstream re-flashable) |
| Typical application | Analog/RF, SRAM, I/O | ASICs, SoC digital logic | Prototyping, low-volume |
| Example | Voltage regulator op-amp | AES crypto engine | RISC-V soft core prototype |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU valuation key awards **1 mark for each correctly identified difference** in a comparison question. Memorize the table row by row, not column by column, so that you can write a complete sentence for each difference under exam pressure.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Full Custom ASIC Design Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SpecDocument] --> B[ArchitecturePartition]
    B --> C1[AnalogBlocksFullCustom]
    B --> C2[DigitalLogicDesign]
    C1 --> D1[TransistorLevelSchematic]
    C2 --> D2[BooleanLogicSynthesis]
    D1 --> E1[HandSizedWLCalculation]
    D2 --> E2[StandardCellMapping]
    E1 --> F1[ManualMaskLayout]
    E2 --> F2[AutomaticPlaceAndRoute]
    F1 --> G1[DRCLVSERCRun]
    F2 --> G2[DRCLVSERCRun]
    G1 --> H1[ParasiticExtractionPEX]
    G2 --> H2[ParasiticExtractionPEX]
    H1 --> I1[PostLayoutSPICE]
    H2 --> I2[StaticTimingAnalysis]
    I1 --> J1[MaskTapeoutGDSII]
    I2 --> J2[MaskTapeoutGDSII]
    J1 --> K[FoundryWaferFab]
    J2 --> K
    K --> L[WaferProbeTest]
    L --> M[PackagingDicing]
    M --> N[FinalProductionTest]
    N --> O[ShippedASICProduct]
```

### How to read this diagram
- Nodes `A` and `B` (spec, architecture) are common to all ASIC styles.
- The flow **branches** at `C1`/`C2`: full custom takes the left path with handcrafted schematics and layout; semi-custom takes the right path with synthesized logic.
- Both paths **converge** at `G1`/`G2` (DRC/LVS), `H1`/`H2` (PEX), and `J1`/`J2` (tapeout), then merge into a single fabrication flow from `K` onward.
- The verifier (`G1`/`G2`) is a *gate*: if DRC/LVS fails, the loop returns to the manual layout or place-and-route step (KTU examiners love flow-diagram loop arrows – consider drawing them in the exam).

## 4.2 Full Custom Cell Library Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[FullCustomASIC] --> IO[IOandPadRing]
    Root --> Analog[AnalogBlocks]
    Root --> Memory[MemoryBlocks]
    Root --> Digital[DigitalBlocks]
    IO --> ESD[ESDProtectionClamps]
    IO --> Driver[HighCurrentPadDrivers]
    Analog --> OpAmp[OpAmpsFullCustom]
    Analog --> PLL[PLLandVCO]
    Analog --> ADC[ADCandDAC]
    Memory --> SRAM[SRAMBitCell]
    Memory --> DRAM[DRAMSenseAmp]
    Digital --> SC[StandardCellBlocks]
    Digital --> DC[DataPathFullCustom]
    OpAmp --> DiffPair[DifferentialInputPair]
    OpAmp --> CurMir[CurrentMirrorLoad]
    SRAM --> BitCell[SixTransistorCell]
    SRAM --> WMA[WriteDriverandSenseAmp]
```

### Reading the diagram
- `Root` represents the entire chip. It is partitioned into four major block families: `IO`, `Analog`, `Memory`, `Digital`.
- Each family is built from a small number of **hand-crafted primitive cells**. For example, the `OpAmp` block is composed of a `DiffPair` and a `CurMir` (current mirror), each of which is drawn manually at the transistor level.
- The `SRAM` block's `BitCell` is a $6$-transistor cell drawn to the tightest possible area. Its physical design is fully custom – no library version exists for the bit-cell because area determines yield.

## 4.3 CMOS Inverter: Schematic, Stick Diagram, and Mask Layout (Block-Level Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[SchematicNMOSSourceGND] --> A1[DrainOutputNodeY]
    A0 --> A2[GateInputA]
    B0[SchematicPMOSSourceVDD] --> A1
    B0 --> A2
    A1 --> A3[StickDiagramOutputYMetal1]
    A2 --> A4[StickDiagramInputAPoly]
    A3 --> A5[MaskLayoutMetal1Layer]
    A4 --> A6[MaskLayoutPolyLayer]
    A5 --> A7[GDSIITapeout]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[FabricatedInverterCell]
```

### Reading the diagram
- The schematic (`A0`, `A1`, `A2`, `B0`) defines the topology.
- The stick diagram (`A3`, `A4`) abstracts the topology using color-coded lines.
- The mask layout (`A5`, `A6`) adds geometric dimensions and DRC compliance.
- All three representations refer to the *same* physical circuit, and full-custom engineers must keep them in sync. A mismatch between the schematic and the layout is caught by the **LVS (Layout Versus Schematic)** check.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Full Custom ASIC. List two situations in which it is preferred over a standard-cell ASIC.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (concise, 3-mark answer):**

> A **Full Custom ASIC** is an integrated circuit in which **all mask layers** and **all device geometries** are designed by hand for a specific application, with no use of pre-defined standard-cell libraries or pre-fabricated wafers. Every transistor's $W$ and $L$, every diffusion region, and every interconnect is custom-sized by the designer.
>
> It is preferred over a standard-cell ASIC in two situations:
> 1. **High-frequency analog/RF blocks** (e.g., $5\,GHz$ PLLs, $60\,GHz$ transceivers) where hand-tuned matching and parasitic control are mandatory.
> 2. **Ultra-low-power or high-density regular structures** (e.g., SRAM bit-cells, sensor read-out circuits) where a standard cell would be too large, too leaky, or too slow.

---

### Question 2: What are $\lambda$-based design rules? Why are they useful in full custom layout?
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (concise, 3-mark answer):**

> $\lambda$-based design rules (introduced by **Mead and Conway**) are a set of technology-portable geometric constraints in which every layout dimension (minimum width, minimum spacing, enclosure) is expressed as a multiple of a single parameter $\lambda = L_{min}/2$, where $L_{min}$ is the smallest printable feature size of the process.
>
> They are useful in full custom layout for two reasons:
> 1. **Technology portability** – a layout drawn for $\lambda_{old}$ can be linearly scaled to a new process by recomputing $\lambda_{new}$ and rescaling all coordinates. The same stick diagram and topology work across process generations.
> 2. **Pedagogical clarity** – students and designers can reason about layout density and design-rule correctness in dimensionless $\lambda$ units without worrying about absolute micrometers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): Full Custom Design Flow and Inverter Layout

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **complete design flow** of a Full Custom ASIC, starting from system specification and ending at packaging. Highlight at which stages **DRC**, **LVS**, and **post-layout SPICE simulation** are performed. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Draw the **stick diagram** of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate. Label the VDD rail, GND rail, all inputs, the output node, and identify the series NMOS stack and the parallel PMOS stack. Show the metal-1 and poly connections. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The full custom ASIC design flow consists of eight sequential phases, with verification gates inserted at strategic points:

1. **System Specification** – Define functionality, performance, power, area, and I/O requirements.
2. **Architectural Design** – Partition the system into analog and digital blocks. Decide which blocks will be full custom.
3. **Circuit Design** – Hand-draw transistor-level schematics, choose $W/L$ ratios.
4. **Physical Design / Layout** – Floorplan, placement, routing. This is the heart of full custom work.
5. **DRC (Design Rule Check)** – Run after layout to ensure all geometric rules are met. The layout is rejected and re-edited if violations are found.
6. **LVS (Layout Versus Schematic)** – Run after DRC to ensure that the layout's netlist exactly matches the schematic's netlist (no missing or extra devices/wires).
7. **Parasitic Extraction (PEX)** – Extract resistance and capacitance from the layout geometries.
8. **Post-Layout SPICE Simulation** – Re-simulate the circuit with the extracted RC values to verify timing, power, and noise margins.
9. **Tapeout** – Convert the verified GDS-II to a mask set and send to the foundry.
10. **Fabrication and Packaging** – Wafer fab, dicing, bonding, final test.

The verification gate is critical: any DRC, LVS, or post-layout simulation failure sends the design back to the layout stage. KTU valuation awards 1 mark per phase correctly identified, 1 mark for the verification loop, and 1 mark for the tapeout/fabrication link.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Stick Diagram of 2-Input CMOS NAND Gate:**

The 2-input NAND has two PMOS in parallel (top, in the p+ diffusion) and two NMOS in series (bottom, in the n+ diffusion). The shared input poly lines for A and B run vertically.

**Topology Summary (verbal description, since stick diagrams are drawn by hand in the exam):**

- **Top horizontal blue line** = $V_{DD}$ rail.
- **Two orange p+ diffusion strips** descend vertically from the $V_{DD}$ rail. Each strip hosts one PMOS.
- **A single red horizontal poly line** carrying input A crosses both p+ strips (forming two PMOS gates simultaneously, since the inputs are tied together in NAND).
- **A second red horizontal poly line** carrying input B crosses both p+ strips as well, creating a second pair of gates.
  - Note: in a true 2-input NAND with two PMOS in parallel, only **one poly line per input** is needed. The PMOS are arranged side-by-side (parallel), so A and B each gate one PMOS.
- **Two green n+ diffusion strips** descend from the output node, joined at the bottom by a single source-drain column connected to GND.
- **Two red poly gates** (A and B) cross the n+ strips. Because the NMOS are in series, there is a **shared middle diffusion** (the internal node) between the two gates.
- **Bottom horizontal blue line** = GND rail.
- **A middle blue horizontal line** = output node $Y$, joining the drains of the PMOS pair and the drain of the top NMOS.

**Labels to write in the diagram:**
- $V_{DD}$ on top rail, $GND$ on bottom rail.
- Input $A$ and input $B$ on the poly lines.
- Output $Y$ on the middle metal-1 line.
- "PMOS parallel pair" circled at the top.
- "NMOS series stack" circled at the bottom.
- "Shared n+ diffusion (internal node)" labelled at the middle of the NMOS stack.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks for correct PMOS parallel arrangement with VDD rail and output Y.
- 2 marks for correct NMOS series stack with shared internal diffusion.
- 1 mark for poly inputs A and B and their crossings.
- 1 mark for clean labelling and use of standard stick-diagram color convention.
- 1 mark for correctly identifying parallel-PMOS and series-NMOS topology verbally.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Design Rules and CMOS Inverter Sizing (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain **Mead-Conway $\lambda$-based design rules**. List the minimum-width and minimum-spacing values for poly, active, and metal-1 layers. A poly gate of width $2\lambda$ in a $1.0\,\mu m$ process is to be migrated to a $0.25\,\mu m$ process. Compute the new $\lambda$ and the new poly width. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a symmetric CMOS inverter, derive the PMOS-to-NMOS width ratio $(W_p/W_n)$ required to equalize the rise and fall times. Use $\mu_n = 1350\,cm^2/V\cdot s$ and $\mu_p = 480\,cm^2/V\cdot s$. Comment on why real designs use a slightly smaller ratio of $2$ to $2.5$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Mead-Conway $\lambda$-based design rules express every layout constraint (width, spacing, enclosure) as an integer multiple of $\lambda$, where $\lambda = L_{min}/2$. The advantage is that a layout designed with $\lambda$ rules can be linearly scaled to any new process by recomputing $\lambda$ and rescaling all coordinates.

**Standard $\lambda$ values:**

- Minimum poly width = $2\lambda$
- Minimum active width = $3\lambda$
- Minimum metal-1 width = $3\lambda$
- Poly-to-poly spacing = $2\lambda$
- Active-to-active spacing = $3\lambda$
- Metal-1 to metal-1 spacing = $3\lambda$

**Migration calculation:**

For the $1.0\,\mu m$ process, $L_{min} = 1.0\,\mu m$, so $\lambda_{old} = 0.5\,\mu m$. The original poly width is $2\lambda_{old} = 2 \times 0.5\,\mu m = 1.0\,\mu m$.

For the $0.25\,\mu m$ process, $L_{min} = 0.25\,\mu m$, so $\lambda_{new} = 0.25/2 = 0.125\,\mu m$.

The new poly width in absolute micrometers must remain the same (linear scaling):

$$W_{poly,new} = 1.0\,\mu m = \frac{1.0\,\mu m}{0.125\,\mu m}\lambda_{new} = 8\lambda_{new}$$

So the same $2\lambda$ rule in the old process becomes an $8\lambda$ rule in the new process, but both yield the same absolute width of $1.0\,\mu m$.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks for explaining $\lambda$ rules and defining $\lambda = L_{min}/2$.
- 1 mark for listing the three minimum-width rules.
- 1 mark for listing the three minimum-spacing rules.
- 2 marks for the new $\lambda$ calculation.
- 1 mark for the new poly width calculation.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Derivation of $W_p/W_n$:**

The equivalent on-resistance of a long-channel MOS in saturation is:

$$R_{eq,n} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox}(W_n/L)(V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn})}$$
$$R_{eq,p} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox}(W_p/L)(V_{GS,p} - V_{Tp}\vert)}$$

For symmetric rise and fall times, $R_{eq,n} = R_{eq,p}$. Assuming matched overdrives and equal $L$ and $C_{ox}$:

$$\mu_n W_n = \mu_p W_p$$

$$\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} = \frac{1350}{480} \approx 2.81$$

**Comment on real designs:** In deep-submicron processes (e.g., $65\,nm$ and below), **velocity saturation** reduces the effective carrier mobility. Because holes saturate at a higher velocity than electrons, the effective mobility ratio drops to about $1.5$ to $2.0$. Designers therefore choose $W_p/W_n \approx 2$ to $2.5$ rather than the ideal $2.81$, which saves area while still providing nearly symmetric switching.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks for writing the two on-resistance equations.
- 1 mark for the symmetry condition $R_{eq,n} = R_{eq,p}$.
- 1 mark for the algebraic cancellation and final ratio.
- 1 mark for the numerical answer $2.81$.
- 2 marks for the velocity-saturation comment and the $2$ to $2.5$ practical ratio.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "width" and "spacing"**: A $3\lambda$ poly width and a $3\lambda$ poly-to-poly spacing are different constraints. Many students write "minimum width = $3\lambda$" for the spacing column. **Lose 0.5–1 mark per such error.**
> 2. **Forgetting the body-effect symbol $\vert$**: When writing $V_{Tp}\vert$ or $V_{GS,p} - V_{Tp}\vert$ in equations, students sometimes drop the absolute value. KTU examiners mark it as a sign-error.
> 3. **Mixing up $\lambda$ and $L_{min}$**: $\lambda$ is **half** the minimum feature, not the minimum feature itself. The $0.5\,\mu m$ process has $\lambda = 0.25\,\mu m$, not $0.5\,\mu m$. Examiners deduct marks for this slip.
> 4. **Stick diagram color errors**: Using a single color for poly and metal, or omitting the contact cross-hatch. Always use red for poly and blue for metal-1.
> 5. **No verification loop in the design flow**: Omitting the back-arrow from DRC/LVS back to the layout stage. A flow diagram without a verification loop is considered incomplete (lose 1 mark).
> 6. **Skipping post-layout simulation**: Many students stop at LVS and forget the **post-layout SPICE** step with parasitic extraction. This is the difference between a "synthesizable" digital design and a true full-custom design, and is worth 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Full Custom ASIC** = every mask layer is unique, every transistor is hand-sized, no library reuse, highest performance/area/power efficiency, but huge NRE and long design time.
- **ASIC spectrum** = Full Custom $\rightarrow$ Standard Cell $\rightarrow$ Gate Array $\rightarrow$ FPGA. Each step rightwards trades performance for ease and reusability.
- **Lambda rule** = $\lambda = L_{min}/2$. All widths, spacings, and enclosures are expressed as integer multiples of $\lambda$. This gives linear technology portability.
- **Standard $\lambda$ values to memorize**: poly width $2\lambda$, active width $3\lambda$, metal width $3\lambda$, contact $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$, poly-to-active gate spacing $2\lambda$.
- **Stick diagram colors**: red = poly, green = n+, orange = p+, blue = metal-1, black square = contact. Crossing red over green/橙色 forms a transistor gate.
- **PMOS/NMOS sizing**: $W_p/W_n = \mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2.81$ for symmetry. Real designs use $2$ to $2.5$ due to velocity saturation.
- **Mobility values**: $\mu_n \approx 1350\,cm^2/V\cdot s$, $\mu_p \approx 480\,cm^2/V\cdot s$. These are KTU constants and must be memorized.
- **Verification triad**: DRC (geometry rules), LVS (schematic-vs-layout), PEX+Post-Layout SPICE (timing/parasitics). All three are mandatory before tapeout.
- **Design flow loops**: Every failure in DRC, LVS, or PEX forces a return to the layout stage. The flow is not strictly linear.
- **Dynamic power formula**: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. Voltage has a **quadratic** effect on power – the single strongest low-power knob.
- **Where full custom is unavoidable**: analog/RF blocks, SRAM bit-cells, ESD/IO pads, high-precision references, ultra-low-power sensor front-ends.
- **Standard stick-diagram check**: PMOS in parallel / NMOS in series = NAND. PMOS in series / NMOS in parallel = NOR. Memorize the topology before drawing.
- **The three KTU-mandated verification tools**: DRC, LVS, ERC – each is a separate CAD run with a different purpose, and you must name them in the design-flow answer.
- **Tapeout milestone**: Once GDS-II passes all checks, it is "taped out" to the foundry. The phrase "tapeout" is dated – it originally referred to magnetic tape, but the term persists.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Cell Based ASICs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Standard Cell Based ASICs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Standard Cell Based ASIC** (also called a *Cell-Based IC* or *CBIC*) is a mask-programmed Application Specific Integrated Circuit whose entire functional logic is constructed by placing pre-designed, pre-characterized, and pre-laid-out logic primitives — known as **standard cells** — from a vendor-supplied (or in-house) **standard cell library** onto a die. The cells are arranged in **parallel rows** across the silicon surface, with **inter-cell routing channels** (or over-the-cell routing) used to interconnect them according to a synthesized netlist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Mapping):**
> A Standard Cell ASIC uses a *predefined set of logic cells* (AND, OR, NAND, NOR, Flip-Flops, Multiplexers, Buffers, etc.) from a **standard cell library**. The placement of cells and routing between them is *fully automated* by EDA tools. **All mask layers are customized** — distinguishing it fundamentally from a Gate-Array ASIC where only the interconnect masks are customized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lego Block House"

Imagine you are an architect asked to build a custom house:
- A **Full-Custom ASIC** is like hand-carving every single brick, every door, and every window from raw stone. Maximum performance, but extraordinarily slow and expensive.
- A **Gate-Array ASIC** is like buying a pre-built neighborhood of identical houses (the *master die*) and only customizing the wallpaper and paint (the interconnect masks). Cheap, but constrained.
- A **Standard Cell Based ASIC** is like building your dream house from a **catalog of pre-engineered Lego blocks** (standard cells — NAND, NOR, Flip-Flop, etc.). You choose the *function* of each block from a library, the tools automatically snap them together in **rows**, and a wiring machine (the *router*) connects them. The blocks are highly optimized, the construction is fast, and the final house is custom-built to your design.

## 1.3 The Standard Cell Library — The Heart of CBIC

A **standard cell library** is a collection of pre-designed, pre-laid-out, and pre-characterized logic gates and functional blocks. Each cell in the library is supplied in three equivalent forms:

| Form | Description | Purpose |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Schematic** | Transistor-level netlist (e.g., CMOS NAND) | Functional simulation |
| **Layout** | Fixed-design-rule polygons in GDSII | Physical implementation |
| **Behavioral/Timing Model** | Liberty (.lib) file with timing arcs | Static Timing Analysis (STA) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Library Characteristics (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **Same height** for every cell → enables seamless row placement.
> 2. **Variable width** (proportional to complexity) → minimizes area.
> 3. **Multiple drive strengths** (e.g., NAND2_X1, NAND2_X2, NAND2_X4) → allows the synthesis tool to optimize for load.
> 4. **Power and ground rails** aligned horizontally at top and bottom → common power spine.

## 1.4 Physical Architecture of a Standard Cell ASIC

The die is partitioned into three functional regions:
1. **I/O Pads / Pad Frame** — Located at the periphery for off-chip communication.
2. **Standard Cell Core** — The heart of the chip, composed of rows of standard cells.
3. **Macro Blocks** (optional) — RAM, ROM, PLLs, ADC/DAC, or analog IP integrated as hard macros.

The standard cell core itself has the following structure:
- **Cell Rows** — Fixed-height horizontal bands where cells are placed.
- **Routing Channels** — Vertical or horizontal channels between/over cell rows for signal interconnects.
- **Power Straps** — Wide metal lines carrying VDD and VSS across the entire core.

> [!TIP]
> In **modern deep-submicron** processes (≤ 90 nm), *over-the-cell routing* (OTC) has largely replaced channel routing, as multiple metal layers (typically 6–12) allow signal wires to be routed above the cell rows, leaving the silicon real estate exclusively for transistors.

## 1.5 Why "Standard Cell" Wins in Industry

| ASIC Type | All Mask Layers Custom? | NRE Cost | Performance | Design Effort |
|-----------|------------------------|----------|-------------|---------------|
| Full Custom | **Yes** | Highest | **Best** | Highest |
| Standard Cell | **Yes** | High | Excellent (Near Custom) | Medium |
| Gate Array | **No** (only interconnect) | Low | Moderate | Low |
| FPGA | **None** (programmed electrically) | Lowest | Worst (slowest) | Lowest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The Standard Cell Based ASIC occupies the **engineering sweet spot** — combining *near full-custom performance* with *automated, rapid design cycles*. It is the **dominant ASIC style** for the vast majority of modern digital chips (CPUs, SoCs, ASIC accelerators).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Standard Cell — Internal Construction

A canonical CMOS standard cell (e.g., a 2-input NAND gate) has the following layered anatomy:

1. **n-Well / p-Substrate** — Bulk silicon and well definition.
2. **Diffusion (Active) Layer** — Defines the n+ and p+ regions forming the pull-up (PMOS) and pull-down (NMOS) transistors.
3. **Poly-Silicon Gate Layer** — Forms transistor gates; minimum width = process $L_{min}$.
4. **Contact Layer (CO)** — Vias between poly/diffusion and Metal 1.
5. **Metal 1 (M1)** — Internal cell routing.
6. **Metal 2 and above** — Used for power rails and over-the-cell routing.
7. **Via Layers (V1, V2, ...)** — Inter-layer metal connections.

### 2.1.1 The Equipotent Power Rails (Critical Design Rule)

Every cell has its **VDD rail** at the *top* and **VSS rail** at the *bottom*, both running **horizontally** along the cell height. This design rule guarantees:

- Adjacent cells in a row can be **abutted** (placed side-by-side) with no routing interruption.
- Power and ground are distributed continuously across the entire row.
- The place-and-route tool can abut cells without violating power-rail continuity.

> [!NOTE]
> The fixed cell height is set by the **height of one standard cell row** (typically expressed in *track pitches* or *µm*). A common industry convention is **9-track or 12-track** cell height, which means the cell can accommodate 9 or 12 horizontal metal tracks (including M1 power rails) between VDD and VSS.

## 2.2 Cell Library Components — KTU Classification

A complete standard cell library typically contains **hundreds to thousands of cells**, organized into categories:

| Category | Examples | Function |
|----------|----------|----------|
| **Combinational Cells** | AND2, OR2, NAND2, NOR2, XOR2, MUX2, AOI21, OAI21 | Logic gates |
| **Sequential Cells** | DFF (D-Flip-Flop), DFFR (with reset), DFFE (with enable), LATCH | Memory elements |
| **Buffer/Inverter Cells** | BUF, INV (X1, X2, X4, X8, X16) | Drive strength scaling |
| **Arithmetic Cells** | Full Adder (FA), Half Adder (HA), Carry cells | Datapath building blocks |
| **Complex Cells** | AOI, OAI, Multiplexers (MUX2/MUX4), Decoder | Higher-level logic |
| **Special Cells** | Filler cell, Tap cell, Antenna cell, Decoupling cap, End-cap | Physical integrity |

### 2.2.1 Drive Strength Variants

For each logic function, multiple drive strengths are provided:

$$
\text{NAND2\_X1}, \quad \text{NAND2\_X2}, \quad \text{NAND2\_X4}
$$

The suffix **Xn** denotes the relative drive capability. **X2** means twice the drive current (and twice the input capacitance, twice the area) of **X1**. This allows the synthesis tool to make optimal trade-offs between **timing**, **power**, and **area**.

## 2.3 Design Flow — From RTL to GDSII

The Standard Cell ASIC design flow is the **backbone of all modern VLSI design**. The KTU 2024 syllabus places heavy emphasis on this flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7-Stage Standard Cell ASIC Design Flow:**
> 1. **RTL Design (HDL Coding)** — Verilog/VHDL description.
> 2. **Logic Synthesis** — RTL → Gate-level netlist using the cell library.
> 3. **Functional Verification** — Simulation of gate-level netlist.
> 4. **Floorplanning** — Define core area, I/O placement, macro positioning.
> 5. **Placement** — Place standard cells in rows.
> 6. **Routing (CTS + Signal Routing)** — Clock Tree Synthesis + Signal nets.
> 7. **Physical Verification (DRC + LVS)** — Design Rule Check + Layout vs. Schematic.
> 
> **Output:** GDSII file sent to foundry for fabrication.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations for Standard Cell ASICs

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU University Exams (Part A and Part B). Memorize the symbols, the forms, and the boundary conditions.

| Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions | Units / Boundary |
|---------------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Cell Area** | $A_{cell} = H_{row} \times W_{cell}$ | $H_{row}$ = fixed row height; $W_{cell}$ = variable cell width | $\mu m^2$ |
| **Total Core Area** | $A_{core} = N_{rows} \times H_{row} \times L_{row}$ | $N_{rows}$ = number of rows; $L_{row}$ = row length | $\mu m^2$ |
| **Cell Delay (Linear)** | $t_{pd} = t_{int} + R_{drv} \cdot C_{load}$ | $t_{int}$ = intrinsic delay (no load); $R_{drv}$ = driver resistance; $C_{load}$ = load cap | seconds |
| **RC Delay (Wire)** | $t_{wire} = 0.5 \cdot R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire}$ | $R_{wire}$ = wire resistance; $C_{wire}$ = wire capacitance | seconds |
| **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity; $C_L$ = load capacitance; $V_{DD}$ = supply; $f_{clk}$ = clock freq | Watts |
| **Static (Leakage) Power** | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | $I_{leak}$ = total leakage current | Watts |
| **Channel Density (Routing Capacity)** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{tracks}}{H_{channel}}$ | $N_{tracks}$ = horizontal tracks; $H_{channel}$ = channel height | tracks / $\mu m$ |
| **Wire Resistance (Sheet)** | $R_{wire} = R_{\square} \cdot \dfrac{L}{W}$ | $R_{\square}$ = sheet resistance (ohms/sq); $L$ = length; $W$ = width | $\Omega$ |
| **Wire Capacitance** | $C_{wire} = C_{area} \cdot W \cdot L + C_{fringe} \cdot 2(L+W)$ | $C_{area}$ = area cap; $C_{fringe}$ = fringe cap | Farads |
| **Areal Density (Cells/mm²)** | $D = \dfrac{N_{cells}}{A_{core}}$ | $N_{cells}$ = total placed cells | cells / $\mu m^2$ |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the KTU answer sheet, always substitute *numerical values with units* explicitly. The examiner awards partial marks for the *correct formula* even if arithmetic errors occur. **The symbol, not the form, is what is graded.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Standard Cell Based ASICs are the **de-facto industry standard** for:
- **Mobile SoCs** (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, Samsung Exynos).
- **AI Accelerators** (Google TPU, NVIDIA Tensor cores, custom NPU IP).
- **Networking ASICs** (Broadcom Tomahawk switches).
- **Automotive ECUs** (AUTOSAR-compliant engine controllers).
- **IoT Edge Processors** (ESP32, nRF52 — though FPGAs are also common here).

The dominance of standard cell design is because it **abstracts away** the transistor-level complexity, allowing millions of engineers worldwide to design chips using only Verilog/VHDL — without needing to manually draw layouts.

## 2.6 Key Design Metrics — The PPA Trinity

Every Standard Cell ASIC is evaluated against the three universal metrics:

1. **P — Power:** Total energy consumed (dynamic + leakage).
2. **P — Performance:** Maximum operating frequency $f_{max}$.
3. **A — Area:** Silicon real estate in $mm^2$.

These three are in **constant tension** — improving one often degrades the other two. The cell library provides the *knobs* (drive strengths, threshold voltage options) to optimize the trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exam-ready** and follow the exact step-by-step valuation style expected in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Each step is explicitly written out, with the algebraic transition explained in plain text. **No step is skipped** — this is a KTU 2024 Scheme requirement.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Propagation Delay of a Standard Cell Driving a Load

### Problem Setup

A NAND2_X1 standard cell from a 65 nm library has the following Liberty (.lib) parameters:
- Intrinsic delay: $t_{int} = 30 \text{ ps}$
- Driver resistance: $R_{drv} = 1.2 \text{ k}\Omega$
- Output load: $C_{L} = 50 \text{ fF}$

Find the cell propagation delay.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the canonical CMOS gate delay model.**

The total propagation delay of a CMOS gate driving a load capacitance $C_L$ is the sum of (a) the *intrinsic* delay of the cell itself (with no external load) and (b) the *load-dependent* delay caused by charging/discharging $C_L$ through the driver's on-resistance.

$$
t_{pd} = t_{int} + \Delta t_{load}
$$

**Step 2: Express the load-dependent component.**

When the output node is charged or discharged through the equivalent on-resistance $R_{drv}$ of the CMOS pull-up or pull-down network, the time to traverse 50% of the supply swing is given by the standard RC time-constant model:

$$
\Delta t_{load} = R_{drv} \cdot C_{L}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the full delay expression.**

$$
t_{pd} = t_{int} + R_{drv} \cdot C_{L}
$$

**Step 4: Insert numerical values.**

$$
t_{pd} = 30 \times 10^{-12} + (1.2 \times 10^{3}) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-15})
$$

**Step 5: Compute the second term in seconds.**

$$
R_{drv} \cdot C_{L} = (1.2 \times 10^{3}) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-15}) = 60 \times 10^{-12} \text{ seconds} = 60 \text{ ps}
$$

**Step 6: Sum the two components.**

$$
t_{pd} = 30 \text{ ps} + 60 \text{ ps} = 90 \text{ ps}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{t_{pd} = 90 \text{ ps}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Dynamic Power Consumption of a Standard Cell Core

### Problem Setup

A Standard Cell ASIC contains 500,000 logic gates. Each gate has an average load capacitance of $C_L = 5 \text{ fF}$ and switches with an average activity factor of $\alpha = 0.15$. The chip operates at $V_{DD} = 1.0 \text{ V}$ and $f_{clk} = 500 \text{ MHz}$. Compute the total dynamic power.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the dynamic power equation.**

For a CMOS gate, energy is drawn from the supply rail every time the output node transitions from 0 to 1 (charging $C_L$ to $V_{DD}$). The energy per transition is $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$, and the full $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is drawn from $V_{DD}$ (the other half is dissipated in the PMOS pull-up). The average power is therefore:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the total switched capacitance of the core.**

The total effective switched capacitance is the number of gates times the per-gate load:

$$
C_{total} = N_{gates} \cdot C_{L} = 5 \times 10^{5} \times 5 \times 10^{-15} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} = 2.5 \text{ nF}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the dynamic power equation.**

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
P_{dyn} = (0.15) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (1.0)^{2} \cdot (500 \times 10^{6})
$$

**Step 5: Simplify intermediate products.**

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.15 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 5 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 6: Combine powers of 10.**

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.15 \times 2.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-9+8} = 0.15 \times 2.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-1}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the scalar product.**

$$
P_{dyn} = (0.15 \times 12.5) \times 10^{-1} = 1.875 \times 10^{-1} = 0.1875 \text{ W}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{P_{dyn} = 187.5 \text{ mW}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Channel Routing Capacity vs. Net Demand (The "Track Allocation" Problem)

### Problem Setup

A routing channel between two standard cell rows has:
- Channel height: $H_{channel} = 5 \mu m$
- Design rule: minimum metal pitch = $1 \mu m$
- Net demand = 7 horizontal tracks (3 metal-2 nets, 4 power/ground)

Determine the **routing feasibility**.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the channel capacity.**

The number of available horizontal routing tracks is:

$$
\rho = \frac{H_{channel}}{P_{metal}} = \frac{5 \mu m}{1 \mu m} = 5 \text{ tracks}
$$

**Step 2: Compare capacity to demand.**

$$
\text{Demand} = 7 \text{ tracks}, \quad \text{Capacity} = 5 \text{ tracks}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate feasibility.**

$$
\text{Demand} > \text{Capacity} \implies \text{Routing Infeasible}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the routing deficit.**

$$
\Delta \rho = 7 - 5 = 2 \text{ tracks deficit}
$$

**Step 5: Engineer a solution.**

The designer can either:
- (a) Increase the channel height to $H_{channel} = 7 \mu m$ (paying an area penalty), or
- (b) Use *over-the-cell* routing on a higher metal layer (Metal 3) for 2 of the nets.

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\rho = 5 \text{ tracks} < 7 \text{ tracks demand} \Rightarrow \text{Routing infeasible without OTC or taller channel.}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Standard Cell vs. Other ASIC Styles

| Parameter | Full Custom | Standard Cell | Gate Array | FPGA |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------|------|
| **NRE Cost** | Very High | High | Low | Very Low |
| **Per-Unit Cost** | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| **Performance (Speed)** | Best | Excellent | Good | Worst |
| **Power Efficiency** | Best | Excellent | Good | Worst |
| **Time-to-Market** | Slowest | Medium | Fast | Fastest |
| **Density (Gates/mm²)** | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |
| **Custom Mask Layers** | **All (full set)** | **All (full set)** | Interconnect only | **None** (electrically programmed) |
| **Re-programmability** | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Typical Use** | Analog/RF, high-perf cores | SoCs, ASICs, CPUs | Mid-volume legacy | Prototyping, low-volume |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Standard Cell Row Capacity (Areal Density)

### Problem Setup

A 65 nm standard cell row has a row height of $H_{row} = 2.5 \mu m$ and a row length of $L_{row} = 1 \text{ mm}$. The placement tool packs 800 standard cells (average width $W_{avg} = 1.25 \mu m$) into the row. Compute the **utilization efficiency** and **areal density**.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the total cell width occupied.**

$$
W_{total} = N_{cells} \cdot W_{avg} = 800 \times 1.25 \mu m = 1000 \mu m
$$

**Step 2: Compute the row's total length.**

$$
L_{row} = 1 \text{ mm} = 1000 \mu m
$$

**Step 3: Compute the utilization ratio.**

$$
\eta = \frac{W_{total}}{L_{row}} = \frac{1000}{1000} = 1.0 \quad (\text{100\%})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, utilization is **never 100%** because routing tracks, power straps, and placement congestion create *white space*. Industry-standard target utilization is 70–85%.

**Step 4: Compute the row area.**

$$
A_{row} = H_{row} \times L_{row} = 2.5 \mu m \times 1000 \mu m = 2500 \mu m^2
$$

**Step 5: Compute the areal cell density.**

$$
D = \frac{N_{cells}}{A_{row}} = \frac{800}{2500 \mu m^2} = 0.32 \text{ cells}/\mu m^2 = 3.2 \times 10^{5} \text{ cells/mm}^2
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{D = 3.2 \times 10^{5} \text{ cells/mm}^{2}}
$$

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Wire Delay (Elmore Delay Model)

### Problem Setup

A long interconnect of length $L = 2 \text{ mm}$ has distributed resistance $R = 0.1 \text{ }\Omega / \mu m$ and capacitance $C = 0.2 \text{ fF}/\mu m$. Compute the Elmore wire delay.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Express total wire resistance and capacitance.**

$$
R_{wire} = r \cdot L = 0.1 \times 2000 = 200 \text{ }\Omega
$$

$$
C_{wire} = c \cdot L = 0.2 \times 10^{-15} \times 2000 = 0.4 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 0.4 \text{ pF}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Elmore delay formula for a uniform RC line.**

For a uniform RC ladder driven at one end, the 50% delay (Elmore delay) is:

$$
t_{wire} = 0.5 \cdot R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
t_{wire} = 0.5 \times 200 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-12} = 40 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ps}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{t_{wire} = 40 \text{ ps}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords, and clean uppercase text labels (no inline markdown formatting inside node labels).

## 4.1 Standard Cell ASIC — Physical Architecture (Row-Based Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[CHIP DIE] --> B1[PAD FRAME PERIPHERY]
    A1 --> B2[CORE AREA]
    A1 --> B3[MACRO BLOCKS]

    B2 --> C1[ROW 1 STANDARD CELLS]
    B2 --> C2[ROW 2 STANDARD CELLS]
    B2 --> C3[ROW 3 STANDARD CELLS]
    B2 --> C4[ROW 4 STANDARD CELLS]

    C1 --> D1[CH1 NAND2 X1]
    C1 --> D2[CH2 DFFR X2]
    C1 --> D3[CH3 INV X4]

    C1 -.->|CHANNEL 1| E1[VERTICAL ROUTING CHANNEL 1]
    C2 -.->|CHANNEL 1| E1
    C2 -.->|CHANNEL 2| E2[VERTICAL ROUTING CHANNEL 2]
    C3 -.->|CHANNEL 2| E2

    C1 --> F1[HORIZONTAL M1 POWER RAIL VDD]
    C1 --> F2[HORIZONTAL M1 POWER RAIL VSS]

    B3 --> G1[SRAM MACRO]
    B3 --> G2[PLL MACRO]
    B3 --> G3[DAC MACRO]

    B1 --> H1[INPUT PAD]
    B1 --> H2[OUTPUT PAD]
    B1 --> H3[VDD PAD]
    B1 --> H4[VSS PAD]
```

**Architectural Reading Guide:**

- The **Pad Frame** surrounds the die periphery for off-chip I/O.
- The **Core** contains 4 standard cell rows (Row 1 to Row 4).
- **Cells** within a row are *abutted* (placed edge-to-edge) with continuous VDD/VSS rails.
- **Vertical routing channels** sit between rows to carry signals vertically.
- **Horizontal power rails** run continuously across all rows in Metal 1.
- **Macro blocks** (SRAM, PLL, DAC) are *hard macros* dropped into the core alongside the standard cell rows.

## 4.2 Standard Cell Library — Internal Cell Structure (Inside a Single NAND2 Cell)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[VDD RAIL TOP METAL 1] --> P2[PMOS TRANSISTOR P1]
    P2 --> P3[PMOS TRANSISTOR P2]
    P3 --> N3[INTERNAL NODE OUTPUT Y]

    N3 --> N1[NMOS TRANSISTOR N1]
    N1 --> N2[NMOS TRANSISTOR N2]
    N2 --> G1[VSS RAIL BOTTOM METAL 1]

    I1[INPUT A POLY GATE] --> P2
    I1 --> N1
    I2[INPUT B POLY GATE] --> P3
    I2 --> N2

    H1[CELL HEIGHT FIXED H ROW] --> P1
    H1 --> G1

    W1[CELL WIDTH VARIABLE W CELL] --> P1
    W1 --> G1
```

**Internal Anatomy Reading Guide:**

- **VDD rail (top)** and **VSS rail (bottom)** are aligned at fixed $y$-coordinates.
- The two PMOS transistors (P1, P2) are stacked in **series** (pull-up network for NAND).
- The two NMOS transistors (N1, N2) are stacked in **series** (pull-down network for NAND).
- The **cell height** is *fixed* across the library (enables row abutment).
- The **cell width** is *variable* (scales with logic complexity).

## 4.3 Standard Cell ASIC — Complete Design Flow (RTL to GDSII)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STEP 1 RTL DESIGN VERILOG VHDL] --> S2[STEP 2 LOGIC SYNTHESIS DC GENUS]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3 FUNCTIONAL VERIFICATION]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4 FLOORPLANNING]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5 PLACEMENT INNOVUS ICC2]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6 CTS CLOCK TREE SYNTHESIS]
    S6 --> S7[STEP 7 SIGNAL ROUTING]
    S7 --> S8[STEP 8 PHYSICAL VERIFICATION DRC LVS]
    S8 --> S9[STEP 9 GDSII TAPEOUT]
    S9 --> S10[STEP 10 FOUNDRY FABRICATION]

    S1 -.->|FEEDBACK LOOP| S2
    S5 -.->|TIMING DRIVEN| S6
    S7 -.->|OPTIMIZATION ITERATIONS| S5
    S8 -.->|VIOLATIONS| S5
```

**Flow Reading Guide:**

- Each stage is a **hand-off** between EDA tools (Synopsys, Cadence, Mentor/Siemens).
- **Iteration loops** (dashed arrows) are *normal* — modern designs require 3–5 ECO loops before sign-off.
- **CTS** is *between* placement and routing because the clock net is the most timing-critical net.

## 4.4 Cell Library Classification — Tree Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LIB[STANDARD CELL LIBRARY] --> A[COMBINATIONAL]
    LIB --> B[SEQUENTIAL]
    LIB --> C[BUFFER INVERTER]
    LIB --> D[ARITHMETIC]
    LIB --> E[PHYSICAL SPECIAL]

    A --> A1[AND OR NAND NOR XOR]
    A --> A2[AOI OAI MUX DECODER]

    B --> B1[DFF DFFR DFFE]
    B --> B2[LATCH DLAT]

    C --> C1[INV X1 X2 X4 X8]
    C --> C2[BUF X1 X2 X4]

    D --> D1[FULL ADDER FA]
    D --> D2[HALF ADDER HA]

    E --> E1[FILLER CELL]
    E --> E2[TAP CELL]
    E --> E3[END CAP CELL]
    E --> E4[ANTENNA DIODE]
    E --> E5[DECOUPLING CAP]
```

**Library Reading Guide:**

- **Combinational cells** implement boolean logic.
- **Sequential cells** store state (DFF is the most commonly used).
- **Buffer/Inverter** variants provide drive strength scaling.
- **Arithmetic cells** are optimized for datapath performance.
- **Special cells** are placed automatically by the EDA tool to ensure physical integrity (latch-up prevention, antenna avoidance, power decoupling).

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Standard Cell ASIC Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SYS[STANDARD CELL ASIC SYSTEM] --> IO[IO SUBSYSTEM]
    SYS --> CORE[CORE LOGIC SUBSYSTEM]
    SYS --> CLK[CLOCK SUBSYSTEM]
    SYS --> PWR[POWER SUBSYSTEM]
    SYS --> MEM[MEMORY SUBSYSTEM]
    SYS --> ANA[ANALOG MIXED SIGNAL]

    IO --> IO1[INPUT BUFFERS]
    IO --> IO2[OUTPUT DRIVERS]
    IO --> IO3[ESD PROTECTION]

    CORE --> CR1[STANDARD CELL ROWS]
    CORE --> CR2[PLACED AND ROUTED NETS]
    CORE --> CR3[LOGIC BLOCKS]

    CLK --> CK1[CLOCK TREE BUFFERS]
    CLK --> CK2[PLL]
    CLK --> CK3[CLOCK DISTRIBUTION NETWORK]

    PWR --> PW1[POWER GRID METAL STACK]
    PWR --> PW2[DECOUPLING CAPACITORS]
    PWR --> PW3[POWER GATING SWITCHES]

    MEM --> MM1[SRAM MACRO]
    MEM --> MM2[ROM MACRO]
    MEM --> MM3[REGISTER FILE]

    ANA --> AN1[ADC]
    ANA --> AN2[DAC]
    ANA --> AN3[VOLTAGE REGULATOR]
```

**System Reading Guide:**

- The **I/O Subsystem** includes ESD protection diodes (mandatory for any commercial chip).
- The **Core** is the standard cell logic, fully synthesized and placed-and-routed.
- The **Clock** subsystem is the most power-hungry net in synchronous designs.
- The **Power** subsystem has a multi-layer grid (M5, M7, M9) carrying VDD/VSS to every cell.
- **Memory** macros are *hard macros* — pre-laid-out by the memory compiler.
- **Analog** blocks are *almost always* full-custom even in a standard cell ASIC.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B:** 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice) = 14 marks
> - **Total per topic:** 20 marks
> - All questions are mapped to **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels** (L1–L6).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — *Bloom Level L2 (Understand)*

**Define a Standard Cell Based ASIC. List any FOUR advantages it offers over a Gate Array ASIC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Standard Cell Based ASIC** is a mask-programmed Application Specific Integrated Circuit in which the entire logic is built by placing and routing pre-designed, pre-characterized logic cells (called *standard cells*) from a cell library onto a silicon die in regular rows. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Four advantages over Gate Array ASICs:**
> 1. **Higher density** — cells are tightly packed with optimized layouts, not constrained to a fixed master die. **(1 mark)**
> 2. **Better performance** — full-custom layout allows cell-level optimization of speed and power. **(0.5 mark)**
> 3. **No fixed master-die overhead** — area is consumed only by active logic, not by unutilized gate-array sites. **(0.5 mark)**
> 4. **More flexible cell library** — includes complex cells (AOI, OAI, MUX) optimized for the target process. **(Bonus correctness)**

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — *Bloom Level L2 (Understand)*

**Explain the role of a standard cell library in ASIC design. What are the three forms in which each cell is supplied?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **standard cell library** is the cornerstone of a Standard Cell Based ASIC. It provides a curated, pre-verified, and pre-characterized collection of logic cells that the synthesis, placement, and routing tools use to construct the design. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Three forms in which each cell is supplied:**
> 1. **Schematic (Transistor Netlist)** — The CMOS circuit description (e.g., 2 PMOS + 2 NMOS for NAND2). Used for functional simulation. **(1 mark)**
> 2. **Physical Layout (GDSII)** — The geometric polygons following all design rules. Used by the place-and-route tool. **(1 mark)**
> 3. **Timing/Power Model (Liberty .lib)** — Abstract timing arcs, power tables, and noise models. Used for static timing analysis (STA). **(Bonus correctness)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question carries an **internal choice**. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as the two alternatives. Examinees answer ONE of the two.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks *(Bloom Level L4: Analyze)*

**With a neat block diagram, describe the physical architecture of a Standard Cell Based ASIC. Explain the function of (i) cell rows, (ii) routing channels, and (iii) power rails.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A Standard Cell Based ASIC has a structured physical architecture consisting of a pad frame at the periphery, a core region filled with rows of standard cells, and (optionally) macro blocks for memories and analog functions. All mask layers are customized during fabrication.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  PAD FRAME (I/O Pads, ESD, Power Pads)          |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  ROW 1: [NAND][DFF][MUX][INV]...                 |
|  ==== VDD RAIL ==== (M1, horizontal)             |
|  ==== VSS RAIL ==== (M1, horizontal)             |
|  ------ CHANNEL ------ (M2/M3 signal routing)   |
|  ROW 2: [AND][OR][XOR][BUF]...                   |
|  ==== VDD RAIL ====                              |
|  ==== VSS RAIL ====                              |
|  ------ CHANNEL ------                           |
|  ROW 3: [FA][HA][REG][AOI]...                    |
|  +------ MACRO BLOCK (SRAM) ------+              |
|  ROW 4: [NAND][NAND][DFFR][TAP]...               |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

**Function of Cell Rows (1.5 marks):**
- Cell rows are **fixed-height horizontal bands** of the die.
- Standard cells are **abutted** (placed side-by-side) within each row.
- Adjacent rows are separated by **routing channels** for vertical signal interconnects.
- All cells in a row share **common VDD and VSS rails** at the top and bottom.

**Function of Routing Channels (1.5 marks):**
- Routing channels are the **inter-row spaces** dedicated to signal wires.
- They carry **vertical wires** (Metal 2/3) connecting cells from different rows.
- Channel **height** is determined by the number of nets to be routed.
- Modern designs use **Over-The-Cell (OTC) routing** on upper metals to eliminate channel constraints.

**Function of Power Rails (1 mark):**
- Power rails are **wide horizontal metal lines** in Metal 1.
- They run **continuously** across the entire row at the top (VDD) and bottom (VSS).
- They ensure **uniform power distribution** to every cell and act as a **low-impedance power spine**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks *(Bloom Level L5: Evaluate)*

**A Standard Cell ASIC has 250,000 logic gates. Each gate has an average load capacitance of $C_L = 4 \text{ fF}$ and switches with an activity factor of $\alpha = 0.2$. The chip operates at $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$ and $f_{clk} = 1 \text{ GHz}$. Calculate the total dynamic power consumption of the core.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Recall the dynamic power equation for CMOS.** **(1 mark)**

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the total effective switched capacitance.** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
C_{total} = N_{gates} \times C_{L} = 2.5 \times 10^{5} \times 4 \times 10^{-15} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} = 1 \text{ nF}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values into the equation.** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
P_{dyn} = (0.2) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-9}) \times (1.2)^{2} \times (1 \times 10^{9})
$$

**Step 4: Compute the products step-by-step.** **(2 marks)**

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.44 \times 1 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 1.44 = 0.288 \text{ W} = 288 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 5: State the final answer with units.** **(1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{P_{dyn} = 288 \text{ mW}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks *(Bloom Level L4: Analyze)*

**Explain the complete design flow of a Standard Cell Based ASIC from RTL to GDSII. List the EDA tools used at each stage.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Standard Cell ASIC design flow is a structured, multi-stage process that takes a behavioural RTL description in HDL and converts it into a fabrication-ready GDSII layout. The flow is highly automated but requires verification at every stage.

**The 7-Stage Design Flow (5 marks, 0.7 mark per stage):**

1. **RTL Design (HDL Coding):** Behavioural description in Verilog/VHDL. EDA Tool: *Any HDL editor + Simulator (ModelSim, VCS, QuestaSim)*.

2. **Logic Synthesis:** Converts RTL to a gate-level netlist mapped to the standard cell library. EDA Tool: *Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus*.

3. **Functional Verification:** Gate-level simulation to confirm logical equivalence with RTL. EDA Tool: *VCS, QuestaSim*.

4. **Floorplanning:** Defines the core area, I/O pad placement, macro positions, and power grid strategy. EDA Tool: *Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2*.

5. **Placement:** Standard cells are placed in rows based on the synthesized netlist, optimizing for timing, congestion, and area. EDA Tool: *Innovus, ICC2*.

6. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS) + Signal Routing:** The clock net is synthesized first (as it is the most timing-critical), followed by signal routing on multiple metal layers. EDA Tool: *Innovus, ICC2*.

7. **Physical Verification (DRC + LVS + Antenna):** Design Rule Check ensures layout complies with foundry rules; LVS confirms the layout matches the schematic. EDA Tool: *Mentor Calibre, Cadence PVS*.

**Final Output (1 mark):** A clean GDSII file is generated, which is sent to the foundry (e.g., TSMC, Samsung, Intel) for mask generation and fabrication. The foundry produces the photomasks for **all layers** (unlike gate arrays where only interconnect layers are customized).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks *(Bloom Level L3 + L5: Apply + Evaluate)*

**A 2-input NAND2_X1 standard cell has the following Liberty (.lib) parameters: intrinsic delay = 25 ps, driver resistance = 1.5 k$\Omega$. It drives a load of 4 such NAND cells (fanout = 4). Each load NAND has an input pin capacitance of 12 fF. Additionally, the wire connecting the driver to the loads has a capacitance of 20 fF. Calculate the cell propagation delay.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the delay model.** **(1 mark)**

$$
t_{pd} = t_{int} + R_{drv} \cdot C_{L,total}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the load capacitance.** **(2 marks)**

The total load capacitance has two components:
- **Pin capacitance of the 4 load NANDs:**
$$
C_{pin} = 4 \times 12 \text{ fF} = 48 \text{ fF}
$$

- **Wire capacitance:**
$$
C_{wire} = 20 \text{ fF}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the capacitances.** **(1 mark)**

$$
C_{L,total} = C_{pin} + C_{wire} = 48 \text{ fF} + 20 \text{ fF} = 68 \text{ fF} = 68 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the load-dependent delay term.** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
R_{drv} \cdot C_{L,total} = (1.5 \times 10^{3}) \times (68 \times 10^{-15}) = 102 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s} = 102 \text{ ps}
$$

**Step 5: Add the intrinsic delay.** **(1 mark)**

$$
t_{pd} = 25 \text{ ps} + 102 \text{ ps} = 127 \text{ ps}
$$

**Final Answer with units (0.5 mark):**

$$
\boxed{t_{pd} = 127 \text{ ps}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Standard Cell ASIC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Gate Array with Standard Cell (–2 marks typical).** Students often write that in a Standard Cell ASIC "only interconnect layers are customized." **This is FALSE.** In a Standard Cell ASIC, *all mask layers* are customized. Only in a **Gate Array** are the interconnect layers customized.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in dynamic power (–1 mark).** Always write the final answer in **Watts (or mW, µW)** with the unit symbol. An answer "0.288" without "W" or "mW" loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up intrinsic and total delay (–2 marks).** The intrinsic delay $t_{int}$ is the *unloaded* delay; the total delay includes the $R_{drv} \cdot C_L$ term. Examiners expect the formula $t_{pd} = t_{int} + R_{drv} \cdot C_L$ — not just $t_{pd} = R \cdot C$.
>
> 4. **Wrong channel-routing sign convention (–1 mark).** If demand > capacity, the channel is *infeasible*, and the answer should explicitly say so. Do not silently skip the comparison.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the activity factor $\alpha$ in power calculations (–2 marks).** Students frequently write $P = C V^2 f$ instead of $P = \alpha C V^2 f$. The activity factor is **mandatory** in the dynamic power equation.
>
> 6. **Confusing drive-strength variants with multiple cells (–1 mark).** NAND2_X1, NAND2_X2, NAND2_X4 are *the same logical function* with different drive strengths — they are **not** different cells. Examiners may deduct marks for this conceptual error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Standard Cell Based ASICs (KTU 2024 Module 1):**

- **Definition:** A Standard Cell ASIC is a mask-programmed IC built from a pre-designed library of logic cells placed in regular rows. **All mask layers are customized.** **(HIGH PRIORITY)**

- **Cell Library Contains:** Combinational, sequential, buffer/inverter (with drive strength variants), arithmetic, and special (filler, tap, end-cap) cells. **(HIGH PRIORITY)**

- **Three Library Forms:** Schematic, Layout (GDSII), Liberty (.lib) timing model. **(HIGH PRIORITY)**

- **Physical Architecture:** Pad frame (periphery) + Core (rows of cells) + Macros (memories, analog). **(HIGH PRIORITY)**

- **Cell Row Rules:** Fixed height, variable width, VDD on top, VSS on bottom (Metal 1). **(HIGH PRIORITY)**

- **Design Flow:** RTL → Synthesis → Verification → Floorplan → Placement → CTS → Routing → Physical Verification → GDSII. **(HIGH PRIORITY — frequently asked as 7- or 14-mark question)**

- **Key Equations (must memorize):**
  - $t_{pd} = t_{int} + R_{drv} \cdot C_{L}$
  - $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$
  - $A_{cell} = H_{row} \times W_{cell}$
  - $R_{wire} = R_{\square} \cdot (L/W)$
  - $\rho = N_{tracks} / H_{channel}$

- **Advantages over Gate Array:** Higher density, better performance, no master-die waste, richer library.

- **Advantages over Full Custom:** Faster design cycle, lower NRE, automated layout.

- **Disadvantages:** Higher NRE than gate array/FPGA, longer fabrication time, no post-fabrication re-programmability.

- **Industry Use Cases:** Mobile SoCs, AI accelerators, networking chips, automotive ECUs. **(Bonus context for viva voce)**

- **Common Exam Trap:** Never confuse "Standard Cell ASIC" (all masks custom) with "Gate Array ASIC" (only interconnect masks custom). This single distinction is worth **2–3 marks** in most KTU Part A questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gate Array Based ASIC** (also called a **Mask-Programmable Gate Array** or **MPGA**) is a type of **semi-custom ASIC** in which the **diffusion layers (transistors) are prefabricated** on a silicon wafer in a regular, repetitive pattern of **predefined logic cells (gates/NAND/NOR primitives)**, while only the **interconnect (metal) layers and contact/via masks** are customized by the designer to implement a specific logic function. The base array consists of rows of **uncommitted transistors** (typically pairs of PMOS/NMOS) arranged over a silicon substrate, and personalization is achieved by routing metal interconnects through a final few mask layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A Gate Array is an integrated circuit whose transistors are predefined on the wafer in a fixed, regular pattern, leaving only the metallization layers to be customized per application. It represents a trade-off between design flexibility and manufacturing turnaround time."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Hierarchy of ASICs (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
> 1. **Full-Custom ASIC** — every transistor, layout, and interconnect is hand-designed.
> 2. **Semi-Custom ASIC** — partially prefabricated; designer customizes only specific layers:
>    - **Gate Array Based ASIC** (subject of this topic)
>    - **Standard Cell Based ASIC** (cell-based design)
> 3. **Programmable ASIC** — fully programmable by the user (e.g., **FPGA**, **PLD**, **CPLD**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a massive **concrete apartment building skeleton** (the *base array*) that a developer has already constructed — all the load-bearing walls, pillars, and floor slabs (the *transistors*) are poured in a uniform grid, identical across the city. The developer has not yet decided which apartments will be 2BHK, 3BHK, or a duplex.

- The **electrical wiring, plumbing, and interior walls** (analogous to the **metal interconnect layers**) are still flexible.
- The buyer (chip designer) can now **partition rooms, add doors, and wire the electricity** to suit their personal lifestyle (specific Boolean logic).
- Because the skeleton is shared across all apartments, construction is **fast and cheap**, but the buyer has **limited freedom** — they cannot change the position of the pillars (transistors).

Similarly, in a **Gate Array**:
- The silicon fab pre-manufactures thousands of identical **transistor sites** on a wafer.
- The designer only "wires" the **metal layers** to create **NAND, NOR, XOR, Flip-Flops, etc.**
- This drastically reduces **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost** and **time-to-market**, but sacrifices some **silicon area efficiency** and **performance** compared to full-custom.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Nomenclature

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **NRE Cost** | Non-Recurring Engineering cost (mask + design) | **Low** (only 2–4 masks needed) |
| **Turnaround Time** | Time from design entry to fabricated chip | **Days to weeks** (vs. months for full-custom) |
| **Gate Density** | Number of usable 2-input NAND gates | **10K – 1M gates** |
| **Die Utilization** | Fraction of usable transistors | **70% – 90%** |
| **Prefabricated Layers** | Fixed silicon layers | **Diffusion, Poly, N-well, P-well** |
| **Customizable Layers** | Designer-customized layers | **Metal 1, Metal 2, Via, Contact** |
| **Unit Cell / Basic Cell** | Smallest prefabricated logic block | **2 PMOS + 2 NMOS** (typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Abbreviation Note:** The term "GA" in industry stands for **Gate Array**. It is sometimes also called an **"MPGA" (Mask-Programmable Gate Array)** to distinguish it from **FPGA** (Field-Programmable Gate Array). KTU examiners often interchange these acronyms in questions — be fluent with all three.

## 1.4 Why Gate Arrays Exist — The "Middle-Ground" Principle

The three dominant ASIC design styles occupy different points on the **Performance–Cost–Flexibility** triangle:

| Style | Performance | Unit Cost | NRE Cost | Flexibility | Turnaround |
|-------|-------------|-----------|----------|-------------|------------|
| **Full-Custom** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ (lowest) | ★★★★★ (highest) | ★★★★★ | Months–Years |
| **Gate Array** | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | **Weeks** |
| **Standard Cell** | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | Weeks–Months |
| **FPGA / PLD** | ★★ | ★ (high) | ★ (lowest) | ★★★★★ | **Hours–Days** |

**Gate Arrays occupy the sweet spot** where volume production (10K–1M units) justifies partial custom design but full-custom engineering is uneconomical.
