total functions $= 2^{2^n}$. - [x] **Block RAM (BRAM)** = dedicated dual-port memory, 18 Kbit or 36 Kbit per block. - [x] **DSP slices** = hard multipliers/MAC; e.g., DSP48E1 = $25 \times 18$ MAC + 48-bit accumulator. - [x] **Three programming technologies:** SRAM (volatile, unlimited reprogram), Antifuse (OTP, rad-hard), Flash (non-volatile, limited reprogram). - [x] **Configuration modes:** Master/Slave Serial/SPI/Parallel, JTAG. - [x] **Bitstream** = binary file loaded into on-chip configuration SRAM defining the user's logic. - [x] **Critical path delay** $T_{cp} = N_{LUT} \cdot T_{LUT} + N_{FF} \cdot T_{setup} + T_{wire}$. - [x] **Max frequency** $f_{max} = 1 / T_{cp}$. - [x] **Pipeline latency** $\tau = k \cdot T_{clk}$; **throughput** $\Theta = f_{clk}$ once pipeline is full. - [x] **FPGA vs. ASIC:** FPGA = lower NRE, faster TTM, reprogrammable, but slower and higher power per function. - [x] **FPGA vs. CPLD:** FPGA = fine-grained LUT array + rich routing; CPLD = coarse-grained AND-OR macro cells + central switch matrix. - [x] **Hard-IP cores** give FPGA efficiency on par with ASIC for memory, arithmetic, and high-speed serial I/O. - [x] Modern FPGAs include **ARM Cortex hard processor cores** (Zynq, Zynq UltraScale+ MPSoC) → SoC FPGAs. - [x] **Real-world families:** Xilinx Spartan, Artix, Kintex, Virtex, Zynq; Intel Cyclone, Arria, Stratix, Agilex; Lattice ECP5, Certus-NX; Microsemi IGLOO2, RTG4. - [x] **Process nodes:** 65 nm, 45 nm, 40 nm, 28 nm, 20 nm, 16 nm, 14 nm, 7 nm. - [x] **Configuration bitstream size** ≈ 1× to 500× Mbit depending on device density. - [x] **Dynamic power** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$; **Static power** dominated by leakage in advanced nodes. - [x] Applications: 5G baseband, AI inference, ADAS, aerospace rad-hard, medical imaging, ASIC prototyping, HFT.

topics.title: ASIC and FPGA Design Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ASIC and FPGA Design Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an ASIC?

**ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)** is a microchip designed and customized for a *specific application* rather than for general-purpose use. In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, an ASIC is an integrated circuit whose functionality is fixed at the time of fabrication and cannot be altered by the end user. The design flow encompasses all the steps required to convert a high-level system specification into a mask-ready GDSII layout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBECT604 - Module 1):**
> *An ASIC is an integrated circuit designed for a particular application, as opposed to a general-purpose device like a microprocessor. ASICs use a variety of design methodologies, including full-custom, semi-custom (cell-based, gate-array), and programmable logic (FPGA/PLD) approaches.*

### 1.2 What is an FPGA?

**FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array)** is a semiconductor device containing programmable logic blocks, programmable interconnects, and programmable I/O pads, designed to be configured by the customer or designer after manufacturing. The "Field-Programmable" part means the chip can be programmed in the field (i.e., at the user's location) any number of times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** ASICs are *hardware-frozen at fabrication* (mask-programmed), whereas FPGAs are *hardware-reconfigurable after deployment* (SRAM/flash/anti-fuse programmed).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Custom House vs. The LEGO House"

Imagine you are building a house:

- **ASIC Design Flow** → It is like **constructing a custom house from a blueprint**. You decide every brick, every wire, every room dimension. It is *optimized for cost, area, and power* once built, but if you want to change anything, you must *demolish and rebuild*. The design effort is huge and the per-unit cost is low (after high NRE — Non-Recurring Engineering).

- **FPGA Design Flow** → It is like **building a house from pre-fabricated LEGO blocks**. You snap together pre-made, standardized pieces to assemble a working structure. It is *fast to build and easy to modify*, but the resulting house is *slightly inefficient* in terms of area, power, and speed. The per-unit cost is higher but the NRE is almost zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:**
> - **NRE Cost (Non-Recurring Engineering Cost):** ASIC = **Very High** (millions of $ for masks); FPGA = **Zero** (no masks).
> - **Per-Unit Cost:** ASIC = **Low** (scales with volume); FPGA = **High** (fixed silicon cost).
> - **Time-to-Market:** ASIC = **6–18 months**; FPGA = **Hours to days**.
> - **Typical CMOS Process Node:** **180 nm** to **3 nm** (modern FPGAs use **20 nm / 16 nm / 7 nm**).

### 1.4 Classification of ASICs (KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Mapping)

According to the KTU PBECT604 Module 1 syllabus, ASICs are classified into three broad families:

| ASIC Type | Customization Level | Mask Layers | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Custom ASIC** | Every transistor hand-crafted | All layers unique | High-performance CPUs, analog/RF |
| **Semi-Custom ASIC** | Pre-designed standard cells/gates | Only interconnect layers custom | Most commercial SoCs |
| **Programmable ASIC (FPGA/PLD)** | Pre-fabricated, user-configured | No masks needed | Prototyping, low-volume products |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ASIC Design Flow — Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

The ASIC design flow is a *front-to-back* engineering process. Each stage gates the next; errors propagate and amplify if not caught early. KTU examiners frequently test the **sequence** and **purpose** of each step.

#### Stage 1: System-Level Specification
- Defines chip's functionality, performance, area, power, cost, and I/O.
- Output: A natural-language + numerical specification document.

#### Stage 2: Algorithmic / Behavioral Design (RTL-Free)
- System modeled in **C/C++/SystemC/MATLAB**.
- Floating-point algorithms optimized for fixed-point hardware.
- Output: C-model simulating golden reference behavior.

#### Stage 3: RTL Design (Register Transfer Level)
- Design described in **HDL (Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog)**.
- Synthesizable, technology-independent code.
- Output: HDL source files (`.v` or `.vhd`).

#### Stage 4: Functional Verification / Simulation
- Testbenches (SystemVerilog UVM, cocotb, etc.) validate RTL against spec.
- **Coverage metrics**: line, branch, toggle, functional coverage.
- Output: Verified RTL with high code coverage (>95% in industry).

#### Stage 5: Logic Synthesis
- HDL → **Gate-level netlist** using standard-cell library.
- Performs optimization: area, timing, power.
- Tools: Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus.
- Output: Gate-level Verilog netlist + `.sdf` delays.

#### Stage 6: Design-for-Test (DFT) Insertion
- Adds **scan chains**, **BIST (Built-In Self-Test)**, **boundary scan (JTAG)**.
- Critical for manufacturability — without DFT, faulty chips cannot be diagnosed.

#### Stage 7: Floorplanning
- Overall chip layout planning: I/O placement, macro placement, power-grid design.

#### Stage 8: Placement & Routing (P&R)
- **Placement**: Places standard cells in rows.
- **CTS (Clock Tree Synthesis)**: Balances clock skew.
- **Routing**: Connects cells using metal layers.
- Tools: Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2.

#### Stage 9: Physical Verification
- **DRC (Design Rule Check)**: Geometry rules.
- **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)**: Netlist vs. layout equivalence.
- **ERC (Electrical Rule Check)**: Antenna, density, electromigration.
- **STA (Static Timing Analysis)**: Setup/hold across PVT corners.

#### Stage 10: GDSII Generation & Tape-Out
- Final database (GDSII — Graphic Data Stream II) sent to foundry (TSMC, Samsung, Intel Foundry).
- Photomasks fabricated → wafer production begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "S-Spec, A-Alg, R-RTL, V-Verify, S-Synth, D-DFT, F-Floor, P-Place, P-Phys, G-GDS":**
> *Speci → Algo → RTL → Verify → Synth → DFT → Floor → Place → Physical-Verify → GDSII*

### 2.2 FPGA Design Flow — Step-by-Step

The FPGA flow is **shorter and cheaper** because the place-and-route is performed on *pre-fabricated silicon*.

#### Stage 1: Design Entry
- HDL (Verilog/VHDL), schematic, or high-level synthesis (HLS from C/C++).
- IP cores instantiated (memory controllers, PCIe, DSP blocks).

#### Stage 2: Functional Simulation
- Behavioral simulation in **ModelSim**, **Vivado Simulator**, or **Questa**.

#### Stage 3: Synthesis
- HDL → FPGA-specific netlist targeting LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, DSPs.
- Tool: Xilinx **Vivado**, Intel **Quartus**, Microsemi **Libero**.

#### Stage 4: Implementation
- **Translation**: Merges netlist with constraints.
- **Mapping**: Logic mapped to LUTs/CLBs.
- **Place & Route**: P&R on FPGA fabric.
- **Bitstream Generation**: Produces `.bit` or `.sof` file.

#### Stage 5: Timing Analysis
- Static timing analysis specific to FPGA primitives.

#### Stage 6: Programming & On-Board Debug
- Bitstream downloaded via **JTAG** or **USB**.
- In-system debug using **ChipScope / Vivado ILA / SignalTap**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity / Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N_{gates}$ | Total gate count | $\sum(\text{cell area})/\text{min-gate-area}$ | gates |
| $f_{max}$ | Maximum clock frequency | $1 / T_{clk,min}$ | Hz |
| $T_{clk,min}$ | Minimum clock period | $T_{cq} + T_{logic} + T_{net} + T_{su}$ | s |
| $T_{su}$ | Setup time constraint | $T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{su}$ | s |
| $T_{hold}$ | Hold time constraint | $T_{cq} + T_{comb} \geq T_{hold}$ | s |
| $P_{total}$ | Total power dissipation | $P_{dyn} + P_{stat}$ | W |
| $P_{dyn}$ | Dynamic power | $\alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | W |
| $P_{stat}$ | Static (leakage) power | $V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | W |
| $Y$ | Yield | $Y = e^{-D \cdot A}$ (Poisson model) | unitless |
| $C_{total}$ | Total ASIC cost | $C_{total} = C_{NRE} + N \cdot C_{unit}$ | $ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for KTU problems:**
> - $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ (switching activity factor)
> - $T_{hold}$ is a *race-through* condition — independent of clock period
> - For $f_{max}$ calculation, the *critical path* (longest combinational path) is the bottleneck

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ASIC Design Flow** underpins every smartphone SoC (Apple A-series, Snapdragon, Exynos), NVIDIA GPUs, and AI accelerators (Google TPU).
- **FPGA Design Flow** dominates aerospace (radiation-tolerant Xilinx Virtex), 5G base stations, automotive prototyping, and high-frequency trading accelerators.
- Modern SoCs frequently use a **mixed approach**: ASIC for volume production, FPGA for early prototyping and validation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Path Delay for Setup Time

The setup time constraint governs the *maximum* operating frequency. We derive it step-by-step as KTU expects in 14-mark questions.

$$
T_{clk} \;\geq\; T_{cq,\,FF} + T_{logic,\,max} + T_{net} + T_{su,\,FF}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **$T_{cq,\,FF}$** — Clock-to-Q delay: the time from the active clock edge to when the flip-flop output becomes valid. This is a property of the flip-flop cell.
2. **$T_{logic,\,max}$** — Combinational logic delay: the propagation delay through the longest chain of gates/blocks between two flip-flops.
3. **$T_{net}$** — Interconnect (wire) delay: routing delay depends on metal layer, length, and congestion.
4. **$T_{su,\,FF}$** — Setup time of the destination flip-flop: the data must be stable for at least this duration *before* the next clock edge.

Therefore, the *minimum* allowable clock period is:

$$
T_{clk,\,min} = T_{cq} + T_{logic,\,max} + T_{net} + T_{su}
$$

The *maximum* clock frequency is the reciprocal:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,\,min}} = \frac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{logic,\,max} + T_{net} + T_{su}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Suppose $T_{cq} = 0.2\,\text{ns}$, $T_{logic,max} = 1.5\,\text{ns}$, $T_{net} = 0.3\,\text{ns}$, $T_{su} = 0.1\,\text{ns}$.

$$
T_{clk,\,min} = 0.2 + 1.5 + 0.3 + 0.1 = 2.1\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{2.1 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 476.19\,\text{MHz}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Power Dissipation

The total power consumed by a CMOS gate has two independent components:

**Dynamic Power** — Power consumed during switching:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity (probability of 0→1 transition per clock cycle)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance (gate + interconnect + parasitic)
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f$ = clock frequency

**Derivation of $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$:**
Energy stored in capacitor per charge cycle $= \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$. During a full 0→1→0 cycle, this energy is dissipated *twice* (once in PMOS charging, once in NMOS discharging, but the *net* energy from supply is $C_L V_{DD}^2$). Per second, there are $f$ cycles, and only $\alpha$ fraction of those result in actual switching:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f
$$

**Static Power** — Leakage current drawn continuously:

$$
P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}
$$

**Total Power:**

$$
P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f + V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
$\alpha = 0.1$, $C_L = 50\,\text{fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.0\,\text{V}$, $f = 500\,\text{MHz}$, $I_{leak} = 10\,\mu\text{A}$.

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.1 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.0^2 \times 500 \times 10^6 = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{W} = 2.5\,\mu\text{W}
$$

$$
P_{stat} = 1.0 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 10\,\mu\text{W}
$$

$$
P_{total} = 2.5 + 10 = 12.5\,\mu\text{W}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: ASIC Cost Model (Crossover Analysis)

A classic KTU 14-mark problem involves finding the **crossover volume** at which ASIC becomes cheaper than FPGA.

**Cost of FPGA (per unit):**
$$
C_{FPGA} = C_{FPGA,fixed}
$$
(FPGA cost is constant per unit — no NRE in the traditional sense.)

**Cost of ASIC (per unit, amortized):**
$$
C_{ASIC}(N) = \frac{C_{NRE}}{N} + C_{ASIC,unit}
$$
where $N$ = production volume, $C_{NRE}$ = one-time mask + design cost.

**Crossover** — set $C_{ASIC}(N) = C_{FPGA}$:

$$
\frac{C_{NRE}}{N} + C_{ASIC,unit} = C_{FPGA}
$$

$$
N_{crossover} = \frac{C_{NRE}}{C_{FPGA} - C_{ASIC,unit}}
$$

**Worked Example:**
$C_{NRE} = \$2{,}000{,}000$, $C_{FPGA} = \$150$, $C_{ASIC,unit} = \$10$.

$$
N_{crossover} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{150 - 10} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{140} \approx 14{,}286 \text{ units}
$$

For production volumes **above 14,286 units**, ASIC is more economical; below that, FPGA wins.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ASIC vs. FPGA

| Parameter | ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|
| **NRE Cost** | Very High (\$M) | **Zero** |
| **Unit Cost** | **Low** (at volume) | High |
| **Time-to-Market** | 6–18 months | **Hours to weeks** |
| **Performance** | **Highest** (custom layout) | Moderate (overhead of programmable fabric) |
| **Power** | **Lowest** | Higher (interconnect capacitance) |
| **Flexibility** | **None** post-fab | **Fully reprogrammable** |
| **Design Effort** | Very High | Moderate |
| **Volume Suitability** | High-volume (>10K) | Low-to-medium volume (<10K) |
| **Testability** | Requires DFT insertion | **Built-in** (JTAG, readback) |
| **NRE Recovery Point** | High | None needed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ASIC Design Flow — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification] --> B[Algorithmic C Model]
    B --> C[RTL Design Verilog VHDL]
    C --> D[Functional Verification]
    D --> E[Logic Synthesis Gate Netlist]
    E --> F[DFT Insertion Scan BIST]
    F --> G[Floorplanning]
    G --> H[Placement and Routing]
    H --> I[Physical Verification DRC LVS STA]
    I --> J[GDSII Tape Out]
    J --> K[Wafer Fabrication Foundry]
```

**Block-by-Block Mapping:**
- **A → B** converts the human-language specification into a behavioral software model.
- **C → D** captures the hardware description and validates it functionally.
- **E** is the *technology-mapping boundary* — beyond this, design is no longer portable.
- **F** inserts test structures invisible to functional logic but vital for ATE.
- **G → H → I** is the *physical-design triangle* — these three steps are highly iterative.
- **J** is the *point of no return* — once masks are made, fixing bugs is prohibitively expensive.

### 4.2 FPGA Design Flow — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Design Entry HDL HLS Schematic] --> B1[Functional Simulation]
    B1 --> C1[Synthesis FPGA Vendor Tool]
    C1 --> D1[Translation and Mapping]
    D1 --> E1[Place and Route on FPGA Fabric]
    E1 --> F1[Timing Analysis FPGA Specific]
    F1 --> G1[Bitstream Generation]
    G1 --> H1[Download via JTAG USB]
    H1 --> I1[On Board Debug ILA SignalTap]
```

**Key Observation:** The FPGA flow **collapses Stages 7–10 of the ASIC flow** (floorplanning, P&R, physical verification) into a *single automated step* in the vendor tool. This is the fundamental reason FPGAs have a faster time-to-market.

### 4.3 ASIC Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ASIC[ASIC] --> FC[Full Custom]
    ASIC --> SC[Semi Custom]
    ASIC --> PLD[Programmable Logic]

    FC --> FC1[Hand crafted transistors]
    FC --> FC2[Custom analog RF]

    SC --> SC1[Standard Cell Based]
    SC --> SC2[Gate Array]
    SC --> SC3[Structured ASIC]

    PLD --> PLD1[FPGA]
    PLD --> PLD2[CPLD]
```

### 4.4 Cost-vs-Volume Crossover Plot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cost_Curves
    A[ASIC Cost per Unit] --> D[Slope 1/N]
    B[FPGA Cost per Unit] --> E[Constant Flat]
    end
    D --> X[Crossover Point N_crossover]
    E --> X
    X --> R[For N greater than N_crossover, ASIC is cheaper]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an ASIC. List the three main categories of ASICs.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)** is a microchip customized for a specific application, as opposed to a general-purpose IC. Once fabricated, its function is fixed.
>
> The three main categories are:
> 1. **Full-Custom ASIC** — every transistor and interconnect is hand-designed.
> 2. **Semi-Custom ASIC** — uses pre-designed standard cells or gate arrays; only metal interconnect is customized (Standard Cell, Gate Array, Structured ASIC).
> 3. **Programmable ASIC (FPGA/CPLD)** — pre-fabricated silicon that is configured by the end user.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the difference between a mask-programmed and a field-programmable device?**

**Model Answer:**

> - **Mask-Programmed Device (ASIC):** The logic function is defined by the *photomasks used in fabrication*. Once manufactured, the function is *permanent and unchangeable*. Examples: standard-cell ASICs, gate arrays.
> - **Field-Programmable Device (FPGA/PLD):** The logic function is defined *after fabrication* by the end user via configuration bits stored in **SRAM**, **Flash**, or **Anti-fuse** cells. It can be *reprogrammed* (SRAM-based) or one-time programmed (anti-fuse) in the field without going back to the foundry.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the complete **ASIC design flow** with a neat block diagram. Briefly describe the function of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Full-Custom**, **Standard-Cell**, and **Gate-Array** ASIC design styles in terms of NRE cost, per-unit cost, design effort, and performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ASIC Design Flow — Stage-by-Stage Explanation (7 marks):**

| Stage | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. **System Specification** | Defines functionality, performance, area, power, cost. Output: spec document. |
| 2. **Algorithmic / Behavioral Design** | C/C++/SystemC golden model. Floating-to-fixed-point conversion. |
| 3. **RTL Design** | Hardware description in Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog — synthesizable, tech-independent. |
| 4. **Functional Verification** | Testbenches + simulation (UVM, cocotb) verify RTL matches spec. Coverage >95%. |
| 5. **Logic Synthesis** | HDL → gate-level netlist using standard-cell library. Optimizes area, timing, power. |
| 6. **DFT Insertion** | Adds scan chains, BIST, JTAG boundary scan for manufacturability. |
| 7. **Floorplanning** | Macro and I/O placement; power-grid design. |
| 8. **Placement & Routing** | Places standard cells, synthesizes clock tree, routes interconnects. |
| 9. **Physical Verification** | DRC, LVS, ERC, STA across PVT corners. |
| 10. **GDSII & Tape-Out** | Final layout database sent to foundry for mask production. |

*Block diagram:* (refer to Section 4.1 above for the Mermaid flowchart.)

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Full-Custom | Standard-Cell | Gate-Array |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NRE Cost** | Very High | High | Moderate |
| **Per-Unit Cost** | Lowest | Low | Low–Moderate |
| **Design Effort** | Very High | High | Moderate |
| **Performance** | **Highest** | High | Moderate |
| **Transistor Customization** | All custom | Pre-designed cells | Only interconnect |
| **Mask Layers Unique** | All | Few top metal | Only top metals |
| **Time-to-Market** | 12–18 months | 6–9 months | 3–6 months |
| **Example** | Microprocessor cores | SoCs (Snapdragon) | Older ASICs |

**Conclusion:** Full-custom gives best PPA (Power-Performance-Area) but worst NRE/time. Gate-array is the fastest semi-custom. Standard-cell is the *industry workhorse*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **FPGA design flow** with a block diagram. How does it differ from the ASIC flow? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A semi-custom ASIC has an NRE cost of **₹1,50,00,000 (1.5 Cr INR)** and a per-unit cost of **₹250**. An equivalent FPGA-based solution costs **₹5,000 per unit**. Find the **crossover production volume** at which ASIC becomes more economical. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FPGA Design Flow (7 marks):**

1. **Design Entry** — HDL (Verilog/VHDL), schematic, or HLS from C/C++.
2. **Functional Simulation** — Behavioral simulation in ModelSim/Vivado Simulator.
3. **Synthesis** — HDL → FPGA primitive netlist (LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, DSPs).
4. **Translation & Mapping** — Netlist merged with user constraints (.xdc/.qsf).
5. **Place & Route** — Automatic placement on FPGA fabric; vendor tool handles routing.
6. **Timing Analysis** — FPGA-specific STA with on-chip delays.
7. **Bitstream Generation** — Produces `.bit` / `.sof` file.
8. **Programming & Debug** — Download via JTAG/USB; on-chip logic analyzer (ChipScope/ILA).

*Block diagram:* (refer to Section 4.2 above.)

**Differences from ASIC flow:**

| Aspect | ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|
| Fabrication | Yes, after tape-out | **No** — pre-fabricated |
| Floorplanning/P&R | Manual / semi-automated | **Fully automated by vendor tool** |
| Physical verification (DRC/LVS) | Required | **Not required** |
| DFT insertion | Required | **Built-in** |
| Time-to-market | Months | Hours to days |
| Re-programmability | None | **Yes** (SRAM FPGAs) |

**(b) Crossover Volume Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $C_{NRE} = ₹1{,}50{,}00{,}000 = ₹1.5 \times 10^8$
- $C_{ASIC,unit} = ₹250$
- $C_{FPGA,unit} = ₹5{,}000$

**Formula:**

$$
N_{crossover} = \frac{C_{NRE}}{C_{FPGA} - C_{ASIC,unit}}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
N_{crossover} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^8}{5000 - 250} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^8}{4750}
$$

**Step-by-step computation:**

$$
N_{crossover} = \frac{150{,}000{,}000}{4750} = 31{,}578.947...
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{crossover} \approx 31{,}579 \text{ units}}
$$

**Conclusion:** For production volumes **greater than ~31,579 units**, the ASIC solution is more economical. For lower volumes, FPGA is preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Students Lose 2 Marks Here:**
> 1. **Sign error** in the denominator: students sometimes write $C_{ASIC} - C_{FPGA}$ which gives a *negative* volume (nonsense). Always keep the *larger* per-unit cost first.
> 2. **Unit mismatch**: keep INR throughout; do not mix ₹ and $ unless an exchange rate is given.
> 3. **Rounding**: the answer must be rounded **up** to the next integer (you cannot produce a fractional chip) — state this clearly.
> 4. **Forgetting NRE**: a common error is to ignore $C_{NRE}$ entirely. The NRE is what makes the per-unit ASIC cost *decrease* with volume.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, ASIC & FPGA Design Flows:**
> - ✅ **ASIC** = Application-Specific IC, function fixed at fab time.
> - ✅ **FPGA** = Field-Programmable Gate Array, configurable post-fab (SRAM/Flash/Anti-fuse).
> - ✅ **Three ASIC categories**: Full-Custom, Semi-Custom (Standard-Cell, Gate-Array), Programmable.
> - ✅ **ASIC flow (10 stages)**: Spec → Algorithmic → RTL → Verify → Synth → DFT → Floorplan → P&R → Phys-Verify → GDSII.
> - ✅ **FPGA flow (8 stages)**: Design-Entry → Sim → Synth → Map → P&R → Timing → Bitstream → Program.
> - ✅ **NRE cost**: ASIC = High (₹1 Cr–₹100 Cr+); FPGA = Zero.
> - ✅ **Crossover volume formula**: $N_{crossover} = C_{NRE} / (C_{FPGA} - C_{ASIC,unit})$.
> - ✅ **Setup time equation**: $T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{logic} + T_{net} + T_{su}$.
> - ✅ **Power equation**: $P_{total} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f + V_{DD} I_{leak}$.
> - ✅ **DFT = Design-for-Test**: scan chains, BIST, JTAG.
> - ✅ **GDSII = final mask database** sent to foundry.
> - ✅ **JTAG = IEEE 1149.1** standard for programming and boundary-scan test.
> - ✅ **Modern trend**: ASIC for high-volume production, FPGA for prototyping & low-volume.
> - ✅ **Bitstream file** is the FPGA equivalent of GDSII — it *configures* the chip, not fabricates it.
> - ✅ **Hold time** is a *race condition* — independent of $T_{clk}$ — always check during STA.
> - ✅ **PVT corners** = Process (typical/fast/slow), Voltage (min/typ/max), Temperature (−40°C / 25°C / 125°C).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Top-Down and Bottom-Up Design Methodologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Top-Down and Bottom-Up Design Methodologies # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Top-Down Design Methodology **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** Top-Down Design Methodology is a hierarchical decomposition strategy in VLSI circuit design where the system is first specified at the highest abstraction level (system/behavioral specification) and then progressively refined and partitioned into smaller, well-defined sub-modules until a level suitable for physical layout implementation is reached. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Highlight:** The top-down approach begins with the **"What"** (specification) and ends with the **"How"** (transistor-level implementation). It is a *refinement-driven* design philosophy. ## 1.2 Bottom-Up Design Methodology **Formal Definition:** Bottom-Up Design Methodology is a synthesis-driven strategy where the designer starts with primitive components (transistors, resistors, standard cells) and builds increasingly complex functional blocks by interconnecting them, finally integrating these blocks to realize the complete target system. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Highlight:** The bottom-up approach begins with the **"How"** (silicon-proven primitive cells) and constructs the **"What"** (system functionality). It is a *composition-driven* design philosophy. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy: Building a House** | Methodology | House Analogy | |-------------|---------------| | **Top-Down** | An architect first draws the full house blueprint (rooms, layout), then hires specialists to design each room (kitchen, bedroom), then specific contractors build walls, electrical, plumbing, finishing. | | **Bottom-Up** | A builder starts with bricks, cement, and steel rods. He builds walls, then assembles rooms, and finally assembles rooms into a complete house. | **For VLSI:** Imagine designing a smartphone chip: - **Top-Down:** "I need a phone SoC" → "I need CPU + GPU + Modem" → "CPU needs ALU, Register File, Control Unit" → "ALU needs full-adders" → "Full-adder needs CMOS gates" → "Gates need transistors." - **Bottom-Up:** "I have NMOS/PMOS transistors" → "Build inverters, NAND, NOR" → "Build flip-flops, latches" → "Build ALUs, mux" → "Build processors" → "Integrate into SoC." > [!TIP] > **Practical Reality in KTU Context:** Modern industrial VLSI design (Synopsys, Cadence flow) is a *hybrid* — predominantly top-down at system level, bottom-up at the cell-library level. ## 1.4 Physical & Standard Metrics In a 2024 KTU VLSI design flow, the standard abstraction levels are characterized by: - **System Level:** Measured in **gigahertz (GHz)** clock targets, **milliwatts (mW)** power budget, **gates/mm²** density. - **Logic Level:** Measured in **gate equivalents**, fan-in/fan-out, propagation delay in **nanoseconds (ns)**. - **Circuit Level:** Characterized by **transistor dimensions in nanometers (nm)** — e.g., **45 nm, 28 nm, 14 nm** process nodes. - **Layout Level:** Measured in **µm²** silicon area, **DRC (Design Rule Check)** compliance in **lambda (λ)** units. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference ## 2.1 The Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn Chart) — The Foundational Model The **Y-chart** (proposed by Gajski and Kuhn, 1983) is the most critical visualization for understanding top-down and bottom-up methodologies in KTU VLSI curricula. It organizes the design process along **three axes of abstraction** intersecting at the center. The three axes are: 1. **Behavioral Domain** — What the system does (algorithms, functional spec). 2. **Structural Domain** — How the system is composed (blocks, interconnections). 3. **Physical Domain** — How the system is realized in silicon (layout, geometry). ## 2.2 Top-Down Design — Step-by-Step Logic The top-down methodology follows a strict **refinement hierarchy**: 1. **System Specification (Behavioral):** Define I/O, timing, power, throughput requirements. No hardware is yet defined. 2. **High-Level Functional Partitioning:** Decompose the system into functional sub-blocks (e.g., datapath, control unit, memory). 3. **Register Transfer Level (RTL) Description:** Express each block in HDL (Verilog/VHDL) using registers, FSMs, and combinational logic. 4. **Logic Synthesis:** Convert RTL to a gate-level netlist using a standard cell library. 5. **Circuit-Level Design:** Optimize gates for delay, power, and area using transistor sizing. 6. **Physical Layout:** Floorplanning, placement, routing, and verification at the silicon layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **"Why Top-Down?"** It enables **early verification**, **faster debugging**, and **architectural exploration** before committing to costly silicon. ## 2.3 Bottom-Up Design — Step-by-Step Logic The bottom-up methodology follows a **composition hierarchy**: 1. **Device Characterization:** Characterize the NMOS/PMOS transistor (I-V curves, threshold voltage, leakage). 2. **Standard Cell Design:** Build primitive cells — inverter, NAND, NOR, XOR, D-flip-flop. 3. **Cell Library Creation:** Characterize each cell for delay, power, area, and output load (the **.lib** file). 4. **Sub-Block Assembly:** Compose larger functional units (e.g., adder, multiplexer) from cells. 5. **Block-Level Integration:** Assemble sub-blocks into the full system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **"Why Bottom-Up?"** It guarantees **silicon-proven, pre-characterized** building blocks, ensuring **predictable performance** and **high yield**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet Since this topic is **methodological** rather than formula-driven, the reference table summarizes the **decision rules, process nodes, and metrics** examiners expect students to recall. | Design Aspect | Top-Down | Bottom-Up | |---------------|----------|-----------| | Starting Point | System Specification | Primitive Devices | | Direction | Specification → Transistor | Transistor → System | | Primary Tool | HDL (Verilog/VHDL), High-Level Synthesis | SPICE, Layout Editor, Cell Characterization | | Verification Style | Simulation-driven (functional) | Characterization-driven (parametric) | | Optimization Level | Architectural, algorithmic | Device, cell, layout | | Typical Modern Application | SoC design, ASIC front-end | Standard cell library, IP core development | | Dominant Industry Era | 1980s onwards (HDL revolution) | 1970s (pre-HDL era, full-custom) | | Risk Profile | High early, decreases as design matures | Low early, increases at system integration | | KTU Bloom's Level Tested | Apply, Analyze | Remember, Understand | | Key Deliverable | RTL/Gate Netlist | Cell Library, Layout Database | | Standard Process Node (nm) | Typical Vdd (V) | Approx. Gate Density (kgates/mm²) | |----------------------------|------------------|-----------------------------------| | 180 | 1.8 | 100 | | 90 | 1.2 | 500 | | 65 | 1.1 | 1000 | | 45 | 1.0 | 2000 | | 28 | 0.9 | 4000 | | 14 | 0.8 | 8000 | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Top-Down** is dominant in **digital ASIC/FPGA design** because HDLs allow millions of gates to be described efficiently. - **Bottom-Up** dominates **analog/mixed-signal IC design** and **standard cell library development** where transistor-level precision is non-negotiable. - The **Y-chart** is a **mandatory diagrammatic question** in KTU Module 1 of VLSI Circuit Design. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Structured Procedures > [!NOTE] > This topic is **methodological**; therefore, classical algebraic derivations are not applicable. Instead, the analytical rigour lies in the **hierarchical decomposition procedure** and the **Y-chart traversal logic**, which are presented below with exhaustive step-by-step reasoning. ## 3.1 Analytical Procedure: Mapping a Design Problem onto the Y-Chart **Problem Scenario:** Design a 4-bit ripple-carry adder using the top-down methodology. **Step 1 — Identify Behavioral Domain (Top Axis):** Define the input-output relationship: $$S_i = A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_i$$ $$C_{i+1} = (A_i \cdot B_i) + (C_i \cdot (A_i \oplus B_i))$$ > *Reasoning:* The behavioral domain answers "What must the circuit do?" — here, it must add two 4-bit numbers and produce a 4-bit sum and 1-bit carry. **Step 2 — Identify Structural Domain (Right Axis):** Decompose into hierarchical structural elements: ``` 4-bit Adder ├── Full Adder 0 (FA0) ├── Full Adder 1 (FA1) ├── Full Adder 2 (FA2) └── Full Adder 3 (FA3) ├── Each FA contains: 2 XOR gates, 2 AND gates, 1 OR gate └── Each gate is built from: NMOS + PMOS transistors ``` > *Reasoning:* The structural domain answers "What blocks compose the system, and how are they connected?" — the ripple-carry topology is the structural blueprint. **Step 3 — Identify Physical Domain (Left Axis):** Map structure to geometry: - Each gate → CMOS transistor schematic (PMOS pull-up, NMOS pull-down). - Each transistor → rectangular diffusion/poly layout with **lambda (λ) rules**. - Total area → µm² or mm²; delay → ns. > *Reasoning:* The physical domain answers "What does the chip look like in silicon?" **Step 4 — Y-Chart Traversal Logic:** The Y-chart dictates that a *complete design* traverses all three axes at *all abstraction levels* (system, algorithmic, RTL, logic, circuit, layout, silicon). Any missing traversal is a design gap. $$\text{Complete Design} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{6} \left( \text{Behavioral}_i \cap \text{Structural}_i \cap \text{Physical}_i \right)$$ **Step 5 — Refinement Delta (Top-Down Logic):** At each downward step, the design gains *implementation detail* and loses *abstraction generality*: $$\text{Abstraction Level}_{n+1} = \text{Abstraction Level}_{n} + \Delta_{\text{refinement}}$$ where $\Delta_{\text{refinement}}$ represents the *additional structural and physical constraints* introduced at the next level down. ## 3.2 Analytical Procedure: Bottom-Up Cell Library Construction **Step 1 — Device Level:** Characterize NMOS and PMOS in the target process. Extract parameters: - $V_{THN}$, $V_{THP}$ (threshold voltages) - $k_n'$, $k_p'$ (transconductance parameters) - $t_{ox}$ (oxide thickness) **Step 2 — Inverter Analysis:** Derive the **switching threshold**: $$V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP} \vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$ > *Reasoning:* A symmetric inverter is the canonical primitive — its $V_M$ sets the noise margin and signal integrity baseline for every subsequent gate built on top of it. **Step 3 — Gate Synthesis:** Build NAND, NOR, XOR from the inverter's CMOS topology. The number of transistors per gate is: | Gate | NMOS Count | PMOS Count | Total | |------|------------|------------|-------| | Inverter | 1 | 1 | 2 | | 2-input NAND | 2 | 2 | 4 | | 2-input NOR | 2 | 2 | 4 | | 2-input XOR | 4 | 4 | 8 | | D Flip-Flop | 8 | 8 | 16 | **Step 4 — Library Characterization:** For each cell, extract: - **Propagation delay:** $t_{pd} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{on}}$ - **Static power:** $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ - **Dynamic power:** $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ - **Input capacitance:** in **femtofarads (fF)** **Step 5 — Library Compilation:** The resulting **.lib (Liberty) file** feeds the top-down synthesis tool — completing the *hybrid* modern VLSI flow. ## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Framework | Evaluation Criterion | Top-Down Bias | Bottom-Up Bias | |---------------------|---------------|----------------| | Time-to-first-prototype | Slower for analog; faster for digital | Faster for analog cells; slower for digital SoC | | Engineering Expertise Required | HDL, system architecture | Device physics, layout, parasitic extraction | | Scalability to Billions of Transistors | Excellent | Poor (manual cell-by-cell impossible) | | KTU Examination Weightage | ~60% (conceptual dominance) | ~40% (foundational) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid) > [!TIP] > The following **Mermaid** diagrams are written to compile safely. All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, and contain no reserved keywords or markdown formatting inside labels. ## 4.1 Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A1[SILICON GEOMETRY LEVEL] --> A2[LAYOUT LEVEL] A2 --> A3[CIRCUIT LEVEL] A3 --> A4[LOGIC LEVEL] A4 --> A5[RTL LEVEL] A5 --> A6[ALGORITHMIC LEVEL] A6 --> A7[SYSTEM LEVEL] B1[PHYSICAL DOMAIN] -.-> A1 B1 -.-> A2 B1 -.-> A3 C1[STRUCTURAL DOMAIN] -.-> A4 C1 -.-> A5 C1 -.-> A6 C1 -.-> A7 D1[BEHAVIORAL DOMAIN] -.-> A7 D1 -.-> A6 D1 -.-> A5 D1 -.-> A4 A7 --- X((DESIGN
CORE)) A3 --- X X --- C1 X --- D1 X --- B1 ``` ## 4.2 Top-Down Design Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD n1[S1 SYSTEM SPECIFICATION] --> n2[S2 FUNCTIONAL PARTITIONING] n2 --> n3[S3 BEHAVIORAL MODELING] n3 --> n4[S4 RTL DESCRIPTION VERILOG] n4 --> n5[S5 LOGIC SYNTHESIS TO GATE NETLIST] n5 --> n6[S6 CIRCUIT OPTIMIZATION TRANSISTOR SIZING] n6 --> n7[S7 PHYSICAL LAYOUT FLOORPLAN PLACEMENT ROUTING] n7 --> n8[S8 VERIFICATION DRC LVS] n8 --> n9[S9 TAPEOUT OR FABRICATION] n8 -.FEEDBACK.-> n3 n8 -.FEEDBACK.-> n4 ``` ## 4.3 Bottom-Up Design Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD m1[B1 DEVICE CHARACTERIZATION NMOS PMOS] --> m2[B2 PRIMITIVE CELL DESIGN INVERTER NAND] m2 --> m3[B3 STANDARD CELL LIBRARY CREATION] m3 --> m4[B4 CELL CHARACTERIZATION DELAY POWER AREA] m4 --> m5[B5 SUB-BLOCK ASSEMBLY ADDER MUX] m5 --> m6[B6 FUNCTIONAL BLOCK DESIGN ALU REGISTER FILE] m6 --> m7[B7 SYSTEM INTEGRATION SOC] m7 --> m8[B8 TOP LEVEL VERIFICATION] m4 -.LIBRARY.-> m5 m4 -.LIBRARY.-> m6 ``` ## 4.4 VLSI Design Hierarchy (Vertical Abstraction) ```mermaid graph TD h1[SYSTEM NOTEBOOK OR PHONE SOC] --> h2[CHIP MULTI CORE PROCESSOR] h2 --> h3[BLOCK CPU GPU MEMORY CONTROLLER] h3 --> h4[UNIT ALU REGISTER FILE DECODER] h4 --> h5[GATE NAND NOR XOR INVERTER] h5 --> h6[TRANSISTOR NMOS PMOS] h6 --> h7[PHYSICAL LAYOUT DIFFUSION POLY METAL] ``` ## 4.5 Hybrid Modern VLSI Design Flow (Top-Down + Bottom-Up) ```mermaid flowchart LR t1[Top-Down Front End] --> mid[Synthesis to Gate Netlist] mid --> t2[Bottom-Up Library Back End] t2 --> t3[Place and Route] t3 --> t4[Tapeout] t4 -.feedback.-> t1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Architecture Note:** The hybrid diagram above represents the dominant industrial reality — top-down specifications are realized using bottom-up constructed standard cell libraries. KTU examiners frequently test this synthesis of both methodologies. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **"Define top-down design methodology. List its key advantages in VLSI circuit design." (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** Top-down design methodology is a hierarchical decomposition approach in VLSI where the system is first specified at the behavioral level and then progressively refined down to the transistor/physical layout level. The design moves from abstract specification to concrete implementation in a stepwise refinement. *Key advantages:* 1. **Early verification** of architectural decisions using HDL simulation. 2. **Faster design cycles** and easier debugging at higher abstraction levels. 3. **Better design reuse** and modularity, enabling concurrent engineering by multiple teams. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often write only the definition without listing *specific* advantages. In a 3-mark KTU question, **at least two clear advantages** are mandatory to score full marks. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **"Differentiate between top-down and bottom-up design approaches with one example each." (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Top-Down | Bottom-Up | |-----------|----------|-----------| | Starting Point | System specification | Primitive cells (transistor/cell) | | Direction | Abstract → Concrete | Concrete → Abstract | | Example | Designing a 32-bit microprocessor starting from its behavioral specification (instruction set architecture) | Building a CMOS standard cell library starting from NMOS/PMOS device characterization | > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Tip:** Examiners specifically look for a *contrast pair* in tabular form. A bullet-list answer without contrast typically scores only 1.5 of 3 marks. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]** **Part (a):** Explain the Y-chart (Gajski-Kuhn chart) used in VLSI design. Describe the three domains and the different abstraction levels along each axis. **(7 marks)** **Part (b):** With a neat diagram, explain the top-down design methodology. List the major steps from system specification to physical layout. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **1. Introduction to Y-Chart (2 marks):** The Y-chart, proposed by Gajski and Kuhn in 1983, is a graphical representation of VLSI design that organizes the design process along three axes (domains) intersecting at the center. It is the universally accepted model for visualizing design complexity. **2. The Three Domains (3 marks):** - **Behavioral Domain (Top Axis):** Describes *what* the system does — algorithms, I/O behavior, functional responses. Free of structural or physical details. - **Structural Domain (Right Axis):** Describes *how* the system is composed — components, interconnections, hierarchy of blocks. - **Physical Domain (Left Axis):** Describes *how* the system is fabricated in silicon — geometric layout, transistor dimensions, routing. **3. Abstraction Levels (2 marks):** Along each domain, the design progresses through levels: **System → Algorithmic → RTL → Logic → Circuit → Layout → Silicon**. The center of the Y represents the design goal that must be consistent across all three domains at every level. > See **Y-Chart Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1** for visual reference. --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **1. Definition (1 mark):** Top-down design methodology is a *refinement-driven* approach where design proceeds from a high-level system specification to a low-level physical implementation through successive, well-defined refinement steps. **2. Major Steps (5 marks):** 1. **System Specification:** Define functionality, performance, power, area, and I/O requirements. 2. **Functional Partitioning:** Decompose system into major functional sub-blocks. 3. **Behavioral Modeling:** Write algorithmic or behavioral HDL models. 4. **RTL Design:** Express the design at the register-transfer level using Verilog/VHDL. 5. **Logic Synthesis:** Use EDA tools (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler) to map RTL to a gate-level netlist using a standard cell library. 6. **Circuit Optimization:** Size transistors for speed, power, and area. 7. **Physical Design:** Floorplan, place, and route the cells into silicon layout. 8. **Verification:** Perform DRC, LVS, and post-layout simulation. **3. Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the **Top-Down Design Flow Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2**. A neatly drawn block diagram earns the dedicated diagram mark. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]** **Part (a):** Explain the bottom-up design methodology. How does it differ from the top-down approach? **(7 marks)** **Part (b):** Discuss the role of standard cell libraries in modern VLSI design. Explain how a CMOS inverter is characterized for inclusion in such a library. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **1. Definition (1.5 marks):** Bottom-up design is a *composition-driven* approach where the design begins with pre-characterized primitive devices (transistors) and progressively builds up to the complete system by composing increasingly complex functional blocks. **2. Major Phases (3.5 marks):** - **Device Characterization:** DC and AC behavior of NMOS/PMOS in the chosen process. - **Primitive Cell Design:** CMOS inverter, NAND, NOR, transmission gate, etc. - **Standard Cell Library:** Each cell is laid out with fixed height and variable width. - **Characterization:** Delay, power, area, input capacitance are extracted for every cell and stored in a **.lib** file. - **Block Assembly:** Larger blocks (adders, multiplexers, register files) are built from library cells. - **System Integration:** Blocks are connected to form the full chip. **3. Differences from Top-Down (2 marks):** | Aspect | Bottom-Up | Top-Down | |--------|-----------|----------| | Driver | Device physics, layout | System specification, HDL | | Industry Era | 1970s and analog design | 1980s onwards, digital ASIC | | Best For | Standard cells, analog IPs | Digital SoCs, FPGAs | --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **1. Role of Standard Cell Libraries (3 marks):** - Provide a **pre-verified, pre-characterized** set of logic gates that synthesis tools can use. - Enable **portability** across process nodes. - **Reduce design time** dramatically — designers need not layout each gate manually. - Ensure **predictable performance** because each cell is silicon-proven. **2. CMOS Inverter Characterization (4 marks):** A CMOS inverter is characterized for the following parameters: - **DC Transfer Characteristics:** Plot $V_{out}$ vs $V_{in}$ to extract the switching threshold: $$V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP} \vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$ - **Noise Margins:** $$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}, \quad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$ - **Propagation Delay:** $t_{pLH}$ and $t_{pHL}$ measured at the 50% transition point. - **Power Dissipation:** Static (leakage) and dynamic ($\alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$). - **Input Capacitance:** The gate capacitance of the NMOS/PMOS pair. The inverter's **.lib** entry feeds the synthesis tool, which in turn enables the top-down RTL-to-layout flow. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When asked about the Y-chart, students often *forget to draw the diagram* — losing up to 2 marks out of 7. Always reserve at least 30 seconds to draw a clean Y-chart. Similarly, when asked about top-down vs bottom-up, examiners expect a *tabulated comparison*, not just a paragraph. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1** - **Y-Chart:** Three domains — *Behavioral, Structural, Physical*; seven levels along each axis from System to Silicon. - **Top-Down:** Specification-driven, refinement path, HDL-centric, dominant in digital SoC design. - **Bottom-Up:** Device-driven, composition path, layout-centric, dominant in cell library and analog design. - **Modern Industrial Flow:** A *hybrid* — top-down front end + bottom-up cell libraries. - **Key Formulas to Memorize:** - Switching threshold: $V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP} \vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$ - Dynamic power: $P_d = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ - Propagation delay: $t_{pd} = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{2 I_{on}}$ - **Standard Units:** Process nodes in **nm**, area in **µm²**, delay in **ns/pico-seconds**, capacitance in **fF**, power in **µW/mW**. - **KTU Bloom's Levels Most Tested:** *Understand, Apply, Analyze* — focus on *why* a methodology is chosen, not just *what* it is. - **Common Confusion:** Top-down does *not* mean starting at the layout level. Many students lose marks by inverting the hierarchy. - **Examiner's Mantra:** A *diagram + table + definition* triple is the safest way to secure full marks in any 7-mark or 14-mark KTU question on this topic.

topics.title: Logical and Physical Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logical and Physical Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 VLSI Logical Design — Formal Definition

**VLSI Logical Design** is the front-end phase of integrated circuit design where the abstract functional specification of a system is transformed into a technology-independent or technology-mapped gate-level netlist. It encompasses **Register Transfer Level (RTL) design**, **logic synthesis**, **technology mapping**, **logic optimization**, and **functional verification**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Logical design transforms a behavioural HDL description (Verilog/VHDL) into a structured, optimized gate-level netlist using logic synthesis, targeting a specific standard-cell library, while satisfying constraints on **area**, **timing (delay)**, and **power dissipation**.

## 1.2 VLSI Physical Design — Formal Definition

**VLSI Physical Design** is the back-end phase where the synthesized gate-level netlist is converted into a geometric layout (GDSII/OASIS) by determining the exact physical placement of every standard cell, macro, and I/O pad on the silicon die, followed by interconnect routing that satisfies the manufacturing design rules of the target technology node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Physical design converts the logical netlist into a geometric database for fabrication by performing **floorplanning, partitioning, placement, clock-tree synthesis (CTS), and routing**, while ensuring **DRC (Design Rule Check)** and **LVS (Layout Versus Schematic)** cleanliness.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Building" Model

Think of the complete VLSI design flow as building a luxury house:

| Design Phase | House Building Equivalent | Key Output |
|--------------|---------------------------|------------|
| **Specification / RTL** | Architectural brief and floor plan sketch | Behavioural intent |
| **Logical Design** | Detailed engineering blueprint (electrical, plumbing schematics) | Gate-level **netlist** |
| **Physical Design** | Actual construction on the plot, brick by brick | **GDSII layout** |
| **Fabrication** | Final painted and furnished house | Packaged silicon chip |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a faulty blueprint produces a weak house, a poorly optimized logical design propagates timing violations and power bloat into the physical layout that cannot always be fixed downstream.

## 1.4 Three Pillars of Optimization

Throughout logical and physical design, the engineer balances three competing metrics, all expressed in the standard VLSI optimization triangle:

- **Area ($A$)** — measured in $\mu m^2$ or **gate equivalents (GE)**
- **Performance / Delay ($t_d$)** — measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**
- **Power ($P$)** — measured in **watts (W)** or **microwatts ($\mu W$)**

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** Modern designs (at 7 nm and below) are constrained by **Power-Delay-Product (PDP)** and **Energy-Delay-Product (EDP)** rather than area alone.

## 1.5 The Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart Abstraction

The **Y-chart** (popularized by Gajski and Kuhn) is the canonical model for understanding the design abstractions in VLSI. It consists of three concentric domains:

1. **Behavioural Domain** — what the circuit does (algorithms, FSMs)
2. **Structural Domain** — how it is built (gates, flip-flops, interconnect)
3. **Geometric (Physical) Domain** — where it is built (layouts, masks)

The three radial axes represent the levels of abstraction: **System**, **Algorithmic (RTL)**, **Logic (Gate)**, **Circuit (Transistor)**, and **Layout**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete VLSI Design Flow

The VLSI design flow is executed as a sequence of well-defined steps. Each step has a verifiable output that is passed as an input to the next.

### 2.1.1 Front-End (Logical) Flow — Step-by-Step

1. **Specification** — Functional intent, performance, power, and area (PPA) targets documented.
2. **RTL Design (HDL Coding)** — Verilog/VHDL code written to express the algorithm.
3. **Functional Simulation** — Behavioural verification with testbenches (zero-delay simulation).
4. **Logic Synthesis** — HDL is translated by a synthesizer (e.g., **Synopsys Design Compiler**, **Cadence Genus**) into generic Boolean gates.
5. **Technology Mapping** — Generic gates are mapped to cells from a specific **standard cell library (PDK)**.
6. **Logic Optimization** — Combinational and sequential optimization for area, speed, and power.
7. **DFT Insertion** — **Design-for-Test** structures (scan chains, BIST, boundary scan) are inserted.
8. **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** — Pre-layout timing sign-off using estimated wire-load models.
9. **Formal Verification (Equivalence Checking)** — RTL vs. netlist logical equivalence is mathematically proven.

### 2.1.2 Back-End (Physical) Flow — Step-by-Step

1. **Floorplanning** — Overall die area, aspect ratio, macro placement, I/O pad placement, and power-grid planning.
2. **Partitioning** — The design is split into manageable blocks to handle complexity.
3. **Placement** — Standard cells are positioned on the rows to minimize wire length and meet timing.
4. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** — A balanced clock distribution network is built to minimize **clock skew**.
5. **Global Routing** — Approximate paths for each net are planned across the routing regions.
6. **Detailed Routing** — Exact metal-layer tracks for every net are assigned.
7. **RC Extraction** — Parasitic resistances and capacitances of the physical interconnect are back-annotated.
8. **Post-Layout STA** — True timing analysis with real parasitics; sign-off timing closure.
9. **Physical Verification** — **DRC**, **LVS**, **Antenna**, and **ERC** checks.
10. **GDSII Generation (Tape-out)** — Final layout database for mask generation.

## 2.2 Logical Effort — The Universal Delay Model

**Logical Effort (LE)** is a back-of-the-envelope analytical method, formalized by Ivan Sutherland and Bob Sproull, that allows delay estimation of any CMOS logic gate without SPICE simulation.

The delay of a single logic stage is modelled as:

$$d = g \cdot h + p$$

where:
- $d$ = **normalized delay** of the stage (in units of $\tau$, the inverter delay)
- $g$ = **logical effort** of the gate (how much worse it drives load compared to an inverter)
- $h$ = **electrical effort** or **fan-out (FO)**, defined as $h = C_{out} / C_{in}$
- $p$ = **parasitic delay** of the gate (due to its internal diffusion capacitance)

The **branching effort** $b$ is introduced when a gate drives multiple loads:

$$h = \frac{C_{on-path} + C_{off-path}}{C_{in}} = g \cdot b$$

The **optimal stage effort** $\hat{f}$ that minimizes total delay along a path of $N$ stages is:

$$\hat{f} = \sqrt[N]{F} \cdot G \cdot B$$

where $F$ is the overall path effort, $G$ is the total logical effort, and $B$ is the total branching effort.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for Module 1 numerical problems. Memorize these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning & Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Logical Effort Delay | $d = g \cdot h + p$ | $d$: normalized delay, $g$: logical effort, $h$: electrical effort, $p$: parasitic delay |
| 2 | Electrical Effort | $h = C_{out} / C_{in}$ | Ratio of load to input capacitance |
| 3 | Branching Effort | $b = (C_{on} + C_{off}) / C_{on}$ | Off-path load penalty factor |
| 4 | Path Effort | $F = G \cdot B \cdot H$ | $G$: path logical effort, $B$: path branching, $H$: path electrical |
| 5 | Optimal Stage Effort | $\hat{f} = \sqrt[N]{F}$ | Minimizes path delay over $N$ stages |
| 6 | Inverter Intrinsic Delay | $d_{inv} = R_{eq} \cdot C_{out}$ | For sizing $R_{eq}$ driver |
| 7 | RC Wire Delay (Elmore) | $t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire}$ | Distributed RC line approximation |
| 8 | Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | $\alpha$: switching activity, $f$: clock frequency |
| 9 | Static (Leakage) Power | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | $I_{leak}$: subthreshold + gate leakage |
| 10 | Power-Delay Product | $PDP = P \cdot t_{pd}$ | Energy metric in joules |
| 11 | Energy-Delay Product | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_{pd}$ | Combined energy-quality metric |
| 12 | Cell Area (Transistors) | $A_{cell} \approx N_{MOS} \cdot W_{min} \cdot L_{min}$ | Estimated layout area |
| 13 | Buffer Chain Optimal Stages | $N_{opt} = \ln(C_L / C_{in}) / \ln(f)$ | For driving large loads |
| 14 | Clock Skew Tolerance | $t_{skew} < t_{hold}$ | Hold-time skew bound |
| 15 | Gate Equivalent | $1 \text{ GE} = 2 \text{ transistors}$ (NAND2) | Standard area metric |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logical Effort** is the de-facto method used by synthesis tools to size gates for minimum delay.
- **Dynamic Power Equation** ($P_{dyn} = \alpha C V^2 f$) is the single most important formula driving modern low-power design — note the **quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$**, which is why voltage scaling is so effective.
- **Elmore Delay** is used by routers to estimate wire delay before detailed RC extraction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Logical Effort Delay Equation

We start from the RC model of a CMOS inverter. A minimum-sized inverter has an equivalent pull-down resistance $R_{eq}$ and intrinsic diffusion capacitance $C_{p}$ (parasitic).

For a logic gate with input capacitance $C_{in}$ driving a load $C_{out}$, the on-resistance scales inversely with the input gate width:

$$R_{gate} = \frac{R_{eq}}{x}$$

where $x$ is the tapering factor (size ratio of the gate relative to a unit inverter). The input capacitance scales linearly:

$$C_{in} = x \cdot c_{inv}$$

where $c_{inv}$ is the input capacitance of a unit inverter.

The absolute delay of the stage (in seconds) is:

$$t_{abs} = R_{gate} \cdot (C_{out} + C_{p}) = \frac{R_{eq}}{x} \cdot (C_{out} + C_{p})$$

Normalizing by the unit inverter delay $\tau = R_{eq} \cdot c_{inv}$:

$$d = \frac{t_{abs}}{\tau} = \frac{(C_{out} + C_{p})}{x \cdot c_{inv}} = \frac{C_{out}}{x \cdot c_{inv}} + \frac{C_{p}}{x \cdot c_{inv}}$$

We define:
- **Logical effort** $g \equiv C_{in,g} / c_{inv}$ — the input cap of the gate relative to inverter
- **Parasitic delay** $p \equiv C_{p} / c_{inv}$ — the intrinsic delay normalized to $\tau$
- **Electrical effort** $h \equiv C_{out} / C_{in} = C_{out} / (x \cdot c_{inv})$

Substituting these definitions:

$$d = g \cdot h + p$$

This is the **fundamental logical effort equation**. Note that $g$ and $p$ are properties of the gate *type*, while $h$ is a property of the *circuit* (load and size).

## 3.2 Derivation of Buffer Chain Optimal Number of Stages

We wish to drive a large load capacitance $C_L$ from a small driver $C_{in}$ with minimum delay. We insert $N$ progressively larger inverters, each stage having tapering factor $f$ (i.e., $C_{i+1} / C_i = f$).

The total capacitance ratio is:

$$\frac{C_L}{C_{in}} = f^N$$

The delay of the entire chain is:

$$D = N \cdot d_{inv} = N \cdot (g \cdot f + p_{inv})$$

For an inverter chain, $g = 1$ and $p_{inv} = 1$ (typical):

$$D = N \cdot (f + 1)$$

Expressing $N$ in terms of $f$:

$$N = \frac{\ln(C_L / C_{in})}{\ln(f)}$$

Substituting into the delay equation:

$$D(f) = \frac{\ln(C_L / C_{in})}{\ln(f)} \cdot (f + 1)$$

To find the minimum delay, we differentiate $D(f)$ with respect to $f$ and set to zero. Using calculus:

$$\frac{\partial D}{\partial f} = \frac{\ln(C_L / C_{in})}{\ln(f)} \cdot 1 - \frac{\ln(C_L / C_{in})}{\ln^2(f)} \cdot \frac{1}{f} \cdot (f + 1) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\frac{f}{f+1} = \frac{1}{\ln(f)}$$

The closed-form transcendental solution is the **Lambert W function**, but for the common case $f = e$ (each stage $e \approx 2.718$ times larger), the equation is approximately satisfied.

The **optimal number of stages** is therefore:

$$N_{opt} = \frac{\ln(C_L / C_{in})}{\ln(e)} = \ln\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right)$$

For a practical example: if $C_L / C_{in} = 1000$, then $N_{opt} = \ln(1000) \approx 6.9 \approx 7$ stages.

## 3.3 Derivation of Elmore Delay for RC Ladder

Consider a distributed wire modelled as $N$ lumped RC segments, each with resistance $r$ and capacitance $c$. The total wire resistance is $R = N \cdot r$ and total capacitance is $C = N \cdot c$.

The **Elmore delay** (50% propagation delay) for a distributed RC line driven by a source resistance $R_s$ is:

$$t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot (R_s \cdot c + (R_s + r) \cdot c + (R_s + 2r) \cdot c + \ldots + (R_s + Nr) \cdot c)$$

$$t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot \left[ N \cdot R_s \cdot c + r \cdot c \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} k \right]$$

The arithmetic series $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} k = N(N-1)/2$ gives:

$$t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot \left[ R_s \cdot C + \frac{R \cdot C}{2} \cdot \frac{(N-1)}{N} \right]$$

As $N \to \infty$ (true distributed line):

$$t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot \left[ R_s \cdot C + \frac{R \cdot C}{2} \right]$$

This is the famous **$\pi$-model** wire delay used in routing estimators.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Logical Effort Sizing

**Problem:** A 4-input NAND gate ($g = 4/3$, $p = 2$) drives an FO4 inverter chain, where the total load capacitance is $C_L = 32 \cdot c_{inv}$. Compute (a) the path effort, (b) the optimal stage effort, and (c) the minimum path delay.

**Solution:**

Given: $g_{NAND4} = 4/3$, $p_{NAND4} = 2$, $N = 1$ (single stage), $C_L / C_{in} = 32$.

**Step (a) — Path Effort:**

$$F = G \cdot B \cdot H = \frac{4}{3} \cdot 1 \cdot 32 = \frac{128}{3} \approx 42.67$$

**Step (b) — Optimal Stage Effort:**

$$\hat{f} = \sqrt[1]{F} = \frac{128}{3} \approx 42.67$$

**Step (c) — Minimum Path Delay:**

$$D = N \cdot (\hat{f} + p_i) = 1 \cdot \left( \frac{128}{3} + 2 \right) = \frac{134}{3} \approx 44.67 \, \tau$$

The minimum delay of this single-stage NAND4 is approximately $44.67 \, \tau$ — quite slow, which is why multi-stage designs are preferred for large fan-outs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef domain fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef level fill:#d9e2f3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef center fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    A1["Behavioural Domain: System"]:::domain
    A2["Behavioural Domain: Algorithm"]:::domain
    A3["Behavioural Domain: RTL"]:::domain
    A4["Behavioural Domain: Logic"]:::domain
    A5["Behavioural Domain: Circuit"]:::domain

    B1["Structural Domain: System"]:::domain
    B2["Structural Domain: Processor"]:::domain
    B3["Structural Domain: Register"]:::domain
    B4["Structural Domain: Gate"]:::domain
    B5["Structural Domain: Transistor"]:::domain

    C1["Geometric Domain: Board"]:::domain
    C2["Geometric Domain: Floorplan"]:::domain
    C3["Geometric Domain: Cell"]:::domain
    C4["Geometric Domain: Stick"]:::domain
    C5["Geometric Domain: Layout"]:::domain

    CTR["VLSI Design Abstractions Y Center"]:::center

    A1 --- A2 --- A3 --- A4 --- A5
    B1 --- B2 --- B3 --- B4 --- B5
    C1 --- C2 --- C3 --- C4 --- C5
    CTR --- A3
    CTR --- B3
    CTR --- C3
```

## 4.2 End-to-End VLSI Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef front fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef back fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef signoff fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000

    P1["Specification"]:::front
    P2["RTL Design Verilog"]:::front
    P3["Functional Simulation"]:::front
    P4["Logic Synthesis"]:::front
    P5["Technology Mapping"]:::front
    P6["DFT Insertion"]:::front
    P7["Pre-Layout STA"]:::front
    P8["Equivalence Checking"]:::front
    P9["Floorplanning"]:::back
    P10["Placement"]:::back
    P11["Clock Tree Synthesis"]:::back
    P12["Global Routing"]:::back
    P13["Detailed Routing"]:::back
    P14["RC Extraction"]:::back
    P15["Post-Layout STA"]:::signoff
    P16["DRC and LVS Check"]:::signoff
    P17["GDSII Tapeout"]:::signoff

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> P9 --> P10 --> P11 --> P12 --> P13 --> P14 --> P15 --> P16 --> P17
```

## 4.3 Logical Design Synthesis Sub-Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef input fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef process fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef output fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    I1["HDL Code RTL"]:::input
    I2["Standard Cell Library PDK"]:::input
    I3["Design Constraints SDC"]:::input
    P1["Translation to GTECH"]:::process
    P2["Generic Boolean Optimization"]:::process
    P3["Technology Mapping to Cells"]:::process
    P4["Timing Driven Optimization"]:::process
    P5["Area and Power Optimization"]:::process
    O1["Gate Level Netlist"]:::output
    O2["SDF Delay File"]:::output
    O3["Synthesis Reports"]:::output

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P3
    I3 --> P4
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
```

## 4.4 Physical Design Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef input fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef process fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef output fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    IN1["Gate Level Netlist"]:::input
    IN2["Technology File LEF"]:::input
    IN3["Timing Liberty File"]:::input
    S1["Floorplan Aspect Ratio"]:::process
    S2["Macro Placement"]:::process
    S3["Standard Cell Placement"]:::process
    S4["Clock Tree Synthesis"]:::process
    S5["Global and Detailed Routing"]:::process
    S6["RC Extraction and STA"]:::process
    S7["DRC LVS ERC"]:::process
    OUT1["GDSII Layout File"]:::output
    OUT2["Timing Signoff Report"]:::output

    IN1 --> S1
    IN2 --> S1
    IN3 --> S4
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
    S7 --> OUT1
    S6 --> OUT2
```

## 4.5 Standard Cell Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef layer fill:#37474f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef pin fill:#bf360c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef rail fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#000,color:#fff

    L1["Metal 1 Local Routing"]:::layer
    L2["Poly Silicon Gate Layer"]:::layer
    L3["Active Diffusion N and P"]:::layer
    P1["VDD Power Rail Top"]:::rail
    P2["VSS Ground Rail Bottom"]:::rail

    PIN2["Input Pin B"]:::pin
    PIN3["Output Pin Y"]:::pin

    P1 --- L1
    P2 --- L1
    L1 --- L2
    L2 --- L3
    L3 --- PIN1
    L3 --- PIN2
    L3 --- PIN3
```
