<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A questions are short-answer conceptual problems, typically requiring a one-paragraph explanation, a labeled diagram, or a short derivation. Word limit: **80–100 words**. Use bullet points for clarity.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE). List any two advantages of MBE over conventional CVD epitaxy.**

**Model Answer:**

> Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) is an ultra-high-vacuum (UHV) thin-film deposition technique in which collimated beams of atoms or molecules from heated effusion cells (Knudsen cells) are directed onto a heated single-crystal substrate, where they undergo surface migration to form an epitaxial layer with monolayer precision.
>
> **Two advantages over CVD epitaxy:**
> 1. **Lower growth temperature** (typically 400–700 °C for Si/GaAs), reducing inter-diffusion and dopant redistribution.
> 2. **In-situ RHEED monitoring** allows real-time, sub-monolayer growth rate and surface quality feedback — impossible in CVD.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Explain the role of ultra-high vacuum (UHV) in MBE. What is the typical base pressure required?**

**Model Answer:**

> Ultra-high vacuum (UHV) is essential in MBE to ensure that thermally generated molecular beams travel from the effusion cells to the substrate without undergoing collisions with residual gas molecules. This guarantees **beam collimation**, **purity of the deposited film**, and **elimination of contaminant incorporation** (especially O₂, CO, H₂O) that would otherwise disrupt epitaxy.
>
> Typical MBE base pressure: **≤ 10⁻¹⁰ Torr** (≈ 10⁻⁸ Pa), achieved using ion pumps, cryopumps, and Ti sublimation pumps.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** (either Q.A or Q.B — internal choice is provided). Sub-part (a) typically tests theory/derivation, sub-part (b) tests numerical or application.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): MBE Process and System Architecture**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of a Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) system. Discuss the role of (i) effusion cells, (ii) mechanical shutters, and (iii) the RHEED system.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle of MBE:**

MBE operates on the principle of physical vapor deposition under UHV conditions. High-purity source materials (e.g., Ga, As, Si) are heated in effusion cells to produce collimated atomic/molecular beams. These beams impinge on a heated single-crystal substrate, where adatoms undergo surface diffusion and incorporate into the lattice in registry with the substrate — building the epitaxial film one monolayer at a time.

**Role of Key Components:**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **(i) Effusion Cells** | Resistively heated PBN crucibles that maintain source material at temperatures producing controlled vapor pressures. Multiple cells (one per element) enable **independent beam control**. |
| **(ii) Mechanical Shutters** | Pneumatic/electromechanical gates in front of each cell, switching in ~0.1 s. They enable **abrupt compositional transitions** and **δ-doping** profiles at monolayer scale. |
| **(iii) RHEED System** | Reflection High-Energy Electron Diffraction — a 5–20 keV electron gun aimed at grazing incidence onto the substrate. The diffracted pattern on a fluorescent screen reveals **surface reconstruction, growth mode (2D vs 3D), and growth rate** via **intensity oscillations** corresponding to monolayer completion. |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the mean free path of N₂ molecules (effective diameter $d = 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m) at $T = 300$ K in an MBE chamber at base pressure $p = 10^{-10}$ Torr. Comment on the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert pressure to SI:

$$p = 10^{-10} \times 133.322 = 1.333 \times 10^{-8} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the mean free path formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\, p}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$\lambda = \frac{(1.3806 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,(3.7 \times 10^{-10})^{2}\,(1.333 \times 10^{-8})}$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator:

$$\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^{2} = 1.4142 \times \pi \times 1.369 \times 10^{-19} = 6.084 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 6.084 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.333 \times 10^{-8} = 8.110 \times 10^{-27}$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:

$$\lambda = \frac{4.142 \times 10^{-21}}{8.110 \times 10^{-27}} = 5.11 \times 10^{5} \text{ m} \approx 511 \text{ km}$$

**Comment:** The mean free path is **~511 km**, which is many orders of magnitude greater than the source-to-substrate distance (~0.3 m). This confirms that N₂ molecules essentially never collide inside the chamber, validating the UHV requirement for producing collimated, contamination-free molecular beams essential for epitaxy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): MBE Flux and Growth Rate**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2 – Internal Choice Alternative]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Hertz–Knudsen equation for the atomic flux leaving an effusion cell. List any three assumptions of the derivation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium inside a Knudsen cell at temperature $T$ and vapor pressure $p$. The flux $J$ (number of molecules crossing unit area per unit time in a chosen direction) is given by the **one-sided Maxwell flux**:

$$J = \frac{1}{4}\,n\,\bar{v}$$

**Step 1:** From the ideal gas law:

$$n = \frac{p}{k_B T}$$

**Step 2:** Mean thermal speed from the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution:

$$\bar{v} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply:

$$J = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{p}{k_B T} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}} = \frac{p}{4} \sqrt{\frac{8}{\pi m k_B T}}$$

**Step 4:** Final form (Hertz–Knudsen equation):

$$\boxed{J = \frac{p}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}}$$

**Three Assumptions:**

1. **Effusive (molecular) flow regime:** The orifice is small compared to the mean free path inside the cell, so the gas leaves as non-interacting molecules (no continuum flow).
2. **Thermal equilibrium** of the source material — temperature and pressure are uniform inside the cell.
3. **Cosine law emission** — the angular distribution of effusing atoms follows $\cos\theta$ (Lambertian).
4. *(Bonus, 4th)* The source behaves as an **ideal gas** (no dissociation/association effects at the orifice).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A Ga effusion cell in an MBE system operates at $T = 1100$ K with a Ga vapor pressure of $p = 10^{-3}$ Torr. Calculate (i) the Ga atomic flux, and (ii) the monolayer deposition time on a (100) GaAs substrate. Given: Molar mass of Ga = 69.72 g/mol; lattice constant of GaAs = 5.653 Å.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Atomic Flux:**

**Step 1:** Convert pressure:

$$p = 10^{-3} \times 133.322 = 0.1333 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute atomic mass:

$$m = \frac{69.72 \times 10^{-3}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 1.158 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Hertz–Knudsen:

$$J = \frac{0.1333}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 1.158 \times 10^{-25} \cdot 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 1100}}$$

**Step 4:** Denominator:

$$2\pi \cdot 1.158 \times 10^{-25} \cdot 1.3806 \times 10^{-23} = 1.004 \times 10^{-47}$$

$$1.004 \times 10^{-47} \cdot 1100 = 1.105 \times 10^{-44}$$

$$\sqrt{1.105 \times 10^{-44}} = 1.051 \times 10^{-22}$$

**Step 5:**

$$J = \frac{0.1333}{1.051 \times 10^{-22}} = 1.27 \times 10^{21} \text{ atoms/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{s)}$$

**Part (ii) — Monolayer Time:**

For (100) GaAs, surface site density $N_s = 2/a^{2}$:

$$N_s = \frac{2}{(5.653 \times 10^{-10})^{2}} = \frac{2}{3.196 \times 10^{-19}} = 6.258 \times 10^{18} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

Wait — this should be $1/a^{2}$ for the cation site density on a (100) surface. Recomputing:

$$N_s = \frac{1}{a^{2}} = 3.129 \times 10^{18} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

(Using $2/a^2$ for full monolayer; $1/a^2$ for cation-only sublayer — both are accepted formulations.)

**Step 6:** Monolayer time:

$$t_{\text{ML}} = \frac{N_s}{J} = \frac{3.129 \times 10^{18}}{1.27 \times 10^{21}} = 2.46 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 2.5 \text{ ms}$$

> **Interpretation:** A monolayer of Ga deposits in ~2.5 ms, corresponding to a growth rate of ~400 ML/s — far above MBE's typical 1 ML/s. The instructor's value of $p = 10^{-3}$ Torr at 1100 K is high; a more realistic MBE flux gives $J \approx 10^{18}$ m⁻² s⁻¹ → $t_{\text{ML}} \approx 3$ s. (Examiner note: students should know order-of-magnitude estimates matter more than exact precision.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert pressure** from Torr to Pa. This single omission causes a factor-of-133 error and an automatic **deduction of 2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up molar mass and atomic mass** — always divide $M$ by $N_A$ to get $m$ in kg.
> 3. **Missing the $\sqrt{2}$ factor** in the mean free path equation. Some students write $\pi d^2$ without $\sqrt{2}$, losing **1 mark**.
> 4. **Not stating UHV justification** — examiners specifically look for the *mean free path >> source-to-substrate distance* argument.
> 5. **Ignoring the factor of 2** in the (100) monolayer surface density. For diamond/zincblende structures, the surface site density is $2/a^2$, not $1/a^2$.
> 6. **In diagrams**: failing to label the RHEED gun, screen, and beam path. Always include a **labeled** block diagram, not an unlabeled sketch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exams!**

### Core Conceptual Takeaways
- ✅ **Epitaxy** = ordered crystalline growth on a crystalline substrate. Two types: **homoepitaxy** (same material) and **heteroepitaxy** (different material).
- ✅ **MBE** is a **UHV-based PVD technique** using **collimated molecular beams** from independently shuttered **Knudsen cells**.
- ✅ Typical MBE base pressure: **≤ 10⁻¹⁰ Torr**.
- ✅ Typical growth rate: **0.1 – 1.0 ML/s** (≈ 0.01 – 1.0 µm/hr).
- ✅ MBE excels at **abrupt heterojunctions**, **δ-doping**, **HEMTs**, and **quantum structures** — but is **slow** compared to CVD/MOCVD.

### Key Equations (Memorize These!)
- ✅ Mean free path: $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\, p}$
- ✅ Hertz–Knudsen flux: $J = \dfrac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}$
- ✅ Growth rate: $R = \dfrac{J \cdot M}{\rho \cdot N_A}$
- ✅ Monolayer time (100): $t_{\text{ML}} = \dfrac{a}{2 R}$

### Process Essentials
- ✅ **Six stages**: source vaporization → beam collimation → shuttering → surface migration → lattice incorporation → RHEED monitoring.
- ✅ **Adatom surface diffusion** governed by $D_s = D_0 \exp(-E_d / k_B T)$ — sets the lower bound on substrate temperature.
- ✅ **Re-evaporation** sets the upper bound on substrate temperature.

### System Components Checklist
- ✅ UHV growth chamber, effusion cells, mechanical shutters, heated substrate manipulator, RHEED gun + screen, load lock, ion/cryo pumps.

### Numerical Traps
- ✅ **Always convert Torr → Pa** (multiply by 133.322).
- ✅ **Use SI units throughout** (m, kg, s, Pa).
- ✅ **Account for $\sqrt{2}$** in mean free path derivation.
- ✅ **Surface site density on (100) zincblende = $2/a^2$**, not $1/a^2$.

### High-Yield Exam Buzzwords (KTU Board Style)
- ✅ "Collimated molecular beams" — always state this, not "vapor."
- ✅ "Knudsen cell" — preferred term over "furnace" or "crucible."
- ✅ "Sub-monolayer compositional control" — key MBE advantage.
- ✅ "Layer-by-layer (Frank–van der Merwe) growth" — default MBE mode.
- ✅ "RHEED oscillations" — for in-situ growth rate monitoring.

### VLSI-Specific Applications (Write At Least 2 in Any Answer)
- ✅ SiGe raised source/drain stressors in **sub-22 nm CMOS** for channel strain engineering.
- ✅ GaN-on-Si power devices using **heteroepitaxy** despite large lattice mismatch.
- ✅ AlGaN/GaN HEMTs for **5G RF front-ends** and **electric vehicle inverters**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When the question asks "explain MBE," always structure your answer as **(1) Definition → (2) Block diagram (labeled!) → (3) Process steps → (4) Key equations → (5) VLSI application**. This 5-part structure reliably scores 12–14/14 marks under KTU's CO-based assessment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Oxidation: Dry and Wet Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Thermal Oxidation: Dry and Wet Oxidation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Thermal Oxidation** is a high-temperature (typically **800 °C – 1200 °C**) chemical process used in VLSI fabrication to grow a high-quality layer of silicon dioxide (SiO₂) on a bare silicon wafer. The wafer is exposed to an oxidizing ambient — either pure molecular oxygen (dry oxidation) or water vapor (wet oxidation) — inside a resistance-heated or radiant-heated furnace tube. The process is called *thermal* because elevated temperature is essential to provide the activation energy required for the oxidant species to diffuse through the growing SiO₂ layer and react with the silicon substrate at the Si/SiO₂ interface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Thermal oxidation is the *only* practical method of producing the "native" gate-quality insulator in silicon technology. Hence, it is the bedrock of every MOS transistor fabricated in the KTU-referenced silicon planar process flow. ### 1.2 The Two Variants | Variant | Reactant | Resulting By-product | Typical Growth Rate | |---|---|---|---| | **Dry Oxidation** | O₂ (g) | None (clean by-product profile) | **Slow** (~0.1 µm/hr @ 1000 °C) | | **Wet Oxidation** | H₂O (v) | H₂ (g) | **Fast** (~1 µm/hr @ 1000 °C) | The governing chemical reactions are: **Dry Oxidation:** $$\text{Si}_{(s)} \; + \; \text{O}_{2\,(g)} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{SiO}_{2\,(s)}$$ **Wet Oxidation:** $$\text{Si}_{(s)} \; + \; 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{SiO}_{2\,(s)} \; + \; 2\,\text{H}_{2\,(g)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** Approximately **44 %** of the final SiO₂ thickness grows *above* the original silicon surface, while **56 %** consumes the underlying silicon substrate. This ratio (the *Pilling–Bedworth ratio* for Si/SiO₂ ≈ 2.2) is critical for step-coverage calculations in subsequent lithography. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Rusting Wafer, but Engineered" Imagine leaving a clean iron nail outside in two environments: dry, cold air versus warm, humid air. In the dry air, a thin, hard, and tightly-adhered rust layer forms very slowly. In humid, warm air, a thicker rust layer forms much faster, but the layer is more porous and less dense. **Thermal oxidation behaves identically:** - A silicon wafer in a **dry O₂ furnace** is the "cold dry air" scenario — the SiO₂ grows **slowly** but produces a **dense, defect-free, high-dielectric-quality** film used as the MOS *gate oxide*. - A silicon wafer in a **wet (H₂O vapor) furnace** is the "warm humid air" scenario — SiO₂ grows **quickly** but with slightly **lower density and more fixed charge**, perfectly suited for *thick field oxide* and device isolation (LOCOS). In both cases, the oxidant must *diffuse inward* through the ever-thickening oxide shell to reach fresh silicon — this diffusion limitation is what makes the growth rate *decrease with time*, giving rise to the famous **Deal–Grove model**. > [!TIP] > **Why does wet oxidation grow faster?** The molecular size of H₂O is smaller than O₂, and its solubility and diffusivity in SiO₂ are higher. Hence, water vapor penetrates the oxide layer ~10× more easily than O₂, accelerating the surface reaction at the Si/SiO₂ interface. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Deal–Grove Growth Model (1965) In **1965**, Bruce Deal and Andrew Grove (working at Fairchild Semiconductor) published a unified model that quantitatively describes thermal oxidation kinetics. It is still the **single most important equation** every KTU VLSI student must memorize. **Assumptions of the model:** 1. The oxidant diffuses through the existing SiO₂ layer in steady state (Fick's First Law). 2. The oxidant reacts with Si only at the Si/SiO₂ interface (first-order surface reaction). 3. The SiO₂/oxidant interface is in quasi-equilibrium, so the oxidant concentration at the outer surface is the **solid solubility C\*** of the oxidant in SiO₂. 4. The silicon substrate is consumed at a fixed stoichiometric ratio. ### 2.2 The Three-Step Reaction Sequence The full oxidation process occurs in **three sequential physical steps**: **Step 1 — Bulk Diffusion (Gas Phase):** The oxidant (O₂ or H₂O) is transported from the high-velocity gas stream through the stagnant boundary layer (the *Henry layer*) above the wafer to the outer SiO₂ surface. Mass transport is typically fast and not rate-limiting. **Step 2 — Diffusive Transport Through the SiO₂ Film:** The oxidant *diffuses* through the existing oxide layer toward the Si/SiO₂ interface. Fick's law applies: $$F_1 \;=\; D \,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$ This is the **rate-limiting step** for long oxidation times (thick oxides). **Step 3 — Surface Reaction at the Si/SiO₂ Interface:** The oxidant chemically reacts with the exposed silicon lattice. First-order kinetics govern: $$F_2 \;=\; k_s \, C_i$$ where $C_i$ is the oxidant concentration at the interface. This is the **rate-limiting step** for short oxidation times (thin oxides). ### 2.3 The Master Equation (Deal–Grove) Combining the three steps and converting thickness (X₀) from concentration (C) via the molar density of SiO₂ $N_1 = 2.2 \times 10^{22}$ cm⁻³, we obtain: $$\boxed{\,X_o^2 \; + \; A\,X_o \; = \; B\,(t + \tau)\,}$$ Where: - $X_o$ = oxide thickness (µm) - $t$ = oxidation time (hr) - $\tau$ = time-shift parameter accounting for any pre-existing native oxide (~ few nm) - $B$ = **parabolic rate constant** (µm²/hr) — controls diffusion-limited regime - $B/A$ = **linear rate constant** (µm/hr) — controls reaction-limited regime - $A$ = characteristic length constant (µm), typically ≈ 0.1 µm for dry, 0.05 µm for wet ### 2.4 Limiting Regimes **Linear Regime (Thin Oxides, t → 0):** When $X_o \ll A$, the quadratic term is negligible: $$X_o \; \approx \; \frac{B}{A}\,(t + \tau)$$ **Parabolic Regime (Thick Oxides, t → ∞):** When $X_o \gg A$, the linear term is negligible: $$X_o \; \approx \; \sqrt{B\,(t + \tau)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Board Exam Insight:** The transition between linear and parabolic regimes occurs at $X_o = A$. A thinner oxide means the *surface reaction* dominates; a thicker oxide means *diffusion* dominates. As growth continues, oxidation *automatically slows down* because each new angstrom of SiO₂ lengthens the diffusion path. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Parabolic rate constant | $B = \dfrac{2 D_{eff} C^{*}}{N_1}$ | µm²/hr | Diffusion-limited growth coefficient | | Linear rate constant | $\dfrac{B}{A} = \dfrac{k_s C^{*}}{N_1}$ | µm/hr | Reaction-limited growth coefficient | | Characteristic length | $A = \dfrac{2 D_{eff}}{k_s}$ | µm | Crossover between regimes | | Arrhenius form of B | $B = B_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a^B}{kT}\right)$ | µm²/hr | Temperature dependence of B | | Arrhenius form of B/A | $\dfrac{B}{A} = \left(\dfrac{B}{A}\right)_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a^{B/A}}{kT}\right)$ | µm/hr | Temperature dependence of linear rate | | Volume expansion | $V_{SiO_2} = 2.2 \, V_{Si}$ | — | 44 % grows above surface, 56 % consumes Si | | Molar density of SiO₂ | $N_1$ | cm⁻³ | $2.2 \times 10^{22}$ cm⁻³ | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Table Symbol Rule:** Whenever a formula contains an absolute value or *vertical bar*, write `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX — never use the raw `|` character — to prevent breaking the markdown table grid. ### 2.6 Arrhenius Parameters (Memorize for KTU Numericals) For **〈100〉 orientation silicon** (the industry-standard orientation): | Ambient | $B$ (µm²/hr) | $B/A$ (µm/hr) | Activation Energy $E_a^B$ | Activation Energy $E_a^{B/A}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Dry O₂** | $B_0 = 0.107$ | $(B/A)_0 = 0.0298$ | **1.32 eV** | **1.76 eV** | | **Wet H₂O** | $B_0 = 0.272$ | $(B/A)_0 = 0.0494$ | **1.19 eV** | **0.93 eV** | > **Engineering takeaway:** Wet oxidation has a *lower* activation energy for the linear rate constant — that is precisely *why* it is so much faster at moderate temperatures. ### 2.7 Dry vs Wet Oxidation — Engineering Trade-off Summary | Property | Dry Oxidation | Wet Oxidation | |---|---|---| | Growth rate | **Slow** | **Fast (≈ 5–10× faster)** | | Oxide density | High, dense | Slightly lower | | Breakdown field | High (~ 10 MV/cm) | Moderate | | Fixed oxide charge $Q_f$ | Low | Higher | | Typical thickness range | 5 – 120 nm (thin/gate oxide) | 100 nm – 1 µm (field oxide) | | Process control | Excellent | Good | | H₂ byproduct | None | Yes (can degrade vacuum pumps) | | Major application | Gate dielectric, tunnel oxide | Field oxide (LOCOS), pad oxide | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Deal–Grove Master Equation **Step 1 — Establish the flux continuity.** In steady state, the flux of oxidant arriving at the outer SiO₂ surface (Step 1) must equal the flux diffusing through the oxide (Step 2), which must equal the flux consumed by the surface reaction at the Si interface (Step 3): $$F_1 \;=\; F_2 \;=\; F_3 \;=\; F$$ **Step 2 — Apply Fick's First Law to the oxide layer.** The oxidant concentration drops linearly from $C^*$ (at the outer surface) to $C_i$ (at the Si interface) over the oxide thickness $X_o$: $$F_1 \;=\; D_{eff}\,\frac{C^{*} - C_i}{X_o}$$ where $D_{eff}$ is the effective diffusion coefficient of the oxidant in SiO₂. **Step 3 — Apply first-order reaction kinetics at the interface:** $$F_2 \;=\; k_s\,C_i$$ **Step 4 — Eliminate $C_i$ by combining Steps 2 and 3.** From Step 3: $$C_i \;=\; \frac{F_2}{k_s} \;=\; \frac{F}{k_s}$$ Substitute into Step 2: $$F \;=\; D_{eff}\,\frac{C^{*} - F/k_s}{X_o}$$ Multiply through by $X_o$: $$F\,X_o \;=\; D_{eff}\,C^{*} \;-\; \frac{D_{eff}\,F}{k_s}$$ Collect the $F$ terms on the left: $$F\left(X_o + \frac{D_{eff}}{k_s}\right) \;=\; D_{eff}\,C^{*}$$ Therefore: $$F \;=\; \frac{D_{eff}\,C^{*}}{X_o + D_{eff}/k_s}$$ **Step 5 — Relate flux to oxide growth rate.** Each SiO₂ molecule formed requires $N_1$ oxidant molecules per unit volume, so: $$\frac{dX_o}{dt} \;=\; \frac{F}{N_1} \;=\; \frac{D_{eff}\,C^{*}}{N_1\,(X_o + D_{eff}/k_s)}$$ **Step 6 — Identify the constants B and A.** Define: $$B \;\equiv\; \frac{2 D_{eff}\,C^{*}}{N_1} \qquad A \;\equiv\; \frac{2 D_{eff}}{k_s}$$ Substitute these definitions and multiply numerator and denominator by 2: $$\frac{dX_o}{dt} \;=\; \frac{B/2}{X_o + A/2}$$ **Step 7 — Separate variables and integrate.** $$2(X_o + A/2)\,dX_o \;=\; B\,dt$$ $$\int_0^{X_o} 2\!\left(X + \frac{A}{2}\right)dX \;=\; \int_0^{t+\tau} B\,dT$$ $$X_o^2 \;+\; A\,X_o \;=\; B\,(t + \tau)$$ This is the celebrated **Deal–Grove quadratic equation**. The integration constant $\tau$ is added to account for the finite time required to grow the initial native oxide layer (~ 1–2 nm). **Step 8 — Solve explicitly for $X_o$:** $$\boxed{\,X_o \;=\; \frac{-A + \sqrt{A^2 + 4B(t+\tau)}}{2}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Note:** In numerical problems, students must explicitly state that the *negative root* is discarded because oxide thickness is physically positive. This is a common place to lose 1–2 marks. ### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example **Problem (Typical KTU Module 2 Question):** A silicon wafer is oxidized in **dry O₂ at 1000 °C** for **2 hours**. Given for 〈100〉 silicon at 1000 °C: $B = 0.165$ µm²/hr, $B/A = 0.0117$ µm/hr. Calculate the final oxide thickness (neglect $\tau$). **Solution:** **Step (i) — Compute A.** $$A \;=\; \frac{B}{B/A} \;=\; \frac{0.165}{0.0117} \;=\; 14.10 \; \mu m$$ **Step (ii) — Check regime.** Estimate $X_o \approx \sqrt{Bt} = \sqrt{0.165 \times 2} = \sqrt{0.33} \approx 0.574$ µm. Since $X_o \ll A$, the **linear regime** applies. **Step (iii) — Apply the linear approximation.** $$X_o \; \approx \; \frac{B}{A}\,(t + \tau) \;=\; 0.0117 \times 2 \;=\; 0.0234 \; \mu m$$ **Step (iv) — Sanity check with the full Deal–Grove equation.** $$X_o \;=\; \frac{-14.10 + \sqrt{14.10^2 + 4(0.165)(2)}}{2}$$ $$=\; \frac{-14.10 + \sqrt{198.81 + 1.32}}{2}$$ $$=\; \frac{-14.10 + \sqrt{200.13}}{2}$$ $$=\; \frac{-14.10 + 14.147}{2}$$ $$=\; \frac{0.047}{2} \;=\; 0.0235 \; \mu m$$ **Result: $X_o \approx 0.0235$ µm = 23.5 nm** — which makes physical sense for dry oxidation at 1000 °C for 2 hours. ### 3.3 Comparison Problem — Switching from Wet to Dry A **100 nm** SiO₂ layer is needed for a gate oxide application. Compare the time required for dry and wet oxidation at 1000 °C. Use the same $B$ and $B/A$ values as above and: - **Wet** at 1000 °C: $B = 0.480$ µm²/hr, $B/A = 0.082$ µm/hr. **Step (i) — Compute A for both ambients.** $$A_{dry} \;=\; \frac{0.165}{0.0117} \;=\; 14.10 \; \mu m$$ $$A_{wet} \;=\; \frac{0.480}{0.082} \;=\; 5.85 \; \mu m$$ **Step (ii) — Solve the Deal–Grove equation for $t$ when $X_o = 0.1$ µm.** Rearranging the master equation: $$t \;=\; \frac{X_o^2 + A\,X_o}{B} \;-\; \tau$$ **Step (iii) — Dry oxidation time.** $$t_{dry} \;=\; \frac{(0.1)^2 + (14.10)(0.1)}{0.165} \;=\; \frac{0.01 + 1.410}{0.165} \;=\; \frac{1.420}{0.165} \;\approx\; 8.61 \; \text{hr}$$ **Step (iv) — Wet oxidation time.** $$t_{wet} \;=\; \frac{(0.1)^2 + (5.85)(0.1)}{0.480} \;=\; \frac{0.01 + 0.585}{0.480} \;=\; \frac{0.595}{0.480} \;\approx\; 1.24 \; \text{hr}$$ **Result:** Dry oxidation requires **~7× longer** than wet oxidation to grow the same 100 nm oxide — a vivid illustration of the engineering trade-off in selecting the ambient. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Where the KTU Examiner Picks Questions | Question Type | Likely KTU Marks | Core Concept Tested | |---|---|---| | Define dry vs wet oxidation with reactions | 3 | Chemical fundamentals | | State & explain Deal–Grove equation | 7 | Model knowledge | | Derive Deal–Grove from Fick's law | 7–10 | Mathematical depth | | Compute $X_o$ given $t$ and ambients | 7 | Numerical application | | Compare oxidation times for two ambients | 14 | Engineering trade-off | | Sketch linear vs parabolic regimes | 3–4 | Conceptual clarity | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Three-Step Deal–Grove Physical Model This block diagram captures the three coupled rate-determining physical processes in thermal oxidation. Each step has a transport mechanism, a governing equation, and an identifying rate constant. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Gas Phase
O2 or H2O vapor]:::gas -->|Step 1 Bulk Diffusion| B[Oxide Outer Surface
C equals C star]:::surf B -->|Step 2 Diffusive Transport
through SiO2 layer| C[SiO2 Si Interface
C equals Ci]:::interface C -->|Step 3 Surface Reaction
Si plus O2 or H2O| D[Fresh Si Consumed
SiO2 grows]:::reaction B -.F1.-> B C -.F2.-> C D -.F3.-> D classDef gas fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 classDef surf fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 classDef interface fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#e64a19,color:#000 classDef reaction fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The leftmost node is the furnace ambient — pure O₂ for dry or H₂O vapor for wet. - The middle nodes represent the two key physical boundaries — the outer SiO₂ surface (where $C = C^*$, the solid solubility) and the Si/SiO₂ interface (where the chemistry actually happens). - The rightmost node is the chemical reaction site — the consumed Si that becomes part of the growing glass. ### 4.2 Linear and Parabolic Regimes — Schematic Growth Curve The following block-graph illustrates the two kinetic regimes and the famous $A$ "crossover length" where the regime transition occurs. ```mermaid flowchart TD T0[Time equals 0
Xo equals 0]:::start --> L1[Linear Regime
Xo approximately B over A times t
Surface reaction controlled]:::linear L1 --> CROSS{Crossover at
Xo equals A}:::cross CROSS --> P1[Parabolic Regime
Xo approximately square root of B t
Diffusion controlled]:::parab P1 --> END[Saturation
Growth slows to 1 over square root t]:::end L1 -. Fast initial growth .-> CROSS P1 -. Slow long term growth .-> END classDef start fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 classDef linear fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef cross fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 classDef parab fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000 classDef end fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thermal Oxidation Furnace The Mermaid safe-fallback architecture for the *physical equipment* involved: ```mermaid flowchart LR GAS[Gas Panel
O2 and H2 and N2 and H2O]:::gas --> MFC[Mass Flow Controllers
MFC1 and MFC2]:::mfc MFC --> FURN[Horizontal Furnace Tube
Temperature 800 to 1200 C]:::furn FURN --> WAF[Quartz Boat with
Silicon Wafers]:::wafer WAF --> SCRUB[Exhaust Scrubber
H2 and acid removal]:::scrub FURN -.Heating elements.-> CTRL[PID Temperature
Controller]:::ctrl CTRL -.Feedback.-> FURN classDef gas fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 classDef mfc fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 classDef furn fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#e64a19,color:#000 classDef wafer fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 classDef scrub fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Dry vs Wet Oxidation — Engineering Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD START{Need an oxide
layer}:::start --> Q1{Desired thickness}:::q Q1 -->|Less than 100 nm| GATE[Gate Oxide
or Tunnel Oxide]:::gate Q1 -->|More than 200 nm| FLD[Field Oxide
or Pad Oxide]:::fld GATE --> DRY[Use Dry Oxidation
O2 ambient
Slow but high quality]:::dry FLD --> WET[Use Wet Oxidation
H2O vapor
Fast thick growth]:::wet DRY --> APP1[Application:
MOSFET gate dielectric
Memory tunnel oxide]:::app1 WET --> APP2[Application:
LOCOS isolation
Sacrificial oxide]:::app2 classDef start fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 classDef q fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 classDef gate fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 classDef fld fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#e64a19,color:#000 classDef dry fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 classDef wet fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c,color:#000 classDef app1 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000 classDef app2 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1.** Distinguish between dry and wet thermal oxidation of silicon. Write the chemical reactions involved in each case. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Dry Oxidation | Wet Oxidation | |---|---|---| | Oxidant | Pure molecular O₂ | Water vapor (H₂O) | | Chemical reaction | $\text{Si} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{SiO}_2$ | $\text{Si} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2$ | | Growth rate | Slow | Fast (~ 5–10× dry) | | Oxide quality | Excellent (low $Q_f$, high density) | Good (higher fixed charge) | | By-product | None | H₂ gas | | Application | Gate oxide | Field / pad oxide | | Typical temperature | 900 – 1100 °C | 800 – 1000 °C | --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2.** What is meant by "linear" and "parabolic" oxidation regimes in the Deal–Grove model? On what factor does the transition between them depend? **Model Answer:** In the Deal–Grove model, oxide growth is governed by: $X_o^2 + A X_o = B(t + \tau)$. - **Linear regime** (thin oxide, $X_o \ll A$): surface reaction at the Si interface is the rate-limiting step. Growth rate is *constant* with time: $X_o \approx (B/A) t$. - **Parabolic regime** (thick oxide, $X_o \gg A$): diffusion of oxidant through SiO₂ is rate-limiting. Growth rate *decreases* as $X_o \approx \sqrt{Bt}$. **Transition condition:** occurs at $X_o \approx A$, the characteristic length constant. The transition depends on (a) temperature, (b) oxidant species (dry vs wet), and (c) silicon crystal orientation. --- ### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the **Deal–Grove equation** $X_o^2 + A X_o = B(t + \tau)$ for thermal oxidation of silicon. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a wet oxidation at 1000 °C, the parabolic and linear rate constants for 〈100〉 Si are $B = 0.480$ µm²/hr and $B/A = 0.082$ µm/hr. Calculate: (i) the time required to grow **0.6 µm** of SiO₂, and (ii) the oxide thickness grown in **30 minutes**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation:** *(Write the full 7-step derivation given in Section 3.1 of these notes. State the three assumptions: steady-state diffusion, first-order surface reaction, equilibrium at the outer surface. Combine Fick's Law and reaction kinetics. Eliminate $C_i$ and integrate to obtain the master equation.)* **Part (b) — Numerical:** **Step (i) — Find A.** $$A = \frac{B}{B/A} = \frac{0.480}{0.082} = 5.854 \; \mu m$$ **Step (ii) — Solve for time when $X_o = 0.6$ µm.** $$t + \tau = \frac{X_o^2 + A X_o}{B} = \frac{(0.6)^2 + (5.854)(0.6)}{0.480} = \frac{0.36 + 3.512}{0.480} = \frac{3.872}{0.480} = 8.07 \; \text{hr}$$ **Answer (i): $t \approx 8.07$ hours** (or 8 hr 4 min). **Step (iii) — Solve for $X_o$ when $t = 0.5$ hr.** $$X_o^2 + 5.854\,X_o = 0.480 \times 0.5 = 0.240$$ $$X_o^2 + 5.854\,X_o - 0.240 = 0$$ Using the quadratic formula (positive root only): $$X_o = \frac{-5.854 + \sqrt{(5.854)^2 + 4(0.240)}}{2}$$ $$= \frac{-5.854 + \sqrt{34.270 + 0.960}}{2} = \frac{-5.854 + \sqrt{35.230}}{2}$$ $$= \frac{-5.854 + 5.935}{2} = \frac{0.081}{2} = 0.0405 \; \mu m$$ **Answer (ii): $X_o \approx 40.5$ nm** in 30 minutes. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **three-step physical model** of thermal oxidation. Identify the rate-limiting step in the linear and parabolic regimes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **120 nm** SiO₂ layer must be grown on a 〈100〉 silicon wafer. Compare the oxidation time required at **1000 °C** in: (i) **Dry O₂** ($B = 0.165$ µm²/hr, $B/A = 0.0117$ µm/hr) (ii) **Wet H₂O** ($B = 0.480$ µm²/hr, $B/A = 0.082$ µm/hr) Neglect the initial oxide time correction $\tau$. Which ambient is preferable for a **gate oxide** and why? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Three-Step Model:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram. Describe Step 1: gas-phase mass transport of oxidant to the wafer surface — usually fast and non-rate-limiting. Step 2: diffusion of oxidant through the existing SiO₂ film governed by Fick's First Law — rate-limiting in the parabolic regime. Step 3: chemical reaction of oxidant with Si at the Si/SiO₂ interface following first-order kinetics — rate-limiting in the linear regime. Include a labelled cross-sectional sketch showing the three regions.)* **Part (b) — Numerical Comparison:** **Step (i) — Dry oxidation time for $X_o = 0.12$ µm.** $$A_{dry} = \frac{0.165}{0.0117} = 14.10 \; \mu m$$ $$t_{dry} = \frac{(0.12)^2 + (14.10)(0.12)}{0.165} = \frac{0.0144 + 1.692}{0.165} = \frac{1.7064}{0.165} = 10.34 \; \text{hr}$$ **Step (ii) — Wet oxidation time for $X_o = 0.12$ µm.** $$A_{wet} = \frac{0.480}{0.082} = 5.854 \; \mu m$$ $$t_{wet} = \frac{(0.12)^2 + (5.854)(0.12)}{0.480} = \frac{0.0144 + 0.7025}{0.480} = \frac{0.7169}{0.480} = 1.49 \; \text{hr}$$ **Step (iii) — Engineering Recommendation.** **Dry O₂ is preferable for gate oxide** applications because: 1. **Higher oxide quality** — denser film, lower fixed charge density $Q_f$, and superior interface trap density $D_{it}$. 2. **Better thickness uniformity** across the wafer, critical for ultra-thin gate dielectrics (sub-10 nm in modern nodes). 3. **Higher breakdown field strength** (~ 10 MV/cm), essential for reliable MOSFET operation. 4. **Better dielectric strength and stoichiometric purity** (no H-related traps from H₂O by-products). The longer oxidation time (~ 10 hr vs 1.5 hr) is an acceptable trade-off because gate oxide area is small and process control is paramount. Wet oxidation would be reserved for **thick field oxide** (~ 500–1000 nm) where speed matters more than atomic-level purity. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Forgetting to discard the negative root** in the quadratic Deal–Grove solution — *–0.5 marks*. > 2. **Mixing up B and B/A** — students often write $t = X_o^2 / B$ when the linear regime is actually active. *–1 to 2 marks*. > 3. **Forgetting to compute A** explicitly in numerical problems. A is not a "given"; it must be derived as $B / (B/A)$. *–1 mark*. > 4. **Not stating the rate-limiting step** explicitly for each regime. *–1 mark*. > 5. **Confusing dry/wet $B$ and $B/A$ values** — use the **〈100〉 silicon** column from the cheat sheet; 〈111〉 values differ. *–1 mark*. > 6. **Omitting units** in the final answer — always write µm or nm explicitly. *–0.5 marks*. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Thermal oxidation** is the high-temperature growth of SiO₂ on silicon using either **dry O₂** or **wet H₂O** as the oxidant. - **Dry oxidation** is slow but produces a **dense, high-quality** oxide used as the **MOS gate dielectric**. - **Wet oxidation** is fast but yields a slightly less dense oxide used for **field oxide** and **LOCOS isolation**. - Chemical reactions: Dry — $\text{Si} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{SiO}_2$; Wet — $\text{Si} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2$. - The **Deal–Grove equation** $X_o^2 + A X_o = B(t+\tau)$ is the **single most important model** in KTU Module 2. - **B (parabolic rate constant)** governs *diffusion-limited* growth — dominant for **thick oxides**. - **B/A (linear rate constant)** governs *surface-reaction-limited* growth — dominant for **thin oxides**. - The transition between linear and parabolic regimes occurs at $X_o \approx A$. - **A** must always be calculated as $A = B / (B/A)$ — it is **not** a separately tabulated constant. - The **Arrhenius form** of B and B/A is essential for temperature-scaling problems: $B = B_0 \exp(-E_a^B/kT)$. - **Pilling–Bedworth ratio** for Si/SiO₂ is **2.2** — meaning 44 % of SiO₂ grows above the original Si surface and 56 % consumes the substrate. - The **〈100〉 orientation** is the industry standard; 〈111〉 has roughly 2× the linear rate constant but higher $Q_f$. - For numericals: **always** solve the full quadratic and discard the negative root; then verify the regime (linear vs parabolic) by checking whether $X_o$ is smaller or larger than $A$. - A typical **gate oxide** is grown **dry** at 800–1000 °C to a thickness of **2–20 nm**. - A typical **field oxide** is grown **wet** at 950–1100 °C to a thickness of **300–1000 nm**.

topics.title: Diffusion and Ion Implantation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diffusion and Ion Implantation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Diffusion — The Formal Definition

**Diffusion** in VLSI technology is the thermally activated, solid-state physical process by which impurity atoms (dopants such as Boron, Phosphorus, or Arsenic) migrate from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration inside a semiconductor crystal (typically Silicon) at elevated temperatures (typically **800 °C to 1200 °C**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBECT604):** Diffusion is a high-temperature process in which dopant atoms move through the silicon lattice via thermal energy, governed by Fick's laws, and is used to form p-n junctions, wells, and buried layers.

It is mathematically described by the **diffusion coefficient D** (in cm²/s), which follows the Arrhenius relation:

$$D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{kT}\right)$$

where **D₀** is the pre-exponential factor, **Eₐ** is the activation energy, **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** is the Boltzmann constant, and **T** is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

### 1.2 Ion Implantation — The Formal Definition

**Ion Implantation** is a low-temperature (room temperature to ~100 °C), highly controlled, non-equilibrium process in which ionized dopant atoms are accelerated by a high electric field (typically **10 keV to 1 MeV**) and physically "shot" into the silicon substrate. The depth of penetration is precisely controlled by the accelerating voltage, and the dopant concentration is precisely controlled by the integrated ion current (dose).

> [!NOTE]
> Ion Implantation is the **dominant doping technique in modern sub-100 nm CMOS technology** because it offers superior dose control, uniformity, reproducibility, and the ability to implant through thin oxides.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "How a First-Year Student Should Picture It"

> [!TIP]
> **Diffusion Analogy — The Ink Drop in Water:**
> Imagine dropping a single drop of red ink into a still glass of water. The ink molecules don't "move" because of an external force — they randomly jiggle (Brownian motion), and *statistically* spread out from where they are concentrated to where they are not. Diffusion in silicon is exactly this — except the "ink" is Boron or Phosphorus atoms, and the "water" is the hot silicon crystal lattice. Temperature is what controls how fast the molecules jiggle, and hence how fast the spreading happens.
>
> **Ion Implantation Analogy — The Ballistic Shooter:**
> Now imagine a machine that fires tiny bullets (ions) at a target (silicon wafer) with a chosen velocity (accelerating voltage). The faster you fire, the deeper the bullets penetrate. If you fire 10¹⁵ bullets per cm², you know exactly how many dopant atoms are inside. The bullets land with a statistical spread (because they are slowed by random collisions), forming a bell-shaped (Gaussian) profile beneath the surface.

### 1.4 The Two Faces of the Same Coin — Why VLSI Needs Both

| Process | Temperature | Profile Shape | Dose Control | Modern Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Diffusion | High (900–1200 °C) | Gradual (erfc / Gaussian) | Indirect (via time/temp) | Drive-in, deep wells, some power devices |
| Ion Implantation | Room temperature | Gaussian (Rp, ΔRp) | Direct (current integration) | Shallow junctions, threshold adjust, poly doping, S/D extension |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Diffusion and Ion Implantation are *competing* processes. In reality, in a modern CMOS process flow, both are used **sequentially and complementarily** — Ion Implantation places the dopants precisely, and a subsequent thermal anneal (RTA) simultaneously activates the dopants and heals the implant damage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fick's Two Laws — The Heart of Diffusion

Diffusion theory in silicon rests on two empirical laws formulated by Adolf Fick in 1855.

#### 2.1.1 Fick's First Law (Steady-State Diffusion)

The flux **J** (atoms per cm² per second) of dopant atoms is proportional to the negative concentration gradient:

$$\vec{J} = -D \,\frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial x}$$

- **J** = diffusion flux (atoms/cm²·s)
- **D** = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- **C(x,t)** = dopant concentration at depth x and time t (atoms/cm³)
- The negative sign indicates atoms flow from **high to low** concentration (down the gradient).

> [!NOTE]
> Fick's First Law applies to **steady-state** conditions where ∂C/∂t = 0. In VLSI processes, this is rarely the case — the concentration profile keeps evolving with time. Hence we need the Second Law.

#### 2.1.2 Fick's Second Law (Time-Dependent Diffusion)

By combining Fick's First Law with the continuity equation (conservation of mass), we obtain the diffusion equation:

$$\frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^{2} C(x,t)}{\partial x^{2}}$$

- This is a **second-order partial differential equation (PDE)** whose solution depends on the **boundary conditions** of the diffusion process.
- The two classical boundary conditions correspond to two industrial processes: **Constant Source** and **Limited Source**.

### 2.2 The Two Industrial Diffusion Modes

#### 2.2.1 Constant Source Diffusion (Predeposition / Infinite Source)

In this mode, the wafer surface is exposed to an **unlimited reservoir** of dopant (e.g., a solid BN source, POCl₃ vapor, or a doped-oxide layer). The surface concentration **C_s** is held constant at the **solid solubility limit** for the entire diffusion duration.

**Boundary conditions:**
- C(0, t) = C_s (constant surface concentration)
- C(x, 0) = C_B (initial background concentration, usually ≪ C_s)
- C(∞, t) = C_B

**Solution (for a semi-infinite wafer with C_B ≈ 0):**

$$C(x,t) = C_s \,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

The **erfc** (complementary error function) is defined as:

$$\mathrm{erfc}(z) = 1 - \mathrm{erf}(z) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_{z}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}} \, du$$

**Junction depth** (where C(x_j) = N_B, the substrate doping):

$$x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt}\;\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{C_s}{N_B}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key observation: **x_j ∝ √t** (not linear with t). This is a **high-yield KTU point** — doubling the time increases the junction depth by only √2 ≈ 1.414×, not 2×.

#### 2.2.2 Limited Source Diffusion (Drive-in / Finite Source)

After predeposition, the dopant-rich surface layer is stripped off, and the wafer is re-heated. The total dopant dose **Q** (atoms/cm²) is now **fixed** and gets redistributed deeper into the wafer.

**Boundary conditions:**
- ∫₀^∞ C(x, t) dx = Q (constant total dose)
- C(∞, t) = 0
- C(x, 0) = Q·δ(x) (initially a δ-function at surface)

**Solution:**

$$C(x,t) = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}}{4Dt}\right)$$

This is a **Gaussian (bell-shaped) profile** with peak at the surface (x = 0):

$$C(0,t) = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}}$$

**Junction depth:**

$$x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt\,\ln\!\left(\frac{Q}{N_B \sqrt{\pi D t}}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare with constant source: in limited source, the surface concentration **decreases** with time, while in constant source, the surface concentration **stays pinned** at C_s.

### 2.3 Ion Implantation — Theory

#### 2.3.1 Range, Projected Range, and Straggle

When an ion of mass **M₁** and energy **E** enters the silicon crystal, it undergoes a **statistical series of nuclear and electronic collisions**, losing energy until it comes to rest. The total path length travelled is called the **range R**. The component of R measured along the **perpendicular to the wafer surface** is called the **Projected Range R_p** (depth of peak concentration). The standard deviation of the distribution is the **Straggle ΔR_p**.

The dopant distribution is modeled as a **Gaussian**:

$$C(x) = \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-R_p)^{2}}{2\,\Delta R_p^{2}}\right)$$

where **φ** (phi) is the implanted **dose** in atoms/cm² and **x** is measured from the wafer surface.

**Peak concentration** (at x = R_p):

$$C_{\text{peak}} = \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p} \approx \frac{0.4\,\phi}{\Delta R_p}$$

> [!TIP]
> **For a quick exam sanity check:** If φ = 10¹⁵ cm⁻² and ΔR_p = 100 nm = 10⁻⁶ cm, then C_peak ≈ 0.4 × 10¹⁵ / 10⁻⁶ = 4 × 10²⁰ cm⁻³, which is realistic for a heavy source/drain implant.

#### 2.3.2 Channeling Effect

If the ion beam enters the silicon crystal **parallel to a major crystallographic axis** (e.g., ⟨100⟩, ⟨110⟩), it experiences **fewer nuclear collisions** because it slides through the open channels between atomic rows. This causes:
- **Deeper penetration** than expected
- A **long tail** in the concentration profile
- **Poor dopant depth control**

**Mitigation strategies:**
1. **Tilt the wafer** by 7°–10° off the major axis during implantation.
2. **Amorphize the surface first** with a pre-implant of Si⁺ or Ge⁺ ions.
3. Use a **screen oxide** (~10–20 nm) to randomize the beam direction.

#### 2.3.3 Annealing (Damage Repair + Dopant Activation)

Ion bombardment creates **crystal defects** (vacancies, interstitials, dislocation loops) and the dopants sit on **interstitial (non-substitutional)** sites where they are **electrically inactive**. A post-implant anneal is required to:
1. **Repair the damage** (recrystallization).
2. **Activate the dopants** (move them to substitutional sites, freeing carriers).
3. **Redistribute** the dopants (controlled, minimal).

**Two annealing regimes:**

| Anneal Type | Temp | Time | Profile Control | Activation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Furnace Anneal | 800–1000 °C | 30–60 min | Poor (heavy diffusion) | Excellent |
| Rapid Thermal Anneal (RTA) | 1000–1100 °C | 1–10 s | Excellent (minimal diffusion) | Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In sub-100 nm CMOS, **RTA (specifically Spike Anneal or Flash Anneal)** is mandatory. Furnace anneal would smear out the ultra-shallow junctions (~10–20 nm).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's 1st Law | J = −D ∂C/∂x | atoms/cm²·s | Steady state |
| 2 | Fick's 2nd Law | ∂C/∂t = D ∂²C/∂x² | atoms/cm³·s | Time-dependent, D assumed constant |
| 3 | Arrhenius Diffusion | D = D₀ exp(−Eₐ/kT) | cm²/s | Higher T ⇒ exponentially larger D |
| 4 | Constant Source Profile | C(x,t) = C_s erfc[x/(2√(Dt))] | atoms/cm³ | C_s pinned at solid solubility |
| 5 | Constant Source x_j | x_j = 2√(Dt) erfc⁻¹(C_s/N_B) | cm | x_j ∝ √t |
| 6 | Limited Source Profile | C(x,t) = (Q/√(πDt)) exp[−x²/(4Dt)] | atoms/cm³ | Gaussian shape, total dose Q fixed |
| 7 | erfc function | erfc(z) = (2/√π) ∫_z^∞ e^(−u²) du | dimensionless | erfc(0)=1, erfc(∞)=0, erfc(1)=0.157 |
| 8 | Implantation Gaussian | C(x) = (φ/√(2π)ΔR_p) exp[−(x−R_p)²/(2ΔR_p²)] | atoms/cm³ | Peak at x = R_p |
| 9 | Peak Concentration | C_peak = 0.4 φ / ΔR_p | atoms/cm³ | Approx. valid for unimplanted surface |
| 10 | Total Dose | φ = ∫₀^∞ C(x) dx | atoms/cm² | Beam current × time / (q × area) |
| 11 | Projected Range (LSS) | R_p ≈ (constant) · Eᵏ | cm | Empirical power-law fit |
| 12 | Boltzmann constant | k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ | J/K | Universal |
| 13 | Silicon permittivity | ε_s = 11.7 × ε₀ = 1.04 × 10⁻¹² | F/cm | ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴ F/cm |
| 14 | Oxidation-Enhanced Diffusion (OED) | D_eff = D₀ + ΔD_OED | cm²/s | Occurs in oxidizing ambient (SiO₂ growth injects Si interstitials) |
| 15 | Sheet Resistance | R_s = 1/(q·μ·N_s·t) | Ω/sq | N_s = sheet carrier conc., t = junction depth |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMOS Threshold Voltage (V_T) Tuning:** Boron implantation through the gate oxide precisely adjusts the channel doping → sets V_T to the design target.
- **Shallow Junction Formation:** As + implant at 5–20 keV with RTA → source/drain extensions in 45 nm and below nodes.
- **Retrograde Wells:** High-energy (1 MeV) implants used to form deep n-wells and p-wells to suppress latch-up.
- **Halo / Pocket Implants:** Tilted implants that suppress short-channel effects in MOSFETs.
- **Polysilicon Gate Doping:** Heavily-doped poly gates for reduced gate depletion in advanced nodes.
- **Solar Cells / Power Devices:** Phosphorus diffusion is still used in industrial solar cell manufacturing for cost reasons.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fick's Second Law from First Law + Continuity

We begin with Fick's First Law and combine it with the law of conservation of matter.

**Step 1:** Write the continuity equation. The rate of increase of dopant concentration in a thin slab of thickness dx between x and x + dx must equal the net flux into that slab:

$$\frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial t}\,dx = J(x) - J(x+dx)$$

**Step 2:** Expand J(x + dx) using a Taylor series (to first order):

$$J(x+dx) = J(x) + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx + \mathcal{O}(dx^{2})$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t}\,dx = J(x) - \left[J(x) + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx\right] = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}\,dx$$

**Step 4:** Cancel dx on both sides:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute Fick's First Law, J = −D ∂C/∂x, into the right-hand side. Assuming **D is independent of x** (constant D approximation, valid for low doping where the dopant does not perturb the lattice):

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) = D\,\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

This is **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**, a linear parabolic PDE whose solution gives the doping profile after any heat treatment.

> [!NOTE]
> If D depends on C (concentration-dependent diffusion, common for high-concentration Boron/Phosphorus), the equation becomes a **non-linear** PDE: ∂C/∂t = ∂/∂x (D(C) ∂C/∂x), and must be solved numerically using process simulators like SUPREM-IV, ATHENA, or Sentaurus.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constant Source Diffusion Profile

We solve Fick's Second Law under the constant-source boundary conditions.

**Step 1 — Use similarity variable.** The PDE ∂C/∂t = D ∂²C/∂x² has the form of a 1-D heat equation. We try a similarity transform by introducing the dimensionless variable:

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$

**Step 2 — Verify by chain rule.** Compute the derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = -\frac{x}{4\sqrt{D}\,t^{3/2}} = -\frac{\eta}{2t}, \qquad \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = \frac{dC}{d\eta}\,\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = -\frac{\eta}{2t}\,\frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\,\frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{4Dt}\,\frac{d^{2} C}{d\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the PDE:**

$$-\frac{\eta}{2t}\,\frac{dC}{d\eta} = D \cdot \frac{1}{4Dt}\,\frac{d^{2} C}{d\eta^{2}}$$

The factor D cancels on both sides, and we get the **ordinary differential equation (ODE)**:

$$\frac{d^{2} C}{d\eta^{2}} + 2\eta\,\frac{dC}{d\eta} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the ODE.** This is a linear second-order ODE. We can write it as:

$$\frac{d}{d\eta}\!\left(\frac{dC}{d\eta}\right) = -2\eta\,\frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

Let p = dC/dη, then dp/dη = −2η p ⇒ dp/p = −2η dη. Integrating:

$$\ln \vert p \vert = -\eta^{2} + \text{const} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = A\,e^{-\eta^{2}}$$

Integrating once more:

$$C(\eta) = A\int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}} du + B = A \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,\mathrm{erf}(\eta) + B$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions.**

- BC1: At x = 0 ⇒ η = 0, C(0) = C_s. Since erf(0) = 0, we get B = C_s.
- BC2: As x → ∞ ⇒ η → ∞, C → 0. Since erf(∞) = 1, we get:

$$0 = A \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot 1 + C_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -\frac{2 C_s}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

**Step 6 — Final assembly:**

$$C(x,t) = C_s - \frac{2C_s}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = C_s \left[1 - \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\right]$$

$$C(x,t) = C_s \,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gaussian Profile of Ion Implantation

**Step 1 — Physical assumption.** Each implanted ion undergoes a statistically independent sequence of collisions. By the **Central Limit Theorem** (the sum of many small random steps → Gaussian), the final depth distribution is Gaussian.

**Step 2 — Normalization.** The probability density for an ion to stop at depth x is:

$$P(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-R_p)^{2}}{2\Delta R_p^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by dose.** The number of ions per unit volume at depth x equals the dose per unit area times the probability of stopping at x:

$$C(x) = \phi \cdot P(x) = \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-R_p)^{2}}{2\Delta R_p^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Verify total dose.** Integrate over all depths from 0 to ∞:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} C(x)\,dx = \phi \int_{0}^{\infty} P(x)\,dx \approx \phi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(x)\,dx = \phi \cdot 1 = \phi \quad \blacksquare$$

(The integral is ≈ φ because the Gaussian has effectively zero probability in x < 0, since ΔR_p ≪ R_p for non-channeled implants.)

> [!NOTE]
> A more accurate model uses a **Pearson-IV** or **dual-Pearson** distribution to capture the asymmetric channeling tail. Production process simulators (Synopsys Sentaurus, TSUPREM-4) use these higher-order moment expansions.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Constant Source Diffusion)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 style]:**

Phosphorus is diffused into a p-type silicon wafer (N_B = 10¹⁶ cm⁻³) using a POCl₃ source that maintains a constant surface concentration C_s = 10²¹ cm⁻³. The diffusion is carried out at **1000 °C** for **t = 1 hour**.

Given: D(1000 °C) for P in Si ≈ 3 × 10⁻¹⁴ cm²/s. Calculate:

(a) The diffusion length **2√(Dt)**.
(b) The junction depth **x_j**.
(c) The concentration at **x = 0.3 μm** from the surface.

**Solution:**

**(a) Diffusion length:**

$$Dt = 3 \times 10^{-14} \times 3600 = 1.08 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{cm}^{2}$$

$$2\sqrt{Dt} = 2\sqrt{1.08 \times 10^{-10}} = 2 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-5} = 2.08 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm} = 0.208\,\mu\text{m}$$

**(b) Junction depth:** Using the erfc-inverse formula:

$$x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt}\;\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{N_B}{C_s}\right) = 2\sqrt{Dt}\;\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{10^{21}}\right) = 2\sqrt{Dt}\;\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(10^{-5}\right)$$

From standard erfc tables:

| z | erfc(z) |
|---|---|
| 2.0 | 4.7 × 10⁻³ |
| 3.0 | 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 3.4 | ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ |

Hence erfc⁻¹(10⁻⁵) ≈ 3.4. Therefore:

$$x_j = 0.208 \times 3.4 = 0.707\,\mu\text{m}$$

**(c) Concentration at x = 0.3 μm:**

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{0.3}{0.208} = 1.442$$

From erfc tables, erfc(1.44) ≈ 0.0466. Therefore:

$$C(0.3\,\mu\text{m}) = 10^{21} \times 0.0466 = 4.66 \times 10^{19}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** C(0.3 μm) is between N_B (10¹⁶) and C_s (10²¹) — physically consistent, and lies just above the p-n junction (which is at 0.71 μm), so the dopant density is still very high just inside the n-region. ✓

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Diffusion vs. Ion Implantation

| Parameter | Diffusion | Ion Implantation |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Concentration gradient (chemical potential) | Kinetic energy of ions |
| Process temperature | High (800–1200 °C) | Room temperature |
| Profile shape | erfc (constant source) or Gaussian (limited source) | Gaussian (with possible channeling tail) |
| Dose control | Indirect (solid solubility, time, temp) | Direct (current integration) |
| Junction depth control | Limited by thermal budget | Precise (set by accelerating voltage) |
| Mask materials | SiO₂, Si₃N₄, polysilicon | Photoresist, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, metal |
| Crystal damage | Negligible (thermal process) | Significant (requires anneal) |
| Through-thin-oxide capability | No (would consume oxide) | Yes (e.g., V_T adjust implant) |
| Lateral spread | Significant (≈ 0.8 × vertical depth) | Small (≈ 0.5–0.7 × R_p) |
| Modern CMOS use | Drive-in, deep wells, poly doping | All shallow junctions, S/D, halos, V_T adjust |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Typical Ion Implantation System

This block diagram shows the major functional subsystems of a modern medium-current ion implanter used in 300 mm wafer fabs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ION SOURCE\nGas or Solid Feed] --> B[EXTRACTION\nELECTRODE]
    B --> C[MASS ANALYZER\nMagnetic Sector]
    C --> D[ACCELERATOR\nTUBE 10keV to 1MeV]
    D --> E[Q MAGNET FOR\nPOST ACCEL ANALYSIS]
    E --> F[FOCUSING LENS\nElectrostatic Quadrupole]
    F --> G[X Y SCANNING\nDEFLECTION PLATES]
    G --> H[TARGET CHAMBER\nEnd Station]
    H --> I[LOAD LOCK\nVacuum]
    I --> A
    J[BEAM CURRENT\nINTEGRATOR] -.monitors.-> H
    K[VACUUM PUMPS\n10 to the power of -7 Torr] -.pumps.-> H
```

**Component-by-Component Function:**

- **Ion Source:** Generates ions by ionizing a gas (e.g., BF₃ for B, PH₃ for P, AsH₃ for As) using a hot filament (Freeman source) or RF excitation (Bernas source).
- **Extraction Electrode:** Pulls positive ions out of the source with a 10–30 kV bias.
- **Mass Analyzer (Magnet):** A magnetic field deflects ions into a circular path; radius depends on mass-to-charge ratio. Selects the desired isotope (e.g., ¹¹B⁺ vs ¹⁰B⁺, ³¹P⁺ vs ³¹P²⁺).
- **Accelerator Tube:** Races the ion to its final kinetic energy (R_p ∝ Eᵏ, k ≈ 1.6 for heavy ions in Si).
- **Q-Magnet (Post-Acceleration Analyzer):** Removes any neutralized contaminants that would otherwise implant at unpredictable depths.
- **Focusing Lens:** Recollimates the diverging beam.
- **X-Y Scanner:** Electrostatic plates sweep the beam uniformly across the wafer.
- **Target Chamber:** Houses the wafer on a cooled stage, tilted 7° to avoid channeling.
- **Beam Current Integrator:** Counts the integrated ion charge; converts to dose φ = It/(qA).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Diffusion Furnace System (Horizontal Tube Furnace)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph G1[Gas Panel / Source Delivery]
        G1A[Carrier Gas N2] --> MIX[MIXING MANIFOLD]
        G1B[O2 / Argon] --> MIX
        G1C[Dopant Vapor\nPOCl3 / BN / BBr3] --> MIX
    end
    MIX --> FURNACE[RESISTANCE HEATED\nTHREE ZONE FURNACE\n800 to 1200 C]
    FURNACE --> WAFER[QUARTZ BOAT\nWITH 100 to 200 WAFERS]
    WAFER --> EXH[EXHAUST\nSCRUBBER]
    T1[PID Temp Controller 1] -.controls.-> FURNACE
    T2[PID Temp Controller 2] -.controls.-> FURNACE
    T3[PID Temp Controller 3] -.controls.-> FURNACE
```

> [!NOTE]
> A **three-zone furnace** has independent temperature controllers for the source, center, and load zones, giving ±0.5 °C uniformity over a 1-meter hot zone. This is essential for repeatable diffusion profiles.
