## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Define **Fick's First Law** and **Fick's Second Law** of diffusion. State one practical situation in VLSI fabrication where each law is applied.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fick's First Law:** It states that the diffusion flux J of dopant atoms is proportional to the negative concentration gradient.

$$J = -D\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

(1 mark) It is used in **steady-state** situations, e.g., describing the constant flux of dopant atoms through a thin membrane during a controlled-source predeposition step. (½ mark)

**Fick's Second Law:** It describes how the concentration profile evolves with time under non-steady conditions.

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

(1 mark) It is the fundamental equation used to **predict the dopant profile** after a furnace diffusion or post-implant anneal, and is the basis for the erfc and Gaussian solutions used in process simulators. (½ mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

What is the **channeling effect** in ion implantation? Mention any two techniques used to suppress it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Channeling effect (1 mark):** When the ion beam enters a single-crystal silicon substrate **parallel to a low-index crystallographic axis** (e.g., ⟨100⟩, ⟨110⟩), the ions experience reduced nuclear collisions and slide through the open channels between atomic rows. This results in **deeper penetration** than predicted and an undesirable **deep tail** in the as-implanted dopant profile.

**Two suppression techniques (1 mark each):**

1. **Wafer Tilt:** The wafer is tilted by **7°–10°** off the ⟨100⟩ axis during implantation so that the ion beam cannot align with a low-index channel.
2. **Pre-amorphization Implant (PAI):** A pre-implant of **Si⁺ or Ge⁺ ions** at high dose amorphizes the top 10–30 nm of the silicon, converting it into a disordered layer. Subsequent dopant ions see a random atomic arrangement and channeling is eliminated.

*(Alternative accepted: use of a screen oxide to randomize the beam direction.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q A(a). [7 marks]** Explain with neat diagrams the **constant-source** and **limited-source** diffusion processes. Derive the dopant concentration profile for the constant-source case.

**Model Solution:**

**Constant Source Diffusion (3 marks):**
- The wafer surface is exposed to an **infinite reservoir** of dopant (e.g., a solid BN source, POCl₃ vapor bubbler, or ion-implanted surface layer with effectively unlimited supply).
- The **surface concentration C_s is held constant** at the solid solubility limit of the dopant in silicon at the diffusion temperature.
- Boundary conditions:
  - C(0, t) = C_s (constant)
  - C(x, 0) = N_B (substrate background)
  - C(∞, t) = N_B
- Profile shape: **complementary error function (erfc)**, monotonically decreasing from C_s at surface to N_B deep in the substrate.
- Typical application: predeposition step, where a thin, highly doped surface layer is laid down before the drive-in.

**Limited Source Diffusion (2 marks):**
- The surface dopant reservoir is **stripped off** after predeposition, and the wafer is re-heated in a clean (inert) ambient.
- The total dose **Q = ∫ C(x,t) dx** is now **fixed** (no new dopant enters or leaves).
- Profile shape: **Gaussian**, with peak concentration at the surface that **decreases** as √(1/t) over time.
- Typical application: drive-in to push the junction deeper while controlling the sheet resistance.

**Derivation of Constant-Source Profile (2 marks):**

Starting from Fick's Second Law ∂C/∂t = D ∂²C/∂x², introduce the similarity variable η = x/(2√(Dt)). The PDE reduces to the ODE d²C/dη² + 2η dC/dη = 0, which integrates (twice) to:

$$C(\eta) = A\cdot \mathrm{erf}(\eta) + B$$

Applying C(η = 0) = C_s ⇒ B = C_s, and C(η → ∞) = 0 ⇒ A = −2C_s/√π. Hence:

$$\boxed{\,C(x,t) = C_s\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\,}$$

---

**Q A(b). [7 marks]** Boron is diffused into a p-type silicon wafer (N_B = 5 × 10¹⁵ cm⁻³) from a constant surface source (C_s = 2 × 10²⁰ cm⁻³) at 1050 °C for **t = 2 hours**. Given D(1050 °C) for B in Si = **1.5 × 10⁻¹³ cm²/s**, calculate:
(i) The junction depth x_j.
(ii) The sheet resistance R_s assuming electron mobility μ_n ≈ 1000 cm²/V·s in the diffused layer.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Junction Depth (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Dt product:**

$$Dt = 1.5 \times 10^{-13} \times (2 \times 3600) = 1.5 \times 10^{-13} \times 7200 = 1.08 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{cm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Diffusion length:**

$$2\sqrt{Dt} = 2\sqrt{1.08 \times 10^{-9}} = 2 \times 3.286 \times 10^{-5} = 6.57 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm} = 0.657\,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Argument of erfc-inverse:**

$$\frac{N_B}{C_s} = \frac{5 \times 10^{15}}{2 \times 10^{20}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Lookup from erfc tables:** erfc⁻¹(2.5 × 10⁻⁵) ≈ **3.30** (interpolating between erfc(3) = 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ and erfc(4) = 1.5 × 10⁻⁸).

**Step 5 — Junction depth:**

$$x_j = 2\sqrt{Dt}\;\mathrm{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{N_B}{C_s}\right) = 0.657 \times 3.30 = 2.17\,\mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) Sheet Resistance (2 marks):**

For a Gaussian-like profile, the average sheet carrier concentration N_s can be approximated by integrating:

$$N_s \approx \int_0^{x_j} C(x)\,dx = C_s \int_0^{x_j}\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\,dx$$

Using the standard integral:

$$N_s = 2C_s\sqrt{\frac{Dt}{\pi}}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{x_j^{2}}{4Dt}\right)\right] \approx \frac{2C_s\sqrt{Dt}}{\sqrt{\pi}} = \frac{2(2 \times 10^{20})(3.286 \times 10^{-5})}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

$$N_s = \frac{1.314 \times 10^{16}}{1.772} = 7.42 \times 10^{15}\,\text{cm}^{-2}$$

Sheet resistance:

$$R_s = \frac{1}{q\,\mu_n\,N_s} = \frac{1}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1000)(7.42 \times 10^{15})} = \frac{1}{1.187 \times 10^{0}} \approx 0.84\,\Omega/\square$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**Q B(a). [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **ion implantation system**. Mention the function of each subsystem.

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (3 marks) — refer to Section 4.1** for the system schematic.

**Working and Subsystem Functions (4 marks):**

1. **Ion Source (½ mark):** Generates a plasma of dopant ions (e.g., BF₃ gas is bled in and ionized by electron bombardment from a hot tungsten filament). Ions are produced in a chamber held at ~10⁻³ Torr.

2. **Extraction Electrode (½ mark):** A 10–30 kV potential extracts the positive ions from the source region through a small aperture, forming a collimated ion beam.

3. **Mass Analyzer — Magnetic Sector (½ mark):** A uniform magnetic field B deflects ions of mass m and charge q into a circular path of radius r = (1/B)√(2mV/q). By adjusting B, only the desired isotope and charge state (e.g., ¹¹B⁺) passes through the exit slit; contaminants are blocked.

4. **Accelerator Tube (½ mark):** A high-voltage column (10 keV – 1 MeV) accelerates the selected ions to their final kinetic energy, which sets the **projected range R_p** in the silicon.

5. **Post-Acceleration Q-Magnet (½ mark):** Removes any **neutral atoms** (formed by charge exchange along the beamline) that would otherwise implant at uncontrolled depths and energies.

6. **Electrostatic Focusing Lens (½ mark):** A set of quadrupole lenses re-collimates the diverging beam to a tight spot (~few mm diameter) at the wafer plane.

7. **X-Y Beam Scanner (½ mark):** Electrostatic deflection plates sweep the beam rapidly in horizontal and vertical directions to ensure uniform dose over the 300 mm wafer area. (Modern tools also use mechanical wafer scanning for high-current implants.)

8. **Target Chamber / End Station (½ mark):** Houses the wafer on a cooled, tilted stage. Tilt of 7° suppresses channeling. The chamber is held at 10⁻⁷ Torr by turbomolecular and cryopumps to prevent beam scatter.

9. **Beam Current Integrator + Dose Control (½ mark):** Faraday cups intercept the beam at four corners of the wafer and integrate the current. Dose φ = ∫(I/A) dt / q, where I is beam current, A is scanned area, t is time, q is the electronic charge. The beam is **automatically shut off** when the target dose is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a beam integrator is critical:** A 1% error in dose translates directly into a 1% error in threshold voltage V_T in CMOS — a major parametric yield loss. Hence the Faraday-cup dose control loop is one of the most tightly specified subsystems in the entire fab.

---

**Q B(b). [7 marks]** Arsenic ions (As⁺) are implanted into silicon at an energy of **80 keV** with a dose of **φ = 5 × 10¹⁵ cm⁻²**. The projected range is **R_p = 60 nm** and the straggle is **ΔR_p = 20 nm**.

Calculate:
(i) The **peak dopant concentration** C_peak.
(ii) The **dopant concentration** at depths x = 40 nm, 60 nm, and 100 nm.
(iii) The **total implanted dose** by integrating the profile from 0 to 200 nm.

**Model Solution:**

Given: R_p = 60 nm = 6 × 10⁻⁶ cm, ΔR_p = 20 nm = 2 × 10⁻⁶ cm, φ = 5 × 10¹⁵ cm⁻².

**(i) Peak Concentration (2 marks):**

$$C_{\text{peak}} = \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta R_p} = \frac{5 \times 10^{15}}{\sqrt{2\pi} \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_{\text{peak}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{15}}{2.507 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{15}}{5.013 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.97 \times 10^{20}\,\text{cm}^{-3} \approx 10^{21}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$$

**(ii) Concentration at Various Depths (3 marks):**

Using the Gaussian formula:

$$C(x) = C_{\text{peak}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - R_p)^{2}}{2\Delta R_p^{2}}\right)$$

**At x = 40 nm:** (x − R_p) = −20 nm, (x − R_p)² / (2ΔR_p²) = 400 / 800 = 0.5
C(40) = 9.97 × 10²⁰ × e^(−0.5) = 9.97 × 10²⁰ × 0.6065 = **6.05 × 10²⁰ cm⁻³**

**At x = 60 nm:** (x − R_p) = 0, exponent = 0
C(60) = C_peak = **9.97 × 10²⁰ cm⁻³**

**At x = 100 nm:** (x − R_p) = +40 nm, (x − R_p)² / (2ΔR_p²) = 1600 / 800 = 2.0
C(100) = 9.97 × 10²⁰ × e^(−2.0) = 9.97 × 10²⁰ × 0.1353 = **1.35 × 10²⁰ cm⁻³**

**(iii) Total Integrated Dose (2 marks):**

The integral of a Gaussian from 0 to ∞ is (ΔR_p)√(π/2) × C_peak = φ by construction. Verifying:

$$\int_{0}^{200\,\text{nm}} C(x)\,dx = \phi \cdot \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\frac{200 - 60}{20\sqrt{2}}\right) - \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\frac{0 - 60}{20\sqrt{2}}\right)\Big/\!2$$

$$\int_{0}^{200} C(x)\,dx \approx 5 \times 10^{15} \times [\mathrm{erf}(4.95) - \mathrm{erf}(-2.12)]/2 \approx 5 \times 10^{15} \times [1.000 - (-0.997)]/2 \approx 5 \times 10^{15}\,\text{cm}^{-2}$$

This matches the implanted dose φ ✓.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students make the following mistakes and lose 1–2 marks:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert nm to cm** before substituting into the formula. The Gaussian formula requires **consistent CGS or SI units**; do not mix nm and cm in the same exponent.
> 2. **Plugging in x in nm while keeping ΔR_p in cm** (or vice versa) — this gives a wildly wrong answer of 10⁻²⁰ cm⁻³ (essentially zero), which the examiner immediately flags as a unit error.
> 3. **Using the wrong formula** for the erfc-inverse: it is erfc⁻¹(N_B/C_s), **not** erf⁻¹(N_B/C_s). The two are reciprocally related and the error is exactly a factor of 2 in the answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Diffusion and Ion Implantation**

- **Fick's First Law** = J = −D ∂C/∂x (steady state, defines flux).
- **Fick's Second Law** = ∂C/∂t = D ∂²C/∂x² (time-dependent, the master PDE).
- **Constant source** profile = C_s · erfc[x / (2√(Dt))]; C_s pinned at solid solubility.
- **Limited source** profile = (Q/√(πDt)) · exp[−x² / (4Dt)]; total dose Q conserved.
- **Junction depth** x_j ∝ √t — *not* linear in t (classic KTU trap question).
- **erfc values to memorize:** erfc(0) = 1, erfc(1) = 0.157, erfc(2) = 0.0047, erfc(3) = 2.2 × 10⁻⁵.
- **Arrhenius behavior:** D = D₀ exp(−Eₐ/kT); a 10 °C rise in temperature can **double** D for typical dopants.
- **Ion implantation profile** is **Gaussian** with peak at R_p; standard deviation ΔR_p; R_p increases with energy (R_p ∝ Eᵏ, k ≈ 1.6).
- **Peak concentration** C_peak = φ / (√(2π) ΔR_p) ≈ 0.4 φ / ΔR_p.
- **Channeling** is suppressed by **7°–10° wafer tilt** and **pre-amorphization** (Si⁺ or Ge⁺).
- **Implant damage** is healed by **RTA (1000–1100 °C, 1–10 s)** — furnace anneal is obsolete for sub-100 nm nodes.
- **Oxidation-Enhanced Diffusion (OED):** During thermal oxidation, Si self-interstitials are injected into the bulk, accelerating B and P diffusion. This is why shallow B junctions in CMOS need careful engineering.
- **Dopant activation** > 95 % requires peak concentration below the **solid solubility limit** at the anneal temperature.
- **Modern CMOS = 100 % ion implantation for shallow junctions**; **diffusion is mostly used for deep wells, drive-in, and some poly/memory doping**.
- **Mask for diffusion:** SiO₂, Si₃N₄, polysilicon (must withstand high temperature).
- **Mask for implantation:** photoresist, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, metal — anything that stops the ions (energy-dependent stopping power).
- **Difference between diffusion and ion implantation** = the most common 3-mark and 7-mark KTU question; must be answered in tabular form with **at least 6 contrasting points**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lithography: Photolithographic Sequence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lithography: Photolithographic Sequence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photolithography** is the foundational pattern-transfer process in Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) fabrication that uses **ultraviolet (UV) light** to project geometric patterns from a photomask onto a photosensitive polymer film (photoresist) previously deposited on a semiconductor wafer. The photolithographic **sequence** refers to the strictly ordered chain of physical and chemical operations—**cleaning, priming, coating, baking, exposure, development, and inspection**—that together translate a two-dimensional mask design into a three-dimensional physical relief on the wafer surface.

The term "**photo**" originates from Greek *phos* (light), and "**lithography**" from *lithos* (stone) + *graphein* (to write)—literally, **"writing with light on a stone"**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Stencil and Spray-Paint Method**
> Imagine you want to paint the KTU logo on a T-shirt. You would:
> 1. Wash and dry the shirt (cleaning),
> 2. Tape it firmly to prevent slipping (priming/adhesion),
> 3. Spray a uniform sticky paint film (photoresist coating),
> 4. Dry it slightly (soft bake),
> 5. Place a stencil (photomask) over it and shine a bright torch (UV exposure),
> 6. Rinse the unwanted parts with water (development), and finally
> 7. Heat-set the paint so it doesn't peel off (hard bake).
>
> **Photolithography is essentially the silicon-industry's ultra-precise version of this stencil-and-paint process**, where the T-shirt is replaced by a silicon wafer and the paint is replaced by a light-sensitive chemical called *photoresist*.

### 1.3 Why Photolithography is the Backbone of VLSI

| Metric | Standard Industrial Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Wavelength of modern exposure source (EUV) | **13.5 nm** |
| Smallest printable feature (current production) | **3 nm node** |
| Number of lithography steps in a modern CPU | **≈ 30 – 80** |
| Cost of an EUV scanner (ASML NXE) | **≈ US $200 million** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBECT604, Module 2), students must master the **sequential flow** of lithographic steps, the **chemistry of photoresists**, and the **process parameters** governing each stage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Complete Photolithographic Sequence — Overview

Photolithography is not a single step but a **pipeline of ten precisely-timed sub-processes**. The order is non-negotiable—skipping or re-ordering any step leads to defective patterns.

**The Ten Canonical Steps (in execution order):**

1. **Wafer Cleaning** – Removal of organic, particulate, and metallic contaminants.
2. **Dehydration Bake & Adhesion Promotion** – Drives off residual moisture.
3. **HMDS Priming** – Hexamethyldisilazane vapour treatment to promote resist adhesion.
4. **Photoresist Coating (Spin Coating)** – Uniform deposition of resist.
5. **Soft Bake (Pre-Bake)** – Removes solvent, stabilizes the film.
6. **Alignment & Exposure** – UV light projected through the photomask.
7. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** – Reduces standing-wave effects (for chemically amplified resists).
8. **Development** – Selective dissolution of exposed/unexposed regions.
9. **Hard Bake (Post-Development Bake)** – Hardens the developed pattern.
10. **Pattern Inspection & Metrology** – SEM/CD-SEM, optical microscopy verification.

### 2.2 Stage-by-Stage Theoretical Breakdown

#### Stage 1: Wafer Cleaning
- **Goal:** Achieve an atomically clean, hydrophilic SiO₂ surface.
- **Methods:** RCA-1 (organic removal using NH₄OH/H₂O₂/H₂O at **75–80 °C**), RCA-2 (ionic removal using HCl/H₂O₂/H₂O).
- **Why?** Contaminants cause **pattern defects, pinholes, and resist lift-off**.

#### Stage 2: Dehydration Bake
- Performed at **150 – 200 °C** for **≈ 5 min** on a hot plate.
- Removes the **physisorbed water monolayer** that forms on a hydrophilic wafer exposed to atmosphere.

#### Stage 3: HMDS Priming
- HMDS reacts with surface –OH groups, forming **Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃** hydrophobic bonds.
- **Why?** Photoresist (hydrophobic) cannot adhere well to a hydrophilic, wet surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemistry:**
> $$\text{Si–OH} + (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{Si–NH–Si(CH}_3)_3 \longrightarrow \text{Si–O–Si(CH}_3)_3 + \text{NH}_3$$

#### Stage 4: Photoresist Coating — Spin Coating

A liquid resist is dispensed onto the wafer, which is then spun at high speed. Centrifugal force spreads the resist into a thin uniform film; solvent evaporation fixes the thickness.

#### Stage 5: Soft Bake
- Removes **≈ 20–30 %** of the solvent content.
- Improves resist adhesion and mechanical stability.
- Typical: **90 – 110 °C** for **≈ 60 s** on a hot plate.

#### Stage 6: Alignment & Exposure
- Wafer is loaded into the **stepper/scanner**.
- **Photomask** is aligned with previously patterned features using **alignment marks**.
- UV light is projected through the mask, modifying the resist's solubility.
- Two reaction mechanisms:
  - **Positive resist:** Exposed regions become **more soluble** (chain scission).
  - **Negative resist:** Exposed regions become **less soluble** (cross-linking).

#### Stage 7: Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)
- Critical for **chemically amplified (CA) resists** used in DUV/EUV lithography.
- Activates the **photoacid generator (PAG)** that catalyses deprotection reactions.
- Reduces **standing-wave interference fringes** caused by reflections from substrate.

#### Stage 8: Development
- Wafer immersed in developer (**TMAH — Tetramethylammonium Hydroxide, 0.26 N**) for **≈ 30–60 s**.
- **Positive resist:** Exposed areas dissolve → pattern matches mask opaque regions.
- **Negative resist:** Unexposed areas dissolve → pattern matches mask clear regions.

#### Stage 9: Hard Bake
- **120 – 180 °C** for **≈ 60–120 s**.
- Drives off residual developer/solvent; improves etch resistance.
- ⚠ Over-baking causes **resist reflow**, distorting CD.

#### Stage 10: Inspection
- **Optical microscopy** for gross defects.
- **SEM / CD-SEM** for critical-dimension measurement.
- **Ellipsometry** for resist thickness verification.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Spin-coat resist thickness | $h \;=\; \dfrac{K \cdot \eta^{\alpha}}{\omega^{\beta}}$ | μm | Relates thickness to spin speed |
| 2 | Empirical thickness formula | $h \;\approx\; \dfrac{K \cdot C^{n}}{\omega^{1/2}}$ | μm | Most cited form (K, n are constants) |
| 3 | Rayleigh Resolution | $R \;=\; k_1 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | nm | Minimum printable half-pitch |
| 4 | Depth of Focus (DOF) | $DOF \;=\; k_2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}$ | nm | Process latitude in z-axis |
| 5 | Modulation Transfer Function | $MTF \;=\; \dfrac{I_{max} - I_{min}}{I_{max} + I_{min}}$ | dimensionless | Image contrast measure |
| 6 | Exposure Dose (fluence) | $D \;=\; I \cdot t$ | mJ/cm² | Energy delivered per unit area |
| 7 | Standing-wave period | $d_{sw} \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda}{2 n \cos\theta}$ | nm | Ripple period inside resist |
| 8 | Etch selectivity | $S \;=\; \dfrac{R_{film}}{R_{substrate}}$ | dimensionless | Mask-to-sub etch ratio |

> Where:
> - $\omega$ = angular spin speed (rad/s or rpm)
> - $\eta$ = resist viscosity (cP)
> - $\lambda$ = exposure wavelength (nm)
> - $NA$ = numerical aperture of lens
> - $k_1, k_2$ = process-dependent constants (Rayleigh factors)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Photolithography directly determines:
- **Transistor density** (Moore's Law) — every shrink requires new litho generation.
- **Yield and defect density** (D₀) — litho defects account for **≈ 60–70 %** of all wafer-defectivity issues.
- **Cost per die** — lithography is the **single most expensive step** in fab (CAPEX + OPEX).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spin-Coating Resist Thickness

The standard **empirical spin-coating equation** is derived from the balance between **centrifugal force** and **viscous shear** in a thin liquid film.

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid element of mass $dm$ at radius $r$ on a wafer spinning at angular velocity $\omega$. The centrifugal force per unit volume is:

$$f_c \;=\; \rho \omega^{2} r$$

**Step 2:** The viscous shear stress $\tau$ in a film of thickness $h$ and viscosity $\eta$ balances the centrifugal driving force:

$$\tau \;=\; \eta \frac{dv}{dz} \;\approx\; \eta \frac{\omega r}{h}$$

**Step 3:** Equating forces and integrating over the spinning duration, with solvent evaporation included as an exponential decay:

$$h(t) \;=\; \frac{h_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{4 \rho \omega^{2}}{3 \eta}\right) h_0^{2} t}}$$

**Step 4:** After the spin stabilises (long $t$, solvent mostly evaporated), the asymptotic steady-state film thickness follows:

$$h_{\infty} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\omega^{1/2}}$$

Hence the well-known **industry formula**:

$$\boxed{\,h \;\approx\; \frac{K \cdot C^{n}}{\omega^{1/2}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (typical AZ-5214E resist):**

Given $C$ = solid content fraction, $\omega$ = spin speed in rpm, and process constants $K \approx 80$, $n \approx 0.5$:

At $\omega = 4000$ rpm:

$$h \;=\; \frac{80 \cdot (0.30)^{0.5}}{(4000)^{0.5}} \;\approx\; \frac{80 \cdot 0.5476}{63.25} \;\approx\; 0.69 \; \mu m$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rayleigh Resolution Criterion

The smallest feature a projection system can print is governed by the **diffraction limit** of the imaging optics.

**Step 1:** When two adjacent point images are spaced such that the central maximum of one falls on the first minimum of the other (Rayleigh criterion), the separation is:

$$\Delta x \;=\; 0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

**Step 2:** For industrial patterning (line/space patterns), a process factor $k_1$ is introduced (with $k_1 \geq 0.25$):

$$\boxed{\,R \;=\; k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}\,}$$

**Step 3:** Numerical example — modern immersion ArF scanner:
- $\lambda = 193$ nm
- $NA = 1.35$
- $k_1 = 0.25$ (theoretical lower limit)

$$R \;=\; 0.25 \cdot \frac{193}{1.35} \;\approx\; 35.7 \; nm \; \text{(half-pitch)}$$

Hence the **smallest printable line/space pitch ≈ 71.4 nm** at $k_1 = 0.25$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Depth of Focus

**Step 1:** For a lens focused at a wafer plane, defocusing by $\pm \Delta z$ blurs the image. The tolerance (DOF) is:

$$DOF \;=\; \pm \frac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** With process factor $k_2$:

$$\boxed{\,DOF \;=\; k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^{2}}\,}$$

**Step 3:** Trade-off insight — increasing $NA$ improves $R$ but degrades $DOF$ quadratically, demanding ultra-flat wafers and stages.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: Positive vs. Negative Photoresist

| Property | Positive Photoresist | Negative Photoresist |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Exposed region | **Soluble** (removed by developer) | **Insoluble** (cross-linked) |
| Resolution | **Higher** (single polymer chain) | Lower (swelling during development) |
| Contrast ($\gamma$) | Higher | Lower |
| Etch resistance | Moderate | Excellent (especially SU-8, polyimide) |
| Common chemistry | DNQ / Novolac, CA-resists | Polyisoprene + bisazide |
| Typical application | Advanced CMOS, memory | MEMS, thick structures, packaging |
| Example commercial | AZ-5214E, SPR-955, JSR-ARX | SU-8 2000, NR7-1500P |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Process Flow Diagram (Photolithographic Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Wafer Incoming"] --> B["Wafer Cleaning RCA 1 and RCA 2"]
    B --> C["Dehydration Bake 150 to 200 C"]
    C --> D["HMDS Priming Adhesion Promoter"]
    D --> E["Photoresist Dispense"]
    E --> F["Spin Coating Uniform Film"]
    F --> G["Soft Bake Solvent Removal"]
    G --> H["Mask Alignment"]
    H --> I["UV Exposure 365 or 248 or 193 nm"]
    I --> J["Post Exposure Bake PEB"]
    J --> K["Development TMAH 0.26 N"]
    K --> L["Hard Bake Pattern Stabilization"]
    L --> M{"Inspection Pass"}
    M -- Yes --> N["Pattern Complete Etch Ready"]
    M -- No --> O["Strip Resist Recycle Wafer"]
    O --> B

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0e7490,stroke:#083344,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0e7490,stroke:#083344,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0891b2,stroke:#164e63,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#0d9488,stroke:#134e4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#0d9488,stroke:#134e4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#059669,stroke:#064e3b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3b0764,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#9f1239,stroke:#3f0011,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Positive vs Negative Resist Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Mask Opaque Region"] --> P2["UV Light BLOCKED"]
    P3["Mask Clear Region"] --> P4["UV Light PASSES"]
    P2 --> P5["Resist Region A Unexposed"]
    P4 --> P6["Resist Region B Exposed"]
    P5 --> P7["Developer DISSOLVES Region A"]
    P6 --> P8["Developer RETAINS Region B"]
    P7 --> P9["Final Pattern"]
    P8 --> P9["Final Pattern"]

    style P1 fill:#334155,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#475569,stroke:#1e293b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style P4 fill:#fde047,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style P5 fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#334155,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style P6 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style P7 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style P8 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style P9 fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Mermaid Block Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LayerStack["Wafer Cross Section After Development"]
        direction TB
        top1["Open Window Exposed and Dissolved"]
        layer2["Remaining Photoresist Pattern"]
        layer3["Underlying Thin Film SiO2 or Metal"]
        layer4["Silicon Substrate"]
        top1 --> layer2
        layer2 --> layer3
        layer3 --> layer4
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **positive** and **negative** photoresists. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Positive Photoresist | Negative Photoresist |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Solubility after exposure | Increases (more soluble) | Decreases (cross-links) |
| Pattern transferred | Same as mask opaque regions | Same as mask clear regions |
| Resolution | Higher | Lower (swelling) |
| Example | AZ-5214E, SPR-955, Shipley S1813 | SU-8, NR7-1500P |

*(Award 2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for valid examples.)*

---

**Q2.** What is the **purpose of soft bake** (pre-bake) in the photolithographic sequence?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Removes **20–30 % of residual solvent** from the spin-coated resist film. *(1 mark)*
2. Improves **resist adhesion** to the wafer surface and **mechanical stability**. *(1 mark)*
3. Reduces **defects** like bubbles, pinholes, and stickiness; prevents mask contamination during exposure. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Q]**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **complete photolithographic sequence** used in VLSI fabrication. State the **purpose** of each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The photolithographic sequence consists of **ten sequential steps**, summarised below:

1. **Wafer Cleaning (RCA-1 and RCA-2):** Removes organic, particulate and ionic contamination to provide a clean hydrophilic surface.
2. **Dehydration Bake (150–200 °C, 5 min):** Drives off physisorbed water.
3. **HMDS Priming:** Vapour-phase HMDS reacts with surface –OH groups, forming a hydrophobic Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃ monolayer that promotes resist adhesion.
4. **Photoresist Coating:** Liquid resist dispensed and spin-coated at 2000–5000 rpm to obtain a uniform film (thickness ≈ 0.5–1.5 μm).
5. **Soft Bake (Pre-bake):** 90–110 °C for ≈ 60 s on a hot plate; removes most solvent and stabilises the film.
6. **Alignment and Exposure:** Wafer is aligned to the mask using alignment marks; UV light (365 / 248 / 193 nm) is projected to modify the resist's solubility.
7. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB):** For chemically-amplified resists, activates the photoacid generator; reduces standing-wave interference.
8. **Development:** Immersion in 0.26 N TMAH for 30–60 s; selectively dissolves exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions.
9. **Hard Bake:** 120–180 °C, 60–120 s; drives off residual developer/solvent and improves etch resistance.
10. **Inspection:** Optical microscopy, SEM, ellipsometry to verify CD, alignment, and defect density.

*(Full marks: 1 mark for the block diagram + 6 marks distributed across the ten steps with purpose.)*

---

**(b)** Derive the **spin-coating thickness equation** and compute the resist thickness for $\omega = 3000$ rpm, given $K = 85$ and $n = 0.5$, $C = 0.30$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with the empirical relation:**

$$h \;=\; \frac{K \cdot C^{n}}{\omega^{1/2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute given values:**

$$h \;=\; \frac{85 \cdot (0.30)^{0.5}}{(3000)^{0.5}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the powers:**

$$(0.30)^{0.5} \;=\; \sqrt{0.30} \;\approx\; 0.5477$$

$$(3000)^{0.5} \;=\; \sqrt{3000} \;\approx\; 54.772$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$h \;=\; \frac{85 \cdot 0.5477}{54.772} \;\approx\; \frac{46.555}{54.772} \;\approx\; 0.850 \; \mu m$$

**Result:** $h \approx 0.85 \; \mu m$

*(Award 2 marks for stating the formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for evaluation, 1 mark for correct final answer with units.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **Rayleigh resolution criterion** for photolithography. A modern immersion ArF scanner operates at $\lambda = 193$ nm with $NA = 1.35$. Calculate the **smallest printable half-pitch** assuming $k_1 = 0.30$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the criterion:**

The Rayleigh criterion defines the minimum resolvable feature when two point sources are separated such that the central maximum of the diffraction pattern of one source coincides with the first minimum of the other.

**Step 2 — Write the formula:**

$$R \;=\; k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$R \;=\; 0.30 \cdot \frac{193 \; \text{nm}}{1.35}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$R \;=\; 0.30 \cdot 142.96 \; \text{nm} \;\approx\; 42.9 \; \text{nm}$$

**Result:** The smallest printable **half-pitch ≈ 42.9 nm**, meaning a full line/space pitch of ≈ 85.8 nm.

*(Award 3 marks for explanation + derivation, 4 marks for numerical calculation.)*

---

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **standing-wave effect** in photoresist and how **post-exposure bake (PEB)** mitigates it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When monochromatic UV light passes through the photoresist, partial reflection occurs at the **resist–substrate interface**. The incident and reflected waves **interfere**, producing a periodic intensity pattern (constructive–destructive fringes) normal to the resist surface — the **standing wave**.

**The period of standing waves is:**

$$d_{sw} \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{2 n \cos\theta}$$

where $n$ is the resist refractive index and $\theta$ the angle of incidence inside the resist.

**Consequence:** The exposed resist has alternating bands of over- and under-exposure, producing wavy sidewalls after development — a major CD-defect source.

**Mitigation by PEB:**
- Performed at 110–130 °C for 60–90 s after exposure.
- Causes **acid diffusion** (in CA-resists) which smooths out the standing-wave ripples.
- The photoacid generated during exposure catalyzes uniform deprotection across the entire exposed region, blurring the interference fringes.

**Numerical check** (typical g/i-line resist, $n = 1.68$, $\theta = 0$):

$$d_{sw} \;=\; \frac{365}{2 \cdot 1.68 \cdot 1} \;\approx\; 108.6 \; \text{nm}$$

This is comparable to feature sizes, confirming the criticality of PEB.

*(Award 3 marks for explanation + diagram, 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for mitigation logic.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing soft bake and hard bake** temperatures and purposes. Soft bake = **lower T, solvent removal**. Hard bake = **higher T, developer removal + etch hardening**. *(–1 mark per instance)*
> 2. **Forgetting HMDS priming** in the flowchart. Many students skip this step, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up positive and negative resist behaviour.** Positive = exposed dissolves; negative = unexposed dissolves.
> 4. **Not stating units** in the spin-coat formula and Rayleigh formula derivations — a frequent 0.5–1 mark penalty.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** in question Q2(a) — even a hand-drawn flowchart earns the full 1 mark for visualisation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Photolithographic Sequence**

- **Photolithography = pattern transfer using UV light through a photomask onto a photosensitive resist-coated wafer.**
- The **10-step sequence**: Cleaning → Dehydration → HMDS → Coat → Soft Bake → Align/Expose → PEB → Develop → Hard Bake → Inspect.
- **Positive resist** — exposed regions dissolve; **Negative resist** — unexposed regions dissolve.
- **Spin-coating equation:** $h = K \cdot C^{n} / \omega^{1/2}$ — thickness decreases with the **square root of spin speed**.
- **Rayleigh resolution:** $R = k_{1} \lambda / NA$ — to shrink $R$, reduce $\lambda$ or increase $NA$.
- **Depth of focus:** $DOF = k_{2} \lambda / NA^{2}$ — inverse quadratic dependence on $NA$.
- **HMDS** converts hydrophilic Si-OH surface to hydrophobic Si-O-Si(CH₃)₃ for better resist adhesion.
- **PEB** is **mandatory** for chemically amplified DUV/EUV resists and reduces standing waves.
- **TMAH (0.26 N)** is the industry-standard developer for positive resists.
- **Resolution limit** is fundamentally set by **diffraction**: smaller $\lambda$ (EUV: 13.5 nm) drives Moore's Law.
- **Lithography is the most expensive & defect-sensitive step** in any modern CMOS flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electron Beam Lithography (EBL)** is a specialized **mask-less lithographic patterning technique** used in VLSI and nanofabrication, in which a **focused beam of accelerated electrons** is raster-scanned (or vector-scanned) across a substrate that has been pre-coated with an **electron-sensitive polymeric resist film**. The localized electron exposure induces physico-chemical transformations — either **chain scission** (in positive resists such as PMMA) or **cross-linking** (in negative resists such as SU-8 or HSQ) — which alter the local solubility of the resist in a subsequent **chemical development** step, thereby transferring the desired geometric pattern onto the substrate.

In the official **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, EBL is positioned as the **highest-resolution direct-write lithography** technique available to the integrated-circuit industry. It is the **workhorse tool for photomask fabrication, advanced R&D, photonics device prototyping, and sub-10 nm research structures**, since optical lithography hits an irreducible resolution floor set by the wavelength of the exposing light.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electron Pen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine you are drawing an extremely intricate masterpiece on a chalk-coated blackboard using an **invisible ultra-fine pen that writes with electrons instead of ink**. The chalk (your *resist*) is sensitive — wherever the pen touches, the chalk either crumbles away easily (positive resist) or hardens and refuses to be wiped (negative resist). After you finish drawing, you wash the board with a chemical "eraser" (the *developer*), and your drawing remains permanently etched.

- The **electron gun** is the *pen's ink reservoir*.
- The **electromagnetic lenses** are the *focusing knobs of the pen*.
- The **beam blanker** is the *lift-pen-up-and-down mechanism*.
- The **deflection coils** are the *steering wheels* that direct where the pen writes.
- The **vacuum chamber** prevents the electron "ink" from being scattered by air molecules.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard operating values are indispensable for EBL numerical problems in KTU examinations:

- **Elementary charge** $\quad e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Electron rest mass** $\quad m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$
- **Planck's constant** $\quad h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **Reduced Planck's constant** $\quad \hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum** $\quad c = 3.0 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Typical accelerating voltage** $\quad V_a = 10\text{–}100\ \text{kV}$ (most common: **30 kV** and **100 kV**)
- **Beam current** $\quad I_b = 1\ \text{pA} \text{–} 100\ \text{nA}$
- **Beam spot size (diameter)** $\quad d = 1\text{–}10\ \text{nm}$ (modern FEG systems)
- **Resolution limit** $\quad < 10\ \text{nm}$ routinely achievable
- **Vacuum pressure** $\quad < 10^{-6}\ \text{Torr}$ (high-vacuum regime)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The resolving power of any lithography is bounded by the **Rayleigh criterion** $R = k_1 \cdot \lambda / \text{NA}$. Since the electron's *de Broglie wavelength* is **thousands of times smaller than optical wavelengths** (e.g., $\lambda_e \approx 0.006\ \text{nm}$ at 30 kV, versus $\lambda = 193\ \text{nm}$ for ArF lithography), EBL inherently beats the diffraction limit of optical lithography by orders of magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and the "How"

EBL operates on the principle of **electron-matter interaction in a high-vacuum environment**. The full process chain can be broken down into the following sequential steps:

### Step 1: Electron Generation (The Source)
- A **filament cathode** (W hairpin, LaB₆ crystal, or field-emission tip) emits electrons via **thermionic emission** or **field emission**.
- These electrons are extracted and accelerated toward an anode by a high electric field, gaining kinetic energy $E = eV_a$.

### Step 2: Beam Collimation & Focusing
- A stack of **electromagnetic lenses** (condenser, objective, sometimes intermediate) shapes the beam into a tight, near-parallel probe with diameter typically 1–10 nm.
- **Apertures** further trim the beam current and convergence angle.

### Step 3: Beam Steering & Blanking
- **Electromagnetic deflection coils** raster or vector-scan the beam across the field.
- The **beam blanker** deflects the beam into a Faraday cup when no exposure is desired (e.g., during stage movement), acting as a high-speed on/off shutter.

### Step 4: Resist Exposure
- Electrons strike the resist film, transferring energy through **elastic scattering** (with resist nuclei) and **inelastic scattering** (with resist electrons), depositing a localized **dose** (typically $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$).
- The deposited dose alters the molecular structure of the resist.

### Step 5: Development
- The exposed wafer is immersed in a **developer** (e.g., MIBK:IPA = 1:3 for PMMA), which selectively dissolves either the exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions.

### Step 6: Pattern Transfer (Optional)
- The developed resist pattern can serve as a mask for **etching** or **lift-off metallization**.

## 2.2 Electron Scattering — The Resolution Limiter

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Important Concept for KTU:** The single biggest reason EBL does NOT infinitely scale to atomic dimensions is **electron scattering inside the resist and substrate**.

Two dominant scattering modes govern the resolution:

**1. Forward Scattering (Small-Angle):**
- Occurs **within the resist film** as the primary electron traverses downward.
- Causes **beam broadening** by an amount $\beta_f$.
- Approximate formula: 
$$\beta_f \approx 0.9 \cdot R_t \cdot \left(\frac{V_a}{100\ \text{kV}}\right)^{-1.75}$$

where $R_t$ is the **resist thickness** and $V_a$ is the accelerating voltage.

**2. Backscattering (Large-Angle):**
- Occurs when primary electrons penetrate into the substrate and are scattered **back into the resist**.
- Creates a wide **"exposure halo"** around the intended beam position.
- **Range of backscattered electrons** (Kanaya-Okayama range):
$$R_{\text{KO}} = \frac{27.6 \cdot A \cdot E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \cdot \rho}$$

where $A$ = atomic weight (g/mol), $E$ = beam energy (keV), $Z$ = atomic number, $\rho$ = density (g/cm³).

## 2.3 Proximity Effect — The Nemesis of EBL

> [!CAUTION]
> **Proximity Effect Definition:** The unwanted exposure of resist regions adjacent to the intended exposure area, caused by **backscattered electrons** and the finite beam size. This causes pattern distortion, line-width variation, and stitching errors.

The total energy deposited at any point is modeled by the **double-Gaussian proximity function**:

$$f(r) = \frac{1}{\pi (1+\eta)} \left[ \frac{1}{\alpha^2} \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{\alpha^2}\right) + \frac{\eta}{\beta^2} \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{\beta^2}\right) \right]$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = forward-scattering range (typically **5–30 nm**)
- $\beta$ = backscattering range (typically **1–10 µm**)
- $\eta$ = backscatter coefficient (typically **0.5–1.5**)
- $r$ = radial distance from beam impact point

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Magnitude | Unit |
|---|--------------------|-----------|-------------------|------|
| 1 | De Broglie wavelength (non-rel.) | $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a}$ | $\sim 0.006$ at 30 kV | nm |
| 2 | De Broglie wavelength (relativistic) | $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a \left(1 + \tfrac{eV_a}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}$ | $\sim 0.0037$ at 100 kV | nm |
| 3 | Relativistic correction factor | $\gamma = 1 + \tfrac{eV_a}{m_0 c^2}$ | $\gamma \approx 1.2$ at 100 kV | — |
| 4 | Forward-scattering range | $\beta_f \approx 0.9 R_t (V_a/100\text{kV})^{-1.75}$ | 5–30 | nm |
| 5 | Backscattering range | $\beta$ (empirical) | 1–10 | µm |
| 6 | Kanaya-Okayama range | $R_{\text{KO}} = 27.6 A E^{1.67} / (Z^{0.89} \rho)$ | varies | µm |
| 7 | Proximity function (double-Gaussian) | $f(r) = \tfrac{1}{\pi(1+\eta)} \left[\tfrac{1}{\alpha^2}e^{-r^2/\alpha^2} + \tfrac{\eta}{\beta^2}e^{-r^2/\beta^2}\right]$ | normalized | — |
| 8 | Exposure dose | $D = I_b \cdot t / A$ | 100–1000 | µC/cm² |
| 9 | Resist sensitivity | $D_0$ (threshold dose) | PMMA $\sim 400$ | µC/cm² |
| 10 | Beam diameter | $d = \sqrt{d_s^2 + d_g^2 + d_c^2}$ | 1–10 | nm |
| 11 | Total exposure time | $T = D \cdot A / I_b$ | minutes to hours | s |
| 12 | Gridded proximity correction dose | $D_i = D_0 \left[1 + \sum_{j \ne i} \frac{f(r_{ij})}{f(0)}\right]^{-1}$ | — | µC/cm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** EBL is indispensable in **photonic crystal fabrication**, **quantum dot / nanowire device research**, **NEMS/MEMS prototyping**, **advanced EUV mask writing**, **small-volume ASIC prototyping**, and **fundamentally, any application where the mask cost is prohibitive or the resolution is beyond optical capability**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the De Broglie Wavelength of an Accelerated Electron

> **Problem Context:** The de Broglie wavelength sets the *theoretical* lower limit of resolution for any particle-beam lithography. We will derive both the **non-relativistic** and the **relativistic-corrected** forms.

### Derivation 3.1.A — Non-Relativistic Case

**Step 1:** Write the energy acquired by an electron accelerated through potential $V_a$.

The work done by the electric field on the electron equals its gain in kinetic energy:

$$E_k = e \cdot V_a$$

**Step 2:** Equate kinetic energy with the classical expression $\tfrac{1}{2} m_0 v^2$.

$$e V_a = \frac{1}{2} m_0 v^2$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the electron velocity $v$.

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V_a}{m_0}}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the de Broglie relation $\lambda = h / (m_0 v)$ for non-relativistic particles.

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{m_0 v} = \frac{h}{m_0 \sqrt{\tfrac{2 e V_a}{m_0}}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a}}$$

**Final non-relativistic result:**

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a}}}$$

### Derivation 3.1.B — Relativistically Corrected Case

**Step 1:** Recognize that at $V_a \ge 10\ \text{kV}$, the electron velocity is a non-negligible fraction of $c$. The total energy is:

$$E_{\text{total}} = \gamma m_0 c^2$$

where the **Lorentz factor** is:

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}}$$

**Step 2:** The kinetic energy is $E_k = eV_a = (\gamma - 1) m_0 c^2$, which gives:

$$\gamma = 1 + \frac{e V_a}{m_0 c^2}$$

**Step 3:** The relativistic momentum is $p = \gamma m_0 v$. The de Broglie wavelength is:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\gamma m_0 v}$$

**Step 4:** Use $v = c \sqrt{1 - \gamma^{-2}}$ to eliminate $v$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\gamma m_0 c \sqrt{1 - \gamma^{-2}}} = \frac{h}{m_0 c \sqrt{\gamma^2 - 1}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\gamma = 1 + \tfrac{eV_a}{m_0 c^2}$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{(m_0 c)^2 \left[\left(1 + \tfrac{eV_a}{m_0 c^2}\right)^2 - 1\right]}}$$

Expanding $(1+x)^2 - 1 = 2x + x^2$ with $x = \tfrac{eV_a}{m_0 c^2}$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a \left(1 + \tfrac{eV_a}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}}$$

**Final relativistic result:**

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a \left(1 + \dfrac{e V_a}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}}}$$

### Numerical Worked Example 3.1

**Question:** Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through $V_a = 30\ \text{kV}$, using both the non-relativistic and relativistic formulas.

**Solution (Non-Relativistic):**

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 30 \times 10^3}}$$

Compute the denominator step-by-step:

$$2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 2.918 \times 10^{-49}$$

$$2.918 \times 10^{-49} \times 30 \times 10^{3} = 8.754 \times 10^{-45}$$

$$\sqrt{8.754 \times 10^{-45}} = 9.357 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{kg·m/s}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{9.357 \times 10^{-23}} = 7.082 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m} = \boxed{0.00708\ \text{nm}}$$

**Solution (Relativistic):**

Compute correction factor: $1 + \tfrac{eV_a}{2 m_0 c^2} = 1 + \tfrac{30 \times 10^3 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2}$

Numerator of correction: $30\,000 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 4.806 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{J}$
Denominator: $2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 9 \times 10^{16} = 1.640 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{J}$

Correction factor: $1 + \tfrac{4.806 \times 10^{-15}}{1.640 \times 10^{-13}} = 1 + 0.0293 = 1.0293$

$$\lambda_{\text{rel}} = \frac{7.082 \times 10^{-12}}{\sqrt{1.0293}} = \frac{7.082 \times 10^{-12}}{1.01455} = \boxed{6.980 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m} = 0.00698\ \text{nm}}$$

The relativistic correction reduces $\lambda$ by about **1.4 %**, which is significant for high-precision EBL work.

## 3.2 Derivation of Exposure Time for a Given Pattern

**Problem Setup:** A square pattern of side $L$ must be written with a dose $D$ using a beam current $I_b$. The beam is parked at each dwell point for time $\tau$. Find the total writing time.

**Step 1:** The **dose** is defined as charge per unit area:

$$D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{I_b \cdot \tau_{\text{per-pixel}}}{a_{\text{pixel}}}$$

where $a_{\text{pixel}}$ is the area of one pixel and $\tau_{\text{per-pixel}}$ is the dwell time.

**Step 2:** The total charge delivered equals beam current times total exposure time:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = I_b \cdot T_{\text{total}}$$

**Step 3:** The total area to be exposed is $A_{\text{pattern}} = L^2$ (for a square). For uniform exposure:

$$T_{\text{total}} = \frac{D \cdot A_{\text{pattern}}}{I_b}$$

**Step 4:** Inserting realistic numbers ($D = 400\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$, $L = 1\ \text{cm}^2$, $I_b = 1\ \text{nA}$):

$$T_{\text{total}} = \frac{400 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C/cm}^2 \times 1\ \text{cm}^2}{1 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}} = 4 \times 10^{5}\ \text{s} \approx \boxed{111\ \text{hours}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** This calculation exposes the **fundamental throughput problem** of EBL — direct-writing a 1 cm² area at high dose with a 1 nA beam takes **over four days**! This is why EBL is restricted to **R&D, mask-making, and small-volume niche production**, not high-volume manufacturing (where optical/EUV lithography dominates).

## 3.3 Proximity Effect — Total Dose at an Arbitrary Point

For a pattern of $N$ point exposures at positions $\vec{r}_j$, each delivering dose $D_j$, the total dose at an arbitrary point $\vec{r}_i$ is:

$$D_{\text{total}}(\vec{r}_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} D_j \cdot f(\vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \vert)$$

The **goal of proximity effect correction (PEC)** is to find $D_j$ such that $D_{\text{total}}(\vec{r}_i) = D_0$ (threshold) at every intended pattern edge.

A widely used approach is **dose modulation**:

$$D_j = D_0 \left[ \sum_{i} f(\vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \vert) \right]^{-1} \cdot \text{(self-term correction)}$$

For the **gridded dose-map** approach, the pattern is divided into $M$ pixels, and the inverted system $D = M^{-1} D_0$ is solved numerically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete EBL System

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture and signal flow** of a modern electron beam lithography tool, from electron source to patterned wafer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electron Source Tungsten LaB6 or FEG] --> B[Extraction Anode High Voltage 10 to 100 kV]
    B --> C[Condenser Lens 1 Beam Current Control]
    C --> D[Beam Limiting Aperture 10 to 100 micron]
    D --> E[Condenser Lens 2 Beam Shaping]
    E --> F[Beam Blanker Electrostatic Deflector On Off Switch]
    F --> G[Deflection Coils XY Vector Scan]
    G --> H[Objective Lens Final Focusing 1 to 10 nm Spot]
    H --> I[Resist Coated Wafer Stage X Y Z Laser Interferometer]
    I --> J[Backscattered Electron Detector Optional for Alignment]
    J --> K[Vacuum Chamber 10 power minus 6 Torr or Better]
    K --> L[Pattern Generator Computer DAC Hardware]
    L --> G
    L --> F

    subgraph SIGNAL_FLOW_CHAIN
        A
        B
        C
        D
        E
        F
        G
        H
        I
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_AND_ENVIRONMENT
        J
        K
        L
    end
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of EBL Patterning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Substrate Cleaning RCA or Solvent Wash] --> P2[Step 2 Resist Spin Coating PMMA 950K 4 percent in Anisol]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Soft Bake 180 degree C for 90 s Hot Plate]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 EBL Exposure Dose 200 to 800 microC per square cm]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Post Exposure Bake Optional For CAR Only]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Development MIBK IPA 1 colon 3 for 60 s]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Rinse IPA Drying N2 Blow or Critical Point Dryer]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Inspection SEM AFM or Optical Microscope]
    P8 --> P9{Pattern Acceptable}
    P9 -- Yes --> P10[Step 9 Pattern Transfer Etching or Lift Off]
    P9 -- No --> P2
```

## 4.3 Comparison: EBL vs. Optical Lithography (Architecture Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Comparative Lithography Architecture] --> C1[EBL Electron Beam]
    C --> C2[Optical Lithography Photolithography]

    C1 --> C1a[Source Tungsten FEG Thermionic Field Emission]
    C1 --> C1b[Mask Requirement Maskless Direct Write]
    C1 --> C1c[Wavelength 0.001 to 0.01 nm De Broglie]
    C1 --> C1d[Resolution Less than 10 nm]
    C1 --> C1e[Throughput Very Low Serial Writing]
    C1 --> C1f[Application R and D Mask Making Nano Patterning]

    C2 --> C2a[Source Mercury Lamp Excimer Laser DUV EUV]
    C2 --> C2b[Mask Requirement Photomask Required]
    C2 --> C2c[Wavelength 365 248 193 13.5 nm]
    C2 --> C2d[Resolution 38 to 90 nm with Multi Patterning]
    C2 --> C2e[Throughput Very High Parallel Exposure]
    C2 --> C2f[Application High Volume Manufacturing HVM]
```
