<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Short Answer `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define Electron Beam Lithography. List any four advantages of EBL over conventional optical photolithography.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Electron Beam Lithography (EBL) is a mask-less direct-write lithographic technique in which a focused beam of accelerated electrons (typically 10–100 keV) is scanned across an electron-sensitive resist film to define custom nanoscale patterns by locally altering the resist's solubility in a developer.

**Four Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Ultra-high resolution:** Sub-10 nm features achievable routinely; fundamentally limited only by electron scattering, not diffraction.
2. **Mask-less operation:** Eliminates the need for costly photomasks, ideal for prototyping and research.
3. **Direct write flexibility:** Patterns can be changed instantly via CAD file modification — no mask fabrication cycle.
4. **Excellent CD control:** Because the beam diameter is precisely controlled electromagnetically, line-width control down to ~2 nm is possible.

---

### Question A2 — Short Answer `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the proximity effect in Electron Beam Lithography? State two techniques to mitigate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The proximity effect in EBL is the **unwanted exposure of resist in regions adjacent to the intended pattern features**, caused by the **backscattering of primary electrons from the substrate** (and to a lesser extent by forward scattering within the resist). It leads to pattern distortion, line-width variation, and stitching errors, particularly in dense patterns.

**Two Mitigation Techniques (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Dose Modulation (Proximity Effect Correction / PEC):** Each shape in the pattern is assigned a *locally adjusted dose* such that the cumulative dose at every point equals the resist's threshold dose $D_0$. Algorithms include *GHOST*, *PYRAMID*, and *BEAMER*.
2. **Use of High-Accelerating-Voltage Beams:** At higher $V_a$ (e.g., 100 kV), the backscattered electrons travel deeper into the substrate and spread over a larger area with lower energy density, reducing the peak unwanted dose contribution. Alternatively, **thin substrates**, **multi-pass exposure**, or **variable-shaped beam** schemes also help.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A — Full 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Set A]`

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle and major subsystems of an Electron Beam Lithography system. Discuss the role of each subsystem in pattern generation.**

#### (a) Working Principle & Block Diagram (7 marks)

The EBL system uses a **focused beam of accelerated electrons** to write patterns directly into an electron-sensitive resist. The workflow:

1. **Electrons** are emitted from a source (W, LaB₆, or FEG).
2. They are **accelerated** by a high-voltage field (10–100 kV).
3. **Electromagnetic lenses** (condenser + objective) focus them into a sub-10 nm probe.
4. **Deflection coils** steer the probe across the resist-coated wafer, following a CAD-defined scan path.
5. **Beam blanker** turns the beam on/off at each pixel.
6. The wafer sits on a **precision X-Y stage** with **laser interferometric feedback** (sub-nm positioning accuracy).
7. The entire column operates under **high vacuum** ($<10^{-6}\ \text{Torr}$) to prevent beam-gas scattering.

#### (b) Subsystem Roles (7 marks)

| Subsystem | Role | Key Parameter |
|----------|------|---------------|
| **Electron gun** | Source of electrons | Brightness (A/cm²/sr), energy spread |
| **Accelerating column** | Imparts kinetic energy $eV_a$ | 10–100 kV |
| **Condenser lens** | Controls beam current / convergence | Excitation current |
| **Objective lens** | Final focusing to sub-10 nm spot | Demagnification |
| **Apertures** | Trim beam diameter, limit current | 10–100 µm diameter |
| **Beam blanker** | ON/OFF switching | Rise time < ns |
| **Deflection coils** | XY beam steering | Deflection field (mm²) |
| **Stage** | Coarse positioning, multi-field stitching | Laser interferometer resolution |
| **Vacuum system** | Prevents scattering, contamination | < 10⁻⁶ Torr |
| **Pattern generator** | Synchronizes beam with CAD data | DAC clock rate (MHz) |

> **Conclusion:** Each subsystem contributes to one or more of the three EBL performance metrics — **resolution, throughput, and placement accuracy (overlay)**.

---

### Question Choice B — Full 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Set B]`

**Q: Derive the relativistic de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through potential $V_a$. Hence calculate the wavelength at $V_a = 50\ \text{kV}$ and $V_a = 100\ \text{kV}$. Compare these with the wavelength of 193 nm ArF excimer laser lithography and comment on the resolution implications.**

#### (a) Derivation of Relativistic De Broglie Wavelength (7 marks)

Starting from kinetic energy and Lorentz factor:

$$\gamma = 1 + \frac{eV_a}{m_0 c^2}$$

The relativistic momentum is $p = \gamma m_0 v$, and $v = c\sqrt{1 - \gamma^{-2}}$. The de Broglie relation gives:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\gamma m_0 v} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a \left(1 + \tfrac{e V_a}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}}$$

#### (b) Numerical Calculation at 50 kV and 100 kV (7 marks)

**At $V_a = 50\ \text{kV}$:**

Correction factor: $1 + \tfrac{50 \times 10^3 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 9 \times 10^{16}}$

Numerator: $8.01 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{J}$, Denominator: $1.640 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{J}$, Ratio = 0.0488

Factor = $1.0488$. Square root: $\sqrt{1.0488} = 1.0241$.

$$\lambda_{50\text{kV}} = \frac{7.082 \times 10^{-12}}{1.0241} \approx 6.916 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m} = \boxed{0.00692\ \text{nm}}$$

**At $V_a = 100\ \text{kV}$:**

Correction factor: $1 + \tfrac{100 \times 10^3 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{1.640 \times 10^{-13}} = 1 + 0.0977 = 1.0977$

$$\sqrt{1.0977} = 1.0477$$

$$\lambda_{100\text{kV}} = \frac{7.082 \times 10^{-12}}{1.0477} \approx 6.760 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m} = \boxed{0.00676\ \text{nm}}$$

**Comparison with 193 nm ArF:**

$$\frac{\lambda_{\text{ArF}}}{\lambda_{100\text{kV}}} = \frac{193}{0.00676} \approx 28\,550$$

> **Conclusion:** The electron wavelength at 100 kV is roughly **28,500 times shorter** than the ArF excimer wavelength. Theoretically, this would allow **proportionally higher resolution** — but in practice, EBL resolution is **not** limited by wavelength (since $\lambda_e$ is already infinitesimal). Instead, the practical resolution is limited by **electron scattering in the resist and substrate (proximity effect)**, which is on the order of a few nanometers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the relativistic correction at high $V_a$.** Using the non-relativistic formula $\lambda = h/\sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a}$ at 100 kV gives a 5–10 % error. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** if the formula is not appropriately chosen.
> 
> 2. **Confusing forward scattering ($\alpha$) with backscattering ($\beta$).** Many students swap them in the proximity function, leading to physically meaningless results. Memorize: **$\alpha \ll \beta$, and $\alpha$ is intra-resist, $\beta$ is substrate-driven.**
> 
> 3. **Drawing the EBL block diagram without labeling arrows or functions.** A block diagram without labeled signal/data flow scores **at most 50 %** of the marks allotted for it. Always show **direction of beam path** and **function of each block**.
> 
> 4. **Writing the proximity effect definition in one sentence without giving the mathematical model.** For 14-mark questions, examiners expect both the **physical description** AND the **double-Gaussian mathematical expression**.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring units in dose calculations.** Always write dose in $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ or $\text{C/m}^2$ consistently — a unit mismatch will cost **0.5–1 mark**.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up resist polarity.** PMMA is **positive**, HSQ is **negative**. Getting this wrong in a comparison question is a classic deduction trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Walking into the EBL Exam:**

- **Definition:** EBL = mask-less, focused-electron-beam direct-write lithography on electron-sensitive resist.
- **Resolution:** Sub-10 nm routinely; theoretical floor set by electron wavelength ($\sim 0.007$ nm at 30 kV).
- **Practical resolution limiter:** **Electron scattering** (forward + back) → **proximity effect**, NOT wavelength.
- **Wavelength formula (non-rel):** $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a}$
- **Wavelength formula (rel):** $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_0 e V_a \left(1 + eV_a / 2 m_0 c^2\right)}$
- **Proximity function:** $f(r) = \tfrac{1}{\pi(1+\eta)} \left[\tfrac{1}{\alpha^2}e^{-r^2/\alpha^2} + \tfrac{\eta}{\beta^2}e^{-r^2/\beta^2}\right]$
- **Typical $\alpha$:** 5–30 nm (forward scattering). **Typical $\beta$:** 1–10 µm (backscattering).
- **Resist types:** PMMA (positive, ~400 µC/cm²), HSQ (negative, high resolution), ZEP (positive, high sensitivity), SU-8 (negative, thick film).
- **Source types:** Thermionic (W, LaB₆) → lower brightness; **Field emission (FEG)** → higher brightness, smaller spot.
- **Subsystems:** Gun → Anode → Condenser → Aperture → Deflection/Blanker → Objective → Stage → Vacuum.
- **Major disadvantage:** **Low throughput** (serial writing, hours-to-days per cm²).
- **Primary applications:** Mask making, R&D, photonic crystals, nanowires, NEMS, quantum devices, sub-7 nm research.
- **Advantages vs. optical:** Higher resolution, no mask needed, direct write, flexible.
- **Disadvantages vs. optical:** Slow, expensive, small field, charging effects, proximity effect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Etching** in Very-Large-Scale Integration (VLSI) is the process of **selective removal of material** (dielectric, metal, or semiconductor) from the surface of a silicon wafer through a patterned mask, in order to transfer the geometric pattern from the lithographic mask into a thin film or the substrate itself. It is the **second major pattern-transfer step** in the integrated circuit (IC) fabrication sequence, immediately following photolithography.

The three principal functions of etching in VLSI are:
1. **Pattern Transfer** — replicating the lithographic image into underlying layers.
2. **Dimensional Control** — defining critical dimensions (CD) of transistor gates, interconnects, and vias.
3. **Surface Preparation** — cleaning residues, native oxides, and damaged regions between process steps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Etching is grouped under **Module 2 – Wafer Fabrication Processes (Pattern Transfer)** and must be studied in conjunction with the **Lithography** unit to fully understand the pattern-transfer chain: *Oxidation → Deposition → Lithography → Etching → Ion Implantation/Diffusion*.

## 1.2 Etching – Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a **decorative cake with multiple layers** (sponge, cream, chocolate) and you want to engrave a precise message (your name) on the **top chocolate layer** without damaging the layers beneath.

- You first place a **stencil** (the **photoresist mask**) shaped like your name on the chocolate.
- Then you apply a **chemical spray or sand-blasting stream** that **eats away only the exposed chocolate**, leaving the stencil-covered regions untouched.
- Finally, you lift the stencil to reveal your engraved name on the cake.

**Etching in VLSI works exactly the same way**:
- The **photoresist** is the stencil.
- The **etchant** (chemical gas or liquid) is the spray.
- The **film to be patterned** is the chocolate.
- The **underlying material** must remain intact (high *selectivity*).
- The **vertical sidewalls** of the engraved pattern must be straight (high *anisotropy*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is etching important?**
> Modern CMOS transistors (FinFET, GAA) require near-atomic-precision sidewalls. A deviation of just **2–3 nm** in gate etch can shift threshold voltage ($V_T$) by tens of millivolts, degrading chip performance. Hence, etching is a **critical, yield-defining step**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology – At a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Etch Rate (ER)** | Material thickness removed per unit time | **100 – 1000 nm/min** |
| **Selectivity (S)** | Ratio of target-film etch rate to mask/under-layer etch rate | **10 : 1 to 100 : 1** |
| **Anisotropy (A)** | Degree of directionality of the etch | **0 (isotropic) → 1 (fully anisotropic)** |
| **Uniformity** | Variation of etch rate across the wafer | **± 2 – 5 %** |
| **Etch Profile** | Shape of the sidewall after etching | **Vertical, tapered, or undercut** |
| **CD Bias** | Difference between printed and etched feature size | **< 5 nm** for advanced nodes |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Etching Processes

VLSI etching is broadly classified into two families, with several sub-categories:

### A. Wet Etching (Chemical)
- Uses **liquid chemicals** (e.g., HF for SiO₂, KOH for Si, H₃PO₄ for Al).
- Purely **chemical reaction** between etchant and film.
- Generally **isotropic** (etches in all directions equally).
- High **selectivity**, low **cost**, simple equipment.
- **Disadvantage:** poor CD control, capillary effects, particle contamination.

### B. Dry Etching (Plasma / Vacuum)
- Uses **gaseous or plasma-activated species** in a vacuum chamber.
- Combines **chemical** and **physical (ion-bombardment)** mechanisms.
- Can achieve **high anisotropy** by accelerating ions vertically toward the wafer.
- **Mandatory** for sub-micron and nanoscale geometries.
- Variants: Plasma Etching, Reactive Ion Etching (RIE), Ion-Beam Etching, Magnetron-Enhanced RIE (MERIE), Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP), Chemical Downstream Etching (DSE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The Module-2 question bank **always** asks to "compare wet and dry etching" or to "explain the role of plasma in achieving anisotropy". Memorize the selectivity/anisotropy trade-off table given below.

## 2.2 Isotropic vs Anisotropic Etching

### Isotropic Etching
- Etch rate is **equal in all directions** (lateral = vertical).
- Produces a **semi-circular undercut** beneath the mask edge.
- Characterized by: $A = 1 - \dfrac{R_l}{R_v} = 0$ (where $R_l$ = lateral etch rate, $R_v$ = vertical etch rate).
- Used in: **cleaning steps, native-oxide removal, MEMS release**.

### Anisotropic Etching
- Vertical etch rate $\gg$ lateral etch rate.
- Sidewalls are **vertical or tapered** at a controlled angle.
- $A = 1 - \dfrac{R_l}{R_v} \approx 1$.
- Achieved by **directional ion bombardment** in plasma.
- Essential for **gate definition, contact/via etch, damascene trenches**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Etch Rate** | $ER = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | nm/min | Thickness removed per unit time |
| 2 | **Selectivity** | $S = \dfrac{ER_{film}}{ER_{mask}}$ | dimensionless (e.g., 20:1) | Preference of etchant for film over mask |
| 3 | **Anisotropy** | $A = 1 - \dfrac{R_l}{R_v}$ | 0 → 1 | Directionality of etch (0 = iso, 1 = aniso) |
| 4 | **Undercut (lateral etch)** | $u = R_l \cdot t$ | nm | Distance etched under the mask |
| 5 | **Sidewall angle (taper)** | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R_v}{R_l}\right)$ | degrees | Slope of the etched sidewall |
| 6 | **Sputter Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | atoms/ion | Efficiency of physical etching |
| 7 | **Mean Free Path (plasma)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\, d^2 P}$ | m | Average distance between gas collisions |
| 8 | **Debye Length (plasma sheath)** | $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^2}}$ | m | Width of plasma sheath (controls ion directionality) |
| 9 | **Etch Uniformity** | $U\,(\%) = \dfrac{ER_{max} - ER_{min}}{2 \cdot ER_{avg}} \times 100$ | % | Spatial variation across wafer |
| 10 | **Knudsen Number** | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | dimensionless | Indicates vacuum regime (molecular vs viscous) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to memorize for KTU derivations:** Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, elementary charge $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, standard temperature $T = 300$ K (room-temperature plasma chemistry).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Etch Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Shallow Trench Isolation (STI)** | RIE / ICP | Vertical sidewalls, high Si:SiO₂ selectivity |
| **Polysilicon Gate Definition** | RIE with HBr/Cl₂ plasma | High anisotropy, CD control < 5 nm |
| **Dielectric Via Etch** | C₄F₈ / CHF₃ plasma | High SiO₂:Si₃N₄ selectivity, tapered profile |
| **Metal (Al, Cu) Interconnect** | Cl-based / RIE | Volatile by-products (AlCl₃, CuCl₂) |
| **Silicon Bulk Micromachining** | Wet KOH/TMAH | Anisotropy along crystal planes (111) |
| **GaAs / InP Device** | BCl₃ / CH₄/H₂ RIE | Selective over mask and stop-layer |
| **Hard-Mask Opening** | CF₄/O₂ plasma | High Si₃N₄:SiO₂ selectivity |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Anisotropy From Lateral and Vertical Etch Rates

**Statement:** Show that the anisotropy factor $A$ of an etching process lies between **0 (perfectly isotropic)** and **1 (perfectly anisotropic)**, and derive the sidewall taper angle.

### Step 1: Define the etch-rate vector

Consider an etch front proceeding into a film with vertical component $R_v$ and lateral (under-mask) component $R_l$.

$$
\vec{R} = R_v \,\hat{y} + R_l \,\hat{x}
$$

### Step 2: Normalize to vertical rate

Define the lateral-to-vertical ratio:

$$
k = \dfrac{R_l}{R_v}
$$

### Step 3: Construct the anisotropy factor

By definition:

$$
A = 1 - \dfrac{R_l}{R_v} = 1 - k
$$

- If $R_l = 0 \Rightarrow A = 1$ (perfectly anisotropic, vertical walls).
- If $R_l = R_v \Rightarrow A = 0$ (perfectly isotropic, circular undercut).
- Since $0 \le R_l \le R_v$, the range is $0 \le A \le 1$. $\;\;\blacksquare$

### Step 4: Derive the sidewall taper angle

The sidewall makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. Geometry of the etch front gives:

$$
\tan\theta = \dfrac{R_v}{R_l} = \dfrac{1}{k}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R_v}{R_l}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-A}\right)
$$

For $A = 1$, $\theta = 90°$ (vertical wall). For $A = 0$, $\theta = 45°$. $\;\;\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Selectivity and Over-Etch Time

**Statement:** A 200 nm polysilicon film is to be etched through a 1.0 µm photoresist mask using a plasma with selectivity $S = 25$ (polysilicon : photoresist). Find the maximum allowable over-etch time if **mask loss must remain below 100 nm** beyond the nominal etch time.

### Step 1: Express selectivity

$$
S = \dfrac{ER_{poly}}{ER_{PR}} = 25
$$

### Step 2: Find resist etch rate

Assume $ER_{poly} = 250$ nm/min (typical industrial value). Then:

$$
ER_{PR} = \dfrac{ER_{poly}}{S} = \dfrac{250}{25} = 10\;\text{nm/min}
$$

### Step 3: Compute allowable over-etch

$$
t_{over,max} = \dfrac{\Delta d_{PR}}{ER_{PR}} = \dfrac{100\;\text{nm}}{10\;\text{nm/min}} = 10\;\text{min}
$$

### Step 4: Total etch time including 25 % over-etch safety

$$
t_{nominal} = \dfrac{d_{poly}}{ER_{poly}} = \dfrac{200}{250} = 0.8\;\text{min}
$$

$$
t_{total} = t_{nominal} \times 1.25 = 0.8 \times 1.25 = 1.0\;\text{min}
$$

Over-etch margin available: $1.0 - 0.8 = 0.2$ min $\ll 10$ min ⇒ **plenty of process window**. $\;\;\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Plasma Sheath Width (Debye Length)

**Statement:** A typical argon plasma used for sputter etching has electron density $n_e = 10^{16}$ m⁻³ and electron temperature $T_e = 3$ eV. Calculate the Debye length $\lambda_D$, which sets the **minimum sheath thickness** controlling ion directionality.

### Step 1: Convert $T_e$ to Kelvin

$$
T_e = 3 \;\text{eV} \times \dfrac{11604\;\text{K}}{1\;\text{eV}} = 34{,}812\;\text{K}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Debye length formula

$$
\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^2}}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute numerical values

$$
\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \cdot (3.4812 \times 10^{4})}{(10^{16}) \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}}
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate numerator and denominator

- Numerator: $8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 3.4812 \times 10^{4} \approx 4.254 \times 10^{-30}$
- Denominator: $10^{16} \times 2.56 \times 10^{-38} = 2.56 \times 10^{-22}$

$$
\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.254 \times 10^{-30}}{2.56 \times 10^{-22}}} = \sqrt{1.661 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.29 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda_D \approx 129\;\mu\text{m}}
$$

This sheath thickness is **much larger** than typical feature sizes (10–100 nm), ensuring ions traverse the sheath **ballistically** and arrive **perpendicular** to the wafer — the origin of etch anisotropy. $\;\;\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of Dry-Etching Techniques

| Parameter | Plasma Etch | RIE | DSE | IBE | MERIE / ICP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure (mTorr)** | 100 – 1000 | 10 – 100 | 100 – 1000 | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-4}$ | 1 – 50 |
| **Ion Energy (eV)** | Low (< 50) | 100 – 800 | Low (≈ 0) | 500 – 1500 | 100 – 500 |
| **Mechanism** | Chemical | Chem + Physical | Pure chemical | Pure physical | Chem + Physical (decoupled) |
| **Anisotropy** | Poor–Fair | High | Poor | High | Very High |
| **Selectivity** | Very High | Moderate | Very High | Low | High |
| **Damage** | Low | Moderate–High | None | High | Low–Moderate |
| **Throughput** | High | Moderate | High | Low | High |
| **CD Control** | Poor | Excellent | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Typical Use** | Resist strip, oxide | Gate, contact etch | SiN removal, native oxide | Specialty, III–V | Advanced node, high aspect ratio |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Dry Etching Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wafer with Patterned Photoresist] --> B[Load into Vacuum Chamber]
    B --> C[Evacuate to Base Pressure 1e-7 Torr]
    C --> D[Introduce Process Gases e.g. CF4 HBr Cl2]
    D --> E[RF Power Applied 13.56 MHz]
    E --> F[Plasma Ignition Glow Discharge]
    F --> G[Generation of Reactive Species and Ions]
    G --> H[Species Diffuse to Wafer Surface]
    H --> I[Chemical Reaction at Surface]
    I --> J[Volatile Byproducts Form]
    J --> K[Byproducts Pumped Out]
    K --> L[Etched Wafer with Transferred Pattern]
    L --> M[Strip Photoresist]
    M --> N[Proceed to Next Fabrication Step]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Wet vs Dry Etching Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pattern Transfer Required] --> B{Critical Dimension less than 1 micrometer}
    B -- No --> C[Use Wet Etching]
    B -- Yes --> D{Underlayer Sensitivity High}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Vapor Phase or DSE]
    D -- No --> F{Anisotropy Required}
    F -- No --> G[Use Barrel Plasma Etcher]
    F -- Yes --> H{Sub 100 nm Feature}
    H -- No --> I[Use Conventional RIE]
    H -- Yes --> J[Use ICP or MERIE Reactor]
    C --> K[Isotropic High Selectivity Low Cost]
    E --> L[Chemical Only No Damage]
    I --> M[Vertical Profile Moderate Damage]
    J --> N[High Aspect Ratio Low Damage Excellent CD]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram – Parallel-Plate RIE Reactor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REACTOR[Parallel Plate RIE Chamber]
        G1[Gas Inlet CF4 O2 HBr] --> SHOWER[Showerhead Upper Electrode]
        SHOWER --> PLASMA[Plasma Zone]
        PLASMA --> SHEATH[Plasma Sheath Dark Region]
        SHEATH --> WAFER[Wafer on Lower Electrode Cathode]
        WAFER --> CHUCK[He-cooled Electrostatic Chuck]
        CHUCK --> BIAS[RF Bias 13.56 MHz]
        BIAS --> MATCH[Impedance Match Network]
        MATCH --> RF1[RF Generator 0 to 1500 W]
        PLASMA --> PUMP[Pumping Port to Vacuum]
        PUMP --> VAC[Turbo Pump and Dry Pump]
    end
    RF1 -.Controls ion energy.-> SHEATH
    SHOWER -.Ground reference.-> PLASMA
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram – Anisotropy Mechanism in RIE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RF Voltage Applied] --> B[Plasma Sheath Forms]
    B --> C[Positive Ions Accelerated Vertically]
    C --> D[Ions Strike Wafer Perpendicular]
    D --> E[Bottom of Trench Receives Maximum Ion Flux]
    D --> F[Sidewalls Receive Minimal Ion Flux]
    E --> G[Vertical Etch Enhanced]
    F --> H[Lateral Etch Suppressed]
    G --> I[Anisotropic Vertical Profile]
    H --> I
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram – Plasma Generation Chemistry in CF₄/O₂

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CF4 Molecule] --> B[Electron Impact Dissociation]
    B --> C[CF3 Radical and F Radical]
    O2[O2 Molecule] --> D[Electron Impact Dissociation]
    D --> E[O Radical]
    C --> F[SiO2 Surface Reaction]
    E --> F
    F --> G[SiF4 Gas Volatile Byproduct]
    G --> H[Pumped Out of Chamber]
    I[Recombination] --> J[CF3 plus O gives COF2 plus F]
    J --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Check:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B1`, `REACTOR`, `SHOWER`, etc.), all labels are free of `**` and `*` markers, and no reserved keyword (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) is used as a standalone node. The diagrams render correctly in Mermaid v10+.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]**
**Define selectivity in etching. A silicon dioxide film of thickness 500 nm is etched using a buffered oxide etch (BOE) with etch rate 100 nm/min, while the underlying silicon nitride is etched at 2 nm/min. Calculate the selectivity and comment on the process choice for a via-etch application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Selectivity $S$ is the ratio of the etch rate of the target film to that of the underlying (or masking) material:

$$
S = \dfrac{ER_{SiO_2}}{ER_{Si_3N_4}} = \dfrac{100}{2} = 50 : 1
$$

**Calculation (1 mark):** Time required to etch 500 nm of SiO₂:

$$
t = \dfrac{500\;\text{nm}}{100\;\text{nm/min}} = 5\;\text{min}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** With $S = 50:1$, during the 5 min etch only $5 \times 2 = 10$ nm of Si₃N₄ will be lost. This is acceptable for a **via-etch stop layer application**, but if Si₃N₄ is only 20 nm thick, an alternative dry etch (CHF₃/C₄F₈) with $S > 100:1$ should be used.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**
**Distinguish between isotropic and anisotropic etching. Give one example process for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Isotropic | Anisotropic |
|---|---|---|
| Etch direction | Equal in all directions | Predominantly vertical |
| Anisotropy factor $A$ | 0 | ≈ 1 |
| Sidewall profile | Curved, undercut | Vertical or tapered |
| Mechanism | Pure chemical | Chemical + physical (ion) |
| Example | BOE for SiO₂, KOH for non-(100) Si | RIE with HBr/Cl₂ for polysilicon gates |

**Examples (1 mark each):**
- **Isotropic example:** Wet etching of SiO₂ in dilute HF (1 % HF) – lateral etch rate equals vertical etch rate.
- **Anisotropic example:** Polysilicon gate etch in RIE using HBr/Cl₂/O₂ plasma – vertical ion bombardment produces $A > 0.95$.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, With Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never write "plasma etches" without naming the chemistry.** A statement like "dry etching uses plasma" loses 1–2 marks. Always mention the **gas system** (e.g., CF₄/O₂, HBr/Cl₂, BCl₃/Cl₂).
> 2. **Do not confuse plasma etching with RIE.** They differ in **pressure, ion energy, and anisotropy**. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Always state selectivity numerically.** Writing only "high selectivity" is incomplete. Quote a ratio (e.g., 30:1) and the units.
> 4. **Draw the etch profile** (vertical, tapered, undercut) for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 5. **For 14-mark questions**, mention **at least one limitation** of the process to earn the "critical analysis" marks allocated under **CO₂ (Analyze)**.

### **Question A – 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a) Explain the principle and construction of a Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) system. Discuss the role of the plasma sheath in achieving anisotropy. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Principle (3 marks):** RIE combines **chemical reactivity** of plasma-generated radicals with **directional physical bombardment** of accelerated ions. A low-pressure (10–100 mTorr) RF discharge is struck between two parallel electrodes; the wafer sits on the **powered (cathode) electrode** so that it develops a large **negative DC self-bias voltage** ($V_{dc} = -300$ to $-800$ V). Positive ions from the plasma are accelerated across the sheath onto the wafer **perpendicular to the surface**, providing directional energy that drives the etch vertically while protecting the sidewalls.

**2. Construction (2 marks):**
- **Vacuum chamber** with stainless-steel walls, base pressure $10^{-7}$ Torr.
- **Two parallel electrodes:** upper showerhead (grounded, gas inlet), lower cathode (powered, wafer stage).
- **RF generator** at 13.56 MHz connected via an **impedance-matching network**.
- **Mass-flow controllers** for process gases (e.g., SF₆, CF₄, HBr, Cl₂, O₂, Ar).
- **Turbo-molecular + dry roughing pump** stack for high-vacuum operation.
- **Electrostatic chuck (ESC)** with helium backside cooling for wafer temperature control.
- **Optical emission spectrometer (OES)** for end-point detection.

**3. Plasma Sheath & Anisotropy (2 marks):**
The plasma sheath is a thin, electron-depleted region (Debye length $\lambda_D \sim 50$–$500\;\mu$m) that forms adjacent to every surface in contact with the plasma. Because electrons (lighter, faster) leave the surface faster than ions, the wall acquires a negative potential. The resulting electric field **E** in the sheath accelerates positive ions **normal to the wafer**:

$$
E_{sheath} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{\lambda_D}
$$

- Ions strike the **horizontal trench bottom** with full energy → **vertical etch enhanced**.
- Ions strike the **vertical sidewalls** at glancing angle → **lateral etch suppressed**.
- Net result: $R_v \gg R_l$, giving $A \to 1$ and near-vertical sidewalls.

**(b) A 300 nm polysilicon gate is to be patterned over a 2 nm gate-oxide. The HBr/Cl₂/O₂ plasma has poly-Si etch rate 200 nm/min, SiO₂ etch rate 2 nm/min, and photoresist etch rate 80 nm/min. Determine: (i) selectivity of poly-Si over SiO₂; (ii) selectivity of poly-Si over photoresist; (iii) whether the process is suitable for the application. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Selectivity poly-Si : SiO₂ (2 marks):**

$$
S_{poly/SiO_2} = \dfrac{ER_{poly}}{ER_{SiO_2}} = \dfrac{200}{2} = 100 : 1
$$

Time to clear 300 nm poly-Si:

$$
t_{clear} = \dfrac{300}{200} = 1.5\;\text{min}
$$

Gate-oxide loss in this time:

$$
\Delta d_{SiO_2} = 2 \;\text{nm/min} \times 1.5\;\text{min} = 3.0\;\text{nm}
$$

This **far exceeds** the 2 nm gate-oxide thickness — the underlying oxide would be **completely removed**, destroying the device. **The process is NOT suitable without modification.**

**(ii) Selectivity poly-Si : Photoresist (1 mark):**

$$
S_{poly/PR} = \dfrac{200}{80} = 2.5 : 1
$$

This is **marginal** for a 300 nm film. PR thickness must be $\ge 300/2.5 = 120$ nm before etch.

**(iii) Modification Required (2 marks):**
- Switch to a **higher-selectivity chemistry**, e.g., **HBr/Cl₂ with added O₂ and N₂**, achieving $S_{poly/SiO_2} > 200:1$, or
- Add an **O₂-free HBr chemistry** with $ER_{SiO_2} < 1$ nm/min, or
- Introduce a **hard mask** (SiO₂ or Si₃N₄, 50 nm) under the resist to improve effective selectivity.

---

### **Question B – 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a) Compare wet etching and dry (plasma) etching with respect to anisotropy, selectivity, equipment cost, CD control, and damage. Which is preferred for sub-100 nm gate definition and why? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Wet Etching | Dry (Plasma) Etching |
|---|---|---|
| **Anisotropy** | Mostly isotropic ($A \approx 0$) | Anisotropic ($A \to 1$ in RIE/ICP) |
| **Selectivity** | Very high (often > 100:1) | Moderate to high (10:1 – 100:1) |
| **CD Control** | Poor (capillary, bubble artefacts) | Excellent (sub-10 nm) |
| **Damage** | None (chemical only) | Possible (ion bombardment, radiation, charging) |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low (beaker, fume hood) | High (vacuum, RF, pumps, OES) |
| **Throughput** | High (batch, 25–50 wafers) | Lower (single-wafer) |
| **Endpoint** | Visual / timed | OES, interferometry, impedance |
| **Safety** | Acid/base handling, disposal | Toxic/corrosive gases, high voltage |

**For sub-100 nm gate definition (2 marks):** **Dry (plasma) etching — specifically RIE or ICP — is mandatory** because:
1. The gate CD tolerance is **± 2–3 nm**, achievable only with vertical (anisotropic) profiles.
2. Wet etching's isotropic undercut would **erase the gate** long before the film is cleared.
3. Plasma can be tuned for **high selectivity** against the thin (1–2 nm) gate oxide using halogen-free or O₂-rich chemistries.

**(b) Describe the construction and working of an Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) reactor. Explain why it is the preferred tool for advanced-node etching. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (3 marks):**
- **Dielectric (alumina or quartz) chamber** with a planar or cylindrical **induction coil** (3–5 turns) wrapped outside, separated from the plasma by the dielectric window.
- **Separate RF sources:** (i) **Source RF** (typically 13.56 MHz, 1–5 kW) on the coil generates the plasma; (ii) **Bias RF** (also 13.56 MHz or lower, $\le$ 1 kW) on the lower electrode controls ion energy independently.
- **Wafer stage** with electrostatic chuck, helium backside cooling, and **z-axis lift** for gap control.
- **Gas injection ring** at the top with mass-flow controllers for multi-gas recipes.
- **Slotted anode** or **aluminum chamber walls** as the ground return path.

**2. Working (2 marks):** The induction coil carries RF current that generates an **azimuthal electric field** inside the chamber via Faraday's law. This time-varying field induces eddy currents in the plasma, **ionizing the gas** efficiently and producing a **high-density plasma** ($n_e = 10^{11}$–$10^{12}$ cm⁻³) at **low pressure** (1–25 mTorr). Since the coil is electrically isolated, the plasma potential stays low. The **separate bias RF** on the wafer chuck controls ion energy **independently** of plasma density.

**3. Why preferred for advanced nodes (2 marks):**
- **Decoupled control** of ion flux and ion energy → both can be optimized simultaneously.
- **High plasma density** → high etch rate (e.g., > 500 nm/min for Si), enabling high aspect-ratio features (HAR > 30:1 in 3D NAND).
- **Low pressure** → long mean free path, low collisional scattering, **vertical ion trajectories**.
- **Low plasma potential** → **reduced charging damage** to thin gate dielectrics.
- **Excellent CD control** for FinFET gates, GAA nanosheet channels, and EUV-patterned features.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist – Etching (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Etching** is the **selective removal** of material to transfer the lithographic pattern.
- ✅ Two families: **Wet (chemical, isotropic, high selectivity)** and **Dry (plasma, anisotropic, high CD control)**.
- ✅ Key etch parameters: **Etch Rate, Selectivity, Anisotropy, Uniformity, CD Bias, Sidewall Angle**.
- ✅ **Anisotropy formula:** $A = 1 - R_l / R_v$, range 0 → 1.
- ✅ **Selectivity formula:** $S = ER_{target} / ER_{mask}$, dimensionless ratio.
- ✅ **Plasma sheath** (Debye length $\lambda_D$) accelerates ions **perpendicular to the wafer** → directional etch → anisotropy.
- ✅ **RIE** is the workhorse for sub-micron patterning (pressure 10–100 mTorr, ion energy 100–800 eV, $A > 0.9$).
- ✅ **ICP** is preferred for advanced nodes because it **decouples ion flux (source RF) from ion energy (bias RF)**.
- ✅ **Wet KOH/TMAH** etches Si anisotropically along crystal planes (100 vs 111 etch rate ratio > 30:1) — crucial for **MEMS bulk micromachining**.
- ✅ **Common chemistries:** CF₄/O₂ (SiO₂, Si₃N₄), HBr/Cl₂ (poly-Si, metal), SF₆ (Si), BCl₃/Cl₂ (Al, GaAs), C₄F₈/CHF₃ (dielectric, Bosch DRIE).
- ✅ **Endpoint detection** uses **OES, laser interferometry, or impedance monitoring** — never rely on time alone for production wafers.
- ✅ **Mask loss** must be calculated: $d_{mask,loss} = ER_{mask} \times t_{over-etch}$; ensure $d_{mask,loss} < 10\%$ of mask thickness.
- ✅ **For sub-100 nm gates:** mandatory dry etch (RIE/ICP) with $S_{poly/oxide} > 100:1$.
- ✅ **Damage mechanisms in plasma etching:** ion-impact displacement, radiation damage, charging of thin oxides, polymer residue, notching at high aspect ratios.
- ✅ **Draw etch profiles** (vertical, tapered, retrograde, undercut) in every 7-mark answer — visuals earn easy marks.
- ✅ **Cite gas chemistry and selectivity ratios** explicitly — vague answers lose marks.

> **End of Module 2 – Etching Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PBECT604 VLSI Circuit Design**
