topics.title: Chemical Vapor Deposition and Physical Vapor Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chemical Vapor Deposition and Physical Vapor Deposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)** is a thin-film deposition process in which one or more volatile precursors react chemically on or near a heated substrate surface to produce a non-volatile solid film, while the by-products of the reaction are transported away via a carrier gas stream. The substrate temperature typically lies in the range of **600 °C – 900 °C** for thermally activated CVD variants, while plasma-enhanced variants operate between **200 °C – 400 °C**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Picture):**
Imagine you are spray-painting a wall, but instead of wet paint, you spray two invisible, dry gases. When the gases touch the warm wall, they *chemically combine* (a reaction) and instantly form a solid, hard, even layer of paint on the wall. The leftover invisible gas drifts away. That is essentially CVD — a **chemical recipe** performed in gas form, with the warm wafer acting as the "kitchen counter" where the solid film bakes itself into existence.

### 1.2 Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)** is a vacuum-based thin-film deposition technique in which a target material is physically transformed into a vapor phase (via thermal evaporation, electron-beam heating, or momentum transfer through ion bombardment) and subsequently transported through a low-pressure ambient to condense as a thin film on a cooler substrate. PVD is fundamentally a **physical** (not chemical) process, conducted at working pressures typically in the range of **10⁻³ mbar – 10⁻⁹ mbar**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Picture):**
Think of boiling water in a sealed pot. Tiny water molecules (the "vapor") rise and hit the cool lid, forming droplets. Now replace the boiling water with a melted metal ingot, and the cool lid with your silicon wafer. The metal atoms physically travel, hit the wafer, and stick. No chemical reaction — just a *physical journey* from hot source to cold substrate. That is PVD.

### 1.3 Position in the VLSI Process Flow

> [!NOTE]
> In a modern CMOS process flow, CVD and PVD are used for **interconnect and contact formation**:
> - **PVD (Sputtering)** → Aluminum, Ti, TiN barrier layers, Cu seed layers
> - **CVD (LPCVD / PECVD)** → Doped polysilicon, SiO₂, Si₃N₄, low-k dielectrics, tungsten plugs

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) — Operational Breakdown

The CVD process is governed by a sequence of mass-transport and surface-reaction steps. Mastering these steps is essential for KTU Part B questions.

**Step 1 — Gas Transport (Bulk Transport):**
Reactant gases (e.g., **SiH₄** for silicon, **TEOS** for SiO₂, **WF₆** for tungsten) are introduced into a reaction chamber via a carrier gas (**N₂, H₂, Ar**). They flow toward the wafer through the boundary layer.

**Step 2 — Gas-Phase Reaction (Homogeneous):**
Some precursors may decompose or react in the gas phase before reaching the wafer. Excessive gas-phase reaction causes particle contamination and poor film quality — a key reason LPCVD is preferred over APCVD for critical films.

**Step 3 — Surface Adsorption:**
Precursor molecules physically or chemically adsorb onto the heated wafer surface. The substrate temperature governs the adsorption rate constant.

**Step 4 — Surface Diffusion & Reaction (Heterogeneous):**
Adsorbed species migrate along the surface and undergo the desired chemical reaction, releasing by-products (e.g., HCl, H₂, CO₂).

**Step 5 — Desorption & Removal of By-Products:**
Volatile by-products desorb from the surface and are swept away by the exhaust gas stream.

**Step 6 — Film Growth:**
The continuous deposition of reacted atoms/molecules produces a uniform, conformal thin film.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the wafer need to be heated?**
> Because the Arrhenius equation tells us that reaction rate $R$ scales exponentially with temperature $T$:
> $$R \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$
> Lower temperature → exponentially slower deposition; higher temperature → faster but may degrade film morphology or damage the wafer.

### 2.2 Sub-Types of CVD

| Variant | Pressure | Temperature | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **APCVD** (Atmospheric Pressure CVD) | **760 Torr** | High | Simple, fast, poor step coverage |
| **LPCVD** (Low Pressure CVD) | **0.1 – 10 Torr** | 600 °C – 800 °C | Excellent uniformity & step coverage |
| **PECVD** (Plasma-Enhanced CVD) | 0.1 – 10 Torr | **200 °C – 400 °C** | Plasma assists reaction → lower temperature |
| **HDPCVD** (High-Density Plasma CVD) | mTorr range | ~400 °C | Excellent gap-fill for deep sub-µm trenches |
| **MOCVD** (Metal-Organic CVD) | varies | 500 °C – 700 °C | Used for compound semiconductors (GaAs, InP) |
| **RTCVD** (Rapid Thermal CVD) | low | very high, short time | Single-wafer, fast ramp |

### 2.3 Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) — Operational Breakdown

PVD relies on physical ejection of atoms from a *target* and their condensation on a *substrate*. There are two principal families:

**Family A — Vacuum Thermal Evaporation:**
A refractory metal filament or a crucible is heated (resistively or via electron beam) until the source material vaporizes. The vapor travels in straight-line (line-of-sight) trajectories and condenses on the cooler substrate.

**Family B — Sputtering (Momentum-Transfer Evaporation):**
A plasma of inert argon ions is generated. Positive Ar⁺ ions are accelerated toward the negatively biased target, physically knocking out (sputtering) target atoms. These ejected atoms travel to the substrate and deposit.

**Sputtering Sub-Types:**

- **DC Sputtering** → used for conductive targets (Al, Ti, Cu)
- **RF Sputtering** → used for insulating targets (SiO₂, Al₂O₃) — the AC coupling prevents charge buildup
- **Magnetron Sputtering** → magnets behind the target confine electrons → higher plasma density → **higher deposition rate** and **lower substrate heating**
- **Ionized PVD (IPVD)** → a secondary RF coil ionizes the sputtered flux → excellent bottom coverage for high-aspect-ratio vias

### 2.4 Step Coverage Comparison (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step coverage** is defined as the ratio of the minimum film thickness (on a sidewall or bottom of a step) to the maximum film thickness (on the top horizontal surface).
> $$\text{Step Coverage (\%)} = \frac{t_{\min}}{t_{\max}} \times 100$$
> For **VLSI interconnects** at sub-µm dimensions, KTU expects a step coverage of **≥ 80%** as a benchmark. LPCVD easily achieves > 90%, while evaporation-based PVD may fall below 30% for high-aspect-ratio features.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deposition rate | $R = \frac{\Delta t}{\Delta \tau}$ | nm/min | Comparing CVD vs PVD |
| Step coverage | $\text{SC} = \dfrac{t_{\min}}{t_{\max}} \times 100$ | % | Evaluating conformality |
| Arrhenius growth rate | $R = R_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | — | Temperature dependence |
| Mean free path | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2 P}$ | m | PVD gas scattering limit |
| Sputtering yield | $Y = \dfrac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}}$ | atoms/ion | Sputter efficiency |
| Film stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu}\,\epsilon$ | Pa | Residual stress control |
| Partial pressure of precursor | $P_i = y_i P_{\text{total}}$ | Torr | Gas-phase chemistry |
| Sticking coefficient | $S_c = \dfrac{\text{atoms sticking}}{\text{atoms impinging}}$ | 0 to 1 | Surface reaction rate |
| Boundary layer thickness | $\delta \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu x}{\rho u_\infty}}$ | m | CVD mass transport |
| Knudsen number | $K_n = \dfrac{\lambda}{L}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime (molecular vs viscous) |
| Deposition uniformity | $U = 1 - \dfrac{t_{\max} - t_{\min}}{t_{\max} + t_{\min}}$ | % | Wafer-scale consistency |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CVD** is the workhorse for **inter-layer dielectrics (ILD)**, **shallow trench isolation (STI)**, and **tungsten contact plugs** in modern sub-22 nm CMOS logic.
- **PVD (sputtering)** dominates **metal interconnect formation** — Al, Cu, Ti, Ta, TaN barriers are all sputter-deposited in 300 mm fabs.
- **HDPCVD** is critical for **STI gap-fill** because it deposits and sputter-etches simultaneously, preventing void formation in narrow trenches.
- **Magnetron sputtering** powers the **hard-disk industry** for thin-film magnetic media deposition.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of CVD Growth Rate

The surface reaction rate $R$ in a thermally activated CVD process follows the Arrhenius form. We start from the kinetic theory of gases and transition-state theory.

**Starting premise (kinetic theory):**
The number of molecules striking unit area of the substrate per unit time (the flux $J$) is given by the Hertz–Knudsen equation:
$$J = \frac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$

where $P$ is the partial pressure of the precursor gas, $m$ is the molecular mass, $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

**Assumption for surface-reaction-limited growth:**
At low temperatures, every impinging molecule does *not* react. Only a fraction, governed by the Boltzmann factor for surmounting the activation energy barrier $E_a$, reacts to form the film. The reaction rate per unit area is therefore:
$$R = \eta \, J \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $\eta$ is a pre-exponential efficiency factor that absorbs the sticking coefficient and reaction pathway constants.

**Substituting $J$ into the rate expression:**
$$R = \eta \cdot \frac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Defining the lumped pre-exponential constant:**
$$R_0 \equiv \frac{\eta P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B}}$$

**Final closed-form expression (the canonical KTU result):**
$$R = \frac{R_0}{\sqrt{T}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> For narrow temperature ranges (as in KTU numericals), the $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$ term is approximately constant and is absorbed into $R_0$, giving the simplified form $R = R_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$. The examiner expects you to write this simplified form unless explicitly asked to derive it from first principles.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Free Path and Its Impact on PVD

In PVD, atoms ejected from the target must travel through a low-pressure gas to reach the substrate. The **mean free path** $\lambda$ determines whether atoms travel ballistically or get scattered.

**Derivation (from kinetic theory, hard-sphere model):**
Two identical hard spheres of diameter $d$ collide when their center-to-center distance equals $d$. The collision cross-section is:
$$\sigma_c = \pi d^2$$

The relative speed of two molecules with Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution is $\bar{v}_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{2}\,\bar{v}$, where $\bar{v} = \sqrt{8 k_B T / (\pi m)}$.

The mean free path is the average distance traveled between successive collisions:
$$\lambda = \frac{\bar{v}}{\bar{v}_{\text{rel}}} \cdot \frac{1}{n \sigma_c} = \frac{\bar{v}}{\sqrt{2}\,\bar{v}\,n\,\pi d^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,n\,\pi d^2}$$

Using the ideal-gas law $P = n k_B T$, we substitute $n = P / (k_B T)$:
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2} \cdot \frac{k_B T}{P}$$

**Final expression (the KTU benchmark formula):**
$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2\,P}$$

**Plug-in numerical check (typical PVD condition):**
For Ar atoms at $T = 300$ K, diameter $d \approx 0.34$ nm, and pressure $P = 10^{-3}$ mbar:

- $k_B T \approx 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300 = 4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J
- $\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,d^2 \approx 1.414 \times 3.1416 \times (0.34 \times 10^{-9})^2 = 5.13 \times 10^{-19}$ m²
- $P = 10^{-3} \times 100$ Pa = 0.1 Pa
- Denominator: $5.13 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.1 = 5.13 \times 10^{-20}$
- $\lambda \approx 4.14 \times 10^{-21} / 5.13 \times 10^{-20} \approx 0.08$ m = **8 cm**

This 8 cm is much larger than a typical source-to-substrate distance of **20 – 40 cm** in a sputtering system, ensuring **ballistic (line-of-sight) transport** — a fundamental reason PVD requires high vacuum.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sticking Coefficient and Its Role

The **sticking coefficient** $S_c$ is the probability that an atom impinging on the substrate remains adsorbed rather than reflecting back. It directly multiplies the impinging flux $J$ to give the net growth rate $R_g$:
$$R_g = S_c \cdot J \cdot \frac{M}{\rho\,N_A}$$

where $M$ is the molar mass of the film, $\rho$ is the film density, and $N_A$ is Avogadro's number. The factor $\frac{M}{\rho N_A}$ converts atoms per area to thickness per time.

> [!NOTE]
> For most metals (Al, Cu, Ti) on Si at room temperature, $S_c \approx 0.5 - 1.0$. For Si on Si at elevated temperature, $S_c \to 1$ (perfect epitaxy). For noble gases, $S_c \approx 0$.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CVD vs PVD

| Parameter | CVD | PVD (Sputtering/Evaporation) |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Nature** | Chemical reaction | Physical ejection |
| **Operating Pressure** | mTorr – 760 Torr | 10⁻³ – 10⁻⁹ mbar |
| **Substrate Temperature** | 200 °C – 900 °C | 25 °C – 400 °C |
| **Step Coverage** | Excellent (≥ 90%) | Moderate to poor (30 – 80%) |
| **Film Purity** | Very high | High (target-limited) |
| **Deposition Rate** | 1 – 100 nm/min | 1 – 1000 nm/min |
| **By-products** | Gaseous (must be exhausted) | None (purely physical) |
| **Equipment Cost** | Moderate to high | Moderate |
| **Conformal Coating** | Yes (LPCVD/PECVD) | Difficult (IPVD helps) |
| **Typical Materials** | SiO₂, Si₃N₄, W, poly-Si | Al, Cu, Ti, Ta, TaN |
| **Directionality** | Isotropic (omnidirectional) | Line-of-sight |
| **Plasma Use** | Optional (PECVD) | Essential (sputtering) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level CVD Reactor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gas Cabinet N2 H2 SiH4 TEOS WF6] --> B[Mass Flow Controllers MFC 1 to 5]
    B --> C[Gas Mixing Manifold]
    C --> D[Quartz Reaction Chamber]
    D --> E[Heated Susceptor Wafer 600 to 800 C]
    E --> F[Thin Film Deposition SiO2 Si3N4 W Poly Si]
    D --> G[Exhaust Line]
    G --> H[Vacuum Pump Roots Blower]
    H --> I[By Product Scrubber HCl H2]
    J[RF Plasma Coil optional PECVD] -.-> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Gases enter the cabinet, get metered by MFCs, mix, and enter the chamber
- The heated susceptor holds the wafer at the reaction temperature
- Plasma is optional (PECVD mode)
- Exhaust is scrubbed before release (safety critical for toxic SiH₄ and HCl)

### 4.2 Block-Level Sputtering PVD System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Substrate Load Lock] --> B[Transfer Chamber HV]
    B --> C[Process Chamber Base Pressure 10 to 8 mbar]
    D[Ar Gas Inlet 99.999 percent Purity] --> C
    C --> E[Target Cathode Ti Al Cu Ta]
    F[DC or RF Power Supply 0.5 to 5 kW] --> E
    G[Magnet Array Behind Target] -.-> E
    E --> H[Ar Plus Ion Bombardment]
    H --> I[Target Atoms Ejected Line of Sight]
    I --> J[Wafer Substrate Heated or Cooled]
    J --> K[Thin Film Condensation]
    C --> L[Cryopump or Turbopump]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Load lock isolates the process chamber, preserving high vacuum
- Magnetrons behind the target trap electrons, boosting plasma density
- Sputtered atoms travel ballistically to the substrate (mean free path large)
- Cryopump/turbopump maintains the low pressure required for ballistic transport

### 4.3 Process Flow Comparison (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CVD_Flow
        direction TB
        cv1[Precursor Vapor Generation] --> cv2[Gas Phase Diffusion]
        cv2 --> cv3[Adsorption on Heated Wafer]
        cv3 --> cv4[Surface Chemical Reaction]
        cv4 --> cv5[By product Desorption]
        cv5 --> cv6[Continuous Film Growth]
    end
    subgraph PVD_Flow
        direction TB
        pv1[Target Atom Ejection] --> pv2[Ballistic Transport in Vacuum]
        pv2 --> pv3[Condensation on Cooler Substrate]
        pv3 --> pv4[Nucleation and Island Growth]
        pv4 --> pv5[Coalescence into Continuous Film]
    end
```

### 4.4 Reaction Examples (KTU-Exam-Relevant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU-listed CVD reactions (memorize for short questions):**

- **Polysilicon (LPCVD):**
$$\text{SiH}_4(g) \xrightarrow{600\,^{\circ}\text{C}} \text{Si}(s) + 2\text{H}_2(g)$$

- **Silicon Dioxide (TEOS-based PECVD):**
$$\text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5\text{)}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{O}_2,\; \text{plasma}} \text{SiO}_2(s) + \text{by-products}$$

- **Tungsten (CVD for contact plugs):**
$$\text{WF}_6(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \xrightarrow{400\,^{\circ}\text{C}} \text{W}(s) + 6\text{HF}(g)$$

- **Silicon Nitride (LPCVD):**
$$3\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 4\text{NH}_3 \xrightarrow{750\,^{\circ}\text{C}} \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4(s) + 6\text{HCl} + 6\text{H}_2$$

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD). List any two advantages of LPCVD over APCVD.*

**Model Answer:**
CVD is a thin-film deposition technique in which volatile precursors react chemically on or near a heated substrate to form a solid film, with by-products removed by a carrier gas. **Two advantages of LPCVD over APCVD:**
1. **Better step coverage** — the lower pressure increases the mean free path, allowing precursors to penetrate high-aspect-ratio features uniformly.
2. **Lower gas-phase reaction** — reduced homogeneous nucleation leads to fewer particles and higher film purity.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between thermal evaporation and sputtering as methods of Physical Vapor Deposition.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Thermal Evaporation | Sputtering |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Heating source until vaporization | Momentum transfer from Ar⁺ ions |
| Pressure | 10⁻⁵ – 10⁻⁶ mbar | 10⁻³ mbar (with Ar) |
| Step coverage | Poor (line-of-sight) | Better, especially with IPVD |
| Target materials | Low melting-point metals | Almost any (incl. alloys, ceramics) |
| Film adhesion | Weak | Strong (energetic atoms) |
| Stoichiometry control | Difficult for compounds | Excellent |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this topic:**
> 1. Students often confuse **step coverage** with **conformality** — they are related but not identical (step coverage is a *geometric ratio*; conformality is a *qualitative descriptor*).
> 2. Many students write "CVD uses plasma" — this is **wrong** for thermal CVD; only **PECVD** uses plasma. Read the question carefully.
> 3. In sputtering yield questions, students often forget the units (atoms/ion) and lose 1 mark.
> 4. Drawing a CVD chamber without labeling **MFCs, susceptor, and exhaust** loses easy schematic marks.
> 5. Numerical problems on **mean free path** — students forget to convert **mbar to Pa** (1 mbar = 100 Pa).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **LPCVD reactor** for depositing polysilicon. List the chemical reaction and the typical deposition parameters (temperature, pressure). **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the working of a **magnetron sputtering system** with a block diagram. Why are magnets used behind the target? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
A Low-Pressure CVD reactor for polysilicon uses silane (SiH₄) as the precursor. The block diagram is given in Section 4.1.

**Working principle:**
1. SiH₄ gas diluted in N₂ is introduced through MFCs into a quartz tube furnace.
2. The wafer sits on a heated susceptor at **600 °C – 650 °C**.
3. The reaction chamber operates at low pressure (**0.25 – 2 Torr**) to suppress gas-phase nucleation.
4. SiH₄ molecules adsorb on the wafer, decompose via pyrolysis, and deposit Si atoms while H₂ desorbs.

**Chemical reaction:**
$$\text{SiH}_4(g) \xrightarrow{625\,^{\circ}\text{C},\; 0.5\,\text{Torr}} \text{Si}(s) + 2\text{H}_2(g)$$

**Typical deposition rate:** 10 – 30 nm/min.
**Step coverage:** Excellent (> 90% even in high-aspect-ratio trenches).
**Film quality:** Amorphous at < 580 °C, polycrystalline at > 600 °C.

**Model Solution (b):**
Magnetron sputtering is a PVD technique where plasma is confined near the target using magnetic fields, increasing deposition rate and reducing substrate damage.

**Working principle (refer to diagram in Section 4.2):**
1. A vacuum chamber is evacuated to **10⁻⁸ mbar** base pressure.
2. Argon is introduced at **10⁻³ mbar**.
3. A high DC (or RF) voltage (–500 V) is applied to the target, with the chamber walls grounded → Ar⁺ ions form and bombard the target.
4. Atoms ejected from the target travel ballistically and condense on the substrate.

**Why magnets behind the target?**
- The magnetic field $B$ confines secondary electrons in a **cycloidal path** near the target.
- Confined electrons undergo more ionizing collisions with Ar → **higher plasma density**.
- Higher plasma density → higher ion bombardment rate → **higher sputtering rate** (typically 5 – 10× higher than diode sputtering).
- Also, the substrate is farther from the dense plasma region → **less substrate heating** and less radiation damage.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Compare **CVD and PVD** in terms of principle, operating pressure, step coverage, and film purity. State two applications of each in VLSI fabrication. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The deposition rate of a CVD polysilicon film follows Arrhenius behavior: $R = R_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$. The rate doubles when the temperature is raised from 900 K to 920 K. Given $k_B = 8.62 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K, calculate the activation energy $E_a$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
(Refer to the **Analytical Comparison Matrix in Section 3.4** for the detailed table.)

**Applications of CVD in VLSI:**
1. **Polysilicon gate** deposition (LPCVD) in MOSFETs.
2. **Inter-layer dielectric (ILD)** — SiO₂ via TEOS-PECVD.
3. **Tungsten contact plug** fill (WF₆ + H₂ CVD).

**Applications of PVD in VLSI:**
1. **Aluminum or Copper interconnect** sputtering for metal layers.
2. **Ti/TiN barrier layer** deposition at via bottoms.
3. **Cu seed layer** deposition before electroplating (IPVD).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $R_2 = 2 R_1$ when $T_1 = 900$ K and $T_2 = 920$ K.
- $k_B = 8.62 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K.

**Step 1 — Set up the ratio:**
$$\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{R_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T_2)}{R_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T_1)} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the ratio:**
$$\exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right] = 2$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**
$$\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \ln 2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the temperature-difference term:**
$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{900} - \frac{1}{920} = \frac{920 - 900}{900 \times 920} = \frac{20}{828{,}000} \approx 2.415 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E_a$:**
$$E_a = k_B \cdot \ln 2 \cdot \frac{1}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}$$
$$E_a = (8.62 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.6931 \times \frac{1}{2.415 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$E_a = 5.974 \times 10^{-5} \times 41{,}408 \text{ eV}$$
$$E_a \approx 2.47 \text{ eV}$$

**Result:** $E_a \approx \mathbf{2.47 \; eV}$ per atom — a realistic value for Si–H bond breaking during SiH₄ pyrolysis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — CVD & PVD Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **CVD = chemical reaction on a heated wafer**; PVD = **physical ejection + ballistic transport**.
- **CVD sub-types:** APCVD, LPCVD, PECVD, HDPCVD, MOCVD, RTCVD.
- **PVD sub-types:** Thermal evaporation, e-beam evaporation, DC/RF sputtering, magnetron sputtering, IPVD.
- **Step coverage order (best to worst):** LPCVD > PECVD > HDPCVD > Magnetron sputtering > Evaporation.
- **LPCVD pressure ≈ 0.1 – 10 Torr**; PVD pressure ≈ **10⁻³ – 10⁻⁹ mbar** (orders of magnitude lower).
- **Mean free path formula:** $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2}\, \pi\, d^2 P)$ — high vacuum is essential for PVD to avoid scattering.
- **Arrhenius growth rate:** $R \propto \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ — the most-tested formula in numerical questions.
- **Sputtering yield** depends on ion energy, ion mass, target mass, and incidence angle — typically peaks around **60° incidence**.
- **Magnetron** magnets trap electrons → higher plasma density → higher sputter rate, lower substrate damage.
- **Step coverage** is defined as $t_{\min} / t_{\max}$ × 100%.
- **Sticking coefficient** $S_c$ multiplies impinging flux to give growth rate.
- **Boundary layer thickness** governs mass transport in CVD; $\delta \approx \sqrt{\mu x / (\rho u_\infty)}$.
- **Plasma is essential in sputtering** but **optional in CVD** (only PECVD uses plasma).
- **HDPCVD** simultaneously deposits and sputter-etches — perfect for STI gap-fill.
- **Reactions to memorize:** SiH₄ → Si + 2H₂ (poly); WF₆ + 3H₂ → W + 6HF (W plug).
- **Common metals deposited by PVD:** Al, Cu, Ti, Ta, TaN (interconnects and barriers).
- **Common dielectrics deposited by CVD:** SiO₂, Si₃N₄, low-k ILDs.
- **Always convert mbar → Pa** in mean-free-path numericals (1 mbar = 100 Pa).
- **PECVD allows deposition at 200 – 400 °C** — critical for **back-end-of-line** where thermal budgets are tight.
- **Conformality** is a *qualitative* term (how well the film follows the surface contour); step coverage is the *quantitative ratio*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFET Fabrication Techniques: Twin-Tub Fabrication Sequence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MOSFET Fabrication Techniques: Twin-Tub Fabrication Sequence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Twin-Tub (Twin-Well) CMOS Fabrication Process** is an advanced CMOS VLSI manufacturing technology in which **two independently optimized diffusion tubs** — one **n-tub (n-well)** to host the PMOS transistors and one **p-tub (p-well)** to host the NMOS transistors — are fabricated side-by-side on a single, lightly-doped silicon substrate. Each tub is created by selective ion implantation through dedicated masks, followed by a high-temperature **drive-in diffusion** step, allowing the process engineer to independently tailor the **doping concentration**, **junction depth**, and **body-effect coefficient** of the n-channel and p-channel devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):** The Twin-Tub process is a CMOS fabrication sequence in which both p-type and n-type tubs are explicitly formed on a common substrate to permit the independent optimization of the NMOS and PMOS transistor parameters such as threshold voltage, body effect, and transconductance.

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Substrate doping (N_epi or P_epi) | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁵ cm⁻³ |
| n-tub surface concentration | 10¹⁵ – 5×10¹⁶ cm⁻³ |
| p-tub surface concentration | 10¹⁵ – 5×10¹⁶ cm⁻³ |
| Tub junction depth (xj) | 2 – 6 µm |
| Gate oxide thickness (tox) | 10 – 50 nm (deep sub-µm) |
| Drive-in temperature | 1100 °C – 1200 °C |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two Neighbourhoods

Imagine a **large apartment complex (the silicon wafer)** sitting on neutral ground. To prevent electrical "disturbances" between two families — the **n-type family (PMOS residents)** and the **p-type family (NMOS residents)** — the city planner carves out **two separate neighbourhoods (the twin tubs)** with controlled population density (doping concentration). 

- Each neighbourhood has its **own municipal rules** (threshold voltage, body bias).
- The neighbourhoods are electrically **isolated** by reverse-biased pn-junctions that form automatically at the tub boundaries.
- The planner (process engineer) can independently adjust the population (doping) of each neighbourhood for **optimal performance** — one family tuned for high current, the other for low leakage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Twin"?** Because both tubs are *deliberately and symmetrically* engineered, unlike the n-well-only process (used in early CMOS) where the substrate itself served as the p-well.

## 1.3 Why Twin-Tub Replaced Earlier Technologies

| Process Generation | p-tub Formation | Drawback |
|---|---|---|
| **p-well process** | Substrate = p-well (fixed) | Poor PMOS performance |
| **n-well process** | Substrate = n-well (fixed) | Poor NMOS performance; latch-up prone |
| **Twin-tub process** | Both tubs formed separately | Independent optimization → superior CMOS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Module-2, Twin-Tub is taught as the *benchmark* CMOS process used as a precursor to modern sub-micron and deep sub-micron technologies (where retrograde wells and epitaxial substrates are added).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why Two Tubs?

The **body-effect coefficient** (γ) of a MOSFET governs how strongly the threshold voltage depends on the source-to-body bias:

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot q \cdot \varepsilon_{si} \cdot N_{A}}}{\sqrt{C_{ox}}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \frac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, N_A}}{C_{ox}} \\
C_{ox} &= \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $q$ | Electron charge = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ | C |
| $\varepsilon_{si}$ | Permittivity of silicon = 11.7 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴ | F/cm |
| $N_A$ | Substrate / tub doping | cm⁻³ |
| $C_{ox}$ | Gate-oxide capacitance per unit area | F/cm² |
| $t_{ox}$ | Gate-oxide thickness | cm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since γ depends on $\sqrt{N_A}$, by choosing the **p-tub doping** $N_{A,p\text{-tub}}$ and the **n-tub doping** $N_{D,n\text{-tub}}$ independently, the designer can match or asymmetrically tune the body effect of NMOS and PMOS. This is impossible in single-well processes.

## 2.2 The Threshold Voltage Equations in Twin-Tub

For the **NMOS** (formed inside the p-tub):
$$
V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, N_{A,p\text{-tub}} \, 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}
$$

For the **PMOS** (formed inside the n-tub):
$$
V_{TP} = V_{FB} - 2\phi_F - \frac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, N_{D,n\text{-tub}} \, 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}
$$

where $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_{A,p\text{-tub}}}{n_i}\right)$ for the p-tub (similar expression for n-tub).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Body-effect coefficient (NMOS) | $\gamma_N = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_{A,p\text{-tub}}}}{C_{ox}}$ | Quantifies Vt shift with VSB |
| 2 | Body-effect coefficient (PMOS) | $\gamma_P = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_{D,n\text{-tub}}}}{C_{ox}}$ | Same for PMOS in n-tub |
| 3 | Threshold voltage (NMOS) | $V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma_N \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ | Includes body bias |
| 4 | Threshold voltage (PMOS) | $V_{TP} = V_{FB} - 2\phi_F - \gamma_P \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ | Sign convention: VTP < 0 |
| 5 | Tub sheet resistance | $R_{sheet} = \dfrac{1}{q \, \mu \, N_{surface} \, x_j}$ | Used for latch-up analysis |
| 6 | Junction depth after drive-in | $x_j \approx 2\sqrt{D \, t}$ | Ficks-law approximation |
| 7 | Latch-up gain product | $\beta_{npn} \cdot \beta_{pnp} < 1$ | Design rule to avoid latch-up |
| 8 | Transconductance | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN})$ | Drive strength of NMOS |
| 9 | Oxide capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.9 \times 8.854\times10^{-14}}{t_{ox}\,\text{(cm)}}$ | F/cm² |
| 10 | Fermi potential (p-tub) | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_{A,p\text{-tub}}}{n_i}\right)$ | Built-in potential term |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** The twin-tub concept is the *direct ancestor* of modern retrograde-well and triple-well processes used in 28 nm, 14 nm, 7 nm CMOS nodes. Understanding twin-tub provides the conceptual scaffolding for any advanced VLSI process flow.

## 2.4 Engineering Advantages of the Twin-Tub Approach

1. **Independent threshold control** for NMOS and PMOS → symmetric CMOS performance.
2. **Reduced body effect** because each tub can be lightly doped while the substrate is held at a moderate level.
3. **Lower latch-up susceptibility** — both tubs are intentionally placed close to the surface, shortening the parasitic thyristor path.
4. **Higher packing density** since both n+ and p+ S/D regions are formed in matched, shallow tubs.
5. **Compatibility with epitaxial wafers** (modern spin-off), giving an ideal vertical doping profile.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Twin-Tub Fabrication Sequence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Twin-Tub process typically requires **6 to 7 masking levels** in its classical form. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step flow.

## Step 0 — Starting Wafer Preparation

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 0.1 | Select a **p-type silicon wafer** of doping $\approx 10^{15}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$ (100) orientation. | Bare p-substrate. |
| 0.2 | Grow a thin **pad oxide** ($\approx 50\,\text{nm}$) thermally at ~1000 °C in dry O₂. | Protects the silicon during subsequent nitride deposition. |
| 0.3 | Deposit a **silicon nitride (Si₃N₄)** layer of $\approx 100\,\text{nm}$ using **LPCVD** at ~800 °C. | Acts as a diffusion/implant mask and as a stress-relief layer. |

**Why pad oxide?** Direct deposition of Si₃N₄ on Si causes excessive stress and dislocations. The pad oxide cushions this stress.

## Step 1 — Mask 1 (M1): n-Tub Photolithography & Implantation

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1.1 | Spin-coat **photoresist**, soft-bake at 90 °C, expose through **M1 (n-tub) mask** in a stepper/i-line aligner. | Patterned PR exposing regions where n-tub must form. |
| 1.2 | Develop PR; **plasma-etch the exposed Si₃N₄** using CF₄/CHF₃ chemistry, stopping on the pad oxide. | Si₃N₄ removed only over n-tub regions. |
| 1.3 | **Ion-implant Phosphorus** at energy ~150 keV, dose ~10¹² – 10¹³ cm⁻². | n-type dopant placed in the silicon under the opened nitride window. |
| 1.4 | Strip the photoresist using O₂ plasma + H₂SO₄/H₂O₂ piranha. | Clean surface. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Dose selection:** Dose must be high enough to *over-compensate* the p-substrate and form a clear n-tub, but low enough to keep junction capacitance manageable.

## Step 2 — Mask 2 (M2): p-Tub Photolithography & Implantation

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 2.1 | Re-coat PR, align **M2 (p-tub) mask**, expose and develop. | Patterned PR exposing p-tub regions. |
| 2.2 | Plasma-etch Si₃N₄ in the p-tub windows. | Si₃N₄ removed over p-tub. |
| 2.3 | **Ion-implant Boron** at energy ~100 keV, dose ~10¹² – 10¹³ cm⁻². | p-type dopant added. |
| 2.4 | Strip PR. | Clean surface. |

## Step 3 — Drive-In Oxidation (Tub Formation)

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | Load wafer into a high-temperature furnace at **1150 °C in dry O₂** for several hours. | Dopants diffuse *downward and sideways*; simultaneously a thick **field oxide (FOX)** grows in the nitride-removed regions. |
| 3.2 | Phosphorus and Boron diffuse to form **n-tub and p-tub** each ~3–6 µm deep. | Twin tubs embedded under field oxide. |
| 3.3 | Strip the **Si₃N₄** in hot phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄ at 180 °C). | Field oxide regions now act as LOCOS isolation. |
| 3.4 | Strip the **pad oxide** in dilute HF. | Bare silicon in active areas. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LOCOS (Local Oxidation of Silicon):** This step grows thick SiO₂ (~500–1000 nm) in the field regions between transistors, providing electrical isolation. The Si₃N₄ inhibits oxide growth in the active regions — hence the name *local* oxidation.

## Step 4 — Gate Oxidation

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 4.1 | RCA clean the wafer. | Removes organics, metals, and native oxide. |
| 4.2 | Grow **gate oxide** ~10–50 nm thick in dry O₂ at 900–1000 °C (with possible HCl anneal for interface quality). | High-quality thin SiO₂ — the heart of the MOSFET channel. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interface trap density** $D_{it}$ should ideally be < 10¹⁰ cm⁻² eV⁻¹ for good 1/f noise and mobility — HCl anneal helps achieve this.

## Step 5 — Mask 3 (M3): Threshold-Voltage Adjust Implant (Optional but KTU-Relevant)

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 5.1 | Coat PR, align **M3 (Vt adjust)** mask, expose and develop. | Opens windows over one device type (e.g., NMOS) to fine-tune VTN. |
| 5.2 | **Boron implant** at low dose (~10¹¹ cm⁻², low energy). | Slightly raises $\vert V_{TN} \vert$ to target value. |
| 5.3 | Strip PR. | Clean surface. |

## Step 6 — Polysilicon Gate Deposition & Mask 4 (M4) Patterning

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 6.1 | **LPCVD polysilicon** ~300–400 nm at 620 °C. | Undoped poly-Si blanket. |
| 6.2 | **POCl₃ doping** or ion-implant phosphorus to make poly-Si highly conductive (n+ poly). | Sheet resistance ~20–50 Ω/sq. |
| 6.3 | Spin PR, align **M4 (gate) mask**, expose and develop. | Patterned PR outlining gate electrodes. |
| 6.4 | **Reactive-ion-etch (RIE)** poly-Si using HBr/Cl₂ chemistry. | Self-aligned gate electrodes. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why self-aligned gate?** The poly-Si gate acts as a *self-aligned mask* for the subsequent S/D implants, eliminating misalignment and dramatically reducing overlap capacitance C_{gd} and C_{gs}.

## Step 7 — Mask 5 (M5): n+ Source/Drain Implant

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 7.1 | Coat PR, align **M5 (n+ S/D)** mask to cover p-tub (NMOS) regions. | NMOS active regions exposed. |
| 7.2 | **Arsenic or Phosphorus implant** at ~80 keV, dose ~5×10¹⁵ cm⁻². | Forms n+ S/D in p-tub. Poly gate is also doped to n+ if it lies in the p-tub. |
| 7.3 | Strip PR. | Clean surface. |

## Step 8 — Mask 6 (M6): p+ Source/Drain Implant

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 8.1 | Coat PR, align **M6 (p+ S/D)** mask to cover n-tub (PMOS) regions. | PMOS active regions exposed. |
| 8.2 | **Boron implant** at ~50 keV, dose ~5×10¹⁵ cm⁻². | Forms p+ S/D in n-tub. |
| 8.3 | Strip PR. | Clean surface. |
| 8.4 | **Anneal** at 900–950 °C for 20–30 min to activate dopants and repair implant damage. | Final S/D regions formed. |

> [!NOTE]
> **LDD (Lightly Doped Drain) option:** In modern sub-µm flows, an n- and p- LDD implant + sidewall spacer step is inserted *before* M5/M6 to suppress hot-carrier effects. KTU 2024 syllabus expects awareness of this even if not full process details.

## Step 9 — Inter-Layer Dielectric, Contact, Metallization

| Sub-step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 9.1 | Deposit **BPSG (Boron-Phospho-Silicate Glass)** ~500–800 nm by LPCVD; reflow at 850 °C. | Planarized ILD. |
| 9.2 | Spin PR, **M7 (contact) mask**, etch contacts using RIE. | Vertical contact holes opened to S/D and gate. |
| 9.3 | Sputter **Al-1%Si** (~1 µm), or deposit **Cu** via damascene (advanced). | Metal layer. |
| 9.4 | **M8 (metal-1) mask**, RIE Al. | First-level interconnect. |
| 9.5 | Repeat dielectric + metal for additional layers. | Multi-level interconnect stack. |
| 9.6 | Deposit **Si₃N₄ passivation**, open bond-pad mask, anneal in forming gas (H₂/N₂). | Chip protected; dangling bonds passivated. |

## 3.1 Analytical Summary of Mask Levels

| Mask | Function | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| M1 | n-tub definition | Phosphorus dose/energy |
| M2 | p-tub definition | Boron dose/energy |
| M3 | Vt adjust (optional) | Low-dose boron |
| M4 | Poly-Si gate | Gate length L |
| M5 | n+ S/D (NMOS) | As/P implant |
| M6 | p+ S/D (PMOS) | B implant |
| M7 | Contacts | RIE oxide |
| M8 | Metal-1 patterning | Al etch |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam tip:** When asked *"How many mask levels in twin-tub CMOS?"* — the canonical answer is **6 masks** (M1–M6) for the transistor itself, plus **2 more** for contacts and metal-1 in a complete process flow.

## 3.2 Numerical Example — Designing a 1 µm NMOS in p-Tub

Given:
- $t_{ox} = 20\,\text{nm} = 2\times 10^{-6}\,\text{cm}$
- $N_{A,p\text{-tub}} = 5\times 10^{15}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$
- $n_i = 1.5\times 10^{10}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$
- $V_{FB} = -0.9\,\text{V}$ (Al gate, n+ poly equivalent)

**Step 1:** Calculate oxide capacitance.
$$
C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854\times 10^{-14}}{2\times 10^{-6}} = 1.726 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{F/cm}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate Fermi potential.
$$
\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{5\times 10^{15}}{1.5\times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(3.33\times 10^{5}) = 0.0259 \times 12.717 = 0.329\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate body-effect coefficient.
$$
\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot 1.6\times 10^{-19} \cdot 11.7\cdot 8.854\times 10^{-14} \cdot 5\times 10^{15}}}{1.726 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot (1.6\times 10^{-19}) \cdot (1.036\times 10^{-12}) \cdot (5\times 10^{15})}}{1.726\times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{1.658 \times 10^{-15}}}{1.726\times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{4.072\times 10^{-8}}{1.726\times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 0.236\,\text{V}^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate threshold voltage (assuming $V_{SB}=0$).
$$
V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F} = -0.9 + 0.658 + 0.236\sqrt{0.658}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{TN} &= -0.9 + 0.658 + 0.236 \times 0.811 \\
&= -0.9 + 0.658 + 0.191 \\
&= -0.051\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply a **Vt adjust implant** of boron (ΔV ≈ +0.45 V) to bring VTN to **+0.4 V** as required.

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example is exactly the type of calculation KTU examiners set in Part-B problems on threshold-voltage design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Down Mask Layout — Twin-Tub CMOS Inverter

The following Mermaid block renders the *top-down mask-level* layout of a CMOS inverter, showing the spatial relationship of the twin tubs and the n+/p+ S/D regions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Wafer Boundary 8 mm x 8 mm p-type 100"]
    nodeB["M1 Mask n-Tub Region contains PMOS devices"]
    nodeC["M2 Mask p-Tub Region contains NMOS devices"]
    nodeD["M3 Mask Vt Adjust Boron implant over NMOS"]
    nodeE["M4 Mask Poly-Si Gate runs horizontally across both tubs"]
    nodeF["M5 Mask n+ S/D in p-Tub forms NMOS source and drain"]
    nodeG["M6 Mask p+ S/D in n-Tub forms PMOS source and drain"]
    nodeH["M7 Mask Contact cuts at Gate Source Drain Drain of inverter"]
    nodeI["M8 Mask Metal-1 Vdd rail Vss rail and output line"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **n-tub and p-tub are formed first (M1, M2)**, and every later mask aligns to the tub boundary — this alignment is critical because the tub-to-substrate junction determines the latch-up immunity.

## 4.2 Process-Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["Start p-type Si wafer 100"]
    stepB["Pad oxide growth 50 nm"]
    stepC["LPCVD Si3N4 100 nm"]
    stepD["M1 n-tub lithography"]
    stepE["Phosphorus ion implant"]
    stepF["M2 p-tub lithography"]
    stepG["Boron ion implant"]
    stepH["Drive-in 1150 C plus LOCOS field oxide"]
    stepI["Strip nitride and pad oxide"]
    stepJ["Gate oxidation 20 nm dry O2"]
    stepK["M3 Vt adjust implant optional"]
    stepL["Poly-Si deposition 350 nm and POCl3 doping"]
    stepM["M4 Gate lithography and RIE"]
    stepN["M5 n+ S/D implant As or P"]
    stepO["M6 p+ S/D implant B"]
    stepP["Dopant activation anneal 900 C"]
    stepQ["BPSG deposition and reflow"]
    stepR["M7 Contact lithography and RIE"]
    stepS["Al sputtering"]
    stepT["M8 Metal-1 lithography and RIE"]
    stepU["Si3N4 passivation and bond-pad open"]
    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK --> stepL --> stepM --> stepN --> stepO --> stepP --> stepQ --> stepR --> stepS --> stepT --> stepU
```

## 4.3 Twin-Tub Process Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["Mask 1 n-Tub Pattern"] --> func1["Phosphorus Implant Energy 150 keV"]
    func1 --> outA["n-Tub Doping 1e16 cm-3"]
    inB["Mask 2 p-Tub Pattern"] --> func2["Boron Implant Energy 100 keV"]
    func2 --> outB["p-Tub Doping 1e16 cm-3"]
    outA --> iso["LOCOS Field Oxide Isolation"]
    outB --> iso
    iso --> gate["Gate Oxide 20 nm"]
    gate --> poly["Poly-Si Gate Definition Mask 4"]
    poly --> nsd["Mask 5 n+ S/D Arsenic Implant"]
    poly --> psd["Mask 6 p+ S/D Boron Implant"]
    nsd --> final["Twin-Tub CMOS Inverter Cell"]
    psd --> final
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why the Mermaid block diagram?** The actual cross-section of a twin-tub device involves many overlapping layers (substrate, pad oxide, nitride, field oxide, gate oxide, poly, n+/p+ S/D, BPSG, metal). Block-level architectural flow is the most effective way to represent the **interactions and dependencies** between process modules in a textbook-acceptable format.

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schema (Textual Schematic)

```
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  Passivation (Si3N4)                       |
|  +----------+ +----------+ +----------+ +----------+        |
|  | Al Metal | | Al Metal | | Al Metal | | Al Metal |        |
|  +----+-----+ +-----+----+ +----+-----+ +-----+----+       |
|       |             |            |             |           |
|  +----+-----+ +-----+----+ +----+-----+ +-----+----+        |
|  |   BPSG   | |   BPSG   | |   BPSG   | |   BPSG   |      |
|  +----+-----+ +-----+----+ +----+-----+ +-----+----+       |
|       v             v            v             v           |
|  +----+ n+ +-+ p+ +---+ +-+ p+ +-+ n+ +---+ p+ +-+ n+      |
|  |   S     |  |   S    |  |  D    |  |   S     |           |
|  +----+----+--+--------+--+-------+--+---------+--+         |
|  |         POLY GATE 1   |  POLY GATE 2         |         |
|  +----------------+ +-----+----+ +----------------+        |
|  |   Gate Oxide   | | Gate Ox | |   Gate Oxide   |         |
|  +----------------+ +---------+ +----------------+         |
|  |  n-Tub (PMOS)  | |   FOX   |  |  p-Tub (NMOS) |         |
|  +----------------+ +---------+ +----------------+         |
|                  p-Substrate (~10^15 cm-3)                 |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
```

> [!NOTE]
> The text schematic shows — left to right — the PMOS (n-tub), the **LOCOS field oxide isolation**, and the NMOS (p-tub), with poly-Si gates straddling the tubs. The deep p-n junctions between the tubs and the substrate form the **reverse-biased isolation diodes** that prevent latch-up.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Twin-Tub CMOS fabrication process. List any two advantages over n-well CMOS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Twin-Tub (Twin-Well) CMOS process is a fabrication technology in which **both an n-tub and a p-tub are explicitly formed on a common silicon substrate** by separate masking, ion-implantation, and high-temperature drive-in steps, before the active transistors are built. This allows the process designer to independently control the doping concentration and depth of each tub.

**Two advantages over n-well CMOS:**
1. **Independent optimization of NMOS and PMOS** — threshold voltage, body effect, and mobility can be tuned for both devices separately, yielding a symmetric, high-performance CMOS inverter.
2. **Reduced latch-up susceptibility** — since both tubs are deliberately placed near the surface and can be heavily doped, the parasitic thyristor gains ($\beta_{npn}$, $\beta_{pnp}$) are reduced, making the latch-up product $\beta_{npn}\cdot\beta_{pnp} < 1$ easier to achieve.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per advantage = 3 marks)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is LOCOS? Why is it used in the twin-tub process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**LOCOS (Local Oxidation of Silicon)** is a process technique in which a patterned layer of **silicon nitride (Si₃N₄)** is used to selectively prevent thermal oxidation in *active* transistor regions while allowing a thick field oxide (SiO₂) to grow in the *field* (isolation) regions. The pad oxide beneath the nitride cushions the stress and prevents defect formation.

**Why it is used in twin-tub:**
1. LOCOS provides **dielectric isolation** between adjacent NMOS and PMOS devices formed in the twin tubs, preventing surface inversion and leakage paths.
2. The field oxide thickness (~500–1000 nm) is sufficient to support metal interconnect lines crossing over the field region without parasitic conduction.
3. LOCOS is a *self-aligned* technique — the nitride mask defines both the active area and the field oxide, eliminating the need for an additional isolation mask.

*(2 marks for LOCOS definition + 1 mark for reason = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either Option A or Option B** (internal choice).

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat process-flow diagram, explain the **step-by-step Twin-Tub CMOS fabrication sequence**. Clearly mention the function of **each masking level** and the typical **ion-implant species and dose** used. (7 marks)

**(b)** An NMOS transistor is fabricated in the p-tub of a twin-tub process. Given $t_{ox} = 25\,\text{nm}$, $N_{A,p\text{-tub}} = 8\times 10^{15}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$, and gate material = n+ polysilicon. Calculate the threshold voltage $V_{TN}$ at $V_{SB} = 0$. (Assume $V_{FB} = -0.85\,\text{V}$, $n_i = 1.5\times 10^{10}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$.) (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Process Flow (7 marks)

| Mask | Step | Process Detail | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| — | 0 | Start with p-type (100) wafer, grow 50 nm pad oxide, deposit 100 nm Si₃N₄ by LPCVD. | 0.5 |
| **M1** | 1 | Photolithography to pattern n-tub windows. Plasma-etch nitride. **Implant Phosphorus at 150 keV, dose 10¹² cm⁻².** Strip PR. | 1.0 |
| **M2** | 2 | Photolithography to pattern p-tub windows. Plasma-etch nitride. **Implant Boron at 100 keV, dose 10¹² cm⁻².** Strip PR. | 1.0 |
| — | 3 | **Drive-in at 1150 °C in dry O₂** for several hours. Dopants diffuse to form n-tub and p-tub (xj ≈ 3–6 µm). Simultaneously **LOCOS field oxide** grows in nitride-removed regions. | 1.0 |
| — | 4 | Strip nitride (H₃PO₄ 180 °C). Strip pad oxide (HF). **Grow gate oxide** 25 nm in dry O₂ at 950 °C. | 0.5 |
| **M3** | 5 | Optional **Vt adjust implant** — low-dose boron (~10¹¹ cm⁻²). | 0.5 |
| **M4** | 6 | Deposit 350 nm **poly-Si** by LPCVD, POCl₃ dope, pattern gate by RIE. | 0.5 |
| **M5** | 7 | **n+ S/D implant** — Arsenic 80 keV, 5×10¹⁵ cm⁻², masked to p-tub. | 1.0 |
| **M6** | 8 | **p+ S/D implant** — Boron 50 keV, 5×10¹⁵ cm⁻², masked to n-tub. **Anneal 900 °C 30 min.** | 1.0 |

*(M7 contact + M8 metal-1 not required for the 7-mark allocation.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Threshold Voltage (7 marks)

**Given:** $t_{ox} = 25\,\text{nm} = 2.5\times 10^{-6}\,\text{cm}$; $N_A = 8\times 10^{15}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$; $V_{FB} = -0.85\,\text{V}$; $n_i = 1.5\times 10^{10}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$; $V_{SB}=0$.

**Step 1 — Oxide capacitance** (1 mark)
$$
C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854\times 10^{-14}}{2.5\times 10^{-6}} = 1.381 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{F/cm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Fermi potential** (1 mark)
$$
\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{8\times 10^{15}}{1.5\times 10^{10}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(5.33\times 10^{5})
$$
$$
= 0.0259 \times 13.186 = 0.342\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Body-effect coefficient** (2 marks)
$$
\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot q \cdot \varepsilon_{si} \cdot N_A}}{C_{ox}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6\times 10^{-19} \times 11.7 \times 8.854\times 10^{-14} \times 8\times 10^{15}}}{1.381 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{2.65\times 10^{-15}}}{1.381 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{5.149\times 10^{-8}}{1.381 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 0.373\,\text{V}^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Threshold voltage** (2 marks)
$$
V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{TN} &= -0.85 + 2(0.342) + 0.373 \times \sqrt{2 \times 0.342} \\
&= -0.85 + 0.684 + 0.373 \times \sqrt{0.684} \\
&= -0.85 + 0.684 + 0.373 \times 0.827 \\
&= -0.85 + 0.684 + 0.308 \\
&= +0.142\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation** (1 mark)
Since $V_{TN} = 0.142\,\text{V}$ is too small for reliable circuit operation (large off-state leakage), an additional **Vt-adjust boron implant** of dose $\approx 4\times 10^{11}\,\text{cm}^{-2}$ would raise the threshold to the design target of ~0.5 V.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare the **n-well, p-well, and twin-tub CMOS** processes with respect to (i) process complexity, (ii) latch-up immunity, and (iii) NMOS/PMOS performance symmetry. Justify why twin-tub is the preferred process for high-density CMOS. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat cross-sectional schematic, describe the **formation of the twin tubs and the LOCOS isolation** in a CMOS process. Mention the role of the pad oxide and silicon nitride layers. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)

| Criterion | n-Well Process | p-Well Process | Twin-Tub Process |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Process complexity | 1 tub + substrate = 1 implant | 1 tub + substrate = 1 implant | 2 explicit tubs = 2 implants + drive-in |
| (ii) Latch-up immunity | Moderate; PMOS in n-well, NMOS in p-substrate | Moderate; NMOS in p-well, PMOS in n-substrate | Excellent; both tubs surface-placed, low-gain parasitic SCR |
| (iii) NMOS/PMOS symmetry | PMOS performance is inferior (high N-well resistance, body effect) | NMOS performance is inferior | **Symmetric**; each device tuned to optimum |

**Why Twin-Tub is preferred for high-density CMOS (3 marks):**

1. **Independent Vt control** allows the threshold voltages of NMOS and PMOS to be matched, giving equal noise margins and symmetric switching — critical for low-power digital design.
2. **Lower body-effect coefficient** in both devices (since each tub can be lightly doped independently) leads to better analog performance (high gain, low channel-length modulation sensitivity).
3. **Reduced latch-up** because the n-tub and p-tub are shallow and well-defined, shortening the base widths of the parasitic npn and pnp transistors, keeping $\beta_{npn}\cdot\beta_{pnp} < 1$.
4. **Higher packing density** — both devices have comparable dimensions, so the layout is balanced and the silicon-area utilization is maximized.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Cross-Section & LOCOS (7 marks)

**Cross-Sectional Description (4 marks):**
The cross-section shows the silicon substrate, the pad oxide (~50 nm), the silicon-nitride layer (~100 nm) on top, the patterned photoresist openings for the n-tub and p-tub, the implanted dopants (P and B) confined beneath the opened nitride windows, the field oxide (~700 nm) grown selectively in those windows, the active n-tub and p-tub regions with their respective doping profiles, and the field oxide acting as isolation between them.

**Role of Pad Oxide (1.5 marks):**
The pad oxide (50 nm thermal SiO₂) grown before nitride deposition serves three purposes:
1. Acts as a **stress-relief cushion** between the Si substrate and the highly stressed Si₃N₄ film, preventing dislocation generation.
2. Acts as a **partial diffusion mask**, slowing dopant out-diffusion during the long high-temperature drive-in.
3. Acts as a **clean interface** for the subsequent gate oxide growth after the nitride is stripped.

**Role of Si₃N₄ (1.5 marks):**
1. Acts as a **selective oxidation mask** — O₂ and H₂O diffuse very slowly through Si₃N₄, so no field oxide grows under nitride-covered regions (active areas).
2. Acts as a **selective implant mask** — ions cannot penetrate 100 nm of nitride at the implant energies used.
3. The *patterned* nitride layer therefore *self-aligns* the field oxide to the active regions, eliminating one masking step.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Twin-Tub & LOCOS questions):**
> 1. **Confusing pad oxide and gate oxide** — pad oxide is *removed* before gate oxidation; reusing pad oxide as gate oxide is a *fatal* mark-losing error (it has poor interface quality).
> 2. **Forgetting drive-in step** — students often describe only the implant but omit the high-temperature diffusion that *actually forms* the tub. Always state both implant AND drive-in.
> 3. **Wrong implant species** — for n-tub, phosphorus (or arsenic) is correct; for p-tub, boron is correct. Reverse them and you lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting LOCOS** — in any "explain twin-tub" question, LOCOS is part of the isolation step; examiners specifically look for the word *LOCOS* or *local oxidation of silicon*.
> 5. **Threshold-voltage formula mistakes** — common slip: writing $V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ but forgetting that $V_{SB}=0$ for a *source-tied* device. Mention body-effect term even at $V_{SB}=0$ because the $\sqrt{2\phi_F}$ term still appears.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (high-density bullet summary)**

- **Twin-Tub = two explicitly formed wells** (n-tub for PMOS, p-tub for NMOS) on a common substrate.
- **Typical mask count:** 6 (M1–M6) for the transistor; 8 (M1–M8) for the full process including contacts and metal-1.
- **Implant species:** n-tub → **Phosphorus** at ~150 keV; p-tub → **Boron** at ~100 keV.
- **Drive-in:** ~1150 °C, several hours, in dry O₂ → forms tubs AND grows field oxide (LOCOS).
- **LOCOS sequence:** pad oxide (50 nm) → Si₃N₄ (100 nm) → pattern → thermal oxidation in nitride-free regions → strip nitride → strip pad oxide.
- **Gate oxide:** ~20–50 nm dry O₂ at 900–1000 °C; HCl anneal recommended.
- **Vt adjust implant:** low-dose boron through a separate mask; critical for fine-tuning $V_{TN}$ to ~0.5 V.
- **Poly-Si gate:** LPCVD, POCl₃-doped, RIE-etched; acts as self-aligned mask for S/D implants.
- **S/D implants:** n+ (As or P) into p-tub → NMOS; p+ (B) into n-tub → PMOS.
- **Activation anneal:** 900–950 °C, 20–30 min in N₂.
- **Body-effect coefficient:** $\gamma \propto \sqrt{N_A / C_{ox}}$ — independent tuning of each tub enables symmetric CMOS.
- **Latch-up rule:** $\beta_{npn}\cdot\beta_{pnp} < 1$; twin-tub reduces $\beta$ by shortening base widths.
- **Threshold voltage formula:**
$$
V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}
$$
- **Twin-tub vs n-well vs p-well:** only twin-tub allows *symmetric* NMOS/PMOS optimization.
- **Engineering payoff:** Twin-tub is the conceptual ancestor of every modern CMOS node (28 nm, 14 nm, 7 nm) which use retrograde wells and triple-well on epitaxial substrates.
- **Modern extensions:** LDD + sidewall spacer, silicide (TiSi₂/CoSi₂/NiSi) on S/D and gate, damascene Cu metallization, low-k ILD, etc. — these are built *on top of* the twin-tub foundation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for Twin-Tub:** "Two tubs, six masks, one drive-in, zero latch-up." — Memorize the mask order **M1 (n-tub) → M2 (p-tub) → M3 (Vt) → M4 (gate) → M5 (n+) → M6 (p+)** and the corresponding implant species. Draw the cross-section showing the LOCOS field oxide between the tubs. Always state the threshold-voltage equation with the body-effect term.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fabrication Process Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fabrication Process Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fabrication Process Flow** in VLSI refers to the precisely sequenced set of physical and chemical unit operations used to transform a raw semiconductor wafer (typically monocrystalline silicon) into a fully functional integrated circuit containing millions to billions of transistors, interconnects, and passive components on a single die.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBECT604)**, the fabrication process flow is studied as a deterministic, planar, sequential layering technique built primarily around the **six (6) fundamental unit processes**:

1. **Oxidation** (thermal growth of SiO₂)
2. **Diffusion** (dopant introduction)
3. **Ion Implantation** (energetic dopant bombardment)
4. **Deposition** (CVD, PVD, Epitaxy)
5. **Lithography** (pattern transfer via photoresist)
6. **Etching** (wet and dry material removal)

**Standard Process Flow Architecture (n-well CMOS):**

$$\text{Wafer} \rightarrow \text{Cleaning} \rightarrow \text{Well Formation} \rightarrow \text{Active Area Definition} \rightarrow \text{Gate Formation} \rightarrow \text{S/D Formation} \rightarrow \text{Interconnects}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Building a Multi-Story Sand Castle"**

Imagine you are constructing a microscopic, multi-story building on a beach using sand, water, and stencils. Each floor of the building corresponds to a **layer** (polysilicon, metal, oxide). The fabrication process flow is the rulebook that dictates the order of operations:

- **Stencil Placement** → Lithography (patterning a photoresist mask)
- **Selective Sand Removal** → Etching (removing unmasked material)
- **Spraying a Fixative Layer** → Deposition/Oxidation (adding a new uniform layer)
- **Selective Tinting** → Diffusion/Ion Implantation (doping specific regions)
- **Repeating for each floor** → Sequential Layering

Just as a builder cannot put windows (metal contacts) before walls (active silicon) are built, the fabrication process flow has a **strict temporal ordering** — violating the sequence ruins the entire chip. The end product is a complex, multi-layer integrated circuit with feature sizes often less than **7 nm** in modern nodes.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Wafer Diameter (modern) | **300 mm (12 inch)** |
| Wafer Thickness | **775 µm** |
| Crystal Orientation | **<100>** (preferred for CMOS) |
| Resistivity (p-type) | **8–12 Ω·cm** |
| Cleanroom Class (Class 1) | **≤ 1 particle/ft³ @ 0.5 µm** |
| Gate Oxide (modern) | **1–2 nm (SiO₂ equivalent)** |
| Operating Temperature (oxidation) | **800–1200 °C** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Unit Processes — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Oxidation
**Why:** Grows a high-quality SiO₂ layer used for isolation (LOCOS), gate dielectric, and passivation.
**How:** Silicon wafer is exposed to **O₂** (dry oxidation) or **H₂O vapor** (wet oxidation) at high temperature.

- **Dry Oxidation:** Slow, dense, high-quality oxide — used for **gate oxide**.
- **Wet Oxidation:** Fast, less dense — used for **field oxide**.

### 2.1.2 Diffusion
**Why:** Introduces dopant atoms (B, P, As) into silicon via concentration gradient.
**How:** Wafer heated in a furnace with dopant source; atoms migrate from high to low concentration.

Two phases:
- **Pre-deposition (deposition step):** Surface concentration set.
- **Drive-in (oxidation step):** Dopants pushed deeper; governed by Fick's laws.

### 2.1.3 Ion Implantation
**Why:** Precise, controllable dopant introduction with sharp junctions.
**How:** Dopant ions are accelerated through an electric field (10–200 keV) and physically bombarded into the silicon.

**Advantages over diffusion:** Precise dose control, low-temperature process, sharp profiles.

### 2.1.4 Deposition
**Why:** Adds thin films of conductors, insulators, or semiconductors.
**How:** 
- **CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition):** Gas-phase chemical reaction.
- **PVD / Sputtering:** Physical ejection of atoms from a target.
- **Epitaxy:** Single-crystal film growth on substrate.

### 2.1.5 Lithography
**Why:** Defines geometric patterns on the wafer.
**How:** Photoresist (positive/negative) is exposed through a photomask; exposed regions become soluble (positive) or insoluble (negative) in developer.

**Resolution limit (Rayleigh criterion):**

$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

Where:
- $R$ = minimum resolvable feature size
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of exposure light
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of the lens
- $k_1$ = process-dependent constant (theoretical minimum = 0.25)

### 2.1.6 Etching
**Why:** Selectively removes unmasked material.
**How:**
- **Wet Etching:** Chemical solution (e.g., HF for SiO₂) — isotropic.
- **Dry Etching (RIE/Plasma):** Ion bombardment — anisotropic, preferred for sub-micron features.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.**

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Growth (Oxidation) | $x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)$ | Deal-Grove model; $A, B$ = rate constants |
| Parabolic Growth (Oxidation) | $x_o^2 = Bt$ (long times) | Diffusion-limited regime |
| Fick's First Law | $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Diffusion flux |
| Fick's Second Law | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Transient diffusion |
| Diffusion Length | $L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot t}$ | Mean distance dopant travels |
| Implantation Range | $R_p$ | Projected range of ion |
| Straggle | $\Delta R_p$ | Standard deviation in range |
| Resolution (Lithography) | $R = k_1 \frac{\lambda}{NA}$ | Minimum feature size |
| Depth of Focus (DOF) | $DOF = \frac{\lambda}{2(NA)^2}$ | Focus tolerance |
| Sheet Resistance | $R_s = \frac{\rho}{t}$ | Thin-film resistance |
| MOSFET Current Saturation | $I_D = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_T)^2$ | CMOS output current |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

The CMOS fabrication process flow underpins **every modern electronic device** — from smartphones (Apple M-series, Snapdragon) and laptops (Intel/AMD x86) to automotive ECUs, IoT sensors, and AI accelerators (NVIDIA GPUs, TPUs). Mastering this flow is essential for:
- Process engineers in fabs (TSMC, Intel, Samsung)
- VLSI circuit designers (verifying layout-to-silicon)
- Device physicists (scaling roadmaps)
- Failure analysis and reliability engineers

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deal-Grove Oxidation Model (Linear & Parabolic Growth)

The Deal-Grove model is the foundational equation for thermal SiO₂ growth and is a **guaranteed KTU 14-mark derivation**.

### Assumptions:
1. Steady-state oxygen diffusion through existing oxide.
2. Reaction at Si–SiO₂ interface is first-order.
3. Oxygen concentration at outer surface is in equilibrium with the gas.

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Flux of oxidant through the oxide layer (Fick's First Law)**

The flux $F_1$ of oxidant (O₂ or H₂O) diffusing through an oxide of thickness $x_o$ is:

$$
F_1 = D \frac{C_0 - C_i}{x_o}
$$

Where $C_0$ = oxidant concentration at outer surface, $C_i$ = concentration at Si–SiO₂ interface, $D$ = diffusion coefficient.

**Step 2: Flux due to chemical reaction at the interface**

The reaction rate at the interface is proportional to $C_i$:

$$
F_2 = k_s \cdot C_i
$$

Where $k_s$ = surface reaction rate constant.

**Step 3: Steady-state condition (flux in = flux consumed)**

In equilibrium, $F_1 = F_2$. Solving for $C_i$:

$$
C_i = \frac{C_0}{1 + \frac{k_s x_o}{D}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute back to get the net flux**

$$
F = \frac{k_s C_0}{1 + \frac{k_s x_o}{D}}
$$

**Step 5: Relate flux to oxide growth rate**

The oxide growth rate is:

$$
\frac{dx_o}{dt} = \frac{F}{N_1}
$$

Where $N_1$ = number of oxidant molecules incorporated per unit volume of oxide (≈ $2.2 \times 10^{22}$ cm⁻³ for O₂ in SiO₂).

**Step 6: Integrate to obtain the Deal-Grove equation**

Substituting and integrating from $x_i$ (initial oxide) at $t=0$ to $x_o$ at time $t$:

$$
\int_{x_i}^{x_o} \left(1 + \frac{2 x_o}{A} \right) dx_o = \int_0^t B \, dt'
$$

Performing the integration:

$$
x_o - x_i + \frac{x_o^2 - x_i^2}{A} = B(t - t_i)
$$

Rearranging to standard form (assuming $x_i \approx 0$ at $t=0$):

$$
x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)
$$

This is the **general Deal-Grove equation**, where:
- $B$ = parabolic rate constant (cm²/s) — diffusion-controlled
- $B/A$ = linear rate constant (cm/s) — reaction-controlled
- $\tau$ = time shift accounting for initial oxide thickness

### Limiting Cases:

**Case 1: Long Oxidation Times (Parabolic Regime)**

When $x_o \gg A$, the linear term is negligible:

$$
x_o^2 \approx Bt \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_o \approx \sqrt{Bt}
$$

This is **diffusion-limited** growth.

**Case 2: Short Oxidation Times (Linear Regime)**

When $x_o \ll A$, the quadratic term is negligible:

$$
x_o \approx \frac{B}{A} \cdot t
$$

This is **reaction-rate-limited** growth.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Dry vs. Wet Oxidation

| Parameter | Dry Oxidation (O₂) | Wet Oxidation (H₂O) |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature Range | 800–1200 °C | 800–1100 °C |
| Growth Rate | **Slow** (~ 100 nm/hr at 1000 °C) | **Fast** (~ 500 nm/hr at 1000 °C) |
| Oxide Quality | **Dense, high-quality** | Less dense, more defects |
| $B$ (parabolic) | 0.0117 µm²/hr | 0.36 µm²/hr |
| $B/A$ (linear) | 0.0050 µm/hr | 0.28 µm/hr |
| Application | **Gate oxide** | **Field oxide (LOCOS)** |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Diffusion vs. Ion Implantation

| Feature | Diffusion | Ion Implantation |
|---|---|---|
| Driving Force | Concentration gradient | Kinetic energy of ions |
| Temperature | High (900–1200 °C) | Low (room temperature) |
| Dose Control | Poor | **Excellent** (C/s) |
| Junction Sharpness | Graded | **Sharp / Abrupt** |
| Lateral Spread | **High** (isotropic) | **Low** (directional) |
| Damage to wafer | None | Yes (requires annealing) |
| Mask Material | SiO₂, Si₃N₄ | Photoresist, oxide, metal |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: n-Well vs. p-Well vs. Twin-Tub

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently asked as a 7-mark question in KTU Module 2.**

| Feature | n-Well Process | p-Well Process | Twin-Tub Process |
|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate | p-type | n-type | **n-type (or low-dope)** |
| NMOS in | p-substrate | p-well | p-well (separately biased) |
| PMOS in | n-well | n-substrate | n-well (separately biased) |
| Body Effect NMOS | **High** | Low | **Low (independent biasing)** |
| Latch-up Susceptibility | Higher for PMOS | Higher for NMOS | **Lowest** |
| Modern Use | Rare | Older | **Standard in deep-submicron CMOS** |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A silicon wafer is oxidized in dry O₂ at 1100 °C for 60 minutes. Given $A = 0.05 \, \mu m$ and $B = 0.10 \, \mu m^2/hr$, find the oxide thickness.

**Solution:**

Using Deal-Grove:

$$
x_o^2 + A x_o = B t
$$

Substitute $B = 0.10 \, \mu m^2/hr$ and $t = 1.0 \, hr$:

$$
x_o^2 + 0.05 x_o - 0.10 = 0
$$

Apply quadratic formula:

$$
x_o = \frac{-0.05 + \sqrt{(0.05)^2 + 4(0.10)}}{2}
$$

$$
x_o = \frac{-0.05 + \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.40}}{2} = \frac{-0.05 + \sqrt{0.4025}}{2}
$$

$$
x_o = \frac{-0.05 + 0.6344}{2} = \frac{0.5844}{2} = 0.2922 \, \mu m
$$

**Answer:** $x_o \approx \mathbf{0.292 \, \mu m = 292 \, nm}$

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the complete **n-well CMOS fabrication process flow** with a neat cross-sectional diagram at every critical step (at least 10 steps). Explain the function of any **five** unit processes in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the **Deal-Grove model**, derive the general oxidation growth equation:

$$x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)$$

Identify the two limiting regimes and justify when each is applicable.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — CMOS n-Well Process Flow (7 marks)**

> **Answer Key / Mark Distribution:**
> - 10 cross-sectional steps (0.5 mark each = 5 marks)
> - 5 process explanations @ 0.4 mark each = 2 marks

**Step 1: p-type <100> silicon wafer** — Starting substrate (~10–20 Ω·cm).

```
   │ p-substrate │
```

**Step 2: Thermal Oxidation** — Pad oxide (~50 nm) for stress relief.

**Step 3: Si₃N₄ Deposition (LPCVD)** — ~100 nm nitride as oxidation mask.

**Step 4: Photolithography (n-well mask)** — Photoresist patterned; nitride etched by RIE in well windows.

**Step 5: Phosphorus Implantation (~10¹³ cm⁻² at 100 keV)** — Forms n-well.

```
   │ n-well │ p-sub │ n-well │
```

**Step 6: Strip Resist & Nitride** — Using H₃PO₄.

**Step 7: LOCOS (Field Oxidation)** — ~500 nm SiO₂ grows only over well windows; nitride blocks elsewhere.

```
   │SiO₂│SiO₂│SiO₂│   (field oxide)
```

**Step 8: Channel Stop Implant (Boron)** — Prevents parasitic conduction.

**Step 9: Gate Oxidation** — Thin (~10 nm) high-quality SiO₂.

**Step 10: Polysilicon Deposition + Doping** — ~300 nm poly, POCl₃ doped.

**Step 11: Gate Patterning** — Photolithography + RIE of polysilicon.

**Step 12: LDD Implants** — Lightly doped drain extensions.

**Step 13: Spacer Formation** — SiO₂/Si₃N₄ sidewall spacers.

**Step 14: S/D Implants** — As (n+) and BF₂ (p+) at high dose.

**Step 15: Anneal + Metallization + Passivation.**

**Function of 5 Unit Processes (2 marks):**

1. **Oxidation:** Provides insulating SiO₂ for gate dielectric and isolation.
2. **Lithography:** Transfers geometric patterns from mask to wafer; defines feature size.
3. **Etching:** Selectively removes unmasked material; must be anisotropic for sub-micron features.
4. **Ion Implantation:** Provides precise, controlled dopant profiles with sharp junctions.
5. **Deposition:** Adds uniform thin films of conductors, insulators, or semiconductors.

#### **Part (b) — Deal-Grove Derivation (7 marks)**

> **Mark Distribution:**
> - 6 derivation steps @ 1 mark each = 6 marks
> - Limiting regimes = 1 mark

**Step 1: Fick's Law for Oxidant Diffusion**

$$
F_1 = D \frac{C_0 - C_i}{x_o}
$$

**Step 2: Reaction Flux at Si–SiO₂ Interface**

$$
F_2 = k_s C_i
$$

**Step 3: Steady-State Equate $F_1 = F_2$**

$$
D \frac{C_0 - C_i}{x_o} = k_s C_i
$$

Solve for $C_i$:

$$
C_i = \frac{C_0}{1 + \frac{k_s x_o}{D}}
$$

**Step 4: Net Flux Expression**

$$
F = \frac{k_s C_0}{1 + \frac{k_s x_o}{D}}
$$

**Step 5: Relate to Growth Rate**

$$
\frac{dx_o}{dt} = \frac{F}{N_1} = \frac{B}{2x_o + A}
$$

(where $A = 2D/k_s$ and $B = 2DC_0/N_1$)

**Step 6: Integration yields Deal-Grove Equation**

$$
\boxed{x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)}
$$

**Limiting Regimes (1 mark):**

- **Linear (short times, $x_o \ll A$):** $x_o \approx \frac{B}{A} t$ — interface-reaction limited.
- **Parabolic (long times, $x_o \gg A$):** $x_o \approx \sqrt{Bt}$ — diffusion limited.
