KVL: $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t / R_p C_L}\right)$. **Step 2 — Define $t_{pLH}$ as the time to reach 50% point:** $V_{out}(t_{pLH}) = 0.5 V_{DD}$. $$ 0.5 V_{DD} = V_{DD}(1 - e^{-t_{pLH} / R_p C_L}) $$ $$ e^{-t_{pLH} / R_p C_L} = 0.5 $$ $$ t_{pLH} = R_p C_L \ln 2 = 0.69\,R_p C_L $$ **Step 3 — Similarly for HIGH-to-LOW:** $$ t_{pHL} = 0.69\,R_n C_L $$ **Step 4 — Average propagation delay:** $$ t_p = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2} = 0.69\,C_L\left(\frac{R_p + R_n}{2}\right) $$ > Text row: This is why **sizing transistors wider (larger $W/L$) reduces $R_{on}$ and hence delay** — a fundamental VLSI design trade-off between speed and area/power. ## 3.4 Derivation of Dynamic Switching Power **Step 1 — Energy stored on $C_L$ at voltage $V_{DD}$:** $$ E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Energy delivered by the supply during charging:** $$ E_{supply} = C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ (The factor of 2 difference arises because half the energy is dissipated as heat in the pMOS, even though it is "just" a switch — this is a classic, often-surprising result.) **Step 3 — Energy per switching transition:** $$ E_{transition} = C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Power with switching activity factor $\alpha$ and clock frequency $f$:** $$ P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f $$ > Text row: This is the **most important equation in modern low-power VLSI design**. Voltage scaling (reducing $V_{DD}$) is the *most effective* power-reduction knob because of the quadratic $V_{DD}^2$ dependence — this is why CPU voltages have dropped from 5V to sub-1V over four decades. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | CMOS Inverter | CMOS NAND (2-input) | CMOS NOR (2-input) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Series nMOS count | 1 | 2 | 1 | | Series pMOS count | 1 | 1 | 2 | | $t_{pHL}$ (worst case) | $0.69 R_n C_L$ | $0.69 (2R_n) C_L$ | $0.69 R_n C_L$ | | $t_{pLH}$ (worst case) | $0.69 R_p C_L$ | $0.69 R_p C_L$ | $0.69 (2R_p) C_L$ | | **Slower transition** | — | HIGH-to-LOW | LOW-to-HIGH | | Preferred use | Universal | Preferred complex gates | Less preferred | | Layout area | Smallest | Moderate | Larger (pMOS in series) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Transistor-Level Schematic ```mermaid graph TD VDD["V_DD Supply"] PMOS["pMOS Transistor MP
Source = V_DD
Gate = Vin
Drain = Vout"] NMOS["nMOS Transistor MN
Drain = Vout
Gate = Vin
Source = GND"] INPUT["Input Vin"] OUTPUT["Output Vout"] GND["GND 0V"] VDD --> PMOS PMOS --> OUTPUT INPUT --> PMOS INPUT --> NMOS NMOS --> OUTPUT NMOS --> GND ``` ## 4.2 CMOS NAND Gate — Pull-Up & Pull-Down Networks ```mermaid graph TD VDD["V_DD"] PUN["PUN Pull Up Network
MP1 parallel MP2
Both pMOS"] PDN["PDN Pull Down Network
MN1 series MN2
Both nMOS"] OUT["Output Y"] GND["GND"] VDD --> PUN PUN --> OUT OUT --> PDN PDN --> GND A["Input A"] --> PUN B["Input B"] --> PUN A --> PDN B --> PDN ``` ## 4.3 CMOS NOR Gate — Pull-Up & Pull-Down Networks ```mermaid graph TD VDD["V_DD"] PUN2["PUN Pull Up Network
MP1 series MP2
Both pMOS"] PDN2["PDN Pull Down Network
MN1 parallel MN2
Both nMOS"] OUT2["Output Y"] GND2["GND"] VDD --> PUN2 PUN2 --> OUT2 OUT2 --> PDN2 PDN2 --> GND2 A2["Input A"] --> PUN2 B2["Input B"] --> PUN2 A2 --> PDN2 B2 --> PDN2 ``` ## 4.4 CMOS Transmission Gate — Bidirectional Switch ```mermaid graph LR IN["Input A"] OUT3["Output B"] NGATE["nMOS Gate Control C"] PGATE["pMOS Gate Control Cbar"] GND3["GND"] VDD3["V_DD"] IN --> NCH["nMOS
Source Drain bidirectional"] IN --> PCH["pMOS
Source Drain bidirectional"] NCH --> OUT3 PCH --> OUT3 NGATE --> NCH PGATE --> PCH NCH -.-> GND3 PCH -.-> VDD3 ``` ## 4.5 CMOS Inverter — Functional Block & VTC Flow ```mermaid graph TD A["Input Voltage Vin"] --> B{"Vin compared to V_M"} B -- Vin less than V_M --> C["Region 1 or 2
pMOS Dominates
Vout = V_DD
Logic 1"] B -- Vin greater than V_M --> D["Region 4 or 5
nMOS Dominates
Vout = 0V
Logic 0"] C --> E["Output HIGH"] D --> F["Output LOW"] E --> G["Steady State
Near Zero Static Power"] F --> G ``` ## 4.6 CMOS Design Flow — Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TD S1["Step 1 Specify Boolean Function"] S2["Step 2 Construct PDN
using nMOS series for AND
parallel for OR"] S3["Step 3 Construct PUN
as DUAL of PDN
series parallel swap"] S4["Step 4 Verify
F AND Fbar complementary"] S5["Step 5 Size Transistors
WP WN for symmetric V_M"] S6["Step 6 Draw Stick Diagram
and Layout"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define CMOS logic and list its two main advantages. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]** **Model Answer:** CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) logic is a digital logic implementation style that uses complementary pairs of nMOS (pull-down) and pMOS (pull-up) transistors to implement logic functions. The defining feature is that the pull-up network (PUN) consists only of pMOS devices, and the pull-down network (PDN) consists only of nMOS devices. **Two main advantages:** 1. **Near-zero static power dissipation:** In steady state, either the PUN or the PDN is OFF, so there is no direct current path from V_DD to GND. Static power is only due to leakage currents. 2. **High noise immunity:** The VTC has sharp transitions, yielding large noise margins ($NM_H$ and $NM_L$ each approximately 0.4 V_DD). ### Question 2: Why are pMOS transistors typically made wider than nMOS transistors in a CMOS inverter? **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 3]** **Model Answer:** In CMOS technology, the hole mobility ($\mu_p$) is 2–3 times lower than the electron mobility ($\mu_n$). For a symmetric inverter with $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, the transconductance parameters $k_n$ and $k_p$ must be equal, where $k = \mu C_{ox} (W/L)$. Since $\mu_p < \mu_n$, to make $k_p = k_n$, the pMOS device must be sized with a **larger $W/L$ ratio** — typically $W_p / W_n \approx \mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$. This compensates for the lower hole mobility and ensures balanced rise/fall times and a switching threshold of $V_{DD}/2$. ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. --- ### Question A: CMOS Inverter Analysis (14 Marks) **(a) Draw the circuit diagram of a CMOS inverter and explain its operation in all five regions of the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC). Derive the expression for the switching threshold $V_M$. [7 Marks]** **(b) Explain the concept of noise margins in a CMOS inverter. Derive the expressions for $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ for a symmetric inverter. A CMOS inverter is operated at $V_{DD} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ with $V_{Tn} = |V_{Tp}| = 0.7\,\text{V}$. Calculate the noise margins. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution for (a):** **Circuit Diagram:** Already shown in Section 4.1. pMOS MP with source at V_DD, drain at V_out; nMOS MN with drain at V_out, source at GND. Common gate at V_in. **Five Regions of VTC:** | Region | Input Voltage | nMOS state | pMOS state | Output | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | $0 \le V_{in} < V_{Tn}$ | Cutoff | Triode | $V_{DD}$ | | 2 | $V_{Tn} \le V_{in} < V_M$ | Saturation | Triode | Falling | | 3 | $V_{in} = V_M$ | Saturation | Saturation | $V_{DD}/2$ | | 4 | $V_M < V_{in} \le V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ | Triode | Saturation | Falling | | 5 | $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ | Triode | Cutoff | $0$ | **Derivation of $V_M$:** (See Section 3.1, steps 1–8.) Final expression: $$ V_M = \frac{\sqrt{k_p}(V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|) + \sqrt{k_n} V_{Tn}}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}} = \frac{r(V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|) + V_{Tn}}{1+r} $$ For symmetric inverter ($k_n = k_p$, $V_{Tn} = |V_{Tp}| = V_T$): $V_M = V_{DD}/2$. **Model Solution for (b):** **Noise Margins Definition:** Noise margin is the maximum noise voltage that can be superimposed on a logic signal without causing erroneous switching. - $V_{IL}$: maximum input voltage still recognized as LOW (slope = −1 point on VTC). - $V_{IH}$: minimum input voltage still recognized as HIGH. - $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ (HIGH-state noise margin). - $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ (LOW-state noise margin). **Formulas for symmetric inverter** (from Section 3.2): $$ V_{IL} \approx \frac{3V_{DD} - 2V_T}{8}, \quad V_{IH} \approx \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_T}{8} $$ $$ NM_H \approx \frac{5V_{DD} - 2V_T}{8}, \quad NM_L \approx \frac{3V_{DD} - 2V_T}{8} $$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given $V_{DD} = 3.3\,\text{V}$, $V_T = 0.7\,\text{V}$. $$ NM_L = \frac{3(3.3) - 2(0.7)}{8} = \frac{9.9 - 1.4}{8} = \frac{8.5}{8} = 1.0625\,\text{V} $$ $$ NM_H = \frac{5(3.3) - 2(0.7)}{8} = \frac{16.5 - 1.4}{8} = \frac{15.1}{8} = 1.8875\,\text{V} $$ $$ \boxed{NM_L \approx 1.06\,\text{V}, \quad NM_H \approx 1.89\,\text{V}} $$ --- ### Question B: CMOS Gates & Transmission Gates (14 Marks) **(a) Draw the transistor-level schematic of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate and a 2-input CMOS NOR gate. Explain their operation using truth tables. Why is NAND preferred over NOR in CMOS design? [7 Marks]** **(b) Explain the operation of a CMOS transmission gate with a neat diagram. List two applications. A CMOS transmission gate is used to pass a signal that swings from 0 to 2.5 V using $V_{DD} = 2.5\,\text{V}$. Explain how both logic 0 and logic 1 are passed cleanly. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution for (a):** **CMOS NAND (2-input):** - 2 pMOS in **parallel** (MP1, MP2) between V_DD and output Y. - 2 nMOS in **series** (MN1, MN2) between output Y and GND. - Inputs A, B connected to the gates of MP1, MP2, MN1, MN2. **Truth Table:** | A | B | MN1 | MN2 (series) | MP1 | MP2 (parallel) | Y | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 0 | 0 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | 1 | | 0 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | 1 | | 1 | 0 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | 1 | | 1 | 1 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | 0 | → $Y = \overline{AB}$ ✓ **CMOS NOR (2-input):** - 2 pMOS in **series** between V_DD and Y. - 2 nMOS in **parallel** between Y and GND. **Truth Table:** | A | B | nMOS (parallel) | pMOS (series) | Y | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 0 | 0 | OFF-OFF | ON-ON | 1 | | 0 | 1 | OFF-ON | ON-OFF | 0 | | 1 | 0 | ON-OFF | OFF-ON | 0 | | 1 | 1 | ON-ON | OFF-OFF | 0 | → $Y = \overline{A + B}$ ✓ **Why NAND is preferred over NOR in CMOS:** 1. **Mobility mismatch:** pMOS is slower than nMOS. In NOR, the pMOS are in *series* (worst case for slow pull-up), making the LOW-to-HIGH transition very slow. In NAND, the pMOS are in *parallel* — both contribute strong drive simultaneously. 2. **Worst-case delay:** For a 2-input NOR, the worst-case $t_{pLH}$ has TWO pMOS in series, doubling the pull-up resistance. NAND has only ONE pMOS in the slow path. 3. **Layout area:** NAND with parallel pMOS occupies less silicon area than NOR with series pMOS in standard cell libraries. 4. **Consequence in industry:** Standard cell libraries (used in all ASIC/FPGA design) overwhelmingly use NAND-based complex gates (AOI, OAI) for performance reasons. **Model Solution for (b):** **Transmission Gate Operation** (diagram from Section 4.4): A transmission gate consists of an nMOS and pMOS transistor connected in parallel, with their gates driven by complementary control signals $C$ and $\bar{C}$. - When $C = 1$ ($V_{DD}$): nMOS gate is HIGH (nMOS ON), pMOS gate is LOW (pMOS ON) → both conduct → switch is **CLOSED**, signal passes from input to output. - When $C = 0$ (GND): nMOS gate is LOW (nMOS OFF), pMOS gate is HIGH (pMOS OFF) → both off → switch is **OPEN**, output is high impedance. **Bidirectional:** The drain and source of either transistor can be the input or output — the device is symmetric. **Passing logic 0 (0V):** The nMOS is strongly ON with $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$. It pulls the output strongly to GND. The pMOS is also ON but with $V_{GS} = 0$ (weak contribution). The nMOS alone provides a strong 0. **Passing logic 1 (2.5V = $V_{DD}$):** The pMOS has $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - 0 = 2.5$ V, so it is strongly ON. It pulls the output strongly up to $V_{DD}$. The nMOS turns OFF as $V_{out}$ approaches $V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$, but the pMOS finishes the job. **Conclusion:** A single transmission gate passes *both* logic 0 and logic 1 *unattenuated* — this is its key advantage over a single nMOS or single pMOS pass transistor, which suffers threshold drop. **Two Applications:** 1. **Multiplexers (MUX):** A 2:1 MUX can be built from two transmission gates sharing a common output. 2. **XOR / XNOR gates:** Built efficiently using 4 transmission gates. 3. **SRAM 6T cell read/write access** (optional 3rd application). 4. **Analog switches** in sample-and-hold circuits and data converters. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to AVOID:** > 1. **Forgetting the body effect** in $V_M$ derivation — when source of nMOS is not at GND (e.g., in a pass-transistor chain), the threshold voltage shifts, altering $V_M$. > 2. **Confusing PUN/PDN rules:** Students often place pMOS in series where parallel is needed (or vice versa). **Remember:** nMOS in series implements AND, nMOS in parallel implements OR, and the PUN is the *Duality* of the PDN. > 3. **Ignoring $V_{Tp}$ sign in formulas:** pMOS has a *negative* threshold. Sign errors in $V_M$ derivation cost full marks. > 4. **Assuming single transistor = transmission gate:** A single nMOS pass transistor has a *threshold drop* ($V_{out,max} = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$). Only a CMOS transmission gate (nMOS + pMOS pair) gives rail-to-rail switching. > 5. **In NOR gate analysis, students frequently write "pMOS in parallel"** — this is WRONG. NOR has pMOS in **series** (which is why it's slow). ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - [x] **CMOS = Complementary MOS** (pMOS pull-up + nMOS pull-down). Two transistors per inverter. - [x] **Golden rule:** In steady state, **only one network conducts** → no static current → near-zero static power. - [x] **VTC has 5 regions**, with $V_M$ in the middle (both transistors in saturation). - [x] **Switching threshold** $V_M = \dfrac{r(V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|) + V_{Tn}}{1+r}$ where $r = \sqrt{k_p/k_n}$. - [x] **Symmetric inverter** ($V_M = V_{DD}/2$) requires $k_p = k_n$ → $W_p \approx 2W_n$ (because $\mu_p < \mu_n$). - [x] **Noise margins** $NM_H \approx NM_L \approx 0.4V_{DD}$ for symmetric design. - [x] **NAND:** pMOS parallel, nMOS series. **NOR:** pMOS series, nMOS parallel. - [x] **NAND preferred** over NOR in CMOS (parallel pMOS = strong pull-up). - [x] **Transmission gate** = nMOS + pMOS in parallel, with complementary gate control. Passes both 0 and 1 cleanly. - [x] **Propagation delay** $t_p = 0.69 R_{on} C_L$. Wider transistors → smaller $R_{on}$ → faster. - [x] **Dynamic power** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — quadratic in $V_{DD}$ (most important power equation). - [x] **Static power** $\approx 0$ in ideal CMOS; dominated by leakage in modern nanometer nodes. - [x] **Body effect** increases $V_T$ when source is not at ground — affects pass-transistor design. - [x] **Duality rule** for CMOS construction: PDN and PUN are duals (series ↔ parallel, nMOS ↔ pMOS). - [x] **Mobility ratio** $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2\text{–}3$ — memorize for sizing problems. - [x] **PDP (Power-Delay Product)** is the universal figure of merit for gate efficiency. - [x] **Threshold drop** issue: single nMOS pass transistor cannot pass full $V_{DD}$ → use transmission gate. - [x] **Standard cell libraries** use NAND/AOI structures, not NOR, for speed. - [x] **Layout rule check:** stick diagrams must respect poly-diffusion spacing, n-well rules, contact cuts. - [x] **SRAM 6T cell** uses 4 nMOS + 2 pMOS — a real-world CMOS application. - [x] **Shorthand device count:** Inverter=2, NAND=4, NOR=4, AOI21=6, MUX (TG-based)=8.

topics.title: Static and Transient Analysis of CMOS Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Static and Transient Analysis of CMOS Inverter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **CMOS Inverter** is the fundamental building block of all digital CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) integrated circuits. It consists of a **PMOS transistor** (pull-up network) and an **NMOS transistor** (pull-down network) connected in series between the supply rail **$V_{DD}$** and ground, with their gates tied together as the input node and the common drain terminal serving as the output node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A CMOS inverter is a ratio-less logic gate in which the output voltage ($V_{OH}$ or $V_{OL}$) is independent of the transistor sizes (W/L ratios) under steady-state conditions, in stark contrast to the resistive-load or pseudo-NMOS inverters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Dual-Faucet Water Tap

Imagine a water tap system with **two valves**:
- The **red valve** (PMOS) opens only when you push the handle **DOWN** and feeds water from a tank above.
- The **blue valve** (NMOS) opens only when you push the handle **UP** and drains water to the ground.

**Only one valve is open at any instant.** When you push up → blue opens, red closes → water drains (output = LOW/0V). When you push down → red opens, blue closes → water fills the pipe (output = HIGH/$V_{DD}$). This is precisely how a CMOS inverter operates: it is a **complementary switch** that never short-circuits $V_{DD}$ to GND during static operation, leading to **near-zero static power dissipation**.

## 1.3 Circuit Topology & Static Behavior

The structure comprises:
- **PMOS source** → connected to **$V_{DD}$**
- **NMOS source** → connected to **GND (0V)**
- **PMOS bulk** → connected to **$V_{DD}$**
- **NMOS bulk** → connected to **GND**
- **Gates tied together** → form the **Input (Vin)**
- **Drains tied together** → form the **Output (Vout)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Static Metrics** (must be memorized for KTU exams):
> - $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ (when input is LOW)
> - $V_{OL} = 0\,V$ (when input is HIGH)
> - Switching Threshold: $V_M$ (where $V_{in} = V_{out}$)
> - $V_{IH}$: minimum input recognized as logic HIGH
> - $V_{IL}$: maximum input recognized as logic LOW
> - **Noise Margins:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ and $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (for $V_{DD}=2.5V$, 90nm node) |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **2.5 V** |
| NMOS Threshold | $V_{tn}$ | **0.4 V** |
| PMOS Threshold | $V_{tp}$ | **−0.4 V** |
| Oxide Capacitance | $C_{ox}$ | $\approx 8.5 \times 10^{-3}\,F/m^2$ |
| Switching Threshold | $V_M$ | $\approx V_{DD}/2$ (symmetric) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Five Operating Regions of the CMOS Inverter

As $V_{in}$ ramps from **0 to $V_{DD}$**, the CMOS inverter passes through **five distinct regions**. Each region is described by the state of the two transistors:

| Region | $V_{in}$ Range | NMOS State | PMOS State | Output Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | $0 \le V_{in} \le V_{tn}$ | **Cutoff** | **Linear (Triode)** | $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ |
| **B** | $V_{tn} \le V_{in} \le V_{out} + V_{tp}$ | **Saturation** | **Linear (Triode)** | $V_{out}$ decreases |
| **C** | $V_{in} = V_M$ | **Saturation** | **Saturation** | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (switching point) |
| **D** | $V_{out} + V_{tp} \le V_{in} \le V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$ | **Linear (Triode)** | **Saturation** | $V_{out}$ continues to fall |
| **E** | $V_{in} \ge V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$ | **Linear (Triode)** | **Cutoff** | $V_{out} = V_{OL} = 0$ |

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Region by Region

### Region A ($V_{in} < V_{tn}$)
- **NMOS:** $V_{GS} = V_{in} < V_{tn}$ → **OFF** ($I_{DN} = 0$)
- **PMOS:** $V_{GS} = V_{in} - V_{DD}$ is very negative → **STRONGLY ON (Linear)**
- Current through both is essentially zero, so there is **no voltage drop** across the PMOS.
- Therefore, $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ (output is HIGH).

### Region B ($V_{tn} \le V_{in} \le V_{M}$)
- **NMOS turns ON in saturation** (since $V_{DS} = V_{out}$ is large).
- **PMOS remains in linear region** (since $V_{DS} = V_{out} - V_{DD}$ is small in magnitude).
- As $V_{in}$ rises, $I_{DN}$ increases, pulling $V_{out}$ down.
- Current continuity: $I_{DN,sat} = I_{DP,lin}$.

### Region C — The Switching Point ($V_{in} = V_M$)
- **Both transistors are in saturation**.
- The defining condition is $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$.
- $I_{DN,sat} = I_{DP,sat}$ is solved to obtain the closed-form expression for $V_M$.

### Region D ($V_{M} \le V_{in} \le V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$)
- **NMOS is in linear region** (small $V_{DS} = V_{out}$).
- **PMOS is in saturation** ($V_{DS} = V_{out} - V_{DD}$ has large magnitude).
- $V_{out}$ continues to fall rapidly.

### Region E ($V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$)
- **PMOS turns OFF** because $\vert V_{GS}\vert = V_{DD} - V_{in} < \vert V_{tp}\vert$.
- **NMOS is in linear region** with very small $V_{DS}$.
- $V_{out} = 0\,V$.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they constitute ~70% of KTU VLSI exam marks in this module.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output High | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | Input = LOW |
| 2 | Output Low | $V_{OL} = 0\,V$ | Input = HIGH |
| 3 | Switching Threshold | $V_M = \dfrac{r(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}}{1 + r}$ | where $r = \dfrac{k_n}{k_p} = \dfrac{\mu_n W_n}{\mu_p W_p} \cdot \dfrac{L_p}{L_n}$ |
| 4 | Symmetric $V_M$ | $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ | when $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$ and $r = 1$ |
| 5 | Noise Margin High | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | $V_{IH} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp}\vert}{8}$ (approx, symmetric) |
| 6 | Noise Margin Low | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | $V_{IL} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{tp}\vert + V_{tn}}{8}$ (approx, symmetric) |
| 7 | Equal Noise Margins | $NM_H = NM_L = \dfrac{3V_{DD} - (V_{tn} + \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{8}$ | Symmetric inverter condition |
| 8 | HL Propagation Delay | $t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L}{k_n(V_{DD}-V_{tn})} \cdot \left[\dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} + \dfrac{2V_{tn}}{3}\right]$ | Discharging through NMOS |
| 9 | LH Propagation Delay | $t_{pLH} = \dfrac{C_L}{k_p(V_{DD}-\vert V_{tp}\vert)} \cdot \left[\dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} + \dfrac{2\vert V_{tp}\vert}{3}\right]$ | Charging through PMOS |
| 10 | Average Delay | $t_p = \dfrac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}$ | Standard KTU definition |
| 11 | RC Model Delay | $t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eqn} \cdot C_L$ | $R_{eqn}$: equivalent NMOS resistance |
| 12 | Rise Time | $t_r = 0.69 \cdot R_{eqp} \cdot C_L$ | 10% to 90% of $V_{DD}$ |
| 13 | Fall Time | $t_f = 0.69 \cdot R_{eqn} \cdot C_L$ | 90% to 10% of $V_{DD}$ |
| 14 | Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity |
| 15 | Static Power | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Due to subthreshold leakage |
| 16 | Short-Circuit Power | $P_{SC} \propto \beta \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 \cdot f \cdot \tau$ | During switching transition |
| 17 | Gate Capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Farads per unit area |
| 18 | Transconductance | $k_n = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W_n}{L_n}$ | NMOS parameter |
| 19 | Parasitic Drain Cap | $C_{db} = C_j \cdot A_d + C_{jsw} \cdot P_d$ | Bottom + sidewall junction |
| 20 | Total Load Cap | $C_L = C_{gdn} + C_{gdp} + C_{db} + C_{sb} + C_w$ | Sum of all caps at output node |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Static analysis determines **noise immunity, fan-out capability, and logic levels** for chip-to-chip communication (e.g., LVTTL, LVDS standards). Transient analysis determines the **maximum operating frequency** of microprocessors (modern CPUs target $t_p < 10\,ps$ per gate).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold $V_M$

**Starting condition:** At the switching point, both transistors are in saturation and $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$.

**Step 1:** Write the saturation current equations for both transistors.

For NMOS in saturation:
$$I_{DN,sat} = k_n (V_{GS,n} - V_{tn})^2 = k_n (V_M - V_{tn})^2$$

For PMOS in saturation:
$$I_{DP,sat} = k_p (V_{GS,p} - V_{tp})^2 = k_p (V_M - V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply KCL — the currents must be equal in magnitude (one flows up, one flows down through the output node).

$$I_{DN,sat} = I_{DP,sat}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and divide by $k_p$ to introduce the ratio $r = k_n / k_p$.

$$r \cdot (V_M - V_{tn})^2 = (V_M - V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$$

**Step 4:** Take the positive square root of both sides (since both factors are positive in the transition region).

$$\sqrt{r} \cdot (V_M - V_{tn}) = (V_M - V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert)$$

**Step 5:** Expand and collect terms containing $V_M$.

$$\sqrt{r} \cdot V_M - \sqrt{r} \cdot V_{tn} = V_M - V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate $V_M$ on the left-hand side.

$$\sqrt{r} \cdot V_M - V_M = \sqrt{r} \cdot V_{tn} - V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert$$

**Step 7:** Factor out $V_M$ and solve.

$$V_M \cdot (\sqrt{r} - 1) = \sqrt{r} \cdot V_{tn} - V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert$$

**Step 8:** Final closed-form expression.

$$V_M = \dfrac{\sqrt{r} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For a symmetric inverter** where $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$ and $\sqrt{r} = 1$ (i.e., $k_n = k_p$), this elegantly simplifies to $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Noise Margins (Approximate Bakoglu Method)

The exact values of $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ require solving transcendental MOSFET equations. The standard KTU approach uses the **linearized approximation** valid when $V_{DD} \gg V_T$.

**Starting condition:** $V_{IL}$ occurs at the boundary where $V_{out} = V_{OH} - \Delta V$ begins to deviate, and $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$.

**For $V_{IL}$:** NMOS is in saturation, PMOS is in linear region.

After lengthy algebra (which the KTU examiner expects you to **state and use**, not re-derive in full):

$$V_{IL} = \dfrac{2V_{out} + V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{tp}\vert - k_r V_{tn}}{2(1 + k_r)}$$

where $k_r = \sqrt{k_n / k_p}$.

**For the symmetric case** ($k_r = 1$, $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$, $V_{out} \approx V_{DD}$):

$$V_{IL} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{tp}\vert + V_{tn}}{8}$$

**Substituting** $V_{OL} = 0$:

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{tp}\vert + V_{tn}}{8}$$

**Similarly, by symmetry:**

$$V_{IH} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp}\vert}{8}$$

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp}\vert}{8} = \dfrac{5V_{DD} - 2V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp}\vert}{8}$$

**For a fully symmetric inverter** ($V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$):

$$NM_H = NM_L = \dfrac{3V_{DD} - 2V_{tn} - 2\vert V_{tp}\vert}{8} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} - 4V_{tn}}{4}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Propagation Delay $t_{pHL}$

**Step 1:** When the input switches from LOW to HIGH, the NMOS turns ON and the PMOS turns OFF. The load capacitance $C_L$ at the output node **discharges** through the NMOS.

**Step 2:** The governing KCL equation at the output node:

$$C_L \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -I_{DN}$$

**Step 3:** During the initial discharge phase, the NMOS operates in **saturation** (since $V_{DS} = V_{out}$ is large). Thus:

$$C_L \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -k_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from the 50% point of the output transition.

**Step 5:** When the output has discharged to $V_{out} = V_{DD}/2$:

$$t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 k_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2}$$

**Step 6:** When the NMOS enters the **linear region** during the final phase, the integration yields an additional term:

$$t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L}{k_n(V_{DD} - V_{tn})} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} + \dfrac{2V_{tn}}{3} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** In the **simplified RC model**, this reduces to $t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eqn} \cdot C_L$, where $R_{eqn}$ is the **average on-resistance** of the NMOS during switching. Both forms are accepted in KTU answer sheets.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter is fabricated in a **0.18 µm process** with $V_{DD} = 1.8\,V$, $V_{tn} = 0.4\,V$, $V_{tp} = -0.4\,V$, $k_n = 250\,\mu A/V^2$, $k_p = 100\,\mu A/V^2$, and $C_L = 50\,fF$. Calculate: (a) $V_M$, (b) $NM_L$, (c) $t_{pHL}$.

### Part (a): Switching Threshold

$$r = \dfrac{k_n}{k_p} = \dfrac{250}{100} = 2.5$$

$$\sqrt{r} = \sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.581$$

$$V_M = \dfrac{1.581 \cdot (1.8 - 0.4) + 0.4}{1 + 1.581} = \dfrac{1.581 \cdot 1.4 + 0.4}{2.581} = \dfrac{2.6134}{2.581} \approx 1.0125\,V$$

### Part (b): Noise Margin Low

$$NM_L = \dfrac{3 \cdot 1.8 - 2 \cdot 0.4 + 0.4}{8} = \dfrac{5.4 - 0.8 + 0.4}{8} = \dfrac{5.0}{8} = 0.625\,V$$

### Part (c): Propagation Delay (HL)

$$t_{pHL} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-15}}{250 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1.8 - 0.4)} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1.8}{2} + \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.4}{3} \right]$$

$$t_{pHL} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-15}}{3.5 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot [0.9 + 0.2667]$$

$$t_{pHL} = 1.4286 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 1.1667 = 1.667 \times 10^{-10}\,s \approx 167\,ps$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Inverter Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vin[Input Voltage Vin]
    Gnd[GND 0V]
    Vdd[VDD Supply]

    subgraph SG1[PMOS Pull Up Network]
        P1[PMOS Device]
        SP[PMOS Source]
        BP[PMOS Bulk to VDD]
        GP[PMOS Gate]
        DP[PMOS Drain]
    end

    subgraph SG2[NMOS Pull Down Network]
        N1[NMOS Device]
        SN[NMOS Source]
        BN[NMOS Bulk to GND]
        GN[NMOS Gate]
        DN[NMOS Drain]
    end

    Cap[Load Capacitance CL]

    Vin --- GP
    Vin --- GN
    Vdd --- SP
    Vdd --- BP
    Gnd --- SN
    Gnd --- BN
    DP --- DN
    DP --- DN --- Cap
    DP --- DN --- Vout[Output Voltage Vout]
```

## 4.2 Voltage Transfer Characteristic Curve Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG3[VTC Operating Regions]
        RA[Region A Vin less than Vtn]
        RB[Region B Vin between Vtn and VM]
        RC[Region C Vin equals VM]
        RD[Region D Vin between VM and VDD minus Vtp]
        RE[Region E Vin greater than VDD minus Vtp]
    end

    NMOS_OFF[NMOS Cutoff]
    PMOS_LIN[PMOS Linear]
    NMOS_SAT[NMOS Saturation]
    BOTH_SAT[Both Saturation]
    PMOS_OFF[PMOS Cutoff]
    NMOS_LIN[NMOS Linear]

    RA --- NMOS_OFF
    RA --- PMOS_LIN
    RB --- NMOS_SAT
    RB --- PMOS_LIN
    RC --- BOTH_SAT
    RD --- NMOS_LIN
    RD --- PMOS_SAT
    RE --- NMOS_LIN
    RE --- PMOS_OFF
```

## 4.3 Parasitic Capacitance Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Out[Output Node Vout]

    subgraph SG4[Intrinsic MOSFET Capacitances]
        Cgsn[NMOS Cgs]
        Cgdn[NMOS Cgd]
        Cgbn[NMOS Cgb]
        Cgsp[PMOS Cgs]
        Cgdp[PMOS Cgd]
        Cgbp[PMOS Cgb]
    end

    subgraph SG5[Junction Capacitances]
        Cdb[NMOS Drain Body Cap]
        Csb[NMOS Source Body Cap]
        Cdb2[PMOS Drain Body Cap]
        Csb2[PMOS Source Body Cap]
    end

    subgraph SG6[Interconnect and Load]
        Cw[Wire Capacitance]
        Cnext[Next Stage Gate Cap]
    end

    Out --- Cgdn
    Out --- Cgdp
    Out --- Cdb
    Out --- Cdb2
    Out --- Cw
    Out --- Cnext

    Cgsn --- GndA[Ground]
    Cgbn --- GndA
    Csb --- GndA
    Cgsp --- VddA[VDD]
    Cgbp --- VddA
    Csb2 --- VddA
```

## 4.4 Propagation Delay Timing Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG7[Input Vin Pulse]
        I1[Vin LOW 0V]
        I2[Vin Rising Edge]
        I3[Vin HIGH VDD]
        I4[Vin Falling Edge]
        I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5[Vin LOW 0V]
    end

    subgraph SG8[Output Vout Response]
        O1[Vout HIGH VDD]
        O2[Vout Falling Delayed by tpHL]
        O3[Vout LOW 0V]
        O4[Vout Rising Delayed by tpLH]
        O5[Vout HIGH VDD]
        O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> O4 --> O5
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the switching threshold voltage ($V_M$) of a CMOS inverter. Derive the condition under which $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **switching threshold voltage** $V_M$ of a CMOS inverter is defined as the point on the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) curve where the input voltage $V_{in}$ equals the output voltage $V_{out}$, i.e., $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$. At this point, both the NMOS and PMOS transistors operate in the **saturation region**, and the small-signal gain $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$.

**Derivation of the symmetric condition:**

The general expression is:

$$V_M = \dfrac{\sqrt{r} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$$

For $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, we require:

$$\dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} \cdot (1 + \sqrt{r}) = \sqrt{r} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}$$

This is satisfied when **$V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$** AND **$\sqrt{r} = 1$** (i.e., $k_n = k_p$). These two simultaneous conditions define a **symmetric CMOS inverter** with equal noise margins.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the parasitic capacitances associated with the output node of a CMOS inverter. How does each affect the propagation delay?**

**Model Answer:**

The output node of a CMOS inverter sees the following parasitic capacitances:

1. **$C_{gd,n}$ (NMOS gate-to-drain overlap capacitance):** Miller-effect amplified during switching, contributing significantly to $t_{pHL}$.

2. **$C_{gd,p}$ (PMOS gate-to-drain overlap capacitance):** Similar Miller effect, contributes to $t_{pLH}$.

3. **$C_{db,n}$ (NMOS drain-to-body junction capacitance):** Voltage-dependent (non-linear), increases delay as $V_{out}$ falls.

4. **$C_{db,p}$ (PMOS drain-to-body junction capacitance):** Same effect during charging.

5. **$C_w$ (interconnect/wire capacitance):** Routing-dependent, becomes dominant in deep-submicron designs.

6. **$C_{g,next}$ (input gate capacitance of the next stage):** Fan-out dependent, scales linearly with fan-out.

**Effect on delay:** Total load $C_L = C_{gd,n} + C_{gd,p} + C_{db,n} + C_{db,p} + C_w + C_{g,next}$. Since $t_p \propto C_L$, minimizing $C_L$ is critical for high-speed operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **CMOS inverter**. Sketch its **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** and identify all five operating regions.

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **switching threshold voltage $V_M$** of a CMOS inverter. For a symmetric inverter with $V_{DD} = 2.5\,V$, $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.5\,V$, and $k_n = k_p = 100\,\mu A/V^2$, calculate $V_M$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $NM_H$, and $NM_L$.

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Diagram (Verbal Description):**
- A PMOS transistor $M_P$ with source tied to $V_{DD}$, drain tied to output.
- An NMOS transistor $M_N$ with source tied to GND, drain tied to output.
- Gates of both tied together form the input $V_{in}$.
- Output node $V_{out}$ is loaded with capacitance $C_L$.

**Operation:**
- When $V_{in} = 0$: NMOS is **OFF**, PMOS is **ON** → $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ (logic HIGH).
- When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$: NMOS is **ON**, PMOS is **OFF** → $V_{out} = 0$ (logic LOW).
- During transition: Both transistors conduct briefly.

**Five Regions of VTC:**

| Region | $V_{in}$ | NMOS | PMOS | $V_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | $0$ to $V_{tn}$ | Cutoff | Linear | $V_{DD}$ |
| B | $V_{tn}$ to $V_M$ | Saturation | Linear | Decreasing |
| C | $V_M$ | Saturation | Saturation | $V_M$ |
| D | $V_M$ to $V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$ | Linear | Saturation | Falling |
| E | $V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$ to $V_{DD}$ | Linear | Cutoff | $0$ |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of $V_M$** (refer to Section 3.1 of these notes):

$$V_M = \dfrac{\sqrt{r} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$$

For the given symmetric inverter: $r = k_n/k_p = 1$, so $\sqrt{r} = 1$, and $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.5\,V$.

**Calculation of $V_M$:**

$$V_M = \dfrac{1 \cdot (2.5 - 0.5) + 0.5}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{2.5}{2} = 1.25\,V$$

**Calculation of $V_{IL}$:**

$$V_{IL} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{tp}\vert + V_{tn}}{8} = \dfrac{3(2.5) - 2(0.5) + 0.5}{8} = \dfrac{7.5 - 1.0 + 0.5}{8} = \dfrac{7.0}{8} = 0.875\,V$$

**Calculation of $V_{IH}$:**

$$V_{IH} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp}\vert}{8} = \dfrac{7.5 + 1.0 - 0.5}{8} = \dfrac{8.0}{8} = 1.0\,V$$

**Calculation of $NM_H$:**

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 2.5 - 1.0 = 1.5\,V$$

**Calculation of $NM_L$:**

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.875 - 0 = 0.875\,V$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** A common student error is using $V_{IL} = V_{IH} = V_{DD}/2$ in noise margin calculations. The KTU evaluator will deduct **2 full marks** for this. Always use the derived Bakoglu formulas.
> 
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** Do not forget to specify that **both transistors are in saturation** at the switching point. Omitting this condition costs **1 mark**.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **transient analysis** of a CMOS inverter. Derive the expression for the **propagation delay $t_{pHL}$** using the RC delay model.

**(b)** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance $C_L = 100\,fF$. The NMOS transconductance parameter is $k_n = 200\,\mu A/V^2$, and the supply voltage is $V_{DD} = 2.0\,V$ with $V_{tn} = 0.4\,V$. Calculate $t_{pHL}$ and the average propagation delay $t_p$ if $t_{pLH} = 80\,ps$.

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Transient Analysis Overview:**
Transient analysis determines the **switching speed** of a CMOS inverter — how fast the output can change states in response to an input transition. The two key parameters are:

- **$t_{pHL}$:** Propagation delay from HIGH-to-LOW transition.
- **$t_{pLH}$:** Propagation delay from LOW-to-HIGH transition.

**RC Delay Model Derivation:**

When input switches from LOW to HIGH at $t = 0$, the PMOS turns OFF and the NMOS turns ON. The output capacitance $C_L$ discharges through the NMOS, modeled as a resistor $R_{eqn}$.

The discharge equation is:

$$C_L \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} + \dfrac{V_{out}}{R_{eqn}} = 0$$

Solving with initial condition $V_{out}(0) = V_{DD}$:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t / (R_{eqn} C_L)}$$

The 50% delay point $V_{out} = V_{DD}/2$ occurs at:

$$t_{pHL} = R_{eqn} \cdot C_L \cdot \ln(2) = 0.69 \cdot R_{eqn} \cdot C_L$$

Where $R_{eqn}$ is computed from the linear-region NMOS on-resistance:

$$R_{eqn} = \dfrac{1}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{t_{pHL} = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})}}$$

A more accurate expression (including both saturation and linear phases) is:

$$t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L}{k_n(V_{DD} - V_{tn})} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} + \dfrac{2V_{tn}}{3} \right]$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $C_L = 100\,fF = 100 \times 10^{-15}\,F$
- $k_n = 200\,\mu A/V^2 = 200 \times 10^{-6}\,A/V^2$
- $V_{DD} = 2.0\,V$
- $V_{tn} = 0.4\,V$
- $t_{pLH} = 80\,ps$

**Method 1: Using the accurate formula:**

$$t_{pHL} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-15}}{200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (2.0 - 0.4)} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2.0}{2} + \dfrac{2 \times 0.4}{3} \right]$$

$$t_{pHL} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-15}}{3.2 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot \left[ 1.0 + 0.2667 \right]$$

$$t_{pHL} = 3.125 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 1.2667 = 3.958 \times 10^{-10}\,s \approx 395.8\,ps$$

**Method 2: Using the simplified RC model:**

$$R_{eqn} = \dfrac{1}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})} = \dfrac{1}{200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.6} = 3125\,\Omega = 3.125\,k\Omega$$

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot 3125 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} = 215.6\,ps$$

**Average Propagation Delay:**

$$t_p = \dfrac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2} = \dfrac{395.8 + 80}{2} = 237.9\,ps \quad \text{(using Method 1)}$$

or

$$t_p = \dfrac{215.6 + 80}{2} = 147.8\,ps \quad \text{(using Method 2)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #3:** Always state **which formula** you are using. The KTU evaluator will deduct marks if you mix the two approaches within the same problem.
> 
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #4:** Unit conversion errors (fF vs F, µA vs A) account for **30% of all mark deductions** in transient analysis problems. Always convert to SI base units before computation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 20 key points before entering the KTU examination hall:**

- [ ] CMOS inverter = 1 PMOS (pull-up) + 1 NMOS (pull-down), series between $V_{DD}$ and GND.
- [ ] **Ratio-less logic:** Static output is independent of (W/L) ratios — only the **dynamic** and **noise** performance depends on sizing.
- [ ] **Five regions of VTC:** A (NMOS off), B (NMOS sat, PMOS lin), C (both sat), D (NMOS lin, PMOS sat), E (PMOS off).
- [ ] $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0\,V$ (rail-to-rail swing).
- [ ] Switching threshold formula: $V_M = \dfrac{\sqrt{r}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}}{1 + \sqrt{r}}$.
- [ ] Symmetric inverter condition: $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert$ AND $k_n = k_p$ → $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.
- [ ] Noise margins: $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$, $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$.
- [ ] Bakoglu's approximation: $V_{IL} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{tp}\vert + V_{tn}}{8}$.
- [ ] Bakoglu's approximation: $V_{IH} = \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp}\vert}{8}$.
- [ ] Equal noise margin (symmetric): $NM_H = NM_L = \dfrac{3V_{DD} - 2(V_{tn} + \vert V_{tp}\vert)}{8}$.
- [ ] **Beta ratio** $r = k_n / k_p$ controls the symmetry and $V_M$ placement.
- [ ] Propagation delay: $t_{pHL} = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})}$ (simplified RC model).
- [ ] Accurate $t_{pHL}$: $\dfrac{C_L}{k_n(V_{DD} - V_{tn})} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} + \dfrac{2V_{tn}}{3} \right]$.
- [ ] Average delay: $t_p = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}) / 2$.
- [ ] **Dynamic power** dominates: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$.
- [ ] **Static power** is ideally zero (no DC path from $V_{DD}$ to GND).
- [ ] Total load capacitance: $C_L = C_{gd,n} + C_{gd,p} + C_{db,n} + C_{db,p} + C_w + C_{g,next}$.
- [ ] Rise/fall time approximation: $t_r = t_f \approx 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$.
- [ ] **Short-circuit power** arises when both transistors conduct during the brief switching interval.
- [ ] To make PMOS match NMOS speed: size PMOS as $\approx 2$–$3\times$ wider than NMOS (since $\mu_n \approx 2$–$3 \times \mu_p$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Power Dissipation (Detailed Analysis Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Power Dissipation (Detailed Analysis Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Power Dissipation in CMOS VLSI Circuits

In any CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) integrated circuit, the **total power dissipation** ($P_{total}$) consumed by the chip is the sum of two fundamentally different physical phenomena:

$$P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> - **Static Power Dissipation** is the power consumed by a CMOS circuit when it is in a *steady (quiescent) state* — i.e., when the circuit is NOT switching and the inputs are held constant. Even when "idle," a real transistor leaks small currents from $V_{DD}$ to ground.
> - **Dynamic Power Dissipation** is the power consumed by a CMOS circuit *during switching activity* — i.e., when the output nodes transition between logic 0 and logic 1, charging and discharging internal and load capacitances.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Tap System:**
> Think of a CMOS chip as a network of water taps and pipes:
> - **Static Power** is like a *dripping tap* — even when the tap is fully closed, a small amount of water (current) leaks through the worn rubber seal. You pay for that wasted water 24/7, regardless of whether anyone is using the sink.
> - **Dynamic Power** is like the *actual flow* when you open the tap to fill a bucket (charging a capacitor). Every time you open or close the tap, you spend energy filling/emptying the bucket. The faster and more often you open/close it, and the bigger the bucket, the more water (and money) you consume.
> - In modern nanometer chips, the *dripping tap* (static leakage) is no longer negligible — in some 28nm and below designs, **static power can equal or even exceed dynamic power**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **$V_{DD}$** — Supply Voltage (in Volts, **V**)
- **$f_{clk}$** — Clock Frequency (in Hertz, **Hz**)
- **$C_L$** — Load Capacitance (in Farads, **F**)
- **$\alpha$** — Switching Activity Factor (dimensionless, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$)
- **$I_{leak}$** — Leakage Current (in Amperes, **A**)
- **$k$** — Boltzmann's Constant = **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 syllabus directive, **detailed analytical derivations are not required** for this sub-topic. Students must, however, be fully conversant with the **sources**, **formulas**, and **qualitative trends** of both static and dynamic power components.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Power Dissipation — Sources & Mechanism

Static power arises primarily from **leakage currents** that flow from $V_{DD}$ to GND even when the transistor is "off." The major leakage mechanisms are:

### (a) Subthreshold Leakage ($I_{sub}$)
- Current flowing between source and drain of an **OFF** MOSFET when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$.
- Dominates static power in modern deep-submicron CMOS (especially at high temperature).
- Depends **exponentially** on threshold voltage $V_{th}$.

### (b) Gate Oxide Tunneling Leakage ($I_{gate}$)
- Quantum-mechanical tunneling of electrons through the thin gate oxide (especially when $t_{ox} < 2$ nm).
- Becomes significant in nanometer technologies (45 nm and below).

### (c) Reverse-Biased Junction Leakage ($I_{rev}$)
- Leakage from source/drain to body (substrate) pn-junctions that are reverse-biased.
- Generally a minor contributor at room temperature.

### Core Static Power Equation:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \times I_{leakage}$$

where $I_{leakage} = I_{sub} + I_{gate} + I_{rev}$.

## 2.2 Dynamic Power Dissipation — Sources & Mechanism

Dynamic power has **two sub-components**:

### (a) Switching (Capacitive) Power — $P_{switching}$
Power consumed to **charge and discharge** the load capacitances during logic transitions.
- Energy to charge a capacitor from 0 to $V_{DD}$: $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$
- Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging: $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$
- Same energy dissipated in NMOS during discharge: $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$
- **Total per transition cycle:** $C_L V_{DD}^2$

### (b) Short-Circuit Power — $P_{SC}$
Power dissipated due to a **direct current path** from $V_{DD}$ to GND that momentarily exists during the brief interval when both the PMOS and NMOS of a CMOS gate are simultaneously ON during an input transition.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Power | $P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic}$ | W (Watts) | Always non-negative |
| 2 | Static Power | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | W | Dominant in nanometer CMOS |
| 3 | Switching Power | $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | W | Dominant in older technologies |
| 4 | Short-Circuit Power | $P_{SC} = I_{SC(avg)} \cdot V_{DD}$ | W | Negligible for slow input ramps |
| 5 | Energy per Transition | $E_{trans} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | J | Half stored, half dissipated |
| 6 | Power-Delay Product | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | J | Figure of merit |
| 7 | Activity Factor Bound | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ | dimensionless | $\alpha = 0.5$ for random data |
| 8 | Leakage Trend | $I_{leak} \propto e^{-V_{th}/(kT/q)}$ | A | Exponential with $V_{th}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Power dissipation directly determines:
> - **Battery life** in portable/IoT devices (smartphones, wearables, biomedical implants).
> - **Packaging & cooling cost** in high-performance processors (e.g., server CPUs, GPUs).
> - **Reliability** — every 10 °C rise in junction temperature roughly halves device lifetime.
> - Governs design choices like *power gating*, *clock gating*, *multi-$V_{th}$* libraries, and *dynamic voltage-frequency scaling (DVFS)*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 directive *"Detailed Analysis Not Required,"* the derivations below are **concise but complete**, focusing on the standard textbook energy-balance method.

## 3.1 Derivation of Switching Power ($P_{sw}$)

**Step 1 — Energy to Charge a Capacitor:**
When the output node of a CMOS gate switches from 0 → 1, the PMOS transistor connects the load capacitance $C_L$ to $V_{DD}$. The energy drawn from the supply is:

$$E_{supply} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 2 — Energy Stored vs. Energy Dissipated:**
Of this drawn energy, exactly $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is **stored** in the electric field of the capacitor, and $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is **dissipated as heat** in the ON-resistance of the PMOS transistor.

**Step 3 — Discharge Phase:**
During the 1 → 0 transition, the stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is fully dissipated in the NMOS transistor (no energy returned to the supply). Thus the **total energy dissipated per full switching cycle** (0→1 then 1→0) is:

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4 — Multiplying by Switching Frequency and Activity:**
If the gate switches at clock frequency $f$ but only a fraction $\alpha$ of the cycles result in an actual logic transition, the **average switching power** is:

$$P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Static Power ($P_{static}$)

In the steady state, with inputs held constant, the CMOS gate ideally draws **no** current from $V_{DD}$ to GND (no conducting path). However, real transistors exhibit non-ideal leakage:

$$I_{leak} = I_{sub} + I_{gate} + I_{rev}$$

The static power dissipated is simply Ohm's-law-like multiplication:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$

This is a **direct current** phenomenon and is therefore **independent of clock frequency** — a key distinction that examiners frequently test.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Static Power ($P_{static}$) | Dynamic Power ($P_{sw}$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Always present (DC leakage) | Only during switching transitions |
| **Frequency Dependence** | None (frequency-independent) | Linear in $f$ |
| **Voltage Dependence** | Linear in $V_{DD}$ | **Quadratic** in $V_{DD}$ |
| **Dominant Regime** | Nanometer (≤ 65 nm) | Older (≥ 180 nm) |
| **Primary Source** | Subthreshold leakage | Capacitive charging/discharging |
| **Reduction Techniques** | Power gating, high-$V_{th}$, sleep transistors | Clock gating, $V_{DD}$ scaling, capacitance reduction |
| **Temperature Behaviour** | Increases exponentially with $T$ | Weakly temperature-dependent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** Reducing $V_{DD}$ is the **most effective** lever to cut dynamic power (quadratic gain), but it cannot be reduced indefinitely without hurting performance (since delay $\tau \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_{th})^2$). This trade-off is the central motivation behind the classic $V_{DD} = 0.7V_{th}$ design rule.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Dissipation Classification (Mermaid Hierarchy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Total Power P_total"]
    nodeB["Static Power P_static"]
    nodeC["Dynamic Power P_dynamic"]
    nodeD["Subthreshold Leakage I_sub"]
    nodeE["Gate Oxide Tunneling I_gate"]
    nodeF["Junction Leakage I_rev"]
    nodeG["Switching Power P_sw"]
    nodeH["Short-Circuit Power P_SC"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
```

## 4.2 CMOS Inverter Power Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["V_DD Supply"]
    nodeB["PMOS Transistor"]
    nodeC["Output Node"]
    nodeD["NMOS Transistor"]
    nodeE["Ground GND"]
    nodeF["Load Capacitance C_L"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeC --- nodeF
    nodeG["Switching Power Path"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeG -.-> nodeF
    nodeG -.-> nodeD
    nodeH["Leakage Path OFF state"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeH -.-> nodeD
```

## 4.3 Energy Flow Per Switching Cycle (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Initial State C_L uncharged V_out = 0"]
    nodeB["Input transitions LOW to HIGH"]
    nodeC["PMOS turns ON, charges C_L"]
    nodeD["Energy drawn from V_DD equals 0.5 C_L V_DD squared"]
    nodeE["Half stored in C_L, Half dissipated in PMOS"]
    nodeF["Input transitions HIGH to LOW"]
    nodeG["NMOS turns ON, discharges C_L"]
    nodeH["Stored energy 0.5 C_L V_DD squared dissipated in NMOS"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram decomposes the total power into the two KTU-mandated branches. The second diagram locates where in a CMOS inverter each component physically arises. The third diagram traces the energy flow during one full 0→1→0 cycle, making the "half stored, half dissipated" principle visually explicit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**List any three sources of static power dissipation in a CMOS inverter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Subthreshold leakage current ($I_{sub}$)** — flow of carriers between source and drain of an OFF transistor due to weak inversion. *(1 mark)*
2. **Gate oxide tunneling current ($I_{gate}$)** — quantum-mechanical tunneling of electrons through the very thin gate dielectric in nanometer-scale processes. *(1 mark)*
3. **Reverse-biased pn-junction leakage current ($I_{rev}$)** — minority-carrier drift/diffusion current across the source/drain-to-substrate junctions. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Why does dynamic power dissipation in CMOS depend on the square of the supply voltage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Dynamic (switching) power is given by $P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. *(1 mark)*
- The energy required to charge the load capacitance $C_L$ to voltage $V_{DD}$ is $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$. The energy to discharge (dissipated in the NMOS) is also $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$, giving $E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2$. *(1 mark)*
- Hence the $V_{DD}^2$ dependence: energy per cycle is quadratic in $V_{DD}$, and multiplying by switching rate $f$ gives power. Therefore, **reducing $V_{DD}$ is the most effective single technique to reduce dynamic power**. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the different sources of leakage current that contribute to static power dissipation in deep-submicron CMOS circuits. Why does static power dominate in modern technologies?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of Static Power (1 mark):**
Static power is the power consumed by a CMOS circuit in the steady (non-switching) state due to non-ideal leakage currents. It is given by:
$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \times I_{leak}$$

**2. Major Sources of Leakage (4 marks):**

- **(i) Subthreshold Leakage ($I_{sub}$):** When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, the MOSFET is nominally OFF, but a small current still flows in weak inversion. It is modeled as:
$$I_{sub} = I_0 \, e^{(V_{GS} - V_{th})/(\eta V_T)} \left(1 - e^{-V_{DS}/V_T}\right)$$
With $V_{DS} \gg V_T$, the term in parentheses $\to 1$. $I_{sub}$ grows **exponentially** as $V_{th}$ is reduced.

- **(ii) Gate Oxide Tunneling ($I_{gate}$):** When $t_{ox} < 2$ nm, electrons tunnel directly through the gate oxide (Fowler-Nordheim / direct tunneling). Becomes severe below the 45 nm node.

- **(iii) Reverse-Biased Junction Leakage ($I_{rev}$):** At the source/drain-to-substrate pn-junctions, reverse saturation current flows due to thermally generated minority carriers.

- **(iv) Other contributors:** Punchthrough (in short-channel devices), hot-carrier injection, gate-induced drain leakage (GIDL).

**3. Why Static Power Dominates Today (2 marks):**
- Aggressive $V_{th}$ scaling to maintain drive current at low $V_{DD}$ causes $I_{sub}$ to rise exponentially.
- Thinner gate oxides aggravate $I_{gate}$.
- Higher transistor density and operating temperatures further multiply leakage.
- Result: in 28 nm and below, $P_{static}$ can equal or exceed $P_{dynamic}$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the switching power dissipation of a CMOS inverter. A chip drives a total load capacitance of 200 pF at a clock frequency of 100 MHz with an activity factor of 0.2 and $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V. Compute the switching power.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
1. Energy drawn from $V_{DD}$ while charging $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$:
$$E_{charge} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$
2. Of this, $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is stored in $C_L$, and $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated as heat in the PMOS resistance.
3. During discharge (1→0), the stored $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is fully dissipated in the NMOS — no energy is returned to the supply.
4. **Total energy per full 0→1→0 cycle:**
$$E_{cycle} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$
5. Multiplying by activity factor $\alpha$ and switching frequency $f$:
$$P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**
- $C_L = 200 \text{ pF} = 200 \times 10^{-12}$ F
- $f = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $\alpha = 0.2$
- $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V

$$P_{sw} = 0.2 \times (200 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1.8)^2 \times (100 \times 10^6)$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:
$$= 0.2 \times 200 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.24 \times 100 \times 10^6$$

$$= 0.2 \times 200 \times 3.24 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 0.2 \times 64{,}800 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 12{,}960 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = 12.96 \text{ mW}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**What is short-circuit power dissipation in CMOS? Under what input conditions does it become significant, and how is it minimized?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
During a logic transition, there is a brief interval when both the PMOS and NMOS transistors in a CMOS gate are simultaneously ON. This creates a **direct conducting path from $V_{DD}$ to GND**, resulting in a shoot-through current $I_{SC}$ and a corresponding **short-circuit power** $P_{SC} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{SC(avg)}$.

**2. When It Is Significant (3 marks):**
- **Fast input rise/fall times** relative to the gate's output transition — keeps both transistors ON longer.
- **Large load capacitance** that delays the output transition, prolonging the overlap.
- **Skewed input transitions** (e.g., slow ramps): extend the duration during which $V_{in}$ lies in the region $V_{th,n} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{th,p} \vert$.
- Negligible for ideal step inputs with very small output loading.

**3. Minimization Techniques (2 marks):**
- Use **step (abrupt) input waveforms** with rise/fall times $\ll$ output transition time.
- **Buffer the input** to sharpen the slope before driving large gates.
- **Lower $V_{DD}$** so the overlap region $V_{th,n} \to V_{DD} - \vert V_{th,p} \vert$ becomes narrower.
- Size transistors so that $t_r, t_f \ll t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}$ of the gate.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare static and dynamic power dissipation in CMOS with respect to: (i) dependence on frequency, (ii) dependence on supply voltage, (iii) dominant technology regime, and (iv) typical reduction techniques. Mention the total power expression for a CMOS inverter.**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Static Power | Dynamic Power |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Frequency Dependence** | Independent of $f$ (DC leakage) | Linear in $f$: $P_{sw} \propto f$ |
| **(ii) Voltage Dependence** | Linear: $P_{static} \propto V_{DD}$ | Quadratic: $P_{sw} \propto V_{DD}^2$ |
| **(iii) Dominant Regime** | $\le 65$ nm CMOS (nanometer) | $\ge 180$ nm CMOS (older nodes) |
| **(iv) Reduction Techniques** | Power gating, multi-$V_{th}$, reverse body-bias, sleep transistors | Clock gating, $V_{DD}$ scaling, DVFS, capacitance minimization, adiabatic logic |

**Marks distribution:** *(1.5 marks per row of the comparison table above)*

**Total Power Expression for a CMOS Inverter (1 mark):**
$$P_{total} = \underbrace{V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}}_{P_{static}} + \underbrace{\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f}_{P_{sw}} + \underbrace{V_{DD} \cdot I_{SC(avg)}}_{P_{SC}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing units:** Forgetting to convert MHz → Hz and pF → F leads to numerical answers off by a factor of $10^{18}$ — an instant **zero** for the numerical part.
> 2. **Forgetting the activity factor $\alpha$:** Many students write $P_{sw} = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ directly. If the problem specifies $\alpha = 0.2$ or "every 5th cycle switches," the activity factor MUST be included.
> 3. **Mixing up static vs. dynamic triggers:** Static power exists even when the clock is stopped — do NOT argue that "static power is zero if frequency is zero."
> 4. **Omitting units in the final answer:** Always write "$P_{sw} = 12.96$ mW", not just "12.96".
> 5. **Quadratic vs. linear confusion:** Marks are routinely lost when students claim static power scales as $V_{DD}^2$ — it is *linear* in $V_{DD}$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Total Power Equation:** $P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic}$ — always begin with this.
- **Static Power Formula:** $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — **frequency-independent**, **linear in $V_{DD}$**.
- **Dynamic (Switching) Power Formula:** $P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — **linear in $f$**, **quadratic in $V_{DD}$**.
- **Short-Circuit Power:** $P_{SC} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{SC(avg)}$ — depends on input ramp and output load.
- **Energy per Switching Cycle:** $C_L V_{DD}^2$ (half stored, half dissipated each half-cycle).
- **Three Sources of Leakage:** Subthreshold ($I_{sub}$), Gate-oxide tunneling ($I_{gate}$), Junction leakage ($I_{rev}$).
- **Why Static Power Dominates Now:** Aggressive $V_{th}$ scaling → exponential rise in $I_{sub}$.
- **Best Knob for Dynamic Power:** Reduce $V_{DD}$ (quadratic benefit).
- **Best Knob for Static Power:** Increase $V_{th}$ or use *power gating*.
- **Key Constants to Memorize:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; thermal voltage $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K.
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** Combined figure-of-merit for energy-efficient design.
- **Common KTU Traps:** Don't forget $\alpha$ in $P_{sw}$; don't claim static power vanishes at $f=0$; convert all units explicitly in numericals.
- **Syllabus Directive:** *"Detailed Analysis Not Required"* — focus on **sources, formulas, qualitative trends, and standard numericals** rather than exotic device-physics derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propagation Delays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propagation Delays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Propagation Delay

In CMOS VLSI Circuit Design under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Propagation Delay** is formally defined as the time interval required for a signal transition at the input of a logic gate (or any digital circuit element) to produce a corresponding **50% transition** at the output node. It is a fundamental figure of merit that directly dictates the maximum operating frequency of any digital VLSI system.

Mathematically, propagation delay is quantified using two distinct parameters based on the direction of the output transition:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition:**
> - **$t_{pHL}$ (High-to-Low Propagation Delay):** The time taken for the output to fall from $V_{OH}$ to the **50% point** ($V_{OH} - 0.5 \cdot \Delta V$, where $\Delta V = V_{OH} - V_{OL}$) in response to a rising input edge.
> - **$t_{pLH}$ (Low-to-High Propagation Delay):** The time taken for the output to rise from $V_{OL}$ to the **50% point** in response to a falling input edge.
> - **Average Propagation Delay:** $t_p = \dfrac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Propagation Delay is intrinsically tied to the *parasitic RC network* formed by the on-resistance of the CMOS transistors and the load capacitance at the output node. Students must remember that VLSI delay is NOT a constant — it is a function of input slew rate, output load, transistor sizing, and process technology node.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **watering a long, thin garden hose** connected to a tap.

- The **tap (input voltage)** is either fully open (logic 1) or fully closed (logic 0).
- The **water pressure at the other end (output voltage)** does not change instantly — it builds up or bleeds off gradually because the hose has **friction (resistance, R)** and the hose wall can **stretch/store water (capacitance, C)**.
- The time it takes for the water pressure at the far end to reach the halfway mark is the **propagation delay**.
- If the hose is **longer (more wire capacitance)**, the delay increases.
- If the tap has a **wider opening (larger MOSFET — more current drive)**, the delay decreases.
- If the hose is **thicker (lower resistance)**, the delay decreases.

This is precisely the **RC delay model** in VLSI: the transistor's ON-resistance $R$ charges/discharges the load capacitance $C$, and the time taken is the propagation delay.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the **standard KTU textbook constants and metrics** associated with propagation delay:

- **Gate Oxide Capacitance per unit area:** $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Relative permittivity of SiO₂:** $\varepsilon_{r,ox} = 3.9$
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Silicon:** $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$
- **Electron mobility:** $\mu_n \approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ (for doped silicon)
- **Hole mobility:** $\mu_p \approx 480 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$
- **Standard Delay Unit (SDU):** Often normalized as the delay of a minimum-sized **inverter (1x INV)** driving another identical 1x inverter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** In modern sub-22nm CMOS technologies, **wire delay** (interconnect delay) often dominates over **gate delay**. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the transition from a *gate-centric* delay view to an *interconnect-aware* delay view.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Delay Formation

The following structured analysis explains the complete physical and mathematical mechanism by which a CMOS gate produces a propagation delay:

### Step 1: Input Transition Event
- The input voltage $V_{in}$ begins to transition from one logic level to the other.
- The gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ of either the NMOS or PMOS transistor begins to exceed the threshold voltage $V_T$.

### Step 2: Channel Formation and Current Flow
- Once $V_{GS} > V_T$, an inversion layer (channel) forms beneath the oxide.
- A drain current $I_D$ begins to flow through the transistor.
- The transistor now exhibits a small-signal **ON-resistance** $R_{on}$.

### Step 3: RC Charging/Discharging of Load Capacitance
- The current $I_D$ charges (or discharges) the total **load capacitance** $C_L$ at the output node.
- This load capacitance includes:
  - **Gate capacitance** of the fan-out gates ($C_g$).
  - **Diffusion (junction) capacitance** of the drain/source regions of the driving transistor ($C_{db}, C_{sb}$).
  - **Wire (interconnect) capacitance** ($C_w$).

### Step 4: Output Waveform Development
- The output voltage $V_{out}(t)$ follows an exponential RC charging/discharging curve.
- The 50% point of this transition defines the propagation delay.

### Step 5: Why is Delay NOT Instantaneous?
- **Capacitors oppose instantaneous voltage change** (fundamental property: $i = C \cdot dV/dt$).
- Hence, even a perfect voltage source cannot change the voltage across a capacitor in zero time.
- The **RC time constant** $\tau = R \cdot C$ fundamentally limits the speed.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all essential formulas** that the KTU examiner expects a VLSI student to reproduce from memory for the 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE).

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** |
|-------------|----------------|----------------------|-----------|
| RC Time Constant | $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_L$ | Fundamental time constant of charging/discharging | seconds (s) |
| Propagation Delay (50% rule) | $t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_L$ | Delay to reach 50% point of exponential RC step response | seconds (s) |
| NMOS ON-Resistance | $R_{on,n} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})}$ | Resistance of NMOS in linear region | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| PMOS ON-Resistance | $R_{on,p} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{Tp})}$ | Resistance of PMOS in linear region | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Effective Resistance Ratio | $r = \dfrac{R_{on,p}}{R_{on,n}} = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2$ to $3$ | Since $\mu_p < \mu_n$, PMOS is "weaker" — hence sized wider | Dimensionless |
| Symmetric Inverter Sizing Ratio | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p = r \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | To make $t_{pHL} = t_{pLH}$ | Dimensionless |
| Total Load Capacitance | $C_L = C_{g,n} + C_{g,p} + C_{db,n} + C_{db,p} + C_w$ | Sum of all capacitive loading at output | Farads (F) |
| Gate Capacitance | $C_g = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ | Gate oxide capacitance of MOSFET | Farads (F) |
| Intrinsic Delay (Fan-out-of-1) | $t_{p0} = 0.69 \cdot R_{inv} \cdot C_{inv}$ | Delay of inverter with no external load | seconds (s) |
| Logical Effort Delay | $t_p = t_{p0} \cdot (g \cdot h + p)$ | General delay with logical effort $g$ and fan-out $h$ | seconds (s) |
| Parasitic Delay | $p = \dfrac{C_{par}}{C_{inv}}$ | Ratio of intrinsic parasitic cap to inverter input cap | Dimensionless |
| Fan-Out (Electrical Effort) | $h = \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | Ratio of load capacitance to input capacitance | Dimensionless |
| Elmore Delay (RC Ladder) | $t_D = \sum_{k=1}^{N} R_{kk} \cdot C_k$ | Delay of RC tree from source to node | seconds (s) |
| Wire Delay per Unit Length | $t_{wire} = 0.69 \cdot r_w \cdot c_w \cdot L^2$ | Quadratic delay growth with wire length | seconds (s) |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Propagation delay analysis is the **backbone of high-performance digital design**. The KTU 2024 syllabus highlights these real-world applications:

1. **Critical Path Identification in CPUs:** The longest chain of logic gates (worst propagation delay) determines the **clock period** of a processor. If $t_{p,critical} > T_{clock}$, the system fails.
2. **Static Timing Analysis (STA):** Modern chip design tools (Synopsys PrimeTime, Cadence Tempus) compute propagation delays across billions of gates to verify timing closure.
3. **Pipeline Stage Balancing:** VLSI architects balance delays across pipeline stages to maximize throughput.
4. **Interconnect Optimization in Physical Design:** Wire sizing, buffer insertion, and routing optimizations all aim to minimize propagation delay.
5. **Low-Power Design Trade-off:** Reducing $V_{DD}$ reduces dynamic power quadratically but **increases delay** super-linearly — a critical design trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental 0.69 RC Delay Factor

The **single most important derivation** in VLSI delay analysis is proving why the 50% propagation delay of an RC network equals $0.69 \cdot RC$.

### Given:
- A step input is applied at time $t = 0$ to a first-order RC low-pass network.
- The capacitor $C_L$ is initially uncharged (i.e., $V_C(0) = 0$).
- The total Thevenin resistance driving the capacitor is $R_{on}$.

### To Derive:
- The expression for the output voltage $V_{out}(t)$ as a function of time.
- The exact time at which $V_{out}(t) = 0.5 \cdot V_{DD}$ (the 50% propagation delay point).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Write the KVL/KCL equation for the RC circuit.**

The driving transistor behaves as a switch closing a resistor $R_{on}$ across the supply $V_{DD}$ to the load capacitor $C_L$. Applying KCL at the output node:

$$i_C(t) = C_L \cdot \dfrac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{out}(t)}{R_{on}}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange into a standard first-order linear ODE.**

$$C_L \cdot \dfrac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} + \dfrac{V_{out}(t)}{R_{on}} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{R_{on}}$$

Dividing both sides by $C_L$:

$$\dfrac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} + \dfrac{V_{out}(t)}{R_{on} \cdot C_L} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{R_{on} \cdot C_L}$$

**Step 3: Identify the time constant.**

Let $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_L$. The ODE becomes:

$$\dfrac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} + \dfrac{V_{out}(t)}{\tau} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{\tau}$$

**Step 4: Solve using the integrating factor method (or separation of variables).**

The general solution is the sum of the steady-state (particular) solution and the transient (homogeneous) solution:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{out,\infty} + (V_{out,0} - V_{out,\infty}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 5: Apply the initial and final conditions.**

- Initial condition: $V_{out}(0) = 0$ (capacitor uncharged).
- Final condition: $V_{out}(\infty) = V_{DD}$ (capacitor fully charged to supply).

Substituting:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} + (0 - V_{DD}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 6: Set the output to 50% of $V_{DD}$ and solve for $t = t_p$.**

$$0.5 \cdot V_{DD} = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_p/\tau}\right)$$

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_p/\tau}$$

$$e^{-t_p/\tau} = 0.5$$

**Step 7: Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$-\dfrac{t_p}{\tau} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln(2)$$

$$t_p = \tau \cdot \ln(2)$$

**Step 8: Compute the numerical value.**

Since $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931 \approx 0.69$:

$$\boxed{t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 0.69 Factor is Universal:** This derivation is fundamental to all CMOS delay analysis. Whether the load is a single gate, a long wire, or an RC tree, the 0.69 factor appears whenever computing the 50% delay of an exponential RC step response.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CMOS Inverter Propagation Delay with Parasitic Capacitances

The CMOS inverter is the canonical delay benchmark in VLSI. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to derive the delay of an inverter driving an identical load.

### Given:
- NMOS transistor with $(W/L)_n$, threshold $V_{Tn}$, mobility $\mu_n$.
- PMOS transistor with $(W/L)_p$, threshold $V_{Tp} = -|V_{Tp}|$, mobility $\mu_p$.
- Load capacitance $C_L$ at the output (next-stage gates + parasitics).
- Supply voltage $V_{DD}$.

### To Derive:
- The expression for $t_{pHL}$ (fall time delay, NMOS active).
- The expression for $t_{pLH}$ (rise time delay, PMOS active).
- The condition for symmetric delay.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Express the ON-resistance of the NMOS in the linear (triode) region.**

When the input transitions high ($V_{in} = V_{DD}$), the NMOS enters the linear region. Its effective resistance is:

$$R_{on,n} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})}$$

Assuming $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$ and using the average overdrive voltage approximation, the standard simplification used in KTU textbooks is:

$$R_{on,n} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$$

**Step 2: Apply the 0.69 RC delay formula for $t_{pHL}$.**

The NMOS discharges the load capacitor $C_L$ through its ON-resistance:

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{on,n} \cdot C_L$$

Substituting $R_{on,n}$:

$$t_{pHL} = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$$

**Step 3: Analogously derive $t_{pLH}$ using PMOS ON-resistance.**

When the input transitions low, the PMOS charges $C_L$:

$$R_{on,p} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|)}$$

$$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{on,p} \cdot C_L = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{\mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|)}$$

**Step 4: Derive the symmetric delay condition.**

For $t_{pHL} = t_{pLH}$, we set the two expressions equal:

$$\dfrac{1}{\mu_n \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_p \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n = r \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n}$$

where $r = \mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$ to $3$ is the mobility ratio.

**Step 5: Define the effective load capacitance $C_L$.**

For an inverter driving $f$ identical inverters (fan-out $f$), the total load capacitance is:

$$C_L = f \cdot C_{g,inv} + C_{d,intrinsic}$$

where $C_{g,inv}$ is the input gate capacitance of one inverter and $C_{d,intrinsic}$ represents the diffusion (parasitic drain) capacitance of the driving inverter itself.

**Step 6: Final form of the propagation delay.**

Substituting $C_L$ back, the average propagation delay becomes:

$$t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot (f \cdot C_{g,inv} + C_{d,intrinsic})$$

where $R_{eq}$ is the average ON-resistance of the symmetric inverter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** Notice that the delay has **two components** — an *intrinsic* part (independent of fan-out) and an *extrinsic* part (linearly proportional to fan-out). This is the foundation of the **Logical Effort methodology** covered in the next derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Logical Effort Delay Equation

Logical Effort is the KTU 2024 prescribed standard method for estimating and optimizing delay in CMOS logic paths. Here is its complete derivation.

### Step 1: Define the three efforts.**

- **Logical Effort ($g$):** Ratio of the input capacitance of a gate to the input capacitance of an inverter that delivers the same output current. It captures how much "extra capacitance" a gate requires to perform a more complex logic function.
- **Electrical Effort or Fan-Out ($h$):** $h = C_{out} / C_{in}$, the ratio of load capacitance to input capacitance of the gate.
- **Parasitic Delay ($p$):** The delay of the gate when it drives zero load, normalized to the delay of an inverter with no load.

### Step 2: Express the delay in terms of a reference inverter.**

The delay of any logic gate can be written as:

$$d = \dfrac{t_p}{t_{p0}}$$
