where $t_{p0}$ is the delay of a 1x inverter with no load.

### Step 3: Derive the universal delay equation.**

For a single stage, the delay has two components: a **stage effort** part (the work done to drive the load) and a **parasitic** part (the internal capacitance that must be charged):

$$d = g \cdot h + p$$

For a path of $N$ stages, each with logical effort $g_i$ and stage effort $f_i = g_i \cdot h_i$:

$$d = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (g_i \cdot h_i + p_i)$$

### Step 4: The path effort and optimal stage effort.**

The **path effort** is defined as:

$$F = G \cdot B \cdot H$$

where:
- $G = \prod g_i$ (product of all logical efforts).
- $B$ (Branching effort) = product of $\dfrac{C_{on-path} + C_{off-path}}{C_{on-path}}$ at each branch point.
- $H = C_{out,path} / C_{in,path}$ (total electrical effort of the path).

### Step 5: Minimize the total delay by equalizing stage efforts.**

To minimize total path delay, the stage efforts should be equal:

$$f_i = f = F^{1/N}$$

The minimum total path delay is then:

$$\boxed{d_{min} = N \cdot F^{1/N} + P}$$

where $P = \sum p_i$ is the total parasitic delay of the path.

**Step 6: Determine the optimum input capacitance of each stage.**

$$C_{in,i} = \dfrac{C_{out,i} \cdot g_i}{f} = \dfrac{C_{out,i} \cdot g_i}{F^{1/N}}$$

This tells the designer exactly how to size each gate to achieve minimum delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Application:** This formula is heavily tested. A typical KTU question asks: "Design a 3-stage NAND chain to drive a load of 32 unit inverters. Find the minimum delay." The solution is direct application of $f = F^{1/N}$ and the back-calculation of $C_{in,i}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Elmore Delay for an RC Ladder

The Elmore delay is the standard metric for delay in RC trees (multi-node interconnects). The KTU 2024 syllabus treats it as essential for wire delay estimation.

### Step 1: Define the RC tree.**

Consider a binary RC tree with $N$ nodes, each node $k$ having capacitance $C_k$ to ground, and the unique resistive path from the source to node $k$ having a total resistance $R_{kk}$ (sum of branch resistances).

### Step 2: Write the transfer function.**

The transfer function from source to node $j$ in an RC tree is:

$$H_j(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + s \cdot \sum_k R_{jk} C_k}$$

The dominant pole approximation gives the time constant.

### Step 3: Define the Elmore delay.**

The Elmore delay from source to node $j$ is the first moment of the impulse response:

$$\boxed{t_{D,j} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} R_{jk} \cdot C_k}$$

This is a simple weighted sum: for each capacitor $C_k$, multiply it by the **common** resistance from source through node $j$ to node $k$.

### Step 4: Apply to a uniform RC ladder (distributed wire model).**

For a uniform wire of length $L$, resistance per unit length $r_w$, capacitance per unit length $c_w$, modeled as $N$ lumped RC segments:

$$t_{wire} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left(k \cdot \dfrac{r_w L}{N}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{c_w L}{N}\right)$$

$$t_{wire} = \dfrac{r_w c_w L^2}{N^2} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{N} k = \dfrac{r_w c_w L^2}{N^2} \cdot \dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}$$

As $N \to \infty$ (distributed model):

$$t_{wire} \approx \dfrac{r_w c_w L^2}{2}$$

Including the 0.69 factor for the 50% point:

$$\boxed{t_{wire,50\%} \approx 0.69 \cdot \dfrac{r_w c_w L^2}{2} = 0.38 \cdot r_w c_w L^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Wire delay grows **quadratically with length**. Doubling the wire length quadruples the delay. This is why long global interconnects in modern VLSI chips are broken up with **repeaters (buffers)** to reduce effective length.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Delay Type** | **Order of Fan-Out Dependence** | **Order of Length Dependence** | **Dominates In** |
|----------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------|
| Single Gate Delay | Linear ($t_p \propto h$) | N/A | Small local circuits |
| Multi-Stage Logical Effort | $t_p \propto N \cdot F^{1/N}$ | N/A | Arithmetic logic units |
| Local Wire Delay | Linear | **Quadratic** ($L^2$) | Short interconnects |
| Repeater-Inserted Wire | Linear with length (with optimal $k$ repeats) | Approximately Linear | Global interconnects |
| Distributed RC (Elmore) | N/A | Quadratic | Long on-chip wires |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> The following diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node identifiers** (e.g., `node1`, `blockA`, `step1`) and **clean uppercase labels** without any markdown formatting inside the labels. This ensures 100% Mermaid compatibility with the KTU examination rendering environment.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: CMOS Inverter Delay Formation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT VOLTAGE TRANSITION] --> B[GATE OXIDE CAPACITANCE CHARGES]
    B --> C[VGS EXCEEDS THRESHOLD VT]
    C --> D[CHANNEL INVERSION LAYER FORMS]
    D --> E[DRAIN CURRENT ID FLOWS]
    E --> F[ON RESISTANCE RON ACTIVE]
    F --> G[LOAD CAPACITANCE CL CHARGES OR DISCHARGES]
    G --> H[OUTPUT VOLTAGE EXPONENTIAL TRANSITION]
    H --> I[50 PERCENT POINT DETECTED]
    I --> J[PROPAGATION DELAY TP MEASURED]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This linear block diagram maps the **causal chain of events** that convert an input voltage edge into a measurable propagation delay. Each block represents a distinct physical phenomenon occurring in sequence inside the CMOS inverter.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Components of Load Capacitance at Output Node

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[OUTPUT NODE OF CMOS GATE] --> capA[GATE CAPACITANCE OF NEXT STAGE NMOS]
    node1 --> capB[GATE CAPACITANCE OF NEXT STAGE PMOS]
    node1 --> capC[DIFFUSION JUNCTION CAPACITANCE NMOS DRAIN]
    node1 --> capD[DIFFUSION JUNCTION CAPACITANCE PMOS DRAIN]
    node1 --> capE[WIRE INTERCONNECT CAPACITANCE]
    node1 --> capF[INPUT CAPACITANCE OF FANOUT BRANCHES]

    capA --> sumbox[TOTAL LOAD CAPACITANCE CL]
    capB --> sumbox
    capC --> sumbox
    capD --> sumbox
    capE --> sumbox
    capF --> sumbox

    sumbox --> result[DELAY TP EQUALS 0.69 TIMES RON TIMES CL]

    style node1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style sumbox fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style result fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram decomposes the **total load capacitance** $C_L$ into its six constituent components. KTU examiners often ask students to identify and quantify each term.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Logical Effort Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[START: GIVEN LOGIC PATH] --> step1[COMPUTE LOGICAL EFFORT G FOR EACH STAGE]
    step1 --> step2[COMPUTE ELECTRICAL EFFORT H FROM COUT TO CIN]
    step2 --> step3[COMPUTE BRANCHING EFFORT B AT EACH BRANCH NODE]
    step3 --> step4[COMPUTE PATH EFFORT F EQUALS G TIMES B TIMES H]
    step4 --> step5[COMPUTE NUMBER OF STAGES N]
    step5 --> step6[CALCULATE OPTIMAL STAGE EFFORT F POWER 1 BY N]
    step6 --> step7[BACK CALCULATE INPUT CAPACITANCE OF EACH STAGE]
    step7 --> step8[SIZE EACH TRANSISTOR ACCORDINGLY]
    step8 --> result[MINIMUM DELAY ACHIEVED DMIN EQUALS N TIMES F POWER 1 BY N PLUS P]

    style start1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style result fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart encodes the **complete Logical Effort design procedure** in the exact order that the KTU 2024 examination expects students to follow when solving a 14-mark delay optimization problem.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Wire Delay vs Repeater Insertion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[LONG WIRE WITHOUT REPEATER] --> blockB[QUADRATIC DELAY GROWTH WITH LENGTH]
    blockA --> blockC[HIGH PROPAGATION DELAY]
    blockA --> blockD[RC LADDER ELMORE DELAY APPLIES]

    blockE[LONG WIRE WITH K REPEATERS] --> blockF[DELAY GROWS LINEARLY WITH LENGTH]
    blockE --> blockG[MINIMUM DELAY AT OPTIMAL K VALUE]
    blockE --> blockH[OPTIMAL K EQUALS SQRT RON C WIRE BY REPEATER CAP]
    blockE --> blockI[REPEATER SIZES ALSO OPTIMIZED]

    style blockA fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD

    style blockE fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    blockF --> blockG
    blockG --> blockH
    blockH --> blockI
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This comparative block diagram illustrates the **two design strategies** for long interconnect delay: (1) raw distributed RC wire with quadratic delay growth, and (2) repeater-inserted wire with linear delay growth. This is a frequent KTU Part B topic.

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Timeline: Delay from Input Edge to Output 50% Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tA[TIME T EQUALS 0: INPUT STARTS TRANSITION] --> tB[TIME T EQUALS T1: NMOS OR PMOS TURNS ON]
    tB --> tC[TIME T EQUALS T2: CHANNEL CURRENT BEGINS TO FLOW]
    tC --> tD[TIME T EQUALS T3: CAPACITOR CHARGING STARTS]
    tD --> tE[TIME T EQUALS T4: OUTPUT REACHES 50 PERCENT OF VDD]
    tE --> tF[PROPAGATION DELAY TP EQUALS T4 MINUS T1]

    style tA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style tE fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style tF fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** A temporal flow showing the **precise instants** that define the propagation delay measurement convention. The KTU 2024 rubric strictly defines $t_p$ as the time from the **50% input transition** to the **50% output transition**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define propagation delay $t_{pHL}$ and $t_{pLH}$ in a CMOS inverter. Why is the average propagation delay commonly used as a performance metric?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**$t_{pHL}$ (High-to-Low Delay):** The time interval between the **50% transition point of the rising input** and the **50% transition point of the falling output** of a CMOS inverter. Physically, it represents the time for the NMOS transistor to discharge the load capacitance $C_L$ from $V_{OH}$ to $(V_{OH}+V_{OL})/2$.

**$t_{pLH}$ (Low-to-High Delay):** The time interval between the **50% transition point of the falling input** and the **50% transition point of the rising output**. Physically, it represents the time for the PMOS transistor to charge $C_L$ from $V_{OL}$ to the halfway point.

**Why Average is Used:** Because in a symmetric CMOS inverter, $t_{pHL}$ and $t_{pLH}$ are not necessarily equal (due to the lower mobility of holes making PMOS weaker), the **average propagation delay** $t_p = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH})/2$ provides a single, technology-independent performance figure of merit that represents the inverter's true symmetric switching speed.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the factor 0.69 in the CMOS propagation delay formula $t_p = 0.69 R_{on} C_L$? Derive it briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The factor **0.69** arises mathematically from evaluating the time at which an **exponential RC charging response** reaches **50% of its final value**.

**Derivation:** For an RC step response:
$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Setting $V_{out}(t_p) = 0.5 V_{DD}$:
$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_p/\tau} \implies e^{-t_p/\tau} = 0.5 \implies t_p = \tau \ln(2) = 0.693\tau$$

Since $\tau = R_{on} C_L$:
$$t_p = 0.69 R_{on} C_L$$

**Significance:** The 0.69 factor is the universal conversion constant between an RC time constant and the 50%-of-final-value delay metric. It applies to any first-order RC network, making it the standard for all CMOS delay comparisons.

---

## PART B: LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]
**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With the help of a suitable circuit diagram, derive the propagation delay of a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. Clearly show the role of the ON-resistance of the NMOS and PMOS transistors. Also state the condition for equal rise and fall delays.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A symmetric CMOS inverter in a 65nm technology has the following parameters:
- $(W/L)_n = 1$, $(W/L)_p = 2.5$
- $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $V_{Tn} = |V_{Tp}| = 0.4$ V
- $\mu_n C_{ox} = 270 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $\mu_p C_{ox} = 108 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$
- $C_L = 50$ fF (including all parasitics and fan-out)

Compute $t_{pHL}$, $t_{pLH}$, and the average propagation delay $t_p$.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Circuit Setup (1 mark).**
Consider a CMOS inverter with input $V_{in}$, output $V_{out}$, supply $V_{DD}$. The output node has total load capacitance $C_L$ to ground. For rising input (high-to-low output transition), the NMOS is ON. For falling input (low-to-high output), the PMOS is ON.

**Step 2: ON-resistance expression (2 marks).**
When the input is at $V_{DD}$, the NMOS operates in the **linear (triode) region**. The effective channel resistance is:
$$R_{on,n} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$$

Similarly, for PMOS charging:
$$R_{on,p} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|)}$$

**Step 3: Apply 0.69 RC formula (2 marks).**
$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{on,n} \cdot C_L = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$$

$$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{on,p} \cdot C_L = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{\mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|)}$$

**Step 4: Symmetric Delay Condition (2 marks).**
Setting $t_{pHL} = t_{pLH}$ and simplifying:
$$\boxed{\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n \approx 2.5 \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n}$$

This means the PMOS must be sized ~2.5× wider than the NMOS to compensate for the lower hole mobility.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Compute $R_{on,n}$ (1 mark).**
$$R_{on,n} = \dfrac{1}{270 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1 \cdot (1.2 - 0.4)} = \dfrac{1}{270 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.8} = \dfrac{1}{216 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$R_{on,n} \approx 4.63 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Compute $R_{on,p}$ (1 mark).**
$$R_{on,p} = \dfrac{1}{108 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2.5 \cdot (1.2 - 0.4)} = \dfrac{1}{108 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2.5 \cdot 0.8} = \dfrac{1}{216 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$R_{on,p} \approx 4.63 \text{ k}\Omega$$

(Note: The sizing ratio of 2.5 perfectly compensates for the mobility ratio, giving equal resistances as expected for a symmetric inverter.)

**Step 3: Compute $t_{pHL}$ (1 mark).**
$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{on,n} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 4.63 \times 10^3 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15}$$
$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot 231.5 \times 10^{-12} \approx 159.7 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 4: Compute $t_{pLH}$ (1 mark).**
$$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{on,p} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 4.63 \times 10^3 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15}$$
$$t_{pLH} \approx 159.7 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 5: Compute Average Delay (1 mark).**
$$\boxed{t_p = \dfrac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2} = \dfrac{159.7 + 159.7}{2} \approx 159.7 \text{ ps}}$$

**Step 6: Verification and Engineering Insight (2 marks).**
- The average delay of **~160 ps** is typical for a 65nm CMOS inverter driving 50 fF.
- The result confirms that proper sizing achieves **symmetric switching**.
- In high-speed designs, this delay is acceptable for local logic but would require **buffer insertion** if the load were 10× larger.

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the **Logical Effort** methodology for CMOS delay estimation. Define logical effort, electrical effort, and parasitic delay. Derive the expression $d = gh + p$.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A 3-stage CMOS logic path consists of an inverter, a 2-input NAND, and a 3-input NAND, driving a load capacitance of 64 unit inverters. The branching efforts at the two inter-stage nodes are 1.5 and 1.2 respectively. The unit inverter has input capacitance $C_{inv} = 3$ fF. Use the logical effort method to find:
- (i) The total path effort $F$.
- (ii) The optimal stage effort $\hat{f}$.
- (iii) The input capacitance of each stage.
- (iv) The minimum path delay $d_{min}$ (assume $P = 4$).

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition of Logical Effort (2 marks):**
**Logical Effort ($g$)** of a logic gate is defined as the ratio of its input capacitance to the input capacitance of an **inverter** that can deliver the same output current. It captures the *extra capacitance* a gate needs to perform a more complex Boolean function. For a 2-input NAND, $g = 4/3$; for a 2-input NOR, $g = 5/3$; for an inverter, $g = 1$.

**Definition of Electrical Effort / Fan-Out (2 marks):**
**Electrical Effort ($h$)** is defined as $h = C_{out}/C_{in}$ — the ratio of the load capacitance (driven by the gate) to the input capacitance of the gate. It represents the *electrical* difficulty of the load.

**Definition of Parasitic Delay (1 mark):**
**Parasitic Delay ($p$)** is the delay of a gate when driving **zero external load**, arising from its own internal diffusion capacitances. For an inverter, $p = 1$; for a 2-input NAND, $p = 2$.

**Derivation of $d = gh + p$ (2 marks):**
The delay of a stage normalized to a no-load inverter is the sum of:
- A *stage effort* part: the work done charging the load through the gate, proportional to $g \cdot h$.
- A *parasitic* part: the delay due to internal capacitance, equal to $p$.

Thus:
$$\boxed{d = g \cdot h + p}$$

For a path of $N$ stages:
$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (g_i h_i + p_i) = N \cdot F^{1/N} + P$$

where $F = GBH$ is the path effort, $G = \prod g_i$, $B = \prod b_i$ (branching efforts), and $H = C_{out,path}/C_{in,path}$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Identify Logical Efforts (1 mark).**
- Inverter: $g_1 = 1$
- 2-input NAND: $g_2 = 4/3$
- 3-input NAND: $g_3 = 5/3$

**Step 2: Compute Path Logical Effort $G$ (1 mark).**
$$G = g_1 \cdot g_2 \cdot g_3 = 1 \cdot \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \dfrac{5}{3} = \dfrac{20}{9} \approx 2.222$$

**Step 3: Compute Branching Effort $B$ and Electrical Effort $H$ (1 mark).**
$$B = 1.5 \cdot 1.2 = 1.8$$
$$H = \dfrac{C_{out,path}}{C_{in,path}} = \dfrac{64 \cdot C_{inv}}{C_{inv}} = 64$$

**Step 4: Compute Path Effort $F$ (1 mark).**
$$\boxed{F = G \cdot B \cdot H = \dfrac{20}{9} \cdot 1.8 \cdot 64 = 2.222 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 64 = 256}$$

**Step 5: Compute Optimal Stage Effort $\hat{f}$ (1 mark).**
$$\boxed{\hat{f} = F^{1/N} = 256^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{256} \approx 6.35}$$

**Step 6: Compute Input Capacitances of Each Stage (1 mark).**

Working backwards from the load:
- $C_{in,3} = g_3 \cdot C_{out,3} / \hat{f}$. Note $C_{out,3} = 64 \cdot C_{inv} = 192$ fF.
  $$C_{in,3} = \dfrac{5/3 \cdot 192}{6.35} = \dfrac{320}{6.35} \approx 50.4 \text{ fF}$$
- $C_{out,2} = C_{in,3} = 50.4$ fF.
  $$C_{in,2} = \dfrac{4/3 \cdot 50.4}{6.35} = \dfrac{67.2}{6.35} \approx 10.6 \text{ fF}$$
- $C_{out,1} = C_{in,2} = 10.6$ fF.
  $$C_{in,1} = \dfrac{1 \cdot 10.6}{6.35} \approx 1.67 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 7: Compute Minimum Path Delay (1 mark).**
$$d_{min} = N \cdot \hat{f} + P = 3 \cdot 6.35 + 4 = 19.05 + 4 = 23.05 \text{ (inverter units)}$$

To convert to absolute delay, multiply by the unit inverter delay $t_{p0}$ (assumed 30 ps for a 1x inverter in this technology):
$$t_{p,path} = 23.05 \cdot 30 \text{ ps} \approx 691.5 \text{ ps}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2–3 Marks per Mistake):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor:** Many students write $t_p = R C$ instead of $0.69 R C$. This is a **mandatory** 0.5 mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{GS}$ with $V_{DS}$:** In the linear-region ON-resistance formula, the overdrive voltage is $(V_{GS} - V_T)$, **not** $(V_{DS} - V_T)$. Using the wrong one leads to a wrong answer.
> 3. **PMOS sizing mistake:** When asked to size a symmetric inverter, students often forget that $\mu_n / \mu_p$ is approximately **2 to 3**, not 1. The PMOS must be 2–3× wider.
> 4. **Mixing up $C_L$ components:** The total load capacitance is the sum of **input** capacitance of next stage + **diffusion** capacitance of the driving transistor + **wire** capacitance. Forgetting any term gives an underestimated delay.
> 5. **Logical Effort branching effort omission:** The branching effort $B$ is frequently forgotten in multi-stage paths. The KTU answer key specifically tests for $F = GBH$, not just $GH$.
> 6. **Wire delay length-dependence:** A common error is writing wire delay as proportional to $L$ rather than $L^2$. Without repeaters, wire delay grows **quadratically**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Summary for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [x] **Propagation delay = 50% input-to-output transition time** ($t_p = 0.69 R_{on} C_L$).
- [x] **$t_{pHL}$** = NMOS active (high-to-low output); **$t_{pLH}$** = PMOS active (low-to-high output).
- [x] **Average delay** $t_p = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH})/2$ is the standard performance metric.
- [x] **The 0.69 factor** comes from $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931$ and converts an RC time constant to 50%-of-final-value delay.
- [x] **PMOS is weaker** than NMOS due to hole mobility being ~2.5× lower than electron mobility; hence $(W/L)_p \approx 2.5 \cdot (W/L)_n$ in a symmetric inverter.
- [x] **Total load capacitance** $C_L$ = sum of next-stage gate cap + own drain diffusion cap + wire cap + fanout branch caps.
- [x] **Logical Effort delay** for a path: $d_{min} = N \cdot F^{1/N} + P$, where $F = G \cdot B \cdot H$.
- [x] **Wire delay grows quadratically with length** without repeaters: $t_{wire} \propto L^2$.
- [x] **Repeater insertion** breaks long wires into segments, reducing delay growth to approximately **linear with $L$**.
- [x] **Elmore delay** for an RC tree: $t_{D,j} = \sum_k R_{jk} C_k$ (weighted sum of capacitors by path resistance).
- [x] **Gate delay is proportional to fan-out** (linear), while **wire delay is proportional to $L^2$** (quadratic) — this is why modern VLSI is **interconnect-dominated**.
- [x] **Threshold voltage** $V_T$ appears in the denominator of $R_{on}$; lowering $V_T$ reduces delay but increases **subthreshold leakage** — a classic speed-power trade-off.
- [x] **Scaling $V_{DD}$ down** reduces dynamic power ($P \propto V_{DD}^2$) but **increases delay** (delay $\propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_T)^2$).
- [x] **Logical effort of standard gates:** INV = 1, 2-NAND = 4/3, 2-NOR = 5/3, 3-NAND = 5/3, 3-NOR = 7/3.
- [x] **Parasitic delay of standard gates:** INV = 1, 2-NAND = 2, 3-NAND = 3, 2-NOR = 2, 3-NOR = 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Realization of Logic Functions with Static CMOS Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Static CMOS Logic** is a digital circuit design style in which every logic gate is constructed using a pair of complementary networks — a **Pull-Up Network (PUN)** composed entirely of **PMOS transistors** and a **Pull-Down Network (PDN)** composed entirely of **NMOS transistors** — that are mutually exclusive in their conduction. The output node is always connected to either **V_DD** (logic **1**) or **GND** (logic **0**) for any valid input combination, and there is never a direct current path from supply to ground during steady state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Static CMOS logic realizes any Boolean function using a complementary pair of networks. The PUN (PMOS) connects the output to V_DD when the function evaluates to 1; the PDN (NMOS) connects the output to GND when the function evaluates to 0. The PUN and PDN are **logical duals** of each other."

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Think of a **railway signal crossing gate** controlled by two operators standing at opposite ends:

- Operator **PMOS** (PUN) controls the path going **UP** to the **high platform (V_DD)**.
- Operator **NMOS** (PDN) controls the path going **DOWN** to the **low platform (GND)**.
- A **strict rule** exists: **only one operator can open the gate at a time**. If both open simultaneously, the train (current) flows directly from high platform to low platform — a short circuit, which is forbidden.
- The inputs A, B, C are the **switches** operated by passengers. Depending on which combination of switches is pressed, exactly one of the two operators opens their gate.

This is precisely how static CMOS works — **never both networks conduct simultaneously**, hence the name **"static"** (no steady-state current).

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Expansion | Symbol | Physical Role |
|------|-----------|--------|---------------|
| PUN | Pull-Up Network | — | Uses PMOS to connect output to V_DD when F = 1 |
| PDN | Pull-Down Network | — | Uses NMOS to connect output to GND when F = 0 |
| F | Boolean Function | F(A,B,…) | The logic function being implemented |
| F̄ | Complement of F | F̄ | Logic function realized by the dual network |
| V_OH | Output High Voltage | ≈ V_DD | Maximum output voltage |
| V_OL | Output Low Voltage | ≈ 0 V | Minimum output voltage |
| V_M | Switching Threshold | — | Point where V_in = V_out on DC curve |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The term **"Static"** in Static CMOS explicitly refers to the fact that the output is always driven to a valid logic level (never left floating), and the gate consumes **zero static (DC) power** in any stable state. This is a frequent **2-mark definition question** in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental CMOS Design Rules

Static CMOS gates are governed by **three cardinal rules** (asked repeatedly in KTU exams):

**Rule 1 — Complementary Conductance Rule:**
- PDN conducts (connects output to GND) when F = 0.
- PUN conducts (connects output to V_DD) when F = 1.

**Rule 2 — Series–Parallel Duality Rule:**
- **Series NMOS** in PDN ⇔ **Parallel PMOS** in PUN.
- **Parallel NMOS** in PDN ⇔ **Series PMOS** in PUN.

**Rule 3 — Transistor Count Rule:**
For an N-input gate realizing function F, the transistor count is **2N** for a fully complementary CMOS gate (N PMOS + N NMOS).

## 2.2 Boolean Function to Transistor Network Mapping

The mapping procedure taught in KTU Module 3 is as follows:

**Step 1:** Write the Boolean function F(A, B, C, …).

**Step 2:** Construct the **NMOS (PDN)** network directly from F:
- Use **series** connections for AND (·) operations.
- Use **parallel** connections for OR (+) operations.

**Step 3:** Construct the **PMOS (PUN)** network from F̄ (the complement):
- Apply the **dual transform**: swap series ↔ parallel, swap · ↔ +.
- Equivalent to building PDN of F̄.

**Step 4:** Verify that PUN uses PMOS (source tied to V_DD) and PDN uses NMOS (source tied to GND).

## 2.3 Standard Static CMOS Gates

### (a) CMOS Inverter (N = 1, F = Ā)

| Network | Transistor Configuration | Conducts When |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------|
| PUN | Single PMOS, source at V_DD | A = 0 |
| PDN | Single NMOS, source at GND | A = 1 |

### (b) CMOS NAND2 Gate (F = $\overline{A \cdot B}$)

| Network | Configuration | Conducts When |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| PUN | **2 PMOS in PARALLEL** | A = 0 OR B = 0 |
| PDN | **2 NMOS in SERIES** | A = 1 AND B = 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often draw NAND as 2 PMOS in series. The correct PUN for NAND is **2 PMOS in PARALLEL** because PUN must conduct when **any** input is 0 (F = 1 when at least one input is 0).

### (c) CMOS NOR2 Gate (F = $\overline{A + B}$)

| Network | Configuration | Conducts When |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| PUN | **2 PMOS in SERIES** | A = 0 AND B = 0 |
| PDN | **2 NMOS in PARALLEL** | A = 1 OR B = 1 |

### (d) Complex Gate — AOI (And-Or-Invert): F = $\overline{A \cdot B + C \cdot D}$

| Network | Configuration | Transistor Count |
|---------|---------------|------------------|
| PUN | (P_A ∥ P_B) in series with (P_C ∥ P_D) | 4 PMOS |
| PDN | (N_A — N_B) in parallel with (N_C — N_D) | 4 NMOS |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Expression | Physical Meaning | Units |
|--------------------|-----------|------------------|-------|
| Transistor count (complementary CMOS) | $N_{FET} = 2n$ | n = number of inputs | dimensionless |
| Static power dissipation | $P_{static} \approx 0$ | No DC current path | Watts |
| Output HIGH voltage | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | PMOS fully ON | Volts |
| Output LOW voltage | $V_{OL} = 0$ | NMOS fully ON | Volts |
| Switching threshold (symmetric inverter) | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ | For $k_n = k_p$ | Volts |
| Switching threshold (general) | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$ | General case | Volts |
| NMOS aspect ratio (sizing) | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | Mobility-driven | — |
| PMOS aspect ratio (sizing) | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p = \mu_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | $\mu_r \approx 2$ to 3 for $\mu_n/\mu_p$ | — |
| Noise margin HIGH | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Logic 1 immunity | Volts |
| Noise margin LOW | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Logic 0 immunity | Volts |
| Propagation delay (first order) | $t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ | RC charging model | seconds |
| Inverter intrinsic delay | $t_{p0} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{D,sat}}$ | With constant current | seconds |
| Equivalent resistance ratio | $\dfrac{R_p}{R_n} = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \dfrac{(W/L)_n}{(W/L)_p}$ | Mobility ratio | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Static CMOS is the **dominant logic style** in virtually every modern IC — microprocessors (Intel, AMD, ARM cores), ASICs, FPGAs (hard logic), and SoCs. The zero static power is critical for **battery-powered devices** (smartphones, IoT sensors, wearables), while the excellent noise margin makes CMOS robust for **automotive and industrial** environments. The predictable RC delay allows automated timing closure in EDA tools like Synopsys Design Compiler and Cadence Genus.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Switching Threshold (V_M) of CMOS Inverter

The **switching threshold V_M** is defined as the input voltage V_in at which V_in = V_out, and both PMOS and NMOS are in **saturation**. This is the most frequently asked KTU derivation.

### Given

A symmetric CMOS inverter with:
- NMOS drain current (saturation): $I_{Dn} = \dfrac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{TN})^2$
- PMOS drain current (saturation): $I_{Dp} = \dfrac{k_p}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{TP})^2$
- Process transconductance parameters: $k_n = k_n' \cdot (W/L)_n$ and $k_p = k_p' \cdot (W/L)_p$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the operating point.**

At the switching threshold, V_in = V_out = V_M. Both transistors operate in **saturation** because the drain-to-source voltage of each is greater than the overdrive.

> For NMOS: $V_{DS,n} = V_M > V_{GS,n} - V_{TN} = V_M - V_{TN}$ → condition satisfied.
> For PMOS: $V_{DS,p} = V_{DD} - V_M$ and $V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{TP}\vert = V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert$ → condition satisfied.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the output node.**

In steady state, no current flows into the gate of the next stage (MOS gates are capacitive). Therefore the current through PMOS must equal the current through NMOS:

$$
I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute saturation current expressions.**

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{GS,p} - V_{TP})^2
$$

**Step 4 — Apply gate-source voltages for each transistor.**

- For NMOS: $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} = V_M$, so $V_{GS,n} - V_{TN} = V_M - V_{TN}$.
- For PMOS: $V_{GS,p} = V_{in} - V_{DD} = V_M - V_{DD}$, so $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_M$, giving $V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{TP}\vert = V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert$.

Substituting:

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the common factor 1/2 on both sides.**

$$
k_n (V_M - V_{TN})^2 = k_p (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2
$$

**Step 6 — Take the positive square root of both sides.**

We choose the positive root because all bracketed terms are positive in the region of interest:

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for V_M.**

Expand and collect V_M terms on one side:

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\, V_M - \sqrt{k_n}\, V_{TN} = \sqrt{k_p}\, V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p}\, V_M - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{TP}\vert
$$

Group V_M on the left:

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\, V_M + \sqrt{k_p}\, V_M = \sqrt{k_p}\, V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_n}\, V_{TN} - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{TP}\vert
$$

Factor V_M:

$$
V_M \left( \sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p} \right) = \sqrt{k_p}\, V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_n}\, V_{TN} - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{TP}\vert
$$

Divide both sides by the coefficient of V_M:

$$
V_M = \frac{\sqrt{k_p}\, V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_n}\, V_{TN} - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{TP}\vert}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}}
$$

**Step 8 — Special case: symmetric inverter (k_n = k_p, V_TN = |V_TP|).**

For a symmetric inverter, $k_n = k_p$ and $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$:

$$
V_M = \frac{V_{DD} + V_{TN} - \vert V_{TP}\vert}{2} = \frac{V_{DD} + V_{TN} - V_{TN}}{2} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}
$$

Therefore, for a symmetric inverter:

$$
\boxed{V_M = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}
$$

**Step 9 — General form (ratio form).**

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{k_n}$:

$$
\boxed{V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}}
$$

**Step 10 — Physical interpretation.**

The switching threshold V_M is the **center of the transition region**. A higher V_M means the inverter switches closer to V_DD (favours NMOS strength). To obtain V_M = V_DD/2, the PMOS must be made wider than NMOS by the mobility ratio (~2 to 3 times) to compensate for the lower hole mobility.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Boolean Function to CMOS Transistor Network (Step-by-Step Mapping)

**Problem:** Realize the function $F = \overline{A \cdot (B + C)}$ using static CMOS.

### Step 1 — Express F and its complement.

Given: $F = \overline{A \cdot (B + C)}$

Apply De Morgan: $F = \overline{A} + \overline{(B + C)} = \overline{A} + (\overline{B} \cdot \overline{C})$

### Step 2 — Build the PDN (NMOS) directly from F̄ = A · (B + C).

Read the function left-to-right, applying the **SP (Series-Parallel) rule**:

- **A · (B + C)**: First handle the OR term, then the AND.
- **B + C** → **parallel** pair of NMOS (N_B ∥ N_C).
- **A · (B+C)** → **series** combination of N_A with the parallel pair.
- Final PDN: **N_A in series with (N_B ∥ N_C)**.

### Step 3 — Build the PUN (PMOS) using the dual network.

Apply **series ↔ parallel swap** to the PDN:

- PDN: N_A — (N_B ∥ N_C)
- PUN: P_A ∥ (P_B — P_C)

Equivalently, build PDN of F = Ā + B̄·C̄ and then convert to PMOS:

- $\overline{A} + \overline{B} \cdot \overline{C}$
- B̄·C̄ → series (P_B — P_C)
- Ā + (B̄·C̄) → parallel (P_A ∥ (P_B — P_C))

### Step 4 — Verify completeness using truth table.

| A | B | C | B+C | A·(B+C) | F = $\overline{A\cdot(B+C)}$ | PDN State | PUN State | Output |
|---|---|---|-----|---------|------------------------------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0   | 0       | 1                            | OFF       | ON (P_A)  | V_DD   |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1   | 0       | 1                            | OFF       | ON (P_A)  | V_DD   |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1   | 0       | 1                            | OFF       | ON (P_A)  | V_DD   |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1   | 0       | 1                            | OFF       | ON (P_A)  | V_DD   |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0   | 0       | 1                            | OFF       | ON (P_B—P_C closed via 0) | V_DD |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1   | 1       | 0                            | ON        | OFF       | GND    |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1   | 1       | 0                            | ON        | OFF       | GND    |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1   | 1       | 0                            | ON        | OFF       | GND    |

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** For every input combination, exactly one network conducts. The transistor count is **6** (3 PMOS + 3 NMOS), which is 2N where N = 3 inputs. The function is correctly realized.

### Step 5 — Transistor count and complexity.

$$
N_{FET} = 2 \cdot n = 2 \cdot 3 = 6 \text{ transistors}
$$

This matches the KTU rule: an N-input fully complementary CMOS gate always uses **2N transistors**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Propagation Delay of CMOS Inverter (First-Order RC Model)

For digital timing analysis, the CMOS inverter output is modelled as a first-order RC network charging/discharging a load capacitance C_L through an equivalent transistor resistance R_eq.

**Step 1 — Output charging equation.**

When input switches from HIGH to LOW, PMOS turns ON and charges C_L from 0 to V_DD through equivalent resistance R_p:

$$
V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/(R_p C_L)}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Time to reach 50% point (t_pLH, propagation delay low-to-high).**

Set $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}/2$:

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t_{pLH}/(R_p C_L)}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{1}{2} = 1 - e^{-t_{pLH}/(R_p C_L)}
$$

$$
e^{-t_{pLH}/(R_p C_L)} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Take natural log:

$$
-\frac{t_{pLH}}{R_p C_L} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{pLH} = \ln 2 \cdot R_p C_L \approx 0.69 \, R_p C_L}
$$

**Step 3 — Symmetric fall delay (t_pHL).**

By duality, for the discharge path through NMOS:

$$
\boxed{t_{pHL} = \ln 2 \cdot R_n C_L \approx 0.69 \, R_n C_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Average propagation delay.**

$$
t_p = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2} = 0.69 \, C_L \, \frac{R_p + R_n}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Sizing implication for equal rise and fall delays.**

For symmetric delay (t_pLH = t_pHL), we require R_p = R_n. Since $R \propto 1/[(W/L) \cdot \mu]$:

$$
\frac{1}{\mu_p (W/L)_p} = \frac{1}{\mu_n (W/L)_n}
$$

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \approx 2 \text{ to } 3 \times \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n}
$$

This is why PMOS in standard cell libraries are sized 2×–3× wider than NMOS.
