# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Valuation Pattern (Background)

KTU 2024 Scheme Part A carries 3 marks each (short answer) and Part B carries 14 marks each (full numerical/derivation). Module 3 questions on Static CMOS Logic typically test:
- Boolean-to-transistor mapping (7 marks)
- DC analysis / switching threshold derivation (7 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Static CMOS Logic. Why is it called "static"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Static CMOS logic is a digital circuit design style in which every gate consists of a **Pull-Up Network (PUN) of PMOS transistors** and a **Pull-Down Network (PDN) of NMOS transistors** connected between V_DD and GND, with the output taken from their common node. (1.5 marks)

It is called **"static"** because the output node is **always connected to either V_DD or GND through a low-resistance path** for any valid input combination. Consequently, the gate consumes **zero static (DC) power** in steady state, and the output level does not depend on the previous state. (1.5 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocation:** 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for the "static" justification.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the three design rules for constructing PUN and PDN in a static CMOS gate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rule 1:** PDN uses only **NMOS** transistors (source at GND); PUN uses only **PMOS** transistors (source at V_DD). (1 mark)

**Rule 2:** PDN and PUN are **logical duals** — a series connection in PDN corresponds to a parallel connection in PUN, and vice versa. (1 mark)

**Rule 3:** The output must be **connected to V_DD through PUN when F = 1**, and to **GND through PDN when F = 0**; both networks must never conduct simultaneously. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Realize the Boolean function $F = \overline{(A + B) \cdot C}$ using static CMOS logic. Draw the transistor-level schematic showing both PUN and PDN. Verify with the truth table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a symmetric CMOS inverter, derive the expression for the switching threshold voltage $V_M$ in terms of $V_{DD}$, $V_{TN}$, and $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ for the case $k_n = k_p$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify F and F̄.**

Given: $F = \overline{(A + B) \cdot C}$

Apply De Morgan: $F = \overline{(A + B)} + \overline{C} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B} + \overline{C}$

So $F̄ = (A + B) \cdot C$

**Step 2 — Build the PDN from F̄ = (A + B) · C.** (2 marks)

- (A + B) → **parallel** pair of NMOS (N_A ∥ N_B).
- (A + B) · C → **series** combination of N_C with the parallel pair (N_A ∥ N_B).
- **Final PDN:** N_C in series with (N_A ∥ N_B).

**Step 3 — Build the PUN as the dual.** (2 marks)

- Series ↔ parallel swap: P_C ∥ (P_A — P_B).
- **Final PUN:** P_C in parallel with (P_A — P_B).

**Step 4 — Verify with truth table.** (2 marks)

| A | B | C | A+B | (A+B)·C | F̄ evaluated | F = $\overline{F̄}$ | PDN State | PUN State | Output |
|---|---|---|-----|---------|-------------|-------------------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0   | 0       | 0           | 1                 | OFF       | ON (P_C)  | V_DD   |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0   | 0       | 0           | 1                 | OFF       | ON (P_A—P_B) | V_DD |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1   | 0       | 0           | 1                 | OFF       | ON (P_C)  | V_DD   |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1   | 1       | 1           | 0                 | ON        | OFF       | GND    |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1   | 0       | 0           | 1                 | OFF       | ON (P_C)  | V_DD   |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1   | 1       | 1           | 0                 | ON        | OFF       | GND    |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1   | 0       | 0           | 1                 | OFF       | ON (P_C)  | V_DD   |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1   | 1       | 1           | 0                 | ON        | OFF       | GND    |

**Step 5 — Transistor count:** 6 transistors (3 PMOS + 3 NMOS). (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition.** (1 mark)
$V_M$ is the input voltage $V_{in}$ at which $V_{in} = V_{out}$, and both transistors are in **saturation**.

**Step 2 — KCL at output node.** (1 mark)
$$
I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}
$$

**Step 3 — Write saturation current expressions.** (2 marks)
$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2
$$

With $V_{GS,n} = V_M$ and $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_M$:

$$
k_n (V_M - V_{TN})^2 = k_p (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Apply $k_n = k_p$ (symmetric inverter).** (1 mark)

Cancel $k_n$ and $k_p$ (equal):

$$
(V_M - V_{TN})^2 = (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Take the positive square root.** (1 mark)

$$
V_M - V_{TN} = V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for V_M.** (1 mark)

$$
2 V_M = V_{DD} + V_{TN} - \vert V_{TP}\vert
$$

$$
\boxed{V_M = \frac{V_{DD} + V_{TN} - \vert V_{TP}\vert}{2}}
$$

For $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$, this reduces to $V_M = V_{DD}/2$. **(Bonus 0.5 mark if mentioned)**

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Realize the Boolean function $F = \overline{A \cdot B + C \cdot D}$ as a single static CMOS complex gate. Show the PUN and PDN, and determine the number of transistors required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CMOS inverter, derive the propagation delay $t_{pLH}$ when the output switches from LOW to HIGH. State the condition for equal rise and fall times and explain how it influences the PMOS aspect ratio. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write F and F̄.** (1 mark)

Given: $F = \overline{A \cdot B + C \cdot D}$

Therefore: $F̄ = A \cdot B + C \cdot D$

**Step 2 — Build the PDN from F̄ = (A·B) + (C·D).** (2 marks)

- A·B → **series** pair of NMOS (N_A — N_B).
- C·D → **series** pair of NMOS (N_C — N_D).
- (A·B) + (C·D) → **parallel** combination of the two series branches.
- **Final PDN:** (N_A — N_B) ∥ (N_C — N_D).

**Step 3 — Build the PUN as the dual.** (2 marks)

- Series ↔ parallel swap.
- (P_A ∥ P_B) in series with (P_C ∥ P_D).
- **Final PUN:** [(P_A ∥ P_B)] — [(P_C ∥ P_D)] between V_DD and output.

**Step 4 — Verification and count.** (2 marks)

- Transistor count: 4 PMOS + 4 NMOS = **8 transistors** (2N where N = 4 inputs). (1 mark)
- The gate is an **AOI22 (And-Or-Invert)** complex gate, realizable in a single stage. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Charging equation for output node.** (2 marks)

When input transitions from HIGH to LOW, PMOS turns ON and charges C_L from 0 to V_DD through equivalent PMOS resistance R_p:

$$
V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/(R_p C_L)}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 50% criterion.** (1 mark)

Set $V_{out}(t_{pLH}) = V_{DD}/2$:

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t_{pLH}/(R_p C_L)}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for t_{pLH}.** (2 marks)

$$
e^{-t_{pLH}/(R_p C_L)} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
-\frac{t_{pLH}}{R_p C_L} = -\ln 2
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{pLH} = \ln 2 \cdot R_p C_L \approx 0.69\, R_p C_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Symmetric delay condition.** (1 mark)

For equal rise and fall delays, $R_p = R_n$. Since $R \propto 1/[\mu \cdot (W/L)]$:

$$
\frac{1}{\mu_p \cdot (W/L)_p} = \frac{1}{\mu_n \cdot (W/L)_n}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for PMOS aspect ratio.** (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \approx 2\text{ to }3 \times \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n}
$$

This compensates for the lower hole mobility, making the gate **symmetric in drive strength** and ensuring balanced propagation delays.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **NAND/NOR Confusion (2-mark penalty):** Drawing 2 PMOS in series for a NAND gate (it must be parallel) or 2 PMOS in parallel for a NOR gate (it must be series). Always derive from F̄ using the SP rule.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Source Connection (1–2 mark penalty):** PMOS sources must be at V_DD; NMOS sources must be at GND. Many students forget to label the source/drain in stick diagrams.
>
> 3. **Transistor Count Miscalculation (1 mark):** Forgetting that a fully complementary CMOS gate uses **2N transistors**, not N. A 3-input NAND uses 6 transistors, not 3.
>
> 4. **Switching Threshold Derivation Mistake (2 marks):** Forgetting to use saturation-region current equations, or missing the square-root step. The V_M derivation is the **most-tested** numerical in this module.
>
> 5. **De Morgan Error (2 marks):** When realizing complex gates, students often apply De Morgan incorrectly on the F̄ expression, leading to a wrong transistor network. Always **double-check** the SP (series-parallel) mapping.
>
> 6. **Not Verifying with Truth Table (1–2 marks):** Even if the schematic looks correct, KTU examiners deduct marks if the truth-table verification is missing or contains errors.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Static CMOS Logic Realization**

- **Definition:** Static CMOS = PUN (PMOS) + PDN (NMOS), always exactly one conducting. **Zero static power.** (✓ 1-mark concept)
- **Cardinal Rule:** PUN conducts when **F = 1**; PDN conducts when **F = 0**. (✓ KTU favourite)
- **SP Rule (Series-Parallel):** Build PDN directly from F̄ using series for · and parallel for +. (✓ 7-mark standard)
- **Duality Rule:** PUN is the dual of PDN — **series ↔ parallel swap**. (✓ tested every year)
- **NAND2:** PUN = 2 PMOS parallel; PDN = 2 NMOS series. (✓ 3-mark direct)
- **NOR2:** PUN = 2 PMOS series; PDN = 2 NMOS parallel. (✓ 3-mark direct)
- **Transistor Count:** $N_{FET} = 2n$ for fully complementary CMOS. (✓ 1-mark fact)
- **Switching Threshold (symmetric):** $V_M = V_{DD}/2$. (✓ 7-mark derivation)
- **General V_M:** $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$. (✓ 7-mark derivation)
- **Propagation Delay:** $t_{pLH} = 0.69\, R_p C_L$; $t_{pHL} = 0.69\, R_n C_L$. (✓ 7-mark derivation)
- **PMOS Sizing Rule:** $(W/L)_p = (\mu_n/\mu_p) \cdot (W/L)_n \approx 2\text{–}3\times (W/L)_n$ for symmetric delay. (✓ 3–7 mark)
- **V_OH = V_DD, V_OL = 0 V** in ideal static CMOS. (✓ 2-mark noise margin basis)
- **Complex Gates (AOI/OAI):** Implement any F̄ expression in a single stage using SP networks. (✓ 7-mark application)
- **Advantage:** Zero static power, full output swing, high noise margin, scalable. (✓ 2-mark theory)
- **Disadvantage:** More transistors than pseudo-NMOS (2N vs N+1); large input capacitance for high-fan-in gates. (✓ 2-mark theory)

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Realization of Logic Functions with Static CMOS Logic**

**Total Marks Distribution (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
- Part A (2 × 3 marks) = **6 marks**
- Part B (1 × 14 marks) = **14 marks**
- **Module Weightage:** ~12–15% of the university exam paper

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout Design and Design Rules: Stick Diagram, Micron Rules, and Lambda Rules (Definitions Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Layout Design and Design Rules: Stick Diagram, Micron Rules, and Lambda Rules (Definitions Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Layout Design — The Bridge Between Schematic and Silicon

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Layout Design is the graphical **geometric representation** of a VLSI circuit's schematic, depicting the precise physical placement and interconnections of transistors, wires (polysilicon and metal layers), contacts, and vias on different mask layers of an integrated circuit. It acts as the **final manufacturable blueprint** handed over to the foundry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Layout Design is NOT the same as Schematic Design. Schematic shows *what is connected* (logical/electrical view); Layout shows *where physically each wire and device sits* on silicon (physical/geometric view).

**Conceptual Analogy — The "House Blueprint" Intuition**
Imagine an architect designs a house on paper (schematic) and then a civil engineer draws a *floor plan with exact dimensions* — where the kitchen sits at coordinate (5m, 3m), the bathroom pipes run along the north wall, and the doors are exactly 0.9 m wide. That floor plan is the **Layout**. Similarly, in VLSI, the layout specifies exact coordinates, widths, and spacings of every conducting layer so that when masks are made and silicon is fabricated, the chip works correctly.

---

### 1.2 Stick Diagram — The "Rough Sketch" of a Layout

**Formal Definition:**
A **Stick Diagram** is a symbolic, *color-coded* cartoon-like representation of a VLSI layout that captures the **topology** (connectivity and relative placement) of devices and interconnects *without committing to absolute dimensions*. Sticks (horizontal/vertical colored lines) represent wires on different layers; filled boxes or "X" marks represent **contacts/vias**; transistor regions are marked with specific symbols.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Stick Diagrams exist:** They allow a VLSI designer to *quickly plan* a layout, check connectivity, identify design errors (e.g., shorts, unconnected nodes), and visualize area usage *before* committing time to drawing the full masked layout. They use **color as a substitute for different process layers**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Children's Map of Roads"**
Think of a stick diagram as a child's hand-drawn map where *red crayon* always means one type of road, *blue* means another, *green* means a third, and where two roads cross only if a bridge (contact) is drawn. There are no exact widths or scales — it just answers the question: *"Which roads connect where?"*

---

### 1.3 Design Rules — The "Traffic Laws" of Fabrication

**Formal Definition:**
**Design Rules** are a set of *geometric constraints* (minimum widths, minimum spacings, minimum overlaps) that a layout must obey to guarantee **reliable and reproducible fabrication** of an integrated circuit for a given process technology. They act as a *contract* between the circuit designer and the fabrication foundry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two widely-used design rule systems exist:
> - **Micron Rules** — dimensions expressed in **absolute micrometers (µm)**
> - **Lambda Rules** — dimensions expressed as **multiples of a single scalable parameter λ (lambda)**

---

### 1.4 Micron Rules — The "Absolute" Rulebook

**Formal Definition:**
**Micron Rules** (also called **micron-based design rules** or **scalable design rules with absolute dimensions**) specify minimum feature sizes and spacings in terms of **absolute physical dimensions measured in micrometers (µm)**. Each rule is hard-coded to a fixed numerical value (e.g., "Poly width must be ≥ **2 µm**").

**Conceptual Analogy — "Custom-Made Traffic Signs"**
If micron rules were traffic laws, they would say *"the speed limit on Highway 47 is exactly 80 km/h"*. Change the road (process) and the rule must be rewritten for the new road. They are tied to *one specific fabrication process*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Disadvantage:** When the foundry migrates to a smaller process node, *every single micron rule must be re-derived and re-tabulated*. Designs are **NOT portable** across technologies.

---

### 1.5 Lambda Rules — The "Scalable" Rulebook

**Formal Definition:**
**Lambda Rules** express all geometric constraints as **multiples of a single characteristic length parameter, λ (lambda)**, which is typically defined as **half the minimum feature size** of the process (often the minimum channel length or minimum drawn gate length). For example, in a 2 µm process, λ = **1 µm**; in a 0.5 µm process, λ = **0.25 µm**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Universal LEGO Bricks"**
Lambda rules are like saying *"the brick length is 2λ, the brick width is λ, and you must leave at least 3λ between any two bricks"*. Now, if the LEGO company shrinks everything by 50%, you just set λ = 0.5 cm and *all rules automatically scale down* — you don't rewrite the manual. This is the **heart of design portability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Mapping:** For most textbook examples (Weste & Harris / Mead & Conway), λ is set such that:
> - Minimum poly width = **2λ**
> - Minimum metal width = **3λ**
> - Minimum spacing between poly = **2λ**
> - Minimum spacing between metal = **3λ**

---

### 1.6 Comparison Snapshot

| Parameter | Micron Rules | Lambda Rules |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensional basis | Absolute (µm) | Relative (× λ) |
| Portability across processes | ✗ Poor | ✓ Excellent |
| Generality | Process-specific | Technology-independent |
| Used by | Industry foundries (modern) | Academic teaching / Mead-Conway |
| Example rule | "Poly width ≥ 0.5 µm" | "Poly width ≥ 2λ" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Stick Diagram — Layer Color Code (KTU Standard)

A stick diagram uses **color coding** as a visual shorthand for fabrication layers. The standard (Mead & Conway) convention taught in KTU syllabus is:

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Stick Diagram Color Code (KTU Module 3):**
> - **Green** → n-type diffusion (n-diff / active n-region)
> - **Brown / Orange** → p-type diffusion (p-diff / active p-region)
> - **Red** → Polysilicon (Poly / Gate)
> - **Blue** → Metal 1 (first interconnect layer)
> - **Yellow** → Metal 2 (second interconnect layer, optional)
> - **Solid Black Box / "X"** → Contact (cut) between Poly & Diffusion or Metal & Diffusion
> - **Crossed "X" without color** → Via between Metal 1 and Metal 2

**Operational Rules inside a Stick Diagram:**
1. Sticks of the *same color* **cannot cross** each other — they would form an electrical short at that layer.
2. Sticks of *different colors* **may cross freely** without electrical connection (they are on different physical layers).
3. A **filled box (contact)** must be drawn whenever two layers must be electrically tied.
4. A transistor exists wherever a **Poly (red) stick crosses a diffusion (green/brown) stick** — this is the *gate of an MOS transistor*.

### 2.2 Anatomy of Design Rules — The Two Categories

Design rules fall broadly into three families of constraints:

**A. Width Rules**
- Minimum width of any layer (poly, metal, diffusion). Prevents the line from being etched away or becoming discontinuous.
- Example: Poly width ≥ 2λ

**B. Spacing Rules**
- Minimum separation between features on the *same layer* (e.g., poly-to-poly spacing) and between *adjacent layers* (e.g., poly-to-diffusion spacing).
- Example: Metal-to-metal spacing ≥ 3λ

**C. Overlap / Extension Rules**
- Minimum area that one layer must extend over another to ensure proper contact formation.
- Example: Poly must extend at least 2λ beyond the diffusion edge on either side of a transistor (gate overlap).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Design Rules — Three Physical Realities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do design rules exist?** Three physical realities of photolithography:
> 1. **Resolution limit of optical lithography** — features below a certain size cannot be reliably patterned.
> 2. **Mask alignment tolerance** — successive masks cannot be perfectly aligned; small misregistration must be tolerated.
> 3. **Etching and doping diffusion tolerances** — sideways etching and lateral dopant diffusion require minimum buffer regions.

Any violation of a design rule risks an open circuit (broken wire), short circuit (merged wires), or a non-functioning transistor.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Micron ↔ Lambda Conversion

The relationship between the two rule systems is purely arithmetic.

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_{\min}}{2}
$$

where $L_{\min}$ is the **minimum channel length** (often equal to the minimum feature size) of the process.

| Quantity | Formula | Typical Numeric Example (2 µm process) |
|---|---|---|
| Lambda value | $\lambda = L_{\min} / 2$ | $2\,\mu m / 2 = \mathbf{1\,\mu m}$ |
| Convert rule to Micron | $\text{Micron size} = N \times \lambda$ | $2\lambda = \mathbf{2\,\mu m}$ |
| Convert Micron to Lambda | $\lambda\text{-count} = \text{Micron} / \lambda$ | $1.5\,\mu m / 0.5\,\mu m = \mathbf{3\lambda}$ |
| Standard minimum poly width | $W_{poly} \geq 2\lambda$ | $\mathbf{2\,\mu m}$ |
| Standard minimum metal width | $W_{metal} \geq 3\lambda$ | $\mathbf{3\,\mu m}$ |
| Standard n-diff spacing | $S_{n} \geq 3\lambda$ | $\mathbf{3\,\mu m}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Units Note (Anti-Pitfall):** Always express the final answer in the unit the question demands. If asked "in microns," never leave the answer as "3λ"; convert using the process's λ value.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stick Diagrams** are heavily used in *educational and pre-layout planning* stages. They are *never sent to the foundry*. Industry tools automatically generate layouts from schematic netlists using **Place & Route (P&R)** tools (e.g., Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC).
- **Micron Rules** are the *industry standard* for modern nanometer processes (e.g., TSMC 28 nm, 7 nm) because absolute precision is essential at advanced nodes where λ-based scaling breaks down.
- **Lambda Rules** remain pedagogically powerful in *university curricula* (including KTU) because they let students grasp design intent *without getting bogged down in process-specific numbers*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The λ-Definition and its Geometric Justification

**Step 1 — Declare the process characteristic length.**
Let the smallest reliably patternable feature in a process be $L_{\min}$, expressed in µm. For the standard textbook CMOS process, this corresponds to the **minimum mask dimension** for polysilicon.

**Step 2 — Define λ.**
The lambda parameter is *defined* as one-half of this minimum feature:

$$
\lambda \;\triangleq\; \frac{L_{\min}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Express typical minimum poly width in terms of λ.**
The minimum poly width rule is conventionally set to the smallest reproducible line, i.e., $W_{poly,\min} = L_{\min}$. Substituting Step 2:

$$
W_{poly,\min} = L_{\min} = 2\lambda
$$

**Step 4 — Express minimum poly-to-poly spacing.**
By the standard scalable rule convention, adjacent poly lines must be separated by at least one minimum feature:

$$
S_{poly\text{-}poly,\min} = L_{\min} = 2\lambda
$$

**Step 5 — Express minimum metal width.**
Metal lines are typically drawn thicker than poly to reduce resistance:

$$
W_{metal,\min} = 1.5 \, L_{\min} = 3\lambda
$$

**Step 6 — Express n-diffusion spacing.**
n+ diffusion regions must avoid merging to prevent unwanted shorts:

$$
S_{n\text{-}diff,\min} = 1.5 \, L_{\min} = 3\lambda
$$

**Final Compact Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{L_{\min}}{2} \\
W_{poly,\min} &= 2\lambda \\
S_{poly,\min} &= 2\lambda \\
W_{metal,\min} &= 3\lambda \\
S_{metal,\min} &= 3\lambda \\
S_{n,\min} &= 3\lambda \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Conversion: Micron ↔ Lambda

**Problem:** A CMOS process has $L_{\min} = 0.8\,\mu m$. Express (a) λ and (b) the minimum poly width $W_{poly,\min}$ in both µm and λ-units.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) } \lambda &= \frac{L_{\min}}{2} = \frac{0.8\,\mu m}{2} = 0.4\,\mu m \\
\text{(b) } W_{poly,\min} &= 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.4\,\mu m = 0.8\,\mu m \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification in λ-units:**

$$
W_{poly,\min} = 2\lambda \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison Table

| Design Rule (Concept) | Micron Rule Statement | Lambda Rule Statement | Equivalent in a 2 µm process |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum poly width | $W_{poly} \geq 2.0\,\mu m$ | $W_{poly} \geq 2\lambda$ | 2 µm ✓ |
| Minimum poly spacing | $S_{poly} \geq 2.0\,\mu m$ | $S_{poly} \geq 2\lambda$ | 2 µm ✓ |
| Minimum metal width | $W_{metal} \geq 3.0\,\mu m$ | $W_{metal} \geq 3\lambda$ | 3 µm ✓ |
| Minimum metal spacing | $S_{metal} \geq 3.0\,\mu m$ | $S_{metal} \geq 3\lambda$ | 3 µm ✓ |
| Min. n-diff spacing | $S_{n} \geq 3.0\,\mu m$ | $S_{n} \geq 3\lambda$ | 3 µm ✓ |
| Min. p-diff spacing | $S_{p} \geq 3.0\,\mu m$ | $S_{p} \geq 3\lambda$ | 3 µm ✓ |
| Contact size | $C = 2\,\mu m \times 2\,\mu m$ | $C = 2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ | 2 µm square ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Schematic to Stick Diagram to Full Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Circuit Schematic (logical netlist)"] --> B["Stick Diagram (color-coded topology, NO dimensions)"]
    B --> C["Apply Design Rules (Micron OR Lambda)"]
    C --> D["Full Masked Layout (exact geometric coordinates)"]
    D --> E["Design Rule Check DRC verification"]
    E --> F{"DRC Clean?"}
    F -->|Yes| G["Send to Foundry for Fabrication"]
    F -->|No| C

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
    style D fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    style E fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style G fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00796B
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- A **schematic** contains only logical symbols (transistors, nets).
- A **stick diagram** is the *intermediate cartoon* — color-coded, dimension-free, but captures all connections.
- **Design rules** are applied *on top of* the stick diagram's topology to convert it into a fully dimensioned layout.
- **DRC (Design Rule Check)** validates the layout; if it fails, the designer iterates.

---

### 4.2 Stick Diagram Legend — Node Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LG["Stick Diagram Legend (Standard)"]
        N1["RED stick = Polysilicon"]
        N2["GREEN stick = n-type diffusion"]
        N3["BROWN stick = p-type diffusion"]
        N4["BLUE stick = Metal layer 1"]
        N5["YELLOW stick = Metal layer 2"]
        N6["Solid Black Box = Contact cut"]
        N7["Crossed X symbol = Via between metals"]
        N8["Poly crossing Diffusion = MOSFET gate"]
    end

    style N1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style N2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style N3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style N4 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
    style N5 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style N6 fill:#212121,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style N7 fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242
    style N8 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
```

---

### 4.3 Block Architecture — How Design Rule Systems Map to Physical Masks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AL["Abstraction Layers"]
        L1["Lambda Rules (technology-independent)"]
        L2["Micron Rules (process-specific, absolute µm)"]
    end

    subgraph PL["Physical Mask Layers in Foundry"]
        M1["Mask 1: n-well"]
        M2["Mask 2: Active / Diffusion"]
        M3["Mask 3: Polysilicon"]
        M4["Mask 4: n+ implant"]
        M5["Mask 5: p+ implant"]
        M6["Mask 6: Contact cuts"]
        M7["Mask 7: Metal 1"]
        M8["Mask 8: Via 1"]
        M9["Mask 9: Metal 2"]
    end

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> M1
    L2 --> M2
    L2 --> M3
    L2 --> M4
    L2 --> M5
    L2 --> M6
    L2 --> M7
    L2 --> M8
    L2 --> M9

    style L1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
    style L2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that whether we use Micron or Lambda rules, they ultimately constrain the **same set of physical masks**. The two systems are just *two languages* to express identical physical constraints.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define a Stick Diagram. List the standard color codes used for polysilicon, n-diffusion, p-diffusion, Metal 1, and contacts.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Stick Diagram is a symbolic, color-coded representation of a VLSI layout that captures the **topology** (placement and connectivity) of devices and interconnects without specifying exact dimensions.

Standard color codes (Mead & Conway / KTU convention):
- **Polysilicon → Red**
- **n-type diffusion → Green**
- **p-type diffusion → Brown**
- **Metal 1 → Blue**
- **Metal 2 → Yellow**
- **Contacts / Vias → Solid black box or "X" mark**

(Definition = 1 mark, color codes = 2 marks = full 3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What are Lambda-based design rules? Why are they preferred for academic use?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Lambda (λ) based design rules express all minimum geometric constraints — widths, spacings, overlaps — as **integer multiples of a single scaling parameter λ**, where λ is defined as **half the minimum feature size** of the process.

They are preferred for academic use because:
1. They are **technology-independent** — the same rule-set scales across process generations simply by changing λ.
2. They simplify learning by removing process-specific numeric clutter.
3. They allow students to focus on **design intent and topology** rather than memorizing absolute micron values.

(Definition = 1 mark, two reasons = 2 marks = full 3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Layout Design** in VLSI. Differentiate clearly between **Micron Rules** and **Lambda Rules** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a **Stick Diagram**. Describe, with a neat sketch in stick-diagram form, the standard color-coding convention used to represent polysilicon, n-diffusion, p-diffusion, metal, and contacts. State the rule that determines where an MOS transistor is formed in a stick diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Layout Design + Micron vs Lambda Rules [7 marks]:**

**Layout Design [2 marks]:**
Layout Design is the geometric representation of a circuit's components (transistors, interconnects, contacts) on the various mask layers of an IC. It specifies the exact physical coordinates, widths, and spacings of every feature, bridging the gap between the schematic (logical view) and the fabricated silicon chip.

**Micron Rules [2.5 marks]:**
- Dimensions are stated in **absolute micrometers (µm)**.
- Each rule is a fixed numerical value for that process.
- Example: *"Minimum poly width = 0.5 µm"*, *"Minimum metal-to-metal spacing = 0.6 µm"*.
- Highly accurate but **not portable** across process nodes.

**Lambda Rules [2.5 marks]:**
- Dimensions are stated as **multiples of λ**, where $\lambda = L_{\min}/2$.
- Example: *"Minimum poly width = 2λ"*, *"Minimum metal spacing = 3λ"*.
- Same rule-set works for any technology simply by redefining λ.
- Excellent for **technology portability** and pedagogy.

**Comparison Table (optional, for full marks):**

| Aspect | Micron Rules | Lambda Rules |
|---|---|---|
| Units | µm | λ |
| Portability | Poor | High |
| Generality | Process-specific | Technology-independent |

---

**(b) Stick Diagram + Color Code + Transistor Identification Rule [7 marks]:**

**Definition [2 marks]:**
A Stick Diagram is a *simplified, dimension-free, color-coded pictorial representation* of a VLSI layout that captures the **topology** of components and interconnections using sticks (lines) on different conceptual layers.

**Color-coding convention [3 marks]:**
- **Red** — Polysilicon
- **Green** — n-type diffusion
- **Brown / Orange** — p-type diffusion
- **Blue** — Metal 1
- **Yellow** — Metal 2
- **Solid black box / "X"** — Contact cut (e.g., between Poly & Diffusion, or Metal & Diffusion)

**MOS Transistor Formation Rule [2 marks]:**
> An MOS transistor is formed in a stick diagram wherever a **Polysilicon (red) stick crosses over a diffusion (green for nMOS, brown for pMOS) stick** without an intervening contact. The crossing region itself represents the **gate** of the transistor; the diffusion on either side becomes the **source** and **drain**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Many students **omit the contact boxes** when drawing stick diagrams. Remember: two wires of different colors *may* cross without a short, but if you *intend* an electrical connection at the crossing, you **must draw a contact box**. Marks are deducted for this.
> 2. Students often forget to mention that the **transistor's source and drain are interchangeable** in a stick diagram — there is no fixed S/D assignment until the layout is annotated with VDD/GND.
> 3. In the Micron-vs-Lambda comparison, students frequently *omit the definition of λ*. Without $\lambda = L_{\min}/2$, the table looks incomplete — deduct 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What are **Design Rules** in VLSI? Explain the three physical realities of lithography that necessitate them. List any **six** standard Lambda-based rules covering poly width, poly spacing, metal width, metal spacing, n-diffusion spacing, and contact size. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CMOS process with $L_{\min} = 0.6\,\mu m$, calculate **(i)** the value of λ, **(ii)** the minimum poly width in µm, and **(iii)** the minimum metal spacing in µm, assuming the standard scalable rule set. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Rules — Definition, Physical Realities, Six Lambda Rules [7 marks]:**

**Definition [1 mark]:**
Design Rules are a set of geometric constraints (minimum widths, spacings, overlaps) that a layout must satisfy to ensure **reliable, manufacturable, and defect-tolerant** IC fabrication for a given process.

**Three Physical Realities of Lithography [3 marks]:**
1. **Resolution Limit** — Features below a certain size cannot be reliably patterned by the optical lithography wavelength. Hence minimum widths must be enforced.
2. **Mask Alignment Tolerance** — Successive photolithographic masks (e.g., poly mask, contact mask) cannot be perfectly aligned. Minimum overlaps/spacings must accommodate worst-case misregistration.
3. **Etching & Dopant Diffusion** — Sideways etching and lateral dopant diffusion cause features to enlarge or shift slightly. Design rules must include buffer regions to absorb these effects.

**Six Standard Lambda Rules [3 marks]:**
1. Minimum poly width: $W_{poly} \geq 2\lambda$
2. Minimum poly-to-poly spacing: $S_{poly} \geq 2\lambda$
3. Minimum metal width: $W_{metal} \geq 3\lambda$
4. Minimum metal-to-metal spacing: $S_{metal} \geq 3\lambda$
5. Minimum n-diffusion spacing: $S_{n\text{-}diff} \geq 3\lambda$
6. Minimum contact size: $C \geq 2\lambda \times 2\lambda$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $L_{\min} = 0.6\,\mu m$

**(i) Value of λ [2 marks]:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{L_{\min}}{2} = \frac{0.6\,\mu m}{2} = 0.3\,\mu m
$$

**(ii) Minimum poly width in µm [2 marks]:**

$$
W_{poly,\min} = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.3\,\mu m = 0.6\,\mu m
$$

**(iii) Minimum metal spacing in µm [3 marks]:**

$$
S_{metal,\min} = 3\lambda = 3 \times 0.3\,\mu m = 0.9\,\mu m
$$

**Final Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| λ | 0.3 µm |
| Minimum poly width | 0.6 µm |
| Minimum metal spacing | 0.9 µm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losses in Numericals:**
> 1. Students write the formula but **forget to substitute the given value** of $L_{\min}$. Always show the substitution step explicitly.
> 2. Unit inconsistency: Some students write "2λ = 0.6" *without the µm unit*. Always carry the unit through.
> 3. Students occasionally confuse $\lambda = L_{\min}/2$ with $\lambda = L_{\min}$. This single error cascades and produces incorrect answers in (ii) and (iii). Lose 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Layout Design** = geometric blueprint of a circuit on silicon masks. Differs from *schematic* (which is logical only).
- **Stick Diagram** = cartoon-like, color-coded, dimension-free representation; used for planning and verifying topology.
- **Stick Diagram Color Codes (KTU Standard):** Polysilicon = **Red**, n-diff = **Green**, p-diff = **Brown**, Metal 1 = **Blue**, Metal 2 = **Yellow**, Contact = **Black box / X**.
- **MOS Transistor Location Rule:** Transistor forms wherever **Poly (red) crosses Diffusion (green/brown)** without a contact box.
- **Design Rules** ensure *manufacturability*; they exist due to **resolution limits, alignment tolerance, and etching/diffusion effects**.
- **Micron Rules** = absolute µm values, process-specific, used in industry.
- **Lambda Rules** = multiples of λ, portable, used in academia (KTU).
- **Lambda Definition:** $\lambda = L_{\min} / 2$ — this is the single most-asked formula in KTU exams.
- **Standard Lambda Values to Memorize:** $W_{poly} = 2\lambda$, $S_{poly} = 2\lambda$, $W_{metal} = 3\lambda$, $S_{metal} = 3\lambda$, $S_{n\text{-}diff} = 3\lambda$, $S_{p\text{-}diff} = 3\lambda$, Contact = $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$.
- **Two-layer crossing without contact ≠ electrical connection**; different-colored sticks *can* cross without shorting, but the same color cannot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout of CMOS Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Layout of CMOS Inverter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Layout of a CMOS Inverter** is the *physical, geometric representation* of the inverter circuit on a silicon die, expressed as a set of two-dimensional geometric patterns in multiple **mask layers**. It is the bridge between the schematic (logical) view of the CMOS inverter — consisting of a PMOS pull-up network and an NMOS pull-down network — and the actual fabricated silicon structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A CMOS inverter layout is the **mask-level geometric description** of the nMOS and pMOS transistors interconnected to realize the logical function $\overline{IN} = \overline{A}$, drawn on a Cartesian grid governed by **λ-based design rules** (or micron rules in deep-submicron nodes).

The inverter consists of:

$$
V_{out} = \overline{V_{in}} \quad ; \quad V_{DD} \rightarrow \text{PMOS Source} \quad ; \quad V_{SS} \rightarrow \text{NMOS Source}
$$

with the **gates tied together** (input) and **drains tied together** (output).

| Component | Doping Type | Connection |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| PMOS (Top) | n-substrate / n-well | Source = V_DD, Drain = Output |
| NMOS (Bottom) | p-substrate | Source = V_SS, Drain = Output |
| Polysilicon | n+ / p+ doped | Common input gate |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" View

Think of the CMOS inverter layout as the **architectural blueprint of a two-storey house** built on a single plot of land.

- The **lower floor (ground level)** is the **NMOS transistor** — strong, fast, and connected to ground (V_SS).
- The **upper floor (first floor)** is the **PMOS transistor** — slightly weaker (due to lower hole mobility $\mu_p$), and connected to the ceiling supply (V_DD).
- The **staircase** connecting both floors is the **metal interconnect (poly + metal1)**, which carries the input signal up and the output signal down.
- The **building codes** (minimum corridor widths, staircase widths, distance between adjacent rooms) are the **λ-design rules** — they ensure two houses don't crash into each other and that the building can be reliably manufactured.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a real architect must respect building codes (fire safety, structural integrity), the VLSI layout designer must obey the **DRC (Design Rule Check)** rules — minimum width, minimum spacing, minimum extension, and minimum enclosure rules.

## 1.3 Why Layout Matters — The Physical Reality

The schematic is a *logical abstraction*. The layout introduces **parasitics**:

- **Gate capacitance** ($C_g$)
- **Diffusion (junction) capacitance** ($C_{diff}$)
- **Parasitic resistance** of polysilicon and diffusion regions
- **Wire capacitance and resistance** of metal interconnect

These parasitics determine the **propagation delay** of the inverter:

$$
t_{pd} = 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_{L}
$$

where $R_{eq}$ is the equivalent on-resistance and $C_L$ is the total load capacitance.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 emphasises that **layout-driven parasitics** are responsible for ~60–70% of the delay in modern sub-100nm designs. Hence, understanding layout is essential, not optional.

## 1.4 The Five Mask Layers of a Standard CMOS Inverter

A standard n-well CMOS process requires the following **mask layers** to be drawn:

1. **n-well mask** (defines the well in which PMOS sits)
2. **Active area (or Diffusion) mask** — defines where transistors are formed
3. **Polysilicon (Poly) mask** — defines the gate
4. **n+ Select mask** — heavily dopes NMOS source/drain
5. **p+ Select mask** — heavily dopes PMOS source/drain
6. **Contact mask** — opens windows between metal1 and poly/diffusion
7. **Metal1 mask** — first-level interconnect

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single transistor is therefore defined by the **intersection** of the **active area** and the **polysilicon gate** (the n+/p+ select layers determine whether it is NMOS or PMOS).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Layout

The construction of a CMOS inverter layout follows a strict logical order. We will elaborate each step thoroughly.

### Step 1 — Decide the nMOS and pMOS Aspect Ratios (W/L)

The mobility ratio $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$ (typically $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$ for a 0.25 $\mu m$ process). To make the **rise time ($t_{PLH}$) equal to the fall time ($t_{PHL}$)** — a critical KTU board requirement — the PMOS must be made **wider** than the NMOS.

For **equal drive strength** in terms of current capability:

$$
\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3
$$

A common KTU 2024 convention is:

$$
\boxed{W_n = 2\lambda, \quad W_p = 4\lambda \;\; \text{or} \;\; W_n = 3\lambda, \quad W_p = 6\lambda}
$$

If $L = 2\lambda$ for both, then the effective aspect ratios are:

- NMOS: $(W/L)_n = 2\lambda / 2\lambda = 1$
- PMOS: $(W/L)_p = 4\lambda / 2\lambda = 2$

> [!NOTE]
> In a **$2\lambda$ process**, the minimum transistor dimension is $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$. The minimum channel length $L_{min} = 2\lambda$.

### Step 2 — Identify the Three Terminals of Each Transistor

For NMOS:
- **Gate** — connected to input $V_{in}$
- **Source** — connected to $V_{SS}$ (GND)
- **Drain** — connected to output $V_{out}$

For PMOS:
- **Gate** — connected to input $V_{in}$
- **Source** — connected to $V_{DD}$
- **Drain** — connected to output $V_{out}$

### Step 3 — Draw the Stick Diagram (Euler's Path Approach)

The **stick diagram** is a colour-coded symbolic representation of the layout. It is the conceptual predecessor of the actual mask-level layout.

| Colour | Layer |
|--------|-------|
| Green | n-diffusion |
| Yellow/Brown | p-diffusion / polysilicon (varies by textbook) |
| Red | Polysilicon (gate) |
| Blue | Metal1 |
| Black | Contact / Via |
| Dotted yellow | n-well |
| Dotted green | p-substrate |

**Euler's Rule for Stick Diagrams:**
> *No transistor terminal (gate, source, drain) may be visited more than once along the Euler path* — except the input node (gates) and output node (drains), which can be revisited to minimize cross-overs.

### Step 4 — Apply λ-Design Rules

The **λ-based design rules** (also called **Mead-Conway rules**) scale all dimensions to a single parameter $\lambda$ so that the same layout is portable across processes.

| Rule | Symbol | Value |
|------|--------|-------|
| Minimum width — diffusion (n+/p+) | — | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum width — polysilicon | — | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum width — metal1 | — | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum spacing — diffusion to diffusion (same type) | — | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum spacing — diffusion to diffusion (different type) | — | $\lambda$ |
| Minimum spacing — poly to poly | — | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum spacing — metal to metal | — | $3\lambda$ |
| Minimum poly extension beyond diffusion (gate) | — | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum diffusion extension beyond poly | — | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum contact size | — | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ |
| Minimum contact to gate spacing | — | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum metal overlap of contact | — | $\lambda$ |
| Minimum well overlap of active | — | $2\lambda$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In exams, students often lose marks by writing **micron rules** when the question asks for **λ-rules** (or vice versa). Always state the rule system explicitly at the top of your answer.

### Step 5 — Translate Stick Diagram to Mask Layout

Convert each coloured stick into a rectangular geometry on the mask layers, respecting the λ-rules above. The result is the **layout** — a top-view 2D drawing ready for fabrication.

### Step 6 — Verify the Euler Path is Valid

A valid Euler path must:
- Traverse **every transistor terminal exactly once**
- Allow gates and drains to be **revisited** only at the start and end
- Result in a layout with **no cross-overs** (or a minimum number of cross-overs)

For the simple CMOS inverter (one NMOS, one PMOS), the canonical Euler path is:

$$
V_{DD} \rightarrow \text{pMOS source} \rightarrow \text{pMOS drain (output)} \rightarrow \text{nMOS drain (output)} \rightarrow \text{nMOS source} \rightarrow V_{SS}
$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \, R_{eqn} \, C_L$ | Fall time (high-to-low) delay | seconds |
| 2 | $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \, R_{eqp} \, C_L$ | Rise time (low-to-high) delay | seconds |
| 3 | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay | seconds |
| 4 | $R_{eqn} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$ | NMOS on-resistance | ohms |
| 5 | $R_{eqp} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}$ | PMOS on-resistance | ohms |
| 6 | $C_g = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ | Gate oxide capacitance | farads |
| 7 | $C_{diff} = C_j \cdot A + C_{jsw} \cdot P$ | Junction (diffusion) capacitance | farads |
| 8 | $C_L = C_{g,p} + C_{g,n} + C_{d,p} + C_{d,n} + C_{wire}$ | Total load capacitance | farads |
| 9 | $A_{cell} = H_{cell} \times W_{cell}$ | Layout cell area | $\mu m^2$ or $\lambda^2$ |
| 10 | $H_{cell} = 2W_p + 12\lambda$ | Cell height (typical) | $\lambda$ |
| 11 | $W_{cell} = 2L + 4\lambda$ | Cell width (typical, with $L=2\lambda$, this is $8\lambda$) | $\lambda$ |
| 12 | $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$ | Electron-to-hole mobility ratio | dimensionless |
| 13 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | $F/m^2$ |
| 14 | $\beta_n / \beta_p = \dfrac{\mu_n (W/L)_n}{\mu_p (W/L)_p}$ | Transconductance ratio (for symmetric inverter) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, the symbol $\vert x \vert$ denotes the **absolute value** of $x$ (NOT a markdown table separator).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CMOS inverter layout is the **fundamental building block** of every digital chip manufactured on Earth — from simple 8-bit microcontrollers (e.g., 8051) to billion-transistor processors (e.g., Apple M-series, AMD Zen). Mastery of its layout underpins:

- **Standard cell library design** (used in EDA synthesis tools like Synopsys Design Compiler)
- **Ring oscillator design** (for delay characterization)
- **Memory cell design** (SRAM, DRAM sense amplifiers)
- **IO pad design** (for high-drive buffers)
- **Clock tree synthesis** (for skew-balanced distribution)

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists layout as a prerequisite for **physical design automation** and **FPGA architecture** in higher semesters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equal Rise/Fall Time Sizing Condition

We want $t_{PHL} = t_{PLH}$. Using the KTU formula sheet:

$$
t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eqn} \cdot C_L
$$

$$
t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eqp} \cdot C_L
$$

Setting $t_{PHL} = t_{PLH}$ and cancelling the common factor $0.69 \cdot C_L$:

$$
R_{eqn} = R_{eqp}
$$

Substituting the resistance expressions:

$$
\frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)}
$$

Inverting both sides:

$$
\mu_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) = \mu_p \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)
$$

For matched threshold magnitudes ($V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert$), the term $(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})$ cancels from both sides:

$$
\mu_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n = \mu_p \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p
$$

Solving for the PMOS aspect ratio:

$$
\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n
$$

Substituting $\mu_n / \mu_p = 2.5$ and $(W/L)_n = 1$:

$$
\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = 2.5 \times 1 = 2.5
$$

If we keep the **channel length L = 2λ** for both transistors, then:

- $W_n = 2\lambda$, so $(W/L)_n = 1$
- $W_p = 5\lambda$, so $(W/L)_p = 2.5$ (in some textbooks, $W_p = 4\lambda$ is used for simplicity)

> [!NOTE]
> For a **slight round-up to multiples of $\lambda$**, the KTU board typically expects $W_p = 5\lambda$ or $W_p = 6\lambda$ depending on the question.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cell Area Estimation (Standard CMOS Inverter)

Assume a layout with the following dimensions (in $\lambda$):

- $L_n = L_p = 2\lambda$ (channel length)
- $W_n = 2\lambda$, $W_p = 4\lambda$ (for symmetric drive)
- Minimum diffusion-to-diffusion spacing (different type) = $\lambda$
- Minimum poly extension = $2\lambda$
- Minimum contact-to-poly spacing = $2\lambda$
- Minimum contact size = $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$
- Minimum metal-to-metal spacing = $3\lambda$

**Cell Width** (horizontal direction):

The width includes the polysilicon gate (length $L = 2\lambda$) plus the diffusion regions of the NMOS and PMOS on either side. Each side requires:

- $W/2$ diffusion width (for source or drain)
- $\lambda$ spacing between drain of PMOS and drain of NMOS (or just the contact)
- $\lambda$ metal-to-metal margin (or contact enclosure)

A common KTU-standard width formula is:

$$
W_{cell} = 2L + 4\lambda = 2(2\lambda) + 4\lambda = 8\lambda
$$

**Cell Height** (vertical direction):

The height includes the PMOS width, the spacing between p-diff and n-diff, the NMOS width, and the boundary contacts:

$$
H_{cell} = W_p + W_n + \text{spacings} = 4\lambda + 2\lambda + 6\lambda = 12\lambda
$$

(where the 6λ accounts for n+/n+ spacing, p+/p+ spacing, poly-to-contact, and well taps).

**Total Cell Area**:

$$
A_{cell} = W_{cell} \times H_{cell} = 8\lambda \times 12\lambda = 96\lambda^2
$$

For a $0.5\mu m$ technology ($\lambda = 0.25 \mu m$):

$$
A_{cell} = 96 \times (0.25)^2 = 96 \times 0.0625 = 6 \, \mu m^2
$$

For a $0.18\mu m$ technology ($\lambda = 0.09 \mu m$):

$$
A_{cell} = 96 \times (0.09)^2 = 96 \times 0.0081 = 0.7776 \, \mu m^2
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Always show the **dimensional breakdown** in the answer. Don't just write "$96\lambda^2$" — break it down into the component widths and heights to demonstrate mastery.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Load Capacitance of an Inverter Driving N Identical Inverters (Fan-out of N)

The total load capacitance on a driving inverter (with intrinsic output node) is:

$$
C_L = C_{int} + C_{ext}
$$

where $C_{int}$ is the **intrinsic capacitance** (own drain diffusion capacitance + routing capacitance) and $C_{ext}$ is the **extrinsic capacitance** (gate capacitances of the fan-out inverters plus interconnect).

For a fan-out of $N$ identical inverters, each with gate capacitance:

$$
C_{g,fanout} = C_{ox} \cdot W_n \cdot L_n + C_{ox} \cdot W_p \cdot L_p
$$

Assuming $(W \cdot L)_p = 2.5 (W \cdot L)_n$ for symmetry:

$$
C_{g,fanout} = C_{ox} \cdot L_n \left[ W_n + W_p \right] = C_{ox} \cdot 2\lambda \left[ 2\lambda + 5\lambda \right] = 14\lambda^2 C_{ox}
$$

For $N$ such inverters in fan-out:

$$
C_{ext} = N \cdot C_{g,fanout} = 14 N \lambda^2 C_{ox}
$$

The total load:

$$
\boxed{C_L = C_{int} + 14 N \lambda^2 C_{ox}}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Propagation Delay with FO4 Loading

Substituting $C_L$ into the delay formula:

$$
t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eqn} \cdot C_L
$$

With $R_{eqn} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$ and $(W/L)_n = 1$:

$$
t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})} \cdot \left[ C_{int} + 14 N \lambda^2 C_{ox} \right]
$$

Factoring out $C_{ox}$:

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{0.69}{\mu_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})} \left[ \frac{C_{int}}{C_{ox}} + 14 N \lambda^2 \right]
$$

For the special case of **Fan-Out of 4 (FO4)** — the standard KTU benchmark — set $N = 4$:

$$
t_{pd, FO4} = \frac{0.69}{\mu_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})} \left[ \frac{C_{int}}{C_{ox}} + 56 \lambda^2 \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FO4 delay** is the most cited metric for technology-node comparison. A 90nm process has FO4 delay $\approx$ 30 ps; a 7nm process has FO4 delay $\approx$ 8 ps.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (KTU University Exam — May 2022):**
A CMOS inverter in a $0.25\mu m$ process ($\lambda = 0.125\mu m$) is laid out with $W_n = 1\mu m$, $L_n = 0.25\mu m$, $W_p = 2.5\mu m$, $L_p = 0.25\mu m$. Calculate:
(a) The cell area in $\mu m^2$
(b) The gate capacitance of the NMOS
(c) The propagation delay for a load of 100 fF, assuming $R_{eqn} = 1.2 k\Omega$

**Solution:**

**(a) Cell Area:**

In terms of $\lambda$: $W_n = 8\lambda$, $L_n = 2\lambda$, $W_p = 20\lambda$, $L_p = 2\lambda$

Standard cell width:

$$
W_{cell} = 2L + 4\lambda = 2(2\lambda) + 4\lambda = 8\lambda = 8 \times 0.125 = 1\mu m
$$

Standard cell height (with $W_p = 20\lambda$ dominant):

$$
H_{cell} = W_p + W_n + 6\lambda = 20\lambda + 8\lambda + 6\lambda = 34\lambda = 34 \times 0.125 = 4.25\mu m
$$

Total area:

$$
A_{cell} = 1 \times 4.25 = 4.25 \, \mu m^2
$$

**(b) Gate Capacitance of NMOS:**

$$
C_{g,n} = C_{ox} \cdot W_n \cdot L_n = C_{ox} \cdot (1\mu m) \cdot (0.25\mu m) = 0.25 \mu m^2 \cdot C_{ox}
$$

If $C_{ox} = 8.46 \, fF/\mu m^2$ (for $t_{ox} = 4 nm$):

$$
C_{g,n} = 0.25 \times 8.46 = 2.115 \, fF
$$

**(c) Propagation Delay:**

$$
t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eqn} \cdot C_L = 0.69 \times 1200 \times 100 \times 10^{-15}
$$

$$
t_{pd} = 8.28 \times 10^{-11} = 82.8 \, ps
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CMOS Inverter Layout Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Schematic: CMOS Inverter] --> B[Choose W/L Ratios for NMOS and PMOS]
    B --> C[Apply Mobility Ratio: Wp = 2 to 3 times Wn]
    C --> D[Identify 4 Terminals: Vin, Vout, VDD, VSS]
    D --> E[Draw Stick Diagram using Euler Path]
    E --> F[Validate Euler Path - no repeated terminals]
    F --> G[Apply Lambda Design Rules]
    G --> H[Translate Sticks to Mask Geometry]
    H --> I[Generate 7 Mask Layers: n-well, Active, Poly, n+, p+, Contact, Metal1]
    I --> J[Run DRC: Design Rule Check]
    J --> K{DRC Pass?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Layout Complete]
    K -- No --> M[Fix Violations and Re-verify]
    M --> H
    L --> N[Extract Parasitics R and C]
    N --> O[Run LVS: Layout vs Schematic]
    O --> P[Fabrication Ready Mask Set]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Layer Stack-up of CMOS Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopLevel["Top Metal Layer (Metal1)"]
        M1[VDD Rail at Top]
        M2[VSS Rail at Bottom]
        M3[Output Metal Line in Middle]
    end

    subgraph MiddleLevel["Polysilicon and Active Layers"]
        P1[Polysilicon Gate - Horizontal Strip]
        A1[Active Region: n+ for NMOS]
        A2[Active Region: p+ for PMOS]
    end

    subgraph BottomLevel["Well and Substrate"]
        W1[n-well Region Enclosing PMOS]
        S1[p-substrate Beneath NMOS]
    end

    subgraph ContactLevel["Contact Layer"]
        C1[Contacts Connect Metal1 to Active]
        C2[Contacts Connect Metal1 to Polysilicon]
    end

    M1 --> A2
    M3 --> P1
    M2 --> A1
    C1 -.via.-> M1
    C1 -.via.-> M2
    C1 -.via.-> M3
    C2 -.via.-> P1
    A2 --> W1
    A1 --> S1
    P1 --> A1
    P1 --> A2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mask Layer Interaction in a CMOS Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[n-well Mask] --> X1[Defines PMOS Body Region]
    L2[Active Area Mask] --> X2[Defines Source and Drain Diffusions]
    L3[Polysilicon Mask] --> X3[Defines Gate Electrode]
    L4[n+ Select Mask] --> X5[Dopes NMOS Source and Drain]
    L5[p+ Select Mask] --> X6[Dopes PMOS Source and Drain]
    L6[Contact Mask] --> X7[Opens Vias for Metal Interconnect]
    L7[Metal1 Mask] --> X8[Forms Input, Output, VDD, VSS Interconnect]
    X2 --> X3
    X3 --> Y1[Transistor Formed at Active-Poly Intersection]
    Y1 --> Z1[Complete CMOS Inverter Layout]
    X5 --> Y1
    X6 --> Y1
    X7 --> Z1
    X8 --> Z1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stick Diagram to Layout Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vin: Poly Line Horizontal] --> B[Crosses n+ Active Vertical for NMOS Gate]
    A --> C[Crosses p+ Active Vertical for PMOS Gate]
    B --> D[NMOS Source: Connected to VSS via Metal1]
    B --> E[NMOS Drain: Connected to Output]
    C --> F[PMOS Source: Connected to VDD via Metal1]
    C --> G[PMOS Drain: Connected to Output]
    E --> H[Output Node Y shared by both drains]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Output Metal1 Line]
    D --> J[VSS Rail at Bottom]
    F --> K[VDD Rail at Top]
```

## 4.5 Layer-by-Layer Construction (Schematic Cross-Section as Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Silicon p-substrate] --> L1a[p+ Bulk Contact for VSS]
    L1a --> L2[Thin Gate Oxide t_ox]
    L2 --> L3a[n+ Source and Drain Formed by Ion Implantation]
    L3a --> L4[Polysilicon Gate Deposited and Patterned]
    L4 --> L5[Sidewall Spacers and LDD Regions]
    L5 --> L6[Contact Holes Etched]
    L6 --> L7[Metal1 Deposited and Patterned for Interconnect]
    L7 --> L8[Final Passivation and Bond Pads]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block diagrams above represent **logical / architectural interactions** of the layers, not their true physical cross-section. For an actual cross-section drawing, refer to your KTU prescribed textbook (Sedra/Smith or Kang/LeBlebici).
