# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Define "stick diagram" in the context of VLSI layout. Why is it used as an intermediate step between the schematic and the mask-level layout?**

**Model Answer:**

A **stick diagram** is a symbolic, colour-coded 2D representation of a CMOS layout in which each colour represents a specific mask layer (e.g., red for polysilicon, green for n-diffusion, blue for metal1).

**Reasons for using stick diagrams:**
1. **Quick prototyping** — the designer can rapidly sketch the topology of the layout without worrying about exact dimensions.
2. **Euler path validation** — it allows visual confirmation that no transistor terminals are revisited improperly, ensuring a **cross-over-free** layout.
3. **Communication** — stick diagrams are easier to read and review in team-based VLSI design than full mask layouts.
4. **Bridge to layout** — once validated, the stick diagram can be translated into a full mask-level layout with proper λ-design rules.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse stick diagram colours. **Red is poly, green is n-diff, yellow/brown is p-diff, blue is metal1**. Memorising this colour code is essential for marks.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: What are the λ-based design rules? Why is the "λ" parameter important in VLSI design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **λ-based design rules** (also called **Mead-Conway rules**) are a set of geometric constraints that scale all minimum dimensions — width, spacing, enclosure, extension — as integer multiples of a single parameter $\lambda$.

**Importance of λ:**
1. **Process portability** — a single layout can be retargeted to different process nodes by simply changing the value of $\lambda$.
2. **Scalability** — if a foundry shrinks $\lambda$ from 0.25 μm to 0.18 μm, the same layout (in λ-units) can be fabricated on the new process.
3. **Simplified design** — designers only need to remember one parameter instead of a long list of micron-based rules.
4. **Manufacturing margin** — all rules are designed with adequate margin to absorb process variations.

Typical example: minimum poly width = $2\lambda$, minimum diffusion width = $3\lambda$, minimum contact size = $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Draw the stick diagram of a CMOS inverter. Explain how the Euler path is identified and used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the mask-level layout of the CMOS inverter. Label all seven mask layers. Calculate the cell area for a $2\lambda$ process with $W_n = 2\lambda$, $W_p = 4\lambda$, $L = 2\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Identify the terminals**

| Transistor | Source | Drain | Gate |
|------------|--------|-------|------|
| NMOS | $V_{SS}$ (GND) | $V_{out}$ | $V_{in}$ |
| PMOS | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{out}$ | $V_{in}$ |

**Step 2: Construct the Euler path**

The Euler path must visit every terminal of both transistors exactly once. We choose the path:

$$
V_{DD} \;\to\; \text{PMOS source} \;\to\; \text{PMOS drain} \;\to\; \text{NMOS drain} \;\to\; \text{NMOS source} \;\to\; V_{SS}
$$

This is a **valid Euler path** because:
- Every terminal is visited exactly once.
- The input node (gates) is connected via a horizontal poly line.
- The output node is shared between the two drains.

**Step 3: Draw the stick diagram**

```
        VDD ─────●───────────
                 │
                 │  (p+ diffusion)
                 │
       ─────────●═══════════●─────  V_out (Metal1)
                 ║           ║
                 ║   (Poly)  ║
                 ║           ║
                 │  (n+ diffusion)
                 │
        VSS ─────●───────────
```

Where `═` is poly (red), `│` is diffusion (green for n+, yellow for p+), and `──` is metal1 (blue).

**Euler Path Validation:** The path traverses all 6 transistor terminals without revisit, confirming a cross-over-free layout.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Translate the stick diagram to mask geometry**

Apply the λ-rules:

| Dimension | Rule | Value |
|-----------|------|-------|
| Poly gate length | $L = 2\lambda$ | $2\lambda$ |
| NMOS width (vertical) | $W_n = 2\lambda$ | $2\lambda$ |
| PMOS width (vertical) | $W_p = 4\lambda$ | $4\lambda$ |
| p+ to n+ diffusion spacing | Different type | $\lambda$ |
| Source/drain extension beyond poly | — | $2\lambda$ |
| Contact size | — | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ |

**Step 2: Calculate the cell dimensions**

**Cell width** (horizontal, along the poly gate):

$$
W_{cell} = 2L + 2\lambda_{\text{ext,poly}} = 2(2\lambda) + 2(2\lambda) = 8\lambda
$$

(2L for the gate length and $2 \times 2\lambda$ for the poly extension on both sides of the diffusion)

**Cell height** (vertical, along the diffusion direction):

$$
H_{cell} = W_p + W_n + 2\lambda_{\text{spacing}} + 2\lambda_{\text{contact margin}} = 4\lambda + 2\lambda + 2\lambda + 2\lambda = 10\lambda
$$

(A common KTU convention is $H_{cell} = W_p + W_n + 6\lambda$ which yields $12\lambda$ with $2\lambda$ extra for contacts.)

**Step 3: Total cell area**

$$
\boxed{A_{cell} = W_{cell} \times H_{cell} = 8\lambda \times 12\lambda = 96\lambda^2}
$$

For a $0.5\mu m$ technology ($\lambda = 0.25\mu m$):

$$
A_{cell} = 96 \times 0.0625 = 6 \, \mu m^2
$$

**Seven Mask Layers Labelled:**

1. **n-well** — rectangular region enclosing the PMOS active area
2. **Active area** — two vertical strips (p+ on top, n+ on bottom) intersected by poly
3. **Polysilicon** — horizontal gate strip
4. **n+ Select** — covers the lower active region (NMOS)
5. **p+ Select** — covers the upper active region (PMOS)
6. **Contact** — three contacts: $V_{DD}$, $V_{out}$, $V_{SS}$
7. **Metal1** — three horizontal lines for $V_{DD}$, $V_{out}$, $V_{SS}$

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the step-by-step construction of a CMOS inverter layout using stick diagrams. Discuss the role of Euler's path in minimising the number of cross-overs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CMOS inverter in a $0.18\mu m$ technology ($\lambda = 0.09\mu m$), with $W_n = 4\lambda$, $W_p = 8\lambda$, $L = 2\lambda$, calculate: (i) cell area, (ii) gate capacitance, assuming $C_{ox} = 8.46 \, fF/\mu m^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Identify the four nodes**

The CMOS inverter has four electrical nodes: $V_{in}$, $V_{out}$, $V_{DD}$, $V_{SS}$.

**Step 2: List the transistor connections**

- **NMOS:** $V_{SS} \to$ source; $V_{out} \to$ drain; $V_{in} \to$ gate
- **PMOS:** $V_{DD} \to$ source; $V_{out} \to$ drain; $V_{in} \to$ gate

**Step 3: Determine the Euler path**

Choose the path:

$$
V_{DD} \to \text{PMOS (S → D)} \to V_{out} \to \text{NMOS (D → S)} \to V_{SS}
$$

with the input $V_{in}$ connected separately via a horizontal poly line crossing both vertical diffusion regions.

**Step 4: Draw the stick diagram (using colour code)**

| Layer | Symbol | Colour |
|-------|--------|--------|
| n+ diffusion | Vertical stick | Green |
| p+ diffusion | Vertical stick | Yellow / Brown |
| Polysilicon | Horizontal stick | Red |
| Metal1 | Horizontal line | Blue |
| Contact | Solid square | Black |
| n-well | Dotted enclosure | Yellow dotted |
| p-substrate | Dotted region | Green dotted |

The stick diagram has the poly gate (red horizontal) crossing both the n+ and p+ vertical sticks, creating the two transistors. The drains of both transistors are connected to the output node (middle horizontal blue line). The PMOS source connects to $V_{DD}$ (top blue line) and the NMOS source to $V_{SS}$ (bottom blue line).

**Step 5: Role of Euler's path**

Euler's path theory guarantees that if a valid Euler path exists, the resulting stick diagram will have **no cross-overs** (or a minimum number of cross-overs). This is critical because:

- **Cross-overs require additional metal layers** (e.g., Metal1 and Metal2) to route signals over each other.
- **Cross-overs increase the cell area** (more metal-to-metal spacing required).
- **Cross-overs increase the parasitic capacitance**, slowing the circuit.

> [!TIP]
> For the simple CMOS inverter, the Euler path is unique and unambiguous — the path is $V_{DD} \to$ PMOS $\to$ Output $\to$ NMOS $\to V_{SS}$. The input is the only side-branch.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Given: $\lambda = 0.09 \mu m$, $W_n = 4\lambda = 0.36 \mu m$, $W_p = 8\lambda = 0.72 \mu m$, $L = 2\lambda = 0.18 \mu m$, $C_{ox} = 8.46 \, fF/\mu m^2$

**(i) Cell Area Calculation:**

$$
W_{cell} = 2L + 4\lambda = 2(2\lambda) + 4\lambda = 8\lambda = 8 \times 0.09 = 0.72 \, \mu m
$$

$$
H_{cell} = W_p + W_n + 6\lambda = 8\lambda + 4\lambda + 6\lambda = 18\lambda = 18 \times 0.09 = 1.62 \, \mu m
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{cell} = 0.72 \times 1.62 = 1.1664 \, \mu m^2}
$$

**(ii) Gate Capacitance Calculation:**

NMOS gate capacitance:

$$
C_{g,n} = C_{ox} \cdot W_n \cdot L_n = 8.46 \times (0.36) \times (0.18)
$$

$$
C_{g,n} = 8.46 \times 0.0648 = 0.5482 \, fF
$$

PMOS gate capacitance:

$$
C_{g,p} = C_{ox} \cdot W_p \cdot L_p = 8.46 \times (0.72) \times (0.18)
$$

$$
C_{g,p} = 8.46 \times 0.1296 = 1.0964 \, fF
$$

Total gate capacitance:

$$
\boxed{C_g = C_{g,n} + C_{g,p} = 0.5482 + 1.0964 = 1.6446 \, fF}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 — Mark-Loss Zones for CMOS Inverter Layout Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing diffusion and poly colours in stick diagram (–2 marks).** Red is **always poly** (gate). Green and yellow are diffusion. Reversing these means the transistors will be of the wrong type — a fundamental error.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the n-well layer in the layout (–2 marks).** The PMOS must sit inside an **n-well** drawn in the p-substrate. Omitting the n-well means the PMOS cannot be fabricated.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the n+ and p+ select masks (–1 mark).** Without these masks, the source/drain regions are not properly doped, and the transistor cannot conduct.
>
> 4. **Incorrect Euler path — revisiting a terminal (–3 marks).** The Euler path must visit every terminal exactly once. If the path revisits the source or drain, the resulting layout will have unwanted cross-overs and extra parasitic capacitance.
>
> 5. **Mismeasuring cell area (–2 marks).** Students often forget to include **poly extension beyond the diffusion** (2λ on each side) when computing the cell width, leading to underestimation of the area.
>
> 6. **Using micron rules when the question asks for λ-rules (–1 mark).** Always state the design rule system explicitly. If the question says "$0.5\mu m$ technology", convert to λ first: $\lambda = 0.25\mu m$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMOS Inverter Layout — High-Density Revision Points**

- ☐ A CMOS inverter is the **NOT gate** with PMOS (pull-up) and NMOS (pull-down) connected in series.
- ☐ The **input** is tied to both gates; the **output** is the shared drain node.
- ☐ The **Euler path** for the inverter is $V_{DD} \to$ PMOS(S→D) $\to$ Output $\to$ NMOS(D→S) $\to V_{SS}$.
- ☐ For **symmetric drive** (equal rise/fall time), $W_p \approx 2.5 \times W_n$ (because $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$).
- ☐ A CMOS inverter requires **7 mask layers**: n-well, active, poly, n+ select, p+ select, contact, Metal1.
- ☐ A transistor is formed at the **intersection of the active area and the polysilicon gate**.
- ☐ The PMOS is in the **n-well**, the NMOS is in the **p-substrate**.
- ☐ λ-based design rules are **process-portable** — change $\lambda$, change the process node.
- ☐ Standard cell width $= 2L + 4\lambda$ (with poly extension).
- ☐ Standard cell height $= W_p + W_n + 6\lambda$ (with contact margins).
- ☐ Total cell area $\approx 96\lambda^2$ for a minimal symmetric inverter.
- ☐ **Propagation delay** $t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ — dominated by layout parasitics.
- ☐ **FO4 delay** is the standard benchmark for technology comparison.
- ☐ **DRC** = Design Rule Check; **LVS** = Layout vs Schematic — both must pass before fabrication.
- ☐ Stick diagram colours: **Red = Poly, Green = n+, Yellow/Brown = p+, Blue = Metal1, Black = Contact, Dotted Yellow = n-well**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When answering layout questions, **always draw the stick diagram first**, then derive the Euler path, then convert to the mask layout with proper dimensions. This three-step structure guarantees full marks in Part B (14 marks) questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two-Input NAND and NOR Gates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Two-Input NAND and NOR Gates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔷 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **CMOS VLSI Circuit Design**, the **Two-Input NAND Gate** and the **Two-Input NOR Gate** are the two fundamental universal logic gates constructed using a complementary pair of **Pull-Up Networks (PUN)** made of **PMOS transistors** and **Pull-Down Networks (PDN)** made of **NMOS transistors**. These gates form the building blocks of all complex digital VLSI systems and are critical for understanding **static CMOS logic design**, **transistor sizing**, **body effect**, and **propagation delay** analysis in sub-micron technologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Statement:**
> A *static CMOS gate* is a ratio-less logic gate in which, for every valid input combination, the output node is connected to **either V_DD (via the PUN) or GND (via the PDN), but never to both simultaneously.** This guarantees **no static power dissipation** under steady-state conditions.

---

## 🔷 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Switchboard Logic"

Imagine a **two-story switchboard** in a building:

- The **upper floor (PUN)** has switches that connect the lamp (output) to the **mains electricity (V_DD)**.
- The **lower floor (PDN)** has switches that connect the lamp to the **earth (GND)**.
- For a **NAND gate**, the upper switches work *independently* (either one can light the lamp), but the lower switches must both be *pressed simultaneously* to cut the connection to earth.
- For a **NOR gate**, the logic is reversed — the lower switches work independently (any one cuts the lamp off), but the upper switches must both be pressed to power the lamp.

This **series vs. parallel arrangement** of switches is the heart of CMOS gate design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Design Rules in Static CMOS:**
> 1. **PUN uses PMOS** → conducts when input = **0 (LOW)**.
> 2. **PDN uses NMOS** → conducts when input = **1 (HIGH)**.
> 3. PUN and PDN are **dual networks** (series ↔ parallel swap).
> 4. Output is **always** driven to a rail — never left floating.

---

## 🔷 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board Exams)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | **V_DD** | 1.8 V – 5 V | Power supply rail |
| NMOS Threshold | **V_TN** | 0.4 V – 0.7 V | PDN switch-on voltage |
| PMOS Threshold | **V_TP** | -0.4 V to -0.7 V | PUN switch-on voltage |
| Noise Margin High | **NM_H** | 0.3·V_DD | Robustness to HIGH noise |
| Noise Margin Low | **NM_L** | 0.3·V_DD | Robustness to LOW noise |
| Static Power | **P_static** | ≈ 0 W | Ideal CMOS advantage |

> [!TIP]
> Examiners in KTU frequently award marks for explicitly stating the *complementary nature* of the PUN and PDN. Always mention **"dual network"** when describing NAND/NOR.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔷 2.1 Two-Input CMOS NAND Gate — Topology & Operation

### 2.1.1 Circuit Structure
A **Two-Input NAND Gate** consists of:
- **2 PMOS transistors (P1, P2) in PARALLEL** forming the PUN.
- **2 NMOS transistors (N1, N2) in SERIES** forming the PDN.
- Inputs **A** and **B** drive the gates of *both* P1 & N1 (input A) and *both* P2 & N2 (input B).
- Output **F** is taken from the common node between PUN and PDN.

### 2.1.2 Operational Truth Table Analysis

| A | B | P1 | P2 | N1 | N2 | Output F = (A·B)' |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | **V_DD (1)** |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | **V_DD (1)** |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | **V_DD (1)** |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | **GND (0)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The output is **LOW only when BOTH A = 1 AND B = 1**. For all other combinations, at least one PMOS conducts, pulling the output to V_DD.

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction (The "Why" Behind Operation)

- **Step 1 → PUN Logic:** PMOS transistors turn ON when V_GS < V_TP. So PMOS is ON for input = 0.
- **Step 2 → PDN Logic:** NMOS transistors turn ON when V_GS > V_TN. So NMOS is ON for input = 1.
- **Step 3 → Parallel-PUN behavior:** Since P1 and P2 are in *parallel*, if **either** input is LOW, the corresponding PMOS conducts → output rises to V_DD.
- **Step 4 → Series-PDN behavior:** Since N1 and N2 are in *series*, BOTH must be ON to create a conducting path from output to GND → output falls to 0 only when A = B = 1.

---

## 🔷 2.2 Two-Input CMOS NOR Gate — Topology & Operation

### 2.2.1 Circuit Structure
A **Two-Input NOR Gate** consists of:
- **2 PMOS transistors (P1, P2) in SERIES** forming the PUN.
- **2 NMOS transistors (N1, N2) in PARALLEL** forming the PDN.
- It is the **exact dual** of the NAND gate.

### 2.2.2 Operational Truth Table

| A | B | P1 | P2 | N1 | N2 | Output F = (A+B)' |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | **V_DD (1)** |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | **GND (0)** |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | **GND (0)** |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | **GND (0)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The output is **HIGH only when A = B = 0**. The moment any input is HIGH, the corresponding NMOS in the parallel PDN pulls the output to GND.

---

## 🔷 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for the Two-Input NAND/NOR topic. Memorize this for direct KTU Board questions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{NAND} = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | Boolean expression | 2-Input NAND |
| 2 | $F_{NOR} = \overline{A + B}$ | Boolean expression | 2-Input NOR |
| 3 | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{n,\,NAND} = 2 \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{n,\,inv}$ | NMOS sizing (series transistors) | NAND |
| 4 | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{p,\,NAND} = \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{p,\,inv}$ | PMOS sizing (parallel transistors) | NAND |
| 5 | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{p,\,NOR} = 2 \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{p,\,inv}$ | PMOS sizing (series transistors) | NOR |
| 6 | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{n,\,NOR} = \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{n,\,inv}$ | NMOS sizing (parallel transistors) | NOR |
| 7 | $V_{SB,\,middle} = V_{out} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_{ox}}{C_{ox}+C_{dep}}\right)$ | Body effect on middle transistor | Series stack |
| 8 | $V_{TN,eff} = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$ | Body-effect adjusted V_TN | Series NMOS |
| 9 | $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,\,PDN} \cdot C_{L}$ | HIGH-to-LOW delay | Both gates |
| 10 | $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,\,PUN} \cdot C_{L}$ | LOW-to-HIGH delay | Both gates |
| 11 | $R_{eq,\,series} = n \cdot R_{eq,\,single}$ | Equivalent resistance of n series transistors | Stacks |
| 12 | $R_{eq,\,parallel} = \dfrac{R_{eq,\,single}}{n}$ | Equivalent resistance of n parallel transistors | Arrays |
| 13 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Dynamic power dissipation | Both gates |
| 14 | $P_{static} \approx 0$ (ideal CMOS) | Static power (no leakage) | Both gates |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These sizing rules ensure that the NAND/NOR gate has **symmetric rise and fall times** matching that of a reference CMOS inverter. The factor of **2×** for series transistors compensates for the fact that series transistors have a **higher effective on-resistance** due to (a) doubled channel resistance and (b) the **body effect** raising the threshold voltage of the middle transistor.

---

## 🔷 2.4 Why Series Transistors Need 2× Width — The "How" Explained

When two NMOS transistors are in **series** (NAND's PDN):
- The total channel length traversed is **2L** instead of L.
- Effective resistance is **2·R_n** instead of R_n.
- To restore equivalent drive current to that of a single transistor, we must double the width.
- Hence: $(W/L)_{each} = 2 \cdot (W/L)_{inv,n}$

When two PMOS are in **parallel** (NAND's PUN):
- Either transistor alone can charge the output capacitance.
- Worst-case path has resistance = R_p (single PMOS).
- Hence: $(W/L)_{each} = 1 \cdot (W/L)_{inv,p}$ is sufficient.

---

## 🔷 2.5 Body Effect in Series-Stacked Transistors

In a **2-input NAND**, the middle NMOS (N2) has its **source terminal** at a voltage higher than ground (it is connected to the drain of N1, which is in turn connected to the output node during discharge). This means **V_SB > 0** for N2, leading to an **elevated effective threshold voltage V_TN,eff**.

Similarly, in a **2-input NOR**, the middle PMOS experiences **body effect**, increasing |V_TP,eff|.

> [!WARNING]
> The body effect in series transistors is a **favourite KTU question**. Always state explicitly that it *degrades switching speed* and *reduces noise margin*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔷 3.1 Derivation I — Transistor Sizing for Symmetric Switching Threshold

### 📌 Problem Setup
A 2-input CMOS NAND gate is to be designed such that its **switching threshold V_M** equals that of a reference CMOS inverter, where the inverter has:
- NMOS aspect ratio: $(W/L)_{n,inv} = k$
- PMOS aspect ratio: $(W/L)_{p,inv} = r \cdot k$ (where r ≈ 2 for symmetric V_M)

### 📌 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the switching threshold condition.

At V_M, the currents from PUN and PDN must be equal, and both A and B are assumed equal to V_M.

**Step 2:** In the NAND gate, the **worst-case discharge path** has both N1 and N2 in series. The equivalent NMOS aspect ratio becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{n,\,eq,\,NAND} &= \frac{1}{\frac{1}{(W/L)_{n,eq,1}} + \frac{1}{(W/L)_{n,eq,2}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2}} \quad \text{(for series combination)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For the NAND to have the same drive strength as the reference inverter (W/L) = k, we need:

$$
\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{n,\,eq,\,NAND} = k
$$

**Step 4:** Since N1 and N2 are typically sized identically (W/L)_n:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{n,\,eq,\,NAND} &= \frac{1}{\frac{2}{(W/L)_n}} = \frac{(W/L)_n}{2} \\
\text{Setting } \frac{(W/L)_n}{2} &= k \\
(W/L)_n &= 2k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Final result:

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{n,\,NAND} = 2 \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{n,\,inv}}
$$

**Step 6 (Parallel PMOS):** For the PUN of the NAND gate, the worst-case charging path has **only one PMOS** conducting (since they are in parallel). Thus:

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{p,\,NAND} = \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{p,\,inv}}
$$

---

## 🔷 3.2 Derivation II — Body Effect on Middle NMOS in NAND Gate

### 📌 Problem Setup
In a 2-input NAND during the discharge transition (A = B = 1 → 0), the source of N1 is at the output node, which transitions from V_DD to 0. Calculate the effective threshold voltage of N1 assuming the body effect.

### 📌 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The body of N1 is tied to GND (substrate), so V_B = 0. The source of N1 is at V_S = V_out.

**Step 2:** Therefore:

$$
V_{SB,1} = V_S - V_B = V_{out} - 0 = V_{out}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the body-effect equation:

$$
V_{TN,eff} = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting V_{SB} = V_{out}:

$$
V_{TN,eff}(V_{out}) = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{out} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** At the start of discharge, V_{out} ≈ V_{DD}:

$$
V_{TN,eff,\,max} = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{DD} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Numerical example with V_TN0 = 0.5 V, γ = 0.4 V^{0.5}, 2φ_F = 0.6 V, V_DD = 3.3 V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{TN,eff} &= 0.5 + 0.4 \left( \sqrt{0.6 + 3.3} - \sqrt{0.6} \right) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.4 \left( \sqrt{3.9} - \sqrt{0.6} \right) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.4 \left( 1.9748 - 0.7746 \right) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.4 \times 1.2002 \\
&= 0.5 + 0.4801 \\
&= 0.9801 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** The threshold has effectively **doubled** from 0.5 V to 0.98 V. This demonstrates the **drastic impact of body effect** on series-stacked NMOS transistors.

---

## 🔷 3.3 Derivation III — Propagation Delay of Two-Input NAND Gate

### 📌 Problem Setup
A 2-input NAND gate drives a load capacitance C_L = 100 fF. Given:
- $(W/L)_n = 2$ (each NMOS)
- $(W/L)_p = 4$ (each PMOS)
- μ_n·C_ox = 100 μA/V², μ_p·C_ox = 50 μA/V²
- V_DD = 2.5 V, V_TN0 = 0.5 V, V_TP0 = -0.5 V

Calculate t_PHL and t_PLH.

### 📌 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 (t_PHL — HIGH-to-LOW):** Both NMOS in series discharge C_L. Effective resistance is 2·R_n.

For a single NMOS with W/L = 2:

$$
R_{n,\,single} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_{TN})} = \frac{1}{100 \times 2 \times (2.5 - 0.5)} \text{ k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_{n,\,single} = \frac{1}{400} \text{ k}\Omega = 2.5 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2:** Series combination:

$$
R_{eq,PDN} = 2 \times 2.5 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3:** Propagation delay:

$$
t_{PHL} = 0.69 \times R_{eq,PDN} \times C_L = 0.69 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-15}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{PHL} = 0.345 \text{ ns}}
$$

**Step 4 (t_PLH — LOW-to-HIGH):** Only one PMOS conducts (worst case). 

For a single PMOS with W/L = 4:

$$
R_{p,\,single} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)} = \frac{1}{50 \times 4 \times (2.5 - 0.5)} \text{ k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_{p,\,single} = \frac{1}{400} \text{ k}\Omega = 2.5 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5:**

$$
\boxed{t_{PLH} = 0.69 \times 2.5 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} = 0.1725 \text{ ns}}
$$

**Step 6 (Average propagation delay):**

$$
t_p = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2} = \frac{0.345 + 0.1725}{2} = 0.259 \text{ ns}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The t_PHL of NAND (0.345 ns) is **higher** than that of a reference inverter with the same transistor count, because of the series NMOS stack. This is a classic KTU conceptual question.

---

## 🔷 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of NAND vs. NOR

| Performance Parameter | 2-Input NAND | 2-Input NOR | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Series Transistors in PDN | 2 (NMOS) | 1 (NMOS, parallel) | **NAND slower** in t_PHL |
| Series Transistors in PUN | 1 (PMOS, parallel) | 2 (PMOS) | **NOR slower** in t_PLH |
| Body Effect Severity | Affects N1 in PDN | Affects P1 in PUN | Both suffer |
| Layout Area | Smaller (PMOS parallel = compact) | Larger (PMOS series = wide) | **NAND** |
| Speed (typical) | Faster t_PLH | Faster t_PHL | Depends on use |
| Preferred in Standard Cell Libraries | **Yes (most used)** | Less common | **NAND** |
| Input Capacitance per Input | C_g,n + C_g,p | C_g,n + C_g,p | Equal |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** NAND gates are **preferred over NOR gates** in CMOS VLSI design because NMOS transistors have higher carrier mobility (μ_n ≈ 2.5·μ_p). Since NAND's series stack is in the **NMOS** (faster devices), and NOR's series stack is in the **PMOS** (slower devices), **NAND is intrinsically faster** when both are properly sized.

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🔷 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Draw the CMOS circuit diagram of a 2-input NAND gate and explain its operation with a truth table.

**📝 Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A 2-input CMOS NAND gate consists of **2 PMOS transistors (P1, P2) connected in parallel** forming the pull-up network, and **2 NMOS transistors (N1, N2) connected in series** forming the pull-down network. Inputs A and B are applied to the gates of one PMOS and one NMOS each.

| A | B | P1, P2 | N1, N2 | Output F |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | ON, ON | OFF, OFF | 1 (V_DD) |
| 0 | 1 | ON, OFF | OFF, ON | 1 (V_DD) |
| 1 | 0 | OFF, ON | ON, OFF | 1 (V_DD) |
| 1 | 1 | OFF, OFF | ON, ON | 0 (GND) |

The output is LOW only when both inputs are HIGH, implementing $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$. The static power dissipation is zero since the PUN and PDN are never simultaneously ON.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q:** Why are NAND gates preferred over NOR gates in CMOS VLSI design? List any two reasons.

**📝 Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Two reasons why NAND gates are preferred over NOR gates in CMOS VLSI design:

1. **NMOS has higher carrier mobility than PMOS** (μ_n ≈ 2.5·μ_p). In a NAND gate, the *series stack* is in the NMOS pull-down network (faster devices). In a NOR gate, the series stack is in the PMOS pull-up network (slower devices). Hence, NAND is intrinsically faster for the same area.

2. **Layout compactness:** In a NAND gate, the PMOS transistors are in parallel, allowing compact layout with shared diffusion regions. In a NOR gate, the PMOS transistors are in series, requiring larger area with more complex routing.

3. **Lower input capacitance and better drive strength** in NAND, leading to better drive capability in cascaded logic stages.

---

## 🔷 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ **Question A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Design a 2-input CMOS NAND gate and a 2-input CMOS NOR gate. Derive the sizing requirements for the transistors in both gates. Compare their performance in terms of propagation delay, area, and body effect.

#### **Part (a)** — Design and Sizing Derivation (7 Marks)

**📝 Model Solution:**

**NAND Gate Design:**
- **PUN:** P1 and P2 (PMOS) in **parallel**, with inputs A and B at their gates.
- **PDN:** N1 and N2 (NMOS) in **series**, with inputs A and B at their gates.
- Output node F is at the common node.

**Sizing Derivation:**
- For the reference inverter, $(W/L)_{n,inv} = k$ and $(W/L)_{p,inv} = r \cdot k$.
- In the NAND's PDN, N1 and N2 are in series. Effective aspect ratio = $(W/L)_n / 2$.
- To match inverter drive: $(W/L)_n / 2 = k \Rightarrow (W/L)_n = 2k$.
- In the NAND's PUN, worst case is one PMOS conducting. Hence $(W/L)_p = r \cdot k$.

**NOR Gate Design:**
- **PUN:** P1 and P2 (PMOS) in **series**.
- **PDN:** N1 and N2 (NMOS) in **parallel**.

**Sizing Derivation:**
- PDN: Worst case = one NMOS → $(W/L)_n = k$.
- PUN: Two PMOS in series → effective ratio = $(W/L)_p / 2 = r \cdot k \Rightarrow (W/L)_p = 2r \cdot k$.

**Summary Table:**

| Gate | $(W/L)_n$ | $(W/L)_p$ |
|---|---|---|
| Inverter (Reference) | $k$ | $r \cdot k$ |
| 2-Input NAND | $2k$ | $r \cdot k$ |
| 2-Input NOR | $k$ | $2r \cdot k$ |

#### **Part (b)** — Performance Comparison (7 Marks)

**📝 Model Solution:**

**1. Propagation Delay:**
- For NAND: t_PHL depends on the series NMOS stack (slower discharge). t_PLH is determined by a single PMOS (faster charge).
- For NOR: t_PLH depends on the series PMOS stack (slower charge). t_PHL is determined by a single NMOS (faster discharge).
- **NAND has higher t_PHL; NOR has higher t_PLH.** Since PMOS is intrinsically slower than NMOS, NOR has a *worse overall t_PLH* contribution.

**2. Area:**
- NAND: PMOS in parallel → compact layout. Total width = $2k + 2r \cdot k = 2k(1+r)$.
- NOR: PMOS in series → wider PMOS ($2r \cdot k$ each, two in series, total stack width = $2r \cdot k$ × 2 = $4rk$). Total area larger.

**3. Body Effect:**
- NAND: Middle NMOS (N1) experiences elevated V_SB during discharge, increasing V_TN,eff.
- NOR: Middle PMOS (P1) experiences elevated |V_SB| during charging, increasing |V_TP,eff|.
- The body effect degrades both, but since NMOS is faster, NAND tolerates the degradation better.

**Conclusion:** NAND gates are preferred in standard-cell libraries due to (a) faster operation, (b) smaller area, and (c) better tolerance to body effect.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (Alternative)** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q:** With neat circuit diagrams, explain the operation of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate and a 2-input CMOS NOR gate. Discuss the effect of body effect on the threshold voltage of series-stacked transistors in these gates.

#### **Part (a)** — Circuit Diagrams and Operation (7 Marks)

**📝 Model Solution:**

**2-Input NAND Gate:**
- Two PMOS (P1, P2) in parallel between V_DD and output.
- Two NMOS (N1, N2) in series between output and GND.
- Truth table: Output = $\overline{A \cdot B}$.

**Operation (all 4 cases):**
- A=0, B=0: P1, P2 ON, N1, N2 OFF → Output = V_DD.
- A=0, B=1: P1 ON, P2 OFF, N1 OFF, N2 ON → Output = V_DD (through P1).
- A=1, B=0: P1 OFF, P2 ON, N1 ON, N2 OFF → Output = V_DD (through P2).
- A=1, B=1: P1, P2 OFF, N1, N2 ON → Output = GND.

**2-Input NOR Gate:**
- Two PMOS (P1, P2) in series between V_DD and output.
- Two NMOS (N1, N2) in parallel between output and GND.
- Truth table: Output = $\overline{A + B}$.

**Operation (all 4 cases):**
- A=0, B=0: P1, P2 ON, N1, N2 OFF → Output = V_DD.
- A=0, B=1: P1 ON, P2 OFF, N1 OFF, N2 ON → Output = GND (through N2).
- A=1, B=0: P1 OFF, P2 ON, N1 ON, N2 OFF → Output = GND (through N1).
- A=1, B=1: P1, P2 OFF, N1, N2 ON → Output = GND.

#### **Part (b)** — Body Effect Analysis (7 Marks)

**📝 Model Solution:**

**Body Effect in NAND:**
During discharge (A=B=1), current flows through N1 (lower) and N2 (upper). The source of N2 is at the output node V_out, while the body is at GND.
- $V_{SB,N2} = V_{out} - 0 = V_{out}$
- At the start of transition, $V_{out} \approx V_{DD}$, so $V_{SB,N2} \approx V_{DD}$.
- This raises $V_{TN,eff}$ significantly:

$$
V_{TN,eff} = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{DD} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert} \right)
$$

- **Consequence:** The middle NMOS turns ON later, slowing down the discharge. This is the **stacking effect**.

**Body Effect in NOR:**
During charging (A=B=0 → 1), current flows through P1 (lower) and P2 (upper). The source of P2 is at V_out, body at V_DD.
- $V_{SB,P2} = V_{out} - V_{DD}$ (negative)
- $|V_{SB,P2}| = V_{DD} - V_{out}$
- This raises $|V_{TP,eff}|$, slowing down the charging.

**Numerical Demonstration** (typical 0.25 μm process, V_DD = 2.5 V, V_TN0 = 0.5 V, γ = 0.4 V^0.5, 2φ_F = 0.6 V):

$$
V_{TN,eff} = 0.5 + 0.4(\sqrt{0.6+2.5} - \sqrt{0.6}) = 0.5 + 0.4(1.764 - 0.775) = 0.5 + 0.396 = 0.896 \text{ V}
$$

Threshold has increased by ~80%, severely degrading the gate's drive strength.

**Mitigation Techniques:**
1. Use a **W/L ratio of 2×** for series transistors to compensate.
2. Use **reverse body bias** to reduce body effect (in advanced technologies).
3. Use **multi-threshold CMOS (MTCMOS)** in low-power designs.

---

## 🔷 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams (PBECT604):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the dual relationship:** Many students state that NAND has PMOS in series. This is *wrong* — the NAND has **NMOS in series** and **PMOS in parallel**. Losing 1-2 marks here is common.
>
> 2. **Wrong sizing formula direction:** Some students apply $(W/L)_n = 2(W/L)_p$ for the NAND, confusing it with the inverter sizing. The correct statement is: *the NMOS in series must be 2× wider than the inverter's NMOS*, not twice the PMOS.
>
> 3. **Ignoring body effect:** When asked to compute threshold voltage, students often use $V_{TN0}$ directly. KTU examiners expect the *body-effect-adjusted* $V_{TN,eff}$ for the middle transistor in a stack. Missing this loses up to 3 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing t_PHL and t_PLH:** Always state the transition explicitly — t_PHL is for HIGH-to-LOW (discharge), t_PLH is for LOW-to-HIGH (charge). The series stack affects these *differently* in NAND vs. NOR.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Boolean expression:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for explicitly writing $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$ or $F = \overline{A + B}$ before drawing the circuit.
>
> 6. **Not drawing the body connection:** Showing the NMOS body tied to GND and PMOS body tied to V_DD in the circuit diagram is mandatory for full marks in the design question.

---

## 🔷 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Two-Input NAND & NOR Gates**

### ✅ **Core Topology**
- **NAND:** PMOS parallel + NMOS series. Implements $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$.
- **NOR:** PMOS series + NMOS parallel. Implements $F = \overline{A + B}$.
- PUN and PDN are **dual networks** (series ↔ parallel swap).

### ✅ **Transistor Sizing (Memorize!)**
- **NAND:** $(W/L)_n = 2 \cdot (W/L)_{n,inv}$ and $(W/L)_p = 1 \cdot (W/L)_{p,inv}$.
- **NOR:** $(W/L)_n = 1 \cdot (W/L)_{n,inv}$ and $(W/L)_p = 2 \cdot (W/L)_{p,inv}$.
- The 2× factor compensates for series-stack resistance doubling.

### ✅ **Body Effect**
- Series-stacked transistors have **elevated V_SB**, raising $V_{TN,eff}$ (or $|V_{TP,eff}|$).
- Equation: $V_{TN,eff} = V_{TN0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
- **NAND:** middle NMOS suffers. **NOR:** middle PMOS suffers.

### ✅ **Delay Characteristics**
- $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{PDN,eq} \cdot C_L$ (discharge path).
- $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{PUN,eq} \cdot C_L$ (charge path).
- NAND: t_PHL degraded (series NMOS), t_PLH normal (single PMOS).
- NOR: t_PLH degraded (series PMOS), t_PHL normal (single NMOS).

### ✅ **Static Power**
- $P_{static} \approx 0$ in static CMOS (PUN and PDN never simultaneously ON).
- Only dynamic power $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ exists.

### ✅ **NAND vs. NOR — Why NAND Wins**
- NMOS is ~2.5× faster than PMOS → series stack in NMOS (NAND) is better than in PMOS (NOR).
- NAND has more compact layout (parallel PMOS = shared diffusion).
- NAND is the **universal preferred gate** in standard-cell libraries.

### ✅ **Boolean Identities to Recall**
- $\overline{A \cdot B} = \bar{A} + \bar{B}$ (DeMorgan's).
- $\overline{A + B} = \bar{A} \cdot \bar{B}$ (DeMorgan's).
- NAND and NOR are **functionally complete** (any Boolean function can be built from either alone).

### ✅ **Key One-Liners for KTU Board Exam**
- *"A NAND gate has parallel PMOS and series NMOS; its dual, the NOR gate, has series PMOS and parallel NMOS."*
- *"Series transistors in CMOS stacks must be sized 2× wider to match inverter drive strength."*
- *"Body effect in the middle transistor of a stack increases effective threshold, slowing the gate."*
- *"NAND is preferred over NOR in CMOS VLSI because NMOS is intrinsically faster than PMOS."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 4 — Basic Concepts in CMOS VLSI Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU PBECT604 (VLSI Circuit Design, 2024 Scheme), the topic **"Basic Concepts"** of Module 4 establishes the foundational logical and electrical building blocks used in the systematic design of CMOS Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) subsystems. These concepts bridge the gap between the transistor-level analysis covered in earlier modules and the higher-level digital subsystem synthesis that defines modern System-on-Chip (SoC) design.

The **core logic families** that constitute the "Basic Concepts" of CMOS VLSI subsystem design are:

- **Static CMOS (Complementary CMOS)**
- **Ratioed Logic (Pseudo-NMOS / DCVSL)**
- **Pass-Transistor Logic (PTL)**
- **Transmission Gate (TG) Logic**
- **Dynamic CMOS Logic**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 4 of PBECT604 typically focuses on **CMOS Subsystem Design and Sequential Logic**. The "Basic Concepts" topic lays the groundwork by reviewing the non-idealities, performance trade-offs, and structural families of CMOS logic gates that engineers must select between when designing datapath elements, arithmetic units, memories, and control logic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Power Grid & Water Pipe" Model**

Imagine a city water distribution network as a metaphor for a CMOS logic gate:

- **PMOS transistors** are like **high-pressure pumps** connected to the water tank (VDD). They can *supply* charge but do it weakly per unit area.
- **NMOS transistors** are like **drainage pipes** connected to the sewer (GND). They can *pull down* charge efficiently.
- The **logic function** of the gate is like a **valve controller**: depending on input combinations, certain pipes open and close to either deliver water (logic 1) to a house or drain it away (logic 0).

In **static CMOS**, both the supply pipes (PMOS pull-up network) and drain pipes (NMOS pull-down network) are present. The output is *always* firmly connected to either VDD or GND — extremely robust, but uses more transistors and area.

In **ratioed logic** (Pseudo-NMOS), the high-pressure pump is *always* on weakly, and only the drain pipes are switched. It's cheaper to build (fewer transistors) but always leaks a little water (static power dissipation) — like a constantly-running pump.

In **dynamic logic**, charge is *temporarily stored* on a tiny capacitor (like a bucket). A precharge phase fills the bucket, and an evaluate phase either keeps it full or drains it. This is fast and compact, but the bucket can leak if you're not careful (charge leakage problem).

This intuition makes the engineering trade-offs crystal clear: *speed vs. area vs. noise-margin vs. power*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard CMOS design metrics and physical parameters are central to Module 4:

- **Supply Voltage ($V_{DD}$)** — typically **1.2 V (for 65 nm/45 nm)** down to **0.7 V (for advanced nodes)**.
- **Threshold Voltage ($V_t$)** — typically **0.3 V to 0.5 V** for modern processes.
- **Oxide capacitance per unit area ($C_{ox}$)** — typically **10 to 25 fF/$\mu m^2$**.
- **Mobility of electrons ($\mu_n$) ≈ 500–1350 $cm^2/V\cdot s$** at room temperature.
- **Mobility of holes ($\mu_p$) ≈ 100–500 $cm^2/V\cdot s$** (roughly 2–3× lower than electrons).
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of silicon ($n_i$) ≈ $1.5 \times 10^{10} /cm^3$** at 300 K.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard CMOS Performance Metrics:**
> - **Propagation Delay ($t_p$)** — measured in **picoseconds (ps)** to **nanoseconds (ns)**.
> - **Power Dissipation ($P$)** — measured in **microwatts ($\mu W$)** to **milliwatts (mW)**.
> - **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** — measured in **femtojoules (fJ)**.
> - **Noise Margin ($NM_H$, $NM_L$)** — measured in **volts (V)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five CMOS Logic Families — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Static CMOS Logic (Complementary CMOS)

**Operational Logic:**
1. The pull-up network (PUN) consists entirely of **PMOS transistors** connected to $V_{DD}$.
2. The pull-down network (PDN) consists entirely of **NMOS transistors** connected to GND.
3. The PUN and PDN are **logically dual networks** — when one conducts, the other is OFF.
4. Output node is always connected to *either* $V_{DD}$ *or* GND (never floating).

**Why it works:**
- PMOS devices turn ON when input is LOW (passing a strong 1).
- NMOS devices turn ON when input is HIGH (passing a strong 0).
- Therefore, the output is *always* driven by a low-impedance path to a supply rail.

**Key Properties (Why engineers love it):**
- **Rail-to-rail output swing** (0 to $V_{DD}$).
- **Zero static power dissipation** (no DC path from $V_{DD}$ to GND in steady state).
- **Excellent noise immunity** — both high noise margin ($NM_H$) and low noise margin ($NM_L$) are large.
- **Symmetric VTC (Voltage Transfer Characteristic)**.
- **Drawback:** Requires $2N$ transistors for an $N$-input gate (e.g., 6 transistors for a 2-input NAND/NOR).

### 2.1.2 Ratioed Logic (Pseudo-NMOS)

**Operational Logic:**
1. A single **always-ON PMOS** load device replaces the entire PMOS pull-up network.
2. Only the NMOS pull-down network is designed per the logic function.
3. The output LOW voltage ($V_{OL}$) is set by the **ratio** of PMOS strength to NMOS strength.

**Why it works (and why it has trade-offs):**
- The PMOS is biased with $V_{GS} = 0$ (gate tied to GND), so it's always in saturation.
- When the NMOS network evaluates to TRUE, current flows from $V_{DD}$ through PMOS to GND, dropping voltage across the PDN.
- The **ratio** $K_{n}/K_{p}$ determines how low $V_{OL}$ sinks.

**Key Properties:**
- **Fewer transistors** ($N+1$ instead of $2N$).
- **Faster** for high-fan-in NOR gates (since the PMOS load is small and constant).
- **Static power dissipation when output is LOW** — current continuously flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND.
- **Reduced noise margin** — $V_{OL}$ is non-zero.
- **Modern usage:** Largely replaced by other techniques except in specific high-speed applications.

### 2.1.3 Pass-Transistor Logic (PTL)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Uses a single NMOS (or PMOS) transistor to "pass" a signal from input to output.
2. The transistor acts as a **voltage-controlled switch**.
3. The output is controlled by the gate signal of the pass transistor.

**The Critical "Threshold Drop" Problem:**
- When an NMOS passes a logic HIGH, the output can only reach $V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ (a degraded "weak 1").
- This is because the NMOS turns OFF when $V_{GS} < V_{tn}$ → when output approaches $V_{DD} - V_{tn}$, the gate-to-source voltage falls below threshold.

**Key Properties:**
- **Fewer transistors** than static CMOS.
- **No static power** in some configurations.
- **Degraded output levels** → cascading requires level restoration.
- **Bidirectional signal flow.**

### 2.1.4 Transmission Gate (TG) Logic

**Operational Logic:**
1. A transmission gate consists of **one NMOS + one PMOS in parallel**, with complementary control signals.
2. When the control signal $C = 1$, both transistors conduct and the input is passed fully (0 to $V_{DD}$).
3. When $C = 0$, both transistors are OFF and the output is isolated (high-impedance).

**Why it works perfectly:**
- The NMOS passes a strong 0; the PMOS passes a strong 1.
- Together, they pass *both* logic levels fully without threshold drop.
- Acts as an **ideal analog switch** (when in the ON state).

**Key Properties:**
- **Full output voltage swing** (rail-to-rail).
- **Low ON-resistance** (parallel combination of NMOS and PMOS).
- **Used extensively** in multiplexers, XOR gates, flip-flops, and SRAM cells.

### 2.1.5 Dynamic CMOS Logic

**Operational Logic:**
1. Operates in **two phases**: **Precharge** and **Evaluate**.
2. **Precharge phase** ($\phi = 0$): PMOS is ON, charging output node capacitance to $V_{DD}$.
3. **Evaluate phase** ($\phi = 1$): NMOS network conditionally discharges the output based on inputs.
4. Output is stored on a **parasitic capacitance** $C_L$ during evaluation.

**Why it works (and why it has pitfalls):**
- Uses only **$N+2$ transistors** (vs. $2N$ for static CMOS).
- **Faster** due to lower input capacitance and absence of PMOS in the pull-up path.
- **Lower power** because no DC current path exists.

**Pitfalls (KTU Critical Concepts):**
- **Charge sharing** — charge redistribution between $C_L$ and internal node capacitances can corrupt the output.
- **Charge leakage** — sub-threshold leakage from the floating output node can destroy the stored value.
- **Cascading problem** — inputs to a second dynamic gate must be stable during precharge of the first gate (domino logic solves this).
- **Clock feedthrough** — capacitive coupling of the clock signal can inject charge into the output.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a high-yield, exam-ready summary of all critical formulas for Module 4 Basic Concepts.**

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** | **Typical Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CMOS Inverter Switching Threshold | $V_M = \dfrac{r(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}}{1 + r}$ | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{p}}{k_{n}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_p W_p}{\mu_n W_n}}$ | Volts (V) |
| NMOS ON-current (Saturation) | $I_{Dn} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W_n}{L_n} (V_{GS} - V_{tn})^2$ | Square-law model (long channel) | Amperes (A) |
| PMOS ON-current (Saturation) | $I_{Dp} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \dfrac{W_p}{L_p} (\vert V_{GS}\vert - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2$ | Square-law model (long channel) | Amperes (A) |
| Intrinsic Delay (First Order) | $t_{p0} = \dfrac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{D,sat}}$ | Time to charge/discharge $C_L$ to $V_{DD}/2$ | Seconds (s) |
| Dynamic Power Dissipation | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity factor | Watts (W) |
| Static Power Dissipation | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | $I_{leak}$ = sub-threshold leakage current | Watts (W) |
| Short-Circuit Power | $P_{sc} = \dfrac{1}{12} k \cdot \tau \cdot f_{clk} (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3$ | During switching transition | Watts (W) |
| Noise Margin High | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Difference between logic 1 output and min input HIGH | Volts (V) |
| Noise Margin Low | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Difference between max input LOW and logic 0 output | Volts (V) |
| Resistance in Deep Triode (NMOS) | $R_{on,n} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{tn})}$ | Linear-region ON resistance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Pass-Transistor Degraded HIGH | $V_{out,high} = V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ | Maximum output when NMOS passes a 1 | Volts (V) |
| Charge Sharing Voltage | $V_{final} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_{internal}}$ | Final voltage after charge redistribution | Volts (V) |
| Power-Delay Product (PDP) | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | Energy consumed per switching event | Joules (J) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on units:** For the inverter switching threshold formula, the ratio $r$ accounts for the **mobility mismatch** between PMOS and NMOS transistors. Designers typically size the PMOS width 2–3× the NMOS width to balance rise and fall times (i.e., to make $V_M \approx V_{DD}/2$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These basic concepts form the **design vocabulary** used in every modern IC:

- **Microprocessors (Intel, AMD, ARM cores):** Use static CMOS extensively for critical paths, dynamic logic in high-performance ALUs, and pass-transistor logic in low-power adders.
- **Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs):** Choose logic families based on the speed/area/power trade-off curve of the target application.
- **Memory chips (SRAM, DRAM):** Use 6T SRAM cells based on cross-coupled inverters (static CMOS) and 1T-1C DRAM cells exploiting capacitor-based charge storage (dynamic principles).
- **RF and Mixed-Signal ICs:** Use transmission gates for analog switches and sample-and-hold circuits.
- **FPGA Lookup Tables (LUTs):** Built using pass-transistor multiplexer trees for configurable logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Switching Threshold Voltage ($V_M$) of CMOS Inverter

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Find the DC input voltage $V_{in} = V_M$ at which $V_{out} = V_{in}$ for a symmetric CMOS inverter, where both NMOS and PMOS are in saturation at the threshold point.

**Step 1 — Identify the operating region at $V_M$:**

At the switching threshold, both transistors are typically in **saturation** (assuming $V_M > V_{tn}$ and $V_M < V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$).

For the **NMOS transistor**:
- $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} = V_M$
- $V_{DS,n} = V_{out} = V_M$
- Saturation condition: $V_{DS,n} \geq V_{GS,n} - V_{tn}$ → $V_M \geq V_M - V_{tn}$ → always true. ✓

For the **PMOS transistor**:
- $V_{GS,p} = V_{in} - V_{DD} = V_M - V_{DD}$
- $V_{DS,p} = V_{out} - V_{DD} = V_M - V_{DD}$
- Saturation condition: $V_{DS,p} \leq V_{GS,p} - V_{tp}$ (note sign convention for PMOS)
- Since $V_{GS,p} = V_M - V_{DD}$ is very negative, this is satisfied. ✓

**Step 2 — Apply the KCL condition at the output node:**

At the switching threshold, the current flowing into the output equals the current flowing out (no net charging of the output capacitance in DC steady state). This means:

$$
I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the saturation current equations:**

$$
\frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W_n}{L_n} (V_{GS,n} - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W_p}{L_p} (V_{GS,p} - V_{tp})^2
$$

Substituting the gate-source voltages:

$$
\frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W_n}{L_n} (V_M - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W_p}{L_p} (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{tp})^2
$$

Since $V_{tp} < 0$ for PMOS, we can write $(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{tp}) = -(V_{DD} - V_M + V_{tp})$. Squaring removes the sign:

$$
\frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W_n}{L_n} (V_M - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W_p}{L_p} (V_{DD} - V_M + \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Define the transconductance ratio $r$:**

Let:

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_p W_p}{\mu_n W_n}}
$$

Taking the square root of both sides of the current equation:

$$
\sqrt{\mu_n} \sqrt{\frac{W_n}{L_n}} (V_M - V_{tn}) = \sqrt{\mu_p} \sqrt{\frac{W_p}{L_p}} (V_{DD} - V_M + \vert V_{tp}\vert)
$$

Dividing both sides by $\sqrt{\mu_n W_n / L_n}$:

$$
(V_M - V_{tn}) = r \cdot (V_{DD} - V_M + \vert V_{tp}\vert)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $V_M$:**

$$
V_M - V_{tn} = r \cdot V_{DD} - r \cdot V_M + r \cdot \vert V_{tp}\vert
$$

Collecting $V_M$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$
V_M + r \cdot V_M = r \cdot V_{DD} + r \cdot \vert V_{tp}\vert + V_{tn}
$$

$$
V_M (1 + r) = r (V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_M = \frac{r (V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}}{1 + r}}
$$

**Step 6 — Special case (symmetric inverter):**

For a symmetric inverter where $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, set $V_M = V_{DD}/2$:

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{r (V_{DD} + \vert V_{tp}\vert) + V_{tn}}{1 + r}
$$

Solving for $r$ yields the required PMOS/NMOS width ratio for symmetric switching.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The mobility mismatch between holes ($\mu_p$) and electrons ($\mu_n$) means PMOS must be ~2–3× wider than NMOS to achieve symmetric switching. This is why CMOS inverters in layout have visibly different transistor widths.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Propagation Delay ($t_{pLH}$) of CMOS Inverter

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Derive the high-to-low propagation delay for a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$, assuming the PMOS is initially charging the output.

**Step 1 — Model the PMOS as a constant current source:**

During the high-to-low transition, the NMOS pulls down the output. In the first-order analysis, we assume the NMOS operates in **saturation** until $V_{out}$ drops below $V_{DD} - V_{tn}$ (approximately half the supply).

**Step 2 — Write the discharge equation:**

The current discharging $C_L$ is given by the saturation current:

$$
I_{Dn} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W_n}{L_n} (V_{GS,n} - V_{tn})^2
$$

Since the input is $V_{DD}$ (steady), $V_{GS,n} = V_{DD}$:

$$
I_{Dn} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W_n}{L_n} (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2
$$

**Step 3 — Express the rate of change of output voltage:**

$$
C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -I_{Dn}
$$

$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{I_{Dn}}{C_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$:**

$$
\int_{V_{DD}}^{V_{DD}/2} dV_{out} = -\frac{I_{Dn}}{C_L} \int_{0}^{t_{pHL}} dt
$$

$$
V_{DD}/2 - V_{DD} = -\frac{I_{Dn} \cdot t_{pHL}}{C_L}
$$

$$
-V_{DD}/2 = -\frac{I_{Dn} \cdot t_{pHL}}{C_L}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $t_{pHL}$:**

$$
t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{Dn}}
$$

Substituting $I_{Dn}$:

$$
\boxed{t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W_n}{L_n} (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Intrinsic delay (normalized form):**

Dividing the numerator and denominator by $V_{DD}$:

$$
t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W_n}{L_n} (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2 / V_{DD}}
$$

In the limit where $V_{DD} \gg V_{tn}$:

$$
t_{p0} \approx \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{D,sat}} \quad \text{(intrinsic delay)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Charge Sharing in Dynamic Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A dynamic logic gate has output capacitance $C_L$ precharged to $V_{DD}$. An internal node with capacitance $C_x$ initially at 0 V is connected during evaluation. Find the final voltage on $C_L$.

**Step 1 — Identify the initial charges:**

- Charge on $C_L$ initially: $Q_L(0) = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$
- Charge on $C_x$ initially: $Q_x(0) = C_x \cdot 0 = 0$

Total initial charge:

$$
Q_{total}(0) = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + 0 = C_L \cdot V_{DD}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of charge:**

When the internal node connects to the output (during evaluation), charge redistributes. The total charge is conserved (assuming no leakage and no clock feedthrough):

$$
Q_{total}(0) = Q_{total}(t)
$$

After redistribution, both capacitors reach a common voltage $V_{final}$:

$$
C_L \cdot V_{DD} = (C_L + C_x) \cdot V_{final}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{final}$:**

$$
\boxed{V_{final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_x}}
$$

**Step 4 — Failure criterion:**

The dynamic gate fails if $V_{final}$ drops below the switching threshold $V_M$ of the next stage (or the noise margin):

$$
\frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_x} > V_{IH,\text{next stage}}
$$

This sets a **minimum $C_L/C_x$ ratio** required for safe operation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Logic Family Trade-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table — Choose the right logic family for your design:**

| **Criterion** | **Static CMOS** | **Pseudo-NMOS** | **Pass Transistor** | **Transmission Gate** | **Dynamic CMOS** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transistor Count** ($N$ input) | $2N$ | $N+1$ | $N$ | $2N$ | $N+2$ |
| **Static Power** | Zero | High (when ON) | Zero | Zero | Zero |
| **Output Swing** | Rail-to-rail | Degraded LOW | Degraded HIGH (NMOS) | Rail-to-rail | Rail-to-rail (in precharge) |
| **Noise Immunity** | Excellent | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Speed** | Moderate | Fast (high fan-in) | Moderate | Fast | Very Fast |
| **Design Complexity** | Low | Low | Moderate | Moderate | High (clock req.) |
| **Typical Application** | General logic | ROM, PLA | Adders, Muxes | Muxes, Xors, latches | High-speed datapath |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
