A design that fails evaluation must be re-synthesized with modified constraints — it must **NEVER** proceed to physical design. --- ### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are the **chief quality inspector at a Rolls-Royce automobile assembly line**: 1. **Logic Synthesis** ≈ The robotic assembly arm that welds the chassis, fits the engine, and bolts the seats (constructing the gate-level netlist from RTL). 2. **Logic Evaluation** ≈ The 72-hour rigorous test drive where inspectors measure: - *Does the car start and run?* → **Functional Verification** - *Does it accelerate from 0–100 km/h in under 3 seconds?* → **Timing Analysis** - *How many liters of fuel per 100 km?* → **Power Analysis** - *Does it fit inside a standard parking slot?* → **Area Estimation** - *Will it pass the European crash test?* → **Testability / Fault Coverage** Just as a Rolls-Royce cannot be shipped without this evaluation, a VLSI chip **cannot** be sent for fabrication without passing logic evaluation. A single timing violation or a missing fault coverage metric could cost millions in re-spins. --- ### 🎯 The Four Pillars of Logic Evaluation (in Bold) | Pillar | Standard Metric | KTU-Mandated Tool/Method | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Performance** | Propagation delay $\bigl(t_{p}\bigr)$, Maximum operating frequency $\bigl(f_{max}\bigr)$ | Static Timing Analysis (STA) | | **Power** | Static + Dynamic power $\bigl(P_{total}\bigr)$ in **Watts (W)** | Power Analysis (vector-based or probabilistic) | | **Area** | Cell area in $\mu m^{2}$ or gate equivalents (GE) | Synthesis reports from Design Compiler | | **Testability** | Stuck-at fault coverage in **%** | ATPG (Automatic Test Pattern Generation) | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Constant (KTU Board Favorite):** The supply voltage $V_{DD}$ for modern CMOS nodes (90 nm down to 7 nm) ranges from **0.7 V to 1.2 V**, and clock frequencies for high-performance ASICs range from **100 MHz to 5 GHz**. These values are standard defaults when not specified in numerical problems. --- ### 🔄 Position of Logic Evaluation in the VLSI Design Flow Logic Evaluation sits **after Technology Mapping** and **before Physical Design (Place & Route)**. It serves as the final logical correctness gate prior to committing the design to silicon. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🧠 Operational Logic — The Five Sequential Steps The Logic Evaluation process is executed in a strict sequential order, with each step gating the next. Skipping a step is the **#1 cause of chip re-spins** in industry. 1. **Functional Verification** - *Why?* A circuit may be timing-clean but functionally wrong (e.g., an AND gate synthesized as an OR due to a tool bug). - *How?* Gate-level simulation using the same testbench as RTL, but now with SDF (Standard Delay Format) back-annotation. - *Pass Criterion:* Output vectors match the golden RTL output bit-for-bit across all test patterns. 2. **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** - *Why?* Dynamic simulation is too slow for billion-gate designs; STA covers all possible paths without simulation vectors. - *How?* The STA engine breaks the circuit into **timing arcs** and **timing paths**, then computes arrival times and required arrival times. - *Pass Criterion:* **Slack $\geq$ 0** on every timing path. Slack = Required Arrival Time − Actual Arrival Time. 3. **Power Analysis** - *Why?* Battery-powered devices (smartphones, IoT) have strict energy budgets. A chip drawing 2 W instead of 500 mW is unmarketable. - *How?* Probabilistic power estimation (toggle rates per node) or vector-based power analysis using gate-level simulation with SAIF/FSDB files. - *Pass Criterion:* Total power $\leq$ Power budget specified in the design constraints. 4. **Area Analysis** - *Why?* Smaller area = more dies per wafer = lower unit cost. A die shrink from 10 mm² to 9 mm² yields ~10% more revenue. - *How?* Synthesizer reports the total combinational + sequential + buffer cell area from the technology library. - *Pass Criterion:* Total cell area $\leq$ Area constraint (e.g., 1 mm²). 5. **Testability Analysis (DFT / ATPG)** - *Why?* Manufacturing defects (bridges, opens) must be screenable. Untestable chips become field failures. - *How?* Scan chain insertion followed by ATPG tool generation of test patterns. - *Pass Criterion:* Stuck-at fault coverage $\geq$ **95%** (industry standard; aerospace/military demands $\geq$ **99%**). --- ### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > These are the **board-exam critical equations**. Memorize units, typical value ranges, and the physical meaning of each term. The vertical bar $\vert$ symbol is rendered as `\vert` to preserve table integrity. | # | Equation | Term-by-Term Meaning | Units | KTU Board Frequency | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$=switching activity, $C_{L}$=load capacitance, $V_{DD}$=supply, $f_{clk}$=clock freq | **Watts (W)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 2 | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Leakage current in OFF transistors | **Watts (W)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 3 | $P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} + P_{short\text{-}circuit}$ | Sum of all power components | **Watts (W)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 4 | $t_{p} = \dfrac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay | **seconds (s)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 5 | $t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \, K_{n} \,(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^{2}}$ | NMOS pull-down delay | **seconds (s)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 6 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{c\text{-}q} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew}}$ | Maximum clock frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 7 | $Slack = T_{required} - T_{actual}$ | Positive slack = pass; Negative slack = violation | **seconds (s)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 8 | $EDP = E_{switch} \cdot t_{p} = C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} \cdot t_{p}$ | Energy-Delay Product (figure of merit) | **Joules·s (J·s)** | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 9 | $Fault Coverage = \dfrac{\text{Detected faults}}{\text{Total faults}} \times 100\%$ | ATPG quality metric | **%** | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 10 | $Area_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_{cell,i} + A_{interconnect}$ | Total silicon real estate | $\mu m^{2}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | --- ### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility Logic Evaluation is the **economic backbone** of the semiconductor industry. Consider the following industrial applications: - **Mobile SoCs (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon):** Logic evaluation is performed iteratively hundreds of times during a chip's design phase to achieve the optimal **PPA sweet spot**. The goal is sub-1 W power at 3+ GHz clock. - **Aerospace & Automotive (ISO 26262, DO-254):** Logic evaluation with formal verification and ≥99% fault coverage is **legally mandated** for safety-critical chips (brake controllers, flight computers). - **AI Accelerators (NVIDIA GPUs, Google TPU):** Massive parallel datapaths require aggressive logic evaluation to balance the 3 P's — performance (TOPS), power (watts), and area (mm²) — often quantified as **TOPS/W** (Tera-Operations Per Second per Watt). > [!NOTE] > **Industry Insight:** The famous **"PPA Pareto Curve"** in VLSI design illustrates that improving one metric (e.g., higher $f_{max}$) almost always degrades another (e.g., higher $P_{dynamic}$). Logic evaluation is the disciplined process of navigating this trade-off curve. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🔬 Derivation 1: Dynamic Power Dissipation in a CMOS Gate The dynamic power of a CMOS gate is dissipated whenever the output node capacitance is charged or discharged. We derive it from first principles. **Step 1 — Define the energy stored in the load capacitor per charging event:** When the output node transitions from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the load capacitor $C_{L}$ is charged from the supply rail. $$E_{charge} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{C} \cdot C_{L} \, dV_{C}$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:** $$E_{charge} = C_{L} \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{C} \, dV_{C} = C_{L} \left[ \dfrac{V_{C}^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{V_{DD}}$$ $$E_{charge} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Account for energy dissipated in the PMOS transistor during charging:** During the charging phase, the PMOS transistor acts as a resistor. The total energy drawn from the supply is the sum of energy stored in the capacitor and energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS. $$E_{supply} = E_{stored} + E_{dissipated} = C_{L} \, V_{DD}^{2}$$ $$\therefore E_{dissipated, charge} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Account for the discharge event (0 → 0 energy):** During discharge, the stored energy $\tfrac{1}{2}C_{L}V_{DD}^{2}$ in the capacitor is dissipated as heat in the NMOS transistor. No energy is drawn from the supply. $$E_{dissipated, discharge} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2}$$ **Step 5 — Total energy dissipated per full switching cycle (0→1→0):** $$E_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} = C_{L} V_{DD}^{2}$$ **Step 6 — Introduce switching activity factor $\alpha$ (probability of a transition per clock cycle):** For real circuits, not every node toggles every cycle. We multiply by $\alpha \in [0, 1]$. $$E_{avg, per\ cycle} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$ **Step 7 — Convert energy per cycle to power (energy × switching frequency):** $$P_{dynamic} = E_{avg, per\ cycle} \times f_{clk}$$ $$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This is the famous **$CV^{2}f$** power equation. Note the **quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$** — this is why modern chips aggressively scale down supply voltage. Reducing $V_{DD}$ from 1.2 V to 0.8 V gives a power reduction factor of $(0.8/1.2)^{2} \approx 0.44$, i.e., a **56% power saving**. --- ### 🔬 Derivation 2: Propagation Delay of a CMOS Inverter (Discharging Case, $t_{pHL}$) **Step 1 — Discharge current through the saturated NMOS transistor:** When the input switches from low to high, the NMOS turns on and discharges $C_{L}$ from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$ (the 50% point defining $t_{pHL}$). The saturation current of the NMOS is: $$I_{Dn,sat} = K_{n} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^{2}$$ For $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$: $$I_{Dn,sat} = K_{n} (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Charge conservation on the load capacitor:** $$C_{L} \cdot \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -I_{Dn}$$ **Step 3 — Integrate from $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ to $V_{out} = V_{DD}/2$:** $$t_{pHL} = \int_{V_{DD}}^{V_{DD}/2} \dfrac{C_{L} \, dV_{out}}{I_{Dn}} = \dfrac{C_{L}}{I_{Dn,sat}} \int_{V_{DD}}^{V_{DD}/2} dV_{out}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:** $$t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_{L} \cdot \left(V_{DD} - \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}\right)}{I_{Dn,sat}} = \dfrac{C_{L} \cdot \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}}{K_{n}(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^{2}}$$ $$\boxed{t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \, K_{n} \, (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^{2}}}$$ **Step 5 — Average propagation delay (symmetric inverter assumption $t_{pLH} \approx t_{pHL}$):** $$t_{p} = \dfrac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2} \approx t_{pHL}$$ --- ### 🔬 Derivation 3: Maximum Clock Frequency ($f_{max}$) from the Timing Budget The longest combinational delay between two flip-flops sets the upper limit on clock frequency. **Step 1 — Identify the four components of one clock period:** $$T_{clk} = t_{c\text{-}q} + t_{logic} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$$ where: - $t_{c\text{-}q}$ = Clock-to-Q delay of the launching flip-flop - $t_{logic}$ = Propagation delay of the combinational logic (critical path) - $t_{setup}$ = Setup time requirement of the capturing flip-flop - $t_{skew}$ = Worst-case clock skew between launching and capturing flip-flops **Step 2 — Invert to get maximum frequency:** $$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk, min}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{c\text{-}q} + t_{logic} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** If a problem gives $t_{c\text{-}q} = 0.2$ ns, $t_{logic} = 1.5$ ns, $t_{setup} = 0.1$ ns, $t_{skew} = 0.05$ ns, then $T_{clk,min} = 1.85$ ns and $f_{max} \approx 540.5$ MHz. **Show every substitution step-by-step — this is where most students lose partial marks.** --- ### 🔬 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Patterns in KTU Exams | Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equation to Use | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Dynamic Power | $C_{L}$, $V_{DD}$, $f_{clk}$, $\alpha$ | $P_{dynamic}$ | $P = \alpha C V^{2} f$ | | Average Delay | $t_{pHL}$, $t_{pLH}$ | $t_{p}$ | $t_{p} = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH})/2$ | | Max Frequency | $t_{c\text{-}q}$, $t_{logic}$, $t_{setup}$ | $f_{max}$ | $f_{max} = 1/(t_{c\text{-}q} + t_{logic} + t_{setup})$ | | Slack Calculation | $T_{required}$, $T_{actual}$ | Slack | $Slack = T_{required} - T_{actual}$ | | Fault Coverage | Detected faults, Total faults | Coverage % | $FC = (\text{detected}/\text{total}) \times 100$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 📊 Diagram 1: VLSI Design Flow — Position of Logic Evaluation This Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete RTL-to-GDSII design flow, highlighting the precise position of the Logic Evaluation stage. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["RTL Design
Verilog or VHDL Source"] node2["Logic Synthesis
Translation plus Optimization plus Mapping"] node3["Gate Level Netlist
Technology Mapped"] node4["LOGIC EVALUATION
The Critical Quality Gate"] node5a["Functional Verification"] node5b["Static Timing Analysis"] node5c["Power Analysis"] node5d["Area Analysis"] node5e["Testability Analysis DFT"] node6{"All Constraints
Satisfied?"} node7["Physical Design
Place and Route"] node8["Fabrication
Tape Out to Foundry"] node9["Re-Synthesize
Modify Constraints"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5a node4 --> node5b node4 --> node5c node4 --> node5d node4 --> node5e node5a --> node6 node5b --> node6 node5c --> node6 node5d --> node6 node5e --> node6 node6 -- YES --> node7 node6 -- NO --> node9 node9 --> node2 node7 --> node8 ``` --- ### 📊 Diagram 2: Logic Evaluation Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture This block diagram maps the internal sub-modules of the Logic Evaluation stage and their interactions. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Gate Level Netlist
Input from Synthesis"] nodeB["Testbench plus Stimulus Vectors"] nodeC["Gate Level Simulator
With SDF Back Annotation"] nodeD["Static Timing
Analysis Engine"] nodeE["Power Estimator
Toggle Based or Probabilistic"] nodeF["Area Report Generator"] nodeG["ATPG plus Fault Simulator"] nodeH["Logic Evaluation
Consolidated Dashboard"] nodeI["PPA plus Testability
Final Report"] nodeJ["Pass or Fail
Sign Off Status"] nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeH nodeD --> nodeH nodeE --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeJ ``` --- ### 📊 Diagram 3: Static Timing Analysis — Setup and Hold Time Concept This diagram visualizes the launch-capture timing relationship and where violations occur. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeP1["Launch Flip Flop FF1
Clock Edge at T0"] nodeP2["Combinational Logic
Critical Path Delay t_logic"] nodeP3["Capture Flip Flop FF2
Clock Edge at T0 plus T_clk"] nodeP4["t_cq plus t_logic
Data Arrival Time"] nodeP5["T_clk minus t_setup
Data Required Time"] nodeP6{"Slack greater than
or equal to Zero?"} nodeP7["Timing MET
Setup Pass"] nodeP8["Timing VIOLATION
Reduce t_logic or Increase T_clk"] nodeP1 --> nodeP2 nodeP2 --> nodeP3 nodeP3 --> nodeP4 nodeP3 --> nodeP5 nodeP4 --> nodeP6 nodeP5 --> nodeP6 nodeP6 -- YES --> nodeP7 nodeP6 -- NO --> nodeP8 ``` --- ### 📊 Diagram 4: Power Dissipation Components in CMOS A hierarchical breakdown of the total power budget in a modern CMOS circuit. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeX1["Total Power P_total"] nodeX2["Dynamic Power P_dynamic"] nodeX3["Static Power P_static"] nodeX4["Short Circuit Power P_sc"] nodeX5["Switching Power
alpha C V squared f"] nodeX6["Glitching Power
Internal Node Hazards"] nodeX7["Subthreshold Leakage"] nodeX8["Gate Oxide Tunneling"] nodeX9["Reverse Biased Junction"] nodeX10["Crowbar Current
During Input Transition"] nodeX1 --> nodeX2 nodeX1 --> nodeX3 nodeX1 --> nodeX4 nodeX2 --> nodeX5 nodeX2 --> nodeX6 nodeX3 --> nodeX7 nodeX3 --> nodeX8 nodeX3 --> nodeX9 nodeX4 --> nodeX10 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define Logic Evaluation. List and briefly explain any four key metrics used to evaluate a synthesized VLSI circuit.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Logic Evaluation is the post-synthesis phase of the VLSI design flow where the gate-level netlist is rigorously analyzed to verify that it satisfies all design constraints before proceeding to physical design. **Four Key Metrics (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Performance (Timing):** Measured by propagation delay $t_p$ and maximum operating frequency $f_{max}$. Determined via Static Timing Analysis (STA). A design must meet all setup and hold time constraints. 2. **Power Dissipation:** The total power consumption $P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$. Critical for battery-powered and thermal-limited applications. $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$. 3. **Area:** The total silicon real estate occupied by all standard cells, reported in $\mu m^2$ or gate equivalents (GE). Smaller area reduces per-die fabrication cost. 4. **Testability:** Quantified as fault coverage percentage, typically obtained via ATPG. Industry standard demands $\geq 95\%$ stuck-at fault coverage; safety-critical applications demand $\geq 99\%$. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** *Differentiate between Static Power Dissipation and Dynamic Power Dissipation in CMOS circuits. Which one dominates in modern nanometer technologies and why?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Static Power | Dynamic Power | |:---|:---|:---| | **Cause** | Leakage currents in OFF transistors | Capacitor charging/discharging during switching | | **Formula** | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | | **Dependence** | Present even when circuit is idle | Proportional to switching activity and clock frequency | | **Dominance Era** | Dominant in sub-90 nm (e.g., 7 nm, 5 nm) nodes | Dominant in older nodes (above 180 nm) | **Dominance in Modern Technologies (1 Mark):** In modern nanometer CMOS technologies (sub-65 nm), **static power dominates** because threshold voltage $V_T$ is aggressively scaled down to maintain performance, which exponentially increases subthreshold leakage current $I_{leak}$. Additionally, gate oxide tunneling leakage becomes significant as oxide thickness approaches 1–2 nm. --- ### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the Logic Evaluation process in detail, listing all the major analysis steps with their purpose and pass criteria. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With the help of necessary equations and derivation steps, compute the dynamic power dissipation of a CMOS circuit with the following specifications: $C_L = 50$ fF, $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $f_{clk} = 500$ MHz, switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$. Also calculate the static power if $I_{leak} = 10$ $\mu$A. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question A #### **Part (a) — Logic Evaluation Process (7 Marks)** Logic Evaluation is executed in the following five sequential steps, each with a specific purpose and pass criterion: **Step 1 — Functional Verification (1.5 Marks):** - **Purpose:** To ensure the synthesized gate-level netlist produces the same logical output as the original RTL for all input combinations. - **Method:** Gate-level simulation is performed using the same testbench used for RTL verification, but the netlist is back-annotated with Standard Delay Format (SDF) timing information. - **Pass Criterion:** Output vectors of the gate-level simulation must match the golden RTL output vectors bit-for-bit for $100\%$ of the applied test patterns. **Step 2 — Static Timing Analysis (STA) (1.5 Marks):** - **Purpose:** To verify that all signal paths in the design meet the timing constraints (clock period, setup, and hold). - **Method:** The STA engine breaks the design into **timing paths** (data path from launching FF to capturing FF). For each path, it computes the **data arrival time** and **required arrival time**, then calculates the **slack**. - **Pass Criterion:** Slack $\geq 0$ on every path. Slack = Required Arrival Time − Actual Arrival Time. **Step 3 — Power Analysis (1.5 Marks):** - **Purpose:** To estimate the total power consumption and verify it is within the power budget. - **Method:** Power is estimated using toggle rates (from SAIF/FSDB files) or probabilistic methods. The tool computes switching power, internal power, and leakage power for every cell and sums them. - **Pass Criterion:** Total power $P_{total} \leq P_{budget}$ specified in the design constraints (SDC file). **Step 4 — Area Analysis (1 Mark):** - **Purpose:** To estimate silicon area and ensure the design fits within the die size budget. - **Method:** The synthesis tool reports total cell area (combinational + sequential + buffers) from the technology library `.lib` file. - **Pass Criterion:** Total cell area $\leq$ Area constraint (e.g., 1 mm² or 100k gate equivalents). **Step 5 — Testability Analysis (1.5 Marks):** - **Purpose:** To ensure manufacturing defects (stuck-at faults, bridges, opens) can be detected by automated test equipment. - **Method:** Scan chains are inserted into flip-flops, and the ATPG tool generates test patterns. A fault simulator computes the **stuck-at fault coverage**. - **Pass Criterion:** Fault coverage $\geq 95\%$ (consumer ICs) or $\geq 99\%$ (aerospace, automotive, medical). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. Students often **omit the pass criterion** for each step. This costs 0.5–1 mark per step. Always state the numerical threshold. > 2. Confusing **STA (static, no vectors)** with **gate-level simulation (dynamic, vector-based)**. These are **two distinct steps**, not the same. > 3. Forgetting that **DFT (Design for Test)** is a prerequisite for ATPG — you cannot run ATPG on a design without scan chains. --- #### **Part (b) — Dynamic Power Computation (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — State the dynamic power formula (1 Mark):** $$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$ **Step 2 — List the given values with units (1 Mark):** - $C_L = 50 \text{ fF} = 50 \times 10^{-15}$ F - $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V - $f_{clk} = 500 \text{ MHz} = 500 \times 10^{6}$ Hz - $\alpha = 0.2$ (dimensionless) **Step 3 — Substitute into the formula (2 Marks):** $$P_{dynamic} = 0.2 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.2)^{2} \times (500 \times 10^{6})$$ **Step 4 — Compute the arithmetic step-by-step (2 Marks):** First, compute $V_{DD}^{2} = (1.2)^2 = 1.44 \text{ V}^2$. Then: $$P_{dynamic} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 500 \times 10^{6}$$ Combine coefficients: $$P_{dynamic} = (0.2 \times 50 \times 1.44 \times 500) \times 10^{-15} \times 10^{6}$$ $$P_{dynamic} = (0.2 \times 50 \times 1.44 \times 500) \times 10^{-9}$$ Compute the numerical coefficient: $$0.2 \times 50 = 10$$ $$10 \times 1.44 = 14.4$$ $$14.4 \times 500 = 7200$$ $$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = 7200 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W} = 7.2 \text{ } \mu\text{W}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the static power (1 Mark):** $$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} = 1.2 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 12 \text{ } \mu\text{W}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the total power and interpret (Bonus for completeness):** $$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} = 7.2 + 12 = 19.2 \text{ } \mu\text{W}$$ **Observation:** In this low-frequency example, static power actually exceeds dynamic power — a hallmark of modern low-power designs where the circuit is mostly idle. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain Static Timing Analysis (STA) in detail. Derive the equation for maximum clock frequency and explain the concepts of setup time and hold time with a clear timing diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A digital circuit has the following timing parameters: $t_{c\text{-}q} = 0.3$ ns, $t_{logic} = 2.0$ ns, $t_{setup} = 0.2$ ns, $t_{skew} = 0.1$ ns. Calculate the maximum clock frequency. If the combinational delay increases by $30\%$, what is the new $f_{max}$? Comment on the impact of clock skew reduction. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question B #### **Part (a) — Static Timing Analysis (7 Marks)** **Definition (1 Mark):** Static Timing Analysis (STA) is a vector-less timing verification method that exhaustively checks every possible timing path in a digital circuit against its timing constraints, without requiring input stimulus vectors. **Need for STA (1 Mark):** Modern ASICs contain billions of gates. Dynamic gate-level simulation is computationally infeasible (would take months). STA analyzes all paths in polynomial time using graph algorithms (Pessimism Removal, Critical Path Analysis), making it the **industry-standard timing sign-off methodology**. **STA Methodology (2 Marks):** 1. The circuit is represented as a **timing graph** — nodes are pins of cells, edges are timing arcs (gates + interconnect). 2. For each timing path (launching FF → combinational logic → capturing FF), STA computes: - **Data Arrival Time (DAT):** The earliest time the data signal reaches the capture FF's D-pin. - **Data Required Time (DRT):** The latest time the data must arrive to meet the setup constraint. 3. **Slack = DRT − DAT.** If slack $\geq 0$ on all paths, the design passes STA. **Setup Time Concept (1.5 Marks):** Setup time $t_{setup}$ is the **minimum time** the data must be stable at the D-input of a flip-flop **before** the active clock edge. If violated, the flip-flop enters a metastable state and may capture incorrect data. **Hold Time Concept (1.5 Marks):** Hold time $t_{hold}$ is the **minimum time** the data must remain stable at the D-input **after** the active clock edge. A hold violation causes a race condition where the new data overwrites the captured value before it is safely latched. **Maximum Clock Frequency Derivation:** The clock period must satisfy: $$T_{clk} \geq t_{c\text{-}q} + t_{logic} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$$ For minimum clock period: $$T_{clk,min} = t_{c\text{-}q} + t_{logic} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{c\text{-}q} + t_{logic} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}}}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — List the given values (0.5 Mark):** - $t_{c\text{-}q} = 0.3$ ns - $t_{logic} = 2.0$ ns - $t_{setup} = 0.2$ ns - $t_{skew} = 0.1$ ns **Step 2 — Calculate $T_{clk,min}$ (1 Mark):** $$T_{clk,min} = 0.3 + 2.0 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 2.6 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate original $f_{max}$ (1 Mark):** $$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2.6 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{10^{9}}{2.6}$$ $$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 384.6 \text{ MHz}}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate new $t_{logic}$ after 30% increase (1 Mark):** $$t_{logic,new} = 2.0 \times 1.30 = 2.6 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate new $T_{clk,min}$ (1 Mark):** $$T_{clk,new} = 0.3 + 2.6 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 3.2 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 6 — Calculate new $f_{max}$ (1 Mark):** $$f_{max,new} = \dfrac{1}{3.2 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{10^{9}}{3.2}$$ $$\boxed{f_{max,new} = 312.5 \text{ MHz}}$$ **Step 7 — Impact of clock skew reduction (1.5 Marks):** Frequency drop due to logic delay increase: $$\Delta f = 384.6 - 312.5 = 72.1 \text{ MHz} \quad (\approx 18.75\% \text{ reduction})$$ **Clock Skew Reduction Impact:** Clock skew enters the timing equation as a positive additive term in the denominator. Reducing $t_{skew}$ from 0.1 ns to 0 ns (ideal clock tree) would improve $f_{max}$ to: $$f_{max, zero\ skew} = \dfrac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 400 \text{ MHz}$$ This represents a **~4% improvement** in maximum frequency. In high-frequency designs (multi-GHz), even picosecond-level skew reduction yields significant performance gains. This is why modern ASICs invest heavily in **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** with symmetric H-tree or mesh topologies. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** $500 \text{ MHz} = 500 \times 10^6$ Hz, not $500$ Hz. Converting to base units **before** substitution is critical. > 2. Forgetting the $V_{DD}^2$ term — using $V_{DD}$ instead of $V_{DD}^2$ gives a power value off by a factor of $V_{DD}$ (here, 1.2×). This is the **#1 mark-losing mistake** in power problems. > 3. **Setup vs. Hold confusion:** Setup limits $f_{max}$ (long-path check), Hold is a race condition (short-path check). They are checked by different STA commands. > 4. Not stating the **direction of slack** explicitly: positive slack = pass, negative slack = violation. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Logic Evaluation** is the post-synthesis gate-level verification and analysis stage in the VLSI design flow. - ✅ The **5-step process** is: Functional Verification → STA → Power Analysis → Area Analysis → Testability (DFT/ATPG). - ✅ The **dynamic power equation** is $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ — note the **quadratic $V_{DD}$ dependence**. - ✅ **Static power** in modern sub-90 nm nodes **dominates** over dynamic power due to subthreshold leakage. - ✅ **Average propagation delay** is $t_p = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH})/2$. - ✅ **Maximum clock frequency** is $f_{max} = 1 / (t_{c\text{-}q} + t_{logic} + t_{setup} + t_{skew})$. - ✅ **Setup time** $t_{setup}$ is the data-stable time **before** the clock edge; **Hold time** $t_{hold}$ is the data-stable time **after** the clock edge. - ✅ **Slack** = Required Arrival Time − Actual Arrival Time. Positive slack = timing pass; Negative slack = violation. - ✅ **Fault coverage** target: $\geq 95\%$ for consumer ICs, $\geq 99\%$ for safety-critical (aerospace, automotive, medical). - ✅ The **PPA trade-off** is the central design challenge — improving one metric almost always degrades another. - ✅ **KTU Board Favourite Numerical:** $P_{dynamic}$ calculation with given $C_L$, $V_{DD}$, $f_{clk}$, and $\alpha$ (expect 5–7 marks in Part B). - ✅ **EDP (Energy-Delay Product)** is the figure of merit for low-power, high-performance optimization. - ✅ **Tools of the trade:** Synopsys PrimeTime (STA), Design Compiler (synthesis + area), Power Compiler (power), TetraMAX (ATPG).

topics.title: Issues in Dynamic Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Issues in Dynamic Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Dynamic CMOS Logic** is a digital circuit design style that uses a clock signal to control the precharge and evaluation phases of operation, rather than relying on a static pull-up/pull-down network to maintain logic levels. In dynamic logic, the output is stored on a parasitic capacitance rather than being actively driven by a steady-state current path. While this style offers superior speed, reduced transistor count, and zero static power dissipation compared to static CMOS, it suffers from several critical reliability and signal-integrity issues that must be carefully managed.

The **Issues in Dynamic Logic** refer to the inherent non-idealities that compromise the correct operation of dynamic gates, including:

1. **Charge Sharing** between the output node and internal parasitic capacitances.
2. **Clock Feedthrough** due to capacitive coupling between clock and signal lines.
3. **Leakage Current** discharging the output node during the evaluation phase.
4. **Cascading (Monotonicity) Problem** preventing direct connection of dynamic gates.
5. **Race Conditions** between cascaded dynamic stages.
6. **Reduced Noise Margins** due to the dynamic nature of the storage node.
7. **Power Dissipation** issues including glitching and clock loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PBECT604-VLSI Circuit Design explicitly addresses the **Issues in Dynamic Logic** as a mandatory topic. Mastering these pitfalls is essential for designing reliable high-performance VLSI systems and forms a recurring question in end-semester examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank with a single input valve and an unpredictable leak**.

- The **water tank** is the output capacitance ($C_L$) of a dynamic gate that stores charge (the water) representing logic '1'.
- The **input valve** (controlled by NMOS transistors) decides whether fresh water (ground or VDD) flows in.
- The **unpredictable leak** is the leakage current that slowly drains the tank even when the valve is closed.

Now, here's the catch: the tank is also connected to a **hidden side pipe** that is shared with the neighbor's tank. If both tanks are interconnected for even a moment, the water levels equalize — this is **charge sharing**. The original logic value can be destroyed, similar to how adding a 4-bit value to a 64-bit value in a register can overflow the result.

This analogy makes the core problem clear: **dynamic logic stores information on fragile capacitive nodes**, which are vulnerable to leakage, coupling, and charge redistribution — making them less robust than static CMOS but significantly faster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for Robustness:** The figure of merit is typically the **Noise Margin** (NM). For dynamic logic, the low-to-high noise margin ($NM_L$) is theoretically 0 because the output makes only a 0→1 transition during evaluation. This is the fundamental reason behind the monotonicity restriction.

---

## 1.3 Why Study These Issues?

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic maps to **Course Outcome CO3** of PBECT604: *"Design and analyze CMOS combinational and sequential circuits including dynamic logic structures."* Understanding these issues is a **mandatory cognitive level (Apply / Analyze)** requirement under **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**, forming the basis for design-oriented 14-mark questions.

---

## 1.4 Summary Callout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** Dynamic logic trades robustness for speed. Every issue we will study is essentially a manifestation of the **fragility of charge stored on a capacitor** in a leaky, coupled, charge-sharing silicon environment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Issue 1: Charge Sharing Problem

### 2.1.1 Mechanism

During the **precharge phase** ($\phi = 0$), the output node capacitance $C_L$ is charged to $V_{DD}$. During the **evaluation phase** ($\phi = 1$), the NMOS pull-down network conditionally discharges the output to ground. However, internal nodes $X$ inside the pull-down network (e.g., between two series NMOS transistors) start with an **unknown voltage** — typically $0\text{ V}$ if precharged by the previous stage, or a **floating value** if it is a true internal node.

If the input combination causes a conducting path from the output node to an internal node $X$ with capacitance $C_X$, **charge redistribution** occurs. The total charge is conserved, but the voltage drops because charge spreads over a larger capacitance.

### 2.1.2 Analytical Formulation

**Charge Sharing Equation (Most Critical Formula):**

$$
V_{out,final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_X}
$$

This is derived from charge conservation: the total charge before sharing equals the total charge after sharing.

**Critical Threshold Condition:**

A common failure criterion is that $V_{out,final}$ must remain above the threshold voltage $V_{Tn}$ of the next-stage NMOS transistor to avoid a logic error:

$$
V_{out,final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_X} > V_{Tn}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
C_X < C_L \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{V_{Tn}} - 1\right)
$$

If $C_X$ exceeds this bound, the output is erroneously interpreted as '0' when it should be '1'.

### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility

In modern sub-32nm technologies, internal node capacitances can be a significant fraction of $C_L$ due to narrow transistors and dense routing. Charge sharing is a **dominant reliability concern** in 6T-SRAM read paths and dynamic content-addressable memories (CAMs).

---

## 2.2 Issue 2: Clock Feedthrough (Capacitive Coupling)

### 2.2.1 Mechanism

The gate-to-drain overlap capacitance $C_{gd}$ of the precharge PMOS transistor couples the clock transitions directly to the output node. When $\phi$ falls from $V_{DD}$ to $0$, a portion of this voltage change appears at the output node through the gate-drain capacitance of the PMOS precharge transistor.

### 2.2.2 Voltage Error Formula

$$
\Delta V_{out} = \frac{C_{gd}}{C_{gd} + C_L} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

This effectively **lowers the precharged voltage below $V_{DD}$**, reducing noise margin. With aggressive device scaling, $C_L$ shrinks while $C_{gd}$ does not scale proportionally, making this error more severe.

---

## 2.3 Issue 3: Subthreshold Leakage

### 2.3.1 Mechanism

During evaluation, the pull-down network is OFF for inputs that should keep the output at '1'. However, subthreshold conduction ($I_{off}$) through these nominally-OFF NMOS transistors slowly discharges $C_L$. Over time, especially for **wide OR-structures** with many parallel paths or **long clock cycles**, $V_{out}$ can droop below $V_{Tn}$ of the next stage, causing a logic failure.

### 2.3.2 Leakage Discharge Time

$$
\tau_{discharge} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{Tn}}{I_{leak}}
$$

For modern deep-submicron processes, $I_{leak}$ can be $\mu$A to nA range, while $C_L$ is fF, giving $\tau$ in $\mu$s to ms — short enough to be problematic in modern GHz clocked designs.

---

## 2.4 Issue 4: Cascading and Monotonicity Problem

### 2.4.1 Fundamental Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monotonicity Requirement:** Inputs to a dynamic gate must be **monotonic** during the evaluation phase — they must change **only from 0→1** and never from 1→0.

This is because a dynamic gate output makes **only a 0→1 transition during evaluation** (or remains at 0). If a 1→0 transition is applied to a dynamic gate's input, the gate may erroneously **re-evaluate** and produce a wrong '0' on an output that should stay at '1'.

### 2.4.2 Cascading Failure

A dynamic gate output can only drive a **static CMOS gate** safely. Connecting two dynamic gates in series causes a 1→0 transition at the second gate's input when the first gate's output transitions to 0, violating monotonicity.

**Solutions:**

| Solution | Description | Cost |
|----------|-------------|------|
| **Domino Logic** | Add a static CMOS inverter at the output of each dynamic stage | +2 transistors/stage |
| **np-CMOS Logic** | Alternate NMOS-only and PMOS-only dynamic stages | Same as Domino |
| **Multiple-Output Domino** | Generate true and complementary outputs | Larger area |

---

## 2.5 Issue 5: Race Condition in Cascaded Dynamic Logic

When two dynamic stages are connected through a static inverter (Domino), the propagation delay must be carefully controlled. If the second stage evaluates **before the first stage's output has settled**, the second stage may evaluate based on stale data. This is mitigated by ensuring that the **evaluation time exceeds the worst-case combinational delay** of the preceding stage.

---

## 2.6 Issue 6: Power Dissipation in Dynamic Logic

Dynamic logic has **zero static power** but higher dynamic power than static CMOS due to:

- **Clock loading on every gate** ($\alpha = 1$ switching activity on the clock node).
- **Glitching power** during evaluation as signals propagate.
- **Short-circuit power** during the brief precharge-evaluate overlap (negligible).

The dominant power component is the **switching power** of charging and discharging $C_L$ every clock cycle.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| $V_{out,final} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_X}$ | Charge sharing output voltage | Volts (V) |
| $C_X < C_L \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{DD}}{V_{Tn}} - 1\right)$ | Charge sharing safe bound | Farads (F) |
| $\Delta V_{out} = \dfrac{C_{gd}}{C_{gd} + C_L} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Clock feedthrough voltage error | Volts (V) |
| $\tau_{discharge} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{Tn}}{I_{leak}}$ | Leakage discharge time constant | Seconds (s) |
| $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Dynamic power dissipation | Watts (W) |
| $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot t_{delay}}$ | Maximum operating frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| $I_{avg} = C_L \cdot \dfrac{V_{DD}}{T_{clk}}$ | Average charging/discharging current | Amperes (A) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The charge sharing equation $V_{out,final} = (C_L \cdot V_{DD})/(C_L + C_X)$ is the single most-tested formula in the VLSI design module. Be prepared to derive it from charge conservation and apply it to numerical problems.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Charge Sharing Voltage Drop

### Problem Setup
A dynamic NAND2 gate has output capacitance $C_L = 50\text{ fF}$. The internal node $X$ between the two series NMOS transistors has a parasitic capacitance $C_X = 30\text{ fF}$ and is initially at $0\text{ V}$. During precharge, the output is charged to $V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$. During evaluation, both inputs go HIGH, creating a path from the output node to node $X$. Calculate the final output voltage and determine if the logic level is preserved.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify Initial Conditions**

Before the conducting path forms:
- Output node voltage: $V_{out,initial} = V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$
- Internal node voltage: $V_X = 0\text{ V}$

**Step 2: Apply Charge Conservation**

The total charge on the system is conserved because no current path to ground or supply exists:

$$
Q_{initial} = Q_{final}
$$

**Step 3: Express Initial Charge**

$$
Q_{initial} = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_X \cdot V_X
$$

Substituting values:

$$
Q_{initial} = 50\text{ fF} \cdot 1.8\text{ V} + 30\text{ fF} \cdot 0\text{ V}
$$

$$
Q_{initial} = 90\text{ fC} + 0\text{ fC} = 90\text{ fC}
$$

**Step 4: Express Final Charge**

After charge sharing, both nodes reach a common voltage $V_{final}$:

$$
Q_{final} = (C_L + C_X) \cdot V_{final}
$$

**Step 5: Equate and Solve**

$$
(C_L + C_X) \cdot V_{final} = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_X \cdot V_X
$$

$$
V_{final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_X \cdot V_X}{C_L + C_X}
$$

With $V_X = 0$:

$$
V_{final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_X}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
V_{final} = \frac{50\text{ fF} \cdot 1.8\text{ V}}{50\text{ fF} + 30\text{ fF}}
$$

$$
V_{final} = \frac{90\text{ fC}}{80\text{ fF}} = 1.125\text{ V}
$$

**Step 7: Verify Logic Integrity**

Assuming a typical NMOS threshold $V_{Tn} = 0.5\text{ V}$:

$$
V_{final} = 1.125\text{ V} > V_{Tn} = 0.5\text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

The logic level is preserved. However, if $C_X$ were $300\text{ fF}$ instead, $V_{final} = 0.257\text{ V}$, which would fail.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Clock Feedthrough Voltage Error

### Problem Setup
A dynamic logic gate has output capacitance $C_L = 20\text{ fF}$ and the precharge PMOS has gate-drain overlap capacitance $C_{gd} = 2\text{ fF}$. Calculate the clock feedthrough voltage error when the clock transitions from $V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$ to $0\text{ V}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: State the Feedthrough Equation**

The voltage change at the output node due to capacitive coupling is given by:

$$
\Delta V_{out} = \frac{C_{gd}}{C_{gd} + C_L} \cdot \Delta V_{clock}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the Clock Swing**

$$
\Delta V_{clock} = V_{DD} - 0 = V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$
\Delta V_{out} = \frac{2\text{ fF}}{2\text{ fF} + 20\text{ fF}} \cdot 1.8\text{ V}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Ratio**

$$
\frac{C_{gd}}{C_{gd} + C_L} = \frac{2}{22} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909
$$

**Step 5: Final Calculation**

$$
\Delta V_{out} = 0.0909 \cdot 1.8\text{ V} = 0.1636\text{ V}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Final Output Voltage**

$$
V_{out,actual} = V_{DD} - \Delta V_{out} = 1.8 - 0.1636 = 1.636\text{ V}
$$

The output precharge voltage is reduced by approximately **9.1%**, slightly degrading the noise margin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Safe Internal Capacitance

### Problem Setup
Given $C_L = 50\text{ fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$, and $V_{Tn} = 0.5\text{ V}$, derive the maximum allowable $C_X$ that does not cause a logic error.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: State the Failure Criterion**

For the output to be valid logic '1':

$$
V_{final} \geq V_{Tn}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Charge Sharing Equation**

$$
\frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_X} \geq V_{Tn}
$$

**Step 3: Cross-Multiply**

$$
C_L \cdot V_{DD} \geq V_{Tn} \cdot (C_L + C_X)
$$

**Step 4: Expand and Rearrange**

$$
C_L \cdot V_{DD} \geq V_{Tn} \cdot C_L + V_{Tn} \cdot C_X
$$

$$
C_L \cdot V_{DD} - V_{Tn} \cdot C_L \geq V_{Tn} \cdot C_X
$$

$$
C_L \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) \geq V_{Tn} \cdot C_X
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $C_X$**

$$
C_X \leq \frac{C_L \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}{V_{Tn}}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
C_X \leq \frac{50\text{ fF} \cdot (1.8 - 0.5)}{0.5} = \frac{50 \cdot 1.3}{0.5}\text{ fF}
$$

$$
C_X \leq 130\text{ fF}
$$

Therefore, the maximum safe internal capacitance is **130 fF**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Issues vs. Solutions

| **Issue** | **Root Cause** | **Primary Solution** | **Trade-off** |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Charge Sharing | Charge redistribution to internal nodes | Precharge internal node via small PMOS (bleeder) | Adds capacitance, slows precharge |
| Clock Feedthrough | $C_{gd}$ coupling of clock to output | Use clocked-CMOS (C²MOS) or differential logic | Higher complexity |
| Leakage Current | Subthreshold conduction | Add **keeper transistor** (weak PMOS feedback) | Competes with pull-down network |
| Cascading Problem | Monotonicity violation | Domino logic (add static inverter) | +2 transistors per stage |
| Race Condition | Asymmetric delays in cascade | Conservative clock-to-Q timing | Lower maximum frequency |
| Noise Margin | Floating output node | Use keepers or pseudo-static logic | Reduced drive strength |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Dynamic Logic Gate with All Issues

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph phaseA[PRECHARGE PHASE CLK equal 0]
        clockLow["CLOCK LOW"]
        prechTrx["PMOS PRECHARGE ON"]
        outNode["OUTPUT NODE CL charged to VDD"]
        clockLow --> prechTrx --> outNode
    end

    subgraph phaseB[EVALUATION PHASE CLK equal 1]
        clockHigh["CLOCK HIGH"]
        prechOff["PMOS PRECHARGE OFF"]
        evalNet["NMOS PULL-DOWN NETWORK EVALUATES"]
        clockHigh --> prechOff
        evalNet --> outNode
    end

    subgraph issueBox[ISSUES DURING OPERATION]
        issue1["CHARGE SHARING: C L to C X redistribution"]
        issue2["CLOCK FEEDTHROUGH: Cgd coupling error"]
        issue3["LEAKAGE: Subthreshold discharge of C L"]
        issue4["CASCADING: Monotonicity violation"]
        issue5["RACE: Eval window timing mismatch"]
        issue1 --> issue2 --> issue3 --> issue4 --> issue5
    end

    subgraph solBox[STANDARD MITIGATIONS]
        sol1["Precharge internal node via small PMOS"]
        sol2["Keeper transistor weak PMOS feedback"]
        sol3["Domino logic static inverter output"]
        sol4["Clocked CMOS C2MOS topology"]
        sol5["Differential cascode voltage switch logic"]
        sol1 --> sol2 --> sol3 --> sol4 --> sol5
    end

    outNode --> issueBox
    issueBox --> solBox
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Charge Sharing Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CL Precharged to VDD"] -->|Conducting NMOS Path| B["Cx Internal Node initially 0V"]
    A -->|Charge Redistribution| C["Vout final = CL VDD divided by CL plus Cx"]
    B --> C
    C --> D{"Vout final greater than Vtn"}
    D -->|YES| E["Logic 1 Preserved"]
    D -->|NO| F["Logic Failure Output drops below threshold"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Cascading Problem and Domino Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dyn1["Dynamic Gate 1 Output transitions 1 to 0"] -->|WRONG: Monotonicity Violation| dyn2["Dynamic Gate 2 Re-evaluates incorrectly"]
    dyn1 -->|CORRECT: Domino Solution| inv["Static CMOS Inverter Buffer"]
    inv -->|Monotonic 0 to 1 signal| dyn2next["Dynamic Gate 2 Evaluates correctly"]
    dyn2 -.->|PROBLEM| failX["Logic Error X"]
    dyn2next -->|SOLUTION| passX["Correct Operation X"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Dynamic Logic Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Input Vector A B C D"] --> prech["Precharge Phase: All Outputs to VDD via PMOS Network"]
    prech --> eval["Evaluation Phase: NMOS Network Pulls Down Selected Outputs"]
    eval --> chargeShare{"Charge Sharing Occur"}
    chargeShare -->|YES| dropV["Output Voltage Drops by delta V"]
    chargeShare -->|NO| holdV["Output Voltage Held at VDD"]
    dropV --> keeper{"Keeper Transistor Present"}
    holdV --> keeper
    keeper -->|YES| restore["Restores Lost Charge"]
    keeper -->|NO| corr["Possible Logic Corruption"]
    restore --> out["Valid Logic Output"]
    corr --> out
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Charge Sharing in Dynamic CMOS Logic. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Charge sharing is a reliability issue in dynamic CMOS logic where the precharged charge on the output node capacitance $C_L$ is redistributed to an internal node capacitance $C_X$ when a conducting path is formed through the NMOS pull-down network during the evaluation phase. This causes the output voltage to drop from $V_{DD}$ to a value given by:

$$
V_{out,final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_X}
$$

If this reduced voltage falls below the threshold voltage $V_{Tn}$ of the next stage, a logic error occurs, corrupting the output.

---

### Question 2: Why is Cascading of Dynamic Logic Gates Directly Not Possible? [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Direct cascading of dynamic logic gates is not possible because dynamic gates require **monotonic input signals** during the evaluation phase. The output of a dynamic gate makes only a **0→1 transition** during evaluation (or remains at 0). When this output drives the next dynamic gate, a **1→0 transition** can be presented to the next gate's input, violating the monotonicity rule and causing erroneous re-evaluation. The standard solution is to use **Domino Logic**, where a static CMOS inverter is added at the output of each dynamic stage, converting the 1→0 transition into a 0→1 transition that is monotonic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry **7 marks each**. Complete, exhaustive model solutions are provided below.

---

### Question A: Charge Sharing Analysis in Dynamic Logic [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** A 4-input dynamic NAND gate has an output capacitance of $C_L = 80\text{ fF}$. During evaluation, a conducting path connects the output to an internal node with capacitance $C_X = 40\text{ fF}$ that was precharged to $0\text{ V}$. If $V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$ and the threshold voltage of the next-stage NMOS is $V_{Tn} = 0.45\text{ V}$, calculate the final output voltage and determine whether the logic level is correctly preserved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **clock feedthrough** phenomenon in dynamic CMOS logic. Derive the expression for the resulting voltage error at the output node and discuss two standard techniques to mitigate it. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Apply the charge conservation principle.**

Initial state: Output node at $V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$, internal node at $0\text{ V}$.

**Step 2: Write the charge conservation equation.**

$$
C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_X \cdot 0 = (C_L + C_X) \cdot V_{final}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $V_{final}$.**

$$
V_{final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_X} = \frac{80\text{ fF} \cdot 1.8\text{ V}}{80\text{ fF} + 40\text{ fF}}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate numerically.**

$$
V_{final} = \frac{144\text{ fC}}{120\text{ fF}} = 1.2\text{ V}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with $V_{Tn}$.**

$$
V_{final} = 1.2\text{ V} > V_{Tn} = 0.45\text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The logic '1' level is **preserved** with a healthy margin of $0.75\text{ V}$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Clock Feedthrough Explanation:**

Clock feedthrough is the unintended coupling of the clock signal to the output node through the **gate-to-drain overlap capacitance** $C_{gd}$ of the precharge PMOS transistor. When the clock transitions sharply from $V_{DD}$ to $0\text{ V}$, a fraction of this voltage change is injected into the output node, reducing the precharged voltage below $V_{DD}$.

**Derivation of Voltage Error:**

The clock and output node are coupled through $C_{gd}$, while the output node is also connected to ground (or to a driver) through $C_L$. By the charge sharing principle:

$$
C_{gd} \cdot V_{DD} + C_L \cdot V_{DD} = (C_{gd} + C_L) \cdot V_{final}
$$

Wait — more precisely, when the clock falls by $V_{DD}$, the charge injected is $\Delta Q = C_{gd} \cdot V_{DD}$. This charge is sunk by the output capacitance:

$$
\Delta V_{out} = \frac{\Delta Q}{C_L} = \frac{C_{gd}}{C_L} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

A more accurate expression including $C_{gd}$ on the output side:

$$
\Delta V_{out} = \frac{C_{gd}}{C_{gd} + C_L} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

**Two Mitigation Techniques:**

1. **Use of a Clocked-CMOS (C²MOS) Register:** A C²MOS gate uses a clocked NMOS pull-up instead of a static PMOS, eliminating the always-on gate-drain overlap.
2. **Use of Differential / Cascode Voltage Switch (CVSL) Logic:** Differential structures inherently reject common-mode clock feedthrough noise by subtracting it out at the differential output.
3. **Use of a Precharge Equalization Transistor:** Adding a small transistor to precharge critical nodes to a known intermediate level reduces the impact of feedthrough.

---

### Question B: Cascading and Keeper Transistor Design [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of **Domino CMOS Logic**. Discuss how it overcomes the cascading problem of dynamic logic and state any two disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A dynamic logic gate has an output capacitance of $C_L = 60\text{ fF}$ and experiences a subthreshold leakage current of $I_{leak} = 5\text{ nA}$ from the pull-down network. The supply voltage is $V_{DD} = 1.2\text{ V}$ and $V_{Tn} = 0.4\text{ V}$. Calculate the maximum evaluation time before the output voltage droops below $V_{Tn}$. Suggest a circuit modification to mitigate this issue. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Domino CMOS Logic Operation:**

A Domino logic gate consists of a **dynamic CMOS stage** followed by a **static CMOS inverter**. The dynamic stage precharges the output node to $V_{DD}$ during $\phi = 0$ and conditionally discharges it to $0\text{ V}$ during $\phi = 1$. The static inverter inverts this signal, so the **output of the Domino gate** makes a **0→1 transition** during evaluation (or remains at 0).

**How it Overcomes Cascading:**

When two Domino gates are cascaded, the output of the first gate (after the inverter) is a **monotonic 0→1 signal**. This monotonic signal is fed as input to the second dynamic stage, satisfying the monotonicity requirement. The name "Domino" arises because the evaluation propagates like falling dominos — each stage triggers the next in a cascade during the evaluation phase.

**Two Disadvantages:**

1. **Limited to Non-Inverting Logic Only:** Because of the inverting buffer, only non-inverting logic (AND, OR) can be implemented. To implement inverting functions (NAND, NOR), more complex structures are needed.
2. **Charge Sharing and Noise Susceptibility Persist:** The dynamic stage remains vulnerable to charge sharing, leakage, and coupling — the inverter only fixes the cascading issue.
3. **Higher Area and Power:** The additional inverter adds area and contributes to clock loading.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Identify the discharge equation.**

The output voltage droops as charge is removed by leakage:

$$
V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} - \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t}{C_L}
$$

**Step 2: Set the failure condition.**

The output droops below $V_{Tn}$ when:

$$
V_{Tn} = V_{DD} - \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{max}}{C_L}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $t_{max}$.**

$$
t_{max} = \frac{C_L \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}{I_{leak}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
t_{max} = \frac{60\text{ fF} \cdot (1.2\text{ V} - 0.4\text{ V})}{5\text{ nA}} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 0.8}{5 \times 10^{-9}}\text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate.**

$$
t_{max} = \frac{48 \times 10^{-15}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 9.6 \times 10^{-6}\text{ s} = 9.6\text{ }\mu\text{s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum evaluation time is **9.6 $\mu$s** before logic corruption occurs.

**Circuit Modification — Keeper Transistor:**

To mitigate this, a **weak PMOS keeper transistor** is added in positive feedback across the output node and the input of the static inverter. When the output node droops slightly, the keeper turns on, restoring the lost charge. The keeper must be **weak enough** to be overpowered by the pull-down network during normal evaluation, but **strong enough** to compensate for the leakage current.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Charge Sharing:** Students often forget to **include the initial voltage of the internal node** ($V_X$) in the charge conservation equation. The full formula is $V_{final} = (C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_X \cdot V_X)/(C_L + C_X)$, not simply $C_L \cdot V_{DD}/(C_L + C_X)$. Marks are deducted for this omission.
> 2. **Monotonicity:** When explaining cascading, students frequently confuse the output transition direction. Remember: **dynamic gate output goes 0→1 (or stays 0)** during evaluation, and **the static inverter output (Domino) goes 1→0**.
> 3. **Keeper Sizing:** A common error is to over-size the keeper. If the keeper is too strong, it cannot be overpowered by the pull-down network, leading to **failing evaluation**. KTU specifically tests this in 14-mark design questions.
> 4. **Clock Feedthrough Direction:** The error is a **voltage reduction** (the output loses charge), not an increase. State the sign explicitly to avoid losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Issues in Dynamic Logic**

- **Charge Sharing:** $V_{final} = (C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_X \cdot V_X)/(C_L + C_X)$. Caused by conducting path from output to internal node during evaluation.
- **Clock Feedthrough:** $\Delta V_{out} = (C_{gd} \cdot V_{DD})/(C_{gd} + C_L)$. Caused by $C_{gd}$ coupling from clock to output.
- **Leakage:** $\tau_{discharge} = C_L \cdot V_{Tn}/I_{leak}$. Mitigated by **keeper transistor** (weak PMOS in feedback).
- **Monotonicity Rule:** Dynamic gate inputs must change **only 0→1** during evaluation. **1→0 transitions cause re-evaluation errors**.
- **Domino Logic = Dynamic + Static Inverter:** Converts 1→0 output to 0→1 output, enabling safe cascading.
- **np-CMOS Logic:** Alternates NMOS-only and PMOS-only dynamic stages; true and complementary outputs.
- **C²MOS:** Clocked-CMOS, used for low-voltage/low-clock-feedthrough dynamic registers.
- **High-Yield Numbers:** $V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$ or $1.2\text{ V}$, $V_{Tn} \approx 0.4\text{–}0.5\text{ V}$, $C_L = 10\text{–}100\text{ fF}$ typical.
- **No Static Power:** Dynamic logic has **zero static power dissipation**, only dynamic power $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$.
- **Most Tested Concept in KTU:** Charge sharing with numerical problem, followed by monotonicity explanation.
- **Standard Mitigation Table (must memorize):**

| **Issue** | **Mitigation** |
|-----------|----------------|
| Charge sharing | Precharge internal node, keeper |
| Clock feedthrough | C²MOS, differential logic |
| Leakage | Keeper transistor |
| Cascading | Domino, np-CMOS |
| Race | Conservative clock timing |

- **Key Design Trade-off:** Dynamic logic is **faster** and **smaller** than static CMOS but **less robust** to noise, leakage, and charge sharing. Use dynamic logic only on **critical paths** of high-performance designs.
- **Energy / Delay:** Dynamic logic typically achieves $C_{out}$ of 1–2 fF per gate, with delays of **50–200 ps** at 1.8 V in 0.18 $\mu$m technology.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Issues in Dynamic Logic.** These notes are optimized for **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech PBECT604-VLSI Circuit Design** end-semester preparation. Pair these notes with circuit simulation (e.g., LTspice or Cadence Virtuoso) for maximum conceptual clarity. Master the **charge sharing derivation** and the **monotonicity rule** — together they account for over 60% of marks in this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Domino Logic** is a specialized form of **dynamic CMOS logic** that uses a single clock phase and a static CMOS inverter at the output of each dynamic gate to restore logic levels, eliminate the threshold voltage drop problem, and enable the **cascading of multiple dynamic logic stages** without signal degradation. It was introduced to overcome the critical limitations of basic dynamic CMOS logic, namely the lack of fan-out capability, susceptibility to noise, charge-sharing hazards, and the inability to directly cascade gates in a pipelined configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Domino Logic falls under *Dynamic CMOS Logic Families* and is a **high-weightage topic** for ESE (End Semester Examination) Part B questions (14 marks). It is frequently coupled with questions on **NP-Domino (Zipper) Logic** and **Cascade Buffer / Logic Style Comparisons**.

In its canonical form, a Domino Logic gate consists of:
1. A **PMOS precharge transistor** (clocked by $\phi$ or $\overline{\phi}$).
2. An **NMOS pull-down (evaluation) network** implementing the Boolean function.
3. A **clocked NMOS footer (evaluate transistor)** controlled by the complementary clock $\overline{\phi}$ to prevent leakage and control evaluation.
4. A **static CMOS inverter** at the output that inverts the dynamic node voltage and provides rail-to-rail output swing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bucket on a seesaw (a tipping bucket)** suspended above a tank of water:
- During **Precharge (Setup)**: A heavy stone (PMOS) pulls the bucket UP and fills it with a known amount of water (high voltage, logic '1').
- During **Evaluation (Action)**: The stone is released, and a series of levers (NMOS network) may tip the bucket. If all levers are correctly arranged (Boolean condition true), the water pours out (output discharges to '0'). If even one lever is missing, the bucket stays up.
- The **inverter (the pail beneath)** catches whatever spills out and converts a small spill into a clean, large "splash" that the next station can clearly read.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The output of a dynamic gate is **monotonically rising** during evaluation. It either stays HIGH (precharged) or falls to LOW, **never rising back up during evaluation**. This is what makes cascading safe: the *static inverter* flips it into a monotonically *falling* signal that cannot accidentally re-trigger a downstream dynamic stage.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Domino CMOS

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **100 MHz – 1 GHz** |
| Precharge Time | $t_{pre}$ | $\approx 30\%$ of $T_{clk}$ |
| Evaluate Time | $t_{eval}$ | $\approx 70\%$ of $T_{clk}$ |
| Output Rise Time | $t_{r}$ | $\leq 0.2\,\tau$ |
| Output Fall Time | $t_{f}$ | $\leq 0.3\,\tau$ |
| Power Supply | $V_{DD}$ | **1.8 V / 1.2 V (modern nodes)** |
| Threshold Voltage | $V_{T0}$ | **0.5 V (typ.)** |

Where $\tau$ is the unit inverter delay.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of a Domino Logic Gate

A Domino Logic gate operates synchronously with a **two-phase non-overlapping clock system**:
- **Phase 1: Precharge ($\phi = 1$, $\overline{\phi} = 0$)**
  - PMOS precharge device turns ON.
  - The dynamic output node $X$ (the *domino node*) is pulled up to $V_{DD}$ (logic '1').
  - The NMOS evaluate footer is OFF, so the pull-down network is **isolated** from ground.
  - Static inverter output $Y = \overline{X} = 0$ (logic '0').

- **Phase 2: Evaluation ($\phi = 0$, $\overline{\phi} = 1$)**
  - PMOS precharge device turns OFF.
  - The NMOS evaluate footer turns ON, enabling the pull-down network.
  - If the Boolean function evaluated by the NMOS network is **TRUE**, a conducting path to ground forms, and node $X$ discharges from $V_{DD}$ to 0. The inverter output $Y$ rises to $V_{DD}$.
  - If the Boolean function is **FALSE**, node $X$ stays at $V_{DD}$, and $Y$ remains at 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Property (Monotonicity):** During evaluation, $X$ can only transition $1 \to 0$, and $Y$ can only transition $0 \to 1$. **It can never rise back during evaluation.** This monotonicity is what permits cascading of $N$ dynamic stages safely.

## 2.2 The Role of the Static Inverter (Buffer)

The static CMOS inverter is **not optional** in Domino logic — it is the defining feature:

- **Logic Restoration:** The dynamic node $X$ suffers a threshold drop of $V_{Tn}$ when "high" in basic dynamic CMOS (output of NMOS-driven precharge). The inverter restores full rail-to-rail swing to $Y$.
- **Fan-Out Drive Capability:** The inverter buffers the high-impedance dynamic node, allowing the gate to drive significant capacitive loads.
- **Noise Immunity:** The inverter's sharp VTC provides a high DC noise margin ($NM_H$, $NM_L$).
- **Direction of Signal:** The inverter ensures that the *next* gate's inputs are all '0' initially, meaning the next dynamic stage's precharge is safe and that the NMOS network of the next stage remains off until evaluation.

## 2.3 Cascading and the "Domino Effect"

Because the output $Y$ of a Domino gate is **monotonically rising** during evaluation, multiple Domino gates can be **chained in series** (cascaded). The outputs of upstream gates only become active (HIGH) *after* the next clock phase begins — exactly like a chain of falling dominoes where each falling domino triggers the next.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** This is why the logic family is called "Domino" — just as tipping one domino causes the next to fall in sequence, the evaluation of one dynamic stage prepares the inputs for the next, propagating the computation through the pipeline in a clean, controlled wave.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Precharge Time | $t_{pre} = 0.5 \cdot C_{X} \cdot V_{DD} / I_{pch}$ | $C_{X}$ = domino node capacitance |
| 2 | Evaluate Time | $t_{eval} = R_{n} \cdot C_{X} \cdot \ln 2$ | $R_{n}$ = effective NMOS on-resistance |
| 3 | High-to-Low Propagation Delay | $t_{PHL} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_{L}$ | Through the NMOS network |
| 4 | Low-to-High Propagation Delay | $t_{PLH} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{p} \cdot C_{L}$ | Through the inverter's PMOS |
| 5 | Charge-Sharing Voltage | $\Delta V = \frac{C_{X}}{C_{X} + C_{L}} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Hazard: $X$ node inadvertently drops |
| 6 | Minimum Clock Period | $T_{clk} \geq t_{pre} + t_{eval} + t_{setup}$ | Pipeline timing constraint |
| 7 | Energy per Transition | $E = C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Dynamic power per clock |
| 8 | Average Dynamic Power | $P = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$ = activity factor |
| 9 | Noise Margin (High) | $NM_{H} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Improved by output inverter |
| 10 | Noise Margin (Low) | $NM_{L} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Static CMOS-like behavior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Domino logic is the workhorse of **high-performance microprocessors** (e.g., Intel Pentium, AMD Athlon critical paths, multiplier arrays, register files, address-generation units) where the evaluation path must be fast and the data activity is high. It offers **1.5×–2× the speed** of static CMOS at the cost of higher design complexity and power.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Precharge Time of a Domino Gate

The dynamic node $X$ charges through the PMOS precharge transistor. Treating the PMOS as a current source $I_{pch}$ during saturation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{pch} &= \frac{1}{2} \mu_{p} C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{p} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^{2} \\
Q_{charge} &= C_{X} \cdot V_{DD} \\
t_{pre} &= \frac{Q_{charge}}{I_{pch}} = \frac{C_{X} \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{pch}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each line:**
- **Line 1:** The PMOS is in saturation during the early phase of precharge (largest $V_{DS}$), so we apply the standard square-law current expression.
- **Line 2:** Total charge required to swing the domino node $X$ from 0 V to full $V_{DD}$.
- **Line 3:** Time = charge / current. Substituting gives the closed-form precharge time.

## 3.2 Derivation: Evaluation (Discharge) Delay

During evaluation, the NMOS network pulls node $X$ from $V_{DD}$ to 0 V. The pull-down network can be modeled as an equivalent on-resistance $R_{n}$ discharging the node capacitance $C_{X}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{X}(t) &= V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t / (R_{n} C_{X})} \\
\text{Setting } V_{X}(t) &= 0.5 V_{DD}: \\
0.5 V_{DD} &= V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t_{eval} / (R_{n} C_{X})} \\
\ln(0.5) &= -t_{eval} / (R_{n} C_{X}) \\
t_{eval} &= R_{n} C_{X} \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \, R_{n} C_{X}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each line:**
- **Line 1:** RC discharge equation.
- **Line 2:** We define $t_{eval}$ as the time to reach the 50% point (standard delay definition).
- **Line 3–4:** Natural log manipulation.
- **Line 5:** Final delay = $0.693 \cdot R_{n} C_{X}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Charge-Sharing Hazard

If internal parasitic capacitance $C_{L}$ at a node inside the NMOS network was precharged to $V_{DD}$ (e.g., from a previous clock), connecting it to $X$ (which is at $V_{DD}$ too) via a conducting path is safe. But if internal nodes were initialized to 0 V, charge-sharing can pull $X$ below $V_{DD}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{initial} &= C_{X} V_{DD} + C_{L} (0) = C_{X} V_{DD} \\
Q_{final} &= (C_{X} + C_{L}) V_{final} \\
\text{Charge conservation: } Q_{initial} &= Q_{final} \\
V_{final} &= \frac{C_{X} V_{DD}}{C_{X} + C_{L}} = V_{DD} \left( \frac{1}{1 + C_{L}/C_{X}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:**
- The stored charge redistributes between $C_X$ and $C_L$.
- If $C_L \gg C_X$, $V_{final}$ can drop below the inverter's $V_{IH}$, causing a **false evaluation** (erroneous 0→1 at output $Y$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall (very common in ESE):** Examiners expect students to mention that **charge sharing is mitigated in Domino logic** by:
> 1. Precharging internal nodes (using a "bleeder" PMOS at every internal node).
> 2. Using a larger $C_X$ relative to $C_L$.
> 3. Adding a **keeper transistor** to prevent node $X$ from drifting.

## 3.4 Derivation: Average Power Dissipation

Each evaluation/precharge cycle toggles the dynamic node by $V_{DD}$, requiring energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cycle} &= \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} = C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} \\
P_{avg} &= \alpha \cdot E_{cycle} \cdot f_{clk} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:**
- Half the energy is dissipated charging, half discharging.
- Only $\alpha$ fraction of nodes switch per cycle (activity factor).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Logic Styles

| Parameter | Static CMOS | Basic Dynamic CMOS | **Domino CMOS** | NP-Domino (Zipper) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| # Transistors (N-input) | $2N$ | $N+2$ | $N+4$ | $2N+4$ |
| Logic Function | Inverting & Non-Inverting | Non-Inverting Only | **Inverting Only** | Both Inverting/Non-Inverting |
| Cascadability | Yes | **No** (monotonicity violation) | **Yes** | Yes |
| Output Swing | Rail-to-Rail | $V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$ (degraded) | **Rail-to-Rail** | Rail-to-Rail |
| Power Dissipation | Lower | Moderate | **Moderate–High** | Higher |
| Speed | Slower | Fastest (no load) | **Fast** | Fast |
| Noise Margin | High | Low | **Moderate–High** | Moderate |
| Charge Sharing Risk | None | High | **Moderate (mitigable)** | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Single Domino Logic Gate Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Clock Signal CLK] -->|phi| B[PMOS Precharge Transistor]
    A[Clock Signal CLK] -->|not phi| C[NMOS Evaluate Footer]
    C --> D[NMOS Pull-Down Evaluation Network]
    B --> E[Dynamic Node X]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Static CMOS Inverter]
    F --> G[Buffered Output Y]
    D --> H[Boolean Inputs A, B, C...]
    H --> D
    E --> I[Parasitic Capacitance Cx]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Cascaded Domino Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    Stage1[Stage 1 Domino Gate] -->|Buffered Output Y1| Stage2[Stage 2 Domino Gate]
    Stage2 -->|Buffered Output Y2| Stage3[Stage 3 Domino Gate]
    Stage3 -->|Buffered Output Y3| Stage4[Stage N Domino Gate]
    CLK[Global Clock phi] --> Stage1
    CLK --> Stage2
    CLK --> Stage3
    CLK --> Stage4
    note1[Monotonic Rising Wavefront] -.-> Stage2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Machine: Precharge / Evaluate Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Precharge
    Precharge: PRECHARGE phase
    Precharge: phi is HIGH
    Precharge: X charges to VDD
    Precharge: Y is forced LOW
    Precharge --> Evaluate: phi goes LOW
    Evaluate: EVALUATE phase
    Evaluate: phi is LOW
    Evaluate: NMOS network active
    Evaluate: X may discharge to GND
    Evaluate: Y may rise to VDD
    Evaluate --> Precharge: next clock cycle
```

## 4.4 Transistor-Level Schematic (Textual)

**Canonical 3-Input AND Domino Gate (Y = NOT (A AND B AND C)):**

```
        VDD
         |
        P1 (PMOS, W/L large)  ← phi (clock)
         |
         +-----> Node X (domino node, has parasitic Cx)
         |
        N1 (NMOS)  ← input A
         |
        N2 (NMOS)  ← input B
         |
        N3 (NMOS)  ← input C
         |
        N4 (NMOS footer, clocked)  ← phi_bar
         |
        GND

      Node X ──[P2 PMOS, small]── VDD    (keeper/bleeder, weak)
      Node X ──[N5 NMOS]── GND          (inverter pull-down)
      Node X ──[P3 PMOS]── VDD          (inverter pull-up)
      Inverter output = Node Y
```

**Functional Truth Table (During Evaluation):**

| A | B | C | Path to GND? | Node X | Output Y = NOT(X) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | x | x | NO (N1 off) | Stays VDD | 0 |
| x | 0 | x | NO (N2 off) | Stays VDD | 0 |
| x | x | 0 | NO (N3 off) | Stays VDD | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | YES (all on) | Discharges | 1 |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Domino CMOS logic. Why is a static inverter used at the output of every dynamic gate in a Domino chain?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Domino CMOS logic is a dynamic logic style that uses a clocked PMOS for precharging and an NMOS pull-down network for evaluation, followed by a **static CMOS inverter** at the output.

The static inverter serves three critical functions (1 mark each):
1. **Logic Level Restoration:** It converts the degraded high output of the dynamic node ($V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$) into a full rail-to-rail $V_{DD}$ logic '1'.
2. **Fan-Out / Drive Capability:** It provides a low-impedance output capable of driving large capacitive loads.
3. **Monotonicity Enforcement:** It converts the monotonically falling internal node into a monotonically rising output, enabling **safe cascading** of multiple dynamic stages.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List any three advantages of Domino CMOS logic over static CMOS logic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Higher Speed:** Only NMOS transistors in the pull-down path, so the effective on-resistance is smaller than the PMOS in static CMOS. Delays are typically **30–50% lower** than static CMOS for the same area.
2. **Lower Input Capacitance:** No PMOS network in parallel, so the input load on driving gates is reduced, leading to faster signal transitions.
3. **Smaller Silicon Area:** For an $N$-input function, Domino uses $N+4$ transistors versus $2N$ for static CMOS — almost **half the area** for large fan-in gates (e.g., wide AND-OR gates in arithmetic units).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a 4-input Domino AND gate. Explain its operation during precharge and evaluation phases with the help of a timing diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is charge sharing in dynamic CMOS logic? Derive the expression for the voltage drop due to charge sharing and explain two methods to overcome it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Circuit Diagram (3 marks):**
The 4-input Domino AND gate has:
- One PMOS precharge transistor $P_1$ (clocked by $\phi$).
- Four NMOS transistors ($N_1, N_2, N_3, N_4$) in series implementing the AND function.
- One clocked NMOS footer $N_5$ controlled by $\overline{\phi}$.
- A static CMOS inverter at the dynamic node $X$.
- A weak **PMOS keeper** $P_2$ between $V_{DD}$ and node $X$ to combat leakage and charge sharing.

**Operation during Precharge ($\phi = 1$) (2 marks):**
- $P_1$ turns ON, charging node $X$ to $V_{DD}$ (logic '1').
- $N_5$ (footer) is OFF, disconnecting the pull-down network from ground — no spurious discharge possible.
- Inverter output $Y$ = 0.

**Operation during Evaluation ($\phi = 0$) (2 marks):**
- $P_1$ OFF, $N_5$ ON.
- If all inputs $A=B=C=D=1$, the NMOS chain conducts, $X$ discharges to GND, and $Y$ rises to $V_{DD}$.
- If any input is 0, the chain is broken, $X$ stays high, and $Y$ stays 0.
- The transition is **monotonic** — safe to cascade.

**Timing Diagram:** During $\phi = 1$, $X = V_{DD}$ and $Y = 0$. When $\phi$ falls and inputs go high, $X$ falls and $Y$ rises. Subsequent stages see monotonic rising inputs.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Definition of Charge Sharing (2 marks):**
Charge sharing is a reliability hazard in dynamic CMOS where the charge stored on the precharged parasitic capacitance of the dynamic node $C_X$ redistributes with an internal parasitic capacitance $C_L$ in the pull-down network, causing the dynamic node voltage to **drop unintentionally** during evaluation.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial charge on } C_X: \quad Q_{i} &= C_X \cdot V_{DD} \\
\text{Final charge (after sharing):} \quad Q_{f} &= (C_X + C_L) \cdot V_{final} \\
\text{By conservation of charge: } Q_i &= Q_f \\
V_{final} &= \frac{C_X \cdot V_{DD}}{C_X + C_L} = V_{DD} \left( \frac{1}{1 + C_L/C_X} \right) \\
\text{Voltage drop: } \Delta V &= V_{DD} - V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_X + C_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Methods to Overcome Charge Sharing (2 marks — 1 mark each):**
