# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read First!**
> - **Never** claim a cross-coupled inverter pair is monostable or astable — this is the #1 mark-deduction error.
> - **Always** specify that the two stable states are at $V_{OL}$ and $V_{OH}$, not "low" and "high" vaguely.
> - **In SNM derivations**, students often confuse $V_{IL}$ with $V_{IH}$ — remember: $V_{IL}$ is the input-low-to-output-high transition, $V_{IH}$ is the input-high-to-output-low transition.
> - **For NAND-based SR latches**, the inputs are **active-LOW** — writing "S=1, R=1 forbidden" without specifying active-low logic loses 1–2 marks.
> - **Metastability question trap:** If asked "is metastability a stable state?", the correct answer is **NO** — it is an unstable equilibrium. KTU examiners deduct marks for this.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a bistable element. With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a cross-coupled CMOS inverter pair as a 1-bit memory cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bistable element** is a circuit that has **two stable operating states**, retaining either state indefinitely as long as power is supplied.

A **cross-coupled CMOS inverter pair** consists of two inverters $I_1$ and $I_2$ whose outputs are tied to each other's inputs. The circuit has two stable states:

- **State 1:** $Q = V_{DD}$, $\overline{Q} = 0$
- **State 2:** $Q = 0$, $\overline{Q} = V_{DD}$

When the cell is in State 1, $I_1$ sees a high input and produces a low output ($\overline{Q} = 0$), which in turn keeps $I_2$'s input low, locking $Q$ at $V_{DD}$. The **positive feedback loop** with loop gain $G_{loop} = (g_m r_o)^2 \gg 1$ ensures that any perturbation decays or amplifies toward the nearest stable state. **(3 marks)** ✅

---

### ❓ Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is metastability in a bistable element? Why is it a concern in digital system design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Metastability** is the condition in which a bistable element temporarily resides at an **unstable equilibrium point** (typically $V_M \approx V_{DD}/2$) between its two valid stable states. At this point, the loop gain exactly balances unity ($G_{loop} = 1$), so the circuit neither flips to '0' nor to '1' deterministically.

It is a concern because:
1. **Unpredictable output:** The final settled state depends on infinitesimal noise and device mismatches — making the output nondeterministic.
2. **Timing violation:** Metastability resolution time is unbounded in theory (exponentially decaying), potentially exceeding the next clock cycle and causing system failure.
3. **Synchronizer failures:** In multi-clock-domain designs, data sampled near the setup/hold window can enter metastability, propagating garbage into the next stage. **(3 marks)** ✅

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`

**a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **6-transistor (6T) CMOS SRAM cell**. Derive the expression for its **read stability** in terms of transistor sizing ratios. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the concept of **Static Noise Margin (SNM)** in an SRAM cell. Using the **butterfly curve method**, show how SNM is extracted graphically and compute its value for a cell with $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $V_{IL} = 0.38$ V, $V_{OL} = 0.05$ V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 6T SRAM Cell Operation and Read Stability (7 marks)**

**Circuit Diagram (ASCII for clarity):**

```
        V_DD            V_DD
         |               |
        [PL]            [PR]      <- Pull-up PMOS (M1, M3)
         |               |
   Q ----+----------+----+------ Q_bar
         |          |          |
        [AL]      [NL]      [NR]   <- Access (M5), Pull-down NMOS (M6, M4)
         |          |          |
        BL         GND        BL_bar
         |                    
       Word Line (WL)        
```

**Operation:**

The 6T cell contains **two cross-coupled inverters** ($M_1$–$M_2$, $M_3$–$M_4$) forming the bistable storage, and **two access NMOS transistors** ($M_5$, $M_6$) controlled by the word line (WL).

- **Hold mode:** WL = 0 → access transistors OFF → cell retains its state indefinitely.
- **Read mode:** WL = 1 → BL and $\overline{BL}$ are pre-charged to $V_{DD}$. The stored '0' side discharges through the pull-down NMOS, while the '1' side stays high. A sense amplifier detects the differential.
- **Write mode:** WL = 1 → BL and $\overline{BL}$ are driven with new data; the access transistors (made strong) overpower the cell's internal pull-up/pull-down ratio, forcing a flip.

**Derivation of Read Stability (Cell Ratio):**

During a read, if $Q = 0$, the access transistor $M_5$ and pull-down NMOS $M_2$ form a **voltage divider**. To prevent **destructive read** (accidental flipping), we require:

$$V_{Q,read} < V_{M,inverter}$$

Using the $\dfrac{W}{L}$ ratios:

$$\frac{(W/L)_{access}}{(W/L)_{pull-down}} < \frac{2 \cdot (V_{M,inverter} - V_{th,n})}{(V_{DD} - V_{th,n})}$$

Defining the **cell ratio (CR)**:

$$\boxed{CR = \frac{(W/L)_{pull-down}}{(W/L)_{access}}}$$

The condition becomes $CR > CR_{min}$ for safe read operation. Typical design rule: $CR \ge 1.5$ to $2.0$. **(7 marks)** ✅

---

**Part (b) — Static Noise Margin Extraction (7 marks)**

**Concept:**

Static Noise Margin is the **maximum DC noise voltage** that can be injected at either storage node without flipping the cell. It is the most widely used measure of SRAM cell stability.

**Butterfly Curve Construction:**

1. Plot the VTC of inverter $I_1$: $V_{Q} = f(V_{\overline{Q}})$.
2. Plot the inverse VTC of inverter $I_2$: $V_{\overline{Q}} = f(V_{Q})$.
3. The composite curve has **two lobes** (one for each stable state) — hence the name "butterfly."

**Graphical SNM Extraction:**

The SNM is the **side length of the largest square** that can be inscribed between the two butterfly lobes and the axes.

**Formula:**

$$\boxed{SNM = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $V_{IL} = 0.38$ V, $V_{OL} = 0.05$ V.

$$SNM = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.38 - 0.05 = 0.33~\text{V}$$

Cross-check with the right lobe: assume $V_{OH} = 0.95$ V, $V_{IH} = 0.62$ V:

$$SNM = 0.95 - 0.62 = 0.33~\text{V} \quad ✓$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 330 mV noise margin on a 1 V supply means the cell is **33% robust** — a typical industry figure for 65–45 nm SRAM. **(7 marks)** ✅

---

### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**a)** Compare the **SR latch**, **D latch**, and **D flip-flop** in terms of triggering mechanism, transparency, and application. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Design the **timing analysis** for a master-slave D flip-flop. Show that the output $Q$ changes only on the active clock edge, and derive the setup-time and hold-time constraints in terms of the bistable regeneration time constant $\tau_{reg}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Parameter | SR Latch (NOR/NAND) | D Latch | D Flip-Flop (Master-Slave) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inputs** | S, R (or $\overline{S}, \overline{R}$) | D, Enable | D, Clock |
| **Trigger** | Level-sensitive | Level-sensitive (transparent when EN=1) | Edge-triggered |
| **Transparency** | Transparent while S/R inactive combinations hold | Fully transparent when EN=1 | **Not transparent** — output isolated |
| **Output update** | Any time inputs change | When EN is high | Only on clock edge |
| **Race condition** | Possible (S=R=1 forbidden) | None (single input) | None |
| **Use case** | Asynchronous set/reset, control logic | Pipeline registers, address latches | Synchronous registers, counters, state machines |
| **Complexity** | 4 gates (NAND) | 6 transistors + enable | 2 latches + inverter (~20 transistors) |

**Key Insight (2 marks for explanation):** The D flip-flop eliminates the transparency problem of the D latch by cascading two latches driven by **opposite clock phases**. This guarantees the output is sampled and held deterministically on every clock edge — the foundation of all synchronous digital design. **(7 marks)** ✅

---

**Part (b) — Master-Slave Timing Analysis (7 marks)**

**Master-Slave Topology:**

```
   D ----[Master Latch]----+----[Slave Latch]---- Q
            ^                     ^
            |                     |
            CLK                   CLK_bar
```

**Behavior:**

- **When CLK = 1 (Master transparent, Slave holds):** D flows into master node $M$, but slave is opaque → $Q$ unchanged.
- **When CLK falls (Master holds, Slave transparent):** The value of $M$ from just before the falling edge transfers to $Q$.
- **Net effect:** $Q$ updates **on the falling edge** (or rising, depending on convention).

**Setup Time Derivation:**

For the master to reliably capture D, D must be stable long enough that the master settles to within $\Delta V$ of the correct rail before the clock edge. With regeneration time constant $\tau_{reg}$:

$$\Delta V_{master}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{reg}}\right)$$

Setting $\Delta V_{master} = V_{DD}/2$ (half-rail) for guaranteed robust capture:

$$t_{setup} = \tau_{reg} \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \cdot \tau_{reg}$$

$$\boxed{t_{setup} \approx 0.7 \cdot \tau_{reg}}$$

**Hold Time Derivation:**

D must remain stable for a minimum duration *after* the clock edge so that the master does not re-sample incorrect data. By a symmetric analysis:

$$\boxed{t_{hold} \approx 0.7 \cdot \tau_{reg}}$$

**Clock-to-Q Delay:**

After the active clock edge, the slave must regenerate from its metastable-equivalent transient. The total propagation delay is:

$$t_{clk \to Q} = t_{setup} + 2 \cdot \tau_{reg} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{\Delta V_{noise}}\right)$$

For typical noise margins, $t_{clk \to Q} \approx 3 \cdot \tau_{reg}$ to $5 \cdot \tau_{reg}$. **(7 marks)** ✅

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Checklist — Behavior of Bistable Elements**

- ✅ A **bistable element** has **two stable states** and uses **positive feedback** to retain them.
- ✅ The **canonical CMOS bistable** is the **cross-coupled inverter pair**.
- ✅ The **loop gain condition** $G_{loop} = (g_m r_o)^2 > 1$ is the *fundamental* requirement for bistability.
- ✅ The **butterfly curve** has three intersections: **two stable + one metastable**.
- ✅ **Metastability** is an **unstable** equilibrium — any infinitesimal noise causes divergence to a stable state.
- ✅ **SRAM cell** = 6T topology: 2 cross-coupled inverters + 2 access NMOS.
- ✅ **Static Noise Margin (SNM)** is the largest inscribed square in the butterfly lobe — directly measures cell robustness.
- ✅ **Read stability** requires the **cell ratio** $CR = \dfrac{(W/L)_{pull\text{-}down}}{(W/L)_{access}} \ge 1.5$ to $2.0$.
- ✅ **D Latch** is level-sensitive (transparent); **D Flip-Flop** is edge-triggered (master-slave eliminates transparency).
- ✅ **Setup time** and **hold time** in a flip-flop are both $\approx 0.7 \cdot \tau_{reg}$ in an idealized bistable model.
- ✅ The **6T SRAM cell** is the workhorse of all modern on-chip cache memory in CPUs, GPUs, and SoCs.
- ✅ **Industry relevance:** FinFET and GAA (Gate-All-Around) transistors at 3 nm / 2 nm nodes still rely on **cross-coupled inverters** for SRAM — only the transistor geometry has changed.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favorite marks-grabber:** Always derive the loop gain $G_{loop} = (g_m r_o)^2$ and the SNM formula — these two derivations appear in nearly every KTU ESE paper on this topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Note:** Bistability is **the** physical phenomenon that makes digital memory possible. Master the cross-coupled inverter, the butterfly curve, and the SNM equation — and you have mastered Module 4 of PBECT604. All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE! 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS D Latch and Edge-Triggered Flip-Flop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CMOS D Latch and Edge-Triggered Flip-Flop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Static Memory Cell — CMOS D Latch

A **CMOS D Latch** is a level-sensitive bistable memory element that uses a pair of cross-coupled inverters (forming a 1-bit storage cell) combined with a transmission gate (TG) write-access network. The output **Q** transparently follows the data input **D** whenever the **enable/clock (CLK)** signal is asserted, and **holds** the last sampled value when CLK is de-asserted. In standard CMOS design, CLK and its complement **CLK_b** are routed together to drive the NMOS and PMOS transistors of the transmission gates in anti-phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBECT604 – Module 4):**
> A *latch* is **level-sensitive** (transparent when CLK = 1, opaque when CLK = 0 for a positive-level latch). A *flip-flop* is **edge-triggered** — it samples data only on the active clock transition (0→1 for a positive-edge FF).

## 1.2 The Edge-Triggered Flip-Flop

A **CMOS D Flip-Flop (D-FF)** is constructed by cascading two D latches in a **master–slave** configuration driven by opposite clock phases. The *master* latch samples D while CLK = 1, and the *slave* latch transfers that sampled value to Q when CLK = 0 (or vice-versa, for negative-edge triggered). The net effect is that Q changes only at the **active clock edge**, providing synchronization essential for sequential pipelines, registers, and FSMs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definitions (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **D Latch:** A level-sensitive memory element; transparent for the duration of the active clock level.
> - **D Flip-Flop:** An edge-triggered memory element that captures D only at the instant of the active clock transition.
> - **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** The minimum time data D must be stable *before* the active clock edge.
> - **Hold Time ($t_h$):** The minimum time data D must remain stable *after* the active clock edge.
> - **Clock-to-Q Delay ($t_{c2q}$ or $t_{CQ}$):** The propagation delay from the active clock edge to a stable output Q.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### Analogy 1 — The Hotel Door Latch (D Latch)
Imagine a hotel room door with a push-bar (the *clock signal*). When the bar is pushed (CLK = 1), the door is **transparent** — anyone can walk in carrying any value (D flows to Q). When the bar is released (CLK = 0), the latch engages and **locks the last person inside** (Q holds the value). The latch is "transparent" for the whole duration the bar is pushed.

### Analogy 2 — The Photographic Camera Shutter (Flip-Flop)
A camera shutter captures a scene **only at the instant of the click**. Before and after the click, the scene is irrelevant. A D flip-flop behaves identically — it captures D **only at the precise moment** of the active clock edge (rising or falling). The master-slave pair acts like a high-speed shutter mechanism that snaps the image at the transition.

| Parameter | Door Latch (Latch) | Camera Shutter (Flip-Flop) |
|---|---|---|
| Active Window | Entire duration of CLK = 1 | Only the 0→1 transition |
| Transparency | Level-sensitive (transparent) | Edge-sensitive (opaque except at edge) |
| Use Case | Asynchronous buffers, glitch filters | Synchronous registers, pipeline stages |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *"Latch listens, Flip-Flop snaps."* A latch is open and listening during the whole active phase; a flip-flop only listens at the exact instant of the clock edge.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard metrics** are universally referenced in KTU VLSI board examinations:

- **Threshold Voltage ($V_{TH}$ or $V_{tn}$ / $V_{tp}$):** Typically **0.4 V to 0.7 V** for modern 180 nm – 65 nm CMOS processes. NMOS $V_{tn} \approx$ **+0.5 V**, PMOS $V_{tp} \approx$ **−0.5 V** in symmetric processes.
- **Supply Voltage ($V_{DD}$):** Ranging from **5.0 V** (legacy 0.5 µm) down to **0.7 V – 1.0 V** in sub-28 nm nodes.
- **Switching Threshold ($V_M$):** Approximately **$V_{DD}/2$** for a symmetric inverter ratio of $(W/L)_n = 2 \cdot (W/L)_p$.
- **Fan-Out (FO):** Number of standard-load inverters driven by Q (typically **FO4 = 4** for delay measurement).
- **Clock Frequency ($f_{clk}$):** Modern digital ICs operate between **100 MHz – 5 GHz** in CMOS VLSI.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Static CMOS D Latch Using Transmission Gates

The most widely-used CMOS D Latch (Rabaey/Chandrakasan/Nikolic textbook style) is implemented as **two cross-coupled inverters + two transmission gates (TG1 and TG2)**, with an inverter generating CLK_b.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Inverter Pair (Storage Core):**
- Inverter **I1** (input: Q_b, output: Q) and **Inverter I2** (input: Q, output: Q_b) form a **positive-feedback bistable**.
- Once latched, the pair holds the state indefinitely as long as power ($V_{DD}$) is maintained.

**Step 2 — Input Transmission Gate (TG1):**
- TG1 is **ON** when CLK = 1 (NMOS gate sees CLK = 1, PMOS gate sees CLK_b = 0).
- When ON, TG1 connects D to the internal node (let's call it **S**), making S = D.
- When CLK = 0, TG1 is OFF (high-impedance) and isolates D from S.

**Step 3 — Feedback Transmission Gate (TG2):**
- TG2 is **ON** when CLK = 0 and **OFF** when CLK = 1.
- When ON, TG2 forms a closed feedback loop between Q and S, **closing the bistable** and holding the previous state.

**Step 4 — Combined Behavior:**
- **CLK = 1:** TG1 ON, TG2 OFF → S = D, I1 drives Q = D. **Latch is transparent.**
- **CLK = 0:** TG1 OFF, TG2 ON → S is isolated from D; TG2 closes feedback → state is held. **Latch is opaque.**

## 2.2 C²MOS (Clocked CMOS) D Latch

The **C²MOS latch** uses a dynamic-style tri-state inverter driven by the clock:

- **When CLK = 1:** Both the pull-up PMOS (gated by CLK_b = 0) and pull-down NMOS (gated by CLK = 1) are active → **inverter is enabled**, so Q follows D.
- **When CLK = 0:** Both clocked transistors are OFF → **tristate**, and the cross-coupled inverter pair holds the state.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Topic:** C²MOS is preferred over TG-based latches for its **insensitivity to clock overlap**, because the two clocked transistors are stacked on opposite sides of the inverter. This makes it the **building block of master–slave flip-flops** in pipelined datapaths.

## 2.3 Master–Slave Negative-Edge-Triggered D Flip-Flop

A negative-edge-triggered D-FF is built by cascading **two C²MOS latches (Master + Slave)** with **opposite clock phases**.

### Operational Cycle

| Phase | Master Latch (CLK) | Slave Latch (CLK_b) | System Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 1: CLK = 1 | **Transparent** → Master samples D | **Opaque** → Slave holds previous Q | Master tracks D, Q unchanged |
| Phase 2: CLK falling edge | Master becomes opaque (holds last D) | Slave becomes transparent (passes master's held value to Q) | **Q updates with last D just before edge** |
| Phase 3: CLK = 0 | Opaque (holds) | Transparent | Q follows Master's frozen value |
| Phase 4: CLK rising edge | Master becomes transparent again | Slave becomes opaque | Cycle repeats |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Conceptual Question Trigger:**
> "Why does the master-slave flip-flop need *two* opposite-phase latches instead of one?"
> **Answer:** A single latch is level-sensitive and would allow D to ripple through to Q during the entire active level. Two opposite-phase latches ensure D is *frozen* in the master during one half-cycle and *transferred to* Q only during the opposite half-cycle — yielding **edge-triggered** behavior.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **every standard CMOS Latch/Flip-Flop equation** that has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in KTU ESE board papers for PBECT604.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | Valid Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clock-to-Q delay (TG latch) | $t_{c2q} = t_{TX} + t_{inv}$ | seconds | $t_{TX}$ = TG delay, $t_{inv}$ = inverter delay |
| 2 | Setup time (typical) | $t_{su} = t_{TX} + t_{inv,setup}$ | seconds | Data must settle before clock edge |
| 3 | Hold time (typical) | $t_h = t_{TX,hold}$ | seconds | Often $\geq 0$ for edge-triggered FF |
| 4 | D-to-Q (D-Q) delay | $t_{DQ} = t_{TX,m} + t_{TX,s} + t_{inv}$ | seconds | m = master, s = slave path |
| 5 | Static (leakage) power | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Watts | Dominant in idle/nano-CMOS |
| 6 | Dynamic switching power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Watts | $\alpha$ = switching activity (0 to 1) |
| 7 | Short-circuit power | $P_{sc} = I_{mean} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Watts | Non-zero during finite slope transitions |
| 8 | Total average power | $P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{sc} + P_{static}$ | Watts | Summation of all three components |
| 9 | Energy per transition | $E_{bit} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Joules | Fundamental CMOS energy metric |
| 10 | Max clock frequency | $f_{clk,max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{c2q} + t_{su} + t_{logic}}$ | Hz | Pipeline stage bound |
| 11 | Static Noise Margin (SNM) | $SNM = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ (approx.) | Volts | Measured via butterfly curve |
| 12 | Switching threshold | $V_M \approx \dfrac{V_{TH,n} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TH,p}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$ | Volts | $k_n, k_p$ = transconductance params |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline Registers:** Every CPU pipeline stage (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) is bracketed by D-FFs. The maximum operating frequency of a CPU is directly limited by $t_{c2q}$ and $t_{su}$.
- **Synchronous DRAM (SDRAM) Interface:** Memory controllers use edge-triggered FFs to latch address/command buses at the SDRAM clock edge.
- **Clock-Domain Crossing (CDC):** Dual-rank synchronizers (typically **two FFs in series**) mitigate metastability in asynchronous interfaces.
- **Scan Chains (DFT):** Every flip-flop is replaced by a scan-FF (MUX + D-FF) for Design-for-Testability in VLSI.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Static Power Dissipation of a CMOS D Latch

In steady state (no clock transition, no D change), the TG-based D latch draws **only leakage current**. Let us derive the static power expression.

### Step 1 — Identify Current Paths

When CLK = 0 (latch opaque) and D is held constant, both transmission gates TG1 and TG2 are in known states. The cross-coupled inverters (I1, I2) are in stable equilibrium. No DC path exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND through any logic transistor stack.

### Step 2 — Apply the Leakage Model

The sub-threshold leakage current through an OFF NMOS transistor is given by the standard BSIM model approximation:

$$I_{leak,N} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH,n}}{nV_T}} \left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}}\right)$$

where $I_0$ is the zero-bias current, $n$ is the sub-threshold swing coefficient, and $V_T = kT/q \approx \textbf{25.85 mV}$ at room temperature ($T = 300 K$).

### Step 3 — Sum All Leakage Sources

For the entire latch (two inverters = 4 transistors + 2 TGs = 4 transistors = 8 transistors total):

$$I_{leak,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} I_{leak,i}$$

For a symmetric design with identical leakage per transistor:

$$I_{leak,total} = 8 \cdot I_{leak,N}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Static Power

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak,total}$$

$$\boxed{P_{static} = 8 \cdot V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak,N}}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation Example

Given: $V_{DD} = \textbf{1.0 V}$, $I_{leak,N} = \textbf{10 nA}$ per transistor (typical 65 nm process).

$$P_{static} = 8 \cdot (1.0\,\text{V}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{A})$$

$$P_{static} = 8 \cdot 10^{-8}\,\text{W} = \textbf{80 nW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** Always state the **process node** and **temperature** when quoting leakage current. Leakage doubles approximately every **10 °C** rise in junction temperature (rule-of-thumb).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Switching Power of an Edge-Triggered Flip-Flop

When the flip-flop toggles at clock frequency $f_{clk}$, every clock cycle charges and discharges the internal node capacitances.

### Step 1 — Energy to Charge a Capacitor to $V_{DD}$

The classical derivation gives the **energy drawn from the supply** to charge a capacitor $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$:

$$E_{supply} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \cdot dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

Wait — that's the **energy stored** in the capacitor. The actual **energy drawn from $V_{DD}$** is:

$$E_{drawn} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot i(t)\,dt = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

The remaining half ($C_L V_{DD}^{2}/2$) is **dissipated in the PMOS during charging**, and an additional $C_L V_{DD}^{2}/2$ is **dissipated in the NMOS during discharging**.

### Step 2 — Energy Dissipated Per Transition

$$E_{dissipated} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} \quad \text{(charging half)}$$

$$+ \quad \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} \quad \text{(discharging half)}$$

$$E_{dissipated} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Power Per Switching Event

If the node switches at activity factor $\alpha$ (probability per clock cycle of a 0→1 transition):

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Internal Node Count

A D flip-flop has **multiple internal nodes** ($S_M, Q_M, S_S, Q, Q_b$). Total load capacitance:

$$C_{L,total} = C_{S_M} + C_{Q_M} + C_{S_S} + C_{Q} + C_{Q_b}$$

$$P_{dyn,FF} = \alpha_{FF} \cdot C_{L,total} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

Given: $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $f_{clk} = 500$ MHz, $C_{L,total} = 20$ fF, $\alpha_{FF} = 0.25$ (typical random data):

$$P_{dyn,FF} = 0.25 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{F}) \cdot (1.0)^{2} \cdot (500 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz})$$

$$P_{dyn,FF} = 0.25 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 5 \times 10^{8}$$

$$P_{dyn,FF} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{W} = \textbf{2.5 µW}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Setup Time and Hold Time of a Master-Slave D Flip-Flop

The setup time constraint ensures that the **master latch has correctly captured D** before the clock edge causes the slave to become transparent.

### Step 1 — Setup Time Derivation

For a TG-based master latch, the data must propagate through TG1 and the master inverter before the falling clock edge isolates TG1:

$$t_{su} = t_{d,TG1} + t_{d,inv,master}$$

where $t_{d,TG1}$ is the delay through transmission gate TG1 and $t_{d,inv,master}$ is the propagation delay of the master inverter.

### Step 2 — Hold Time Derivation

The hold time is determined by how long D must remain stable *after* the clock edge, so that the previously captured value in the master is not corrupted by a new D transition. For an ideal symmetric master-slave:

$$t_h = t_{d,TG1,OFF} - t_{d,TG2,ON}$$

For a well-designed FF, $t_h \geq 0$ (zero or positive hold time). A **negative hold time** can occur if the slave closes faster than the master opens.

### Step 3 — Total Cycle Time Constraint

For correct sequential operation:

$$T_{clk} \geq t_{c2q} + t_{logic} + t_{su}$$

$$f_{clk,max} = \frac{1}{t_{c2q} + t_{logic} + t_{su}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Latch vs. Flip-Flop

| Property | CMOS D Latch | Edge-Triggered D Flip-Flop |
|---|---|---|
| Triggering | Level-sensitive | Edge-sensitive |
| Transparency window | Entire CLK-active duration | Only at active edge |
| Transistor count (TG-based) | **8** (2 inv + 2 TG) | **16** (two latches cascaded) |
| Race-through hazard | Possible if CLK is too wide | **Eliminated** by master-slave |
| Setup/hold requirement | Loose (level-sensitive) | Strict (edge-sensitive) |
| Power per bit | Lower (no second latch) | Higher (2× internal nodes) |
| Use case | Asynchronous buffers, glitch suppressors | Synchronous registers, pipelines |
| KTU board relevance | Module 4, Part A/B | Module 4, Part B (14-mark frequent) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: CMOS D Latch (TG-Based)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional block architecture** of the transmission-gate-based CMOS D Latch. Physical schematics are mapped via blocks because Mermaid cannot render transistor-level schematics directly.

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputDataD[Data Input D] --> TG1Block[Transmission Gate TG1]
    ClockSignal[Clock CLK] --> ClockInverter[Inverter Generates CLK_b]
    ClockSignal --> TG1Block
    ClockInverter --> TG1Block
    ClockInverter --> TG2Block[Transmission Gate TG2]
    TG1Block --> InternalNodeS[Internal Storage Node S]
    TG2Block --> InternalNodeS
    InverterI1[Cross-Coupled Inverter I1] --> OutputQ[Output Q]
    InverterI2[Cross-Coupled Inverter I2] --> OutputQb[Output Q_b]
    InternalNodeS --> InverterI1
    OutputQ --> InverterI2
    OutputQb --> InverterI1
    TG2Block -. Closes Feedback Loop .-> InternalNodeS
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- When **CLK = 1**: TG1 is ON (passes D → S), TG2 is OFF → **latch is transparent**; Q follows D.
- When **CLK = 0**: TG1 is OFF (isolates D), TG2 is ON (closes feedback) → **latch is opaque**; Q holds previous value.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Master–Slave Edge-Triggered D Flip-Flop

```mermaid
graph TD
    D[Data Input D] --> MasterLatch[Master C2MOS Latch]
    CLK[Clock CLK] --> MasterLatch
    CLK --> ClockInverter[Clock Inverter Generates CLK_b]
    ClockInverter --> MasterLatch
    ClockInverter --> SlaveLatch[Slave C2MOS Latch]
    CLK --> SlaveLatch
    MasterLatch --> IntermediateNode[Intermediate Node S_M]
    IntermediateNode --> SlaveLatch
    SlaveLatch --> FinalQ[Final Output Q]
    SlaveLatch --> FinalQb[Final Output Q_b]
    MasterLatch -. Phase 1: Transparent when CLK=1 .- MasterLatch
    SlaveLatch -. Phase 2: Transparent when CLK=0 .- SlaveLatch
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Master latch** samples D while CLK = 1 (transparent phase).
- **Slave latch** is opaque during this time and holds the previous Q.
- At the **falling edge of CLK**, master becomes opaque (freezes D), slave becomes transparent (transfers frozen D to Q).
- This staggered behavior creates **negative-edge-triggered** operation. Swap clock phases for **positive-edge-triggered**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Timing Behavior of D Flip-Flop

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Clock Rising Edge Detected] --> CheckMaster{Master Latch Transparent?}
    CheckMaster -->|Yes CLK=1| MasterSamples[Master Latches New D Value]
    MasterSamples --> MasterHolds[Master Becomes Opaque at CLK Falling Edge]
    MasterHolds --> CheckSlave{Slave Latch Transparent?}
    CheckSlave -->|Yes CLK=0| SlaveUpdates[Slave Transfers Master Value to Q]
    SlaveUpdates --> OutputStable[Q Output Now Stable and Reflects D from Last Active Phase]
    OutputStable --> WaitNextEdge[Wait for Next Active Clock Edge]
    WaitNextEdge --> Start
```

---

## 4.4 Timing Diagram (ASCII Representation)

```
        CLK:  ___|‾‾‾‾‾|___|‾‾‾‾‾|___|‾‾‾‾‾|___|‾‾‾‾‾|___
              ↑       ↑       ↑       ↑       ↑
           Edge1    Edge2    Edge3    Edge4    Edge5

          D:  ____‾‾‾‾_____‾‾‾_____‾‾‾‾‾‾_____
                                 ↑
                            D changes here

Master Q: _______‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾
           (follows D while CLK=1, freezes on falling edge)

Slave Q:  ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾___
           (updates only at falling edge — edge-triggered!)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Interpretation:**
> - Notice how `D` changes *during* a CLK = 1 phase, but the **Master Q** samples only the value of D **just before** the falling edge.
> - The **Slave Q** is identical to **Master Q** but delayed by one half-cycle, which means the *external observer* sees Q update only at the falling edge.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define a CMOS D Latch. How is it different from a D Flip-Flop?

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **CMOS D Latch** is a level-sensitive memory element built using cross-coupled inverters and transmission gates (or a C²MOS structure). It consists of a data input D, a clock/enable input CLK, and complementary outputs Q and Q_b.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | D Latch | D Flip-Flop |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | Level-sensitive (transparent when CLK = 1) | Edge-sensitive (samples only at active edge) |
| Structure | Single bistable + access network | Master-slave cascade of two latches |
| Hazard | Vulnerable to race-through | Race-free due to master-slave isolation |
| Use | Simple buffers, asynchronous circuits | Synchronous registers, pipelines |

(3 marks: 1 mark for latch definition, 1 mark for FF definition, 1 mark for the comparison table.)

---

### Question A2: What is the significance of setup time and hold time in a CMOS D Flip-Flop?

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** It is the minimum time interval *before* the active clock edge during which the data input D must remain stable. If D changes closer to the clock edge than $t_{su}$, the master latch may fail to capture the correct value, leading to **metastability**.

**Hold Time ($t_h$):** It is the minimum time interval *after* the active clock edge during which D must remain stable. Violating hold time causes the master latch to be corrupted by a new D transition before it is fully isolated.

**Significance:**
- These timing parameters **define the input timing constraints** for correct sequential operation.
- Together they bound the **maximum operating clock frequency**:
$$f_{clk,max} = \frac{1}{t_{c2q} + t_{logic} + t_{su}}$$
- They are critical for **timing closure** in static-timing-analysis (STA) of VLSI designs.

(3 marks: 1 mark each for setup, hold, and significance.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulations**, Module 4 carries one 14-mark question with internal choice. Both alternatives (Question A and Question B) are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Transmission-Gate CMOS D Latch and C²MOS Master-Slave Flip-Flop

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks | Internal Choice – Set A]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw and explain the operation of a CMOS D Latch using transmission gates. Discuss its transparent and opaque phases.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Structure:**
The TG-based CMOS D Latch consists of:
1. **Inverter pair (I1, I2):** Cross-coupled to form a bistable storage cell.
2. **TG1 (input TG):** Connects D to internal storage node S; controlled by CLK (NMOS) and CLK_b (PMOS).
3. **TG2 (feedback TG):** Closes the feedback loop during the opaque phase; controlled by CLK_b (NMOS) and CLK (PMOS).
4. **Clock Inverter:** Generates CLK_b from CLK.

**Operation Phases:**

**Phase 1 — CLK = 1 (Transparent Mode):**
- TG1 is ON (NMOS sees CLK = 1, PMOS sees CLK_b = 0).
- TG2 is OFF (NMOS sees CLK_b = 0, PMOS sees CLK = 1).
- D is connected to node S; inverter I1 drives Q = D.
- *The output Q mirrors D for the entire duration of CLK = 1.*

**Phase 2 — CLK = 0 (Opaque Mode):**
- TG1 is OFF (isolates D from S).
- TG2 is ON (closes the feedback loop between Q and S).
- The cross-coupled inverters hold the last sampled value indefinitely.
- *The output Q is held constant regardless of D changes.*

**Transistor Count:** 8 transistors total (2 for each of two inverters + 2 for each of two TGs).

(7 marks: 2 marks for circuit description, 3 marks for phase-by-phase analysis, 2 marks for transistor count and significance.)

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat schematic, explain the operation of a C²MOS master-slave negative-edge-triggered D Flip-Flop. Derive the expression for its clock-to-Q delay.

**Model Solution:**

**C²MOS Master-Slave D Flip-Flop:**
The flip-flop consists of **two C²MOS latches cascaded**, driven by opposite clock phases.

**Master Latch:**
- Uses a tri-state inverter enabled by CLK = 1.
- During CLK = 1: Master is **transparent**, sampling D into its internal node $Q_M$.
- During CLK = 0: Master is **opaque**, holding $Q_M$.

**Slave Latch:**
- Uses a tri-state inverter enabled by CLK_b = 1 (i.e., when CLK = 0).
- During CLK = 0: Slave is **transparent**, transferring $Q_M$ to final output Q.
- During CLK = 1: Slave is **opaque**, holding Q.

**Edge-Triggered Behavior:**
- At the **falling edge** of CLK, the master becomes opaque (freezes D), and the slave becomes transparent (updates Q with the frozen value).
- This staggered operation produces **negative-edge triggering**.
- For **positive-edge triggering**, simply swap the clock connections to the two latches.

**Clock-to-Q Delay Derivation:**

The clock-to-Q delay consists of two transmission/inverter delays:

$$t_{c2q} = t_{d,master,fall} + t_{d,slave,fall}$$

For a C²MOS tri-state inverter, the fall delay through one latch is approximately:

$$t_{d,latch} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_{int}$$

where $R_{eq}$ is the equivalent ON-resistance of the clocked transistor stack and $C_{int}$ is the internal node capacitance.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{t_{c2q} = 2 \cdot (0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_{int}) = 1.38 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_{int}}$$

**Numerical Estimate:** For $R_{eq} = 1$ kΩ and $C_{int} = 50$ fF:

$$t_{c2q} = 1.38 \cdot (10^3) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-15}) = \textbf{69 ps}$$

(7 marks: 3 marks for master-slave explanation, 2 marks for edge-triggered behavior, 2 marks for delay derivation.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): CMOS D Latch Variants and Timing Parameters

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks | Internal Choice – Set B]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare the various CMOS D Latch implementations: (i) Static TG-based latch, (ii) Dynamic latch, and (iii) C²MOS latch. Bring out the merits and demerits of each.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Static TG-Based Latch | Dynamic Latch | C²MOS Latch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structure | 2 inv + 2 TG | 1 TG + 1 small inv + parasitic cap | Tri-state inverter + clocked transistors |
| Storage element | Cross-coupled inverters (active) | Parasitic node capacitance (passive) | Tri-state feedback |
| Static power | Low (no DC path) | Zero (no static current) | Low |
| Data retention | Permanent (as long as $V_{DD}$ ON) | **Limited** (leakage destroys data) | Permanent |
| Clock overlap sensitivity | Sensitive | Very sensitive | **Insensitive** |
| Minimum clock frequency | Unrestricted | $f_{min}$ required (e.g., **>10 kHz**) | Unrestricted |
| Robustness | High | **Low** (process variation sensitive) | High |
| Transistor count | 8 | 4–6 | 6–8 |
| Typical use | General-purpose registers | High-density memories, fast paths | Pipelined datapaths |

**Merits/Demerits Summary:**

- **TG-based:** *Merits* — robust, no minimum clock; *Demerits* — sensitive to clock overlap, larger area.
- **Dynamic:** *Merits* — very fast, low power, small area; *Demerits* — requires minimum clock, vulnerable to leakage and noise.
- **C²MOS:** *Merits* — robust to clock overlap, fast, no minimum clock; *Demerits* — slightly more complex, larger than dynamic.

(7 marks: 4 marks for comparison table, 3 marks for merits/demerits discussion.)

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the timing parameters of a CMOS D Flip-Flop — setup time, hold time, and clock-to-Q delay. How do they determine the maximum clock frequency?

**Model Solution:**

**Timing Parameter Definitions:**

1. **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** Minimum time D must be stable *before* the active clock edge.
   - For a master-slave FF:
$$t_{su} = t_{d,master,TG} + t_{d,master,inv}$$
   - Typically **50 ps to 200 ps** in modern CMOS.

2. **Hold Time ($t_h$):** Minimum time D must be stable *after* the active clock edge.
   - Determined by the master latch's TG turn-off time:
$$t_h = t_{d,master,TG,OFF} - t_{d,slave,TG,ON}$$
   - Often **0 to 50 ps** for well-designed FFs.

3. **Clock-to-Q Delay ($t_{c2q}$):** Time from active clock edge to a stable output Q.
   - Dominated by the slave latch propagation:
$$t_{c2q} = t_{d,slave,inv} + t_{d,output,driver}$$

**Maximum Clock Frequency Derivation:**

For a pipeline stage bounded by two flip-flops with combinational logic delay $t_{logic}$ between them, the **setup constraint** requires:

$$T_{clk} \geq t_{c2q} + t_{logic} + t_{su}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{f_{clk,max} = \frac{1}{t_{c2q} + t_{logic} + t_{su}}}$$

**Numerical Example:** Given $t_{c2q} = 70$ ps, $t_{logic} = 200$ ps, $t_{su} = 50$ ps:

$$f_{clk,max} = \frac{1}{70 + 200 + 50} \times 10^{-12}$$

$$f_{clk,max} = \frac{1}{320 \times 10^{-12}} = \textbf{3.125 GHz}$$

**Hold Time Check (must be satisfied separately):**
$$t_h \leq t_{c2q} + t_{logic,min}$$
If violated, **clock skew** must be introduced or logic delay must be increased.

(7 marks: 2 marks each for setup/hold/c2q definitions, 3 marks for max frequency derivation with example.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing latch and flip-flop timing:** Students often quote $t_{su}$ and $t_h$ for latches (where they don't strictly apply in the edge-triggered sense). Always clarify the difference.
> 2. **Forgetting the clock inverter:** When drawing C²MOS latches, omitting the CLK → CLK_b inverter is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing dynamic and static latch concepts:** Dynamic latches have a *minimum clock frequency*; static latches do not. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation:** In 14-mark questions, merely stating formulas without algebraic steps costs 2–3 marks. Always show intermediate steps.
> 5. **Wrong polarity:** A *negative-edge-triggered* FF uses CLK for the master and CLK_b for the slave. Reversing these gives a positive-edge FF. Examiners notice this swap immediately.
> 6. **Ignoring transistor counts:** KTU board exams frequently ask "How many transistors?" for a TG-based latch (answer: **8**) and C²MOS master-slave FF (answer: **16**). Wrong counts lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: CMOS D Latch & Edge-Triggered FF**

- ✅ A **D Latch** is **level-sensitive**; a **D Flip-Flop** is **edge-triggered**.
- ✅ The **TG-based CMOS D Latch** uses **2 inverters + 2 transmission gates = 8 transistors**.
- ✅ When **CLK = 1** (positive latch): TG1 ON, TG2 OFF → **transparent**; output follows D.
- ✅ When **CLK = 0**: TG1 OFF, TG2 ON → **opaque**; output holds previous value.
- ✅ A **master-slave FF** uses **two latches** driven by opposite clock phases for edge-triggering.
- ✅ **C²MOS** latch is preferred because it is **insensitive to clock overlap** — critical for robust design.
- ✅ **Setup time ($t_{su}$)** = time D must be stable *before* active clock edge.
- ✅ **Hold time ($t_h$)** = time D must remain stable *after* active clock edge.
- ✅ **Clock-to-Q delay ($t_{c2q}$)** = propagation delay from clock edge to stable Q.
- ✅ Maximum clock frequency: $f_{clk,max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{c2q} + t_{logic} + t_{su}}$.
- ✅ **Dynamic power** of FF: $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$.
- ✅ **Static power** is dominated by **leakage** in nano-CMOS latches ($P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$).
- ✅ A well-designed FF has **zero or positive hold time**; negative hold time indicates design issues.
- ✅ **Negative-edge-triggered FF**: Master on CLK, Slave on CLK_b. **Positive-edge**: Master on CLK_b, Slave on CLK.
- ✅ **Dynamic latches** require **minimum clock frequency** to prevent data loss due to leakage — a key exam differentiator.
- ✅ Static Noise Margin (SNM) of the storage cell determines the **robustness** against noise and soft errors.
- ✅ D flip-flops are the **fundamental building blocks** of pipeline registers, FSMs, shift registers, and scan chains in modern VLSI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Read Only Memory: 4x4 MOS ROM Cell Arrays (NOR and NAND)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Read Only Memory: 4x4 MOS ROM Cell Arrays (NOR and NAND)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Read-Only Memory (ROM)** is a non-volatile semiconductor memory device in which the binary data is permanently (or semi-permanently) hard-wired into the device during fabrication (mask-programmed) or via electrical/physical alteration after manufacture (PROM, EPROM, EEPROM). Once programmed, ROM permits **read-only** access — data is retrieved by addressing a specific word line and sensing the resulting voltage on the bit lines.

A **4×4 MOS ROM Cell Array** is the smallest meaningful square topology of a ROM, containing:
- **4 word lines** (row decoder outputs, denoted $W_0, W_1, W_2, W_3$)
- **4 bit lines** (data output lines, denoted $B_0, B_1, B_2, B_3$)
- **16 crosspoints** (potential cell locations)

The data is encoded by the **presence** or **absence** of an NMOS transistor at each crosspoint, making the ROM essentially a *programmable pull-down network*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Definition:** A ROM is a combinational logic network whose function table is "frozen" into silicon. The address lines are the inputs, and the bit lines are the outputs — ROM is therefore nothing but a *look-up table* implemented in hardware.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Vending Machine Grid**
> Imagine a 4×4 grid of buttons (rows) and indicator lights (columns). For each button pressed, a specific pattern of lights turns on — but this pattern is **physically engraved** on the machine's internal wiring. You can read the pattern (press a button → see lights), but you cannot change which lights turn on for a given button.
>
> - The **rows** are *word lines* (address inputs).
> - The **columns** are *bit lines* (data outputs).
> - A **wired connection** at a crosspoint means "pull this light OFF when its row is pressed" → stores a **logic 0**.
> - An **empty crosspoint** (no connection) means "leave this light ON" → stores a **logic 1**.

The **NOR ROM** is like a parallel parking lot — every spot is accessible directly from the road. The **NAND ROM** is like cars parked nose-to-tail in a narrow alley — you must move the first to reach the last.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Significance |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Supply voltage $V_{DD}$ | **5 V** (legacy) / **3.3 V** / **1.8 V** | Power supply for pull-up loads |
| Threshold voltage $V_{TN}$ | **0.7 V** (NMOS, enhancement) | Cell transistor turn-on |
| Feature size $\lambda$ | **0.25 µm – 65 nm** | Scaling metric |
| Cell area (NOR) | $A_{cell} = 8\lambda \times 6\lambda$ | Larger, faster |
| Cell area (NAND) | $A_{cell} = 6\lambda \times 4\lambda$ | Smaller, slower |

> [!NOTE]
> The metric **$\lambda$ (lambda)** is half the minimum feature size and is the standard unit of measurement in KTU's VLSI layout design problems.

## 1.4 Reference Truth Table for our 4×4 Examples

Throughout these notes, we will program the ROM with the following 4-bit words:

| Address (Decimal) | Word Line | $D_3$ | $D_2$ | $D_1$ | $D_0$ | Hex |
|:-----------------:|:---------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|
| 0 | $W_0$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 5 |
| 1 | $W_1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 6 |
| 2 | $W_2$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | C |
| 3 | $W_3$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 3 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

### A) NOR-Based ROM Architecture

- **Structure**: Each bit line has a **PMOS pull-up** to $V_{DD}$ in series with a parallel combination of NMOS transistors (one per row).
- **Programming convention**: 
  - Transistor **present** at cell $(W_i, B_j)$ → stores **logic 0** (when $W_i$ is high, the parallel NMOS pulls $B_j$ to GND).
  - Transistor **absent** (open) → stores **logic 1** (no conduction path; $B_j$ stays at $V_{DD}$ via the pull-up).
- **Read operation**:
  1. Address bits are decoded by the **row decoder**; exactly **one** $W_i$ goes HIGH.
  2. For each bit line $B_j$, all NMOS transistors whose gates are tied to **non-selected** word lines remain OFF.
  3. If a transistor is **present** at $(W_i, B_j)$, it turns ON and pulls $B_j$ to **GND (0)**.
  4. If **absent**, the pull-up keeps $B_j$ at **$V_{DD}$ (1)**.
  5. Bit-line sense amplifiers read the resulting voltage levels.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "NOR"?** Each bit line performs a wired-NOR of the selected word line and the cell programming. The output is HIGH only when the word line is LOW (non-selected). When a word line is selected and a transistor is programmed, the output goes LOW — a classic NOR truth table behavior.

### B) NAND-Based ROM Architecture

- **Structure**: Each bit line has a **PMOS pull-up** to $V_{DD}$, and **NMOS transistors in series** form the pull-down path. Each row contributes **one** series transistor per bit line.
- **Programming convention**:
  - Transistor **present** at cell $(W_i, B_j)$ → contributes to the series pull-down.
  - Transistor **absent** (open) → breaks the series chain, permanently keeping $B_j$ at logic 1.
  - **Logic 0 output** requires **ALL** series transistors in the column to be ON (all $W_i$ HIGH simultaneously) — i.e., unprogrammed NAND ROMs output 1 by default.
- **Read operation**:
  1. **All** word lines are driven HIGH (the row decoder does *not* de-select rows; instead, it de-asserts the bit-line sense enable).
  2. For each bit line, if **every** transistor in the column is present and ON, the series chain conducts and pulls $B_j$ to GND (logic 0).
  3. If **any** transistor in the series is absent (or OFF), the chain is broken and the pull-up keeps $B_j$ at $V_{DD}$ (logic 1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programming Trick in NAND ROM:** Because "0" requires *all* transistors present and "1" requires *at least one* missing, NAND ROMs store the **bit-inverted** version of the desired data. The mask programmer uses the *complement* of the target truth table.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value |
|:------:|:-------:|:---------------:|
| $W_L$ | Pull-up / Pull-down ratio | $W_L = (W/L)_{pu} / (W/L)_{pd}$ |
| $t_{pHL}$ | High-to-Low delay (bit line) | $t_{pHL} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{eqn} \cdot C_{bit}$ |
| $t_{pLH}$ | Low-to-High delay (bit line) | $t_{pLH} \approx R_{pu} \cdot C_{bit} \cdot \ln 2$ |
| $P_{static}$ | Static power (NOR ROM, unselected) | $P_{static} = V_{DD}^2 / R_{pu}$ per bit line |
| $P_{dyn}$ | Dynamic power (NAND ROM) | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_{bit} V_{DD}^2 f$ |
| $A_{cell}^{NOR}$ | NOR cell area | $\approx 8\lambda \times 6\lambda = 48\lambda^2$ |
| $A_{cell}^{NAND}$ | NAND cell area | $\approx 6\lambda \times 4\lambda = 24\lambda^2$ |
| $I_{leak}$ | Subthreshold leakage per cell | $I_0 \cdot 10^{(V_{GS}-V_{TH})/S}$ |
| $N$ | Number of address bits | $N = \log_2(\text{number of rows})$ |
| $M$ | Number of data bits | $M = \text{number of bit lines}$ |
| Word-line cap | Word-line load | $C_{WL} = M \cdot C_{g,cell} + C_{wire}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** In all problems involving the bit-line RC delay, $R_{eqn}$ is the equivalent pull-down resistance of *all* parallel transistors activated. In the **NOR ROM**, only the selected row contributes (1 path). In the **NAND ROM**, all $N$ rows contribute in **series** — so $R_{eqn}^{NAND} = N \cdot R_{cell}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why ROM NOR/NAND? |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Microcode / Firmware** | Stores the boot instructions of a CPU — must be present at power-on |
| **Lookup Tables (LUTs)** | FPGAs use small ROMs (4×4 to 64×1) to implement arbitrary combinational logic |
| **Character Generators** | Video display ROMs that map ASCII codes to 5×7 dot-matrix glyphs |
| **Code Converters** | BCD-to-7-segment, Binary-to-Gray, etc., implemented as direct look-up |
| **PLDs (PAL/GAL)** | Use AND/NAND planes architecturally identical to ROM decoder/array |
| **D/A Converters** | R-2R or current-steering DACs often use ROM-programmed transistor widths |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Cross-Connect:** The NOR/NAND ROM is the **physical incarnation** of Sum-of-Products (SOP) and Product-of-Sums (POS) Boolean logic. NOR ROM = AND plane + OR plane; NAND ROM = OR plane + AND plane. This is the foundation of Programmable Logic Arrays (PLAs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Programming the 4×4 NOR ROM

### Step 1 — Lay out the word lines and bit lines

The 4×4 NOR ROM is a grid with $N=4$ word lines ($W_0, W_1, W_2, W_3$) running horizontally and $M=4$ bit lines ($B_0, B_1, B_2, B_3$) running vertically. Each bit line is terminated at the top with a PMOS pull-up transistor whose gate is tied to GND (always ON, active load).

### Step 2 — Map the truth table to transistor placement

Using the rule: **Transistor present ⇒ stores 0**, we inspect each word of the target data:

For **$W_0 = 1\,0\,1\,0$** (i.e., $B_3 B_2 B_1 B_0 = 1\,0\,1\,0$):
- $D_3 = 1$ → no transistor at $(W_0, B_3)$
- $D_2 = 0$ → transistor at $(W_0, B_2)$
- $D_1 = 1$ → no transistor at $(W_0, B_1)$
- $D_0 = 0$ → transistor at $(W_0, B_0)$

For **$W_1 = 0\,1\,0\,1$**:
- $D_3 = 0$ → transistor at $(W_1, B_3)$
- $D_2 = 1$ → no transistor at $(W_1, B_2)$
- $D_1 = 0$ → transistor at $(W_1, B_1)$
- $D_0 = 1$ → no transistor at $(W_1, B_0)$

For **$W_2 = 1\,1\,0\,0$**:
- $D_3 = 1$ → no transistor at $(W_2, B_3)$
- $D_2 = 1$ → no transistor at $(W_2, B_2)$
- $D_1 = 0$ → transistor at $(W_2, B_1)$
- $D_0 = 0$ → transistor at $(W_2, B_0)$

For **$W_3 = 0\,0\,1\,1$**:
- $D_3 = 0$ → transistor at $(W_3, B_3)$
- $D_2 = 0$ → transistor at $(W_3, B_2)$
- $D_1 = 1$ → no transistor at $(W_3, B_1)$
- $D_0 = 1$ → no transistor at $(W_3, B_0)$

### Step 3 — Build the 4×4 transistor matrix

$$
T_{NOR} = \begin{bmatrix} B_3 & B_2 & B_1 & B_0 \\ \hline 0 & T & 0 & T \\ T & 0 & T & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & T & T \\ T & T & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{matrix} W_0 \\ W_1 \\ W_2 \\ W_3 \end{matrix}
$$

Here, "$T$" denotes a programmed transistor (NMOS present); "$0$" denotes an open cell (no transistor).

### Step 4 — Read-cycle analysis (verify correctness)

When address = 00 (i.e., $W_0$ is HIGH, others LOW):

$$
B_0 = \overline{W_0 \cdot 1 + W_1 \cdot 0 + W_2 \cdot 1 + W_3 \cdot 0} = \overline{1} = 0
$$

$$
B_1 = \overline{W_0 \cdot 0 + W_1 \cdot 1 + W_2 \cdot 1 + W_3 \cdot 0} = \overline{0} = 1
$$

$$
B_2 = \overline{W_0 \cdot 1 + W_1 \cdot 0 + W_2 \cdot 0 + W_3 \cdot 1} = \overline{1} = 0
$$

$$
B_3 = \overline{W_0 \cdot 0 + W_1 \cdot 1 + W_2 \cdot 0 + W_3 \cdot 1} = \overline{0} = 1
$$

Output: $(B_3, B_2, B_1, B_0) = (1, 0, 1, 0)$ ✓ — matches the target word for $W_0$.

### Step 5 — Pull-up ratio derivation

To guarantee a clean LOW level on the bit line, the on-resistance of the programmed NMOS must be much smaller than the PMOS pull-up:

$$
R_{pd,on} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{GS}-V_{TN})}
$$

$$
R_{pu,on} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p (V_{DD}-\vert V_{TP} \vert)}
$$

For a valid logic LOW ($V_{OL} \leq 0.2 V_{DD}$):

$$
V_{OL} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_{pd,on}}{R_{pu,on} + R_{pd,on}} \leq 0.2 V_{DD}
$$

Solving the inequality:

$$
R_{pd,on} \leq 0.25 \cdot R_{pu,on}
$$

Substituting the resistance expressions and assuming $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \mu_p$:

$$
\frac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n} \leq 0.25 \implies \frac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} \leq 0.625
$$

Therefore, the **pull-up ratio** for a NOR ROM must satisfy:

$$
W_L = \frac{(W/L)_{pu}}{(W/L)_{pd}} \leq 0.625
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU problems, if a fixed ratio of $W_p/L_p = 2$ and $W_n/L_n = 4$ is given, the ratio is $0.5$, which satisfies the constraint — the cell correctly pulls down to a valid LOW.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Programming the 4×4 NAND ROM

### Step 1 — Understand the inverted programming rule

In a NAND ROM, a **logic 0** output requires **all** $N$ series transistors to be ON. Therefore, a cell $(W_i, B_j)$ must contain a transistor if the *complement* of the desired data bit is 0 at that row-column pair.

The stored data in NAND ROM is the **bitwise complement** of the target data:

$$
D_{NAND}(W_i, B_j) = \overline{D_{target}(W_i, B_j)}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the complement of the target truth table

| Word Line | $D_3$ | $D_2$ | $D_1$ | $D_0$ | $\overline{D_3}$ | $\overline{D_2}$ | $\overline{D_1}$ | $\overline{D_0}$ |
|:---------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:----------------:|:----------------:|:----------------:|:----------------:|
| $W_0$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $W_1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $W_2$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $W_3$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |

### Step 3 — Build the 4×4 NAND transistor matrix

A "1" in the complemented table means: **transistor present** (so that when all word lines are HIGH, the bit line goes LOW — reading back the *original* 0). A "0" means: **transistor absent** (bit line stays HIGH — reading back the *original* 1).

$$
T_{NAND} = \begin{bmatrix} B_3 & B_2 & B_1 & B_0 \\ \hline T & 0 & T & 0 \\ 0 & T & 0 & T \\ T & T & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & T & T \end{bmatrix} \begin{matrix} W_0 \\ W_1 \\ W_2 \\ W_3 \end{matrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Bit-line equivalent resistance derivation

Each bit line in a 4×4 NAND ROM has **4 NMOS transistors in series** in its pull-down path. The equivalent ON-resistance is:

$$
R_{eq}^{NAND} = 4 \cdot R_{cell} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD}-V_{TN})}
$$

For a comparison, in the NOR ROM, only **one** transistor is activated per read cycle, so:

$$
R_{eq}^{NOR} = R_{cell}
$$

Therefore, the bit-line discharge time constant for NAND is roughly **4× slower** than NOR (assuming identical transistor sizing).

### Step 5 — Read-cycle verification (NAND ROM)

In the read mode, the row decoder asserts the selected word line, and all **other** word lines are held HIGH as well (this is the inverse of NOR operation). For address = 11 ($W_3$ selected, with the row decoder pattern chosen so that *all* unselected word lines also stay high in the NAND addressing scheme, or specifically the column sense line is grounded by the decoder):

For bit line $B_0$ when $W_3$ is the *only* low-selected line (in a true single-line select mode), the chain contains a missing transistor at $(W_3, B_0)$, so:

$$
B_0 = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_{pu}}{R_{pu} + R_{eq}^{NAND,broken}} = V_{DD} \quad \text{(logic 1)} \checkmark
$$

For bit line $B_1$ in the same condition, the chain has a missing transistor at $(W_3, B_1)$:

$$
B_1 = V_{DD} \quad \text{(logic 1)} \checkmark
$$

For bit line $B_2$ (transistor present at $(W_3, B_2)$, but $(W_0, B_2)$ and $(W_1, B_2)$ also present), if all word lines are driven HIGH except one which breaks the chain — the bit line evaluates accordingly.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget the *complement* when programming a NAND ROM. The mask used by the foundry is the **inverted** version of the desired data.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | NOR ROM | NAND ROM | Winner |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|
| Cell area | $48 \lambda^2$ | $24 \lambda^2$ | **NAND** (denser) |
| Read access time | Fast (1 transistor) | Slow (4 series Rs) | **NOR** |
| Static power (unselected) | Low (no DC path) | **High** (DC path through all pull-ups & selected chain) | **NOR** |
| Static power (selected) | **High** (DC through pull-up) | Low (no DC if sized correctly) | **NAND** |
| Capacitive load on word line | $M \cdot C_g$ | $M \cdot C_g$ (same) | Tie |
| Capacitive load on bit line | $N \cdot C_{d} + C_{wire}$ | $N \cdot C_{d} + C_{wire}$ (same) | Tie |
| Programming complexity | Direct data | Complement of data | **NOR** |
| Soft-error sensitivity | Higher (small stored charge) | Lower | **NAND** |
| Typical use | Cache tag, BIOS | Mass storage (NAND Flash) | Application-specific |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — 4×4 NOR ROM

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Address A0] --> DEC[Row Decoder 2x4]
    A2[Address A1] --> DEC
    DEC --> WL0[Word Line W0]
    DEC --> WL1[Word Line W1]
    DEC --> WL2[Word Line W2]
    DEC --> WL3[Word Line W3]
    WL0 --> ARRAY[NOR ROM Cell Array 4x4]
    WL1 --> ARRAY
    WL2 --> ARRAY
    WL3 --> ARRAY
    ARRAY --> SA0[Sense Amp B0]
    ARRAY --> SA1[Sense Amp B1]
    ARRAY --> SA2[Sense Amp B2]
    ARRAY --> SA3[Sense Amp B3]
    SA0 --> OUT0[Data Out D0]
    SA1 --> OUT1[Data Out D1]
    SA2 --> OUT2[Data Out D2]
    SA3 --> OUT3[Data Out D3]
    VDD[VDD Supply] --> PU0[PMOS Pullup B0]
    VDD --> PU1[PMOS Pullup B1]
    VDD --> PU2[PMOS Pullup B2]
    VDD --> PU3[PMOS Pullup B3]
    PU0 --> SA0
    PU1 --> SA1
    PU2 --> SA2
    PU3 --> SA3
```

```mermaid

    A1[Address A0] --> DEC[Row Decoder 2x4]

    NPU2 --> NSA2

    NSA2 --> NOUT2[Data Out D2]

```

```mermaid
graph TB
    W0[Word Line W0] --- N03T[NMOS at W0 B3: ABSENT]
```

## 4.4 NAND ROM Cell Matrix — Series Transistor Chains

```mermaid
graph LR
    W0N[Word Line W0] --- C30A[NMOS W0 to B3]
    C30A --- C31A[NMOS W1 to B3]
    C31A --- C32A[NMOS W2 to B3]
    C32A --- C33A[NMOS W3 to B3]
    C33A --- SB3[Bit Line B3 Sense]
    W1N[Word Line W1] --- C00A[NMOS W0 to B0]

    C01A --- C02A[NMOS W2 to B0]
    C02A --- C03A[NMOS W3 to B0]
    C03A --- SB0[Bit Line B0 Sense]
    W2N[Word Line W2] --- C10A[NMOS W0 to B1]
    C10A --- C11A[NMOS W1 to B1]
    C11A --- C12A[NMOS W2 to B1]

    W3N[Word Line W3] --- C20A[NMOS W0 to B2]
```
