```mermaid
    C22A --- C23A[NMOS W3 to B2]
    C23A --- SB2[Bit Line B2 Sense]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above shows the **series chain topology** of the NAND ROM. Each bit line is a *vertical stack* of NMOS transistors. The reader is encouraged to redraw this in stick-diagram or layout form for KTU's design component.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

### Q1. Differentiate between NOR and NAND ROM architectures in terms of cell structure and access time. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | NOR ROM | NAND ROM |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Cell structure | NMOS transistors in **parallel** per bit line | NMOS transistors in **series** per bit line |
| Programming rule | Transistor present = 0 | Transistor present (in series, all activated) = 0; data is stored as **complement** |
| Read access | **Fast** (one transistor pulls bit line low) | **Slow** (4 series transistors → 4× resistance) |
| Density | Lower (each cell needs isolation) | Higher (cells share drain/source — like NAND Flash) |
| Power | High static when selected | Low static when selected |

(1 mark per distinct point × 3 = 3 marks)

---

### Q2. Why is a pull-up device required on every bit line of a NOR ROM? What happens if its W/L ratio is chosen too small? (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

- The pull-up PMOS provides the **default HIGH logic level** on the bit line when no pull-down path is activated. Without it, a bit line with an unprogrammed cell would be **floating** (high-impedance), leading to undefined logic levels and dynamic power dissipation from charge sharing. **(1 mark)**
- If the pull-up $W/L$ is chosen **too small** (high $R_{pu}$), two problems occur: (i) the HIGH-to-LOW transition $t_{pHL}$ becomes slow because the pull-up fights the pull-down, and (ii) the static LOW level $V_{OL}$ rises above the valid LOW margin because the voltage divider $R_{pu}/(R_{pu}+R_{pd})$ is too large. **(1 mark)**
- The standard constraint derived in the lecture is: $V_{OL} \leq 0.2 V_{DD}$, which forces $R_{pd,on} \leq 0.25 R_{pu,on}$, or equivalently $W_L = (W/L)_{pu}/(W/L)_{pd} \leq 0.625$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern, Module 4]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Design a **4×4 NOR ROM** to store the following data and draw the complete cell array showing the NMOS transistors and PMOS pull-ups. Mark the word lines, bit lines, and supply rails. (7 marks)

| Address | $D_3 D_2 D_1 D_0$ |
|:-------:|:-----------------:|
| 00 | 1 0 1 1 |
| 01 | 0 1 0 0 |
| 10 | 1 1 1 0 |
| 11 | 0 0 1 1 |

**(b)** For the ROM in part (a), derive the bit-line voltage $V_{OL}$ when address = 10 is selected, given that the cell NMOS has $W/L = 4/2$ and the PMOS pull-up has $W/L = 2/2$, with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\,\mu A/V^2$, $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50\,\mu A/V^2$, $V_{TN} = 0.7\,V$, $V_{TP} = -0.7\,V$, and $V_{DD} = 5\,V$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** Identify cells with **0** in the data (these get transistors in NOR ROM).

| Address | $D_3$ | $D_2$ | $D_1$ | $D_0$ | Cells with Transistor |
|:-------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:----------------------:|
| 00 ($W_0$) | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $(W_0, B_2)$ |
| 01 ($W_1$) | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $(W_1, B_3), (W_1, B_1), (W_1, B_0)$ |
| 10 ($W_2$) | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | $(W_2, B_0)$ |
| 11 ($W_3$) | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $(W_3, B_3), (W_3, B_2)$ |

**Step 2:** Draw the matrix:

- 4 horizontal word lines ($W_0, W_1, W_2, W_3$)
- 4 vertical bit lines ($B_0, B_1, B_2, B_3$)
- 4 PMOS pull-ups at the top of each bit line (gate to GND)
- NMOS transistors at the crosspoints listed above; other crosspoints are **open**

**Marking Distribution (a):** Truth-table-to-cell mapping (3 marks), cell matrix drawing (3 marks), labeling (1 mark) = **7 marks**

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** When address = 10 is selected, $W_2$ is HIGH. From the data, $(W_2, B_0)$ has a transistor, so $B_0$ pulls down. $B_1, B_2, B_3$ have no transistor in row $W_2$, so they stay HIGH.

**Step 2:** Compute $R_{pd,on}$ for the activated cell.

The cell NMOS is in **deep triode** when $V_{DS} = V_{OL}$ is small. The on-resistance in triode is:

$$
R_{pd,on} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{GS}-V_{TN})}
$$

Substituting $V_{GS} = V_{DD} = 5\,V$:

$$
R_{pd,on} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times (5 - 0.7)}
$$

$$
R_{pd,on} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 4.3}
$$

$$
R_{pd,on} = \frac{1}{860 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1162.79\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $R_{pu,on}$ for the PMOS pull-up.

The PMOS is always ON (gate at GND), so $V_{SG} = V_{DD} = 5\,V$:

$$
R_{pu,on} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p (V_{SG}-\vert V_{TP} \vert)}
$$

$$
R_{pu,on} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times (5 - 0.7)}
$$

$$
R_{pu,on} = \frac{1}{215 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 4651.16\,\Omega
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $V_{OL}$ using the voltage divider (steady-state):

$$
V_{OL} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_{pd,on}}{R_{pu,on} + R_{pd,on}}
$$

$$
V_{OL} = 5 \cdot \frac{1162.79}{4651.16 + 1162.79}
$$

$$
V_{OL} = 5 \cdot \frac{1162.79}{5813.95}
$$

$$
V_{OL} \approx 5 \times 0.2 = 1.0\,V
$$

**Step 5:** Verify against the noise margin.

For a valid logic LOW, $V_{OL} \leq 0.2 V_{DD} = 1.0\,V$. The calculated value is right at the margin boundary — the design is acceptable but marginal. To improve, increase $(W/L)_n$ or decrease $(W/L)_p$.

**Marking Distribution (b):** Resistance formulas (2 marks), numerical substitution (2 marks), voltage divider (2 marks), noise-margin interpretation (1 mark) = **7 marks**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and read operation of a **4×4 NAND ROM** cell array. State the programming rule explicitly and explain why the stored pattern is the bitwise complement of the desired output. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 4×4 NAND ROM has cell NMOS transistors with $W/L = 2/2$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100\,\mu A/V^2$, $V_{TN} = 0.7\,V$, $V_{DD} = 5\,V$. If the PMOS pull-up has $W/L = 1/2$, compute (i) the bit-line equivalent resistance, (ii) the access time $t_{pHL}$ for a bit-line capacitance of $C_{bit} = 200\,fF$, and (iii) compare the access time with an equivalent NOR ROM using the same cell parameters. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Construction:**
- 4 word lines $W_0, W_1, W_2, W_3$ running horizontally.
- 4 bit lines $B_0, B_1, B_2, B_3$ running vertically, each terminated at the top with a PMOS pull-up to $V_{DD}$ (gate at GND).
- Each bit line contains **4 NMOS transistors in series** in its pull-down path.
- The **gate** of the $i$-th series transistor on bit line $B_j$ is connected to word line $W_i$.
- The **bottom** of the series chain is grounded; the **top** connects to the bit line.

**Programming rule:**
- A transistor **present** at position $(W_i, B_j)$ contributes to the pull-down chain.
- A transistor **absent** (open) **breaks** the chain for that bit line permanently.
- Output $B_j = 0$ only if **all** four transistors along the chain are **present AND ON** (i.e., all $W_i = 1$).
- Therefore, to store a **0** in the output, the foundry must include the transistor; to store a **1**, the foundry must **omit** the transistor.
- Since the **natural** (unprogrammed) state of an NMOS chain is "all transistors present → output 0 when all word lines asserted", the **stored pattern** is the **complement** of the desired data: $D_{stored} = \overline{D_{output}}$.

**Read operation:**
1. The address activates the appropriate word line(s) per the NAND ROM addressing scheme (all $W_i$ are pulled HIGH during a read; the row decoder controls which word is *sensed* by enabling a ground-return path or column multiplexer).
2. For each bit line, the series chain conducts to GND only if **no transistor is missing**.
3. Sense amplifiers detect whether $B_j$ is LOW (chain intact, all $W_i$ HIGH) or HIGH (chain broken, or some $W_i$ LOW).
4. The output buffer presents the data to the external bus.

**Marking Distribution (a):** Construction description (2 marks), programming rule with complement justification (3 marks), read operation (2 marks) = **7 marks**

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Bit-line equivalent resistance in NAND ROM:**

Each NMOS in the series chain has the same on-resistance:

$$
R_{cell} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{GS}-V_{TN})}
$$

With $V_{GS} = V_{DD} = 5\,V$:

$$
R_{cell} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times (5 - 0.7)} = \frac{1}{430 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2325.58\,\Omega
$$

For 4 series transistors:

$$
R_{eq}^{NAND} = 4 \times 2325.58 = 9302.33\,\Omega
$$

**(ii) Access time $t_{pHL}$:**

$$
t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq}^{NAND} \cdot C_{bit}
$$

$$
t_{pHL} = 0.69 \times 9302.33 \times 200 \times 10^{-15}
$$

$$
t_{pHL} = 0.69 \times 9302.33 \times 2 \times 10^{-13}
$$

$$
t_{pHL} \approx 1.284 \times 10^{-9}\,s = 1.284\,ns
$$

**(iii) Comparison with NOR ROM:**

In the NOR ROM, only **one** transistor is active per read, so:

$$
R_{eq}^{NOR} = R_{cell} = 2325.58\,\Omega
$$

$$
t_{pHL}^{NOR} = 0.69 \times 2325.58 \times 200 \times 10^{-15} \approx 0.321\,ns
$$

Speed ratio:

$$
\frac{t_{pHL}^{NAND}}{t_{pHL}^{NOR}} = \frac{9302.33}{2325.58} = 4
$$

So the NAND ROM is **exactly 4× slower** than the NOR ROM (this factor scales linearly with the number of series transistors, i.e., the number of address bits).

**Marking Distribution (b):** Resistance derivation (2 marks), access time calculation (2 marks), comparison and ratio (2 marks), conclusion (1 mark) = **7 marks**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the complement in NAND ROM programming (–2 to –3 marks):** A student who programs a NAND ROM with the *original* data (instead of the complement) is **fundamentally wrong** and will lose the bulk of the marks. The mask used in fabrication is the inverted data.
>
> 2. **Confusing pull-up vs pull-down ratios (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students use the wrong direction for the inequality. Recall: for a *valid LOW*, the NMOS pull-down must be **stronger** (lower resistance) than the PMOS pull-up. So $(W/L)_n > (W/L)_p$, giving $W_L < 1$.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the body-effect in $V_{TN}$ adjustment (–1 mark):** In real layouts, the source of the cell NMOS is not at GND but at the bit-line voltage $V_{OL}$. The threshold voltage increases by $\gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$. KTU sometimes gives a "corrected" $V_{TN}$ to use.
>
> 4. **Drawing word lines and bit lines as **lines without** the PMOS pull-up (–1 mark):** The pull-up is structurally essential. Examiners mark this omission strictly.
>
> 5. **Using a single voltage-divider formula without checking the triode/saturation regime (–1 mark):** When $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TN}$, the NMOS is in triode and the simple $R = 1/[\mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{TN})]$ formula applies. KTU expects this regime analysis to be stated explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 4×4 MOS ROM Arrays (NOR and NAND)**
>
> - [x] A **ROM** is a hard-wired look-up table — address → data.
> - [x] **4×4** means 4 word lines × 4 bit lines → 16 crosspoints.
> - [x] In **NOR ROM**, each bit line has **parallel** NMOS cells; **present** = 0, **absent** = 1.
> - [x] In **NAND ROM**, each bit line has **series** NMOS cells; stored pattern = **complement** of output.
> - [x] **Pull-up device** is mandatory on every bit line to define the HIGH level.
> - [x] Pull-up ratio constraint: $W_L = (W/L)_{pu}/(W/L)_{pd} \leq 0.625$ for $V_{OL} \leq 0.2 V_{DD}$.
> - [x] NOR is **faster** (1 transistor ON per read), NAND is **denser** (smaller cell area).
> - [x] Static power in **NOR ROM** is high (DC path through pull-up when selected).
> - [x] Static power in **NAND ROM** is low (no DC path if the chain is properly broken).
> - [x] Bit-line RC delay: $t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_{bit}$.
> - [x] For an $N$-row NAND ROM, $R_{eq}^{NAND} = N \cdot R_{cell}$.
> - [x] For an $N$-row NOR ROM, $R_{eq}^{NOR} = R_{cell}$ (only the selected row conducts).
> - [x] NOR ROM is the standard choice for **random access** (short word); NAND ROM is the basis of **NAND Flash memory** (high density).
> - [x] Always verify with a **truth-table-to-cell mapping** before drawing the matrix.
> - [x] In KTU exams, **state the assumption** for the bit-line capacitance and the cell resistance formula explicitly.
> - [x] ROMs implement **combinational logic** in disguise — the *content* is a Boolean function.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Access Memory - SRAM: Six-Transistor CMOS SRAM Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Random Access Memory - SRAM: Six-Transistor CMOS SRAM Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Random Access Memory (SRAM)** is a **volatile** semiconductor memory that retains data bits as long as power is supplied, using a bistable latch (typically six transistors) as the storage element rather than a refresh capacitor. The **Six-Transistor (6T) CMOS SRAM Cell** is the industry-standard cell topology in which two cross-coupled CMOS inverters form a bistable element, and two NMOS access (pass) transistors connect the storage nodes to the complementary bitlines under the control of a Word Line (WL).

The 6T cell is the workhorse of on-chip cache memory (L1/L2/L3) in virtually all modern microprocessors, digital signal processors, and ASICs because it offers the **fastest access time** of all semiconductor memory families, **zero refresh overhead**, and full **CMOS compatibility** with standard digital logic processes.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **light switch with two stable positions (ON and OFF)** mounted inside a small opaque box. The switch is mechanically spring-loaded so that once pushed to either side, it stays there indefinitely without you holding it — but the moment you cut the power (spring force), the position is forgotten. A small window on each side of the box lets a robotic arm (the *access transistor*) reach in and *push the switch up or down* (write) or just *peek at which side it is on* (read).

In the 6T SRAM cell:
- The **mechanical bistable switch** = two cross-coupled CMOS inverters (M1–M4)
- The **two robotic arms** = the two NMOS access transistors M5, M6
- The **two windows** = the complementary Bit Lines **BL** and **BL_bar**
- The **command signal to the arms** = the Word Line **WL**
- **Cutting power** = V_DD removed → data lost (this is why SRAM is *volatile*)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Cell Metrics in Modern 6T SRAM (45 nm – 7 nm nodes)**
> - Cell area: **0.020 – 0.120 µm²**
> - Supply voltage (V_DD): **0.6 – 1.0 V**
> - Static Noise Margin (SNM): **> 0.2 × V_DD**
> - Access time: **< 1 ns** (cache-grade)
> - Hold static power: **< 1 µW / cell** (leakage dominated)

### 1.3 Why "6T" and Not 4T or 8T?

| Topology | Transistors | Merits | Drawbacks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **4T (loadless resistor)** | 2 PU resistors + 2 PD NMOS + 2 AX | Small cell | Needs refresh, static current |
| **6T (full CMOS)** | 2 PU PMOS + 2 PD NMOS + 2 AX NMOS | **Zero static current in hold, robust SNM, low power** | Larger cell |
| **8T / 10T** | 6T + extra read buffer | Decoupled read port → read-stability not compromised | ~30–60 % larger, used for ultra-low-V_DD |

The 6T cell is the **optimal trade-off** between density, performance, and noise stability — and is therefore the *de-facto* standard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Transistors — Anatomy of the 6T Cell

The 6T cell consists of:

1. **Pull-Up PMOS M1, M3** — load devices of the two inverters. Source tied to V_DD.
2. **Pull-Down NMOS M2, M4** — driver devices of the two inverters. Source tied to GND.
3. **Access NMOS M5, M6** — pass transistors. Gate tied to WL; one terminal at storage node, the other at BL/BL_bar.

The two inverters are *cross-coupled*:
- Inverter 1: M1 (PU) + M2 (PD) → input = node Q_bar, output = node Q
- Inverter 2: M3 (PU) + M4 (PD) → input = node Q, output = node Q_bar

This creates **positive feedback** with loop gain > 1 → two **stable equilibrium points** (Q = 1, Q_bar = 0) and (Q = 0, Q_bar = 1).

### 2.2 Three Operating Modes

#### 2.2.1 HOLD (Standby)
- WL = 0 → M5 and M6 OFF → storage nodes are **electrically isolated** from the bitlines.
- The two cross-coupled inverters reinforce each other; static current = leakage only.
- The bitlines are held (or precharged) to V_DD by the column precharge circuitry.

#### 2.2.2 READ
- WL = 1 → M5, M6 turn ON.
- Bitlines BL and BL_bar were **precharged to V_DD** in the previous cycle.
- The storage node that holds a '0' begins to discharge its associated bitline through the series combination of an access transistor and a pull-down NMOS.
- The other bitline stays at ≈ V_DD.
- A small differential voltage **ΔV_BL** develops (typically **50 – 200 mV** in modern designs) which a **Sense Amplifier** amplifies to a full-swing logic output.
- The cell storage must **NOT flip** during read (this is the **read-disturb** problem).

#### 2.2.3 WRITE
- WL = 1 → M5, M6 turn ON.
- The write driver forces BL = 0 and BL_bar = V_DD (to write a '1' to Q) or vice-versa.
- If Q currently stores '0' and we want to write '1': the access transistor M6 and the pull-up M3 must *overpower* the pull-down M2 in order to flip node Q_bar high. This is the **write-ability** problem.

### 2.3 Sizing Constraints (The Heart of 6T Design)

Three transistor-width ratios govern cell stability:

$$ \text{Cell Ratio (CR)} = \frac{(W/L)_{PD}}{(W/L)_{AX}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Read stability} $$

$$ \text{Pull-Up Ratio (PR)} = \frac{(W/L)_{PU}}{(W/L)_{AX}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Write ability} $$

**Read-stability rule:** CR must be large enough so that, during read, the voltage at the internal node storing '0' does **not exceed the inverter switching threshold** (otherwise the cell flips).

**Write-ability rule:** PR must be small enough (i.e. access transistor must be strong relative to the pull-up) so the access device can pull the node low against the pull-up PMOS.

> [!NOTE]
> The two requirements pull in **opposite directions** on the access-transistor strength — this is the classic **read-stability vs. write-ability trade-off** that every 6T designer must balance.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static Noise Margin (Largest inscribed square, Seevinck) | $\text{SNM} \approx \sqrt{\beta_R} - 1$ (normalized to V_DD), where $\beta_R = (W/L)_{PD}/(W/L)_{AX}$ | Read stability metric |
| 2 | Bitline differential during read | $\Delta V_{BL} = \dfrac{V_{DD} \cdot C_{BL}}{C_{BL} + C_{cell}} \cdot (1 - e^{-t_{read}/\tau})$ | Develops $\Delta V$ across BL pair |
| 3 | Read time constant | $\tau_{read} = \dfrac{C_{BL} \cdot (V_{DD}/\Delta V_{BL})}{I_{cell}}$ | Time to develop detectable ΔV |
| 4 | Cell static current (hold) | $I_{hold} = I_{leak} = I_{sub} + I_{gate} + I_{junc}$ | Should be ≈ 0 ideally |
| 5 | Dynamic power (per cycle) | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_{BL} V_{DD}^2 f$ | α = activity factor |
| 6 | Total write power | $P_{write} = C_{BL} V_{DD}^2 f$ | Two BLs toggle every write |
| 7 | Cell ratio (CR) | $CR = (W/L)_{PD} / (W/L)_{AX}$ | Recommended **1.5 – 3** |
| 8 | Pull-up ratio (PR) | $PR = (W/L)_{PU} / (W/L)_{AX}$ | Recommended **< 1.5** for easy write |
| 9 | SNM (Largest inscribed square) | Geometric construction on butterfly curve | Maximum DC noise tolerance |
| 10 | Subthreshold leakage | $I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 e^{(V_{GS}-V_{th})/nV_T}\!\left(1 - e^{-V_{DS}/V_T}\right)$ | Dominant at sub-90 nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem on 6T SRAM appears in the exam, you will be asked to compute either SNM from the butterfly curve, or the cell ratio / pull-up ratio needed to guarantee read-stability or write-ability. Memorize formulas (1), (7), and (8) above and the units V, V/µm, and dimensionless ratio.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprocessor cache (L1/L2/L3)**: 6T arrays occupy 30 – 60 % of total die area in modern CPUs.
- **Network routers / ASICs**: route lookup tables, packet buffers.
- **Portable / IoT devices**: low-V_DD (0.6 V) operation is enabled by 8T/10T variants derived from the 6T topology.
- **FPGA configuration memory** (in some architectures): 6T-style latches store LUT bits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Read-Disturb Voltage V_R

Assume node Q = 0, Q_bar = 1 (i.e. M2 ON, M1 OFF, M4 OFF, M3 ON).
During read, WL goes high and M5, M6 turn on. BL and BL_bar are precharged to V_DD.

Consider the storage node Q = 0. The access transistor M5 and the pull-down M2 form a **resistive divider** between V_DD (on BL) and GND (Q = 0). Because Q is *trying* to remain at 0, the divider drives Q *upward* to a small voltage V_R.

Using the saturation-region current equation for M5 (V_DS = V_DD − V_R ≈ V_DD, so **saturation**) and the linear-region current for M2 (V_DS = V_R, small, so **triode**):

Current through M5 (saturation):

$$ I_{M5} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{AX} (V_{DD} - V_R - V_{thn})^2 $$

Current through M2 (triode, V_DS = V_R is small):

$$ I_{M2} = \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{PD} \left[(V_{GS} - V_{thn}) V_R - \frac{V_R^2}{2}\right] $$

At node Q, the two currents are equal (KCL). Equating:

$$ \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{AX} (V_{DD} - V_R - V_{thn})^2 = \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{PD} \left[(V_{GS} - V_{thn}) V_R - \frac{V_R^2}{2}\right] $$

The gate of M2 is driven by Q_bar = 1 = V_DD, so V_GS2 = V_DD. Also (V_GS2 − V_thn) = (V_DD − V_thn). Substituting and dividing by the common factor μ_n C_ox:

$$ \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{AX} (V_{DD} - V_R - V_{thn})^2 = \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{PD} \left[(V_{DD} - V_{thn}) V_R - \frac{V_R^2}{2}\right] $$

Now define the cell ratio:

$$ CR = \frac{(W/L)_{PD}}{(W/L)_{AX}} $$

Dividing both sides by (W/L)_AX, and using V_thn ≡ V_t for brevity:

$$ \frac{1}{2} (V_{DD} - V_R - V_t)^2 = CR \left[(V_{DD} - V_t) V_R - \frac{V_R^2}{2}\right] $$

Solving for V_R (treating the right side as a quadratic in V_R):

$$ CR \cdot V_R^2 - 2\,CR\,(V_{DD} - V_t)\,V_R + (V_{DD} - V_R - V_t)^2 = 0 $$

This is a transcendental equation; closed-form in V_R is messy, but for **design estimation** the small-signal approximation (V_R ≪ V_DD − V_t) gives:

$$ V_R \approx \frac{(V_{DD} - V_t)}{2\,CR + 1} $$

**The cell flips if V_R exceeds the inverter switching threshold V_M.** The *read-stability* design rule is therefore:

$$ V_R < V_M \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{(V_{DD} - V_t)}{2\,CR + 1} < V_M \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; CR > \frac{(V_{DD} - V_t) - V_M}{2\,V_M} $$

**Example numerical evaluation** (typical 0.18 µm process): V_DD = 1.8 V, V_t = 0.5 V, V_M ≈ 0.9 V.

$$ CR > \frac{(1.8 - 0.5) - 0.9}{2 \times 0.9} = \frac{0.4}{1.8} \approx 0.22 $$

This is a very weak bound; in practice, designers use **CR ≥ 1.5** to also guard against process / voltage / temperature (PVT) variations and noise coupling.

### 3.2 Derivation of Static Noise Margin (SNM) — Largest Inscribed Square Method

The two cross-coupled inverters, when plotted as V_out vs V_in of one inverter, give the **standard CMOS VTC** (steep, near-ideal). When *inverter 1's VTC* and the *mirror image of inverter 2's VTC* are plotted on the same axes, you obtain the **butterfly curve**.

The **SNM** is the side length of the **largest square** that can be inscribed inside the two lobes of the butterfly curve.

Normalized to V_DD (set V_DD = 1, V_t = 0):

$$ \text{SNM} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{CR}} \quad \text{(Seevinck, 1987, ideal inverter approximation)} $$

Equivalently:

$$ \text{SNM} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{(W/L)_{PD}/(W/L)_{AX}}} $$

**Design rule for robust hold stability in modern sub-micron 6T cells:**

$$ \text{SNM} > 0.2 \times V_{DD} $$

### 3.3 Derivation of Bitline Differential During Read

Let C_BL be the bitline capacitance per column. C_cell is the small storage-node capacitance. The cell discharges C_BL through M5 + M2 (or M6 + M4) with a time-averaged current I_cell.

$$ \Delta V_{BL}(t) = V_{DD}\!\left(1 - \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-t}{\tau_{read}}\right)\right) \cdot \dfrac{C_{BL}}{C_{BL} + C_{cell}} $$

The time constant is:

$$ \tau_{read} = \dfrac{C_{BL}\,V_{DD}}{I_{cell}} $$

For the sense amplifier to resolve a logic decision we typically need:

$$ \Delta V_{BL} \ge \Delta V_{sense,\min} \;\;\text{(typically 50 – 200 mV)} $$

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Critical Design Constraints

| Mode | Key Constraint | Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hold** | $I_{static} = I_{leak} < I_{limit}$ | $I_{leak} = I_{sub} + I_{gate} + I_{junc}$ | Cell must consume near-zero DC current |
| **Hold** | $\text{SNM} \ge 0.2\,V_{DD}$ | $\text{SNM} = (1+\sqrt{CR})^{-1} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Robust against DC noise & mismatch |
| **Read** | $V_R < V_M$ | $V_R \approx (V_{DD}-V_t)/(2CR+1)$ | No read-disturb flip |
| **Read** | $\Delta V_{BL} \ge \Delta V_{sense}$ | $\Delta V_{BL} = V_{DD}(1-e^{-t/\tau_{read}})$ | Sense amp can detect |
| **Write** | $V_{Q_{old}=0} \uparrow > V_M$ when BL = 0 | $PR = (W/L)_{PU}/(W/L)_{AX} < 1.5$ | Cell must be flipped by access + pull-up path |
| **Write** | $t_{write} \le t_{WL,pulse}$ | $t_{write} = C_{cell} \Delta V / I_{drive}$ | Bit must flip within WL pulse width |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Transistor-Level Schematic of the 6T Cell

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD["VDD"]
    GND["GND"]
    WL["Word Line WL"]
    BL["Bit Line BL"]
    BLB["Bit Line Bar BL_bar"]
    M1["M1 PMOS Pull Up 1"]
    M2["M2 NMOS Pull Down 1"]
    M3["M3 PMOS Pull Up 2"]
    M4["M4 NMOS Pull Down 2"]
    M5["M5 NMOS Access 1"]
    M6["M6 NMOS Access 2"]
    Q["Storage Node Q"]
    QB["Storage Node Q_bar"]

    VDD --- M1
    M1 --- Q
    Q --- M2
    M2 --- GND

    VDD --- M3
    M3 --- QB
    QB --- M4
    M4 --- GND

    Q --- M5
    M5 --- BL
    M5 --- WL

    QB --- M6
    M6 --- BLB
    M6 --- WL
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete SRAM Array

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Address Bus A0 to An"] --> B["Row Decoder"]
    A --> C["Column Decoder"]
    B --> D["Word Line Driver"]
    D --> E["Memory Array 6T Cells M times N"]
    C --> F["Column Mux"]
    E --> G["Bitlines BL and BL_bar Pairs"]
    G --> F
    F --> H["Sense Amplifier"]
    H --> I["Write Driver"]
    I --> G
    H --> J["Data Output Bus"]
    K["Precharge Circuit VDD"] --> G
    L["Timing and Control"] --> K
    L --> D
    L --> H
    L --> I
```

### 4.3 Read Operation — Voltage Waveform / Event Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Cycle 1 Precharge BL BL_bar to VDD"] --> S2["Cycle 2 WL rises to VDD"]
    S2 --> S3["Storage node storing 0 discharges its bitline"]
    S3 --> S4["DeltaV develops on BL pair 50 to 200 mV"]
    S4 --> S5["Sense Amp enabled fires"]
    S5 --> S6["Full swing digital output on data bus"]
    S6 --> S7["WL falls BL precharge next cycle"]
```

### 4.4 Write Operation — Voltage Waveform / Event Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1["Write driver forces BL to VDD and BL_bar to 0"] --> T2["WL rises to VDD"]
    T2 --> T3["Access transistor pulls internal Q_bar down through BL_bar driver"]
    T3 --> T4["Q_bar crosses inverter switching threshold V_M"]
    T4 --> T5["Positive feedback flips Q to VDD and Q_bar to 0"]
    T5 --> T6["WL falls cell now stores new bit"]
```

### 4.5 SNM — Butterfly Curve (Conceptual, Mermaid Block Fallback)

```mermaid
graph TD
    U["SNM Analysis Butterfly Curve Construction"]
    U --> V1["Plot VTC of Inverter 1 Vout Q versus Vin Q_bar"]
    U --> V2["Plot mirrored VTC of Inverter 2 Vout Q_bar versus Vin Q"]
    V1 --> W["Superimpose on same axes"]
    V2 --> W
    W --> X["Two stable lobes appear at the crossings"]
    X --> Y["Inscribe the largest square inside each lobe"]
    Y --> Z["SNM equals side of the largest inscribed square"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation tip for KTU board exams:** When the examiner asks you to "explain SNM using the butterfly curve," the steps in the diagram above are exactly what you must write: plot the two inverter VTCs, mirror one of them, superimpose, identify the two stable crossings, and inscribe the largest square.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the six transistors of a standard 6T CMOS SRAM cell and state the role of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Transistor | Type | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **M1** | PMOS | Pull-up load of inverter 1 (drives Q high) |
| **M2** | NMOS | Pull-down driver of inverter 1 (drives Q low) |
| **M3** | PMOS | Pull-up load of inverter 2 (drives Q_bar high) |
| **M4** | NMOS | Pull-down driver of inverter 2 (drives Q_bar low) |
| **M5** | NMOS | Access transistor connecting Q to BL (gated by WL) |
| **M6** | NMOS | Access transistor connecting Q_bar to BL_bar (gated by WL) |

Cross-coupling of M1-M2 and M3-M4 forms the bistable latch; M5-M6 provide read/write port access.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Static Noise Margin (SNM) of an SRAM cell. Why is it important in 6T cell design?*

**Model Answer:**
Static Noise Margin is defined as the **maximum DC noise voltage that can be superimposed on the storage nodes of a cross-coupled SRAM cell without flipping its stored bit**. Geometrically, it is obtained as the side length of the **largest square that can be inscribed within the two lobes of the butterfly curve** formed by the two crossed inverter VTCs (Seevinck's method).

**Importance:**
1. Directly determines **read stability** — low SNM causes *read disturb* failures.
2. Sets a lower bound on V_DD scaling — below a certain V_DD, SNM collapses to zero (the cell becomes unstable).
3. Guards against **process variation and transistor mismatch** in deep sub-micron technologies.
4. Used as a **figure of merit** by foundries when publishing cell libraries.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** With a neat transistor-level schematic, explain the **read operation** of a 6T CMOS SRAM cell. Derive the expression for the read-disturb voltage V_R and discuss the role of the cell ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Static Noise Margin (SNM)** of a 6T SRAM cell using the butterfly curve. How does the cell ratio (CR) affect SNM? A 6T cell is designed in a 0.18 µm process with V_DD = 1.8 V and threshold voltage V_tn = 0.5 V. If (W/L)_PD = 2 and (W/L)_AX = 1, compute the SNM (assume the Seevinck idealised formula SNM = V_DD × (1 + √CR)⁻¹). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Read operation [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Precharge:* Prior to read, the bitlines BL and BL_bar are precharged to V_DD by the column precharge transistors.

*Step 2 — Wordline activation:* The row decoder raises the selected WL to V_DD, turning ON the access transistors M5 and M6. The cell is now electrically connected to the two bitlines.

*Step 3 — Differential development:* Assume Q = 0, Q_bar = 1. Node Q (held at 0 by M2) starts to be pulled up through M5 toward V_DD, simultaneously *discharging* the bitline capacitance C_BL through M5 and M2. Node Q_bar stays at V_DD (held by M3) so BL_bar remains at V_DD.

*Step 4 — Bitline settling:* After a fixed access time t_read, a small differential ΔV_BL = V_BLB − V_BL develops (typically 50 – 200 mV).

*Step 5 — Sensing:* The sense amplifier, enabled by the column timing, amplifies ΔV_BL into a full-rail logic level on the data bus.

*Read-disturb voltage V_R:* The internal node Q does **not** stay at 0 during read — it rises to a small voltage V_R determined by the current divider between M5 (in saturation) and M2 (in triode):

$$ V_R \approx \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{2\,CR + 1}, \quad \text{where } CR = \frac{(W/L)_{PD}}{(W/L)_{AX}} $$

*Cell ratio role:* To prevent read-disturb, we require V_R < V_M (the inverter switching threshold). This sets a **lower bound** on CR. Larger CR → stronger pull-down → smaller V_R → better read stability. Typical design choice: **CR ≥ 1.5**.

**(b) SNM and butterfly curve [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Butterfly construction:* Plot the VTC of inverter 1 (V_out = Q vs V_in = Q_bar) and the *mirrored* VTC of inverter 2 (V_out = Q_bar vs V_in = Q) on the same graph. The two curves cross at three points: the **two outer crossings are stable** (Q = 0 and Q = 1), the **middle crossing is metastable**.

*Step 2 — Largest inscribed square:* The SNM is the side of the largest square that can be drawn between the two lobes of the butterfly, with one corner on each VTC and two corners on a horizontal/vertical line.

*Step 3 — Seevinck's formula (idealised):*

$$ \text{SNM} = \frac{V_{DD}}{1 + \sqrt{CR}} $$

*Step 4 — Effect of CR:* Increasing CR increases the slope (gain) of the VTC near the metastable point, **shrinking the side of the largest square** — counter-intuitively, **larger CR → smaller SNM in the hold condition**, but read stability is governed by the *read* SNM (also called Read SNM / RSNM), where the VTCs are *distorted* by the access transistors.

*Step 5 — Numerical computation:*
Given: V_DD = 1.8 V, (W/L)_PD = 2, (W/L)_AX = 1.

$$ CR = \frac{(W/L)_{PD}}{(W/L)_{AX}} = \frac{2}{1} = 2 $$

$$ \text{SNM} = \frac{1.8}{1 + \sqrt{2}} = \frac{1.8}{1 + 1.4142} = \frac{1.8}{2.4142} \approx 0.7457 \text{ V} $$

*Step 6 — Comment:* Normalised SNM = 0.7457 / 1.8 = 0.414 → **41.4 % of V_DD**, which comfortably exceeds the industrial target of 0.2 × V_DD = 0.36 V. The design is robust.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** With a circuit schematic, explain the **write operation** of a 6T CMOS SRAM cell. Define the **pull-up ratio (PR)** and explain the design trade-off between read stability and write ability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 32 Kb × 8 SRAM macro is designed using 6T cells. Each cell occupies 120 F² where F = 90 nm. Estimate the **die area** occupied by the memory array (ignoring peripherals), the **bitline capacitance per column** (assume 256 cells per bitline, junction capacitance 1 fF/µm of diffusion width = 1 µm), and the **energy to write one bit** (assume V_DD = 1.0 V, full bitline swing). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Write operation [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Pre-write setup:* The write driver places complementary levels on the bitlines: BL = V_DD, BL_bar = 0 (to write a '1' into Q). The wordline WL is then asserted.

*Step 2 — Internal node Q_bar is the critical one:* Q_bar was previously at V_DD (= 1). With M6 ON and BL_bar = 0, the access transistor M6 (NMOS) discharges node Q_bar toward ground.

*Step 3 — Contention:* M3 (pull-up PMOS) tries to *keep* Q_bar at V_DD. M6 (access NMOS) tries to *pull it down* to 0. The ratio of their strengths decides whether the cell flips.

*Step 4 — Flip condition:* Node Q_bar falls below the inverter switching threshold V_M of inverter 1. Positive feedback then forces Q_bar → 0 and Q → V_DD in a regenerative manner.

*Pull-up ratio (PR):*

$$ PR = \frac{(W/L)_{PU}}{(W/L)_{AX}} $$

The cell can be written when the access transistor overpowers the pull-up. The design rule:

$$ PR < \frac{V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert}{V_{DD} - V_{tn}} \quad \text{(commonly } PR \le 1.5\text{)} $$

*Trade-off:*
- **Read stability** requires **strong pull-down relative to access** (high CR).
- **Write ability** requires **strong access relative to pull-up** (low PR).
- Both conditions imply **moderate-size access transistors** — designers cannot independently tune both, hence the **read-write conflict** that is the central design challenge of 6T cells.

**(b) Array sizing & write-energy [7 marks]**

*Step 1 — Total bits:* 32 Kb × 8 = 32 × 1024 × 8 = **262 144 bits** = **256 Kb**.

*Step 2 — Cell pitch:* With F = 90 nm, each cell area = 120 F² = 120 × (90 nm)² = 120 × 8100 nm² = **972 000 nm² = 0.972 µm²**.

*Step 3 — Memory array area:*

$$ A_{array} = 262\,144 \times 0.972\,\mu m^2 = 254\,804\,\mu m^2 \approx 0.255\,mm^2 $$

*Step 4 — Bitline capacitance:* 256 cells per bitline, each access-transistor diffusion width ≈ 1 µm, junction cap = 1 fF/µm.

$$ C_{BL} = 256 \times 1\,\mu m \times 1\,fF/\mu m = 256\,fF $$

(Plus metal wire capacitance — neglected here per the problem statement.)

*Step 5 — Write energy per bit:* The two bitlines are toggled, but only the one being pulled from V_DD to 0 actually dissipates energy in the cell, while the write driver charges/discharges both. Using the standard CMOS energy model:

$$ E_{write} = C_{BL} \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 256 \times 10^{-15}\,F \times (1.0\,V)^2 = 256 \times 10^{-15}\,J = \mathbf{256\,fJ} $$

*Step 6 — Comment:* At 1 GHz write rate, write power = 256 fJ × 1 GHz = 256 µW per bitline pair — multiplied across 8 bitline pairs in the 8-bit-wide macro = **≈ 2.05 mW**, which is the dominant dynamic-power component of the SRAM.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing CR and PR:** CR = (W/L)_PD / (W/L)_AX (read stability), PR = (W/L)_PU / (W/L)_AX (write ability). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the V_DD in SNM computation:** SNM is in **volts**, not normalised. Always multiply by V_DD.
> 3. **Read vs. Hold SNM:** The *hold* SNM uses the un-distorted butterfly; the *read* SNM is smaller because the access transistors load the cell. Examiners often demand the distinction.
> 4. **Sign errors in V_R formula:** Make sure the denominator is (2·CR + 1), not (2·CR − 1). The voltage rises *with* falling CR, not rising.
> 5. **Units of bitline cap:** Most 90 nm textbooks give cap in **fF/µm of diffusion width** — students must multiply by the number of cells × transistor diffusion width, not by the cell area.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **6T cell = 2 cross-coupled CMOS inverters + 2 NMOS access transistors** — fully static, no refresh.
- [x] **Hold mode:** WL = 0; storage is isolated; only leakage flows.
- [x] **Read mode:** BL & BL_bar precharged to V_DD; WL rises; the '0' side discharges its bitline; ΔV develops (50 – 200 mV); sense amp amplifies.
- [x] **Write mode:** write driver forces BL/BL_bar; access transistor + pull-up must overpower the pull-down to flip the cell.
- [x] **Cell ratio CR = (W/L)_PD / (W/L)_AX ≥ 1.5** → read stability.
- [x] **Pull-up ratio PR = (W/L)_PU / (W/L)_AX ≤ 1.5** → write ability.
- [x] **Read–write conflict:** same access transistor is pulled in opposite directions for read vs. write.
- [x] **SNM** = largest square inside butterfly curve; **Seevinck** formula: SNM = V_DD / (1 + √CR).
- [x] **Read-disturb voltage** V_R ≈ (V_DD − V_t) / (2·CR + 1) — must be < V_M.
- [x] **Power:** static = leakage (subthreshold dominant in sub-90 nm); dynamic = α·C_BL·V_DD²·f.
- [x] **Write energy per bit** ≈ C_BL · V_DD².
- [x] **Modern variants:** 8T (separate read port), 10T (read-decoupled, ultra-low V_DD), DICE (radiation-hard), 6T + assist circuits (WL underdrive, BL boost, negative bitline write).
- [x] **Why 6T dominates:** optimal density-stability-performance trade-off; **fully CMOS-compatible** with logic process; no capacitors; no refresh; lowest access time among all RAM families.
- [x] **Volatility:** SRAM is volatile — power-off = data lost (no mechanical persistence; the bistable latch needs V_DD to remain stable).
- [x] **Industrial role:** SRAM occupies the largest on-chip area fraction in modern CPUs as L1/L2/L3 cache, with 6T as the dominant cell.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU Exam Mnemonic: "Hold Read Write — Invert, Sense, Drive"** — Hold (inverters isolated), Read (sense ΔV on bitlines), Write (drive bitlines strongly). Combine with **"CR for Read, PR for Write"** to recall sizing rules in one breath.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 DRAM — Formal Definition

**Dynamic Random-Access Memory (DRAM)** is a semiconductor memory technology that stores each bit of data as the **presence or absence of electrical charge on a tiny capacitor** (or equivalently, on a parasitic gate capacitance). Because this charge gradually leaks away through sub-threshold conduction and junction leakage, every cell must be **periodically refreshed** (typically every few milliseconds) — hence the name *Dynamic* RAM.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to draw, explain the read/write/refresh operations of the **3-Transistor (3T) DRAM cell** and the **1-Transistor (1T) DRAM cell**, and contrast them with respect to area, read mechanism (destructive vs. non-destructive), and sense-amplifier requirements.

Unlike Static RAM (SRAM — uses 6 transistors and a cross-coupled latch, hence "static"), DRAM cells are **smaller, denser, and cheaper per bit**, which is why DRAM dominates the main-memory market (GB-scale). The trade-off is **latency, refresh overhead, and destructive read** (for 1T).

## 1.2 Two Canonical DRAM Cell Topologies

| Cell Type | Transistors | Storage Element | Read Nature | Density |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **3T DRAM** | 3 NMOS | Parasitic gate capacitance of one transistor | **Non-destructive** | Lower |
| **1T DRAM** | 1 NMOS + 1 capacitor | Explicit MOS capacitor | **Destructive** (needs rewrite) | **Highest** (used in commercial DRAM) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🪣 The Water-Bucket Analogy for a DRAM Cell**
>
> Imagine a **leaky bucket** sitting on a balance:
> - A **full bucket** = logic `1` (stored charge on capacitor)
> - An **empty bucket** = logic `0`
> - The bucket **leaks** slowly → so you must keep pouring water back in periodically → this is the **REFRESH** operation.
> - To *read* the level, you must either (a) **peek inside** (non-destructive, like 3T) or (b) **pour the bucket's contents into a measuring cylinder** and see how much water was there (destructive, like 1T — the bucket is now empty and must be refilled).

## 1.4 Why DRAM Exists (vs. SRAM)

A single 6T-SRAM cell occupies **≈ 120–150 F²** (where **F** is the minimum feature size). A 1T-DRAM cell occupies only **≈ 6–10 F²** — roughly **15–20× smaller**. This density advantage is the single most important reason DRAM is the technology of choice for main memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Per-bit Transistor Count (a key KTU viva question):**
> - 6T SRAM → 6 transistors / bit
> - 3T DRAM → 3 transistors / bit
> - 1T DRAM → 1 transistor + 1 capacitor / bit ✅ **(density champion)**

# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3-Transistor (3T) DRAM Cell

### 2.1.1 Cell Structure

The classical 3T cell uses **three NMOS transistors (M1, M2, M3)** and exploits the **gate capacitance of M2** (`C_G`) as the storage node. No explicit capacitor is needed.

- **M1** → Write access transistor (gated by **Write Word Line, WWL**)
- **M2** → Storage transistor (gate = **storage node X**, parasitic `C_G` is the data carrier)
- **M3** → Read access transistor (gated by **Read Word Line, RWL**)
- **Data Line (DL)** → used to write data into the cell
- **Read Bit Line (RBL)** → used to read data out of the cell

The storage node **X = gate of M2** floats when WWL is OFF, retaining the previously written charge on `C_G` (plus overlap and junction capacitances).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading on the Schematic:** Two separate bit lines (DL for write, RBL for read) provide **isolated write and read paths** — this is what makes the **read operation non-destructive**.

### 2.1.2 Write Operation (`X ← D`)

- **Step 1:** Drive **WWL = V_DD** (turn ON M1).
- **Step 2:** Drive **DL = V_DD** (for writing `1`) or **DL = 0 V** (for writing `0`).
- **Step 3:** Charge flows through M1 onto `C_G`, raising/lowering node X to the DL voltage.
- **Step 4:** Lower **WWL = 0 V** (turn OFF M1). Node **X is now floating** — its charge is held on `C_G`.

Resulting stored voltage at X:
$$V_X \;\approx\; V_{DD}\;\;(\text{logic 1}) \quad \text{or} \quad V_X \;\approx\; 0\;\;(\text{logic 0})$$

### 2.1.3 Read Operation (Non-Destructive)

- **Step 1:** **Precharge RBL to V_DD** (or a reference high voltage) using a precharge transistor.
- **Step 2:** Raise **RWL = V_DD** (turn ON M3).
- **Step 3:** If **V_X was high** → M2 is ON → RBL discharges through the series M2–M3 stack toward ground.
- **Step 4:** If **V_X was low** → M2 is OFF → RBL remains high.
- **Step 5:** A **sense amplifier** detects the differential between the discharged RBL and a reference RBL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because the storage charge sits on the **gate of M2** (a high-impedance node), the read path (M2's drain/source and M3) does **not** pull significant charge off `C_G`. Therefore the read is **non-destructive** — the same stored data can be read many times without refresh solely due to read activity.

### 2.1.4 Refresh Operation

Because the charge on `C_G` leaks away through the **sub-threshold leakage of M1 and M2** and through the **reverse-biased pn-junction leakage** at node X, the data decays. Refresh = simply **re-execute the write operation** for the same data (which is, of course, read first into a sense amp, then re-driven onto DL).

The maximum refresh interval is:
$$t_{\text{REFRESH}} \;=\; \frac{C_G \cdot \Delta V_{\min}}{I_{\text{leak}}}$$

where `ΔV_min` is the minimum voltage swing at node X still recognizable by the sense amplifier, and `I_leak` is the total leakage current at node X.

### 2.1.5 Cell-Level Sizing Trade-off

A larger `C_G` stores more charge → longer retention → easier read, but **larger area**. A typical design choice is to use the **gate capacitance of an oversized M2** to maximize `C_G` without paying area penalty on a separate capacitor.

## 2.2 The 1-Transistor (1T) DRAM Cell

### 2.2.1 Cell Structure

The 1T cell is the **industry-standard DRAM cell**. It uses:

- **One NMOS access transistor** (M_A), gated by the **Word Line (WL)**.
- **One explicit storage capacitor (C_S)** — implemented as a deep-trench or stacked capacitor in modern process nodes.
- **Bit Line (BL)** connected to the drain of M_A.
- The **cell plate** (the other terminal of `C_S`) is tied to `V_{PLATE}` = `V_{DD}/2` (a mid-rail reference).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why V_DD/2 on the cell plate?** Because the storage node can swing both above and below `V_{DD}/2` (when a `1` is written to `V_{DD}` and a `0` to `0 V`), a **mid-rail plate voltage** keeps the oxide field symmetric and prevents long-term bias-temperature instability (BTI) damage.

### 2.2.2 Write Operation (`C_S ← D`)

- **Step 1:** Activate **WL = V_DD + V_TN** (or `V_DD` with boosted WL) to fully pass the data voltage.
- **Step 2:** Drive **BL = V_DD** (for `1`) or **BL = 0 V** (for `0`).
- **Step 3:** Charge flows onto `C_S` until the storage node `V_S = BL`.
- **Step 4:** Lower WL. The charge is now trapped on `C_S`, isolated by the OFF access transistor.

### 2.2.3 Read Operation — The Heart of 1T DRAM (Charge Sharing)

- **Step 1:** **Precharge BL to V_DD/2** (or to a fixed reference `V_{REF}`). The bit-line capacitance is `C_B` (large, parasitic — typically 100–500 fF per column).
- **Step 2:** Activate **WL = V_DD**. The access transistor turns ON, and the storage node `V_S` is now electrically connected to the bit line.
- **Step 3:** **Charge sharing** occurs between `C_S` and `C_B` until both nodes reach a common voltage.
- **Step 4:** The sense amplifier (a cross-coupled latch) detects the small differential and amplifies it to a full-rail value.

The key equation (charge conservation) is:
$$C_S \cdot V_S \;+\; C_B \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \;=\; (C_S + C_B) \cdot V_{BL,\text{final}}$$

Solving:
$$V_{BL,\text{final}} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot V_S + C_B \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_S + C_B} \;=\; \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \;+\; \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_B}\left(V_S - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right)$$

The differential developed on the bit line (with respect to `V_{DD}/2`) is:
$$\Delta V_{BL} \;=\; \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_B} \cdot \left(V_S - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right) \;\approx\; \frac{C_S}{C_B} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \quad (\text{since } C_S \ll C_B)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Read is DESTRUCTIVE!** Charge sharing has *moved* the charge from `C_S` onto `C_B`. After the read, the storage node is left at `V_{DD}/2` — i.e., the data has been destroyed. A **rewrite (write-back)** is therefore mandatory after every read, and this is handled automatically by the sense amplifier (which both senses *and* re-drives the data).

### 2.2.4 Sense Amplifier

A **differential sense amplifier** (typically a cross-coupled CMOS latch, or in older DRAMs, an NMOS-only latch) senses the small `ΔV_BL` (often only **~50–200 mV**) and amplifies it to a full-rail logic level. Modern DRAMs also use **equilibrate and precharge transistors** to ensure both bit lines of a paired column are at exactly `V_{DD}/2` before the WL is fired.

### 2.2.5 Refresh

Just like 3T, charge leaks off `C_S` through:

1. **Sub-threshold leakage** of the access transistor.
2. **Junction leakage** at the storage-node diffusion.
3. **Gate-tunneling leakage** (at advanced nodes, BTI-related).

The refresh interval is:
$$t_{\text{REFRESH}} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot \Delta V_{\min}}{I_{\text{leak}}}$$

For a 1 Gb DDR4 device, `t_REFRESH` ≈ **64 ms**, divided across **~16 k refresh commands**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$Q \;=\; C \cdot V$$ | Fundamental charge–voltage relation for the storage capacitor. | C, F, V |
| 2 | $$V_{BL,\text{final}} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot V_S + C_B \cdot V_{REF}}{C_S + C_B}$$ | Bit-line voltage after charge sharing (1T DRAM read). | V |
| 3 | $$\Delta V_{BL} \;\approx\; \frac{C_S}{C_B} \cdot \left(V_S - V_{REF}\right)$$ | Differential developed on bit line; **read signal**. | V |
| 4 | $$t_{\text{REFRESH}} \;=\; \frac{C \cdot \Delta V_{\min}}{I_{\text{leak}}}$$ | Maximum time between refreshes. | s |
| 5 | $$A_{\text{cell}} \;\approx\; 6F^2 \text{ to } 10F^2 \quad (1T), \quad \approx 50F^2 \quad (3T)$$ | Cell area in terms of feature size F. | m² |
| 6 | $$V_S \;\approx\; V_{DD} - V_{TN}$$ (max stored `1`) | Limited by NMOS pass-transistor threshold drop. | V |
| 7 | $$I_{D,\text{leak}} \;\propto\; I_0 \cdot 10^{\,V_{GS}/S}$$ (sub-threshold) | Determines refresh rate at 3T and 1T cells. | A |
| 8 | $$\text{Capacitance ratio } r \;=\; \frac{C_S}{C_B}$$ | Ideally `r ≥ 0.1` for adequate sense margin. | dimensionless |
| 9 | $$t_{\text{RC}} \;\approx\; R_{BL} \cdot C_B$$ | Bit-line RC time constant, sets read access speed. | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hint for Exams:** The read signal `ΔV_BL` scales as `C_S / C_B`. Designers therefore try to **maximize C_S** (deep-trench / stacked capacitors) and **minimize C_B** (short bit lines, twisted differential routing).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Commercial DRAM (DDR, LPDDR, GDDR, HBM)** uses the **1T cell** exclusively, scaled down to ≈ **6F²** in modern nodes.
- The 3T cell is **pedagogical** and is used in some **logic-embedded DRAM (eDRAM)** and in certain **image-sensor pixel designs** (where the 3T active-pixel sensor is essentially a 3T DRAM cell!).
- **DRAM scaling limits** (capacitor aspect ratio, leakage, refresh power) are the reason the industry is now exploring **3D-stacked DRAM, HBM, and capacitor-less DRAM (gain cells, 2T/2C cells, IGZO FETs, FeRAM, MRAM, ReRAM)**.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bit-Line Voltage After Charge Sharing (1T DRAM)

This is the most heavily tested derivation in KTU VLSI papers for this module. We derive it from **first principles** (charge conservation) in exhaustive detail.

### 3.1.1 Setup

- Storage capacitor `C_S` initially charged to voltage `V_S` with respect to the cell plate (which sits at `V_{DD}/2`).
- Bit line precharged to `V_{REF} = V_{DD}/2`, with bit-line capacitance `C_B`.
- Word line activated; access transistor turns ON; `C_S` and `C_B` become parallel.
- No external source delivers current during charge sharing (the access transistor is ON, but the bit line is floating, i.e., the sense amplifier is disabled at this moment).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total initial charge (just before WL is fired):**

The charge on `C_S` is:
$$Q_{S,i} \;=\; C_S \cdot \left(V_S - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right)$$

(referenced to the cell plate at `V_{DD}/2`).

The charge on `C_B` is:
$$Q_{B,i} \;=\; C_B \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - 0\right) \;=\; C_B \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

(referenced to ground; we are using ground as the global reference throughout).

Total initial charge (with respect to ground):
$$Q_{\text{initial}} \;=\; C_S \cdot \left(V_S - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right) + C_B \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Final configuration (charge sharing complete):**

After WL is activated, `C_S` and `C_B` are in parallel (both have their bottom plate at ground/AC ground, and their top plates connected to the same node — the bit line). They reach a common voltage `V_{BL,f}`. Total capacitance = `C_S + C_B`.

Total final charge:
$$Q_{\text{final}} \;=\; (C_S + C_B) \cdot V_{BL,f}$$

**Step 3 — Apply charge conservation** (no path to ground / supply during the brief charge-sharing window):
$$Q_{\text{initial}} \;=\; Q_{\text{final}}$$
$$C_S \cdot \left(V_S - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right) + C_B \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \;=\; (C_S + C_B) \cdot V_{BL,f}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for `V_{BL,f}`:**

$$V_{BL,f} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot \left(V_S - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right) + C_B \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_S + C_B}$$

**Step 5 — Re-arrange to expose the mid-rail term:**

$$V_{BL,f} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot V_S - C_S \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} + C_B \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_S + C_B}$$

$$V_{BL,f} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot V_S + (C_B - C_S) \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_S + C_B}$$

**Step 6 — Express the differential `ΔV_BL` relative to `V_{DD}/2`:**

$$\Delta V_{BL} \;\equiv\; V_{BL,f} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

$$\Delta V_{BL} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot V_S + (C_B - C_S) \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_S + C_B} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

Take common denominator `(C_S + C_B)`:
$$\Delta V_{BL} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot V_S + (C_B - C_S) \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - (C_S + C_B) \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_S + C_B}$$

Expand the last term:
$$\Delta V_{BL} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot V_S + C_B \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - C_S \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - C_S \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - C_B \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}}{C_S + C_B}$$

The `+ C_B · V_{DD}/2` and `− C_B · V_{DD}/2` cancel:
$$\Delta V_{BL} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot V_S - C_S \cdot V_{DD}}{C_S + C_B} \;=\; \frac{C_S \cdot \left(V_S - V_{DD}\right)}{C_S + C_B}$$

Hmm — that is for `V_S` referenced to ground and `V_{REF}=0`. Let us redo with the **standard form** that textbooks use, where `V_S` is measured relative to `V_{DD}/2` and `V_{REF} = V_{DD}/2`:

Re-doing the algebra with `V_S' = V_S − V_{DD}/2` and `V_{REF} = V_{DD}/2`:
$$\boxed{\;V_{BL,f} \;=\; V_{REF} + \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_B} \cdot (V_S - V_{REF})\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta V_{BL} \;=\; \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_B} \cdot (V_S - V_{REF}) \;\approx\; \frac{C_S}{C_B} \cdot (V_S - V_{REF}) \quad \text{since } C_S \ll C_B\;}$$

This is the **canonical 1T-DRAM read signal equation**. ✅

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

Given:
- `C_S = 30 fF`, `C_B = 300 fF`, `V_{DD} = 1.2 V`, `V_{REF} = V_{DD}/2 = 0.6 V`.
- Stored `1` → `V_S = V_{DD} = 1.2 V`.
- Stored `0` → `V_S = 0 V`.

**Read a stored '1':**
$$\Delta V_{BL} \;=\; \frac{30}{30 + 300} \cdot (1.2 - 0.6) \;=\; \frac{30}{330} \cdot 0.6 \;\approx\; 0.0545 \text{ V} \;\approx\; 54.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Read a stored '0':**
$$\Delta V_{BL} \;=\; \frac{30}{330} \cdot (0 - 0.6) \;\approx\; -54.5 \text{ mV}$$

So the sense amplifier must reliably detect **≈ ±55 mV** around `V_{DD}/2 = 600 mV`. This is why sense amplifiers are **sensitive, low-offset, latched designs** (e.g., cross-coupled latch, current-mirror sense amp).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check on `C_S / C_B`:** In modern DRAM, `C_S ≈ 20–30 fF` and `C_B ≈ 200–500 fF`, giving a ratio of **0.04 to 0.15** — a 4–15 % read signal. The 3T cell typically achieves a **larger read signal** (closer to 30–50 %) at the cost of larger cell area.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Refresh Time

### 3.2.1 Setup

- Storage node holds charge `Q = C · V_S`.
- Leakage current `I_leak` drains this charge.
- Minimum recognizable voltage swing at storage node: `ΔV_min = V_S(initial) − V_S(min)`.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

Charge removed by leakage over time `t`:
$$Q_{\text{lost}} \;=\; I_{\text{leak}} \cdot t$$

This must not exceed the charge budget:
$$C \cdot \Delta V_{\min} \;=\; I_{\text{leak}} \cdot t_{\text{REFRESH}}$$

Solving for `t_REFRESH`:
$$\boxed{\;t_{\text{REFRESH}} \;=\; \frac{C \cdot \Delta V_{\min}}{I_{\text{leak}}}\;}$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example

Given:
- `C_S = 25 fF`, `ΔV_min = 0.2 V`, `I_leak = 1 pA` (typical at room temperature for a 1T cell).

$$t_{\text{REFRESH}} \;=\; \frac{25 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 0.2}{1 \times 10^{-12}} \;=\; \frac{5 \times 10^{-15}}{10^{-12}} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \;=\; 5 \text{ ms}$$

(For commercial DRAMs, `I_leak` is on the order of **fA**, not pA, giving refresh times of **tens of milliseconds** — the higher number used in textbooks comes from smaller leakage.)

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: 3T vs. 1T DRAM (KTU Favourite)

| Parameter | 3T DRAM | 1T DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| **Transistors per cell** | 3 | 1 |
| **Storage element** | Gate cap of M2 (parasitic) | Explicit capacitor (deep-trench / stacked) |
| **Bit lines** | Separate write (DL) & read (RBL) | Single shared BL |
| **Word lines** | Two (WWL, RWL) | One (WL) |
| **Cell area** | ≈ 40–60 F² | ≈ 6–10 F² ✅ (industry standard) |
| **Read nature** | Non-destructive ✅ | Destructive — write-back required |
| **Read signal magnitude** | Large (full-swing RBL discharge) ✅ | Small (charge-sharing `ΔV` ≈ 50 mV) |
| **Sense amplifier complexity** | Simple (single-ended) | Differential, low-offset (latch-type) ✅ |
| **Refresh complexity** | Refresh = re-write (requires read first) | Refresh = same as read + write-back (auto) ✅ |
| **Power (refresh)** | Lower per cell | Higher (whole rows refreshed) |
| **Static noise margin** | Moderate | Limited by `C_S/C_B` ratio |
| **Use case** | Educational, eDRAM, image sensors ✅ | Commercial DRAM (DDR, LPDDR, HBM) ✅ |

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 3T DRAM Cell — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WWL[Write Word Line WWL] -->|gate| M1
    DL[Data Line DL] --> M1
    M1 -->|drain| nodeX
    nodeX[Storage Node X = gate of M2] -->|gate| M2
    M2 -->|source| GND_A[Ground]
    M2 -->|drain| nodeY
    RWL[Read Word Line RWL] -->|gate| M3
    M3 -->|drain| nodeY
    M3 -->|source| RBL[Read Bit Line RBL]
    RBL --> SA[Single Ended Sense Amplifier]
    SA --> DO[Data Out]

    classDef sigNode fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mos fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class nodeX sigNode
    class M1,M2,M3 mos
    class WWL,RWL,DL,RBL ctrl
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `WWL` controls M1 → write path.
- `RWL` controls M3 → read path.
- Storage is on the **gate of M2** (yellow node X).
- `RBL` is precharged; if X was high, M2 conducts, and RWL pulling high turns on M3, dumping charge off RBL → sense amplifier fires.

## 4.2 1T DRAM Cell — Functional Block Diagram (with Sense Amp)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WL[Word Line WL] -->|gate| MA[Access NMOS M_A]
    BL[Bit Line BL] --> MA
    MA --> VS[Storage Node V_S]
    VS --> CS[Storage Capacitor C_S]
    CS --> PLATE[Cell Plate V_DD slash 2]
    BLB[Bit Line Bar BLB] --> REF[Precharge to V_DD slash 2]
    REF --> SA
    BL --> SA
    SA[Differential Latch Sense Amplifier] --> OUT[Full Rail Data Out]
    SA -->|auto write back| VS

    classDef cap fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef sense fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#be185d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class CS,VS,PLATE cap
    class WL,BL,BLB,REF,OUT ctrl
    class SA sense
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Single access transistor + single storage capacitor.
- BL and BLB (its complement, on a paired column) are both precharged to `V_{DD}/2`.
- When WL fires, charge sharing produces a small `ΔV` between BL and BLB.
- The **cross-coupled latch sense amplifier** amplifies this small difference to a full rail and simultaneously **rewrites the cell** (write-back), eliminating the need for a separate refresh for this read.

## 4.3 Operational State Machine — 1T DRAM Read Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Precharge: Activate PRE
    Precharge --> Equalize: EQ transistor ON, BL slash BLB both at V_DD slash 2
    Equalize --> WordlineOn: WL rises to V_DD plus V_T
    WordlineOn --> ChargeShare: C_S and C_B share charge
    ChargeShare --> Sense: Sense amp enables, detects Delta V_BL
    Sense --> Amplify: Latch amplifies to full rail
    Amplify --> WriteBack: Selected cell rewritten with original data
    WriteBack --> WordlineOff: WL lowered
    WordlineOff --> Precharge: PRE re-enabled for next cycle
    Precharge --> Idle
```

## 4.4 Side-by-Side Comparison Schematic (Text Form)

| Element | 3T Cell | 1T Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Access | M1 (write) + M3 (read) | Single MA |
| Storage | Gate of M2 (parasitic) | Explicit C_S |
| WL count | 2 | 1 |
| BL count | 2 (DL + RBL) | 1 (BL, paired with BLB in array) |
| Read signal | RBL goes from V_DD → 0 (large) | ΔV_BL ≈ 50 mV (small) |
| After read | Data preserved (non-destructive) | Data destroyed (destructive) |
| Write-back | Not needed for read | Auto by sense amp |
