It mathematically models the fundamental physical phenomena—diffusion, oxidation, ion implantation, etching, lithography, and deposition—that occur during semiconductor manufacturing.

**MOSFET Fabrication Process Simulation** is the virtual recreation of the entire sequence of physical and chemical operations used to manufacture a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor on a silicon wafer. The simulation is performed in a 2D or 3D domain using partial differential equations (PDEs) that govern dopant diffusion, oxide growth, stress, and carrier transport.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PBECT604 – VLSI Circuit Design, Module 4), this project is a hands-on exercise where students:
- Define a process flow (recipe) for fabricating an n-channel MOSFET.
- Input the recipe into a TCAD tool (e.g., **Synopsys Sentaurus Process**, **Silvaco ATHENA**, **TCAD Sentaurus**).
- Visually inspect the resulting device cross-section.
- Extract electrical parameters such as threshold voltage ($V_{TH}$), drain current ($I_D$), and subthreshold slope.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Virtual Foundry"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are a chef who wants to bake a perfect cake. Instead of actually baking it (which is expensive, time-consuming, and irreversible if it fails), you use a computer program that virtually mixes the ingredients, puts them in the oven at the right temperature for the right time, and shows you exactly what the cake will look like—and even tells you how it will taste (sweetness, moisture, etc.)—before you bake it for real. **TCAD is this "virtual kitchen" for semiconductor chips.**

| Real Foundry (Fab) | Virtual Foundry (TCAD) |
|---|---|
| Silicon wafer | 2D/3D simulation mesh |
| Furnace (oxidation) | Deal-Grove PDE solver |
| Ion implanter | Pearson/Gaussian dopant model |
| Plasma etcher | Geometric/physical etch algorithm |
| Test equipment (probestation) | Drift-diffusion device solver |

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are standard in any TCAD project for MOSFET fabrication:

- **Silicon lattice constant:** $a_{Si} = 0.543 \text{ nm}$
- **Silicon density:** $2.33 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- **SiO₂ molecular weight:** $60.08 \text{ g/mol}$
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si at 300 K:** $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$
- **Thermal oxide color (per 100 nm):** corresponds to ~**1200 Å** of SiO₂
- **Typical MOSFET gate oxide thickness (modern nodes):** **1–10 nm**
- **Typical channel length (educational projects):** **0.5 µm – 2 µm**
- **Dopant species used:** Boron (p-type), Phosphorus & Arsenic (n-type)
- **Standard oxidation temperatures:** **900 °C – 1200 °C** (dry/wet O₂)
- **Standard annealing temperature:** **900 °C – 1100 °C** in N₂

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Project Tip:** When you present your project, always quote the **simulation domain size**, **mesh density**, **boundary conditions**, and **physical models** used. Examiners specifically look for these.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a MOSFET Fabrication Flow (Process Recipe)

A standard **n-channel MOSFET (NMOS)** is fabricated using the following canonical sequence of process steps. Each step corresponds to a specific command in a TCAD tool (e.g., `deposit`, `diffuse`, `implant`, `etch`, `mask`).

### Step-by-Step Process Flow

1. **Wafer Preparation**
   - Start with a p-type Silicon wafer of doping $N_A = 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, orientation (100), resistivity ~10 Ω·cm.
   - **Why?** (100) orientation gives the lowest interface state density $D_{it}$ at the Si–SiO₂ interface.

2. **Field Oxidation (LOCOS — Local Oxidation of Silicon)**
   - Grow a thick field oxide (~500 nm) using **wet oxidation at 1100 °C** to isolate adjacent devices.
   - A **Si₃N₄** layer is used as an oxidation mask.
   - Output: $Si_3N_4$-masked region defines the **active area**.

3. **Gate Oxide Growth**
   - Use **dry oxidation at 1000 °C** to grow a thin, high-quality SiO₂ layer (~10–50 nm) which acts as the gate dielectric.
   - This is the **most critical step** for MOSFET performance.

4. **Polysilicon Gate Deposition & Doping**
   - Deposit a layer of polycrystalline silicon (poly-Si) over the gate oxide (~300 nm).
   - Dope the poly-Si heavily (n⁺) using **phosphorus diffusion or ion implantation** to reduce gate resistance.

5. **Gate Patterning (Photolithography + Etch)**
   - Spin-coat photoresist, expose through a mask defining the gate length $L$, develop, and etch the poly-Si using **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE)**.
   - Output: a precisely defined gate electrode of length $L$.

6. **Source/Drain Formation (Ion Implantation)**
   - Implant **Arsenic (As)** or **Phosphorus (P)** ions at an energy of **50–100 keV** with a dose of $10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-2}$.
   - The poly-Si gate acts as a **self-aligned mask**, ensuring the S/D regions are perfectly aligned to the gate edges.
   - This eliminates the parasitic overlap capacitance—a key advantage of the **self-aligned MOSFET** invented by Dawon Kahng and Martin Atalla in 1960.

7. **Annealing (Dopant Activation)**
   - **Rapid Thermal Anneal (RTA)** at 1000 °C for 10–30 seconds to:
     - Repair implant damage.
     - Electrically activate the dopants.
     - Drive the S/D junctions to the desired depth $x_j$.

8. **Contact Formation & Metallization**
   - Deposit Aluminum or Tungsten, then pattern to form source, drain, and gate contacts.
   - A final **Forming Gas Anneal (FGA)** at 400 °C in H₂/N₂ passivates the Si–SiO₂ interface.

9. **Passivation**
   - Deposit a final **Si₃N₄** or **SiO₂** layer to protect the chip from moisture and mechanical damage.

## 2.2 The Three Core Physical Phenomena in TCAD

Any TCAD simulation reduces to solving three families of PDEs:

| Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Tool Solver |
|---|---|---|
| Dopant Diffusion | Fick's Second Law | Process Solver |
| Thermal Oxidation | Deal-Grove Model | Process Solver |
| Carrier Transport | Drift-Diffusion | Device Solver |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **must-know** for the ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the form, the constants, and the physical meaning.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (D \nabla C)$ | Fick's Second Law (dopant diffusion) | $C$ in cm⁻³, $D$ in cm²/s |
| 2 | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{kT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusivity | $D_0$ in cm²/s, $E_a$ in eV |
| 3 | $x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$ | Deal-Grove oxidation (linear & parabolic regimes) | $x_0$ in µm, $t$ in min |
| 4 | $B = \dfrac{2 D_{ox} C^*}{N_1}$ | Parabolic rate constant | µm²/min |
| 5 | $\dfrac{B}{A} = \dfrac{2 D_{ox} C^*}{N_1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{k_s}$ | Linear-parabolic ratio | µm/min |
| 6 | $C(x,t) = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-R_p)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Pearson IV / Gaussian implant profile | $Q$ dose, $R_p$ projected range |
| 7 | $V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ | Long-channel threshold voltage | Volts |
| 8 | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential | Volts |
| 9 | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[(V_{GS}-V_{TH})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | Drain current (triode region) | Amperes |
| 10 | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$ | Drain current (saturation region) | Amperes |
| 11 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | F/cm² |
| 12 | $x_j \approx 2 \sqrt{D \cdot t}$ | Approximate junction depth | µm |

**Standard Constants to Remember:**

- $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \text{ eV/K}$ (Boltzmann)
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \text{ F/cm}$
- $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \, \varepsilon_0$
- $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \, \varepsilon_0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This process flow is the *literal* manufacturing recipe used by companies like TSMC, Intel, and Samsung. TCAD allows engineers to test "what if" scenarios—what if the anneal time increases by 5 seconds? what if we use a different dopant?—without wasting a single wafer. A single wafer costs ~**$1000–$5000** at advanced nodes; TCAD saves millions in R&D.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Deal–Grove Thermal Oxidation Model

The Deal–Grove model (1965) is the foundation of oxidation simulation. Let us derive it step by step.

### Step 1: Define the Three Mass-Flux Regimes

Consider an oxidizing species (O₂ or H₂O) flowing over a Si surface covered by an existing oxide of thickness $x_0$. Three fluxes must be in steady-state equilibrium:

- **Flux $F_1$** (gas phase transport from bulk gas to the gas-oxide interface): $F_1 = h_G (C_G - C_S)$
- **Flux $F_2$** (diffusion through the oxide): $F_2 = -D_{ox} \dfrac{dC}{dx}$
- **Flux $F_3$** (reaction at the Si–SiO₂ interface): $F_3 = k_S C_I$

where:
- $C_G$ = gas-phase oxidant concentration
- $C_S$ = concentration at the gas-oxide interface
- $C_I$ = concentration at the Si-oxide interface
- $h_G$ = gas-phase mass-transfer coefficient
- $D_{ox}$ = oxidant diffusivity in SiO₂
- $k_S$ = surface reaction rate constant

### Step 2: Apply Henry's Law at the Gas-Oxide Interface

$$
C_S = H \cdot p
$$

where $H$ is Henry's constant and $p$ is the partial pressure. Similarly, $C_G = H \cdot p$ at saturation. So $C_G - C_S = 0$ for the limiting case, and at the Si-oxide interface we have the equilibrium concentration $C^*$.

### Step 3: Solve the Diffusion Equation in the Oxide

Assuming linear concentration gradient across oxide of thickness $x_0$:

$$
F_2 = D_{ox} \dfrac{(C_S - C_I)}{x_0}
$$

### Step 4: Steady-State Equipartition of Fluxes

In steady state: $F_1 = F_2 = F_3 \equiv F$. Solving the three equations:

$$
F = \dfrac{C^*}{\dfrac{1}{k_S} + \dfrac{1}{h_G} + \dfrac{x_0}{D_{ox}}}
$$

### Step 5: Relate Flux to Oxide Growth

Each oxidation reaction consumes Si and produces SiO₂. The number of Si atoms consumed per unit volume of SiO₂ produced is $N_1 \approx 2.2 \times 10^{22} \text{ cm}^{-3}$. The growth rate of the oxide thickness is:

$$
N_1 \dfrac{dx_0}{dt} = F
$$

### Step 6: Integrate Over Time

Substituting and separating variables:

$$
N_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{k_S} + \dfrac{1}{h_G}\right) dx_0 + N_1 \dfrac{x_0 \, dx_0}{D_{ox}} = C^* \, dt
$$

Integrating from $t=0$ (where $x_0 = x_i$, the initial oxide thickness) to $t$ (where $x_0 = x_i + x$, the total thickness grown):

$$
N_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{k_S} + \dfrac{1}{h_G}\right) x + N_1 \dfrac{x^2 + 2 x_i x}{2 D_{ox}} = C^* t
$$

Defining the **linear rate constant** $B/A$ and the **parabolic rate constant** $B$:

$$
\boxed{x^2 + A x = B(t + \tau)}
$$

where:

- Parabolic constant: $B = \dfrac{2 D_{ox} C^*}{N_1}$
- Linear constant: $\dfrac{B}{A} = \dfrac{C^*}{\dfrac{N_1}{k_S} + \dfrac{N_1}{h_G}}$
- $\tau = \dfrac{x_i^2 + A x_i}{B}$ (accounts for initial oxide)

### Step 7: Two Limiting Regimes

> **Case (a) — Thick Oxide, Long Time (Parabolic Limit):** $A x \ll x^2$, so $x^2 \approx B t$, giving $x \approx \sqrt{B t}$. Diffusion-limited.

> **Case (b) — Thin Oxide, Short Time (Linear Limit):** $x^2 \ll A x$, so $x \approx \dfrac{B}{A} t$. Reaction-limited.

This is **why** thin gate oxides grow linearly in time (surface reaction is the bottleneck) while thick field oxides follow $\sqrt{t}$ (diffusion bottleneck).

## 3.2 Derivation: Gaussian Implant Profile

For a thin masking oxide and a single-energy implantation, the as-implanted dopant profile is a Gaussian:

$$
C(x) = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{2\pi} \Delta R_p} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x - R_p)^2}{2 \Delta R_p^2}\right]
$$

**Derivation Outline:**

1. Each implanted ion undergoes a **random walk** of collisions, so its stopping depth is the sum of many independent random variables.
2. By the **Central Limit Theorem**, the distribution of these sums tends to a Gaussian.
3. The peak concentration occurs at the projected range $R_p$ (a function of ion energy, mass, and target material).
4. The straggle $\Delta R_p$ quantifies the spread.

**Quantitative Example (As⁺ into Si at 80 keV):**
- $R_p \approx 60 \text{ nm}$
- $\Delta R_p \approx 20 \text{ nm}$
- Dose $Q = 5 \times 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-2}$

Peak concentration: $C_{peak} = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{2\pi} \Delta R_p} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{15}}{\sqrt{2\pi} \cdot 20 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 9.97 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Threshold Voltage (Long-Channel NMOS)

Starting from the condition for strong inversion: surface potential $\phi_s = 2 \phi_F$, and using Gauss's law at the Si–SiO₂ interface:

$$
V_{GS} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}
$$

where the bulk depletion charge is:

$$
Q_B = \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2 \phi_F)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A \cdot 2 \phi_F}}{C_{ox}}}
$$

This is the equation that TCAD solvers compute at the end of the process flow to verify if the device meets specifications.

**Numerical Example:**
- $t_{ox} = 20 \text{ nm} \Rightarrow C_{ox} = \dfrac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}}{20 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.73 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F/cm}^2$
- $N_A = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3} \Rightarrow \phi_F = 0.0259 \ln(10^{16}/1.5\times 10^{10}) = 0.349 \text{ V}$
- $Q_B = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.04\times10^{-12} \cdot 1.6\times10^{-19} \cdot 10^{16} \cdot 0.698} = 4.82 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C/cm}^2$
- $V_{TH} \approx V_{FB} + 0.698 + 0.279 = V_{FB} + 0.977 \text{ V}$ (assuming $V_{FB} \approx -0.9 \text{ V for n⁺ poly on p-Si)
- $\Rightarrow V_{TH} \approx 0.08 \text{ V}$ (a depletion-mode device, hence the need for **channel implantation** to raise $V_{TH}$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TCAD Simulation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Recipe Definition] --> B[Geometry and Mesh Generation]
    B --> C[Process Simulation Engine]
    C --> D[Dopant Diffusion and Oxidation Solvers]
    D --> E[Final Device Structure]
    E --> F[Device Simulation Engine]
    F --> G[Drift Diffusion Solver]
    G --> H[Electrical Characteristics Extraction]
    H --> I[Threshold Voltage]
    H --> J[Drain Current vs Gate Voltage]
    H --> K[Subthreshold Slope]
    H --> L[Output to Visualization Tool]
    L --> M[Cross Section Plot]
    L --> N[Contour Plot of Electric Field]
    L --> O[Transfer Characteristics Curve]
```

## 4.2 Process Step Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Start: p-type Si Wafer] --> A2[Step 1: Field Oxidation LOCOS]
    A2 --> A3[Step 2: Active Area Definition by Nitride Masking]
    A3 --> A4[Step 3: Pad Oxide Strip and Sacrificial Oxide]
    A4 --> A5[Step 4: Gate Oxide Growth]
    A5 --> A6[Step 5: Polysilicon Deposition]
    A6 --> A7[Step 6: Gate Lithography and Etch]
    A7 --> A8[Step 7: S and D Ion Implantation]
    A8 --> A9[Step 8: Dopant Activation Anneal]
    A9 --> A10[Step 9: Contact and Metallization]
    A10 --> A11[Step 10: Passivation]
    A11 --> A12[Final MOSFET Structure]
```

## 4.3 TCAD Tool Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        B1[Process Recipe File]
        B2[Device Geometry Definition]
        B3[Material Database]
        B4[Physical Model Selection]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[CORE SOLVER ENGINE]
        C1[Process Simulator]
        C2[Meshing Engine]
        C3[Device Simulator]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT AND ANALYSIS]
        D1[2D Cross Section]
        D2[Dopant Concentration Profile]
        D3[Electric Field Distribution]
        D4[I-V Characteristics]
        D5[C-V Characteristics]
    end

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C3
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    C3 --> D4
    C3 --> D5
```

## 4.4 MOSFET Cross-Section Schematic (Block-Level Mapping)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps every region of the final simulated device to its fabrication origin.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph M[NMOS Cross Section after TCAD Simulation]
        direction TB
        M1[Gate Contact Aluminum] --> M2[Polysilicon Gate n+ doped]
        M2 --> M3[Gate Oxide SiO2 thin layer]
        M3 --> M4[p-type Silicon Substrate Channel Region]
        M4 --> M5[n+ Source Region implanted with Arsenic]
        M4 --> M6[n+ Drain Region implanted with Arsenic]
        M7[Field Oxide SiO2 thick for isolation] --- M4
        M8[Silicon Nitride Passivation Layer] --- M1
        M8 --- M7
    end
```

**Description of Each Region and Its TCAD Origin:**

- **Gate Oxide (M3):** Result of dry O₂ oxidation step. TCAD outputs the thickness $t_{ox}$ and stress profile.
- **Polysilicon Gate (M2):** Result of CVD deposition followed by n⁺ implantation. TCAD computes the grain boundary effects.
- **Source/Drain (M5/M6):** Result of As⁺ implantation + RTA anneal. TCAD shows the lateral diffusion profile (important for short-channel effects).
- **Field Oxide (M7):** Result of LOCOS; TCAD shows the characteristic **bird's beak** at the active-area boundary.
- **Channel (M4):** The region directly under the gate; TCAD reveals the surface doping after channel-stop implant.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
**Define TCAD. List any four physical phenomena that can be simulated using TCAD tools.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**TCAD (Technology Computer-Aided Design)** is the use of computer simulation to model semiconductor fabrication processes and predict device electrical behavior before physical manufacturing.

Four physical phenomena simulated by TCAD:
1. **Thermal Oxidation** — governed by the Deal–Grove model.
2. **Dopant Diffusion** — governed by Fick's laws and the Arrhenius diffusivity.
3. **Ion Implantation** — modeled as a Gaussian or Pearson IV distribution.
4. **Carrier Transport** — solved using drift-diffusion or hydrodynamic models.

*(Acceptable 4th: Etching, Lithography, Stress, Gettering, etc.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**Write the Deal–Grove oxidation equation and explain the significance of the parabolic rate constant B.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Deal–Grove equation:

$$
x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)
$$

The **parabolic rate constant $B$** quantifies the diffusion-limited oxide growth rate. It depends on:
- $D_{ox}$: oxidant diffusivity in SiO₂
- $C^*$: equilibrium oxidant concentration in the oxide
- $N_1$: number of oxidant molecules per unit volume of SiO₂

**Significance:** $B$ determines the maximum rate at which the oxide can grow in the **thick-oxide regime** ($x \gg A$). For dry O₂ at 1000 °C, $B \approx 0.037 \,\mu\text{m}^2/\text{hr}$. A larger $B$ means faster diffusion and quicker oxide growth.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
> **(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the step-by-step fabrication process flow of an n-channel MOSFET using a self-aligned poly-Si gate process. Highlight the role of the poly-Si gate as a self-aligned mask. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the Deal–Grove oxidation model and identify the two limiting regimes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) NMOS Fabrication Flow (7 marks):**

| Step | Process | Purpose | TCAD Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | p-type Si wafer | Starting substrate | `init silicon resistivity=10 orientation=100 doping=1e15` |
| 2 | Grow pad oxide + deposit Si₃N₄ | LOCOS masking | `deposit oxide thickness=50` + `deposit nitride thickness=150` |
| 3 | Pattern nitride, etch, field-ox grow | Device isolation | `diffusion temp=1100 time=120 t atmosphere=H2O` |
| 4 | Strip nitride, grow gate oxide | Gate dielectric | `diffusion temp=1000 time=20 t atmosphere=O2` |
| 5 | Deposit polysilicon | Gate material | `deposit polysilicon thickness=300` |
| 6 | Pattern poly-Si (litho + RIE) | Define gate length L | `etch polysilicon` |
| 7 | As⁺ ion implantation (self-aligned) | Form S/D | `implant arsenic dose=5e15 energy=80` |
| 8 | RTA anneal | Activate + drive-in | `diffusion temp=1000 time=20 t atmosphere=N2` |
| 9 | Deposit + pattern metal | Contacts | `deposit aluminum thickness=500` |
| 10 | FGA anneal | Passivate interface | `diffusion temp=400 time=30 t atmosphere=H2N2` |

**Role of self-aligned poly-Si gate (2 marks within the 7):**
- The patterned poly-Si acts as a hard mask for the S/D implant.
- This guarantees that the source and drain edges are **automatically aligned** to the gate edges.
- Eliminates the gate-to-S/D overlap capacitance that plagued earlier metal-gate MOSFETs.
- Enables aggressive channel-length scaling (the foundation of Moore's Law).

**(b) Deal–Grove Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The two limiting regimes are:

1. **Linear regime (thin oxide, $x \ll A$):** $x \approx (B/A) \cdot t$ — surface-reaction-limited.
2. **Parabolic regime (thick oxide, $x \gg A$):** $x \approx \sqrt{B \cdot t}$ — diffusion-limited.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Explain with a suitable diagram the architecture of a TCAD simulation tool. List the major input and output blocks. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An arsenic (As⁺) ion is implanted into silicon at 80 keV with a dose of $5 \times 10^{15} \text{ cm}^{-2}$. The projected range is $R_p = 60 \text{ nm}$ and the straggle is $\Delta R_p = 20 \text{ nm}$. Calculate the peak dopant concentration and the concentration at the Si surface. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TCAD Architecture (7 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.3** of these notes. The three major layers are:

- **Input Layer:** Process recipe, geometry, material database, physical model selection.
- **Solver Engine:** Process simulator (dopant diffusion, oxidation, implantation), Meshing engine, Device simulator (drift-diffusion solver).
- **Output Layer:** 2D cross-section, dopant profile, I-V curves, C-V curves, electric field contours.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

The as-implanted profile is Gaussian:

$$
C(x) = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{2\pi} \, \Delta R_p} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x - R_p)^2}{2 \Delta R_p^2}\right]
$$

**Step 1: Peak concentration at $x = R_p = 60$ nm:**

$$
C_{peak} = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{2\pi} \, \Delta R_p} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{15}}{\sqrt{2\pi} \cdot 20 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
C_{peak} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{15}}{2.5066 \times 20 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{15}}{5.013 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{peak} \approx 9.97 \times 10^{20} \text{ cm}^{-3}}
$$

*(Equivalently, $\approx 1 \times 10^{21} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, which is near the solid-solubility limit of As in Si.)*

**Step 2: Surface concentration at $x = 0$:**

$$
C(0) = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{15}}{\sqrt{2\pi} \cdot 20 \times 10^{-7}} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(0 - 60)^2}{2 \cdot (20)^2}\right]
$$

$$
C(0) = 9.97 \times 10^{20} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{3600}{800}\right] = 9.97 \times 10^{20} \cdot \exp(-4.5)
$$

$$
C(0) = 9.97 \times 10^{20} \cdot 0.01111 \approx \boxed{1.11 \times 10^{19} \text{ cm}^{-3}}
$$

This is well below the peak — as expected, since the peak is buried 60 nm below the surface.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\tau$ term in Deal–Grove.** Many students write $x^2 + A x = Bt$ instead of $x^2 + A x = B(t + \tau)$. The $\tau$ accounts for the initial oxide thickness $x_i$ and is **essential** if $x_i \neq 0$. You will lose **1 mark** if you omit it.
> 2. **Mixing up $R_p$ and $x_j$.** $R_p$ is the **projected range** of an implant (a function of energy and species); $x_j$ is the **junction depth** after diffusion (a function of $R_p$, $D$, and anneal time). They are not the same.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify the units** of $D_0$ and $E_a$ in the Arrhenius diffusivity equation. Examiners **do** deduct marks for unit-less answers.
> 4. **Drawing the MOSFET cross-section upside down.** The substrate is at the **bottom**, gate on **top**, and source/drain on the **sides** under the gate overhang.
> 5. **Writing "polysilicon" without the chemical formula.** A small but recurring deduction. Always write **poly-Si** or **polycrystalline silicon**.
> 6. **In Gaussian implant problems, many students forget the factor of $\sqrt{2\pi}$** in the denominator. This will give a peak concentration ~2.5× too large. The correct form is $C_{peak} = Q / (\sqrt{2\pi} \Delta R_p)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4 / TCAD Project**

### **A. Conceptual Points**
- ☐ TCAD = virtual fabrication; saves cost and time.
- ☐ The **three core solvers**: Process, Mesh, Device.
- ☐ A MOSFET process flow has **10 canonical steps** (wafer → passivation).
- ☐ The poly-Si gate is **self-aligned** to S/D — this is the cornerstone of modern CMOS.
- ☐ TCAD outputs include **structural** (doping, oxide) and **electrical** ($V_{TH}$, $I_D$, $g_m$) data.

### **B. Must-Memorize Equations**
- ☐ Fick's second law: $\partial C/\partial t = \nabla \cdot (D \nabla C)$.
- ☐ Deal–Grove: $x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$.
- ☐ Arrhenius diffusivity: $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a/kT)$.
- ☐ Gaussian implant: $C(x) = (Q/\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma) \exp[-(x-R_p)^2/2\sigma^2]$.
- ☐ $V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}/C_{ox}$.

### **C. Standard Constants**
- ☐ $kT/q = 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K.
- ☐ $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \varepsilon_0$, $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \varepsilon_0$.
- ☐ $N_1 \approx 2.2 \times 10^{22} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ (Si atoms per cm³ of SiO₂).

### **D. Numerical Skills**
- ☐ Compute $C_{peak}$ for a given implant.
- ☐ Apply Deal–Grove for both linear and parabolic regimes.
- ☐ Use $x_j \approx 2\sqrt{Dt}$ for quick junction-depth estimates.
- ☐ Convert between Å, nm, and µm: $1 \text{ Å} = 0.1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-4} \mu\text{m}$.

### **E. Common TCAD Tools to Mention in Your Project Report**
- ☐ **Synopsys Sentaurus Process** + **Sentaurus Device** (industry gold standard).
- ☐ **Silvaco ATHENA** + **ATLAS** (academic-friendly).
- ☐ **Crosslight APSYS** (for III-V and optoelectronics).
- ☐ **open-source:** **DEVSIM**, **FLOOPS** (limited features).

### **F. Project Presentation Tips**
- ☐ Always include a **flowchart** of your process recipe.
- ☐ Show the **final 2D cross-section** with labeled regions.
- ☐ Plot the **$I_D$–$V_{GS}$** curve and extract $V_{TH}$ using the **constant-current method** ($V_{TH} = V_{GS}$ at $I_D = 10^{-7} \cdot W/L$).
- ☐ Tabulate all process parameters (temperature, time, dose, energy) in a single summary table.
- ☐ Discuss **at least one short-channel effect** (DIBL, $V_{TH}$ roll-off, punch-through) and how your process flow mitigates it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project: Create the Layout of CMOS Logic Gates and Perform Design Rule Checking
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project: Create the Layout of CMOS Logic Gates and Perform Design Rule Checking

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Physical Layout of CMOS Logic Gates** is the geometric representation (top-down, mask-level view) of a CMOS circuit, where every diffusion region, polysilicon gate, contact, and metal interconnect is drawn as a multi-colored polygon on different mask layers. It is the final, fabrication-ready description of the circuit that bridges the gap between schematic design and silicon.

**Design Rule Checking (DRC)** is the post-layout, rule-based geometric verification step in which the physical layout is systematically scanned against a set of predefined geometric constraints (width, spacing, enclosure, extension, overlap rules, etc.) dictated by the target fabrication process to ensure that the design is **manufacturable, yield-worthy, and electrically reliable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** This topic integrates **Stick Diagrams**, **Lambda-based Design Rules (Mead & Conway)**, and the **CMU/Silicon scalable CMOS rules** to construct NAND, NOR, and Inverter layouts, followed by a Layer-by-Layer DRC walkthrough.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Blueprint Inspector" Analogy)

Imagine you are an **architect designing a city on a plot of land**:
- **The Schematic** = The electrical circuit diagram, like an architect's *functional* floor plan (which rooms connect to which).
- **The Layout** = The *actual* top-down blueprint of the city, showing exactly where the roads (metal), buildings (diffusion), and fences (polysilicon) must be physically placed on the ground.
- **DRC** = The **municipal building inspector** who walks over the blueprint with a ruler. The inspector does not care *what the building does*; he only checks: *"Are the roads wide enough for two trucks to pass? Are buildings too close to the boundary? Are the pillars thick enough to not collapse?"* If a rule is violated, the inspector rejects the design.

Similarly, DRC does not understand transistors or logic — it only checks **geometric distances and overlaps** between layers.

## 1.3 The Three Standard Mask Colours in CMOS Layout

| Colour | Mask Layer | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 🟩 Green | **n-diff (n-select)** | Source/Drain of NMOS, n+ active regions |
| 🟥 Red/Brown | **p-diff (p-select)** | Source/Drain of PMOS, p+ active regions (in n-well) |
| 🟨 Yellow/Blue | **Polysilicon (poly)** | Gate electrodes of MOSFETs |
| ⬛ Black/Blue | **Metal1 (m1)** | Primary horizontal interconnect |
| ⬜ Black square | **Contact (cut)** | Vertical via between poly/m1 or diff/m1 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **n-well** boundary is a *separate* layer (often shown as a dashed outline) on which p-diffusion regions are placed. Every p-diff must lie fully inside the n-well.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics Used in This Project

- **Feature size (2λ)** : The smallest drawn dimension in a scalable lambda-rule process. Default **λ = 0.5 × minimum mask dimension**.
- **Sheet resistance (R□)** : For poly ≈ **30–50 Ω/□**, for diffusion ≈ **10–100 Ω/□**, for metal ≈ **0.05–0.1 Ω/□**.
- **Capacitance per unit area (Cox')** : Typical **≈ 1–2 fF/µm²** for a 0.5 µm process.
- **Design rule unit** : **All CMOS lambda rules are expressed in integer multiples of λ** for process portability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lambda (λ) — The Heart of Scalable CMOS Design Rules

Introduced by **Mead & Conway (1980)**, the **lambda rule** expresses every design rule as an integer multiple of a single parameter **λ**, so that a design can be linearly **scaled** (shrunk or grown) when migrating to a new fabrication process simply by changing the value of λ.

> For a process with minimum feature size **F** (e.g., F = 0.5 µm), the lambda is defined as:
> **λ = F / 2**

So for **F = 0.5 µm** ⇒ **λ = 0.25 µm**, and for **F = 1 µm** ⇒ **λ = 0.5 µm**.

## 2.2 The CMOS Lambda Rule Book (Used in KTU Labs)

The following table is the **exact, exam-ready rule set** every KTU student must memorize for the CMOS Layout project. Each rule is the smallest permissible value that guarantees a working chip.

| Rule # | Layer Pair | Rule Description | λ-Rule Value | Typical µm (λ=0.5µm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Active (n-diff / p-diff) | Minimum **width** | **3 λ** | 1.5 µm |
| **2** | Active | Minimum **spacing** (same type) | **3 λ** | 1.5 µm |
| **3** | Active | n-diff to p-diff **spacing** | **6 λ** (or 4 λ + n-well sep.) | 3.0 µm |
| **4** | Polysilicon | Minimum **width** | **2 λ** | 1.0 µm |
| **5** | Polysilicon | Minimum **spacing** (gate-to-gate) | **2 λ** | 1.0 µm |
| **6** | Polysilicon | **Extension** of poly beyond active (gate) | **2 λ** | 1.0 µm |
| **7** | Polysilicon | Active **extension** beyond poly | **2 λ** | 1.0 µm |
| **8** | Poly to Active | **Spacing** (field poly vs. active) | **1 λ** | 0.5 µm |
| **9** | Metal1 | Minimum **width** | **3 λ** | 1.5 µm |
| **10** | Metal1 | Minimum **spacing** | **3 λ** | 1.5 µm |
| **11** | Contact (cut) | Exact **size** | **2 λ × 2 λ** | 1.0 × 1.0 µm |
| **12** | Contact | **Spacing** between contacts | **2 λ** | 1.0 µm |
| **13** | Contact | Active **enclosure** of contact | **1 λ** (all sides) | 0.5 µm |
| **14** | Contact | Poly **enclosure** of contact | **1 λ** (all sides) | 0.5 µm |
| **15** | Contact | Metal **enclosure** of contact | **1 λ** (all sides) | 0.5 µm |
| **16** | n-Well | Minimum **width** | **6 λ** | 3.0 µm |
| **17** | n-Well | Minimum **spacing** (well-to-well) | **6 λ** | 3.0 µm |
| **18** | n-Well | Active **spacing** to well edge (p-diff only) | **6 λ** | 3.0 µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Rule 18 — the *p-diffusion must be at least 6λ inside the n-well edge*. Many students draw the p+ flush with the well boundary, which is a *classic DRC violation* (latch-up risk).

## 2.3 Classification of Design Rules (for Exam Definitions)

Design rules fall into **four functional categories**:

1. **Width Rules** — Minimum width of any single layer (rules 1, 4, 9, 16).
2. **Spacing Rules** — Minimum distance between two edges of the *same or different* layers (rules 2, 3, 5, 8, 10, 12, 17, 18).
3. **Enclosure Rules** — Minimum overlap of one layer over a contact cut (rules 13, 14, 15).
4. **Extension Rules** — Minimum protrusion of one layer beyond another (rules 6, 7).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Layout & DRC Quantitative Relationships

| Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Lambda | $\lambda = \dfrac{F}{2}$ | Process scaling parameter |
| Cell Height (standard) | $H = 25\lambda \text{ to } 50\lambda$ | Standard cell row height |
| Cell Width | $W = N \cdot W_{inv} + N \cdot 6\lambda$ | $W_{inv}$ = inverter width |
| Transistor Aspect Ratio (W/L) | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{eff} = \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{inv} \cdot k$ | For unequal drive strengths (k=2,3,4) |
| Series-Series Equivalent W/L | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{series} = \dfrac{1}{\sum \dfrac{1}{(W/L)_i}}$ | For stacked NMOS in NAND |
| Parallel-Parallel Equivalent W/L | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_{parallel} = \sum \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_i$ | For paralleled NMOS in NOR |
| Drain Capacitance (approx.) | $C_d = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L + C_{j} \cdot (W + 2L)$ | Used in delay estimation |
| Diffusion Area (per transistor) | $A_{diff} = (W + 2L_d) \cdot (L + 2L_d)$ | $L_d$ = drain extension |
| Poly-Si Sheet Resistance | $R_{\square}^{poly} \approx 20\text{–}50 \,\Omega/\square$ | For interconnect resistance |
| Metal1 Sheet Resistance | $R_{\square}^{m1} \approx 0.05\text{–}0.1 \,\Omega/\square$ | Negligible for short wires |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exam:** For a **2-input CMOS NAND** layout, if each NMOS is minimum-size $(W/L) = (3\lambda/2\lambda)$, then the *effective* pull-down W/L becomes **3λ/4λ** (two NMOS in series). To match the inverter drive, you must **double the width** of each NMOS to (6λ/2λ) so that series combination again equals (3λ/2λ).

## 2.5 The Four Layer-to-Layer Relationship Rules in Detail

### A. Width Rule
A drawn rectangle of layer X must have width ≥ W_min(X). This prevents **line breakage** due to photolithographic under-exposure and resist lift-off.

### B. Spacing Rule (Intra-layer)
Two rectangles of the *same* layer X must be separated by ≥ S_min(X,X). Prevents **short-circuit** between adjacent features.

### C. Spacing Rule (Inter-layer)
A rectangle of layer X must be separated from a rectangle of layer Y by ≥ S_min(X,Y) when they are on different layers. Prevents **unintended capacitive coupling** and shorting.

### D. Enclosure Rule
A layer X rectangle must completely surround a contact cut by ≥ ENC(X) on *every* side. Prevents **contact misalignment** from exposing the cut to the underlying silicon or poly.

### E. Extension Rule
One layer must extend beyond the other by ≥ EXT. For example, polysilicon must extend **2λ beyond the active region** on each side of a transistor to form a proper MOS gate.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Time-to-market**: DRC tools (Cadence PVS, Mentor Calibre, Synopsys IC Validator) catch errors in **seconds** that would otherwise lead to multi-million-dollar wafer re-spins.
- **Yield Maximization**: A design that passes DRC has drastically higher *parametric yield*.
- **Portability**: A λ-rule design can be re-targeted to a new fab by simply scaling — the design is *future-proof*.
- **IP Reuse**: Standard cell libraries (e.g., TSMC 28 nm, Intel 7 nm) are all built on hardened, DRC-clean layouts.

# 3. Step-by-Step Layout Construction Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 General CMOS Layout Construction Algorithm (Universal)

Every CMOS gate layout is built using the **same 6-step procedure** (this is the **gold algorithm** for the KTU project):

> **Step 1 — Identify the Logic Function & Transistor Netlist.**
> Write the Pull-Up Network (PUN, PMOS) and Pull-Down Network (PDN, NMOS).

> **Step 2 — Draw the Stick Diagram.**
> Use Euler's rule (max one crossing per node) to obtain a planar stick diagram. Colour-code: **blue = poly, green = n-diff, red = p-diff, black = metal1, X = contact**.

> **Step 3 — Convert Stick Diagram to Mask Layout.**
> Assign widths based on W/L ratios. Apply λ-rules to every edge. Maintain poly-over-diffusion for gates (overlap = 2λ on each side).

> **Step 4 — Place Contacts.**
> Every input/output that must be connected to metal must have a 2λ×2λ contact surrounded by 1λ enclosure of both poly/diff and metal1.

> **Step 5 — Verify Euler Path Continuity.**
> Walk the Euler path on both poly (input signal) and on the diffusion (output nodes). The same Euler path should drive *both* PUN and PDN to minimize poly length.

> **Step 6 — Run DRC.**
> Sweep the layout for the 18 rules above. Output a violation report. Fix and re-verify.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — CMOS Inverter Layout (Detailed Derivation)

### Schematic
A CMOS inverter has **one PMOS** (in n-well, source tied to VDD) and **one NMOS** (in p-substrate, source tied to GND). Both gates are tied together to the input **A**, and both drains are tied together to the output **Y**.

### Step-by-Step Mask-Layer Construction

**Step 1:** Draw the n-well as a large rectangle, width 12λ, height 12λ. Centre it on the cell.

**Step 2:** Inside the n-well, draw the **p-diffusion** (red) as a horizontal bar of dimensions 6λ (W, vertical) × 12λ (length, horizontal). This forms the PMOS active area.

**Step 3:** Outside the n-well (in p-substrate), draw the **n-diffusion** (green) directly below the p-diff, with a 6λ vertical gap (Rule 3, n-diff to p-diff spacing). Dimensions: 3λ × 12λ. (NMOS is typically minimum-size for matched area.)

**Step 4:** Draw a vertical **polysilicon** rectangle, 2λ wide, crossing both the p-diff and n-diff horizontally, centred. The poly extends 2λ above the p-diff (Rule 6) and 2λ below the n-diff. This single poly line forms both gates simultaneously and is the **input A**.

**Step 5:** Add the **VDD metal1 rail** along the top of the cell, connecting to the left end of the p-diff via a contact (cut = 2λ×2λ, surrounded by 1λ enclosure of p-diff and 1λ enclosure of metal1).

**Step 6:** Add the **GND metal1 rail** along the bottom of the cell, connecting to the left end of the n-diff via a contact (same enclosure rules).

**Step 7:** Add a **Y-output metal1 line** connecting the *right* drain of both the p-diff and n-diff. This requires **two contacts** — one on the p-diff (right end) and one on the n-diff (right end), both enclosed by metal1.

**Step 8:** Add a **body tie (substrate contact)**: a separate p+ diffusion region in the p-substrate, connected to GND via a contact, **at least 6λ away from the n-diff** (avoids latch-up).

**Step 9:** Add a **n-well tie**: a separate n+ diffusion region inside the n-well, connected to VDD via a contact, **at least 6λ from the p-diff**.

### Final Layout Dimension Audit

Let cell pitch = **P**. Total cell width for an inverter (minimum size, 3λ PMOS / 3λ NMOS) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cell} &= 2\lambda \, (\text{poly overlap left}) + 2\lambda \, (\text{poly width}) + 2\lambda \, (\text{poly overlap right}) + 4\lambda \, (\text{drain ext.}) \\
&= 10\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

The cell height (top of n-well to bottom of substrate tie):

$$
H_{cell} = 6\lambda \,(\text{p-diff W}) + 6\lambda \,(\text{n-diff–p-diff gap}) + 6\lambda \,(\text{n-diff W}) + 4\lambda \,(\text{rails/sub-ties}) = 22\lambda
$$

For a **micron-scale** process (λ = 0.5 µm), this gives a 5 µm × 11 µm inverter — a very compact standard cell.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — CMOS 2-Input NAND Gate Layout

### Transistor Netlist
- **PDN:** Two NMOS in **series** (N1 on top, N2 on bottom). N1 drain = Y, N1 source = N2 drain, N2 source = GND.
- **PUN:** Two PMOS in **parallel** (P1 and P2). P1 and P2 sources both tied to VDD; both drains tied to Y.
- **Inputs:** A drives N1 and P1; B drives N2 and P2.

### Layout Strategy
- Use a **single vertical poly line for A** crossing both P1 and N1, and a **second vertical poly line for B** crossing both P2 and N2.
- Place P1 and P2 **side-by-side** in the n-well (parallel ⇒ separate p-diff islands).
- Place N1 and N2 **end-to-end in one continuous n-diff strip** (series ⇒ single shared diffusion island between them).
- Connect the drains of P1, P2, and N1 with a single **horizontal metal1 line (output Y)**.

### Euler Path Analysis
- **Poly Euler path:** A → B (input A crosses the top devices, input B crosses the bottom devices) — single poly line, no crossing needed.
- **Diffusion Euler path:** In the **n-diff**: drain of N1 → shared source/drain → source of N2 → GND contact.
- In the **p-diff**: Source(VDD) → drain(Y) on the left, and Source(VDD) → drain(Y) on the right (two parallel PMOS share VDD on top and Y on the bottom).

The fact that A and B enter as two parallel poly lines that **don't cross each other** confirms the layout is **planar** (Euler's rule satisfied).

### Dimension Calculation (Sizing for Inverter-Equivalent Drive)

For the pull-down (series NMOS), each NMOS needs double width to match the inverter's drive. So $(W/L)_{N1} = (W/L)_{N2} = (6\lambda/2\lambda)$. Total n-diff horizontal length:

$$
L_{n-diff} = 2\lambda + 4\lambda + 2\lambda + 4\lambda + 2\lambda = 14\lambda
$$

Where the 2λ's are poly extensions and the 4λ's are the gate lengths plus the shared-drain extension.

For the pull-up (parallel PMOS), each PMOS can be minimum size $(W/L)_{P} = (3\lambda/2\lambda)$. Total p-diff layout length:

$$
L_{p-diff} = 2\lambda + 4\lambda + 4\lambda + 4\lambda + 2\lambda = 16\lambda
$$

(Slightly wider because the two PMOS are placed side by side rather than sharing a drain.)

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — CMOS 2-Input NOR Gate Layout

### Transistor Netlist
- **PDN:** Two NMOS in **parallel** (N1, N2). Sources share GND, drains share Y.
- **PUN:** Two PMOS in **series** (P1, P2). P1 source = VDD, P1 drain = P2 source, P2 drain = Y.

### Layout Strategy
- The PMOS are now in **series**, so they must be drawn in a **single horizontal p-diff strip** (shared drain/source between them).
- The NMOS are in **parallel**, so they need **two separate n-diff islands** side by side.
- The output Y is connected via metal1.

### Euler Path Analysis
- For **NOR**, the poly Euler path crosses the poly lines — meaning there is a **node crossing**. A *single-stripe* layout is **not possible** without a metal jumper. Therefore, NOR gates typically require a **slightly more complex routing** (a small metal1 "jog" or poly jog) — this is a classic KTU question.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often draw the NOR layout as if it were a NAND (series on top, parallel on bottom) — the **pull-up and pull-down networks must always be drawn as series in PDN ↔ parallel in PUN for the same function**, never identical.

## 3.5 DRC Walkthrough on the Inverter Layout

After drawing the inverter, we run DRC systematically. Below is the **violation matrix** that the checker would output:

| Layer | Rule Checked | Drawn | Required | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| p-diff | Width | 6λ | ≥ 3λ | ✅ Pass |
| p-diff | Inside n-well | yes | required | ✅ Pass |
| p-diff to n-well edge | Spacing | 3λ | ≥ 6λ | ❌ **FAIL** — increase n-well size or shift p-diff |
| n-diff | Width | 3λ | ≥ 3λ | ✅ Pass |
| n-diff to p-diff | Spacing | 6λ | ≥ 6λ | ✅ Pass |
| Poly | Width | 2λ | ≥ 2λ | ✅ Pass |
| Poly over p-diff (gate) | Extension | 2λ | ≥ 2λ | ✅ Pass |
| Poly over n-diff (gate) | Extension | 2λ | ≥ 2λ | ✅ Pass |
| Contact (top-left) | Size | 2λ×2λ | 2λ×2λ | ✅ Pass |
| Contact | Active enclosure | 1λ all sides | ≥ 1λ | ✅ Pass |
| Contact | Metal enclosure | 1λ all sides | ≥ 1λ | ✅ Pass |
| Metal1 (VDD rail) | Width | 3λ | ≥ 3λ | ✅ Pass |
| Metal1 to poly | Spacing | 3λ | ≥ 1λ (field) | ✅ Pass |
| Body tie (p+ in p-sub) | To n-diff spacing | 6λ | ≥ 6λ | ✅ Pass |

> After fixing the one failure (shifting the p-diff further into the n-well), the layout is **DRC-clean** and ready for fabrication.

## 3.6 The CMOS Inverter Layout — Annotated Cross-Section

The cross-section of the inverter along the poly gate (vertical cut) reveals the physical structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Top to bottom (along cut-line through PMOS):} \quad & \\
& \text{Metal1 (VDD contact)} \\
& \text{Contact cut (2λ×2λ)} \\
& \text{p+ diffusion (depth ≈ 0.2 µm, doping ≈ } 10^{20}\text{/cm}^3) \\
& \text{n-Well (depth ≈ 1–2 µm, doping ≈ } 10^{16}\text{/cm}^3) \\
& \text{p-Substrate (depth ≈ 500 µm, doping ≈ } 10^{15}\text{/cm}^3) \\
& \text{Thin gate oxide (SiO}_2\text{, thickness } t_{ox} \approx 10\text{ nm)} \\
& \text{Polysilicon gate (height ≈ 0.4 µm, doped n+)} \\
& \text{n+ diffusion (source/drain of NMOS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **threshold voltage $V_T$** of the PMOS is *negative* (typically $-0.7\text{ V}$ to $-1.0\text{ V}$), and of the NMOS is *positive* ($+0.5\text{ V}$ to $+0.7\text{ V}$). The poly gate is **n+ doped** for both, which gives a surface work-function suitable for symmetric threshold adjustment.

## 4.1 CMOS Design Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    specA[System Specification] --> specB[RTL Design]
    specB --> specC[Logic Synthesis]
    specC --> gata1[Gate Level Netlist]
    gata1 --> gata2[Transistor Level Schematic]
    gata2 --> stickA[Stick Diagram]
    stickA --> stickB[Euler Path Verification]
    stickB --> layA[Physical Layout Design]
    layA --> layB[Apply Lambda Rules]
    layB --> drcA[DRC Verification]
    drcA --> drcB{DRC Clean?}
    drcB -- No --> layA
    drcB -- Yes --> lvsA[LVS Layout vs Schematic]
    lvsA --> lvsB{LVS Match?}
    lvsB -- No --> gata2
    lvsB -- Yes --> extA[Extraction of Parasitics]
    extA --> simA[Post Layout Simulation]
    simA --> simB{Meets Specs?}
    simB -- No --> specA
    simB -- Yes --> tapeA[Tape Out to Fab]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vddA[VDD Rail] --- p1A[P diff P1 source]

```

## 4.4 DRC Verification Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Layout File GDS II] --> readA[Read by DRC Engine]
    readA --> extA[Layer Extraction and Boolean Operations]
    extA --> ruleA[Apply Width Rules]
    extA --> ruleB[Apply Spacing Rules]
    extA --> ruleC[Apply Enclosure Rules]
    extA --> ruleD[Apply Extension Rules]
    ruleA --> outA[Error Database]
    ruleB --> outA
    ruleC --> outA
    ruleD --> outA
    outA --> visA[Violation Marker in Layout]
    outA --> logA[ASCII Report File]
    visA --> fixA[Engineer Fixes Layout]
    fixA --> startA
    logA --> endA[Final DRC Clean Report]
```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question]
**Define Design Rule Checking (DRC). List any four categories of CMOS design rules with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Design Rule Checking (DRC) is the post-layout geometric verification process in which the physical layout is systematically verified against a set of pre-defined manufacturing constraints (design rules) to ensure the design is fabricable with acceptable yield.

The four categories are:

1. **Width Rule** — e.g., Minimum poly width = 2λ.
2. **Spacing Rule** — e.g., Minimum spacing between two metal1 lines = 3λ.
3. **Enclosure Rule** — e.g., Diffusion must enclose a contact cut by 1λ on every side.
4. **Extension Rule** — e.g., Polysilicon must extend 2λ beyond the active (diffusion) region on each side of the gate.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is meant by the lambda (λ) design rule? Why is it preferred over micron-based rules?**

**Model Answer:**

The **lambda (λ) design rule** is a *scalable*, process-independent set of geometric constraints where every rule is expressed as an integer multiple of a single parameter λ, where λ = (minimum feature size)/2.

**Advantages over micron rules:**

- **Portability** — A design drawn in λ can be migrated to a new process simply by changing λ (linear scaling). A 2λ poly width in 0.5 µm process (1 µm wide) automatically becomes 1 µm in 0.25 µm process by setting λ = 0.125 µm.
- **Simplicity** — Only one number (λ) needs to be remembered.
- **Educational value** — Easy to teach, learn, and apply in classroom projects.
- **No IP loss** — The same schematic and layout remain valid across process generations (within scaling limits).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In real industry (TSMC, Intel, Samsung), **micron-based and "fat-wire" rules** are now preferred because λ-rules are *too conservative* for advanced nodes and ignore direction-dependent effects (stress, lithography). A student who says "λ-rules are always used in industry" will lose 1 mark.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified from Past Paper]

**a)** With a neat stick diagram and mask layout, explain the design of a **CMOS 2-input NAND gate**. State the Euler paths for both poly and diffusion. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Perform a complete **DRC check** on your NAND layout. Tabulate all 18 rules and indicate PASS/FAIL with the exact violation values. Suggest a minimum modification to make the layout DRC-clean. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Netlist:**

- PDN: N1 (drain = Y, gate = A) in series with N2 (drain = N1-source, gate = B, source = GND).
- PUN: P1 and P2 in parallel. P1 (source = VDD, gate = A, drain = Y); P2 (source = VDD, gate = B, drain = Y).

**Step 2 — Euler Path Analysis:**

- **Poly Euler path:** Input A → Input B (top to bottom). The two poly lines are **vertical and parallel** with no crossing — this confirms a **single-polysilicon-stripe planar layout** is possible.
- **n-Diffusion Euler path:** GND → N2-source → N2-drain/N1-source (shared node) → N1-drain (Y). This is a **continuous, unbroken path** of length ≈ 14λ.
- **p-Diffusion Euler path:** Y → P1-drain → P1-source (VDD) — first branch; Y → P2-drain → P2-source (VDD) — second branch. Each PMOS occupies a **separate p-diff island** (parallel network ⇒ separate diffusions).

**Step 3 — Sizing for Inverter-Equivalent Drive:**

Each NMOS in the series pull-down must be sized at $(W/L) = (6\lambda/2\lambda)$ so that the series combination gives an effective $(W/L) = (3\lambda/2\lambda)$, matching the inverter's NMOS.

Each PMOS remains at $(W/L) = (3\lambda/2\lambda)$ because they are in parallel (no drive loss).

**Step 4 — Layout Coordinates (in λ units, origin at lower-left):**

- n-Well: 8λ wide × 14λ tall, placed at (1λ, 8λ) to (9λ, 22λ).
- p-Diff (P1): 6λ wide × 6λ tall, at (1λ, 14λ) to (7λ, 20λ).
- p-Diff (P2): 6λ wide × 6λ tall, at (7λ, 14λ) to (13λ, 20λ). [Spacing between P1 and P2 = 0λ because they are *parallel* and share the VDD/Y metal — actually they are drawn **with 3λ spacing** to satisfy active-to-active spacing.]
- n-Diff: 3λ wide × 14λ tall, at (1λ, −2λ) to (4λ, 12λ).
- Poly A: 2λ wide, vertical, at x = 4λ, extending from y = 12λ to y = 22λ.
- Poly B: 2λ wide, vertical, at x = 8λ, extending from y = 12λ to y = 22λ.
- VDD rail: 3λ wide metal1 at y = 23λ, from x = 0 to x = 14λ, with contacts at (2λ, 22.5λ) and (12λ, 22.5λ).
- GND rail: 3λ wide metal1 at y = −3λ, with contact at (2λ, −2.5λ).
- Y output: 3λ wide metal1 at y = 6λ, with contacts at (2λ, 6.5λ) on the shared N1/N2 drain, at (4λ, 17.5λ) on P1 drain, and at (10λ, 17.5λ) on P2 drain.

**Step 5 — Stick Diagram (textual representation):**

```
       VDD ──┬─────────────┬─── (P1, P2 sources)
            │             │
       ─────┼───A─────────┼─── (poly A crosses both P1 and N1)
            │             │
            └────B────────┘   (poly B crosses both P2 and N2)
            │             │
            A─Y           B─Y
            │             │
       GND──┴─────────────┴─── (N2 source, only one GND contact at bottom)
```

(Each vertical "│" represents n-diff, each "A" / "B" represents poly crossing the diff, each "Y" represents the Y metal connection.)

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

The DRC table below checks every rule from the lambda rule book against the drawn layout above.

| # | Rule | Drawn | Required | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | p-diff width | 6λ | ≥ 3λ | ✅ Pass |
| 2 | p-diff to p-diff spacing (P1–P2) | 6λ | ≥ 3λ | ✅ Pass |
| 3 | n-diff to p-diff spacing | 6λ (vertical gap) | ≥ 6λ | ✅ Pass |
| 4 | n-diff width | 3λ | ≥ 3λ | ✅ Pass |
| 5 | n-diff to n-diff spacing | n/a (single island) | ≥ 3λ | ✅ N/A |
| 6 | Poly width | 2λ | ≥ 2λ | ✅ Pass |
| 7 | Poly to poly spacing (A–B) | 4λ | ≥ 2λ | ✅ Pass |
| 8 | Poly extension over p-diff | 2λ | ≥ 2λ | ✅ Pass |
| 9 | Poly extension over n-diff | 2λ | ≥ 2λ | ✅ Pass |
| 10 | Poly to field diff (none here) | n/a | ≥ 1λ | ✅ N/A |
| 11 | Metal1 width | 3λ | ≥ 3λ | ✅ Pass |
| 12 | Metal1 to Metal1 spacing | 4λ | ≥ 3λ | ✅ Pass |
| 13 | Contact size | 2λ×2λ | 2λ×2λ | ✅ Pass |
| 14 | Contact spacing | 4λ | ≥ 2λ | ✅ Pass |
| 15 | Contact enclosure by active (p-diff side) | 1λ | ≥ 1λ | ✅ Pass |
| 16 | Contact enclosure by active (n-diff side) | 1λ | ≥ 1λ | ✅ Pass |
| 17 | Contact enclosure by metal1 | 1λ | ≥ 1λ | ✅ Pass |
| 18 | n-Well width | 8λ | ≥ 6λ | ✅ Pass |
| 19 | n-Well to p-diff edge | 1λ | ≥ 6λ | ❌ **FAIL** |

**Violation #19 Fix:** The p-diff is only 1λ from the n-well boundary; it must be ≥ 6λ. **Modification:** Increase the n-well width from 8λ to 16λ (extend it on both sides by 4λ) so that the p-diff sits at least 6λ inside the well on all sides.

After this single fix, the layout is **DRC-clean**.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**a)** Draw the **stick diagram and CMOS layout** for a **2-input NOR gate**. Identify the Euler path and justify whether a single poly stripe is possible. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare the **layout area, transistor count, and Euler-path complexity** of NOR vs NAND. Which gate is preferred in standard cell libraries and why? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Netlist:**

- PDN: N1 and N2 in parallel. N1 (gate = A, drain = Y, source = GND); N2 (gate = B, drain = Y, source = GND).
- PUN: P1 and P2 in series. P1 (source = VDD, gate = A, drain = internal node M); P2 (source = M, gate = B, drain = Y).

**Step 2 — Euler Path:**

- For the **PMOS series network**, the poly must visit A first, then B, with no crossing. The Euler path on poly is **A → B** (one direction).
- For the **NMOS parallel network**, the poly must branch to A on one diffusion island and to B on another — there is **a topological crossing** between the poly branches of the parallel NMOS network and the series PMOS network.

**Conclusion:** A **single-polysilicon-stripe** layout is **NOT possible** for the 2-input NOR in its canonical form, because the parallel NMOS forces two separate poly lines entering from the same side. A **single row** layout requires a **metal jumper** or a **poly jog** in the upper (PMOS) network to avoid crossing.

**Step 3 — Layout Sketch (simplified coordinate list, λ units):**

- n-Well: at (1λ, 6λ) to (15λ, 18λ), size 14λ × 12λ.
- p-Diff (PUN, single island, series): 6λ wide × 6λ tall, at (4λ, 10λ) to (10λ, 16λ).
- n-Diff (N1, left): 3λ wide × 6λ tall, at (1λ, 0λ) to (4λ, 6λ).
- n-Diff (N2, right): 3λ wide × 6λ tall, at (7λ, 0λ) to (10λ, 6λ).
- Poly A: 2λ wide vertical, at x = 4λ, from y = 0 to y = 16λ (passes through N1, P1).
- Poly B: 2λ wide vertical, at x = 8λ, from y = 0 to y = 16λ (passes through N2, P2).
- VDD rail (metal1): 3λ wide at y = 18λ, contacting P1 source at (7λ, 16.5λ).
- GND rail (metal1): 3λ wide at y = −2λ, with two contacts at (2λ, −1.5λ) and (8λ, −1.5λ) — one for each NMOS source.
- Y output (metal1): 3λ wide horizontal at y = 8λ, connecting P2 drain (9λ, 7.5λ), N1 drain (2λ, 7.5λ), N2 drain (8λ, 7.5λ) — three contacts.
- Metal jumper for the internal P1-drain/P2-source node M: small metal1 square at (7λ, 13λ), contacting the p-diff at the middle.

**Step 4 — Stick Diagram (textual):**

```
       VDD ──────────
                   │
               ──A─┤   (poly A enters at top of P1)
                   │
                   M   (internal node, metal jumper)
                   │
               ──B─┤   (poly B enters at top of P2)
                   │
                   Y
                  / \
            A── /   \ ──B
                |   |
            GND─┘   └─GND   (two separate n-diff islands)
```

**Step 5 — Final Verification:**

A **single poly stripe** would require that A and B enter the cell at the *same* edge and leave at the *same* edge without crossing. In the NOR, A controls N1 (left n-diff) and P1 (top of p-diff), while B controls N2 (right n-diff) and P2 (bottom of p-diff). To have a single poly stripe, A would have to cross P2 (wrong — A only controls P1). Therefore, a single stripe is impossible. **Two poly stripes are mandatory**, and the layout is wider than the NAND.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

| Parameter | 2-Input NAND | 2-Input NOR | Winner (Compactness) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total transistors | 4 (2P + 2N) | 4 (2P + 2N) | **Tie** |
| Series NMOS (PDN) | 2 (stack) | 0 (parallel) | NAND has stack |
| Series PMOS (PUN) | 0 (parallel) | 2 (stack) | NOR has stack |
| p-Diff islands | 2 (separate) | 1 (single) | NOR more compact in p-diff |
| n-Diff islands | 1 (single) | 2 (separate) | NAND more compact in n-diff |
| Cell width (approx.) | 16λ | 14λ | **NOR slightly narrower** |
| Cell height (approx.) | 22λ | 22λ | **Tie** |
| Poly stripes needed | 1 (planar) | 2 (crossing exists) | **NAND is planar** |
| Euler path planar? | Yes | No (needs metal jumper) | **NAND preferred** |
| Drive strength loss | PDN: 2× wider needed; PUN: full | PDN: full; PUN: 2× wider needed | **Asymmetric** |
| Input capacitance | A sees 1 NMOS + 1 PMOS; B sees 1 NMOS + 1 PMOS | A sees 1 NMOS + 1 PMOS; B sees 1 NMOS + 1 PMOS | **Tie** |
| **Standard cell library preference** | **Strongly preferred** | Used less often | **NAND** |

**Why NAND is preferred in standard cell libraries:**

1. **Planar layout** — A single continuous poly line is possible because the PDN is series and the PUN is parallel (Euler rule satisfied trivially).
2. **Smaller cell area per function** — Single poly row, single n-diff row, and only 2 p-diff islands give a tight pitch.
3. **Easier to scale** — No metal jumpers, simpler routing over the cell.
4. **Lower input capacitance variation** — All inputs see the same load.
5. **Lower diffusion capacitance on output** — NAND's PUN has only 2 transistors in parallel sharing a single Y drain node, minimizing the drain cap.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often wrongly conclude that **NOR is preferred** because the series PMOS is "above" the NMOS and seems simpler — but the **lack of Euler-path planarity** is the actual deal-breaker. Always justify cell choice by **Euler planarity + area + drive symmetry**, not by the order of networks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **10 High-Yield Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ⭐ **DRC is a geometric check, not an electrical check.** It only validates distances, widths, and overlaps.
- ⭐ **Lambda (λ) = F/2**, where F is the minimum feature size of the process.
- ⭐ The **18 standard λ-rules** (width, spacing, enclosure, extension) form the backbone of every CMOS layout exam.
- ⭐ **Polysilicon width = 2λ** is the universal default; diffusion width is typically **3λ**.
- ⭐ **Contact cut is always 2λ × 2λ**, surrounded by **1λ enclosure** on every side.
- ⭐ **p-diffusion must lie 6λ inside the n-well boundary** (latch-up prevention — most-violated rule).
- ⭐ **Euler's rule**: a stick diagram is planar only if there is **at most one node crossing per signal net**. The NAND is planar; the NOR is *not* (requires metal jumper).
- ⭐ For **series-stacked transistors**, you must **increase the W** to maintain the effective W/L of a single device.
- ⭐ **Body ties** (p+ to GND, n+ to VDD) are mandatory and must be **≥ 6λ** from the closest active area.
- ⭐ A **DRC-clean** layout is necessary but **not sufficient** — **LVS** (Layout vs Schematic) and **parasitic extraction** must follow to ensure electrical correctness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Day Tip:** Always draw the layout with a **grid background** (each cell = 1λ) and label **every dimension** explicitly. Examiners award partial marks for *correct intent* even if the drawing is slightly off. State the **Euler path first**, then the layout — this shows the design methodology clearly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project: Design and Simulate Basic Memory Cells Including SRAM and DRAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT604 - VLSI CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project: Design and Simulate Basic Memory Cells Including SRAM and DRAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Memory Cells in VLSI

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBECT604 – VLSI Circuit Design)**, a **memory cell** is the smallest addressable unit of storage fabricated on a silicon die. It must reliably retain a binary state (`0` or `1`) and allow the state to be read or rewritten through electrical access transistors. The two canonical CMOS-compatible cell families considered in this project module are:

- **SRAM (Static Random-Access Memory):** A bistable cross-coupled inverter pair with dedicated access transistors that holds data as long as supply power is maintained, with no periodic refresh.
- **DRAM (Dynamic Random-Access Memory):** A single access transistor plus a storage capacitor (`1T1C`) that stores charge representing a binary value, requiring periodic refresh because of sub-threshold and junction leakage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Project Specification:**
> The design component of Module 4 requires the student to **schematic, size, and simulate** the read, write, hold, and (for DRAM) refresh behaviour of representative SRAM and DRAM cells using an industry-standard EDA tool such as **NGSPICE / HSPICE / LTspice / Cadence Spectre** with a **180 nm / 90 nm / 65 nm** CMOS technology node.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of an integrated memory chip as a vast **library** of tiny lockers.

- **SRAM** is like a locker with a **spring-loaded double door and magnetic latch**. Once you snap a parcel in, the latch holds it firmly — but the locker is bulky because it needs a full mechanical latching mechanism. You can read or write any parcel instantly, but the locker takes up more floor space.
- **DRAM** is like a locker with a **single bucket placed on a small platform**. You drop a coin (charge) into the bucket to record a "1" and empty it for a "0". The bucket is small and cheap, so you can pack millions of them into the library. However, the bucket is **slightly leaky** — the water slowly evaporates, so a librarian must walk around every few milliseconds topping up the buckets. The bucket is leaky but very dense.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants & Standard CMOS Memory Metrics (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **Thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV** at **$T = 300$ K**.
> - **CMOS supply rail $V_{DD}$** — typically **1.8 V (180 nm)**, **1.0 V (90 nm)**, **0.7 V (65 nm)**.
> - **6T SRAM cell area** at 180 nm ≈ **120–150 F²** (F = minimum feature size).
> - **DRAM cell area (1T1C)** ≈ **6–8 F²** — hence its density advantage.
> - **Refresh interval $t_{REF}$** for commercial DRAM ≈ **64 ms**.
> - **Read Static Noise Margin (Read SNM)** target ≥ **0.4 × $V_{DD}$**.
> - **Hold Static Noise Margin (Hold SNM)** target ≥ **0.2 × $V_{DD}$**.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum feature size | F | 0.18 | µm |
| Supply voltage | VDD | 1.8 | V |
| NMOS threshold | VTHN | 0.45 | V |
| PMOS threshold | VTHP | -0.50 | V |
| Oxide thickness | tox | 4.0 | nm |
| Storage capacitance | Cs | 25–30 | fF |
| Bit-line capacitance | CBL | 100–250 | fF |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the bold numbers matter:** Examiners often award partial credit for quoting the correct **$V_{DD}$** for the technology node and the **SNM target ratio**. Memorise that **Read SNM ≈ 0.4 × $V_{DD}$** and **Hold SNM ≈ 0.2 × $V_{DD}$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6T CMOS SRAM Cell — Operational Decomposition

The standard CMOS SRAM cell is a **6-transistor (6T) bistable** structure. It comprises two cross-coupled CMOS inverters (M1–M2 and M3–M4) that form the storage latch, plus two NMOS access transistors (M5 and M6) that connect the internal storage nodes (`Q` and `QB`) to the bit-lines (`BL` and `BLB`) when the word-line (`WL`) is asserted.

**Read Operation Logic (Why and How):**
- Pre-charge both `BL` and `BLB` to $V_{DD}$.
- Assert `WL = V_{DD}$`. The access transistors turn ON.
- The node holding logic `0` (e.g., `Q = 0`) pulls the corresponding bit-line low through the pull-down NMOS.
- A sense amplifier detects the differential voltage between `BL` and `BLB`.
- **Design constraint:** during read, the storage node voltage must not flip the cell. This is the *read-disturb* problem, controlled by the **Cell Ratio (CR)**.

**Write Operation Logic (Why and How):**
- Drive the bit-lines with the new data (e.g., `BL = 0`, `BLB = V_{DD}$`).
- Assert `WL = V_{DD}$`. The access transistor that drives the `1`-side storage node LOW must overcome the pull-up PMOS of the opposite inverter.
- **Design constraint:** write is governed by the **Pull-up Ratio (PR)**.

**Hold Operation Logic (Why and How):**
- `WL = 0`. Access transistors are OFF. The cross-coupled inverters reinforce each other through positive feedback — data is held statically.
- This is the most stable condition; SNM is highest.

## 2.2 The 1T1C DRAM Cell — Operational Decomposition

- **Write:** Word-line `WL` is asserted. The access transistor (NMOS) connects the storage capacitor $C_S$ to the bit-line `BL`, which has been driven to $V_{DD}$ (for `1`) or `0` (for `0`). Charge $Q = C_S \cdot V_{BL}$ is stored on $C_S$.
- **Read:** Pre-charge `BL` to $V_{DD}/2$ (a balance voltage). Assert `WL`. Charge-sharing occurs between $C_S$ and $C_{BL}$. The resulting small bit-line voltage deviation (a few hundred mV) is amplified by a sense amplifier.
- **Refresh:** Periodically read-and-rewrite every cell to compensate sub-threshold and junction leakage current.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

The table below is the **single most important reference** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on memory cells. It consolidates all derivable quantities, design ratios, and stability metrics.

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR | Cell Ratio | $\displaystyle \text{CR} = \frac{(W/L)_{PD}}{(W/L)_{AX}}$ | unitless | PD = pull-down, AX = access; must be $\geq 1.2$ for read stability |
| PR | Pull-up Ratio | $\displaystyle \text{PR} = \frac{(W/L)_{PU}}{(W/L)_{AX}}$ | unitless | PU = pull-up PMOS; must be $\leq 1.5$ for write-ability |
| SNM | Static Noise Margin | Graphical butterfly-curve method | V | Read SNM target $\geq 0.4 \cdot V_{DD}$ |
| $\Delta V_{BL}$ | DRAM Read Bit-line Swing | $\displaystyle \Delta V_{BL} = V_S \cdot \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_{BL}}$ | V | Typically 100–250 mV |
| $V_S$ | DRAM Storage Voltage | $V_S$ approaches $V_{DD}/2$ on a read | V | Mid-level precharge |
| $t_{RET}$ | DRAM Retention Time | $\displaystyle t_{RET} = \frac{C_S \cdot \Delta V_{S}}{I_{LEAK}}$ | s | Must exceed refresh interval |
| $I_{LEAK}$ | Leakage Current | Sum of sub-$V_{TH}$ and junction leakage | A | Node-dependent |
| $t_{A}$ | Word-line Access Time | $\displaystyle t_A \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{AX} \cdot C_{BL}$ | s | RC-dominated delay |
| $P_{HOLD}$ | Hold Power | $P = V_{DD} \cdot I_{LEAK,total}$ | W | Set by sub-threshold leakage |
| DRV | Data Retention Voltage | $V_{DD,\min}$ at which Hold SNM = 0 | V | Used for low-power SRAM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical: In all the table rows above, the math symbols use `\vert`-style absolute value or normal parentheses, never the raw `|` character, to preserve KTU-friendly markdown rendering.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 6T SRAM cell is the **workhorse of register files, L1/L2 CPU caches, and look-up tables** in every modern processor (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, ARM Cortex). The 1T1C DRAM cell is the foundation of **main memory (DDR4/DDR5, LPDDR) and graphics DRAM (GDDR6X)**. The design ratios CR and PR, along with the SNM butterfly analysis, are the same metrics used by industry engineers at Intel, TSMC, and Samsung to qualify cells before tape-out. The KTU 2024 scheme places these concepts in Module 4 specifically because they integrate all prior module learning: MOSFET I-V, CMOS inverter VTC, parasitic capacitance, and noise margins.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern **8T, 10T, and 12T SRAM cells** are evolutionary variants of the basic 6T cell — they add extra transistors to **decouple read and write paths**, eliminating the read-disturb problem and enabling operation down to near-threshold voltages for ultra-low-power IoT devices. The KTU 2024 project does not require 8T, but understanding the 6T trade-off is the conceptual foundation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Read Stability Condition and Cell Ratio (CR)

**Statement:** During a read access, the storage node holding `0` rises above ground because of the voltage divider formed by the access transistor (M5) and the pull-down transistor (M1). The condition for *read stability* is that this node voltage must remain below the switching threshold of the opposite inverter (M3–M4), otherwise the cell flips.

**Step 1 — Identify the voltage divider.** When `WL = V_{DD}` and `BL = V_{DD}` (pre-charged), the access transistor M5 and pull-down M1 form a series stack from $V_{DD}$ to GND. The intermediate node voltage is the storage node `Q`.

**Step 2 — Use the long-channel saturation/linear current model.** Assume both M1 and M5 operate in the linear (triode) region because the overdrive voltage at `Q` is small. Equate the drain currents:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{D,M5} &= I_{D,M1} \\[4pt]
\mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{AX}\!\left[(V_{GS,AX} - V_{THN})V_{DS,AX} - \tfrac{1}{2}V_{DS,AX}^{2}\right] &= \mu_n C_{ox}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{PD}\!\left[(V_{GS,PD} - V_{THN})V_{DS,PD} - \tfrac{1}{2}V_{DS,PD}^{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the circuit boundary conditions.** With `WL = BL = V_{DD}` and `Q = V_{Q}`:
- $V_{GS,AX} = V_{DD} - V_Q$
- $V_{DS,AX} = V_{DD} - V_Q$
- $V_{GS,PD} = V_Q$
- $V_{DS,PD} = V_Q$

Substituting and dividing both sides by the common factor $\mu_n C_{ox}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{AX}\!\left[(V_{DD} - V_Q - V_{THN})(V_{DD} - V_Q) - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{DD} - V_Q)^{2}\right] &= \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{PD}\!\left[(V_Q - V_{THN})V_Q - \tfrac{1}{2}V_Q^{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic simplification.** Expand the left bracket:
$(V_{DD} - V_Q - V_{THN})(V_{DD} - V_Q) - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{DD} - V_Q)^2 = (V_{DD} - V_Q)^2 - V_{THN}(V_{DD} - V_Q) - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{DD} - V_Q)^2$

This reduces to $\tfrac{1}{2}(V_{DD} - V_Q)^2 - V_{THN}(V_{DD} - V_Q)$.

Similarly, the right bracket simplifies to $\tfrac{1}{2}V_Q^2 - V_{THN} V_Q$.

**Step 5 — Form the cell ratio.** Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{(W/L)_{PD}}{(W/L)_{AX}} &= \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}(V_{DD} - V_Q)^2 - V_{THN}(V_{DD} - V_Q)}{\tfrac{1}{2}V_Q^2 - V_{THN} V_Q} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \text{CR} &= \frac{(V_{DD} - V_Q)(V_{DD} - V_Q - 2V_{THN})}{V_Q(V_Q - 2V_{THN})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Enforce the read-stability inequality.** For the cell not to flip, the storage-node voltage $V_Q$ must be less than the trip-point $V_{INV}$ of the cross-coupled inverter, typically $V_{DD}/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_Q &< V_{INV} = V_{DD}/2 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \text{CR} &> \frac{(V_{DD}/2)(V_{DD}/2 - 2V_{THN})}{(V_{DD}/2 - V_{THN})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Plug in numerical values (180 nm, $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $V_{THN} = 0.45$ V).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR}_{\min} &> \frac{(0.9)(0.9 - 0.9)}{(0.9 - 0.45)^2} = \frac{(0.9)(0)}{(0.45)^2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**This indicates a singularity at the $V_{DD}/2$ choice**; a more practical design uses $V_Q \le 0.2 V_{DD}$, which yields the industry-standard rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\text{CR} \geq 1.2 \text{ to } 1.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical KTU exam answer.

## 3.2 Derivation: Write Margin and Pull-up Ratio (PR)

**Statement:** For a successful write, the access transistor must pull the `1`-side storage node below the switching threshold of the cross-coupled inverter against the pull-up PMOS.

**Step 1 — Write equation.** When writing a `0` to a node previously holding `1`:
- `BL = 0`, `WL = V_{DD}`
- Node `Q` must drop below the trip-point of the M3–M4 inverter (≈ $V_{DD}/2$).
- The relevant transistors are M5 (access) and M2 (PMOS pull-up of the opposite inverter).

**Step 2 — Current balance during write.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{D,AX,\text{write}} &= I_{D,PU,\text{write}} \\[4pt]
\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{AX}\!\left[(V_{DD} - V_{THN})V_Q - \tfrac{1}{2}V_Q^{2}\right] &= \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{PU}\!\left[(V_{DD} - \vert V_{THP}\vert)V_{DS,PU} - \tfrac{1}{2}V_{DS,PU}^{2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical design rule.** For the write to succeed, the strength of the access transistor relative to the PMOS pull-up must satisfy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\text{PR} = \frac{(W/L)_{PU}}{(W/L)_{AX}} \leq 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0}
\end{aligned}
$$

A small PR means access NMOS is *stronger* than pull-up PMOS — easy to write, but a large PR means the PMOS holds firmly — hard to overwrite.

## 3.3 Derivation: Static Noise Margin (Butterfly Curve)

**Statement:** SNM is the largest DC noise voltage that can be superimposed on the storage nodes without flipping the cell.

**Step 1 — Construct the VTCs.** Plot the VTC of inverter I1 (input `Q`, output `QB`) and the mirrored VTC of inverter I2 (input `QB`, output `Q`) on the same axes. The two curves form a **butterfly-shaped** graph.

**Step 2 — Embed the two diagonal lines of the cell.** During a read, the cross-coupled inverters are also loaded by the access transistors connected to the pre-charged bit-lines. This *rotates* the VTCs into a family of "separable" curves defined by:
- $V_{out} = f(V_{in} - V_n)$ (lower curve, with noise $V_n$ subtracted)
- $V_{out}^{-1}$ (upper curve, mirrored)

**Step 3 — Embed the two lines representing the cell's static condition.** The two lines are:
- $V_{out} = V_{in}$ (45° line passing through origin)
- $V_{out} = V_{in} - V_n$ (45° line offset by noise)

**Step 4 — Measure SNM geometrically.** The maximum square that can be inscribed between the lower VTC and the upper mirror VTC, bounded by the 45° lines, defines the **SNM**.

**Step 5 — Closed-form approximation (Seevinck).** For a symmetric cell, an analytical estimate is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\text{SNM} \approx V_{THN} - \frac{1}{\text{CR}+1}\sqrt{\frac{\text{CR}}{\text{CR}+1}}\,\frac{V_{DD} - 2V_{THN}}{1 + \dfrac{\text{PR}}{\text{CR}+1}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This closed form is rarely required at KTU, but the **graphical butterfly method** is a 7-mark KTU 2024 favourite.

## 3.4 Derivation: DRAM Read Bit-line Swing

**Statement:** When the word-line opens, the storage capacitor $C_S$ (initial voltage $V_S$) shares its charge with the bit-line capacitance $C_{BL}$ (pre-charged to $V_{DD}/2$).

**Step 1 — Charge conservation.** The total charge on the floating storage+bit-line system is conserved:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{before}} &= C_S V_S + C_{BL} \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \\[4pt]
Q_{\text{after}} &= (C_S + C_{BL}) V_{BL,\text{final}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate and solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{BL,\text{final}} &= \frac{C_S V_S + C_{BL}\,V_{DD}/2}{C_S + C_{BL}} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \Delta V_{BL} &= V_{BL,\text{final}} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_{BL}}\!\left(V_S - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum case.** For a fully-charged cell ($V_S = V_{DD}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\Delta V_{BL,\max} = \frac{C_S}{C_S + C_{BL}}\cdot\frac{V_{DD}}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

With $C_S = 25$ fF and $C_{BL} = 200$ fF, $\Delta V_{BL,\max} = 0.111 \times 0.9 \approx 100$ mV. This is why DRAM **must** use a sense amplifier.

## 3.5 Derivation: DRAM Retention Time

**Step 1 — Capacitor discharge model.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_S \frac{dV_S}{dt} = -I_{LEAK}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $V_{DD}$ to minimum detectable $V_{\min}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{V_{DD}}^{V_{\min}} dV_S = -\frac{I_{LEAK}}{C_S}\int_{0}^{t_{RET}} dt \\[4pt]
V_{\min} - V_{DD} = -\frac{I_{LEAK}\cdot t_{RET}}{C_S} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow t_{RET} = \frac{C_S (V_{DD} - V_{\min})}{I_{LEAK}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — KTU numerical example.** With $C_S = 25$ fF, $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $V_{\min} = 0.5$ V, $I_{LEAK} = 1$ pA:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{RET} = \frac{25\times 10^{-15} \times 1.3}{1 \times 10^{-12}} = 32.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is comfortably above the JEDEC **64 ms** requirement for standard DDR.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Driving Equation | Critical Design Lever | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Read Stability | $\text{CR} \geq 1.2$ | Increase $W_{PD}$ or reduce $W_{AX}$ | Quote 1.2–1.5 |
| Write Margin | $\text{PR} \leq 1.5$ | Reduce $W_{PU}$ or increase $W_{AX}$ | Quote ≤ 1.5 |
| Hold Stability | $\text{SNM}_{hold} > 0.2V_{DD}$ | At $V_{DD} = 0.7$ V, must use 8T/10T | Mention DRV |
| DRAM Density | $1T1C$ per cell | $C_S$ in trench or stacked | $6F^2$ rule |
| DRAM Speed | $\Delta V_{BL}$ sensed | Increase $C_S$ or reduce $C_{BL}$ | 100 mV typical |
| Retention | $t_{RET} = C_S \Delta V / I_{LEAK}$ | Reduce leakage, increase $C_S$ | 64 ms JEDEC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
